<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Short Answer) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the Reliability Function $R(t)$ and state any three of its mathematical properties.**

**Model Answer:**

The Reliability Function $R(t)$ is defined as the probability that a component performs its intended function without failure for a specified duration $t$ under stated conditions. Mathematically:

$$
R(t) = P(T > t)
$$

where $T$ is the random time-to-failure.

**Three properties:**
1. **Initial Condition:** $R(0) = 1$ — A new component is functional at $t = 0$.
2. **Terminal Condition:** $R(\infty) = 0$ — Every component eventually fails.
3. **Monotonicity:** $R(t)$ is a non-increasing function of $t$, i.e., $R(t_1) \geq R(t_2)$ for $t_1 < t_2$.

---

### Question 2 (Short Answer) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Derive the relationship between the Reliability Function $R(t)$ and the Hazard Function $\lambda(t)$.**

**Model Answer:**

Starting from the definition of hazard function $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ and substituting $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$:

$$
\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

Separating variables and integrating from $0$ to $t$:

$$
\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du = -\ln R(t)
$$

Exponentiating:

$$
R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du\right)
$$

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A system consists of three independent components connected in series. The reliability of each component after 1000 hours is as follows: $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.90$, and $R_3 = 0.85$.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Compute the system reliability $R_s(1000)$. Also compute the unreliability of the system.**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** For components in series, the system functions only if **all** components function. Since the components are independent, we apply the product rule:

$$
R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given values:

$$
R_s(1000) = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.85
$$

**Step 3:** Compute step-by-step:

$$
0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.855
$$

$$
0.855 \times 0.85 = 0.72675
$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, system reliability at 1000 hours:

$$
R_s(1000) \approx 0.7268 \text{ or } 72.68\%
$$

**Step 5:** Unreliability is the complement:

$$
F_s(1000) = 1 - R_s(1000) = 1 - 0.7268 = 0.2732 \text{ or } 27.32\%
$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**If the components were instead connected in parallel, compute the new system reliability at 1000 hours. Comment on the result.**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** For components in parallel, the system functions if **at least one** component functions. The unreliability is the product of individual unreliabilities:

$$
F_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} F_i(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} [1 - R_i(t)]
$$

**Step 2:** Compute individual unreliabilities:

$$
F_1 = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05
$$

$$
F_2 = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10
$$

$$
F_3 = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15
$$

**Step 3:** Compute system unreliability:

$$
F_s(1000) = 0.05 \times 0.10 \times 0.15 = 0.00075
$$

**Step 4:** System reliability:

$$
R_s(1000) = 1 - F_s(1000) = 1 - 0.00075 = 0.99925
$$

**Comment:** The parallel configuration provides a **dramatic improvement** in reliability (from 72.68% in series to 99.925% in parallel). This illustrates the **redundancy principle** in reliability engineering — adding backup components significantly enhances system survival probability, but at the cost of increased weight, cost, and complexity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**The failure density function of an electronic component is given by $f(t) = \dfrac{1}{1000} e^{-t/1000}$ for $t \geq 0$ (with $t$ measured in hours).**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Determine: (i) the Reliability Function $R(t)$, (ii) the Hazard Function $\lambda(t)$, and (iii) the MTTF.**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Recognize that $f(t) = \dfrac{1}{1000} e^{-t/1000}$ is the PDF of an **exponential distribution** with parameter $\theta = 1000$ hours.

**Step 2 — Reliability Function $R(t)$:**

$$
F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(u)\, du = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{1}{1000} e^{-u/1000}\, du
$$

$$
F(t) = \left[-e^{-u/1000}\right]_{0}^{t} = 1 - e^{-t/1000}
$$

Therefore:

$$
R(t) = 1 - F(t) = e^{-t/1000}
$$

**Step 3 — Hazard Function $\lambda(t)$:**

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\frac{1}{1000} e^{-t/1000}}{e^{-t/1000}} = \frac{1}{1000}
$$

The hazard function is **constant** at $\lambda = 1/1000$ per hour, which is the signature of exponential distribution.

**Step 4 — Mean Time To Failure (MTTF):**

For exponential distribution, MTTF equals the scale parameter:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-t/1000}\, dt
$$

$$
\text{MTTF} = \left[-1000 \cdot e^{-t/1000}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = 1000 \text{ hours}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Compute: (i) the probability that the component survives beyond 2000 hours, (ii) the probability that it survives beyond 3000 hours given it has already survived 1000 hours.**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Probability of surviving beyond 2000 hours:**

$$
R(2000) = e^{-2000/1000} = e^{-2}
$$

Using $e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$:

$$
R(2000) \approx 0.1353 \text{ or } 13.53\%
$$

**Step 2 — Conditional reliability for 3000 hours given survival to 1000 hours:**

Using the conditional reliability formula:

$$
R(t \mid T) = \frac{R(T+t)}{R(T)}
$$

Here, we want survival beyond 3000 hours (so total time = 3000) given survival to 1000 hours (so $T = 1000$, additional time $t = 2000$):

$$
R(2000 \mid 1000) = \frac{R(1000 + 2000)}{R(1000)} = \frac{R(3000)}{R(1000)}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute individual reliabilities:

$$
R(1000) = e^{-1000/1000} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679
$$

$$
R(3000) = e^{-3000/1000} = e^{-3} \approx 0.0498
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the ratio:

$$
R(2000 \mid 1000) = \frac{0.0498}{0.3679} \approx 0.1353
$$

**Interpretation:** This result equals $R(2000) \approx 0.1353$. This confirms the **memoryless property** of the exponential distribution — the component's future survival probability does not depend on its past. A 1000-hour-old component has the same reliability outlook as a brand-new component.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Reliability Function Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the integration limits:** When deriving $R(t) = e^{-\int \lambda(u)du}$, students often write $\int \lambda(t)\, dt$ without limits. Always specify $0$ to $t$.
>
> 2. **Confusing MTTF with MTBF:** MTTF applies to **non-repairable** systems; MTBF applies to **repairable** systems. KTU expects this distinction.
>
> 3. **Series vs. Parallel misuse:** Series configuration: $R_s = \prod R_i$. Parallel configuration: $R_s = 1 - \prod(1-R_i)$. Mixing these is the #1 source of marks lost in Part B.
>
> 4. **Units inconsistency:** Failure rate $\lambda$ has units of $hr^{-1}$, so $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ requires $\lambda t$ to be dimensionless. Always verify units.
>
> 5. **Forgetting $R(0) = 1$:** Boundary condition is essential. Examiners deduct marks if boundary conditions are not stated explicitly.
>
> 6. **Conditional reliability sign error:** $R(t \mid T) = \dfrac{R(T+t)}{R(T)}$, not $\dfrac{R(T)}{R(T+t)}$. Reversing this loses full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** $R(t) = P(T > t)$ — the survival probability of a component at time $t$.
- **Complementary Relation:** $R(t) + F(t) = 1$ — always pair reliability with unreliability.
- **Boundary Conditions:** $R(0) = 1$ and $R(\infty) = 0$ — must be stated for full marks.
- **Monotonicity:** $R(t)$ is non-increasing — once failed, the component stays failed.
- **PDF Connection:** $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ — the density of failure times.
- **Hazard Connection:** $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du\right)$ — the most powerful working formula.
- **MTTF Formula:** $\text{MTTF} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$ — area under the reliability curve.
- **Series System:** $R_s = \prod R_i$ — all components must survive.
- **Parallel System:** $R_s = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$ — at least one must survive.
- **Conditional Reliability:** $R(t \mid T) = \dfrac{R(T+t)}{R(T)}$ — survival for additional time.
- **Exponential Special Case:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — constant failure rate, memoryless property.
- **Always check units:** $\lambda t$ must be dimensionless inside the exponential.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MTTF and MTBF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** MTTF and MTBF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Reliability Engineering**, the expected lifetime behavior of components and systems is quantified using two closely related, yet fundamentally distinct, statistical measures: **MTTF** and **MTBF**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)**
> **MTTF** is defined as the *expected (average) time until a component fails for the first time*. It is the arithmetic mean of the **Time-To-Failure (TTF)** random variable. **MTTF applies strictly to NON-REPAIRABLE systems** (also called one-shot or disposable items).

> [!IMPORTANT] **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)**
> **MTBF** is defined as the *expected (average) time interval between two consecutive failures* of a repairable system. It is the arithmetic mean of the **Time-Between-Failures (TBF)** random variable. **MTBF applies to REPAIRABLE systems** that are restored to a "good-as-new" state after each repair.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE] **The Two-Bulb Analogy**
> Imagine you work in a factory that uses light bulbs. The factory manager wants to know how long, on average, the bulbs last.
>
> - **The Disposable Bulb (MTTF):** You buy a cheap LED bulb. When it burns out, you throw it away and replace it with a brand-new one. You record how long the *first bulb* lasted, the *second bulb*, the *third bulb*, and so on. The average of these recorded lifetimes is the **MTTF**. The system is **never repaired**, only replaced.
>
> - **The Industrial Generator (MTBF):** Now consider a large diesel generator. When it breaks down, a technician fixes it and brings it back to working order. You measure the time from *failure 1 to failure 2*, from *failure 2 to failure 3*, and so on. The average of these intervals is the **MTBF**. The system is **repaired**, not discarded.
>
> *Think of MTTF as "How long until this item gives up?" and MTBF as "How long can I use this thing before it breaks again, assuming I fix it every time?"*

### 1.3 The Third Vital Statistic: MTTR

> [!IMPORTANT] **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)**
> **MTTR** is the average time required to repair a failed system and restore it to operational status. It includes diagnosis, active repair, and reassembly time.
>
> **Universal Relationship for Repairable Systems:**
>
> $$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$$
>
> However, when repair times are very small compared to operating times, engineers often approximate:
>
> $$\text{MTBF} \approx \text{MTTF}$$

### 1.4 Why Distinguish MTTF vs MTBF?

The distinction is not merely semantic. The mathematics of the two quantities differs based on whether the system is **renewed** after failure:

| System Type | Action After Failure | Relevant Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-Repairable** (Resistor, Capacitor, Bulb, Fuse) | Discard & Replace | **MTTF** |
| **Repairable** (Generator, Car, Server, Pump) | Restore to "As-Good-As-New" | **MTBF** |

> [!WARNING] **Common KTU Board Mistake:** Writing "MTBF of a bulb" or "MTTF of a generator" leads to mark deduction in ESE answers. Always match the metric to the system type.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Foundation (Why the Averages Are Defined This Way)

The reliability function $R(t)$ represents the probability that a component survives beyond time $t$. Its derivative is the **probability density function of failure**, $f(t)$:

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Since $R(t)$ is monotonically decreasing for aging systems, $f(t) \geq 0$.

The **expected value** of any non-negative random variable $X$ with density $f(x)$ is:

$$E[X] = \int_{0}^{\infty} x \cdot f(x) \, dx$$

By substituting $X = T$ (failure time) and applying integration by parts, we arrive at the most elegant formulation:

> [!NOTE] **Reliability Integral Theorem**
> The mean life of a non-repairable component is equal to the *area under the reliability curve* from $t=0$ to $t=\infty$.
>
> $$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$$
>
> This identity is known as the **Reliability Integral**, and it is the single most important formula in Module 1 of PEMET747.

### 2.2 The Exponential Distribution Shortcut

For the special (and very common) case of **constant failure rate** $\lambda$ (the Exponential reliability model), we have:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

Substituting into the reliability integral gives:

$$\text{MTTF}_{\text{exponential}} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

For **repairable systems** whose times between failures are exponentially distributed with rate $\lambda$, each "renewal" produces an independent identically distributed (i.i.d.) TBF random variable, so:

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{exponential}} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Key Conclusion:** For exponential (constant hazard) systems, **MTTF = MTBF = 1/λ**. This is why many KTU numerical problems simply ask for "mean life" and the answer is the reciprocal of the failure rate.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Description | Valid For | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\,dt$ | Expected value of TTF | All distributions | hours, cycles, km |
| $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$ | Area under reliability curve | All distributions | hours, cycles, km |
| $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Operating time plus repair time | Repairable systems | hours |
| $\text{MTBF}_{\text{exp}} = \text{MTTF}_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Constant hazard model | Exponential distribution | hours |
| $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Steady-state uptime fraction | Repairable, exponential | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTTF}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Failure rate reciprocal | Exponential distribution | failures/hour |
| $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-t / \text{MTTF}}$ | Reliability at time $t$ | Exponential distribution | dimensionless (0 to 1) |

> [!WARNING] **Pipe-Symbol Trap:** In the formulas above, conditional notation like $R(t\,\vert\,\text{exp})$ has been written using $\vert$ as a separator instead of the vertical bar character to keep the markdown table stable.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** MTTF of bearings, avionics; MTBF of engines between overhauls.
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** MTTF of electrolytic capacitors (used to determine warranty period).
- **Data Centers:** MTBF of hard drives used to predict annual failure rates and storage redundancy needs.
- **Power Systems:** MTBF of circuit breakers used in grid reliability indices like SAIDI and SAIFI.
- **Automotive:** MTTF of brake pads (wear-out item, non-repairable) vs MTBF of the engine block (repairable).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: MTTF as Expected Value of Failure Time

We want to derive $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$ rigorously.

**Step 1 — Start from the definition of expected value.**
The mean time to failure is, by definition, the expected value of the random time-to-failure $T$, whose density function is $f(t)$:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Recall the relation between $f(t)$ and $R(t)$.**
By definition, $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$. Differentiating $R(t)$ and taking the negative gives back the probability density of failure.

**Step 3 — Apply integration by parts.**
Let $u = t$ and $dv = f(t)\, dt$, so $du = dt$ and $v = -R(t)$. Then:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt = \Big[-t \cdot R(t)\Big]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the boundary term.**
For any physically realistic system, $R(t) \to 0$ faster than $t \to \infty$ (i.e., the survival probability vanishes at infinity), so $-t \cdot R(t)\vert_{t \to \infty} = 0$. At $t = 0$, $-t \cdot R(0) = 0$ since $R(0) = 1$ and $t = 0$. Hence the boundary term vanishes:

$$\Big[-t \cdot R(t)\Big]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - 0 = 0$$

**Step 5 — Arrive at the final identity.**

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt}$$

This confirms the **Reliability Integral Theorem**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: MTTF for the Exponential Distribution

**Step 1 — Write the exponential reliability function.**
For constant failure rate $\lambda$:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the reliability integral.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate.**
The antiderivative of $e^{-\lambda t}$ is $-\dfrac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate limits.**
At $t \to \infty$: $e^{-\lambda \cdot \infty} \to 0$, so the upper term is $0$.
At $t = 0$: $-\dfrac{1}{\lambda} e^{0} = -\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$.

Therefore:

$$\text{MTTF} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude the result.**

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_{\text{exponential}} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: MTBF for a Repairable Exponential System

**Step 1 — Define the renewal process.**
After each repair, the system is "as good as new." The successive time-between-failures $T_1, T_2, T_3, \ldots$ are independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) exponential random variables with parameter $\lambda$.

**Step 2 — Express MTBF as expectation of TBF.**

$$\text{MTBF} = E[T_i] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Recognize this is the Gamma/Erlang-1 integral.**
The integral is the standard expectation of an exponential distribution:

$$\text{MTBF} = \lambda \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda^2} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

(One can also obtain this by recalling that $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ and that for exponential renewal with negligible repair time, $\text{MTTR} \to 0$.)

**Step 4 — Conclude the result.**

$$\boxed{\text{MTBF}_{\text{exponential}} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem:** The reliability of an industrial pump is modeled by an exponential distribution with failure rate $\lambda = 0.0025$ failures per hour. Calculate (i) the MTTF, (ii) the reliability at $t = 100$ hours, and (iii) the steady-state availability if MTTR is 8 hours.

**Solution:**

**(i) MTTF:**
The pump is non-repairable in this scenario. Using the exponential formula:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0025} = 400 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) Reliability at 100 hours:**

$$R(100) = e^{-\lambda \cdot 100} = e^{-0.0025 \times 100} = e^{-0.25} \approx 0.7788$$

So there is approximately **77.88%** probability that the pump survives beyond 100 hours.

**(iii) Steady-state availability (now treating the pump as repairable):**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{400}{400 + 8} = \frac{400}{408} \approx 0.9804$$

The pump is available approximately **98.04%** of the time.

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation for Valuation:** Always explicitly state *which* system type (repairable vs non-repairable) you are assuming before plugging numbers into the formula. This is a frequent KTU grading checkpoint.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: How MTTF and MTBF Are Computed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start with Reliability Function R of t] --> B{Is the System Repairable?}
    B -- No --> C[Compute MTTF via Reliability Integral]
    B -- Yes --> D[Compute MTTF via Reliability Integral]
    C --> E[Apply Distribution Law]
    D --> F[Add MTTR to Obtain MTBF]
    F --> G[Apply Distribution Law]
    E --> H{Failure Rate Constant?}
    G --> H
    H -- Yes --> I[MTTF equals MTBF equals 1 over lambda]
    H -- No --> J[Numeric or Monte Carlo Evaluation]
    I --> K[Final Mean Life Metric]
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: Lifecycle of a Repairable System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[State 1: Operating Good as New] -->|Failure Event| S2[State 2: Failed Down]
    S2 -->|Repair Action Duration MTTR| S1
    S1 -.Time elapsed TBF.-> S3[Next Failure After MTBF Interval]
    S3 -.Cycle Repeats.-> S2
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The horizontal solid arrows show the alternating **Operating → Failed → Operating** states.
- The dashed arrows represent the **MTBF interval** measured between successive failures.
- The downward transition corresponds to MTTR (Mean Time To Repair).
- The diagram illustrates that **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR**, with MTTF being the "up" duration and MTTR the "down" duration of a single cycle.

### 4.3 Conceptual Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Under Study] --> B{Replace or Repair after Failure}
    B -- Discard and Replace --> C[Non Repairable]
    B -- Restore to Working --> D[Repairable]
    C --> E[Use MTTF]
    D --> F[Use MTBF]
    E --> G[Example Light Bulb Capacitor Fuse]
    F --> H[Example Generator Server Car Engine]
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** When answering KTU 2-mark or 3-mark questions, students often draw a small flowchart like the one above. Examiners award full credit when both branches are correctly labeled and a representative example is given for each.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define MTTF and MTBF. State the fundamental relationship between them for repairable systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** The arithmetic mean of the time elapsed until the first failure of a *non-repairable* component, given by the expected value of the Time-To-Failure random variable.

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$$

**MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** The arithmetic mean of the time interval between two successive failures of a *repairable* system that is restored to a like-new condition after each repair.

$$\text{MTBF} = E[T_i], \quad i = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Fundamental Relationship for Repairable Systems:**

$$\boxed{\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

where MTTR is the Mean Time To Repair. For systems where MTTR is negligible, MTBF ≈ MTTF. *(Full 3 marks: 1 mark each for the two definitions and 1 mark for the relationship with MTTR definition.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] For an exponentially distributed failure time with rate $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour, compute the MTTF and the reliability at $t = 800$ hours.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — MTTF calculation (2 marks):**
For the exponential distribution, the failure rate is constant and:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 2000 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 — Reliability at 800 hours (1 mark):**

$$R(800) = e^{-\lambda \cdot 800} = e^{-(5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 800} = e^{-0.4} \approx 0.6703$$

Therefore, MTTF = 2000 hours and $R(800) \approx 0.6703$ (or 67.03%).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Module 1 Internal Choice Note:** As per KTU 2024 scheme ESE pattern, the student must answer **either Question A or Question B** from the Module 1 section. Both are provided below for full preparation coverage.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define the reliability function $R(t)$ and the failure rate $\lambda(t)$. Derive the relation $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ and explain the meaning of MTTF using the reliability integral. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A repairable industrial motor has an exponential failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0008$ failures/hour. If the MTTR is 12 hours, determine (i) MTTF, (ii) MTBF, and (iii) the steady-state availability. Also find the reliability of the motor for a 250-hour mission if treated as non-repairable. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions and Derivation (7 marks):**

*Reliability function:* $R(t)$ is the probability that a component performs its intended function without failure for a duration $t$, given that it was functioning at $t = 0$.

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

*Failure rate:* $\lambda(t)$ is the instantaneous rate of failure at age $t$, conditional on survival up to that age.

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

*Derivation of $f(t)$:* The probability that failure occurs in an infinitesimal interval $[t, t+dt]$ is $f(t)\, dt$. The unreliability is $1 - R(t) = F(t)$, the cumulative distribution function. Differentiating:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\big[1 - R(t)\big] = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

*Meaning of MTTF via reliability integral:* The mean time to failure equals the area under the reliability curve from 0 to infinity. This is because $R(t)$ represents the surviving fraction at age $t$, and integrating over all $t$ gives the expected lifetime.

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$$

This formula is universal (applies to any lifetime distribution), making it the most general definition used by reliability engineers.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.0008$/hour, MTTR = 12 hours.

**(i) MTTF (2 marks):** For an exponential distribution:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0008} = 1250 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) MTBF (2 marks):** Using the relationship:

$$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 1250 + 12 = 1262 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Steady-state availability (1 mark):**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{1262}{1262 + 12} = \frac{1262}{1274} \approx 0.9906$$

The motor is available approximately **99.06%** of the time.

**Reliability for 250-hour mission (2 marks):** Treating the motor as non-repairable during the mission:

$$R(250) = e^{-\lambda \cdot 250} = e^{-0.0008 \times 250} = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187$$

Therefore, the probability of successful 250-hour operation is approximately **81.87%**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Distinguish clearly between MTTF and MTBF. Why is MTTF used for fuses and MTBF for diesel generators? Provide one real-world example for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A digital communication system has 500 components in series, each with exponential failure rate $\lambda_i = 10^{-5}$/hour. Compute the system MTTF and reliability at $t = 100$ hours. If the system is repairable with MTTR = 20 hours, find the MTBF and availability. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Distinction (7 marks):**

| Aspect | MTTF | MTBF |
|---|---|---|
| System Type | Non-repairable | Repairable |
| Random Variable | Time-To-Failure (TTF) | Time-Between-Failures (TBF) |
| Cycle | Single use until first failure | Multiple cycles of fail-repair-fail |
| Practical Use | Warranty, life prediction | Maintenance scheduling |
| Relationship | $\text{MTTF} = \int R(t)\,dt$ | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ |

*Why fuses use MTTF:* When a fuse blows (fails), it cannot be repaired in place — it must be discarded and replaced with a new one. Therefore, only the time to the first failure is meaningful, making MTTF the appropriate metric.

*Why diesel generators use MTBF:* A diesel generator is repaired (not scrapped) when it fails. It is restored to operational condition and continues in service. The relevant engineering metric is the average time the engineer can expect between successive breakdowns, i.e., MTBF.

*Real-world example of MTTF:* A laptop battery — once its capacity degrades, it is replaced, not repaired.

*Real-world example of MTBF:* An aircraft engine — overhauled and returned to service after each maintenance event.

---

**(b) Series System Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** 500 components in series, each $\lambda_i = 10^{-5}$/hour.

**Step 1 — System failure rate (1 mark):**
For independent exponential components in series:

$$\lambda_{\text{sys}} = \sum_{i=1}^{500} \lambda_i = 500 \times 10^{-5} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ /hour}$$

**Step 2 — System MTTF (2 marks):**

$$\text{MTTF}_{\text{sys}} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{sys}}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 200 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 — Reliability at 100 hours (1 mark):**

$$R(100) = e^{-\lambda_{\text{sys}} \cdot 100} = e^{-5 \times 10^{-3} \times 100} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$$

So the system has about **60.65%** probability of operating successfully for 100 hours.

**Step 4 — MTBF with MTTR = 20 hours (2 marks):**

$$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 200 + 20 = 220 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5 — Availability (1 mark):**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{220}{220 + 20} = \frac{220}{240} \approx 0.9167$$

The system is available about **91.67%** of the time.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PEMET747 MTTF/MTBF Problems**
>
> 1. **Mixing MTTF and MTBF:** Using MTTF when the question asks about a repairable system (or vice versa) typically loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Writing "MTTF = 200" without specifying *hours*, *cycles*, or *kilometers* costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the reliability integral:** For non-exponential distributions (Weibull, Gamma), the simple $1/\lambda$ shortcut is invalid. You MUST integrate $R(t)$ from 0 to infinity.
> 4. **Wrong sign in derivative:** Writing $f(t) = +\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ instead of $-$ is a fundamental error that propagates through the entire derivation.
> 5. **MTTR omission:** When asked for MTBF of a repairable system without explicit MTTR, state the assumption "MTTR is negligible" or "MTTR = 0" before writing MTBF ≈ MTTF.
> 6. **In Series Systems:** Many students incorrectly multiply reliabilities by $R_i(t)$ and then divide by $\lambda_{\text{sys}}$ by accident. Always sum the failure rates first.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MTTF** = Mean Time To Failure → applies to **non-repairable** systems.
- **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures → applies to **repairable** systems.
- **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair → duration of the "down" state.
- **Master Formula (Reliability Integral):**
  $$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$$
- **Exponential Special Case:**
  $$\text{MTTF} = \text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$
- **Repairable Relationship:**
  $$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$$
- **Steady-State Availability:**
  $$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$$
- **Reliability at time t (exponential):**
  $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-t/\text{MTTF}}$$
- **Series Exponential Failure Rate:**
  $$\lambda_{\text{sys}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$
- Always **state the assumption** (constant $\lambda$, as-good-as-new repair, negligible MTTR, independence) before plugging numbers.
- **Units discipline:** Write units (hours, cycles, km) in your final numerical answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazard Rate Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hazard Rate Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Hazard Rate Function**, denoted as $h(t)$ (also known as the **Instantaneous Failure Rate**, **Force of Mortality**, or **Conditional Failure Rate**), is defined as the **limit of the conditional probability of failure in a small time interval $[t, t+\Delta t]$, given that the system has survived up to time $t$, divided by the length of the interval**, as the interval approaches zero.

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}$$

Mathematically, in its most exam-ready form, it is the ratio of the **Probability Density Function (PDF)** of failure to the **Reliability Function (Survival Function)**:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style)**
> "The hazard rate function represents the **instantaneous rate of failure** of a component or system at time $t$, given that it has been operating successfully up to that time. It is mathematically expressed as the ratio of the unconditional failure density to the survival probability."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition

Imagine a classroom of **100 students** who have all reached Class 10 without failing any year. The **hazard rate** answers this question:

> *"Out of those 100 students who are currently in Class 10, how many (on average) are expected to drop out / fail during the 11th year of their academic life, per unit time?"*

- If $h(t) = 0.05$ per year for Class 10 students → it means roughly **5 out of 100** students who have survived till Class 10 will drop out in the coming year.
- The hazard rate is **NOT a probability**. It is a **rate** (failures per unit time). Hence, $h(t) > 1$ is perfectly valid in high-stress components.

### Real-World Engineering Example
Consider a **ball bearing** in a rotating machine. After 1000 hours of successful operation, the engineer asks: *"What is the rate at which bearings are failing right now (per hour), among the bearings that have survived till 1000 hours?"* — This instantaneous rate is precisely the **Hazard Rate Function**.

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight for KTU Students**
> $h(t)$ is **conditional on survival**. It does not ask "Will it fail in 1000 hours?" — it asks "Given it has already survived, what is the **rate** of failure *right now*?"

---

## 1.3 Core Properties of the Hazard Rate Function

1. **Always Non-Negative**: $h(t) \geq 0$ for all $t \geq 0$. A negative hazard rate is physically meaningless.
2. **Not a Probability Density**: It can exceed 1 (e.g., $h(t) = 2.5$ per hour is valid). It has units of *failures per unit time* (e.g., $\text{hr}^{-1}$, $\text{cycles}^{-1}$).
3. **No Upper Bound**: Unlike probabilities bounded by 1, the hazard rate is **unbounded** above.
4. **Shape Determines Aging**: The monotonicity of $h(t)$ indicates whether the component is **improving** (decreasing), **constant** (memoryless), or **deteriorating** (increasing) with time.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Highlighted

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Engineering Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Hazard Rate (Failure Rate)** | $\mathbf{h(t)}$ | **Failures per hour, per cycle, per km** |
| **Reliability** | $\mathbf{R(t)}$ | Dimensionless (Probability, 0 to 1) |
| **Cumulative Hazard** | $\mathbf{H(t)}$ | Dimensionless |
| **Mean Time To Failure** | $\mathbf{MTTF}$ | Hours, cycles, km |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation Logic Flow — From Probability to Hazard Rate

The hazard rate is constructed through a **three-layer conditional probability chain**:

### Layer 1 — Joint Event Probability
The probability of failure in $[t, t+\Delta t]$ **AND** survival till $t$ is:

$$P(t \leq T \leq t + \Delta t) = F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)$$

### Layer 2 — Conditional Probability via Bayes' Rule
Since survival till $t$ means $T > t$, the conditional probability is:

$$P(t \leq T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t) = \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{1 - F(t)} = \frac{R(t) - R(t+\Delta t)}{R(t)}$$

### Layer 3 — Instantaneous Rate (Divide and Take Limit)

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{R(t) - R(t+\Delta t)}{\Delta t \cdot R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **The Master Identity**
> $$\boxed{h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{d}{dt}\left[\ln R(t)\right]}$$
> This single equation is the **gateway to 80% of KTU Hazard Rate problems**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hazard Rate Definition** | $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ | Instantaneous failure rate given survival |
| 2 | **Cumulative Hazard Function** | $H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx$ | Total accumulated hazard up to time $t$ |
| 3 | **Reliability from Hazard** | $R(t) = \exp\left(-H(t)\right) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx\right)$ | Survival probability expressed in terms of $h(t)$ |
| 4 | **PDF from Hazard** | $f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t) = h(t) \cdot \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx\right)$ | Failure density using hazard function |
| 5 | **CDF from Hazard** | $F(t) = 1 - \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx\right)$ | Cumulative failure probability |
| 6 | **Conditional Failure Probability** | $P(t \leq T \leq t+\Delta t \mid T > t) \approx h(t)\cdot \Delta t$ | Approximate failure in next $\Delta t$ |
| 7 | **MTTF in terms of h(t)** | $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx\right) dt$ | Mean life expressed via hazard |
| 8 | **Constant Hazard (Exponential)** | $h(t) = \lambda$ (constant) | Memoryless behavior |
| 9 | **Weibull Hazard** | $h(t) = \alpha \beta t^{\beta-1}$ | Models increasing/decreasing failure |
| 10 | **Linearly Increasing Hazard** | $h(t) = a + bt$ | Wear-out dominated systems |

> [!NOTE] **KTU Board Tip**: Memorize Rows 1, 3, 4, 6, 7 — these alone solve 90% of the Part A and Part B questions in Module 1.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility Across Industries

| Industry | Use of Hazard Rate Function |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Predicting fatigue failure of turbine blades; **mandatory** in FAA reliability certification |
| **Nuclear Power** | Core melt-down risk modeling using **non-constant hazard** (Weibull) |
| **Automotive** | Warranty analysis — modeling component aging over years |
| **Electronics** | Semiconductor burn-in testing (infant mortality region) |
| **Biomedical Devices** | Pacemaker battery depletion rate over 10-year life |
| **Civil/Structural** | Bridge cable corrosion progression modeling |

---

## 2.4 Why the Hazard Rate is a Superior Metric

- It is **mathematically well-defined** even when $R(t)$ becomes very small (denominator issues do not arise).
- It directly reveals the **aging characteristic** of a component (IFR, DFR, CFR).
- It separates the **memory effect** of the failure distribution from the probability scale.
- It forms the foundation of **Proportional Hazards Models (Cox Model)** used in advanced reliability and survival analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — From Reliability to Hazard Rate

We start with the **first principles of conditional probability** and the **limit definition** of a derivative.

### Given
- $F(t)$ is the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of failure
- $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ is the Reliability (Survival) Function
- $f(t) = dF(t)/dt$ is the Probability Density Function (PDF)

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad P(t \leq T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t) &= \frac{P(t \leq T \leq t + \Delta t)}{P(T > t)} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad &= \frac{F(t + \Delta t) - F(t)}{1 - F(t)} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad &= \frac{\left[1 - R(t + \Delta t)\right] - \left[1 - R(t)\right]}{R(t)} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4:} \quad &= \frac{R(t) - R(t + \Delta t)}{R(t)} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 5:} \quad h(t) &= \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{1}{\Delta t} \cdot \frac{R(t) - R(t + \Delta t)}{R(t)} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 6:} \quad &= \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{R(t) - R(t + \Delta t)}{\Delta t} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 7:} \quad &= \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \left(-\frac{dR(t)}{dt}\right) \\[6pt]
\text{Step 8:} \quad h(t) &= -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Summary of Steps:**
- **Steps 1–2**: Apply the definition of conditional probability to the failure event.
- **Steps 3–4**: Substitute $F = 1 - R$ to express everything in terms of reliability.
- **Steps 5–6**: Take the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$ to convert a finite-difference to a derivative.
- **Step 7**: Recognize the derivative of a decreasing function as a negative quantity; the two negatives cancel.
- **Step 8**: Combine to yield the final master identity.

> [!IMPORTANT] Since $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$, we can also write: $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ — the most popular KTU form.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — From Hazard Rate to Reliability

This derivation is the **reverse of the above** and is the most asked 14-mark derivation in KTU exams.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad h(t) &= -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad h(t) \cdot dt &= -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad \int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx &= -\int_{R(0)}^{R(t)} \frac{du}{u} \quad \text{(where } u = R(x)\text{)} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4:} \quad \int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx &= -\left[\ln R(x)\right]_{R(0)}^{R(t)} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 5:} \quad \int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx &= -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0) \\[6pt]
\text{Step 6:} \quad \text{Assuming } R(0) = 1 \text{ (system initially good)}: \quad \int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx &= -\ln R(t) \\[6pt]
\text{Step 7:} \quad \ln R(t) &= -\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx \\[6pt]
\text{Step 8:} \quad \boxed{R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Summary of Steps:**
- **Step 1**: Start with the master identity.
- **Step 2**: Separate variables — move $R(t)$ to one side, $h(t)dt$ to the other.
- **Step 3**: Integrate both sides. Lower limit on left is $0$ (initial time); on right, the lower limit is $R(0)$.
- **Step 4**: Standard integral of $1/u$ gives $\ln u$.
- **Step 5**: Apply the limits of integration.
- **Step 6**: Use the engineering boundary condition $R(0) = 1$ (no failure at $t=0$).
- **Steps 7–8**: Exponentiate both sides to obtain $R(t)$ as a function of $h(t)$.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — PDF from Hazard Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad f(t) &= h(t) \cdot R(t) \quad \text{(from master identity)} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad f(t) &= h(t) \cdot \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a single-step result derived directly from the master identity and Derivation 2.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation 4 — MTTF via Hazard Function

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad \text{MTTF} &= \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad &= \int_{0}^{\infty} \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx\right) dt
\end{aligned}
$$

**Special Case — Constant Hazard (Exponential Distribution)**:
If $h(t) = \lambda$ (a constant), then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3:} \quad R(t) &= e^{-\lambda t} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4:} \quad \text{MTTF} &= \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 5:} \quad &= 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE] This proves the well-known result: for a **constant hazard rate**, the MTTF equals **$1/\lambda$** — a guaranteed KTU question.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Property | Constant $h(t)$ | Increasing $h(t)$ (Wear-out) | Decreasing $h(t)$ (Infant Mortality) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Distribution | Exponential | Weibull ($\beta > 1$) | Weibull ($\beta < 1$) |
| Memory | Memoryless (No aging) | Aging present | "Burn-in" effect |
| Reliability Decay | Pure exponential | Faster than exponential | Slower than exponential |
| Engineering Phase | Useful Life | Wear-out phase | Early failures |
| $\frac{dh}{dt}$ | $0$ | $> 0$ | $< 0$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — From Failure Density to Hazard Rate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["F(t) Cumulative Failure Probability"]:::input
    node2["R(t) Reliability Function = 1 minus F(t)"]:::process
    node3["f(t) PDF of Failure = derivative of F(t)"]:::process
    node4["h(t) Hazard Rate = f(t) divided by R(t)"]:::output
    node5["H(t) Cumulative Hazard = integral of h(t)"]:::output
    node6["Engineering Insight: Aging Behavior"]:::result

    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node3
    node2 --> node4
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node4 --> node6

    classDef input fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 The Famous Bathtub Curve — Hazard Rate vs Time

The **Bathtub Curve** is the single most-tested diagram in KTU Reliability Engineering. It maps the **three distinct life phases** of a typical engineering component.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE1["PHASE 1: INFANT MORTALITY"]
        nodeA["h(t) DECREASING"]
        nodeB["Cause: Defects, Poor Quality"]
        nodeC["Action: Burn-In Testing"]
    end

    subgraph PHASE2["PHASE 2: USEFUL LIFE"]
        nodeD["h(t) APPROXIMATELY CONSTANT"]
        nodeE["Cause: Random Stresses"]
        nodeF["Action: Preventive Maintenance"]
    end

    subgraph PHASE3["PHASE 3: WEAR-OUT"]
        nodeG["h(t) INCREASING"]
        nodeH["Cause: Aging, Corrosion, Fatigue"]
        nodeI["Action: Replacement, Overhaul"]
    end

    PHASE1 --> PHASE2
    PHASE2 --> PHASE3

    classDef phaseStyle fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class PHASE1,PHASE2,PHASE3 phaseStyle
```

### Phase-Wise Detailed Mapping

| Phase | Shape of $h(t)$ | Dominant Failure Mechanism | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Infant Mortality** | Decreasing (high initially) | Manufacturing defects, poor workmanship | **Burn-in screening** at factory |
| **Useful Life** | Approximately constant | Random overstress, accidents | Scheduled preventive maintenance |
| **Wear-out** | Increasing (rising sharply) | Fatigue, corrosion, wear | **Planned replacement** before end-of-life |

> [!NOTE] **KTU Board Note**: If asked to "sketch the bathtub curve and explain," always label all three regions, indicate the **trend (monotonicity) of $h(t)$**, and give **one engineering example per region**.

---


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define the Hazard Rate Function. Explain its physical significance with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

The Hazard Rate Function $h(t)$ is defined as the **instantaneous rate of failure at time $t$**, conditional on the survival of the component up to that time.

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Physical Significance:** $h(t)$ measures **how likely a component is to fail in the next instant, given that it has already survived till time $t$**. For example, in a fleet of 1000 electric motors that have been running for 5000 hours, the hazard rate at $t = 5000$ hours tells us the number of motors (per unit time) expected to fail among the survivors. A high $h(t)$ indicates a component entering the wear-out phase; a low $h(t)$ indicates either infant mortality phase ending or a highly reliable design.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**State and prove the relationship between the reliability function and the hazard rate function.**

**Model Answer:**

Starting from the definition:

$$h(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Separating variables:

$$h(t)\, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

Integrating both sides from $0$ to $t$, with the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$:

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx = -\ln R(t)$$

Therefore:

$$R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx\right)$$

This is the **fundamental relationship** linking reliability to the hazard rate.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT] As per **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation**, candidates must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B from the same module.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **reliability function $R(t)$** in terms of the **hazard rate function $h(t)$**, starting from first principles. Clearly state all assumptions and boundary conditions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a system, the hazard rate is given by $h(t) = 0.002\,t$ per hour (linearly increasing hazard). Find:
- (i) The reliability function $R(t)$
- (ii) The probability density function $f(t)$
- (iii) The MTTF of the system. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

**Step 1:** Write the definition of hazard rate:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 2:** Apply conditional probability:

$$P(t \leq T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t) = \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{1 - F(t)} = \frac{R(t) - R(t+\Delta t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 3:** Take the limit:

$$h(t) = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \left(-\frac{dR(t)}{dt}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange:

$$h(t)\, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 5:** Integrate from $0$ to $t$ (Boundary condition: $R(0) = 1$):

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx = -\int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{du}{u} = -\ln R(t) + \ln(1) = -\ln R(t)$$

**Step 6:** Final expression:

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx\right)}$$

**Assumptions:**
- Component is non-repairable
- System is "as-good-as-new" at $t = 0$, hence $R(0) = 1$
- Hazard rate is well-defined and integrable

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

**Given:** $h(t) = 0.002\,t$ (per hour)

**(i) Reliability Function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(t) &= \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} 0.002\, x\, dx\right) \\[6pt]
&= \exp\left(-0.002 \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{2}\bigg|_{0}^{t}\right) \\[6pt]
&= \exp\left(-0.001\, t^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-0.001\, t^{2}}}$$

**(ii) Probability Density Function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(t) &= h(t) \cdot R(t) = 0.002\, t \cdot e^{-0.001\, t^{2}} \\[6pt]
\boxed{f(t) &= 0.002\, t \cdot e^{-0.001\, t^{2}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Mean Time To Failure (MTTF):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF} &= \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-0.001\, t^{2}}\, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the standard Gaussian integral formula $\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-a t^{2}}\, dt = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\pi/a}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{where } a &= 0.001, \quad \text{so } \sqrt{\pi/a} = \sqrt{\pi/0.001} = \sqrt{3141.59} \approx 56.05 \\[6pt]
\text{MTTF} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 56.05 \approx 28.025 \text{ hours} \\[6pt]
\boxed{\text{MTTF} &\approx 28.03 \text{ hours}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Bathtub Curve** of hazard rate. Identify the three regions and describe the **physical causes** and **remedial measures** for failures in each region. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A component has a constant hazard rate of $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour. Compute:
- (i) The reliability at $t = 1000$ hours
- (ii) The probability that the component survives till 2000 hours, given it has survived 1000 hours
- (iii) The MTTF of the component. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

The **Bathtub Curve** is the qualitative plot of the **hazard rate $h(t)$ versus time $t$** for a typical engineering component, exhibiting three distinct regions:

**Region 1 — Infant Mortality Region (Burn-in):**
- $h(t)$ is **high initially** and **decreases** with time.
- **Cause:** Manufacturing defects, poor quality control, substandard materials, workmanship errors.
- **Remedy:** **Burn-in testing** at the factory, where components are operated under stress for a defined period; those that fail are discarded.

**Region 2 — Useful Life Region (Steady State):**
- $h(t)$ is **approximately constant** (low and flat).
- **Cause:** Random failures due to unpredictable overstresses, environmental shocks, accidents.
- **Remedy:** **Preventive maintenance** scheduling, condition monitoring, redundancy design.

**Region 3 — Wear-out Region:**
- $h(t)$ is **low initially but increases sharply** with time.
- **Cause:** Aging, fatigue, corrosion, wear, material degradation.
- **Remedy:** **Planned replacement** of components before end-of-life; refurbishment and overhaul.

> **Conclusion:** Reliability engineers aim to operate components **only within Region 2** by applying burn-in (to skip Region 1) and planned replacement (to avoid Region 3).

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Given:** $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour (constant hazard ⇒ **exponential distribution**)

**(i) Reliability at $t = 1000$ hours:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(1000) &= e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(5 \times 10^{-4})(1000)} = e^{-0.5} \\[6pt]
\boxed{R(1000) &\approx 0.6065}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Conditional Probability of survival from 1000 to 2000 hours:**

$$P(T > 2000 \mid T > 1000) = \frac{R(2000)}{R(1000)}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(2000) &= e^{-(5 \times 10^{-4})(2000)} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679 \\[6pt]
P(T > 2000 \mid T > 1000) &= \frac{0.3679}{0.6065} \approx 0.6065
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{P(T > 2000 \mid T > 1000) = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065}$$

**Key Insight:** This illustrates the **memoryless property** of the exponential distribution — the conditional survival probability over any 1000-hour interval is the **same** as the unconditional survival at $t = 1000$.

**(iii) MTTF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF} &= \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-4}} \\[6pt]
\boxed{\text{MTTF} &= 2000 \text{ hours}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing $h(t)$ with $f(t)$**: $h(t)$ is a **rate** (per unit time), not a probability density. It can exceed 1.
> 2. **Forgetting the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$**: When integrating to derive $R(t)$ from $h(t)$, the constant of integration is fixed by $R(0) = 1$. Omitting this step loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the conditional nature**: Writing $P(T \leq t+\Delta t)$ instead of $P(T \leq t+\Delta t \mid T > t)$ is a **fatal conceptual error**.
> 4. **Misapplying units**: If $h(t)$ is in $\text{hr}^{-1}$, $t$ must be in hours. Mixing units in numerical problems (e.g., 1000 hours with $h$ in $\text{min}^{-1}$) is a common mistake.
> 5. **Bathtub curve labeling**: Skipping the cause-and-remedy mapping for each region costs easy marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition (3 lines)**: $h(t) = f(t)/R(t) = -[dR(t)/dt]/R(t) = -d[\ln R(t)]/dt$.
- **Master Identity**: $R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx\right)$ — memorize the derivation.
- **$h(t)$ is NOT a probability** — it is a **rate** with units of inverse time.
- **$h(t) \geq 0$ always**; no upper bound.
- **$R(0) = 1$** is the universal boundary condition; $R(\infty) = 0$ for non-repairable systems.
- **Constant hazard ⇒ Exponential distribution** ⇒ MTTF $= 1/\lambda$ ⇒ **memoryless property**.
- **Linearly increasing hazard $h(t) = a + bt$** gives **Rayleigh distribution** in reliability.
- **Weibull hazard $h(t) = \alpha\beta t^{\beta-1}$**: $\beta < 1$ = infant mortality, $\beta = 1$ = constant, $\beta > 1$ = wear-out.
- **Bathtub curve has 3 regions**: Infant Mortality (decreasing) → Useful Life (constant) → Wear-out (increasing).
- **Conditional failure probability** in a small interval: $P(\text{fail in } \Delta t) \approx h(t) \cdot \Delta t$.
- **Cumulative Hazard**: $H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(x)\, dx = -\ln R(t)$.
- **KTU Examiner expects**: Sketch + Label + Boundary Condition + Numerical Application in every 14-mark answer.
- **Famous one-liner for viva**: *"Hazard rate is the conditional rate of failure — the price you pay for survival, paid per unit time."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bathtub Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bathtub Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Bathtub Curve** is the canonical graphical representation of the **time-dependent failure rate** (hazard rate) of a non-repairable population of components, systems, or mechanical assemblies, plotted as a function of operational age or usage cycles. In the KTU 2024 Reliability Engineering syllabus, it is the foundational qualitative model used to describe how the *instantaneous failure rate* $\lambda(t)$ evolves over the entire life-cycle of an engineering asset.

The curve derives its name from its distinctive shape: a high left-hand tail that drops sharply, a long flat central trough, and a steeply rising right-hand tail — visually identical to the cross-section of a household bathtub.

The three characteristic regions are:

1. **Region I – Infant Mortality (Burn-in / Debugging Phase):** Decreasing failure rate.
2. **Region II – Useful Life (Normal Operating Phase):** Approximately constant failure rate.
3. **Region III – Wear-Out (End-of-Life Phase):** Increasing failure rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the 2024 scheme, the bathtub curve is treated as the *qualitative bridge* linking elementary probability concepts to advanced reliability modeling. Examiners frequently anchor 3-mark and 7-mark questions on identifying the regions, the dominant failure mechanisms in each region, and the choice of appropriate statistical distributions.

### 1.2 The Hazard Rate Function – The True Y-Axis

The vertical axis of the bathtub curve is **not** the number of failures — it is the **instantaneous failure rate**, also called the **hazard rate** or **force of mortality**, denoted $\lambda(t)$ (with units typically expressed as *failures per million hours* or *FPMH*).

$$
\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T \le t + \Delta t \mid T \ge t)}{\Delta t}
$$

In plain language, $\lambda(t)$ answers the question: *"Given that a component has already survived up to time $t$, what is the probability it will fail in the next infinitesimally small instant $dt$?"*

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The Human Life Story

Imagine the failure rate of a brand-new car:

- **Region I (Infant Mortality):** On day one, the car is most likely to fail because of **manufacturing defects** — a loose wire, a wrongly torqued bolt, a defective sensor. As these "weak units" fail early and are weeded out, the surviving population becomes more reliable. This is why manufacturers often run a **burn-in test** (analogous to an extended test drive before shipping) to deliberately flush out early failures.

- **Region II (Useful Life):** Once the lemons are gone, the remaining cars fail only due to **random, unpredictable events** — a stone hits the windshield, a sudden voltage spike fries the ECU. These accidents are memoryless, meaning **how long the car has been running does not affect the chance of the next random failure**. The curve becomes flat.

- **Region III (Wear-Out):** After years of use, the engine accumulates wear, rubber seals harden, bearings fatigue, and the probability of failure rises sharply. The car is now in its **senescence phase**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memoryless Property:** Region II is mathematically modelled by the **Exponential Distribution**, which is the *only* continuous distribution in which the component "forgets" its age. Mathematically: $P(T > t + s \mid T > t) = P(T > s)$.

### 1.4 Failure Rate Magnitude – Industry Standard Metric

The **Failure Rate ($\lambda$)** is typically expressed in industry using the following **bold** standard unit:

- **FIT (Failures In Time) = 1 failure per $10^9$ device-hours**

A high-reliability IC may be specified at **< 10 FIT**, while a complex electromechanical assembly may be in the range of **1000–5000 FIT**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Regions — Exhaustive Logical Breakdown

#### **Region I — Infant Mortality Phase**

- **Shape of $\lambda(t)$:** Monotonically **decreasing** (convex curve approaching a minimum).
- **Dominant Failure Mechanism:** Manufacturing defects, sub-standard material inclusions, improper assembly tolerances, design oversights, quality-control escapes.
- **Statistical Model:** **Weibull Distribution with shape parameter $\beta < 1$** (typically $0.5 \le \beta \le 1$). The Rayleigh distribution is a special case with $\beta = 2$.
- **Engineering Mitigation Strategy:**
  - **Burn-in / Debugging:** Deliberate operation under stress to precipitate early failures.
  - **Environmental Stress Screening (ESS):** Vibration, thermal cycling, humidity exposure.
  - **Improved Quality Control (QC) and Six-Sigma methodologies.**
  - **Incoming inspection and component derating.**
- **Goal:** Push the curve *downward and leftward* so that the system enters Region II as early as possible.

#### **Region II — Useful Life Phase**

- **Shape of $\lambda(t)$:** **Constant** at a value $\lambda_0$ (the lowest, most stable failure rate of the curve).
- **Dominant Failure Mechanism:** Random external stresses — over-voltage transients, mechanical shock, environmental accidents, operator errors, chance overloads.
- **Statistical Model:** **Exponential Distribution** — uniquely characterized by its constant hazard.
- **Engineering Mitigation Strategy:**
  - **Preventive Maintenance** is *counter-productive* in this region (replacing a healthy part may introduce infant-mortality risk).
  - **Redundancy** (parallel/standby systems) is the preferred reliability-enhancement technique.
  - **Robust Design** against environmental stresses (EMI shielding, surge protection).
- **Key Property:** **Memorylessness** — the component has no "memory" of its operating time.

#### **Region III — Wear-Out Phase**

- **Shape of $\lambda(t)$:** Monotonically **increasing** (concave upward, often exponential or polynomial growth).
- **Dominant Failure Mechanism:** Cumulative degradation — fatigue crack propagation, corrosion, dielectric breakdown, bearing wear, solder joint creep, thermal aging.
- **Statistical Model:** **Weibull Distribution with shape parameter $\beta > 1$** (typically $\beta = 2$ to $\beta = 5$ for mechanical systems; $\beta = 3$ to $\beta = 4$ for electronics).
- **Engineering Mitigation Strategy:**
  - **Scheduled Preventive Replacement** based on Mean Life $E(T)$.
  - **Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)** using vibration analysis, oil debris sensors, thermography.
  - **Design for replacement** of wear-prone subassemblies.
  - **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)** strategies.

### 2.2 Mathematical Foundation – Linking $\lambda(t)$ to Reliability

The bathtub curve is fundamentally a plot of $\lambda(t)$ versus $t$. From this single function, all other reliability metrics are derived.

The **Reliability Function** (probability of survival up to time $t$):

$$
R(t) = P(T > t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u) \, du\right)
$$

The **Cumulative Distribution Function** (probability of failure by time $t$):

$$
F(t) = 1 - R(t)
$$

The **Probability Density Function** of failure time:

$$
f(t) = \lambda(t) \cdot R(t) = \lambda(t) \cdot \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u) \, du\right)
$$

The **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)**:

$$
\text{MTTF} = E(T) = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt
$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Region** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hazard (Failure) Rate | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | All | Failures / hour |
| Reliability Function | $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\displaystyle\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(u)\,du\right)$ | All | Dimensionless probability |
| CDF of Failure | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | All | Dimensionless probability |
| PDF of Failure | $f(t) = \lambda(t) \cdot R(t)$ | All | 1 / time |
| MTTF (General) | $\text{MTTF} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$ | All | Hours / cycles |
| MTTF (Exponential) | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Region II | Hours |
| Reliability (Exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Region II | $t$ in hours |
| Weibull Hazard | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | Region I ($\beta<1$), Region III ($\beta>1$) | $t, \eta$ in hours, $\beta$ dimensionless |
| Weibull Reliability | $R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$ | Region I, III | Dimensionless |
| FIT Conversion | $1 \text{ FIT} = 10^{-9}$ failures / hour | All | Industry-standard unit |
| Memoryless Property | $P(T>t+s \mid T>t) = P(T>s)$ | Region II only | Holds *only* for Exponential |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for Exams:** Do not confuse **MTTF** (Mean Time To Failure — for non-repairable items) with **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures — for repairable systems). The bathtub curve is defined for a *population* of non-repairable items, so MTTF is the correct metric.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Reliability prediction per MIL-HDBK-217 uses constant failure rates (Region II assumption) for electronic parts.
- **Nuclear Power Plant:** Components are routinely replaced *before* entering Region III to maintain a $10^{-6}$ / reactor-year core-damage frequency.
- **Automotive Warranty Analysis:** Warranty curves exhibit the burn-in shape within the first 6 months of vehicle life.
- **Semiconductor Manufacturing:** Accelerated Life Testing (ALT) using the Arrhenius model quantifies the wear-out onset of ICs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Reliability Function from the Hazard Function

**Starting Point:** The probability that a component survives a small interval $\Delta t$, *given* it has already survived to time $t$, is:

$$
P(T > t + \Delta t \mid T > t) = 1 - \lambda(t) \, \Delta t + o(\Delta t)
$$

**Step 1 — Conditional probability expansion:**

$$
P(T > t + \Delta t \mid T > t) = \frac{P(T > t + \Delta t)}{P(T > t)} = \frac{R(t + \Delta t)}{R(t)}
$$

**Step 2 — Equate and rearrange:**

$$
\frac{R(t + \Delta t)}{R(t)} = 1 - \lambda(t) \, \Delta t
$$

$$
R(t + \Delta t) - R(t) = -\lambda(t) \, R(t) \, \Delta t
$$

**Step 3 — Take the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$:**

This yields the first-order linear differential equation:

$$
\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\lambda(t) \, R(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate:**

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = -\lambda(t) \, dt
$$

$$
\int_{R(0)}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u) \, du
$$

**Step 5 — Apply initial condition $R(0) = 1$ (component is "as good as new" at $t=0$):**

$$
\ln R(t) = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u) \, du
$$

$$
\boxed{R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u) \, du\right)}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Exponential Special Case (Region II)

When the failure rate is constant, $\lambda(t) = \lambda$, the integral collapses to a simple product:

**Step 1 — Substitute constant hazard:**

$$
\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u) \, du = \lambda \int_{0}^{t} du = \lambda t
$$

**Step 2 — Insert into the general reliability expression:**

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 3 — Derive the PDF by differentiation:**

$$
f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = \lambda \, e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the hazard is constant:**

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\lambda e^{-\lambda t}}{e^{-\lambda t}} = \lambda
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the MTTF:**

$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Weibull Distribution Generalization (Regions I & III)

The Weibull distribution is the most flexible two-parameter model used to capture the entire bathtub shape through its **shape parameter $\beta$**.

**Step 1 — Start with the Weibull reliability function:**

$$
R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]
$$

where $\eta > 0$ is the **scale parameter** (characteristic life) and $\beta > 0$ is the **shape parameter**.

**Step 2 — Compute the Weibull PDF:**

$$
f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Derive the Weibull hazard rate:**

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret $\beta$:**

- $\beta < 1 \Rightarrow \lambda(t)$ is **decreasing** $\Rightarrow$ **Region I** (Infant Mortality).
- $\beta = 1 \Rightarrow \lambda(t) = \dfrac{1}{\eta}$ is **constant** $\Rightarrow$ **Region II** (Exponential special case with $\lambda = 1/\eta$).
- $\beta > 1 \Rightarrow \lambda(t)$ is **increasing** $\Rightarrow$ **Region III** (Wear-out).

**Step 5 — MTTF for the Weibull distribution** (uses the Gamma function $\Gamma(\cdot)$):

$$
\text{MTTF} = \eta \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)
$$

For the most common case $\beta = 2$ (Rayleigh distribution):

$$
\text{MTTF} = \eta \, \Gamma(1.5) = \eta \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \approx 0.8862 \, \eta
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Demonstration

Assume a system has the following piecewise hazard rate:

- **Region I:** $\lambda(t) = 0.5 \, e^{-0.8 t}$ (decreasing)
- **Region II:** $\lambda(t) = 0.1$ per hour (constant)
- **Region III:** $\lambda(t) = 0.1 + 0.005 \, (t - 1000)$ for $t > 1000$ hours

**Reliability at $t = 1000$ hours (end of Region II):**

$$
R(1000) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{1000} 0.1 \, dt\right) = e^{-100} \approx 3.72 \times 10^{-44}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The above numerical result is *theoretically illustrative only*. In practice, the bathtub regions are stitched together by a continuous piecewise-defined $\lambda(t)$, and the reliability is computed continuously across the entire timeline. A common KTU exam mistake is to multiply reliabilities of regions without proper convolution.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
    B -.-> B1["HAZARD DECREASING BETA LESS THAN 1 WEIBULL"]
```
