## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
*Define the bathtub curve. Name its three regions and identify the statistical distribution that best models each region.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The bathtub curve is a plot of the instantaneous failure rate $\lambda(t)$ of a non-repairable component versus its operating time $t$.

1. **Region I – Infant Mortality:** Modelled by the **Weibull distribution with shape parameter $\beta < 1$**.
2. **Region II – Useful Life:** Modelled by the **Exponential distribution** (constant $\lambda$).
3. **Region III – Wear-out:** Modelled by the **Weibull distribution with shape parameter $\beta > 1$**.

*(Award full 3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for naming all three regions + 1 mark for distributions.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State and explain the memoryless property. Which region of the bathtub curve exhibits it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The memoryless property states that the probability of a component surviving an additional time $s$, given it has already survived to time $t$, depends only on $s$ and not on $t$:

$$
P(T > t + s \mid T > t) = P(T > s)
$$

This property is exhibited **only in Region II (Useful Life)** of the bathtub curve, where the failure rate is constant and the time-to-failure follows the **Exponential distribution**. Mechanistically, it implies that random failures (e.g., transients, shocks) do not depend on the component's age.

*(Award full 3 marks: 1 mark for the equation + 1 mark for identifying Region II + 1 mark for physical interpretation.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Define the hazard rate function $\lambda(t)$ and derive the relationship $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(u)\,du\right)$ from first principles. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The failure rate of an electronic control unit is observed to be constant at $\lambda = 250$ FIT. Compute the reliability for a mission duration of $t = 10{,}000$ hours and the corresponding MTTF. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Define hazard rate as conditional probability density:

$$
\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T < t + \Delta t \mid T \ge t)}{\Delta t}
$$

- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Express conditional probability using $R(t)$:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \left(-\frac{dR}{dt}\right)
$$

- **Step 3 (1 mark):** Form the differential equation:

$$
\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\lambda(t) \, R(t)
$$

- **Step 4 (1 mark):** Separate variables and integrate from $0$ to $t$:

$$
\int_{R(0)}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(u)\,du
$$

- **Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply boundary condition $R(0) = 1$:

$$
\ln R(t) - \ln 1 = -\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(u)\,du
$$

- **Step 6 (1 mark):** Final exponentiation:

$$
\boxed{R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(u)\,du\right)}
$$

- **Step 7 (1 mark):** Interpretation: $R(t)$ is the probability of *no failures* during $[0, t]$.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\lambda = 250$ FIT $= 250 \times 10^{-9}$ failures/hour.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert to per-hour rate.

$$
\lambda = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ / hour}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute reliability for $t = 10{,}000$ hours.

$$
\lambda t = (2.5 \times 10^{-7})(10{,}000) = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0025
$$

$$
R(10{,}000) = e^{-0.0025} \approx 0.99750
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute MTTF.

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 4{,}000{,}000 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Express in years for intuitive comparison.

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{4{,}000{,}000}{24 \times 365} \approx 456.6 \text{ years}
$$

**Final Result:** $R(10{,}000) = 0.9975$ and $\text{MTTF} \approx 4 \times 10^6$ hours.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the bathtub curve. Discuss the failure mechanisms, the appropriate statistical distribution, and the engineering mitigation strategy for each of its three regions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A mechanical bearing has a Weibull-distributed time-to-failure with scale parameter $\eta = 5000$ hours and shape parameter $\beta = 3$. Calculate the reliability at $t = 2000$ hours, the failure rate at that instant, and the MTTF. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Descriptive Sketch (7 marks):**

- **Step 1 (2 marks):** Neatly draw a bathtub-shaped curve with axes labelled $\lambda(t)$ and $t$, marking all three regions.
- **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** **Region I:** $\lambda(t)$ decreasing; cause — manufacturing defects; model — Weibull with $\beta < 1$; mitigation — burn-in / ESS.
- **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** **Region II:** $\lambda(t)$ constant; cause — random shocks; model — Exponential; mitigation — redundancy, surge protection.
- **Step 4 (2 marks):** **Region III:** $\lambda(t)$ increasing; cause — wear, fatigue, corrosion; model — Weibull with $\beta > 1$; mitigation — preventive replacement, CBM.

**(b) Weibull Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\eta = 5000$ hours, $\beta = 3$, $t = 2000$ hours.

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute reliability using the Weibull reliability function.

$$
R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right] = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{2000}{5000}\right)^{3}\right]
$$

$$
\left(\frac{2000}{5000}\right)^{3} = (0.4)^{3} = 0.064
$$

$$
\boxed{R(2000) = e^{-0.064} \approx 0.9380}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the failure rate at $t = 2000$ hours.

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} = \frac{3}{5000}\left(\frac{2000}{5000}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
\lambda(2000) = \frac{3}{5000} \times (0.4)^{2} = \frac{3 \times 0.16}{5000} = \frac{0.48}{5000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda(2000) = 9.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ / hour} = 96{,}000 \text{ FIT}}
$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Compute the MTTF using the Gamma function.

$$
\text{MTTF} = \eta \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right) = 5000 \times \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{3}\right) = 5000 \times \Gamma(1.3333)
$$

Using $\Gamma(1.3333) \approx 0.89298$ (from Gamma function tables):

$$
\boxed{\text{MTTF} \approx 5000 \times 0.89298 \approx 4464.9 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Engineering Insight (Bonus understanding):** Since $\beta = 3 > 1$, the bearing is in its **wear-out phase** at $t = 2000$ hours, and the failure rate is rising rapidly. Preventive replacement should be scheduled well before the characteristic life $\eta = 5000$ hours.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing MTTF and MTBF:** MTTF applies to *non-repairable* items; MTBF applies to *repairable* systems. The bathtub curve is for non-repairable populations.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert FIT:** A value like $\lambda = 250$ must be converted to $2.5 \times 10^{-7}$ / hour before multiplication with time in hours.
> 3. **Stating "Exponential" without qualifying $\beta$:** Region I and III are *not* Exponential; they are Weibull. Only Region II is Exponential.
> 4. **Ignoring the Gamma function in MTTF of Weibull:** Many students incorrectly write MTTF $= \eta$ — this holds only for $\beta = 1$.
> 5. **Plotting the wrong Y-axis:** The bathtub curve plots $\lambda(t)$, not $f(t)$ or $F(t)$. These three curves have entirely different shapes.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** The bathtub curve plots the **instantaneous failure rate $\lambda(t)$** versus operating time $t$ for a non-repairable population.
- **Region I — Infant Mortality:** Decreasing hazard; manufacturing defects; **Weibull with $\beta < 1$**; mitigation via **burn-in**.
- **Region II — Useful Life:** Constant hazard $\lambda$; random external shocks; **Exponential distribution**; mitigation via **redundancy and protection**.
- **Region III — Wear-out:** Increasing hazard; fatigue, corrosion, aging; **Weibull with $\beta > 1$**; mitigation via **preventive replacement**.
- **Master Formula:** $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(u)\,du\right)$.
- **Exponential Special Case:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$.
- **Weibull Hazard:** $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ — the sign of $(\beta - 1)$ determines the region.
- **Weibull MTTF:** $\text{MTTF} = \eta \, \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$.
- **Unit Standard:** **1 FIT = $10^{-9}$ failures per hour**.
- **Memoryless Property** holds **only** in Region II (Exponential).
- **Boundary condition:** $R(0) = 1$ (as-good-as-new at $t = 0$).
- **KTU Exam Pattern:** Expect 3-mark conceptual questions (region identification, memoryless property) and 7-mark derivations (linking $\lambda$ to $R$, MTTF computation).
- **Examiner Traps:** Never confuse MTTF with MTBF; never forget FIT-to-per-hour conversion; never state Region I or III as Exponential.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation of the Reliability Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Derivation of the Reliability Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Reliability Function** $R(t)$, also known as the **Survival Function** $S(t)$, is formally defined as the probability that a component, system, or product will perform its intended function without failure for a specified period of time, under stated operating conditions.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

where:
- $T$ is a non-negative random variable representing the **Time-to-Failure (TTF)** of the component or system.
- $t$ is any specified mission time or operating duration.
- $P(T > t)$ is the probability that the time-to-failure $T$ exceeds the specified time $t$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1, the Reliability Function is the foundational building block. Every subsequent concept in reliability engineering, including the Hazard Function, Failure Rate, and Mean Time To Failure (MTTF), is mathematically derived from $R(t)$. Mastery of this derivation is mandatory for ESE questions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you purchase **100 identical LED bulbs** from a manufacturer, all rated for the same specifications, and you switch them on simultaneously under standard room conditions.

- Some bulbs will fail quickly (manufacturing defects, weak solder joints).
- Some will fail after a few hundred hours.
- A few will last for thousands of hours.

Now, the question is: *"What is the probability that a randomly chosen bulb from this batch will still be glowing (surviving) at exactly $t = 1000$ hours?"*

That probability is precisely the **Reliability Function** $R(t)$.

If you plot the **percentage of bulbs still working** against time, you obtain a curve that starts at 1.0 (or 100%) at $t = 0$ and monotonically decreases to 0 as $t \to \infty$. This curve is the visual representation of $R(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions of the Reliability Function:**
> 1. $R(0) = 1$ — At time zero, the component is brand new and has 100% probability of survival.
> 2. $R(\infty) = 0$ — Given enough time, every component will eventually fail.
> 3. $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$ — Reliability is a probability and is bounded between 0 and 1.
> 4. $R(t)$ is a **monotonically non-increasing** function of time.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reliability | $R(t)$ | Dimensionless | Probability of survival up to time $t$ |
| Time-to-Failure | $T$ | Hours / Cycles / Miles | Random variable of failure time |
| Failure Rate | $\lambda(t)$ | Failures per hour | Instantaneous rate of failure |
| Mission Time | $t$ | Hours | Operating duration of interest |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Reliability Function is **dimensionless** because it represents a probability. However, the time parameter $t$ carries the unit of the operating life, which could be hours (electronics), kilometers (tires), or cycles (mechanical switches).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: From Probability to Reliability

The derivation of the Reliability Function rests on three pillars of probability theory:

### Pillar 1: The Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)

For a non-negative continuous random variable $T$ (time-to-failure), the CDF is defined as:

$$F(t) = P(T \leq t)$$

This represents the probability that the component **fails on or before** time $t$.

### Pillar 2: The Complement Rule of Probability

Since the sample space is partitioned into two mutually exclusive and exhaustive events: $\{T \leq t\}$ and $\{T > t\}$, we have:

$$P(T \leq t) + P(T > t) = 1$$

### Pillar 3: The Probability Density Function (PDF)

The PDF of the time-to-failure is given by the derivative of the CDF:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt}$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Deduction

The reliability function is built through the following logical chain:

1. **Identify the event of interest:** "The component survives beyond time $t$." This is $\{T > t\}$.
2. **Use the complement rule:** The probability of survival is the complement of the probability of failure.
3. **Link to CDF:** Since $F(t) = P(T \leq t)$, then $P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)$.
4. **Link to PDF:** Since $F(t) = \int_0^t f(u) \, du$, then $R(t)$ can also be expressed as an integral of the PDF from $t$ to infinity.

## 2.3 The Hazard Function Connection

The **Hazard Function** $h(t)$ (also called the instantaneous failure rate or force of mortality) represents the conditional probability density of failure at time $t$, given survival up to time $t$:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

This can be rearranged to yield the **Cox-Proportional Style Identity**:

$$R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_0^t h(u) \, du\right)$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary/Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R(t) = P(T > t)$ | Fundamental definition | $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$ |
| 2 | $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ | Complement of CDF | Valid for all $t \geq 0$ |
| 3 | $R(t) = \int_t^{\infty} f(u) \, du$ | Integral form using PDF | $f(u) \geq 0$ |
| 4 | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | Failure probability | $0 \leq F(t) \leq 1$ |
| 5 | $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ | PDF as negative derivative of R(t) | $f(t) \geq 0$ |
| 6 | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Hazard (failure rate) function | Units: 1/time |
| 7 | $R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_0^t h(u) \, du\right)$ | Exponential of cumulative hazard | $R(0) = 1$ |
| 8 | $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$ | Mean Time To Failure | Units: time |
| 9 | $R(0) = 1$, $R(\infty) = 0$ | Boundary conditions | Mandatory for all derivations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Note for LaTeX:** When writing reliability formulas, **never** use the vertical bar $\vert$ in markdown tables. For conditional probability, use the proper notation $P(T > t)$ or $R(t) = P(T > t)$ directly without dividing bars inside table cells.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The reliability function is the cornerstone of:

- **Aerospace Engineering:** Predicting the probability of mission success for satellites and aircraft engines.
- **Power Systems:** Designing redundancy in power grids where component failure can cause blackouts.
- **Manufacturing Quality Control:** Computing warranty costs and replacement schedules.
- **Medical Device Industry:** Ensuring that pacemakers and insulin pumps operate reliably for years.
- **Automotive Industry:** Estimating the survival probability of brake systems, airbags, and batteries.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Rule of Thumb:** For a system with constant failure rate $\lambda$ (the exponential distribution), the reliability function simplifies beautifully to $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. This is the most commonly tested special case in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reliability from the Cumulative Distribution Function

**Goal:** Show that $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$.

### Step 1: Start with the axioms of probability
For any random variable $T$, the entire probability space is normalized to 1:

$$P(\Omega) = 1$$

### Step 2: Partition the sample space
The two complementary events are "failure on or before time $t$" and "survival beyond time $t$":

$$P(T \leq t) + P(T > t) = 1$$

### Step 3: Substitute the definition of CDF
By definition, $F(t) = P(T \leq t)$. Substituting:

$$F(t) + P(T > t) = 1$$

### Step 4: Isolate the survival probability
Rearranging for $P(T > t)$:

$$P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)$$

### Step 5: Apply the definition of the reliability function
Since $R(t) = P(T > t)$ by fundamental definition:

$$\boxed{R(t) = 1 - F(t)}$$

**Logic of conversion:** This derivation uses only the complement rule of probability. The conversion from $P(T > t)$ to $1 - F(t)$ is valid because the CDF captures all failure probability up to time $t$, and subtracting it from unity leaves only the survival probability.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reliability from the Probability Density Function

**Goal:** Show that $R(t) = \int_t^{\infty} f(u) \, du$.

### Step 1: Express the CDF as an integral of the PDF
By the fundamental theorem of calculus applied to a non-negative random variable $T$:

$$F(t) = \int_0^t f(u) \, du$$

### Step 2: Apply the total probability axiom for the infinite horizon
Since the component must fail at some time between $0$ and $\infty$ with probability 1:

$$F(\infty) = \int_0^{\infty} f(u) \, du = 1$$

### Step 3: Subtract the CDF from unity
Using the result of Derivation 1, $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$:

$$R(t) = 1 - \int_0^t f(u) \, du$$

### Step 4: Split the integral from 0 to infinity
Using the property that $\int_0^{\infty} = \int_0^t + \int_t^{\infty}$:

$$R(t) = \int_0^{\infty} f(u) \, du - \int_0^t f(u) \, du$$

### Step 5: Combine the integrals
By linearity of integration:

$$R(t) = \int_t^{\infty} f(u) \, du$$

**Logic of conversion:** The conversion from $1 - \int_0^t$ to $\int_t^{\infty}$ relies on the fact that the entire probability mass under the PDF equals 1. Subtracting the area under the curve from 0 to $t$ leaves the tail area, which represents survival.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Reliability from the Hazard Function

**Goal:** Show that $R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_0^t h(u) \, du\right)$.

### Step 1: Start with the definition of the Hazard Function
The hazard function is the conditional failure density given survival:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

### Step 2: Express the PDF in terms of reliability
Cross-multiplying and using the result of Section 3.1 that $f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$:

$$h(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

### Step 3: Rearrange to isolate the differential
Multiplying both sides by $dt$:

$$h(t) \, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

### Step 4: Integrate both sides from 0 to $t$
Applying the limits of integration:

$$\int_0^t h(u) \, du = -\int_0^t \frac{1}{R(u)} \, dR(u)$$

### Step 5: Evaluate the right-hand integral
The integral of $\frac{1}{R(u)} dR(u)$ is $\ln R(u)$. Thus:

$$\int_0^t h(u) \, du = -[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)]$$

### Step 6: Apply the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$
Since $\ln R(0) = \ln 1 = 0$:

$$\int_0^t h(u) \, du = -\ln R(t)$$

### Step 7: Solve for $R(t)$
Multiplying by $-1$ and exponentiating:

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_0^t h(u) \, du\right)}$$

**Logic of conversion:** This derivation uses separation of variables and integration. The conversion from the logarithmic form to the exponential form is essential because it allows engineers to compute reliability from empirically observed failure rates.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) from Reliability

**Goal:** Show that $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$.

### Step 1: Start with the definition of expected value
For a non-negative random variable $T$:

$$E[T] = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

### Step 2: Apply integration by parts
Using the identity $\int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du$ with $u = t$ and $dv = f(t) dt$:

$$E[T] = [t \cdot F(t)]_0^{\infty} - \int_0^{\infty} F(t) \, dt$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the boundary term
As $t \to \infty$, $F(t) \to 1$, but $t \cdot F(t)$ does not converge for heavy-tailed distributions. For the standard reliability case, this term is handled by limits. For distributions with finite mean:

$$[t \cdot F(t)]_0^{\infty} = 0$$

### Step 4: Substitute $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$

$$E[T] = -\int_0^{\infty} (1 - R(t)) \, dt = \int_0^{\infty} R(t) \, dt - \int_0^{\infty} 1 \, dt$$

The second integral diverges, but the difference is well-defined and equals:

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \int_0^{\infty} R(t) \, dt}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** In KTU exams, students often confuse $E[T]$ (which uses the PDF) with $\int_0^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$. The integration by parts is mandatory. Writing the MTTF formula without the derivation will cost method marks.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Reliability Forms for Standard Distributions

| Distribution | PDF $f(t)$ | Reliability $R(t)$ | Hazard $h(t)$ | MTTF |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Exponential | $\lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ (constant) | $1/\lambda$ |
| Weibull | $\frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\alpha)^\beta}$ | $e^{-(t/\alpha)^\beta}$ | $\frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | $\alpha \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ |
| Rayleigh | $\frac{t}{\sigma^2} e^{-t^2/(2\sigma^2)}$ | $e^{-t^2/(2\sigma^2)}$ | $\frac{t}{\sigma^2}$ | $\sigma \sqrt{\pi/2}$ |
| Normal | $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(t-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}$ | $1 - \Phi\left(\frac{t-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | $\frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | $\mu$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Pattern Insight:** KTU board examiners frequently test the **Exponential Distribution** because of its constant failure rate property (memoryless). The Weibull Distribution is the second most common because it generalizes the exponential and is widely used in mechanical fatigue analysis.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: The Reliability Function Ecosystem

The following diagram illustrates the **interconnected network of reliability functions** and how the Reliability Function serves as the central hub from which all other reliability metrics are derived.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Random Variable T: Time to Failure"]
    nodeB["Cumulative Distribution Function F of t = P of T less than or equal to t"]
    nodeC["Reliability Function R of t = P of T greater than t"]
    nodeD["Probability Density Function f of t = dF of t divided by dt"]
    nodeE["Hazard Function h of t = f of t divided by R of t"]
    nodeF["Cumulative Hazard Function H of t = integral of h of u du from 0 to t"]
    nodeG["Mean Time To Failure MTTF = integral of R of t dt from 0 to infinity"]
    nodeH["Exponential Special Case R of t = exp of negative lambda t"]
    nodeI["Weibull Generalization R of t = exp of negative t over alpha to the power beta"]
    
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    
    style nodeA fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeB fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeC fill:#15803d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeD fill:#a16207,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeE fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeF fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeG fill:#581c87,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeH fill:#0f766e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeI fill:#9a3412,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Bath-Tub Curve Mapping

The reliability function's behavior over a component's lifetime is classically depicted by the **Bath-Tub Curve**, which has three distinct regions. The following block diagram maps these regions to their corresponding hazard function characteristics.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    region1["REGION 1: INFANT MORTALITY"]
    desc1["High initial failure rate due to manufacturing defects, weak joints, contamination. Reliability drops rapidly."]
    lambda1["Hazard Function h of t: DECREASING"]
    
    region2["REGION 2: USEFUL LIFE"]
    desc2["Constant, low failure rate due to random external stresses. Reliability decreases linearly on log scale."]
    lambda2["Hazard Function h of t: CONSTANT lambda"]
    
    region3["REGION 3: WEAR OUT"]
    desc3["Rapidly increasing failure rate due to material degradation, fatigue, corrosion. Reliability plummets."]
    lambda3["Hazard Function h of t: INCREASING"]
    
    region1 --> desc1
    desc1 --> lambda1
    region2 --> desc2
    desc2 --> lambda2
    region3 --> desc3
    desc3 --> lambda3
    
    lambda1 --> relCurve["Reliability Function R of t: S-shaped decay across all three regions"]
    lambda2 --> relCurve
    lambda3 --> relCurve
    
    style region1 fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style region2 fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style region3 fill:#ea580c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style relCurve fill:#1e40af,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: Derivation Sequence of the Reliability Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Start: Time to Failure T is a Random Variable"]
    stepB["Define the Event of Survival: T greater than t"]
    stepC["Apply the Complement Rule of Probability"]
    stepD["R of t = 1 minus F of t"]
    stepE["Express F of t as Integral of f of u du from 0 to t"]
    stepF["R of t equals integral of f of u du from t to infinity"]
    stepG["Use Hazard Function h of t equals f of t divided by R of t"]
    stepH["Separate Variables and Integrate"]
    stepI["R of t = exp of negative integral of h of u du from 0 to t"]
    stepJ["Final Result: Reliability Function in Three Equivalent Forms"]
    
    startA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
    
    style startA fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style stepJ fill:#14532d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Reliability Function $R(t)$. State and justify its two fundamental boundary conditions.**

**Model Answer:**

The Reliability Function $R(t)$ is defined as the probability that a component or system performs its intended function without failure for a specified duration $t$, under stated operating conditions.

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

The two fundamental boundary conditions are:

1. **$R(0) = 1$:** At the instant the component begins operation (time zero), it is brand new and has not yet been subjected to any stress. Therefore, the probability of survival is unity (100%).

2. **$R(\infty) = 0$:** As time approaches infinity, every component will eventually fail due to wear, fatigue, or accumulated damage. Hence the probability of indefinite survival approaches zero.

These conditions are justified by the axioms of probability and the physical certainty of eventual failure for any non-perfect component.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Derive the relationship $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ between the Probability Density Function and the Reliability Function.**

**Model Answer:**

Starting with the fundamental relationship between reliability and the CDF:

$$R(t) = 1 - F(t)$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dF(t)}{dt}$$

By definition, the PDF is the derivative of the CDF:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt}$$

Substituting this into the differentiated equation:

$$\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -f(t)$$

Multiplying both sides by $-1$:

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

This relationship is crucial because it allows computation of the failure density directly from the reliability function, and it confirms that since $R(t)$ is monotonically decreasing, $f(t)$ must be non-negative.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Module 1, Internal Choice Option A

**(a)** Derive the Reliability Function $R(t)$ from the probability density function $f(t)$ of the time-to-failure random variable. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mechanical component has a time-to-failure distribution given by the Weibull PDF:
$$f(t) = \frac{2}{1000}\left(\frac{t}{1000}\right) e^{-(t/1000)^2}, \quad t \geq 0$$
Determine: (i) The Reliability Function $R(t)$, (ii) The probability that the component survives beyond 800 hours, (iii) The Mean Time To Failure (MTTF). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** The CDF is defined as the integral of the PDF from 0 to $t$:

$$F(t) = \int_0^t f(u) \, du$$

**Step 2:** Using the complement rule of probability, since $P(T \leq t) + P(T > t) = 1$:

$$R(t) = 1 - F(t) = 1 - \int_0^t f(u) \, du$$

**Step 3:** Recognizing that the total probability under the PDF equals 1:

$$\int_0^{\infty} f(u) \, du = 1$$

**Step 4:** Splitting the infinite integral into two parts using the additive property of integrals:

$$\int_0^{\infty} f(u) \, du = \int_0^t f(u) \, du + \int_t^{\infty} f(u) \, du = 1$$

**Step 5:** Solving for the tail integral:

$$\int_t^{\infty} f(u) \, du = 1 - \int_0^t f(u) \, du$$

**Step 6:** Equating with the reliability function from Step 2:

$$\boxed{R(t) = \int_t^{\infty} f(u) \, du}$$

This is the integral form of the reliability function, representing the area under the PDF curve from time $t$ to infinity.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Reliability Function:**

Using the result $R(t) = \int_t^{\infty} f(u) \, du$:

$$R(t) = \int_t^{\infty} \frac{2}{1000}\left(\frac{u}{1000}\right) e^{-(u/1000)^2} \, du$$

Let $w = (u/1000)^2$, then $dw = \frac{2u}{1000^2} du = \frac{2u}{10^6} du$. Substituting:

$$R(t) = e^{-(t/1000)^2}$$

**(ii) Survival probability at $t = 800$ hours:**

$$R(800) = e^{-(800/1000)^2} = e^{-0.64}$$

$$R(800) = 0.5273 \text{ or } 52.73\%$$

**(iii) Mean Time To Failure:**

Using the formula $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^{\infty} e^{-(t/1000)^2} \, dt$$

Let $v = t/1000$, so $dt = 1000 \, dv$:

$$\text{MTTF} = 1000 \int_0^{\infty} e^{-v^2} \, dv = 1000 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$$

$$\text{MTTF} = 500\sqrt{\pi} \approx 886.23 \text{ hours}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Module 1, Internal Choice Option B

**(a)** Starting from the Hazard Function definition, derive the expression $R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_0^t h(u) \, du\right)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a system with constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.001$ failures per hour, compute: (i) The reliability at $t = 500$ hours, (ii) The reliability at $t = 1000$ hours, (iii) The Mean Time To Failure, (iv) The probability that the system fails between 500 and 1000 hours. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** The Hazard Function is defined as the conditional failure density given survival:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2:** Using the relationship $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$:

$$h(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Rearranging by multiplying both sides by $R(t) \, dt$:

$$h(t) \cdot R(t) \, dt = -dR(t)$$

**Step 4:** Dividing by $R(t)$ and rearranging:

$$h(t) \, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 5:** Integrating both sides from 0 to $t$:

$$\int_0^t h(u) \, du = -\int_0^t \frac{dR(u)}{R(u)}$$

**Step 6:** Evaluating the right-hand integral as $\ln R(u)$:

$$\int_0^t h(u) \, du = -[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)]$$

**Step 7:** Applying the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$, so $\ln R(0) = 0$:

$$\int_0^t h(u) \, du = -\ln R(t)$$

**Step 8:** Solving for $R(t)$ by exponentiation:

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_0^t h(u) \, du\right)}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

For constant failure rate, $h(t) = \lambda = 0.001$ per hour. By the exponential model:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**(i) Reliability at $t = 500$ hours:**

$$R(500) = e^{-0.001 \times 500} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

**(ii) Reliability at $t = 1000$ hours:**

$$R(1000) = e^{-0.001 \times 1000} = e^{-1} = 0.3679$$

**(iii) Mean Time To Failure:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.001} = 1000 \text{ hours}$$

**(iv) Probability of failure between 500 and 1000 hours:**

$$P(500 < T \leq 1000) = F(1000) - F(500) = [1 - R(1000)] - [1 - R(500)] = R(500) - R(1000)$$

$$P(500 < T \leq 1000) = 0.6065 - 0.3679 = 0.2386$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Differentiation:** Students often write $f(t) = +\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$ instead of $-\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$. This is the most common error and costs 2-3 marks. Remember: $R(t)$ decreases, so its derivative is negative, and the negative sign is required to make $f(t)$ positive.
> 2. **Forgetting the Boundary Condition $R(0) = 1$:** In Derivation 3 (Hazard to Reliability), students must explicitly state and use $R(0) = 1$ to evaluate the constant of integration. Skipping this step is worth 1 mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing $P(T > t)$ with $P(T \geq t)$:** For continuous distributions, these are equal, but the exam expects the strict inequality form $P(T > t)$.
> 4. **MTTF Derivation Steps:** Writing the MTTF formula $\int_0^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$ without the integration by parts derivation loses 4 out of 7 marks. The integration by parts is mandatory.
> 5. **Units in Final Answer:** Forgetting to include units (hours, cycles, km) in the MTTF answer costs 0.5 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Definition:** $R(t) = P(T > t)$ — the probability of survival beyond time $t$.
- **Three Equivalent Forms of $R(t)$:** (1) $1 - F(t)$, (2) $\int_t^{\infty} f(u) \, du$, (3) $\exp\left(-\int_0^t h(u) \, du\right)$.
- **Mandatory Boundary Conditions:** $R(0) = 1$ and $R(\infty) = 0$.
- **Monotonicity:** $R(t)$ is a monotonically non-increasing function of time.
- **PDF Relationship:** $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ — the negative sign is critical.
- **Hazard Relationship:** $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ — used to derive the exponential form.
- **Exponential Special Case:** For constant failure rate $\lambda$, $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ and MTTF $= 1/\lambda$.
- **MTTF Formula:** $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$ — derivable via integration by parts.
- **Weibull Form:** $R(t) = e^{-(t/\alpha)^\beta}$ — generalizes the exponential; $\beta = 1$ reduces to exponential.
- **Engineering Applications:** Aerospace mission success probability, warranty analysis, power grid redundancy, medical device longevity, and automotive component survival.
- **Exam Pattern:** KTU 2024 examiners test both the **derivation methodology** (worth 4-5 marks) and the **numerical computation** (worth 2-3 marks) within the same sub-question. Always show all intermediate algebraic steps.
- **Key Trap:** Never confuse $E[T]$ computed via PDF ($E[T] = \int_0^{\infty} t f(t) \, dt$) with the integral of $R(t)$. They are equal in value, but only through the integration by parts derivation.
- **Bath-Tub Insight:** The reliability function is the visual mirror of the bath-tub hazard curve: steep drop in Region 1, slow decline in Region 2, and rapid drop in Region 3.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Failure and Failure Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Failure

In the context of **Reliability Engineering**, **failure** is formally defined as the **termination of the ability of an item to perform its required function**. This definition, adopted from the fundamental standards of reliability theory, recognizes that failure is not merely a "breakdown" but any deviation from specified performance criteria.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A failure occurs when a system, component, or sub-system is unable to meet the performance requirements specified in its design specification. Performance is judged against **functional**, **operational**, and **environmental** tolerances.

### Three Pillars of Failure Recognition

| Pillar | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Functional Failure** | Item cannot perform its intended primary function | A pump cannot deliver rated flow |
| **Operational Failure** | Item operates but outside acceptable limits | A voltage regulator outputting 240V instead of 220V ± 5% |
| **Catastrophic Failure** | Sudden, total, and often irreversible loss of function | Transformer explosion, shaft fracture |

## 1.2 Definition of Failure Mode

A **Failure Mode** is the *manner* or *mechanism* by which a failure is observed. While "failure" is the event, "failure mode" describes **how** the failure manifests physically to the observer or the system.

$$ \text{Failure} = \text{Event (What happened)} $$
$$ \text{Failure Mode} = \text{Observable Manifestation (How it appeared)} $$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Heart Pacemaker**
>
> Imagine a heart pacemaker:
> - **Failure** = The patient experiences an irregular heartbeat (event occurred).
> - **Failure Mode** = The cause was identified as **battery depletion**, or alternatively as a **lead wire fracture**, or as a **software glitch**.
>
> Same consequence (failure) — but completely different modes! A reliability engineer must understand the *mode* to design countermeasures.

### Another Analogy — Car Engine

- **Failure**: Car stops on the highway
- **Failure Modes**:
  - Fuel pump burnout
  - Belt snapping
  - Overheating / coolant leak
  - Spark plug fouling
  - Crankshaft fracture

Each mode requires a different preventive maintenance strategy. Hence, **identifying failure modes is the first step toward designing reliability into a product**.

## 1.4 Classification of Failure (High-Level Overview)

Failures are broadly classified into:

1. **Catastrophic Failures** — Sudden, complete cessation of function (e.g., shaft snapping). Hard to predict.
2. **Degradation Failures** — Gradual deterioration of performance over time (e.g., bearing wear). Predictable through condition monitoring.
3. **Intermittent Failures** — Irregular occurrence, often environment-dependent (e.g., loose connector).
4. **Wear-out Failures** — Time-dependent, expected in later life stages.
5. **Infant Mortality (Early Life) Failures** — Due to manufacturing defects, installation errors, or poor quality control.

## 1.5 Key Terminology Mapping

| Term | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reliability** | $R(t)$ | Probability of survival up to time $t$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **Unreliability (CDF)** | $F(t)$ | Probability of failure before time $t$ | Dimensionless |
| **Failure Density (PDF)** | $f(t)$ | Instantaneous rate of failure occurrences | per unit time |
| **Failure (Hazard) Rate** | $\lambda(t)$ | Conditional rate of failure at time $t$, given survival till $t$ | per unit time (often **FPMH** or **% per 1000 hrs**) |
| **Mean Time To Failure** | $MTTF$ | Expected time to first failure for non-repairable items | hours / cycles |
| **Mean Time Between Failures** | $MTBF$ | Average time between successive failures (repairable) | hours / cycles |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Reliability Function — The Mathematical Backbone

The fundamental relationship between reliability and failure is established through the **reliability function**:

$$ R(t) = P(T > t) $$

Where $T$ is the random variable representing the time-to-failure. The complementary function is the **cumulative distribution function (CDF) of failure**:

$$ F(t) = P(T \leq t) = 1 - R(t) $$

The **probability density function (PDF)** of failure is the derivative:

$$ f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt} $$

## 2.2 The Failure (Hazard) Rate — Heart of Reliability Analysis

The **instantaneous failure rate** $\lambda(t)$ is the rate at which items that have survived until time $t$ will fail in the next instant:

$$ \lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Relationship (Memorize for KTU):**
> From the differential equation $\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda(t) \, dt$, integrating from $0$ to $t$:
> $$ R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau\right) $$
> For **constant** $\lambda$ (useful life period), this reduces to the famous **Exponential Reliability Equation**:
> $$ R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} $$

## 2.3 The Three Failure Regimes — Bathtub Curve Concept

The **Bathtub Curve** is the graphical representation of $\lambda(t)$ versus time, displaying three distinct phases:

| Phase | Time Period | Failure Rate Behavior | Dominant Failure Modes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase I — Infant Mortality** | Early life | **Decreasing** $\lambda(t)$ | Manufacturing defects, poor quality, installation errors, material flaws |
| **Phase II — Useful Life** | Normal operating life | **Constant** $\lambda(t)$ | Random failures, external shocks, human error, environmental extremes |
| **Phase III — Wear-out** | Late life | **Increasing** $\lambda(t)$ | Fatigue, corrosion, wear, creep, aging, oxidation |

## 2.4 Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) and MTBF

The **expected time to failure** is computed from the PDF:

$$ MTTF = E[T] = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \int_0^{\infty} R(t) \, dt $$

For the **exponential distribution** (constant failure rate):

$$ MTTF = \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda} $$

For **repairable systems**, the equivalent metric is:

$$ MTBF = MTTR + MTTF_{\text{operational}} \approx \frac{1}{\lambda} \quad (\text{when MTTR} \ll \text{MTTF}) $$

Where **MTTR** is *Mean Time To Repair*.

## 2.5 Classification of Failure Modes (Detailed)

### A. Mechanical Failure Modes

| Failure Mode | Mechanism | Typical Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Ductile Fracture** | Plastic deformation prior to break | Overload, material softening |
| **Brittle Fracture** | Sudden break with negligible deformation | Low temp, high strain rate, defects |
| **Fatigue Failure** | Cyclic stress-induced crack initiation & growth | Variable loading, vibration |
| **Creep Failure** | Time-dependent deformation under constant load | High temperature, sustained stress |
| **Wear** | Material loss due to relative motion | Friction, abrasion, adhesion |
| **Buckling** | Sudden lateral deflection under compression | Slenderness, eccentric load |
| **Yielding** | Permanent plastic deformation | Stress beyond yield strength |
| **Corrosion Fatigue** | Combined cyclic stress + corrosive environment | Marine, chemical environments |

### B. Electrical / Electronic Failure Modes

| Failure Mode | Description |
|---|---|
| **Open Circuit** | Complete loss of continuity (burnt trace, broken wire) |
| **Short Circuit** | Unintended current path (insulation breakdown) |
| **Parameter Drift** | Gradual change in value (resistance, capacitance, gain) |
| **Thermal Runaway** | Self-reinforcing temperature increase leading to destruction |
| **Dielectric Breakdown** | Insulation failure under high voltage |
| **Solder Joint Failure** | Intermittent contact, often from thermal cycling |

### C. Software / System Failure Modes

- **Logic Error** — Wrong output under specific input conditions
- **Timing Failure** — Missed deadline in real-time systems
- **Overflow / Truncation** — Numerical computation error
- **Memory Leak** — Gradual resource exhaustion
- **Interface Mismatch** — Incompatibility between subsystems

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Failure Mode Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study failure modes in KTU?**
> 1. **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** — directly uses these modes
> 2. **RBD (Reliability Block Diagram)** construction — depends on mode identification
> 3. **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** — uses failure modes as basic events
> 4. **Design for Reliability (DfR)** — informs material/process selection
> 5. **Maintenance Strategy** — predictive vs preventive decisions

## 2.7 The "Why" Behind the Bathtub Curve — Physical Reasoning

- **Phase I (Infant Mortality)**: Defective items fail quickly; substandard materials, poor assembly. The weak population is "weeded out." This is why **burn-in testing** is performed.
- **Phase II (Useful Life)**: Remaining items are sound; failures are *random* (Poisson process). Rate is **constant** because each instant is independent of past.
- **Phase III (Wear-out)**: Cumulative damage (fatigue, corrosion, wear) accumulates; failure rate **rises** sharply. This is where **replacement** or **overhaul** is scheduled.

## 2.8 The "How" — Mathematical Model of Each Region

| Region | Reliability Model | Failure Rate Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Infant Mortality** | Weibull ($\beta < 1$) | $\lambda(t) = \alpha \beta t^{\beta - 1}$, $\alpha, \beta > 0$ |
| **Useful Life** | Exponential | $\lambda(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}$ |
| **Wear-out** | Weibull ($\beta > 1$) | $\lambda(t) = \alpha \beta t^{\beta - 1}$ |
| **Constant Region Special Case** | Exponential | $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ |

The **Weibull distribution** is the most general 2-parameter model:

$$ R(t) = e^{-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}} $$

- $\eta$ = **scale parameter** (characteristic life)
- $\beta$ = **shape parameter** (determines failure mode behavior)
  - $\beta < 1$ → Infant mortality
  - $\beta = 1$ → Exponential (constant rate, random failures)
  - $\beta > 1$ → Wear-out

## 2.9 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F(t) + R(t) = 1$ | Complementary probabilities |
| 2 | $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ | Failure density |
| 3 | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Hazard rate definition |
| 4 | $R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau}$ | General reliability from hazard |
| 5 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential reliability (constant $\lambda$) |
| 6 | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | MTTF for exponential |
| 7 | $MTTF = \int_0^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$ | General MTTF integral |
| 8 | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | Weibull reliability |
| 9 | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | Weibull failure rate |
| 10 | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential CDF |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All exams in KTU 2024 scheme for PEMET747 expect students to fluently inter-convert equations 2, 3, 4, and 5. Master this!**

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Hazard Rate to Reliability Function

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU examinations.

**Starting Point — Definition of conditional failure rate:**

$$ \lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \,\vert\, T > t)}{\Delta t} $$

**Step 1** — Express the conditional probability:

$$ \lambda(t) = \frac{P(t < T \leq t+\Delta t)}{P(T > t) \cdot \Delta t} = \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{R(t) \cdot \Delta t} $$

**Step 2** — Take the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$:

$$ \lambda(t) = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} $$

This reproduces the fundamental relation from Section 2.2.

**Step 3** — Substitute $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$:

$$ \lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} $$

**Step 4** — Rearrange the differential equation:

$$ \frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda(t)\,dt $$

**Step 5** — Integrate from $0$ to $t$, using the initial condition $R(0) = 1$:

$$ \int_0^t \frac{dR(\tau)}{R(\tau)} = -\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau $$

$$ \ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = -\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau $$

$$ \ln R(t) - \ln 1 = -\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau $$

**Step 6** — Exponentiate to obtain the **General Reliability Function**:

$$ \boxed{R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau\right)} $$

**Step 7 (Special Case)** — If $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (constant, useful-life region):

$$ R(t) = \exp\left(-\lambda \int_0^t d\tau\right) = \exp(-\lambda t) $$

Hence, the famous **exponential reliability formula** is derived.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: MTTF for the Exponential Distribution

**Given:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ and $\lambda = \text{constant}$

**Step 1** — The MTTF is the expected time to failure:

$$ MTTF = E[T] = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\,dt $$

**Step 2** — Use integration by parts: $u = t$, $dv = f(t)\,dt$, so $du = dt$, $v = F(t)$.

$$ MTTF = \left[ t \cdot F(t) \right]_0^{\infty} - \int_0^{\infty} F(t)\,dt $$

**Step 3** — Evaluate the boundary term. As $t \to \infty$, $F(t) \to 1$, but $t \to \infty$ faster. So the limit is 0. At $t = 0$, the term is 0. Hence the boundary contribution vanishes.

**Step 4** — Substitute $F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$ MTTF = -\int_0^{\infty} (1 - e^{-\lambda t})\,dt = \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt $$

**Step 5** — Perform the integration:

$$ MTTF = \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_0^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda} $$

**Result:**

$$ \boxed{MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}} $$

This is the foundational result for the exponential model and is critical for KTU board exams.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)

For **repairable systems**, the cycle consists of:

- **Time to failure** (TTF) → exponential with mean $1/\lambda$
- **Time to repair** (TTR) → mean = MTTR

**Therefore:**

$$ MTBF = MTTF + MTTR = \frac{1}{\lambda} + MTTR $$

**Availability** of the system becomes:

$$ A = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} = \frac{MTBF - MTTR}{MTBF} = \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + MTTR} $$

**Simplified form:**

$$ A = \frac{1}{1 + \lambda \cdot MTTR} $$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — KTU Board Style

> **[Question]:** A system has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.001$ failures per hour. Find:
> (a) The reliability for 500 hours of operation.
> (b) The mean time to failure.
> (c) The probability of failure in the interval (100, 500) hours.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Using the exponential reliability formula:

$$ R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.001)(500)} = e^{-0.5} $$

$$ R(500) = 0.6065 \approx 60.65\% $$

**Part (b):** Using $MTTF = 1/\lambda$:

$$ MTTF = \frac{1}{0.001} = 1000 \text{ hours} $$

**Part (c):** Probability of failure in interval $(100, 500)$:

$$ P(100 < T \leq 500) = F(500) - F(100) = [1 - R(500)] - [1 - R(100)] $$

$$ = R(100) - R(500) = e^{-0.1} - e^{-0.5} $$

$$ = 0.9048 - 0.6065 = 0.2983 $$

**Result:** Approximately **29.83%** chance of failure occurring between 100 and 500 hours.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Connecting Failure Modes to Distributions

| Failure Mode | Best-Fit Distribution | Shape Parameter $\beta$ | Failure Rate Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Infant mortality (defects) | Weibull | $\beta < 1$ | Decreasing |
| Random shocks | Exponential | $\beta = 1$ | Constant |
| Wear (mechanical) | Weibull | $\beta > 1$ | Increasing |
| Corrosion | Lognormal | $\mu, \sigma$ parameters | Increasing-then-decreasing |
| Fatigue (crack growth) | Weibull | $\beta \approx 2$ to $4$ | Increasing |
| Electronic aging | Lognormal / Weibull | $\beta \approx 2$ to $3$ | Increasing |

---

## 3.6 The Reliability-Physics Derivation (Brief)

The **Arrhenius model** relates failure rate to temperature:

$$ \lambda(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (k_B T)} $$

Where:
- $E_a$ = activation energy (eV)
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann's constant = $8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor

This explains why electronic components fail faster at higher temperatures — a key concept in **thermal reliability engineering**.

## 4.6 Series System Reliability Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input] --> B1[Component 1]
    B1 --> B2[Component 2]
    B2 --> B3[Component 3]
    B3 --> C[Output]

    A --> A3[lambda3]
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
    B1 --> R1[R1 = e to power minus lambda1 t]
    B2 --> R2[R2 = e to power minus lambda2 t]
    B3 --> R3[R3 = e to power minus lambda3 t]
    R1 --> D[System Reliability Rs = R1 times R2 times R3]
    R2 --> D
    R3 --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> For a **series system** of $n$ independent components, the system reliability is the product of individual reliabilities:
> $$ R_{\text{system}}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = \exp\left(-\lambda_{\text{eq}} \cdot t\right) \quad \text{where} \quad \lambda_{\text{eq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i $$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define the term "failure" as used in reliability engineering. Distinguish between catastrophic failure and degradation failure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **failure** is defined as the termination of the ability of an item to perform its required function, or the inability of a system or component to meet its specified performance criteria (1 mark).
>
> **Catastrophic failure** is a sudden, complete, and often irreversible cessation of function. The item stops working entirely without prior warning, e.g., a shaft breaking or a transformer exploding (1 mark).
>
> **Degradation failure** is a gradual deterioration of the item's performance over time, often detectable through condition monitoring. The item continues to function but with progressively worsening performance, e.g., bearing wear or insulation aging (1 mark).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is a "failure mode"? List any four common mechanical failure modes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **failure mode** is the manner or mechanism by which a failure is physically observed or manifested in a system or component. It describes *how* the failure becomes evident (1 mark).
>
> Four common mechanical failure modes (2 marks):
> 1. **Ductile fracture** — plastic deformation followed by rupture
> 2. **Brittle fracture** — sudden break with minimal plastic deformation
> 3. **Fatigue failure** — failure under cyclic loading
> 4. **Creep** — time-dependent deformation under constant load at elevated temperature
>
> *(Other acceptable answers: wear, corrosion, buckling, yielding)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the reliability function $R(t)$ and the failure rate $\lambda(t)$. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system consists of three components in series, with constant failure rates of $\lambda_1 = 0.002$/hr, $\lambda_2 = 0.003$/hr, and $\lambda_3 = 0.005$/hr respectively. Find:
   (i) The reliability of the system for 100 hours of operation. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The mean time to failure of the system. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) The probability that the system fails within 200 hours. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Starting Definition (1 mark):**

The conditional failure rate is defined as:

$$ \lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t} $$

**Step 1: Express using joint probability (1 mark)**

$$ \lambda(t) = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} $$

**Step 2: Substitute $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ (1 mark)**

$$ \lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} $$

**Step 3: Separate variables and integrate (2 marks)**

$$ \frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda(t)\,dt $$

$$ \int_0^t \frac{dR(\tau)}{R(\tau)} = -\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau $$

$$ \ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = -\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau $$

**Step 4: Apply initial condition $R(0) = 1$ and exponentiate (1 mark)**

$$ R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau\right) $$

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Failure rate $\lambda(t)$ is a well-defined function of time
- Failures are statistically independent
- Items are non-repairable during the observation period

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Equivalent failure rate for series system (1 mark):**

$$ \lambda_{\text{eq}} = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 = 0.002 + 0.003 + 0.005 = 0.010 \text{ per hour} $$

**Step 2 (i) — Reliability at $t = 100$ hrs (3 marks):**

$$ R_{\text{system}}(100) = e^{-\lambda_{\text{eq}} \cdot t} = e^{-(0.010)(100)} = e^{-1} = 0.3679 $$

$$ R_{\text{system}}(100) \approx 36.79\% $$

**Step 3 (ii) — Mean Time to Failure (2 marks):**

$$ MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{1}{0.010} = 100 \text{ hours} $$

**Step 4 (iii) — Probability of failure within 200 hours (2 marks):**

$$ F(200) = 1 - R(200) = 1 - e^{-(0.010)(200)} = 1 - e^{-2} $$

$$ = 1 - 0.1353 = 0.8647 \approx 86.47\% $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **Bathtub Curve** in detail. Describe the three phases with respect to failure rate behavior and the dominant failure modes in each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures per hour:
   (i) Determine the reliability at 2000 hours. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) Find the design life $t$ such that $R(t) = 0.95$. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) If a system has $MTTR = 4$ hours, calculate the steady-state availability. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** The **Bathtub Curve** is a graphical representation of the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ versus operational time, displaying three distinct phases. It is fundamental in understanding the life-cycle behavior of repairable and non-repairable items.

**Phase I — Infant Mortality Period (2 marks):**
- Failure rate is **high but rapidly decreasing**
- Dominant failure modes: manufacturing defects, poor workmanship, material flaws, installation errors
- Strategy: **burn-in testing** to eliminate weak units before deployment
- Modeled by **Weibull distribution with $\beta < 1$**

**Phase II — Useful Life Period (2 marks):**
- Failure rate is **constant and at its minimum**
- Dominant failure modes: random failures, external shocks, human errors, environmental extremes
- Strategy: **corrective and preventive maintenance** as needed
- Modeled by the **Exponential distribution**
- Constant $\lambda$ implies memoryless property: $R(s+t \mid T > s) = R(t)$

**Phase III — Wear-out Period (2 marks):**
- Failure rate **increases rapidly** with time
- Dominant failure modes: fatigue, corrosion, wear, creep, aging, oxidation
- Strategy: **scheduled replacement / overhaul** before end of useful life
- Modeled by **Weibull distribution with $\beta > 1$**

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.0005$/hr, MTTR = 4 hrs

**(i) Reliability at $t = 2000$ hrs (3 marks):**

$$ R(2000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.0005)(2000)} = e^{-1} $$

$$ R(2000) = 0.3679 \approx 36.79\% $$

**(ii) Design life for $R(t) = 0.95$ (2 marks):**

$$ 0.95 = e^{-0.0005 \, t} $$

$$ \ln(0.95) = -0.0005 \, t $$

$$ -0.05129 = -0.0005 \, t $$

$$ t = \frac{0.05129}{0.0005} = 102.58 \text{ hours} $$

**(iii) Steady-state availability (2 marks):**

$$ MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0005} = 2000 \text{ hours} $$

$$ A = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} = \frac{2000}{2000 + 4} = \frac{2000}{2004} $$

$$ A = 0.9980 = 99.80\% $$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up MTTF and MTBF** — MTTF applies to **non-repairable** items, MTBF to **repairable** systems. (Penalty: -1 to -2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the complementary relation** $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ when asked for probability of failure. (Penalty: -1 mark)
> 3. **Incorrect units in MTTF** — Always express in hours, cycles, or km as appropriate.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations — Examiners expect "constant $\lambda$" or "statistical independence" to be explicitly mentioned.
> 5. **Confusing failure (event) with failure mode (mechanism)** in 3-mark definition questions. (Penalty: -1 mark)
> 6. **Series system reliability**: Forgetting that the failure rates **add** in series, not the reliabilities. Use $\lambda_{\text{eq}} = \sum \lambda_i$ first.
> 7. **Calculation errors with exponentials**: Always keep sufficient decimal places; use $e^{-1} = 0.3679$, $e^{-2} = 0.1353$, $e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$ as memorized values.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Failure and Failure Modes"**

- [x] **Failure** = termination of the ability of an item to perform its required function (per KTU/RES standard definition).
- [x] **Failure Mode** = *how* the failure is observed/manifested; **Failure** = *what* happened.
- [x] Three pillars of failure: **Functional**, **Operational**, **Catastrophic**.
- [x] Failure classifications: **Catastrophic**, **Degradation**, **Intermittent**, **Wear-out**, **Infant Mortality**.
- [x] **Bathtub Curve** has three regions: Infant Mortality (decreasing $\lambda$), Useful Life (constant $\lambda$), Wear-out (increasing $\lambda$).
- [x] **Reliability function:** $R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau) d\tau}$ — the master equation.
- [x] **Exponential model:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — used when $\lambda$ is constant (Useful Life phase).
- [x] **Hazard rate:** $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$.
- [x] **MTTF (exponential)** $= \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$.
- [x] **MTBF** $= MTTF + MTTR$.
- [x] **Availability:** $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \lambda \cdot MTTR}$.
- [x] **Weibull distribution:** $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$ — most flexible 2-parameter model.
- [x] **Weibull shape parameter** $\beta$: $< 1$ (infant mortality), $= 1$ (random), $> 1$ (wear-out).
- [x] **Series system:** $\lambda_{\text{eq}} = \sum \lambda_i$ and $R_{\text{sys}} = \prod R_i$.
- [x] **Memoryless property** of exponential: $P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t)$.
- [x] **Arrhenius model:** $\lambda(T) = A e^{-E_a/(k_B T)}$ — temperature accelerates failure.
- [x] **Common mechanical failure modes:** ductile/brittle fracture, fatigue, creep, wear, corrosion, buckling.
- [x] **Common electronic failure modes:** open circuit, short circuit, parameter drift, thermal runaway.
- [x] **Burn-in** is used to eliminate infant mortality failures.
- [x] **FMEA** relies heavily on correct identification of failure modes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation to Memorize First:**
> $$ \boxed{R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \text{ (constant }\lambda\text{)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}} $$
> This single chain covers approximately 40% of all KTU PEMET747 Part B questions on Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Causes of Failures and Unreliability; Hazard Models: Constant Hazard Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Causes of Failures and Unreliability; Hazard Models: Constant Hazard Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Failure" in Reliability Engineering?

In KTU terminology, **failure** is defined as the *termination of the ability of an item to perform its required function*. When an item (a component, subsystem, or system) fails, it can no longer meet the performance specifications for which it was designed. The **unreliability** of a system, denoted $Q(t)$ or $F(t)$, is the probability that the system will *not* perform its intended function under stated conditions for a specified period of time $t$.

Formally:

$$F(t) = P(T \leq t)$$

where $T$ is the random variable representing the *time-to-failure*. Reliability is simply the complement:

$$R(t) = 1 - F(t) = P(T > t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Reliability is *always* a function of time. Saying "this product is reliable" without specifying the mission time $t$ is meaningless in the KTU framework.

### 1.2 Causes of Failures — Taxonomy

Failures do not occur randomly in a vacuum; they have identifiable root causes. KTU classifies them into three broad families:

| Category | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Intrinsic Failures** | Built into the product due to design, manufacturing, or material deficiencies | Poor solder joint, impurity in semiconductor wafer |
| **Extrinsic Failures** | Caused by external stresses beyond design limits | Voltage spike, humidity, vibration, operator misuse |
| **Induced Failures** | Caused by the *act of using* the product — wear, fatigue, corrosion | Bearing wear, battery depletion, electromigration |

### 1.3 The Conceptual Analogy: The Human Body

Think of a complex system as a **human body**:

- **Intrinsic failures** are like **genetic disorders** — you were born with a weak heart valve because the design (DNA) had a flaw.
- **Extrinsic failures** are like **accidents** — you broke your leg falling from a bike (a stress event the body wasn't designed to handle gracefully).
- **Induced failures** are like **ageing** — joints naturally wear out after decades of walking, regardless of how careful you are.

Just like a person has an *infant-mortality period* (early childhood diseases), a *healthy useful life* (adulthood), and a *wear-out phase* (old age), engineered systems follow a similar **bathtub curve** of failure rates.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bathtub Curve Concept:** A foundational KTU concept — the failure rate $h(t)$ versus time $t$ has three regions: (1) *Infant Mortality* (decreasing hazard due to debugging weak units), (2) *Useful Life* (roughly constant hazard, dominated by random extrinsic failures), and (3) *Wear-out* (increasing hazard due to degradation). The Constant Hazard Model mathematically describes the middle flat region.

### 1.4 The Hazard (Failure Rate) Function — Formal Definition

The **hazard rate** $h(t)$, also called the *instantaneous failure rate* or *force of mortality*, is defined as:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}$$

In words: given that the item has *survived* up to time $t$, what is the rate at which it is expected to fail in the next infinitesimal instant? Its unit is **failures per unit time** (e.g., **fit** = failures in $10^9$ device-hours, or **% per 1000 hours**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why hazard matters:** The hazard function is the *most physically intuitive* reliability metric. An engineer doesn't naturally think in terms of $R(t) = 0.97$; they think "this bearing fails at a rate of $5 \times 10^{-4}$ failures per hour." KTU examinations frequently ask you to extract $h(t)$ from a given distribution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Foundational Functions of Reliability

Any lifetime distribution is fully characterized by **three coupled functions**. Master these relationships — KTU questions almost always test the conversion between them.

- **Reliability Function** $R(t)$: probability of survival beyond $t$.
- **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** $F(t)$: probability of failure by time $t$.
- **Probability Density Function (PDF)** $f(t)$: instantaneous rate of failure occurrence at exact time $t$.

The fundamental relationship:

$$F(t) + R(t) = 1$$

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

### 2.2 Deriving the Hazard Function from the Above

The conditional probability of failure in $[t, t+\Delta t]$ given survival up to $t$ is:

$$P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t) = \frac{F(t + \Delta t) - F(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{R(t) - R(t + \Delta t)}{R(t)}$$

Dividing by $\Delta t$ and taking the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$:

$$h(t) = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \left(-\frac{dR(t)}{dt}\right) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

This is the **master equation** linking hazard, density, and reliability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of $h(t)$:** It is the *conditional* rate of failure — it answers "how dangerous is this instant for a unit that has already survived?"

### 2.3 The Cumulative Hazard Function

KTU also tests the **cumulative hazard function**:

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(u)\, du$$

This gives the *total accumulated failure intensity* up to time $t$. From the master equation, we can integrate to obtain a direct link between $R(t)$ and $H(t)$:

$$R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is one of the most powerful equations in reliability.** Given *any* $H(t)$, you can immediately compute $R(t)$ by exponentiation. The Constant Hazard Model is simply the case where $H(t) = \lambda t$ is linear.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Constant Hazard Model)

| Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Hazard rate | $h(t) = \lambda$ | Constant; failures per unit time |
| Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability, dimensionless, $0 \le R \le 1$ |
| Unreliability (CDF) | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Cumulative failure probability |
| Probability Density | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential distribution PDF |
| Cumulative Hazard | $H(t) = \lambda t$ | Linear in $t$ |
| MTTF (Mean Time To Failure) | $\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | For non-repairable items |
| MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) | $\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | For repairable items (after renewal) |
| Median Life (50% failure) | $t_{0.5} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} \approx \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$ | Time at which $R = 0.5$ |
| Variance of life | $\text{Var}(T) = \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}$ | Equals $(\text{MTTF})^2$ |
| Conditional Reliability | $R(t \mid t_0) = e^{-\lambda(t - t_0)}$ | **Memoryless property** |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU TRAP:** In the Constant Hazard Model, $\text{Var}(T) = (\text{MTTF})^2$ — a unique property of the exponential distribution. Examiners love testing whether you confuse this with the normal distribution where $\text{Var} = \sigma^2$ and $\text{MTTF} = \mu$ are independent parameters.

### 2.5 The Memoryless Property — Engineering Implication

A defining feature of the Constant Hazard (Exponential) Model is:

$$P(T > t_0 + t \mid T > t_0) = P(T > t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

A unit that has *already* survived for $t_0$ hours is *no more or less likely* to fail in the next $t$ hours than a brand-new unit.

**Real-world implication:** For components in their useful-life phase (e.g., a resistor in a stable circuit), preventive replacement based on age is *useless* — replacing it at 5000 hours vs 10000 hours yields no reliability benefit. This is counterintuitive to non-experts, and KTU often tests this through conceptual multiple-choice or short-answer questions.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of the Constant Hazard Model

- **Useful-life phase of the bathtub curve:** Most appropriate when failure is dominated by *random external shocks* (e.g., cosmic-ray-induced bit-flips in memory).
- **Complex systems with many components:** Due to the *Central Limit Theorem-like* behavior, the time-to-failure of a complex system with no dominant wear-out mechanism often converges to exponential.
- **Conservative initial design estimates:** When only limited failure data is available, assuming constant hazard is the *maximum-entropy* (least-biased) choice.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Constant Hazard to the Exponential Distribution

**Given:** The hazard rate is constant: $h(t) = \lambda$ for all $t \geq 0$.

**Step 1** — Write the master hazard-density-reliability relation.

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

This step comes from the definition of conditional failure probability; it is the master identity that connects all three functions for any lifetime distribution.

**Step 2** — Use $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$.

$$\lambda = \frac{-dR(t)/dt}{R(t)}$$

This substitution uses the identity $f(t) = -dR/dt$, derived from $f(t) = dF/dt = d(1-R)/dt = -dR/dt$. It is the standard way to convert a hazard specification into a differential equation in $R(t)$.

**Step 3** — Rearrange into a separable ODE.

$$\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda \, dt$$

We multiply both sides by $R(t)$ and divide by $\lambda$ to separate variables — this is the classical technique for solving first-order linear ODEs in reliability theory.

**Step 4** — Integrate from $0$ to $t$, applying the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$.

$$\int_{0}^{t} \frac{dR(u)}{R(u)} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} du$$

$$\ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = -\lambda t$$

The boundary condition $R(0) = 1$ reflects the certainty that a new item is functional at $t=0$. This gives $\ln R(0) = 0$.

**Step 5** — Exponentiate to obtain the Reliability function.

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

This is the **Exponential Reliability Law** — the central result of the Constant Hazard Model. KTU questions will frequently give you $R(t)$ and ask you to extract $\lambda$ via $\lambda = -\ln R(t) / t$.

**Step 6** — Derive the CDF and PDF.

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

These follow directly from the relationships $F + R = 1$ and $f = dF/dt$ established in Section 2.1.

**Step 7** — Derive the MTTF.

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

Integration by parts, or the standard gamma integral $\int_0^\infty t e^{-\lambda t} dt = 1/\lambda^2$, gives:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

This result is fundamental: in the constant-hazard regime, the *mean life is simply the reciprocal of the failure rate*. KTU problems frequently give MTTF and ask for the failure rate, or vice-versa.

**Step 8** — Derive the variance (using $E[T^2]$).

$$E[T^2] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t^{2} \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \frac{2}{\lambda^{2}}$$

$$\text{Var}(T) = E[T^2] - (E[T])^{2} = \frac{2}{\lambda^{2}} - \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}} = \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}$$

This is the variance result. Note that $\text{Var}(T) = (\text{MTTF})^2 = 1/\lambda^2$, which uniquely identifies the exponential distribution and is a common KTU verification question.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Computation Patterns

| Problem Statement | Required Computation | Key Formula |
|---|---|---|
| "Find the probability of failure by time $T$" | Compute $F(T)$ | $F(T) = 1 - e^{-\lambda T}$ |
| "Find the probability of survival up to $T$" | Compute $R(T)$ | $R(T) = e^{-\lambda T}$ |
| "Find the failure rate given MTTF" | Solve for $\lambda$ | $\lambda = 1/\text{MTTF}$ |
| "Find mission time for required reliability $R$" | Solve $R = e^{-\lambda t}$ for $t$ | $t = -\ln R / \lambda$ |
| "Find the median life" | Solve $R(t) = 0.5$ | $t_{0.5} = \ln 2 / \lambda$ |
| "Conditional reliability after $t_0$" | Use memoryless property | $R(t \mid t_0) = e^{-\lambda(t-t_0)}$ |
| "Failure rate after $t_0$ given $R(t)$" | Use master identity | $\lambda(t_0) = -R'(t_0)/R(t_0)$ |

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Full Solution

> **Problem (KTU Style):** A sensor has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.002$ failures per hour. Compute: (a) the reliability for a 100-hour mission, (b) the probability of failure in the interval $[100, 200]$ hours, (c) the MTTF, and (d) the median life.

**Solution (a):** Use $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$R(100) = e^{-(0.002)(100)} = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187$$

So there is approximately an **81.87%** probability that the sensor survives 100 hours. This is a direct application of the constant-hazard exponential reliability law with $\lambda t = 0.2$ as the dimensionless cumulative hazard.

**Solution (b):** The probability of failure in $[100, 200]$ given survival at 100 is computed using conditional probability.

$$P(100 < T \leq 200) = F(200) - F(100) = R(100) - R(200)$$

$$= e^{-0.2} - e^{-0.4} \approx 0.8187 - 0.6703 = 0.1484$$

So about **14.84%** of sensors fail in that window. The trick is recognizing that the joint event "survive to 100 *and* fail before 200" equals $R(100) - R(200)$.

**Solution (c):** Use the MTTF identity for the constant-hazard model.

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.002} = 500 \text{ hours}$$

This is the *expected* time to failure — half the sensors are expected to fail by this time (actually only 63.2% — see part d), since the median is less than the mean for exponential distributions.

**Solution (d):** Solve $R(t_{0.5}) = 0.5$.

$$e^{-0.002 \, t_{0.5}} = 0.5$$

$$t_{0.5} = \frac{\ln 2}{0.002} \approx 346.57 \text{ hours}$$

The median is less than the mean (346.6 < 500), confirming the right-skewed nature of the exponential distribution. KTU examiners often ask this comparison to test conceptual understanding of distribution shape.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Failure Cause Taxonomy (Block-Level Architecture)

The following block diagram maps the relationships between the three families of failure causes, their sub-mechanisms, and the corresponding reliability-engineering mitigation strategy.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CAUSES OF FAILURE] --> B[INTRINSIC]
    A --> C[EXTRINSIC]
    A --> D[INDUCED]

    B --> B1[Design Flaws]
    B --> B2[Manufacturing Defects]
    B --> B3[Material Impurities]

    C --> C1[Environmental Stress]
    C --> C2[Misuse or Operator Error]
    C --> C3[External Shocks]

    D --> D1[Mechanical Wear]
    D --> D2[Fatigue Cracking]
    D --> D3[Chemical Degradation]

    B1 --> M1[Mitigation: Robust Design, FMEA, Design Reviews]
    B2 --> M2[Mitigation: Quality Control, Burn-in Testing]
    B3 --> M3[Mitigation: Supplier Audits, Incoming Inspection]
    C1 --> M4[Mitigation: Derating, Environmental Shielding]
    C2 --> M5[Mitigation: Training, User Manuals]
    C3 --> M6[Mitigation: Redundancy, Surge Protection]
    D1 --> M7[Mitigation: Lubrication, Scheduled Replacement]
    D2 --> M8[Mitigation: Life-Cycle Testing, Fatigue Analysis]
    D3 --> M9[Mitigation: Coatings, Material Selection]

    M1 --> R[INCREASED RELIABILITY R t]
    M2 --> R
    M3 --> R
    M4 --> R
    M5 --> R
    M6 --> R
    M7 --> R
    M8 --> R
    M9 --> R
```

### 4.2 The Reliability Function Block — Functional Flow

This diagram captures how the *three* fundamental reliability quantities ($R$, $F$, $f$, $h$) are computed from one another inside the Constant Hazard Model.

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[Given Constant Lambda] --> L2[Reliability R of t equals exp of minus lambda t]
    L2 --> L3[CDF F of t equals 1 minus exp of minus lambda t]
    L2 --> L4[PDF f of t equals lambda times exp of minus lambda t]
    L3 --> L4
    L2 --> L5[Hazard h of t equals lambda constant]
    L4 --> L5
    L2 --> L6[MTTF equals 1 over lambda]
    L5 --> L6
    L2 --> L7[Median Life equals ln 2 over lambda]
    L5 --> L7
    L2 --> L8[Variance equals 1 over lambda squared]
    L6 --> L8
```

### 4.3 Bath-Tub Curve and Constant Hazard Region

This block diagram aligns the **three operational phases** of a system's life with the appropriate reliability model, emphasizing where the Constant Hazard Model applies.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Phase 1 Infant Mortality] --> P2[Phase 2 Useful Life]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Wear Out]

    P1 --> Q1[Decreasing Hazard, Dominated by Intrinsic Defects]
    P2 --> Q2[Constant Hazard, Dominated by Random External Shocks]
    P3 --> Q3[Increasing Hazard, Dominated by Induced Degradation]
```
