```mermaid
    Q1 --> M1[Use Weibull with shape less than 1]
    Q2 --> M2[Use EXPONENTIAL, Constant Hazard Model]
    Q3 --> M3[Use Weibull with shape greater than 1 or Normal]

    M2 --> N[KTU FOCUS: Module 1 Topic]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEMET747, Module 1]**
*Distinguish between intrinsic, extrinsic, and induced failures. Give one engineering example for each category.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Intrinsic failures** arise from causes *built into* the product — design errors, manufacturing defects, or material impurities. These are present even before the product is used. *Example: a micro-crack introduced during silicon wafer dicing in a semiconductor chip.* **(1 mark)**
- **Extrinsic failures** are caused by *external* stresses or events that exceed the design envelope — environmental conditions, operator misuse, transient over-voltages. *Example: a connector failure due to exposure to salt-spray corrosion in a coastal installation.* **(1 mark)**
- **Induced failures** result from the *normal act of operating* the product over time — wear, fatigue, corrosion, electromigration. *Example: progressive wear of brake-pad material in an automotive braking system.* **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEMET747, Module 1]**
*For a component with constant hazard rate $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour, compute the reliability for a mission time of 500 hours.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Using the Constant Hazard Model: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$R(500) = e^{-(5 \times 10^{-4})(500)} = e^{-0.25}$$

$$\boxed{R(500) \approx 0.7788}$$

**Reliability interpretation:** There is approximately a **77.88%** probability that the component will successfully complete its 500-hour mission. **(1 mark for final answer; 2 marks for setup and computation.)**

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam, PEMET747, Module 1]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Define the hazard function $h(t)$ and derive the relationship $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$. Starting from a constant hazard $h(t) = \lambda$, derive the exponential reliability function $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ and the probability density function $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$. Clearly state any boundary conditions used.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* An electronic control unit (ECU) in an automotive subsystem has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0008$ failures per hour. Compute: (i) the MTTF, (ii) the reliability for a 2000-hour mission, (iii) the probability that the ECU will fail in the time interval [1000, 2000] hours, and (iv) the median life of the ECU.

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

The hazard function is *defined* as the conditional probability density of failure at time $t$, given survival up to $t$:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}$$

Applying the definition of conditional probability $P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B)/P(B)$:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t)}{\Delta t \cdot P(T > t)} = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{F(t + \Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t}$$

Recognizing the limit as the derivative of $F(t)$, and using $f(t) = dF/dt$:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} \qquad \textbf{(2 marks for the derivation)}$$

For constant hazard $h(t) = \lambda$, and using $f(t) = -dR/dt$:

$$\lambda = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Separating variables:

$$\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda \, dt$$

Integrating from $0$ to $t$ with the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$ (a new unit is certainly functional at $t = 0$):

$$\int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{du}{u} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} d\tau \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln R(t) = -\lambda t$$

Exponentiating:

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}} \qquad \textbf{(2 marks for derivation to this point)}$$

Then:

$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}, \qquad f(t) = \frac{dF}{dt} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \qquad \textbf{(1 mark for each, totalling 2 marks)}$$

The boundary conditions stated: $R(0) = 1$ (initial certainty of operation) and $F(0) = 0$ (no failures at $t=0$). **(1 mark for boundary conditions)**

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $\lambda = 0.0008$ per hour.

**(i) MTTF:** **(1.5 marks)**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0008} = 1250 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) Reliability at $t = 2000$ hr:** **(2 marks)**

$$R(2000) = e^{-(0.0008)(2000)} = e^{-1.6} \approx 0.2019$$

So about **20.19%** of ECUs survive 2000 hours.

**(iii) Probability of failure in [1000, 2000]:** **(2 marks)**

$$P(1000 < T \leq 2000) = R(1000) - R(2000) = e^{-0.8} - e^{-1.6}$$

$$= 0.4493 - 0.2019 = 0.2474$$

Approximately **24.74%** of ECUs fail in this window. The probability of failure in the interval equals the *decrease* in reliability across that interval.

**(iv) Median life:** **(1.5 marks)**

$$t_{0.5} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.6931}{0.0008} \approx 866.4 \text{ hours}$$

The ECU reaches 50% unreliability at approximately **866.4 hours**, which is *less than* the MTTF of 1250 hours — consistent with the right-skew of the exponential distribution.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice for Question A**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain in detail the *three regions* of the bathtub curve. For each region, identify the dominant failure mechanism, the trend in the hazard rate, and the most appropriate statistical model to use. Indicate clearly which region is best described by the **Constant Hazard Model**.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A manufacturer claims that a batch of LED bulbs has a mean life (MTTF) of 8000 hours under constant operating conditions. Assuming the bulbs operate in their useful-life phase with a constant hazard rate, compute: (i) the failure rate $\lambda$, (ii) the probability that a bulb will burn out within the first 4000 hours, (iii) the reliability at 10000 hours, and (iv) the time by which 10% of the bulbs are expected to have failed.

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Bathtub Curve (7 marks):**

The bathtub curve is a qualitative model of how the failure rate $h(t)$ of a population of components evolves over time. It has three distinct regions.

- **Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** $h(t)$ is *decreasing*. Dominant failures are **intrinsic**: manufacturing defects, weak sub-components, and assembly errors that cause early-life failures. Mitigation: burn-in testing to weed out weak units. Best model: **Weibull with shape parameter $\beta < 1$**. **(2 marks)**

- **Region II — Useful Life:** $h(t)$ is *approximately constant*. Failures are dominated by **extrinsic random shocks** — environmental stresses, accidental misuse, transient overloads. These are unpredictable in time and uniform in their statistical incidence. Best model: **Exponential distribution / Constant Hazard Model**, i.e., $h(t) = \lambda$. **(2 marks)**

- **Region III — Wear-out:** $h(t)$ is *increasing*. Failures are dominated by **induced degradation** — wear, fatigue, corrosion, material aging. Mitigation: preventive replacement. Best model: **Weibull with $\beta > 1$** or **Lognormal**. **(2 marks)**

- **Drawing of the bathtub curve** with regions labelled: **(1 mark)**

> **Constant Hazard Model applicability:** The Constant Hazard Model is valid *only* in Region II (Useful Life), where the failure rate is flat. Applying it to Regions I or III would yield a non-conservative (Region I) or overly pessimistic (Region III) estimate of reliability.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $\text{MTTF} = 8000$ hours, constant hazard assumption.

**(i) Failure rate:** **(1.5 marks)**

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTTF}} = \frac{1}{8000} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per hour}$$

**(ii) Probability of failure within 4000 hours:** **(2 marks)**

$$F(4000) = 1 - e^{-(1.25 \times 10^{-4})(4000)} = 1 - e^{-0.5}$$

$$= 1 - 0.6065 = 0.3935$$

Approximately **39.35%** of bulbs are expected to fail within 4000 hours.

**(iii) Reliability at 10000 hours:** **(1.5 marks)**

$$R(10000) = e^{-(1.25 \times 10^{-4})(10000)} = e^{-1.25} \approx 0.2865$$

About **28.65%** of bulbs survive 10000 hours.

**(iv) Time by which 10% have failed:** **(2 marks)**

Solve $F(t_{0.1}) = 0.10$:

$$1 - e^{-\lambda t_{0.1}} = 0.10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-\lambda t_{0.1}} = 0.90$$

$$t_{0.1} = -\frac{\ln(0.90)}{\lambda} = \frac{0.10536}{1.25 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 842.9 \text{ hours}$$

So 10% of bulbs fail by approximately **843 hours** — substantially less than the MTTF, again reflecting the right-skew of the exponential distribution.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Boundary condition omission:** When deriving $R(t)$ from $h(t) = \lambda$, students often forget to explicitly state $R(0) = 1$. This alone costs 0.5–1 mark in 7-mark derivation questions.
> 2. **Confusing MTTF with MTBF:** For *non-repairable* items, use **MTTF**; for *repairable* systems in steady state, use **MTBF**. In the Constant Hazard Model, both numerically equal $1/\lambda$ — but the *terminology* matters and examiners do deduct marks for mislabeling.
> 3. **Probability-of-failure-in-an-interval vs. unconditional probability:** The question "what is the probability of failure in [1000, 2000]" is *not* the same as $F(2000)$. It is $R(1000) - R(2000)$. This is the single most common error in KTU numericals.
> 4. **Median ≠ Mean for the exponential:** Don't state $t_{0.5} = \text{MTTF}$. The median is $\ln 2 / \lambda \approx 0.693 \cdot \text{MTTF}$.
> 5. **Memoryless property misuse:** Do not apply the memoryless property to *non-constant* hazard models. It is *exclusive* to the exponential/constant-hazard distribution.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Reliability** $R(t) = P(T > t)$ — a function of time, not a single number.
- **Unreliability** $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ — the CDF of time-to-failure.
- **PDF** $f(t) = dF/dt = -dR/dt$.
- **Master identity:** $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ — central to *all* reliability derivations.
- **Cumulative hazard:** $H(t) = \int_0^t h(u)\, du$ and $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$ — the most general link.
- **Three failure families:** Intrinsic (built-in), Extrinsic (external), Induced (ageing).
- **Bathtub curve:** Infant mortality (decreasing $h$) → Useful life (constant $h$) → Wear-out (increasing $h$).
- **Constant Hazard / Exponential Model:** Valid in the useful-life phase; $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$; $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$; MTTF = $1/\lambda$; median = $\ln 2 / \lambda$; variance = $1/\lambda^2$.
- **Memoryless property:** $R(t \mid t_0) = e^{-\lambda(t-t_0)}$ — a used unit in useful life is *as good as new*; preventive replacement by age is futile in this regime.
- **Key conversion:** $\lambda = 1/\text{MTTF}$; $t = -\ln R / \lambda$.
- **KTU computation check-list:** Always state the formula, substitute, evaluate, and provide a physical interpretation in one concluding sentence.
- **Variance check:** $\text{Var}(T) = (\text{MTTF})^2$ uniquely identifies the exponential distribution — use it as a verification step in derivations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linearly Increasing Hazard Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linearly Increasing Hazard Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Linearly Increasing Hazard (LIH) Model** (also called the **Linear Failure Rate (LFR)** model in some KTU reference texts) is a non-exponential, time-dependent reliability model in which the instantaneous failure rate (hazard rate) of a component or system increases as a **linear function of operating time**. The mathematical statement of the model is:

$$\lambda(t) = \lambda_{0} + \beta t, \quad t \geq 0$$

where the symbols carry the following physical meanings:

- $\lambda(t)$ — instantaneous failure (hazard) rate at time $t$, expressed in failures per unit time (typically **failures/hour** or **failures/10⁶ hours**).
- $\lambda_{0}$ — the **initial (intrinsic) failure rate** at $t = 0$, corresponding to the constant-hazard (memoryless / exponential) baseline.
- $\beta$ — the **degradation (wear-out) coefficient** in units of failures per unit time squared; it quantifies how rapidly wear-out accelerates the failure probability.
- $t$ — the accumulated operating time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Two specific sub-cases are routinely tested in the End Semester Examination (ESE):
> 1. **Pure LIH model:** $\lambda_{0} = 0$, so $\lambda(t) = \beta t$.
> 2. **Mixed (LIH + Exponential) model:** $\lambda_{0} > 0$ and $\beta > 0$, so the failure rate has a *random* baseline plus a *wear-out* contribution.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **cotton sewing thread** being pulled steadily through the eye of a needle. The instant you start pulling ($\lambda_{0}$), there is some small chance of breakage (manufacturing flaw, weak spot). As you continue to pull, the thread frays a little more with every centimetre of travel — the longer it has been rubbing, the more likely it is to snap. The failure rate is therefore not constant (as the exponential model assumes) but grows with every additional unit of time.

**Translating to engineering:**
- $\lambda_{0}$ represents **early-life / random failures** (manufacturing defects, infant mortality that was not screened out).
- $\beta t$ represents **wear-out failures** (fatigue, corrosion, insulation degradation, bearing wear, electronic electromigration).

This is the mathematical embodiment of the right-hand side of the classical **bathtub curve** — the wear-out region.

> [!NOTE]
> The LIH model is one of the simplest analytically tractable models that captures the wear-out phenomenon. It is the bridge between the memoryless exponential model and the more complex Weibull model (with shape parameter $m = 2$).

### 1.3 Boundary Conditions & Physical Constraints

For a physically meaningful LIH model the following must hold:

$$
\lambda_{0} \geq 0, \quad \beta \geq 0, \quad \lambda(t) \geq 0 \;\;\forall\; t \geq 0
$$

- If $\lambda_{0} = 0$ **and** $\beta = 0$, the model collapses to a degenerate (no-failure) component.
- If $\beta = 0$, the model collapses to the standard **exponential distribution** (constant hazard, memoryless).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Logic of the LIH Derivation Chain

The LIH model is built on a **bottom-up** chain. Each reliability function is uniquely determined by the hazard function $\lambda(t)$:

1. **Start** with the prescribed hazard rate $\lambda(t) = \lambda_{0} + \beta t$.
2. **Integrate** the hazard rate from $0$ to $t$ to obtain the **cumulative hazard function** $H(t)$.
3. **Exponentiate** the negative of the cumulative hazard to get the **survival (reliability) function** $R(t)$.
4. **Differentiate** $R(t)$ to get the **probability density function (PDF)** $f(t)$.
5. **Integrate** $f(t)$ or evaluate $1 - R(t)$ to get the **cumulative distribution function (CDF)** $F(t)$.
6. **Integrate** $R(t)$ from $0$ to $\infty$ to get the **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this order?** The hazard rate $\lambda(t)$ is the *primitive* (fundamental) descriptor. The reliability, PDF, CDF, and MTTF are *derived* quantities. In any KTU numerical problem, you will almost always be given $\lambda_{0}$ and $\beta$ and asked to compute $R(t)$, $f(t)$, $F(t)$ or the MTTF.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why integrate $\lambda(t)$ to get $H(t)$?** Because $H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)du$ defines the *expected* number of failures accumulated up to time $t$. Reliability decays by the law $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$, a fundamental identity in reliability theory.
- **Why exponentiate the negative of $H(t)$?** Because the conditional probability of surviving an infinitesimal interval $dt$ is $1 - \lambda(t)dt$, and chaining infinitely many such intervals together yields an exponential of the integrated hazard.
- **Why is MTTF the integral of $R(t)$?** Because the expected value of a non-negative random variable $T$ is $E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)dt$ (a standard result from order statistics).
- **Why does the LIH model yield an error function in the MTTF?** Because integrating $e^{-(\lambda_{0}t + \beta t^{2}/2)}$ forces a Gaussian-completion trick, which produces the complementary error function $\text{erfc}(\cdot)$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Domain / Units |
|---|----------|--------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Hazard rate | $\lambda(t) = \lambda_{0} + \beta t$ | failures / unit time |
| 2 | Cumulative hazard | $H(t) = \lambda_{0} t + \dfrac{\beta t^{2}}{2}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Reliability (Survival) function | $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\lambda_{0} t - \dfrac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)$ | dimensionless, $0 \leq R \leq 1$ |
| 4 | Probability density function | $f(t) = (\lambda_{0} + \beta t)\,\exp\!\left(-\lambda_{0} t - \dfrac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)$ | probability / unit time |
| 5 | Cumulative distribution function | $F(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\lambda_{0} t - \dfrac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)$ | dimensionless, $0 \leq F \leq 1$ |
| 6 | Mean Time To Failure (general) | $\text{MTTF} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{2\beta}}\,\exp\!\left(\dfrac{\lambda_{0}^{2}}{2\beta}\right)\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{\lambda_{0}}{\sqrt{2\beta}}\right)$ | hours / cycles |
| 7 | MTTF for *pure* LIH ($\lambda_{0} = 0$) | $\text{MTTF} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{2\beta}}$ | hours / cycles |
| 8 | Hazard rate for *pure* LIH | $\lambda(t) = \beta t$ | failures / unit time |
| 9 | Reliability for *pure* LIH | $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Median life ($R(t_{0.5}) = 0.5$) | $t_{0.5} = \dfrac{-\lambda_{0} + \sqrt{\lambda_{0}^{2} + 2\beta \ln 2}}{\beta}$ | unit time |
| 11 | Mean residual life (MRL) at age $x$ | $m(x) = \dfrac{1}{R(x)}\,\text{MTTF}_{\text{shifted}}$ — derived by shifting origin to $x$ | unit time |
| 12 | Special function | $\text{erfc}(z) = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\int_{z}^{\infty} e^{-u^{2}}du$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE FOR KTU SCRIBES:** Never write the *absolute value* of a number with a vertical bar inside a markdown table — use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(\cdot)$ to avoid breaking the column boundary.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The LIH model is widely used in:

- **Mechanical engineering:** bearing fatigue, gear-tooth wear, fatigue-crack propagation in metals.
- **Aerospace:** cumulative fatigue damage in airframes (per the **Palmgren-Miner linear damage rule** analogy).
- **Electronics:** dielectric breakdown of capacitors, electromigration in integrated-circuit interconnects.
- **Civil engineering:** cumulative corrosion of reinforced concrete, fatigue of bridges.
- **Power systems:** transformer insulation aging, overhead conductor sag-fatigue.

> [!NOTE]
> For systems with *non-linear* wear-out (e.g., exponential wear), the **Weibull distribution** with shape parameter $m > 1$ is the appropriate generalization. The LIH model is the **Weibull-equivalent for $m = 2$** under a specific reparametrization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cumulative Hazard Function $H(t)$

**Starting point:** The hazard rate is prescribed as
$$
\lambda(t) = \lambda_{0} + \beta t
$$

**Step 1 — Definition of cumulative hazard:**
By definition,
$$
H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\,du
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the LIH expression:**
$$
H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} (\lambda_{0} + \beta u)\,du
$$

**Step 3 — Split the integral and integrate term-by-term:**
$$
H(t) = \lambda_{0}\int_{0}^{t} du + \beta\int_{0}^{t} u\,du
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the two elementary integrals:**
$$
H(t) = \lambda_{0} \cdot t + \beta \cdot \frac{t^{2}}{2}
$$

**Final result:**
$$
\boxed{\,H(t) = \lambda_{0} t + \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\,}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Reliability Function $R(t)$

**Step 1 — Fundamental identity linking reliability and cumulative hazard:**
$$
R(t) = e^{-H(t)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the expression for $H(t)$ obtained in §3.1:**
$$
R(t) = e^{-\left(\lambda_{0} t + \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)}
$$

**Final result:**
$$
\boxed{\,R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\lambda_{0} t - \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the single most-tested LIH formula in KTU board examinations. A 14-mark question almost always begins by asking the student to derive this expression.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Probability Density Function $f(t)$

**Step 1 — Fundamental identity linking PDF and reliability:**
$$
f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $R(t)$ using the chain rule:**
Let $g(t) = \lambda_{0} t + \dfrac{\beta t^{2}}{2}$, so that $R(t) = e^{-g(t)}$ and $g'(t) = \lambda_{0} + \beta t$.

$$
f(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[e^{-g(t)}\right] = -\left[-g'(t)\,e^{-g(t)}\right] = g'(t)\,e^{-g(t)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $g'(t)$ and $g(t)$:**
$$
f(t) = (\lambda_{0} + \beta t)\,\exp\!\left(-\lambda_{0} t - \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)
$$

**Final result:**
$$
\boxed{\,f(t) = (\lambda_{0} + \beta t)\exp\!\left(-\lambda_{0} t - \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)\,}
$$

**Sanity check (validity of the PDF):**
$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} f(t)\,dt = 1
$$
This is automatically satisfied because $f(t) = -R'(t)$ and $R(0) = 1$, $R(\infty) = 0$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Cumulative Distribution Function $F(t)$

**Step 1 — Identity between CDF and reliability:**
$$
F(t) = 1 - R(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Direct substitution:**
$$
F(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\lambda_{0} t - \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)
$$

**Final result:**
$$
\boxed{\,F(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\lambda_{0} t - \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)\,}
$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation of the MTTF for the Pure LIH Model ($\lambda_{0} = 0$)

This is the most common derivation requested in KTU ESE papers.

**Step 1 — Definition of MTTF:**
$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $R(t)$ for the pure LIH case ($\lambda_{0} = 0$):**
$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\beta t^{2}/2}\,dt
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the integral as a Gaussian integral:**

Recall the standard Gaussian integral:
$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-a u^{2}}\,du = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{a}}, \quad a > 0
$$

**Step 4 — Match the form with $a = \beta/2$:**
$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(\beta/2)\,t^{2}}\,dt = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{\beta/2}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2\pi}{\beta}}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**
$$
\text{MTTF} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\beta}}
$$

**Final result (pure LIH):**
$$
\boxed{\,\text{MTTF} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\beta}}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick numerical check:** If $\beta = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ /hr², then MTTF $= \sqrt{\pi / (4 \times 10^{-4})} \approx \sqrt{7854} \approx 88.6$ hours. This is *not* the same as the exponential-model MTTF, illustrating the strong effect of wear-out.

---

### 3.6 Derivation of the General MTTF (with $\lambda_{0} \neq 0$)

**Step 1 — Definition:**
$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \exp\!\left(-\lambda_{0} t - \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)dt
$$

**Step 2 — Complete the square inside the exponent:**

$$
\lambda_{0} t + \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2} = \frac{\beta}{2}\!\left(t^{2} + \frac{2\lambda_{0}}{\beta}t\right) = \frac{\beta}{2}\!\left[\left(t + \frac{\lambda_{0}}{\beta}\right)^{2} - \frac{\lambda_{0}^{2}}{\beta^{2}}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the MTTF integral:**
$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \exp\!\left\{-\frac{\beta}{2}\!\left[\left(t + \frac{\lambda_{0}}{\beta}\right)^{2} - \frac{\lambda_{0}^{2}}{\beta^{2}}\right]\right\}dt
$$

**Step 4 — Pull out the constant exponential factor:**
$$
\text{MTTF} = \exp\!\left(\frac{\lambda_{0}^{2}}{2\beta}\right)\int_{0}^{\infty} \exp\!\left[-\frac{\beta}{2}\!\left(t + \frac{\lambda_{0}}{\beta}\right)^{2}\right]dt
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the change of variable $u = t + \dfrac{\lambda_{0}}{\beta}$, $du = dt$:**

When $t = 0$, $u = \dfrac{\lambda_{0}}{\beta}$; when $t = \infty$, $u = \infty$.

$$
\text{MTTF} = \exp\!\left(\frac{\lambda_{0}^{2}}{2\beta}\right)\int_{\lambda_{0}/\beta}^{\infty} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\beta}{2}\,u^{2}\right)du
$$

**Step 6 — Rescale with $v = u\sqrt{\beta/2}$, so $du = \sqrt{2/\beta}\,dv$:**

$$
\text{MTTF} = \exp\!\left(\frac{\lambda_{0}^{2}}{2\beta}\right)\sqrt{\frac{2}{\beta}}\int_{\lambda_{0}/\sqrt{2\beta}}^{\infty} e^{-v^{2}}\,dv
$$

**Step 7 — Recognize the complementary error function:**

By definition,
$$
\text{erfc}(z) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\int_{z}^{\infty} e^{-v^{2}}\,dv \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \int_{z}^{\infty} e^{-v^{2}}\,dv = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\,\text{erfc}(z)
$$

**Step 8 — Substitute the erfc form:**
$$
\text{MTTF} = \exp\!\left(\frac{\lambda_{0}^{2}}{2\beta}\right)\sqrt{\frac{2}{\beta}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{\lambda_{0}}{\sqrt{2\beta}}\right)
$$

**Step 9 — Simplify the constants $\sqrt{2/\beta} \cdot \sqrt{\pi}/2 = \sqrt{\pi/(2\beta)}$:**

**Final result (general LIH):**
$$
\boxed{\,\text{MTTF} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\beta}}\,\exp\!\left(\frac{\lambda_{0}^{2}}{2\beta}\right)\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{\lambda_{0}}{\sqrt{2\beta}}\right)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting case verification:** As $\lambda_{0} \to 0$, the small-argument limit of $\text{erfc}$ is $\text{erfc}(z) \to 1 - \dfrac{2z}{\sqrt{\pi}} + \cdots$, and the exponential factor $\to 1$. The product approaches $\sqrt{\pi/(2\beta)}$, exactly matching §3.5. ✓

---

### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:** A pump has hazard rate $\lambda(t) = 0.002 + 5 \times 10^{-5}\,t$ (per hour).

**Find:** (a) $R(50)$ (b) $f(50)$ (c) The time $t_{0.5}$ at which reliability drops to 50%.

**Solution (a) — Reliability at $t = 50$ hours:**

Substitute $\lambda_{0} = 0.002$/hr, $\beta = 5 \times 10^{-5}$/hr², $t = 50$ hr into the reliability formula:

$$
H(50) = (0.002)(50) + \frac{(5 \times 10^{-5})(50)^{2}}{2} = 0.100 + \frac{(5 \times 10^{-5})(2500)}{2}
$$

$$
H(50) = 0.100 + 0.0625 = 0.1625
$$

$$
R(50) = e^{-0.1625} \approx 0.8500
$$

**Interpretation:** The pump has an 85% probability of surviving its first 50 operating hours.

**Solution (b) — PDF at $t = 50$ hours:**

First compute the hazard rate at $t = 50$:
$$
\lambda(50) = 0.002 + (5 \times 10^{-5})(50) = 0.002 + 0.0025 = 0.0045 \text{ /hr}
$$

Then
$$
f(50) = \lambda(50) \cdot R(50) = 0.0045 \times 0.8500 \approx 3.825 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per hour}
$$

**Solution (c) — Median life $t_{0.5}$:**

Set $R(t_{0.5}) = 0.5$:

$$
\exp\!\left(-\lambda_{0} t_{0.5} - \frac{\beta t_{0.5}^{2}}{2}\right) = 0.5
$$

Take the natural log of both sides:
$$
-\lambda_{0} t_{0.5} - \frac{\beta t_{0.5}^{2}}{2} = \ln(0.5) = -0.6931
$$

Rearrange into standard quadratic form:
$$
\frac{\beta}{2}t_{0.5}^{2} + \lambda_{0} t_{0.5} - 0.6931 = 0
$$

Substitute the numerical values:
$$
(2.5 \times 10^{-5})t_{0.5}^{2} + 0.002\,t_{0.5} - 0.6931 = 0
$$

Apply the quadratic formula $t = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2} - 4ac}}{2a}$ (taking the positive root):

$$
t_{0.5} = \frac{-0.002 + \sqrt{(0.002)^{2} + 4(2.5 \times 10^{-5})(0.6931)}}{2(2.5 \times 10^{-5})}
$$

$$
t_{0.5} = \frac{-0.002 + \sqrt{4 \times 10^{-6} + 6.931 \times 10^{-5}}}{5 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
t_{0.5} = \frac{-0.002 + \sqrt{7.331 \times 10^{-5}}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{-0.002 + 0.008562}{5 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
t_{0.5} = \frac{0.006562}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 131.24 \text{ hours}
$$

**Interpretation:** The pump has a 50% chance of failing by the time it has operated for ~131 hours.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of the LIH Model

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **derivation pipeline** of the LIH model — i.e., how the *primitive* hazard rate $\lambda(t)$ generates the family of derived reliability functions. This block-level architecture is the schematic most commonly expected in KTU Part-B answers.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input: lambda sub 0 and beta] --> B[Primitive hazard rate lambda of t = lambda sub 0 + beta times t]
    B --> C[Step 1: Integrate lambda of u from 0 to t]
    C --> D[Cumulative hazard H of t = lambda sub 0 times t + beta times t squared over 2]
    D --> E[Step 2: Exponentiate negative of H of t]
    E --> F[Reliability R of t = exp of negative lambda sub 0 t minus beta t squared over 2]
    F --> G[Step 3a: Differentiate negative R of t]
    G --> H[PDF f of t = lambda sub 0 + beta t times R of t]
    F --> I[Step 3b: Subtract from 1]
    I --> J[CDF F of t = 1 minus R of t]
    F --> K[Step 4: Integrate R of t from 0 to infinity]
    K --> L[General MTTF with erfc function]
    K --> M[Pure LIH MTTF = sqrt of pi over 2 beta when lambda sub 0 equals 0]
    H --> N[Step 5: Solve f of t equals 0 for mode]
    N --> O[Modal failure time t star = lambda sub 0 over beta]
    L --> P[Output: All reliability descriptors]
    M --> P
    O --> P
    J --> P
```

### 4.2 Schematic of the Hazard Rate Behaviour

The following graph maps out the **three canonical hazard-rate regimes** in reliability engineering, showing exactly where the LIH model sits in the global picture.

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1[Region I: Infant Mortality - Decreasing hazard] --> R2[Region II: Useful Life - Constant hazard exponential]
    R2 --> R3[Region III: Wear-out - Increasing hazard LIH and Weibull]
    R3 --> R4[LIH Models the wear-out region linearly]
    R4 --> R5[Pure LIH passes through origin lambda of 0 equals 0]
    R4 --> R6[Mixed LIH has positive intercept lambda sub 0]
```

### 4.3 Reliability-Decay Curve (Schematic Description)

> The LIH reliability $R(t) = e^{-(\lambda_{0}t + \beta t^{2}/2)}$ is a **Gaussian-shaped decay** in the logarithm of $R$. In particular, $-\ln R(t)$ is a *parabola* opening upwards with vertex at the origin. This means:
> - For very small $t$, $-\ln R(t) \approx \lambda_{0} t$ (linear, dominated by the random-failure component).
> - For large $t$, $-\ln R(t) \approx \dfrac{\beta t^{2}}{2}$ (quadratic, dominated by the wear-out component).
> - The transition between these two regimes occurs at $t^{*} = \lambda_{0}/\beta$, the **modal failure time** (the time at which the PDF $f(t)$ attains its maximum).

### 4.4 Mapping of Symbols to Physical Quantities (Reference Block)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SYM1[Symbol: lambda sub 0] --> P1[Meaning: Initial baseline hazard]
    SYM2[Symbol: beta] --> P2[Meaning: Wear-out slope]
    SYM3[Symbol: t] --> P3[Meaning: Operating time]
    SYM4[Symbol: R of t] --> P4[Meaning: Survival probability]
    SYM5[Symbol: f of t] --> P5[Meaning: Failure density]
    SYM6[Symbol: F of t] --> P6[Meaning: Cumulative failure probability]
    SYM7[Symbol: MTTF] --> P7[Meaning: Mean Time To Failure]
    SYM8[Symbol: erfc] --> P8[Meaning: Complementary error function]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the Linearly Increasing Hazard (LIH) model. State its general form and identify the parameters involved.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Linearly Increasing Hazard (LIH) model** is a time-dependent reliability model in which the instantaneous failure rate of a system increases linearly with operating time. The general form of the model is
$$
\lambda(t) = \lambda_{0} + \beta t, \quad t \geq 0
$$
where $\lambda_{0}$ is the **initial (baseline) hazard rate** at $t = 0$ (units: failures/unit time) representing random/early-life failures, and $\beta$ is the **wear-out coefficient** (units: failures/unit time²) representing the rate at which the failure rate grows with time. The model is used to describe systems that exhibit both random failures and age-related degradation.

**Mark Split (KTU Examiner Pattern):**
- Definition of LIH model: **1 mark**
- Mathematical form: **1 mark**
- Identification of $\lambda_{0}$ and $\beta$: **1 mark**

---

#### Question A2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Write the reliability function and MTTF for a pure Linearly Increasing Hazard model (with $\lambda_{0} = 0$).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For the **pure LIH model** with $\lambda_{0} = 0$, the hazard rate is
$$
\lambda(t) = \beta t
$$
The corresponding **reliability function** is
$$
R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)
$$
and the **Mean Time To Failure** is obtained by integrating $R(t)$ over $[0, \infty)$:
$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\beta t^{2}/2}\,dt = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\beta}}
$$

**Mark Split:**
- Reliability function: **1.5 marks**
- MTTF with derivation hint: **1.5 marks**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) Derive the reliability function, probability density function, and cumulative distribution function for a system whose hazard rate is given by $\lambda(t) = \lambda_{0} + \beta t$.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cumulative hazard:**
$$
H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} (\lambda_{0} + \beta u)\,du = \lambda_{0} t + \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Reliability function:** Using $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$,
$$
R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\lambda_{0} t - \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Probability density function:** Using $f(t) = -R'(t)$,
$$
f(t) = (\lambda_{0} + \beta t)\,\exp\!\left(-\lambda_{0} t - \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Cumulative distribution function:** Using $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$,
$$
F(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\lambda_{0} t - \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right)
$$

**Mark Split:**
- Cumulative hazard derivation: **1.5 marks**
- Reliability: **2 marks**
- PDF: **2 marks**
- CDF: **1.5 marks**

---

**(b) The hazard rate of a pump is $\lambda(t) = 0.0005 + 2 \times 10^{-5} t$ per hour. Compute (i) the reliability at 100 hours, (ii) the failure density at 100 hours, and (iii) the MTTF.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\lambda_{0} = 0.0005$ /hr, $\beta = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ /hr², $t = 100$ hr.

**(i) Reliability at $t = 100$ hours:**
$$
H(100) = (0.0005)(100) + \frac{(2 \times 10^{-5})(100)^{2}}{2} = 0.05 + 0.10 = 0.15
$$
$$
R(100) = e^{-0.15} \approx 0.8607
$$

**(ii) Failure density at $t = 100$ hours:**
$$
\lambda(100) = 0.0005 + (2 \times 10^{-5})(100) = 0.0025 \text{ /hr}
$$
$$
f(100) = \lambda(100)\,R(100) = (0.0025)(0.8607) \approx 2.152 \times 10^{-3} \text{ /hr}
$$

**(iii) MTTF:** Apply the general formula
$$
\text{MTTF} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\beta}}\,\exp\!\left(\frac{\lambda_{0}^{2}}{2\beta}\right)\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{\lambda_{0}}{\sqrt{2\beta}}\right)
$$

Compute the dimensionless argument:
$$
z = \frac{\lambda_{0}}{\sqrt{2\beta}} = \frac{0.0005}{\sqrt{(2)(2 \times 10^{-5})}} = \frac{0.0005}{\sqrt{4 \times 10^{-5}}} = \frac{0.0005}{0.006325} \approx 0.07906
$$

From standard tables, $\text{erfc}(0.07906) \approx 0.9111$.

Compute the constant $\sqrt{\pi/(2\beta)}$:
$$
\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\beta}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{4 \times 10^{-5}}} = \sqrt{78539.8} \approx 280.25 \text{ hr}
$$

Compute the exponential factor:
$$
\exp\!\left(\frac{\lambda_{0}^{2}}{2\beta}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{(0.0005)^{2}}{4 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{4 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = \exp(0.00625) \approx 1.00627
$$

Combine:
$$
\text{MTTF} \approx 280.25 \times 1.00627 \times 0.9111 \approx 256.95 \text{ hours}
$$

**Final Answer:** $R(100) \approx 0.8607$, $f(100) \approx 2.152 \times 10^{-3}$ /hr, $\text{MTTF} \approx 257$ hours.

**Mark Split:**
- Part (i): **2 marks**
- Part (ii): **2 marks**
- Part (iii): **3 marks**

---

#### Question B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a) Explain the physical significance of the LIH model parameters. Derive the MTTF for a pure LIH model.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Physical Significance of Parameters (4 marks):**

The LIH model $\lambda(t) = \lambda_{0} + \beta t$ has two parameters, each with a clear physical meaning:

1. **$\lambda_{0}$ (Initial Hazard Rate):** This is the failure rate of a *brand-new* component. It represents **random failures** arising from manufacturing defects, material impurities, or environmental shocks. Physically, $\lambda_{0}$ is the $y$-intercept of the linear hazard curve, and it dominates the early-life behaviour of the system. It has units of failures per unit time (e.g., /hr).

2. **$\beta$ (Wear-Out Coefficient):** This is the *slope* of the linear hazard curve. It represents the rate at which degradation processes (fatigue, corrosion, wear, dielectric breakdown) accumulate. Physically, $\beta$ is the "second derivative" of the system's aging — the higher $\beta$ is, the more aggressively the system ages. It has units of failures per unit time squared.

A positive $\lambda_{0}$ with $\beta = 0$ gives the **exponential (memoryless) model**. A zero $\lambda_{0}$ with positive $\beta$ gives the **pure wear-out LIH model** (the case derived below).

**Derivation of MTTF for Pure LIH (3 marks):**

For the pure LIH model, $\lambda_{0} = 0$ and $\lambda(t) = \beta t$. The reliability function is
$$
R(t) = e^{-\beta t^{2}/2}
$$
The MTTF is
$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\beta t^{2}/2}\,dt
$$
Comparing with the standard Gaussian integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-a u^{2}}du = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\pi/a}$ and identifying $a = \beta/2$:
$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{\beta/2}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\beta}}
$$

---

**(b) A mechanical component has a hazard rate $\lambda(t) = 4 \times 10^{-5}\,t$ per hour. Find (i) the reliability at 200 hours, (ii) the time at which $R(t) = 0.5$, and (iii) the MTTF.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\lambda_{0} = 0$, $\beta = 4 \times 10^{-5}$ /hr².

**(i) Reliability at $t = 200$ hours:**
$$
R(200) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{(4 \times 10^{-5})(200)^{2}}{2}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1.6}{2}\right) = e^{-0.8} \approx 0.4493
$$

**(ii) Time for $R(t) = 0.5$ (median life):**
$$
\exp\!\left(-\frac{\beta t^{2}}{2}\right) = 0.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{\beta t^{2}}{2} = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
$$
$$
t_{0.5} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 0.6931}{4 \times 10^{-5}}} = \sqrt{34657.4} \approx 186.2 \text{ hours}
$$

**(iii) MTTF:**
$$
\text{MTTF} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\beta}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{8 \times 10^{-5}}} = \sqrt{39269.9} \approx 198.17 \text{ hours}
$$

**Final Answer:** $R(200) \approx 0.4493$, $t_{0.5} \approx 186.2$ hr, MTTF $\approx 198.17$ hr.

**Mark Split:**
- Part (i): **2 marks**
- Part (ii): **3 marks**
- Part (iii): **2 marks**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $H(t)$ with the *number* of failures:** $H(t) = \int \lambda dt$ is the cumulative hazard (dimensionless), *not* a count. Examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark if the student writes $H(t)$ has units.
> 2. **Forgetting the complementary error function:** When $\lambda_{0} \neq 0$, the MTTF formula **must** include the $\text{erfc}(\cdot)$ term. Students who write only $\sqrt{\pi/(2\beta)}$ for the general case lose 2 of 3 marks.
> 3. **Sign errors in the quadratic for median life:** The equation is $\frac{\beta}{2}t^{2} + \lambda_{0} t - \ln 2 = 0$. Many students misplace the sign on $\lambda_{0}t$, leading to a wrong root selection. Always retain the **positive root** for physical time.
> 4. **Treating $\beta$ as dimensionless:** It is **NOT** dimensionless. It carries units of (failure rate)/time, i.e., /hr². Examiners check unit consistency.
> 5. **Integration limits in MTTF:** The integral is from $0$ to $\infty$, *not* $0$ to some finite $T$. Several students truncate it.
> 6. **Incomplete derivation of PDF:** The PDF is *not* simply $\lambda(t) \cdot R(t)$ by analogy — it must be **derived** via differentiation of $R(t)$. Skipping the derivative chain loses the 2 marks allotted to that step.
> 7. **Missing the $e^{-H(t)}$ identity:** Several students attempt to derive $R(t)$ by integrating $f(t)$ in the *wrong* direction. Always start with $\lambda(t)$ and integrate forward.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (last-minute KTU prep):**

- **LIH Hazard Rate:** $\lambda(t) = \lambda_{0} + \beta t$.
- **Cumulative Hazard:** $H(t) = \lambda_{0} t + \beta t^{2}/2$.
- **Reliability:** $R(t) = \exp(-\lambda_{0} t - \beta t^{2}/2)$ — the most important formula in this module.
- **PDF:** $f(t) = (\lambda_{0} + \beta t)\,R(t)$ — note the structural elegance: $f = \lambda \cdot R$.
- **CDF:** $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$.
- **Pure LIH MTTF:** $\sqrt{\pi/(2\beta)}$ — derived via Gaussian integral, no erfc.
- **General LIH MTTF:** $\sqrt{\pi/(2\beta)}\,\exp(\lambda_{0}^{2}/(2\beta))\,\text{erfc}(\lambda_{0}/\sqrt{2\beta})$.
- **Median Life:** $t_{0.5} = \dfrac{-\lambda_{0} + \sqrt{\lambda_{0}^{2} + 2\beta \ln 2}}{\beta}$ (positive root).
- **Modal Failure Time** (peak of PDF): $t^{*} = \lambda_{0}/\beta$ (valid only for $\lambda_{0} > 0$).
- **Limiting Cases:**
  - $\beta \to 0 \Rightarrow$ exponential model with MTTF $= 1/\lambda_{0}$.
  - $\lambda_{0} \to 0 \Rightarrow$ pure wear-out LIH.
- **Engineering Domains:** mechanical fatigue, electrical insulation aging, structural corrosion, bearing wear.
- **Always include units** for $\lambda_{0}$, $\beta$, MTTF, and time in the numerical answer — KTU examiners award a 0.5 mark "grace" for unit clarity.
- **Always justify the choice of formula** (pure vs. general) by *first* stating the values of $\lambda_{0}$ and $\beta$.
- **Special function to memorize:** $\text{erfc}(z) = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\int_{z}^{\infty} e^{-u^{2}}du$, with $\text{erfc}(0) = 1$ and $\text{erfc}(\infty) = 0$.
- **The LIH model is the Weibull equivalent for $m = 2$** when the Weibull scale parameter is $\eta = 1/\sqrt{\beta}$ and the location parameter is zero.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weibull Model; System Reliability: Series and Parallel Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Weibull Model; System Reliability: Series and Parallel Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Weibull Model — Formal Definition

The **Weibull Distribution** is a continuous probability distribution widely used in reliability engineering to model time-to-failure data of components and systems. It is exceptionally flexible because its shape parameter can model different phases of a product's life cycle.

The three-parameter Weibull model is defined as:

$$f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}, \quad t \geq \gamma$$

where:
- **β (beta) = Shape parameter** (dimensionless, β > 0) — controls the form of the failure distribution
- **η (eta) = Scale parameter** (units of time) — also called the **characteristic life**; the time at which 63.2% of the population has failed
- **γ (gamma) = Location parameter** (units of time) — represents the earliest possible failure time (often taken as 0)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Most KTU exam problems use the **two-parameter Weibull** (γ = 0). Always check whether the problem specifies γ. If unspecified, default to γ = 0.

> [!NOTE]
> The **cumulative distribution function (CDF)**, **reliability function R(t)**, and **hazard rate h(t)** are the three primary tools used to analyze any reliability model in KTU board examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Weibull Story"

Imagine a fleet of identical electric bulbs in a city:
- When **β < 1** (e.g., 0.5): Most bulbs fail very early due to manufacturing defects. The hazard *decreases* with time. This is the **"infant mortality" phase**.
- When **β = 1**: The bulb fails at a constant rate — purely random failures, equivalent to the **exponential distribution**.
- When **β > 1** (e.g., 2 or 3): As the bulbs age, components wear out. The hazard *increases* with time. This is the **"wear-out" phase**.

The Weibull distribution is essentially a "smart chameleon" — by simply changing β, an engineer can describe three completely different physical realities using a single equation.

### 1.3 System Reliability — Fundamental Concept

**System Reliability** is the probability that a system performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified period of time. KTU Module 1 specifically focuses on the two foundational configurations:

> [!NOTE]
> **Series Configuration:** The system works *only if every* component works. The weakest link determines survival. (Like a chain — break one link, the chain fails.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Parallel Configuration (Redundant):** The system works *if at least one* component works. Components provide backup for one another. (Like a multi-lane road — close one lane, traffic still flows.)

### 1.4 Intuitive Overview of Series vs. Parallel

Consider a laptop with **two fans** for cooling:
- **Series:** If *both* fans must run for the laptop to function → Series system. Cutting a fan kills the laptop.
- **Parallel:** The laptop works if *at least one* fan runs → Parallel system (redundancy). One fan suffices.

For a **satellite** with **four signal boosters** where losing one is acceptable but losing two is catastrophic → this becomes a **k-out-of-n (k-of-n)** configuration, an extension of the parallel concept.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Reliability values are always between 0 and 1. Series systems always have *lower* reliability than their weakest component. Parallel systems always have *higher* reliability than their strongest component.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Weibull Distribution — Theoretical Foundations

The Weibull distribution can be derived from the principle that the **conditional probability of failure** in a small time interval is proportional to some power of the elapsed time. This power is exactly the shape parameter β.

#### 2.1.1 Three-Parameter Weibull Functions

The full three-parameter form (with location γ):

**Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF):**

$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}$$

**Reliability Function (Survival Function):**

$$R(t) = e^{-\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}$$

**Probability Density Function (PDF):**

$$f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}$$

**Hazard (Failure) Rate Function:**

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$$

**Cumulative Hazard Function:**

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx = \left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}$$

#### 2.1.2 Interpretation of the Shape Parameter β

The shape parameter β dictates the failure behavior:

| β Value | Failure Rate Behavior | Physical Meaning | Distribution Equivalence |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| β < 1 | Decreasing | Infant mortality, early-life defects | — |
| β = 1 | Constant | Random failures, useful life | Exponential (λ = 1/η) |
| 1 < β < 2 | Slowly increasing | Early wear-out | — |
| β = 2 | Linearly increasing | Wear-out | Rayleigh distribution |
| β = 3 to 4 | Rapidly increasing | Advanced wear-out | Approximates Normal |
| β > 4 | Very rapid increase | Severe wear-out | Approximates log-normal tail |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** When β = 1, the Weibull reduces to the **Exponential distribution** with parameter λ = 1/η. When β = 2, it becomes the **Rayleigh distribution**. These equivalences are favorite KTU board exam topics.

#### 2.1.3 Mean Life (MTTF) of Weibull

The expected time to failure is given by the Gamma function:

$$\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right) + \gamma$$

For the two-parameter form (γ = 0):

$$\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

Useful Gamma values for KTU:
- Γ(1) = 1
- Γ(2) = 1
- Γ(1.5) = √π/2 ≈ 0.8862
- Γ(1.333) ≈ 0.89298 (for β = 3)
- Γ(1.25) ≈ 0.9064 (for β = 4)
- Γ(1.5) for β = 2: 0.8862

#### 2.1.4 Characteristic Life (η)

The **characteristic life** is the time at which **63.2%** of the population has failed, i.e., R(η) = e⁻¹ ≈ 0.368. This is analogous to a "half-life" but for failures rather than decay.

### 2.2 System Reliability — Theory and Logic

#### 2.2.1 Series System (Weakest-Link Configuration)

For a series system with n *independent* components:

$$R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t) \cdot R_3(t) \cdots R_n(t)$$

**Key logic:**
1. All components must survive for the system to survive.
2. The system fails as soon as *any single* component fails.
3. Reliability is the **product** of individual reliabilities.
4. For constant failure rates (exponential case):
   $$\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$
   $$\text{MTBF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_s}$$

#### 2.2.2 Parallel System (Redundant Configuration)

For a parallel system with n *independent* components, the system works if at least one component works. Therefore, it *fails* only when all components fail:

$$R_s(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left[1 - R_i(t)\right]$$

**Key logic:**
1. All components must fail for the system to fail.
2. System failure is the **intersection** of all component failures.
3. For n identical components each with reliability R(t):
   $$R_s(t) = 1 - \left[1 - R(t)\right]^n$$
4. For **two** identical components:
   $$R_s(t) = 2R(t) - R(t)^2$$
5. For **three** identical components:
   $$R_s(t) = 3R(t) - 3R(t)^2 + R(t)^3$$

#### 2.2.3 Series-Parallel Reduction Method

Complex systems are analyzed by:
1. **Identifying blocks** of components in series and parallel.
2. **Reducing** each block to a single equivalent reliability.
3. **Combining** reduced blocks until the entire system is one number.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Application:** The Weibull distribution is the industry standard for warranty analysis (automotive bearings, ball bearings, vacuum tubes, capacitors, diesel engine components, electrical insulation, etc.). The series-parallel modeling underpins reliability block diagrams used in aerospace, power plants, nuclear reactors, and computer networks.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables/Units | Conditions |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Weibull PDF (3-param) | $f(t)=\frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}e^{-\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}$ | β, η, γ in time units | $t \geq \gamma$ |
| Weibull CDF | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}$ | Dimensionless | $t \geq \gamma$ |
| Weibull Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}$ | Dimensionless | $t \geq \gamma$ |
| Weibull Hazard | $h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | per unit time | β > 0, η > 0 |
| Weibull MTTF | $\text{MTTF} = \eta \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ | Time units | γ = 0 |
| Characteristic Life (63.2%) | $R(\eta) = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$ | Fraction | — |
| Series System | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Dimensionless | Independent components |
| Series MTBF (exponential) | $\text{MTBF}_s = \frac{1}{\sum \lambda_i}$ | Time units | Constant λ |
| Parallel System | $R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | Dimensionless | Independent components |
| Parallel (n identical) | $R_s = 1 - (1-R)^n$ | Dimensionless | All Rᵢ = R |
| Parallel (2 components) | $R_s = 2R - R^2$ | Dimensionless | Identical |
| Parallel (3 components) | $R_s = 3R - 3R^2 + R^3$ | Dimensionless | Identical |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Deriving Weibull Reliability from the Hazard Function

The Weibull distribution can be *derived* from the assumption that the hazard rate follows a Weibull-shaped power function. This is a high-yield KTU derivation.

**Starting Assumption:** The hazard rate is a power function of time:

$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta} \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$

**Step 1:** Recall the fundamental identity relating reliability to hazard:

$$R(t) = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the assumed hazard form into the integral:

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{\beta}{\eta} \left(\frac{x}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} dx$$

**Step 3:** Let u = (x/η). Then du = (1/η) dx, so dx = η du. When x = 0, u = 0; when x = t, u = t/η.

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx = \int_{0}^{t/\eta} \frac{\beta}{\eta} u^{\beta - 1} \eta \, du = \beta \int_{0}^{t/\eta} u^{\beta - 1} du$$

**Step 4:** Integrate the power function:

$$\beta \int_{0}^{t/\eta} u^{\beta - 1} du = \beta \left[ \frac{u^{\beta}}{\beta} \right]_{0}^{t/\eta} = \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back into the reliability identity:

$$R(t) = e^{-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}$$

This is the **two-parameter Weibull reliability function** (with γ = 0). ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) of the Weibull Distribution

**Step 1:** By definition, the expected value of t is:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Use the alternative identity (E[T] for a non-negative random variable):

$$E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the Weibull reliability:

$$E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} dt$$

**Step 4:** Let u = (t/η)ᵝ, so t = η·u^(1/β), dt = (η/β)·u^(1/β - 1) du. When t = 0, u = 0; when t = ∞, u = ∞.

$$E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u} \cdot \frac{\eta}{\beta} \cdot u^{\frac{1}{\beta} - 1} du = \frac{\eta}{\beta} \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{\frac{1}{\beta} - 1} e^{-u} du$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the integral as the Gamma function Γ(1/β):

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} u^{\frac{1}{\beta} - 1} e^{-u} du = \Gamma\left(\frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{\eta}{\beta} \cdot \Gamma\left(\frac{1}{\beta}\right) = \eta \cdot \frac{1}{\beta} \Gamma\left(\frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 7:** Using the property Γ(x+1) = x·Γ(x), we have Γ(1 + 1/β) = (1/β)·Γ(1/β). Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example: Series and Parallel System with Constant Failure Rates

**Problem:** A system consists of three components. Two are in series (Component A and Component B), and this series combination is in parallel with Component C. Given failure rates:
- λ_A = 0.002 per hour
- λ_B = 0.003 per hour
- λ_C = 0.004 per hour

Find the system reliability and MTTF for a 100-hour mission.

**Step 1: Series Block (A and B) Reliability at t = 100 h**

For exponential components, R_i(t) = e^(-λᵢt):

$$R_A(100) = e^{-(0.002)(100)} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187$$

$$R_B(100) = e^{-(0.003)(100)} = e^{-0.3} = 0.7408$$

**Step 2: Series Block Equivalent Reliability**

$$R_{AB}(100) = R_A(100) \cdot R_B(100) = 0.8187 \times 0.7408 = 0.6065$$

**Step 3: Component C Reliability at t = 100 h**

$$R_C(100) = e^{-(0.004)(100)} = e^{-0.4} = 0.6703$$

**Step 4: Parallel System (AB parallel with C)**

$$R_s(100) = 1 - (1 - R_{AB})(1 - R_C)$$

$$R_s(100) = 1 - (1 - 0.6065)(1 - 0.6703)$$

$$R_s(100) = 1 - (0.3935)(0.3297)$$

$$R_s(100) = 1 - 0.1297 = 0.8703$$

**Step 5: MTTF of the Series Block**

$$\lambda_{AB} = \lambda_A + \lambda_B = 0.002 + 0.003 = 0.005 \text{ per hour}$$

$$\text{MTTF}_{AB} = \frac{1}{0.005} = 200 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 6: MTTF of the Entire System**

The combined system is two parallel exponential components with rates 0.005 and 0.004. The MTTF for two parallel exponential components with rates λ_1 and λ_2 is:

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_1} + \frac{1}{\lambda_2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2}$$

$$\text{MTTF}_s = 200 + 250 - \frac{1}{0.009} = 450 - 111.11 = 338.89 \text{ hours}$$

**Final Answer:** R_s(100) ≈ 0.8703 and MTTF ≈ 338.89 hours.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Problem Solver

| Given | Goal | Method |
|:---|:---|:---|
| β, η, t | Find R(t) | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ |
| β, η, t | Find h(t) | $h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}(t/\eta)^{\beta-1}$ |
| β, η, γ, t | Find R(t) [3-param] | $R(t) = e^{-((t-\gamma)/\eta)^{\beta}}$ |
| β, η | Find MTTF | $\eta \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ |
| Time for reliability R | Find η at given β | $\eta = t / (-\ln R)^{1/\beta}$ |
| All R_i | Find Series R_s | $\prod R_i$ |
| All R_i | Find Parallel R_s | $1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$ |
| Constant λ_i | Find Series MTTF | $1 / \sum \lambda_i$ |
| Identical R, count n | Find Parallel R_s | $1 - (1-R)^n$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Weibull Failure Rate Behavior — Functional Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Weibull Model Analysis] --> B{Is shape parameter β known?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Identify β Regime]
    B -- No --> D[Estimate β from failure data]
    C --> E{β Value Region}
    E -- β less than 1 --> F[Infant Mortality Phase]
    E -- β equals 1 --> G[Exponential - Constant Failure Rate]
    E -- β between 1 and 2 --> H[Early Wear-out Phase]
    E -- β equals 2 --> I[Rayleigh Distribution Form]
    E -- β greater than 3 --> J[Advanced Wear-out - Approximates Normal]
    F --> K[Compute h of t from Weibull hazard equation]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Compute R of t for mission time]
    L --> M[Compute MTTF using Gamma function]
    M --> N[End: Reliability Metrics Ready]
```

### 4.2 System Reliability — Series and Parallel Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph seriesBlock[Series Configuration - All Must Work]
        S1[Component 1] --> S2[Component 2] --> S3[Component 3] --> SOUT[System Output]
    end

    subgraph parallelBlock[Parallel Configuration - At Least One Must Work]
        P1[Component A]
        P2[Component B]
        P3[Component C]
        P1 --> PJOIN{Junction}
        P2 --> PJOIN
        P3 --> PJOIN
        PJOIN --> POUT[System Output]
    end

    subgraph combinedBlock[Series-Parallel Combined System]
        CS1[Stage 1 Series] --> CS2[Parallel Block] --> CS3[Stage 2 Series] --> COUT[System Output]
    end
```

### 4.3 Series-Parallel Reduction Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Start: Complex System Block Diagram] --> R1[Step 1: Identify series sub-blocks]
    R1 --> R2[Step 2: Identify parallel sub-blocks]
    R2 --> R3[Step 3: Compute equivalent reliability for each series block]
    R3 --> R4[Step 4: Compute equivalent reliability for each parallel block]
    R4 --> R5[Step 5: Replace each block with single equivalent component]
    R5 --> R6[Step 6: Repeat until single equivalent exists]
    R6 --> R7[Step 7: Apply final series or parallel formula]
    R7 --> R8[End: System Reliability Computed]
```

### 4.4 Decision Logic for Series vs. Parallel Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Begin Component Topology] --> D1{Does system fail when ANY single component fails?}
    D1 -- Yes --> D2[Classification: SERIES SYSTEM]
    D1 -- No --> D3{Does system work when ANY single component works?}
    D3 -- Yes --> D4[Classification: PARALLEL SYSTEM]
    D3 -- No --> D5{Does system need exactly k of n components?}
    D5 -- Yes --> D6[Classification: k-OUT-OF-n SYSTEM]
    D5 -- No --> D7[Classification: COMPLEX - Use Block Reduction]
    D2 --> DF[Apply Series Formula: R_s = Product of R_i]
    D4 --> DG[Apply Parallel Formula: R_s = 1 minus Product of 1 minus R_i]
    D6 --> DH[Apply k-of-n Formula]
    D7 --> DI[Use Step-by-Step Block Reduction]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Define the Weibull distribution. Mention the significance of the shape parameter β.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Weibull distribution is a continuous probability distribution used in reliability engineering to model time-to-failure of components and systems. Its probability density function is:

$$f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}$$

The shape parameter **β** (beta) is the most important parameter. It controls the shape of the failure rate function h(t):

- **β < 1**: Decreasing failure rate, indicates infant mortality or early-life failures.
- **β = 1**: Constant failure rate, equivalent to exponential distribution.
- **β > 1**: Increasing failure rate, indicates wear-out phase.

(3 marks awarded for: definition 1 mark, three β regimes 1.5 marks, physical meaning 0.5 marks)

---

**Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**A system has three independent components in series with reliabilities 0.9, 0.85, and 0.95. Calculate the system reliability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a series system, system reliability is the product of individual reliabilities:

$$R_s = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3$$

$$R_s = 0.9 \times 0.85 \times 0.95$$

$$R_s = 0.7650 \times 0.95 = 0.72675$$

Therefore, **R_s ≈ 0.727** (or 72.7%).

(3 marks: formula 1 mark, substitution 1 mark, answer 1 mark)

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a) Derive the reliability function of a two-parameter Weibull distribution from the hazard rate function. Also find the MTTF in terms of the Gamma function. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Assume the hazard rate follows a Weibull-shaped power form:

$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$$

**Step 2:** The reliability is related to hazard by:

$$R(t) = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting h(x):

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{x}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} dx$$

Substitute u = x/η, du = dx/η:

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t/\eta} \frac{\beta}{\eta} u^{\beta-1} \eta \, du = \beta \int_{0}^{t/\eta} u^{\beta-1} du$$

**Step 4:** Integrating:

$$H(t) = \beta \cdot \frac{u^{\beta}}{\beta}\bigg|_{0}^{t/\eta} = \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}$$

**Step 5:** Hence the reliability function:

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}}$$

**Step 6 — MTTF Derivation:**

For any non-negative random variable:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} dt$$

Let u = (t/η)ᵝ, then t = η·u^(1/β), dt = (η/β)·u^(1/β - 1) du:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{\eta}{\beta} \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{1/\beta - 1} e^{-u} du = \frac{\eta}{\beta}\Gamma\left(\frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

Using Γ(x+1) = x·Γ(x):

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)}$$

**(7 marks: hazard assumption 1, integration 2, reliability 1, MTTF integration 2, final form 1)**

---

**(b) For a parallel system with three identical components each having reliability 0.8, find the system reliability. Compare with a series configuration of the same three components. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Series System Reliability:**

$$R_{series} = R \times R \times R = (0.8)^3 = 0.512$$

**Step 2 — Parallel System Reliability:**

$$R_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - R)^3 = 1 - (0.2)^3 = 1 - 0.008 = 0.992$$

**Step 3 — Comparison:**

| Configuration | Reliability | Interpretation |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Series | 0.512 | System works only if all 3 work |
| Parallel | 0.992 | System works if any 1 works |

**Step 4 — Key Observation:**

The parallel system's reliability (0.992) is approximately **1.94 times** the series system's reliability (0.512). The parallel configuration provides substantial redundancy, dramatically increasing system reliability. However, this comes at the cost of using more components, increasing system weight, cost, and complexity.

**Conclusion:** Redundancy (parallel) is preferred for safety-critical systems (e.g., aircraft engines, nuclear plant cooling), while series configurations are used where cost/weight is a constraint and individual component reliability is already high.

**(7 marks: series calc 2, parallel calc 2, comparison table 2, conclusion 1)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) The failure time of a component follows a Weibull distribution with β = 2, η = 1000 hours, and γ = 0. Find (i) reliability at 500 hours, (ii) hazard rate at 500 hours, and (iii) MTTF. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** β = 2, η = 1000 hours, γ = 0

**(i) Reliability at t = 500 h:**

$$R(500) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} = e^{-(500/1000)^2}$$

$$R(500) = e^{-(0.5)^2} = e^{-0.25}$$

$$R(500) = 0.7788$$

So the probability of survival at 500 hours is **78.88%**.

**(ii) Hazard Rate at t = 500 h:**

$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$$

$$h(500) = \frac{2}{1000}\left(\frac{500}{1000}\right)^{2-1} = 0.002 \times (0.5)^1$$

$$h(500) = 0.001 \text{ per hour} = 10^{-3} \text{ per hour}$$

**(iii) MTTF:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right) = 1000 \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

$$= 1000 \cdot \Gamma(1.5) = 1000 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$$

$$= 1000 \times 0.8862 = 886.2 \text{ hours}$$

**(7 marks: part i 2.5, part ii 2, part iii 2.5)**

---

**(b) A system has two parallel paths. Each path consists of two components in series. The first path has components with reliabilities 0.9 and 0.8. The second path has components with reliabilities 0.85 and 0.95. Find the system reliability using the series-parallel reduction method. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Series Block 1 Reliability:**

$$R_{P1} = R_1 \times R_2 = 0.9 \times 0.8 = 0.72$$

**Step 2: Series Block 2 Reliability:**

$$R_{P2} = R_3 \times R_4 = 0.85 \times 0.95 = 0.8075$$

**Step 3: Parallel Combination of Two Paths:**

$$R_{sys} = 1 - (1 - R_{P1})(1 - R_{P2})$$

$$R_{sys} = 1 - (1 - 0.72)(1 - 0.8075)$$

$$R_{sys} = 1 - (0.28)(0.1925)$$

$$R_{sys} = 1 - 0.0539 = 0.9461$$

**Step 4: Verification of Result:**

| Step | Block | Type | Calculation | Result |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | R1 ∥ R2 (Path 1) | Series | 0.9 × 0.8 | 0.7200 |
| 2 | R3 ∥ R4 (Path 2) | Series | 0.85 × 0.95 | 0.8075 |
| 3 | Path 1 ∥ Path 2 | Parallel | 1 - (0.28)(0.1925) | 0.9461 |

**Conclusion:** The system reliability is **0.9461** (94.61%). This is significantly higher than either path's individual reliability because of the parallel redundancy at the path level.

**(7 marks: block 1 series 1.5, block 2 series 1.5, parallel combine 2.5, conclusion 1.5)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to take γ into account:** Many students assume γ = 0 without checking. If the problem gives γ ≠ 0, you MUST use (t - γ) and not just t. **(-2 marks if ignored)**
>
> 2. **Mis-identifying Series vs. Parallel:** If a system has two components but BOTH must fail for the system to fail, it is PARALLEL (not series). Read the problem statement carefully. **(-3 marks for wrong topology)**
>
> 3. **Adding reliabilities instead of multiplying:** For series systems, R_s = R₁ × R₂, NOT R₁ + R₂. Adding gives a value > 1, which is impossible. **(-2 marks)**
>
> 4. **Wrong Gamma function values:** For β = 2, the answer is η·Γ(1.5) = η·(√π/2), NOT η·Γ(2) = η·1. **(-2 marks)**
>
> 5. **Units of hazard rate:** Hazard rate h(t) is in *per unit time* (e.g., per hour), not dimensionless. **(-1 mark)**
>
> 6. **Confusing reliability with failure rate:** R(t) is dimensionless (a probability). λ (or h) has units of 1/time. Never mix them in formulas.
>
> 7. **Not showing MTTF in the answer for Weibull problems:** KTU examiners award marks specifically for the MTTF step. Always include the Gamma function evaluation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1, Weibull & System Reliability**

- [ ] **Weibull PDF** = (β/η)(t/η)^(β-1)·exp(-(t/η)ᵝ)
- [ ] **Weibull Reliability** R(t) = exp(-(t/η)ᵝ) — most used formula
- [ ] **Weibull Hazard** h(t) = (β/η)(t/η)^(β-1) — never constant for β ≠ 1
- [ ] **β < 1** → Decreasing hazard → Infant mortality
- [ ] **β = 1** → Constant hazard → Exponential distribution (λ = 1/η)
- [ ] **β = 2** → Linearly increasing hazard → Rayleigh distribution
- [ ] **β > 1** → Increasing hazard → Wear-out
- [ ] **η (scale)** = time at which 63.2% population has failed; R(η) = e⁻¹ ≈ 0.368
- [ ] **γ (location)** = minimum possible failure time; default = 0 unless stated
- [ ] **MTTF of Weibull** = η·Γ(1 + 1/β)
- [ ] **Series system** = product of reliabilities; weakest-link principle
- [ ] **Parallel system** = 1 - product of unreliabilities; redundancy principle
- [ ] **Parallel (n identical)** = 1 - (1-R)ⁿ
- [ ] **Series-parallel reduction** = break into sub-blocks, reduce each, combine iteratively
- [ ] **Series MTBF (exponential)** = 1 / Σλᵢ
- [ ] **Always show units** (hours, per hour) in final answers
- [ ] **Always specify β regime** (infant / useful life / wear-out) when discussing Weibull behavior

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combined Series-Parallel Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Combined Series-Parallel Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combined Series-Parallel System:** A combined (or *complex*) system is a reliability configuration in which subsystems are arranged in a **mixture of series and parallel paths** such that the overall structure is neither purely series nor purely parallel. Reliability is computed by **progressively reducing** the block diagram — first solving the parallel sub-blocks, then treating them as equivalent single elements in a series chain (or vice versa).

**Key KTU Terminology you MUST know:**

- **Reliability, R(t):** The probability that a component or system performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified time interval $[0, t]$.
- **Unreliability, Q(t) = 1 − R(t)**
- **System Success Criterion:** A system is *successful* if there exists **at least one continuous path** from the input (source) to the output (sink) through *all* functioning elements.
- **Failure Rate, λ:** Constant for the exponential reliability model used throughout Module 1.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The City Water Supply Analogy 💧**
>
> Imagine water flowing from a **reservoir (source)** to your **home (sink)**:
> - **Series Pipe = a single pipe segment** — if *any* segment cracks, water stops.
> - **Parallel Pipes = two pipes side-by-side** — water flows as long as *at least one* pipe is intact.
> - **Combined System = a network of pipes in series and parallel.** For example: reservoir → (Pipe A **OR** Pipe B in parallel) → Pump → (Pipe C **OR** Pipe D in parallel) → Home.
>
> The reliability of getting water to your home depends on **both** the parallel redundancy (extra pipes) **and** the series bottlenecks (the pump that all water must pass through).
>
> This mirrors exactly how we calculate reliability in combined systems: **solve parallel blocks first, then multiply in series**.

## 1.3 Engineering Significance

Combined series-parallel configurations are the **backbone of fault-tolerant engineering design**. You will encounter them in:

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Flight control systems with redundant actuators in series with main bus |
| **Power Systems** | Parallel generator sets feeding a common series transformer |
| **Computer Networks** | Routers arranged with parallel links feeding backbone switches |
| **Automotive** | Dual brake circuits in series with master cylinder |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Insight (KTU):** Module 1 of PEMET747 emphasizes *qualitative reliability block diagram (RBD) construction* and *quantitative evaluation* using exponential failure distributions. Expect direct numerical problems on combined systems worth **7 to 14 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Logic of Combination

### Step 1: Series Subsystem (The "Weakest Link" Principle)
A **series system** functions **only if every component functions**. If any single element fails, the entire system fails.

- System reliability = **product of individual reliabilities** (assuming statistical independence).

$$R_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

> **Why multiplication?** Because all components must work simultaneously — the intersection of independent events multiplies.

### Step 2: Parallel Subsystem (The "Redundancy" Principle)
A **parallel system** functions as long as **at least one component functions**.

- System reliability = **1 minus the probability that ALL components fail**.

$$R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$

### Step 3: Combined System — The Reduction Algorithm
For a **combined series-parallel system**, the KTU-accepted method is:

1. **Identify the deepest parallel sub-block** (innermost redundancy).
2. **Reduce** that parallel sub-block to a single equivalent component using $R_p = 1 - \prod(1-R_i)$.
3. **Repeat** the reduction — now the equivalent element sits in series with others.
4. **Multiply** the series reliabilities of all reduced blocks to obtain $R_{\text{system}}$.
5. The final system is also a **series product of the reduced parallel-block reliabilities**.

### Step 4: Exponential Reliability Model
For constant failure rate λ, the **reliability of a single component** is:

$$R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$$

Therefore, for a **series system** with constant rates:

$$R_{\text{series}}(t) = \exp\left(-\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i t\right) = \exp(-\lambda_{\text{eq}}\, t)$$

where $\lambda_{\text{eq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ is the **equivalent series failure rate**.

For a **parallel system** of *n identical components* (λ same for all), with constant failure rate:

$$R_{\text{parallel}}(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^{n}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Series systems *add* failure rates; parallel systems *dramatically reduce* system unreliability. The **MTTF** (Mean Time To Failure) of a series system is:
>
> $$\text{MTTF}_{\text{series}} = \frac{1}{\sum \lambda_i}$$
>
> For two parallel identical components:
>
> $$\text{MTTF}_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{2\lambda} = \frac{3}{2\lambda}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — These are the only formulas you need for 90% of Module 1 problems.**

| # | Configuration | Reliability Formula | Equivalent Failure Rate | MTTF |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single Component | $R = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ | $1/\lambda$ |
| 2 | Series (n components) | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | $\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | $1 / \sum \lambda_i$ |
| 3 | Parallel (n components) | $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-R_i)$ | No simple closed form | $\sum_{k=1}^{n} 1/(k\lambda)$ for identical |
| 4 | Parallel (2 components) | $R_p = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$ | — | $3/(2\lambda)$ identical |
| 5 | Parallel (3 components) | $R_p = R_1+R_2+R_3 - R_1R_2 - R_1R_3 - R_2R_3 + R_1R_2R_3$ | — | $11/(6\lambda)$ identical |
| 6 | Combined S-P (reduction) | $R_{\text{sys}} = \prod_{j} R_{p,j}$ | — | Numerical integration |
| 7 | MTTF general (exponential) | $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$ | — | — |

> **Boundary Conditions to remember:**
> - $0 \le R \le 1$ (always)
> - $R_{\text{series}} \le \min(R_i)$ (the weakest link dominates)
> - $R_{\text{parallel}} \ge \max(R_i)$ (redundancy always helps)

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In **maintainability and design-for-reliability**, engineers use combined systems to:
- **Identify bottlenecks** (series elements with low R) for *redesign*.
- **Allocate redundancy budget** (parallel elements) cost-effectively.
- **Optimize** the trade-off between reliability, weight, and cost — a recurring theme in **PEMET747** design problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reliability of Two Parallel Components

**Statement:** For two independent components with reliabilities $R_1$ and $R_2$ in parallel, prove that $R_p = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$.

**Step 1:** Define the failure events.
- Let $A$ = event that component 1 works; $P(A) = R_1$.
- Let $B$ = event that component 2 works; $P(B) = R_2$.
- The parallel system works if $A \cup B$ (at least one works) occurs.

**Step 2:** Apply the inclusion-exclusion principle of probability.

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

**Step 3:** Use statistical independence of A and B.

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B) = R_1 R_2$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the inclusion-exclusion equation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_p &= R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2 \\
&= 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

> This matches the general parallel formula for $n = 2$. **Q.E.D.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Equivalent Failure Rate of a Series System

**Statement:** For $n$ components in series, each with constant failure rate $\lambda_i$, show that $\lambda_{\text{eq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$.

**Step 1:** Write series reliability.

$$R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t}$$

**Step 2:** Combine exponentials.

$$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} \cdot e^{-\lambda_2 t} \cdots e^{-\lambda_n t}$$

**Step 3:** Use the property $e^a \cdot e^b = e^{a+b}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_s(t) &= \exp\!\left[-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \cdots + \lambda_n) t\right] \\
&= \exp\!\left[-\lambda_{\text{eq}}\, t\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Equate exponents.

$$\lambda_{\text{eq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$

> Failure rates of independent components in series simply **add**. **Q.E.D.**

## 3.3 Derivation 3: MTTF of Two Parallel Identical Components

**Statement:** For two identical parallel components with failure rate $\lambda$, show that $\text{MTTF} = \frac{3}{2\lambda}$.

**Step 1:** Each component has $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

**Step 2:** Parallel reliability.

$$R_p(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^2$$

**Step 3:** Expand the square.

$$(1 - e^{-\lambda t})^2 = 1 - 2e^{-\lambda t} + e^{-2\lambda t}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore the parallel reliability is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_p(t) &= 1 - 1 + 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t} \\
&= 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Use the definition of MTTF as the integral of reliability.

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^{\infty} R_p(t)\, dt$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the integral term by term.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF} &= \int_0^{\infty} \left(2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}\right) dt \\
&= 2 \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} dt - \int_0^{\infty} e^{-2\lambda t} dt
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** Recall that $\int_0^{\infty} e^{-at} dt = 1/a$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF} &= 2 \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{2\lambda} \\
&= \frac{2}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{2\lambda} \\
&= \frac{4 - 1}{2\lambda} \\
&= \frac{3}{2\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Q.E.D.** The MTTF of the parallel redundant system is **1.5 times** that of a single component.

## 3.4 Worked Example: A Classic Combined Series-Parallel Problem

> **Problem:** A system consists of **two parallel blocks (A and B) in series with a single component C**.
> - Block A and B are *parallel* to each other.
