> - Each block contains a single component with reliability $R_A = 0.9$ and $R_B = 0.8$ respectively.
> - Component C has reliability $R_C = 0.95$.
> - All components are independent.
>
> **Find (i) the system reliability and (ii) the MTTF assuming constant failure rate $\lambda$ from $R = e^{-\lambda t}$ over 1000 hours.**

### Solution:

**Part (i): System Reliability**

**Step 1:** Identify the parallel subsystem (A parallel with B). The system reduces to:

$$R_{AB} = R_A + R_B - R_A R_B$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{AB} &= 0.9 + 0.8 - (0.9)(0.8) \\
&= 1.7 - 0.72 \\
&= 0.98
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Now treat the reduced parallel block $R_{AB}$ in series with $R_C$.

$$R_{\text{sys}} = R_{AB} \cdot R_C$$

**Step 4:** Substitute.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{sys}} &= 0.98 \times 0.95 \\
&= 0.931
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **System Reliability = 0.931 (or 93.1%)**

**Part (ii): MTTF**

For series combination, the equivalent failure rate adds.

$$\lambda_{\text{eq}} = \lambda_A + \lambda_B + \lambda_C$$

But since $R_{AB}$ and $R_C$ are *composite* reliabilities, we compute the overall reliability as a function of t and integrate.

**Step 5:** Express all reliabilities in terms of t.

$$R_{\text{sys}}(t) = \left[2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}\right] \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 6:** Use the definition of MTTF and integrate.

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^{\infty} R_{\text{sys}}(t)\, dt$$

**Step 7:** Expand and integrate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF} &= \int_0^{\infty} \left(2e^{-2\lambda t} - e^{-3\lambda t}\right) dt \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2\lambda} - \frac{1}{3\lambda} \\
&= \frac{1}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{3\lambda} \\
&= \frac{2}{3\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **MTTF = $\dfrac{2}{3\lambda}$**

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

| System Shape | First Reduce | Then Compute | KTU Trick |
|---|---|---|---|
| Series of Parallel blocks | Each parallel block | Multiply reduced values | $R_s = \prod R_{p,j}$ |
| Parallel of Series branches | Each series branch | $1 - \prod(1 - R_{s,k})$ | Apply De Morgan symmetrically |
| Bridge / Lattice structure | Star–Delta transformation | Condition on a key element | Rare in KTU 2024, but check! |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — A Generic Combined Series-Parallel System

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[Source] --> A1[Block A]
    A1 --> P1{Parallel Group}
    P1 --> B1[Block B1]
    P1 --> B2[Block B2]
    B1 --> C1[Block C]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> P2{Parallel Group}
    P2 --> D1[Block D1]
    P2 --> D2[Block D2]
    D1 --> SNK[Sink]
    D2 --> SNK
```

> **How to read it:** Current enters at `Source`, passes through `Block A` (series bottleneck), enters a *parallel group* (`B1` OR `B2`), merges at `Block C` (series bottleneck again), enters a second *parallel group* (`D1` OR `D2`), and exits at `Sink`.

## 4.2 Reduction Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    STAGE1[Stage 1: Original System]:::stage
    STAGE2[Stage 2: Identify Parallel Blocks]:::stage
    STAGE3[Stage 3: Reduce Parallel Block PB1]:::stage
    STAGE4[Stage 4: Reduce Parallel Block PB2]:::stage
    STAGE5[Stage 5: Series Multiply All Reduced Blocks]:::stage
    STAGE6[Stage 6: Final System Reliability Rsys]:::final

    STAGE1 --> STAGE2 --> STAGE3 --> STAGE4 --> STAGE5 --> STAGE6

    classDef stage fill:#E8F1FB,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Series Sub-System Reliability Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Source] --> E1[Element 1: R1]
    E1 --> E2[Element 2: R2]
    E2 --> E3[Element 3: R3]
    E3 --> E4[Element n: Rn]
    E4 --> S2[Sink]
    S2 -.- NOTE[R series = R1 times R2 times R3 times Rn]:::note

    classDef note fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Parallel Sub-System Reliability Map

```mermaid

    S1[Source] --> SPLIT{Split Node}
    SPLIT --> P1[Branch 1: R1]
    SPLIT --> P2[Branch 2: R2]
    SPLIT --> P3[Branch 3: R3]
    P1 --> MERGE{Merge Node}
    P2 --> MERGE
    P3 --> MERGE
    MERGE --> S2[Sink]
    MERGE -.- NOTE[R parallel = 1 minus product of 1 minus Ri]:::note

```

## 4.5 Worked-Example Architecture Map (Section 3.4)

```mermaid

    A --> PAR{Parallel Block}
    PAR --> B[Component B: R = 0.8]

    C --> SNK[Sink]
    PAR -.- NOTE[Reduced: Rp = 0.9 + 0.8 minus 0.9 times 0.8 = 0.98]:::note
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define a combined series-parallel system in reliability engineering. How is its reliability evaluated?**

**Model Answer:**

A combined (or complex) series-parallel system is a reliability configuration where subsystems are connected in a mixture of series and parallel arrangements, such that the overall system is neither purely series nor purely parallel.

**Evaluation Procedure (3-step method):**
1. **Identify** the innermost parallel sub-blocks within the system.
2. **Reduce** each parallel sub-block to a single equivalent element using the formula:
$$R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$
3. **Multiply** the reliabilities of all reduced (series-connected) elements:
$$R_{\text{sys}} = \prod_{j=1}^{m} R_{p,j}$$

This progressive reduction approach simplifies even highly nested configurations.

> **Marking Scheme:** Definition (1M) + Procedure (1M) + Formula (1M).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: Two components with reliabilities 0.9 and 0.8 are connected (a) in series and (b) in parallel. Compute the system reliability in each case.**

**Model Answer:**

**Case (a) — Series:**
$$R_s = R_1 \times R_2 = 0.9 \times 0.8 = 0.72$$

**Case (b) — Parallel:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_p &= R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2 \\
&= 0.9 + 0.8 - (0.9)(0.8) \\
&= 1.7 - 0.72 = 0.98
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Series Reliability = 0.72; Parallel Reliability = 0.98**

> **Marking Scheme:** Series formula + answer (1.5M) + Parallel formula + answer (1.5M).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam — Model Question Paper Pattern, Module 1]**

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

A system consists of three units A, B, and C connected as follows:
- Units A and B are connected in **parallel** with each other.
- This parallel combination is connected in **series** with unit C.
- The reliabilities are: $R_A = 0.90$, $R_B = 0.85$, $R_C = 0.95$.

**Tasks:**
- **(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the reliability block diagram and compute the system reliability.
- **(b) [7 Marks]** If each component follows an exponential reliability model with the given reliabilities corresponding to $t = 1000$ hours, determine the MTTF of the system.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — System Reliability [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Draw the RBD (as in Section 4.5 above).

**Step 2:** Compute the parallel reliability of A and B.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{AB} &= R_A + R_B - R_A R_B \\
&= 0.90 + 0.85 - (0.90)(0.85) \\
&= 1.75 - 0.765 \\
&= 0.985
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the series reliability with C.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{sys}} &= R_{AB} \times R_C \\
&= 0.985 \times 0.95 \\
&= 0.93575
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **$R_{\text{sys}} \approx 0.9358$ (or 93.58%)**

---

#### Part (b) — MTTF [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Since the system has a constant equivalent failure rate (series of exponentials with a parallel sub-system reducible to exponentials), we evaluate $R_{\text{sys}}(t)$ as a function of time.

**Step 2:** Use the exponential model: $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$.

- From $R_A(1000) = 0.90 \Rightarrow \lambda_A = -\frac{1}{1000}\ln(0.9) = 1.0536 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour.
- From $R_B(1000) = 0.85 \Rightarrow \lambda_B = -\frac{1}{1000}\ln(0.85) = 1.6253 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour.
- From $R_C(1000) = 0.95 \Rightarrow \lambda_C = -\frac{1}{1000}\ln(0.95) = 5.1293 \times 10^{-5}$ per hour.

**Step 3:** System reliability as a function of t:

$$R_{\text{sys}}(t) = \left[2e^{-(\lambda_A + \lambda_B)t} - e^{-2(\lambda_A + \lambda_B)t}\right] \cdot e^{-\lambda_C t}$$

**Step 4:** MTTF = $\int_0^\infty R_{\text{sys}}(t)\, dt$.

After expansion, the integral becomes a sum of simple exponentials:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF} &= \frac{2}{\lambda_A + \lambda_B + \lambda_C} - \frac{1}{2(\lambda_A + \lambda_B) + \lambda_C} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute numerical values.
- $\lambda_A + \lambda_B = 2.6789 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour.
- $\lambda_A + \lambda_B + \lambda_C = 3.1918 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour.
- $2(\lambda_A + \lambda_B) + \lambda_C = 5.8730 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF} &= \frac{2}{3.1918 \times 10^{-4}} - \frac{1}{5.8730 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= 6266.4 - 1702.7 \\
&\approx 4563.7 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **MTTF ≈ 4564 hours**

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

A combined system is configured as follows:
- **Block 1:** Two components in parallel, $R_{1A} = 0.92$, $R_{1B} = 0.88$.
- **Block 2:** Two components in parallel, $R_{2A} = 0.90$, $R_{2B} = 0.85$.
- **Block 1 and Block 2 are in series.**

**Tasks:**
- **(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the RBD and compute the system reliability.
- **(b) [7 Marks]** Assuming exponential failure with the given $R$ values at $t = 500$ hours, find the equivalent failure rate of the system and its MTTF.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — System Reliability [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Reduce Block 1 (parallel).

$$R_{p1} = 0.92 + 0.88 - (0.92)(0.88) = 1.80 - 0.8096 = 0.9904$$

**Step 2:** Reduce Block 2 (parallel).

$$R_{p2} = 0.90 + 0.85 - (0.90)(0.85) = 1.75 - 0.765 = 0.985$$

**Step 3:** System reliability (series of reduced blocks).

$$R_{\text{sys}} = R_{p1} \times R_{p2} = 0.9904 \times 0.985 = 0.9755$$

> ✅ **$R_{\text{sys}} \approx 0.9755$ (or 97.55%)**

---

#### Part (b) — Equivalent Failure Rate and MTTF [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Compute individual failure rates from $R_i(500) = e^{-\lambda_i \cdot 500}$.

- $\lambda_{1A} = -\frac{\ln 0.92}{500} = 1.677 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour.
- $\lambda_{1B} = -\frac{\ln 0.88}{500} = 2.552 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour.
- $\lambda_{2A} = -\frac{\ln 0.90}{500} = 2.107 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour.
- $\lambda_{2B} = -\frac{\ln 0.85}{500} = 3.251 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour.

**Step 2:** For the parallel blocks, write $R_{p}(t)$ and find the equivalent rate approximately by evaluating reliability at t=500 and using the equivalent exponential.

Equivalently, $\ln(R_{\text{sys}}(500)) = \ln(0.9755) = -0.02484$.

$$\lambda_{\text{eq}} = -\frac{\ln(0.9755)}{500} = 4.968 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per hour}$$

**Step 3:** MTTF for an exponential system with this equivalent rate.

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{1}{4.968 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 20129 \text{ hours}$$

> ✅ **$\lambda_{\text{eq}} \approx 4.97 \times 10^{-5}$ per hour; MTTF ≈ 20,129 hours**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> Students frequently **lose marks** in the following ways:
>
> 1. **Forgetting to apply inclusion–exclusion for parallel systems** — they incorrectly write $R_p = R_1 + R_2$ (which exceeds 1 when both are large). Always use $R_p = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$.
> 2. **Mixing up the order of reduction** — if a *series* sub-block is inside a *parallel* structure, the reduction order flips. Re-read the RBD carefully.
> 3. **Confusing MTTF and MTBF** — for non-repairable exponential systems, MTTF = MTBF = $1/\lambda$. The KTU board specifically tests this distinction.
> 4. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — always state failure rate in "per hour" or "failures per 10$^6$ hours (FITS)".
> 5. **Skipping the RBD diagram** — for 7-mark questions, an RBD is **mandatory**. Skipping it costs 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Treating dependent failures as independent** — only assume independence when the problem states it.

---

## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Combined Series-Parallel Systems**

- ✅ A **combined system** is neither purely series nor purely parallel — it is a *nested* mixture.
- ✅ **Reduction Rule:** Always solve the **innermost** sub-block first; reduce parallel blocks using $R_p = 1 - \prod(1-R_i)$.
- ✅ **Series Reliability = Product** of individual reliabilities: $R_s = \prod R_i$.
- ✅ **Parallel Reliability = 1 − Product of unreliabilities**: $R_p = 1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$.
- ✅ Series systems have **equivalent failure rate = sum of individual rates**.
- ✅ Parallel redundancy **drastically increases** both reliability and MTTF (e.g., 2 identical parallel components → MTTF = $1.5/\lambda$).
- ✅ For **exponential reliability**, the time dependence is $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$; MTTF = $\int_0^\infty R(t)\, dt = 1/\lambda$ for a single exponential.
- ✅ For **composite exponential systems**, MTTF requires explicit integration of the *product* of subsystem reliabilities.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 weightage:** 7–14 mark problems on this topic are *guaranteed* in every end-semester paper.
- ✅ Always **draw the RBD** before calculating; this earns easy partial credit.
- ✅ **Assumption of independence** is critical — re-state it in your answer for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: k-out-of-m Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** k-out-of-m Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **k-out-of-m system** (also called a **k-out-of-m:F system** — pronounced "k-out-of-m, Function") is a redundancy configuration consisting of **m identical or non-identical components**, in which the system functions successfully **if and only if at least k components are operating correctly**.

Equivalently, the system fails only when the number of functioning components drops below **k**, i.e., when **more than (m − k) components have failed**.

The complementary structure is the **k-out-of-m:G system** (Gather), in which the system succeeds only when *exactly* k components are working — used in signal-gathering or voting contexts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme emphasises the **k-out-of-m:F (Function)** variant, as it is the most industrially relevant configuration. Series and parallel systems are treated as **degenerate boundary cases** of the general k-out-of-m framework.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Analogy — Commercial Aircraft Engines:**
Imagine a wide-body commercial aircraft equipped with **4 engines**. Federal aviation regulations require that the aircraft remain airborne on **at least 2 engines** during cruise to land safely. This is precisely a **2-out-of-4:F system** — the aircraft functions if **k = 2** or more engines work out of the **m = 4** total.

If one engine fails, the system still operates (3 working ≥ 2). If two fail, it is still functional (2 working = 2). If three fail, the aircraft can no longer maintain controlled flight and the system fails.

| System Type | k value | m value | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure Series | k = m | All must work | Single point of failure chain |
| Pure Parallel | k = 1 | Any one works | Maximum redundancy |
| 2-out-of-3 | k = 2 | m = 3 | Majority voting / TMR |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Cases:**
> - **k = m** → **Series system** (all components must work).
> - **k = 1** → **Parallel system** (at least one component must work).
> - **k > m/2** → **Majority voting logic** (commonly used in fault-tolerant computing).

## 1.3 Physical Metrics & Standard Symbols

The following **standard reliability symbols** are used throughout KTU 2024 Scheme examinations:

- **R** = Reliability of each component (probability of success)
- **Q** = Unreliability of each component = **1 − R**
- **R_s** = System reliability (probability the system performs its intended function)
- **m** = Total number of components in the configuration
- **k** = Minimum number of components that must function for system success
- **λ** = Failure rate (failures per unit time, assuming exponential distribution)
- **t** = Mission time or operating duration

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU board exam, always state the assumption of **statistical independence** of component failures when invoking the binomial or product reliability formulas. Examiners explicitly look for this phrase.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Breakdown

The k-out-of-m system follows a **threshold logic** pattern. Below is the structured decision hierarchy:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Threshold:** Determine the minimum number of surviving components (k) required for system success.
- **Step 2 — Count Success States:** The system succeeds when the number of working components X satisfies **X ≥ k**.
- **Step 3 — Enumerate Outcomes:** For m components, X can take integer values in {0, 1, 2, ..., m}.
- **Step 4 — Apply Probability Distribution:** Each outcome i has an associated probability given by the **Binomial distribution** (for identical, independent components).
- **Step 5 — Sum the Favourable Cases:** Add the probabilities of all cases where **i ≥ k**.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

The binomial model is valid because:

1. **Independence** — Each component either succeeds or fails without influencing others.
2. **Identicality** — Every component has the same success probability R.
3. **Bounded Trials** — There are a fixed number m of trials.

The generalisation to **non-identical components** uses the **multinomial expansion** or direct enumeration over all possible subsets.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises every formula you must memorise for the KTU Module 1 examination on k-out-of-m systems. Master these and you will not lose marks on derivations.

| # | Configuration / Case | Reliability Formula | Boundary / Special Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Identical components (general)** | $R_s = \sum_{i=k}^{m} \binom{m}{i} R^{i} (1-R)^{m-i}$ | Valid for any k, m |
| 2 | **Series system** (k = m) | $R_s = R^m$ | Special case of Formula 1 |
| 3 | **Parallel system** (k = 1) | $R_s = 1 - (1-R)^m$ | Special case of Formula 1 |
| 4 | **2-out-of-3 (TMR voting)** | $R_s = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R)$ | k=2, m=3 |
| 5 | **2-out-of-4** | $R_s = R^4 + 4R^3(1-R) + 6R^2(1-R)^2$ | k=2, m=4 |
| 6 | **3-out-of-4** | $R_s = R^4 + 4R^3(1-R)$ | k=3, m=4 |
| 7 | **Non-identical (general)** | $R_s = \sum_{i=k}^{m} \left[ \sum_{\vert S \vert = i} \prod_{j \in S} R_j \prod_{j \notin S} (1-R_j) \right]$ | Enumerate all subsets of size i |
| 8 | **Expected number of failures** | $E[X_{fail}] = m(1-R)$ | Binomial mean |
| 9 | **Variance of working components** | $Var(X) = mR(1-R)$ | Binomial variance |
| 10 | **Exponential mission reliability** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Used when failure rate λ is given |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Pitfall:** When writing the binomial coefficient, KTU examiners prefer the form $\binom{m}{i}$ over $_mC_i$ or $C(m,i)$. Always use the standardised notation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

The k-out-of-m architecture is ubiquitous in modern engineering:

- **Aerospace:** Multi-engine aircraft (e.g., 2-out-of-4), rocket stage redundancy.
- **Nuclear Power:** Multiple redundant cooling pumps (e.g., 3-out-of-5).
- **Telecommunications:** Multi-path routing in mesh networks (3-out-of-5 link redundancy).
- **Data Centres:** N+1 or 2N redundant server clusters.
- **Medical Devices:** Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) in pacemakers and radiotherapy machines.
- **Automotive:** Brake-by-wire systems with multiple microcontrollers requiring 2-out-of-3 agreement.

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU examination, when a question describes a real-world scenario, immediately map it to a k-out-of-m structure and extract the values of k and m before writing the formula. This shows the examiner you understand the **engineering semantics**, not just the mathematics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the General Reliability Formula (Identical Components)

**Objective:** Derive the closed-form expression for $R_s$ of a k-out-of-m system where all components have identical reliability **R** and fail independently.

### Step 1 — Define the Random Variable

Let **X** denote the number of components that are **functioning** at a random instant. Since each of the m components independently succeeds with probability **R**, the random variable X follows a **Binomial distribution**:

$$X \sim Binomial(m, R)$$

### Step 2 — Write the Binomial Probability Mass Function

For any integer i (where $0 \leq i \leq m$), the probability that exactly **i** components are functioning is:

$$P(X = i) = \binom{m}{i} R^{i} (1-R)^{m-i}$$

**Explanation of terms:**
- $\binom{m}{i}$ — Number of ways to choose i working components out of m total.
- $R^{i}$ — Probability that the chosen i components all succeed.
- $(1-R)^{m-i}$ — Probability that the remaining (m − i) components all fail.

### Step 3 — Apply the k-out-of-m Success Condition

The system functions if and only if the number of working components is **at least k**, i.e., $X \geq k$. Therefore:

$$R_s = P(X \geq k)$$

### Step 4 — Convert to a Summation

Since X is a discrete random variable taking integer values, we convert the cumulative probability into a finite sum:

$$
R_s = P(X \geq k) = \sum_{i=k}^{m} P(X = i)
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the Binomial PMF

Substituting the expression from Step 2 into the summation from Step 4:

$$
R_s = \sum_{i=k}^{m} \binom{m}{i} R^{i} (1-R)^{m-i}
$$

**This is the master formula.** The derivation is now complete.

### Step 6 — Verification via Boundary Cases

**Case A — k = m (Series System):**
Substituting k = m, the sum collapses to a single term with i = m:

$$
R_s = \binom{m}{m} R^{m} (1-R)^{0} = 1 \cdot R^{m} \cdot 1 = R^{m}
$$

This matches the known series system formula. ✓

**Case B — k = 1 (Parallel System):**
Using the binomial complement identity $\sum_{i=0}^{m} \binom{m}{i} R^{i}(1-R)^{m-i} = 1$:

$$
R_s = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \binom{m}{i} R^{i} (1-R)^{m-i} = 1 - \binom{m}{0} R^{0}(1-R)^{m} = 1 - (1-R)^{m}
$$

This matches the known parallel system formula. ✓

## 3.2 Worked Example — 2-out-of-3 System

**Problem:** A 2-out-of-3 system uses three components, each with reliability R = 0.9. Calculate the system reliability.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:** k = 2, m = 3, R = 0.9, hence Q = 1 − R = 0.1.

**Step 2 — Apply the master formula:**

$$
R_s = \sum_{i=2}^{3} \binom{3}{i} (0.9)^{i} (0.1)^{3-i}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the sum into individual terms:**

$$
R_s = \binom{3}{2}(0.9)^{2}(0.1)^{1} + \binom{3}{3}(0.9)^{3}(0.1)^{0}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each term:**

For i = 2:
$$
\binom{3}{2}(0.9)^{2}(0.1)^{1} = 3 \times 0.81 \times 0.1 = 0.243
$$

For i = 3:
$$
\binom{3}{3}(0.9)^{3}(0.1)^{0} = 1 \times 0.729 \times 1 = 0.729
$$

**Step 5 — Sum the terms:**

$$
R_s = 0.243 + 0.729 = 0.972
$$

**Interpretation:** The system has a 97.2% probability of success, which is significantly higher than any single component (90%) but lower than a fully tripled parallel system (99.9%). This illustrates the engineering trade-off between redundancy and reliability.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

The table below shows how system reliability $R_s$ scales with component reliability R for different k-out-of-m configurations. This is a **high-yield table** for KTU numerical problems.

| R | Series (3-out-of-3) | 2-out-of-3 | 1-out-of-3 (Parallel) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.50 | 0.125 | 0.500 | 0.875 |
| 0.70 | 0.343 | 0.784 | 0.973 |
| 0.90 | 0.729 | 0.972 | 0.999 |
| 0.95 | 0.857 | 0.993 | 0.9999 |
| 0.99 | 0.970 | 0.9997 | ≈ 1.000 |

> [!NOTE]
> Observe that the **2-out-of-3 system** already provides reliability exceeding 0.99 at R = 0.95, while the parallel (1-out-of-3) saturates near 1.0. This is why TMR is the optimal balance between cost and reliability for fault-tolerant computing.

## 3.4 Worked Example — 2-out-of-4 with Exponential Failure

**Problem:** A 2-out-of-4 system operates for t = 1000 hours. Each component has failure rate λ = 0.001 per hour. Find the system reliability.

**Step 1 — Compute component reliability using exponential distribution:**

$$
R = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.001)(1000)} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 2-out-of-4 formula (Row 5 of cheat sheet):**

$$
R_s = R^4 + 4R^3(1-R) + 6R^2(1-R)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term numerically:**

$R^4 = (0.3679)^4 \approx 0.01832$

$4R^3(1-R) = 4(0.3679)^3(0.6321) \approx 4(0.04979)(0.6321) \approx 0.12590$

$6R^2(1-R)^2 = 6(0.3679)^2(0.6321)^2 \approx 6(0.1353)(0.3996) \approx 0.32450$

**Step 4 — Sum the contributions:**

$$
R_s \approx 0.01832 + 0.12590 + 0.32450 = 0.46872
$$

**Interpretation:** Despite having 4 redundant components, the system reliability drops to ~46.9% after 1000 hours because the individual component reliability itself is low (only 36.8%). This teaches the lesson that **system reliability is bounded above by the weakest component**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a k-out-of-m System

The diagram below shows the **logical flow** of decision-making in a 2-out-of-4 system. Each component independently feeds into a central voting/threshold logic unit, which decides system success based on the count of "UP" signals.

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Component 1] --> nodeV[Threshold Logic Unit]
    node2[Component 2] --> nodeV
    node3[Component 3] --> nodeV
    node4[Component 4] --> nodeV
    nodeV --> nodeCount{Count Working Components}
    nodeCount -- Count greater than or equal to k --> nodeSuccess[System SUCCESS]
    nodeCount -- Count less than k --> nodeFail[System FAILURE]
    nodeV --> nodeK[Parameter k equals 2]
    nodeV --> nodeM[Parameter m equals 4]
```

## 4.2 Reliability Block Diagram Comparison

The mermaid diagram below contrasts a **series**, **parallel**, and **2-out-of-3** configuration at the block-diagram level, illustrating the logical flow of redundancy.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Input] --> A2[Comp 1]
    A2 --> A3[Comp 2]
    A3 --> A4[Comp 3]
    A4 --> A5[Output]
    B1[Input] --> B2[Comp 1]
    B1 --> B3[Comp 2]
    B1 --> B4[Comp 3]
    B2 --> B5[Output]
    B3 --> B5
    B4 --> B5
    C1[Input] --> C2[Comp 1]
    C1 --> C3[Comp 2]
    C1 --> C4[Comp 3]
    C2 --> CV[Voting Logic 2 out of 3]
    C3 --> CV
    C4 --> CV
    CV --> C5[Output]
```

## 4.3 Success State Enumeration Diagram

The state-machine diagram below enumerates the possible states of a 3-component k-out-of-m system, from "all working" (state 3) to "all failed" (state 0).

```mermaid
graph TD
    st3[State 3: All 3 Working] --> st2[State 2: 2 Working, 1 Failed]
    st2 --> st1[State 1: 1 Working, 2 Failed]
    st1 --> st0[State 0: All 3 Failed]
    st3 -. System works if k is less than or equal to 3 .-> st3
    st2 -. System works if k is less than or equal to 2 .-> st2
    st1 -. System works if k equals 1 .-> st1
    st0 -. System always fails here .-> st0
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed arrows annotate the **boundary condition** for system success at each state. For a 2-out-of-3 system, states 2 and 3 yield success; for a 1-out-of-3 parallel system, states 1, 2, and 3 all yield success; for a series (3-out-of-3) system, only state 3 yields success.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define a k-out-of-m system. With suitable examples, explain how series and parallel systems can be treated as special cases of a k-out-of-m system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A k-out-of-m system is a redundant configuration of m components in which the system performs its intended function if and only if at least k components are functioning.

- **Series system** is obtained when **k = m**, meaning all m components must function. Failure of even one component causes system failure. Example: a chain of Christmas lights where every bulb must work.

- **Parallel system** is obtained when **k = 1**, meaning the system succeeds as long as at least one component works. Example: two flashlights wired in parallel for a single bulb — either one can illuminate it.

(Scoring: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for series case, 1 mark for parallel case.)

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: State the reliability formula for a k-out-of-m system with identical and independent components. Mention the conditions under which the formula is valid.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The system reliability of a k-out-of-m configuration with identical, independent components is:

$$
R_s = \sum_{i=k}^{m} \binom{m}{i} R^{i} (1-R)^{m-i}
$$

**Conditions of validity:**
1. All m components have the same reliability R.
2. Component failures are statistically **independent**.
3. The system succeeds when at least k components function.

(Scoring: 2 marks for the formula, 1 mark for conditions.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**A (a)** Define a k-out-of-m system. Derive the general expression for the reliability of a k-out-of-m system with identical components. Show that series and parallel systems are special cases. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
A k-out-of-m system contains m components and functions successfully if and only if at least k of them are working. The system fails when fewer than k components operate, i.e., when more than (m − k) components have failed.

**Step 2 — Derivation (3 marks):**
Let X be the number of working components. X follows a Binomial distribution with parameters m and R:

$$P(X = i) = \binom{m}{i} R^{i} (1-R)^{m-i}$$

The system succeeds when X ≥ k:

$$
R_s = P(X \geq k) = \sum_{i=k}^{m} \binom{m}{i} R^{i} (1-R)^{m-i}
$$

**Step 3 — Special cases (2 marks):**

For k = m (series):

$$
R_s = \binom{m}{m} R^{m} (1-R)^{0} = R^{m}
$$

For k = 1 (parallel), using the binomial theorem:

$$
R_s = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \binom{m}{i} R^{i} (1-R)^{m-i} = 1 - (1-R)^{m}
$$

---

**A (b)** A 2-out-of-4 system uses identical components, each with reliability R = 0.95. The system must operate for t = 200 hours, and each component has a constant failure rate λ = 0.0005 per hour. Calculate the system reliability. Compare it with a pure parallel system of 4 components under the same conditions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute component reliability at time t:**

$$
R = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.0005)(200)} = e^{-0.1} \approx 0.9048
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 2-out-of-4 formula:**

$$
R_s^{(2/4)} = R^{4} + 4R^{3}(1-R) + 6R^{2}(1-R)^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation:**

$R^4 = (0.9048)^4 \approx 0.6703$

$4R^3(1-R) = 4(0.9048)^3(0.0952) \approx 4(0.7408)(0.0952) \approx 0.2822$

$6R^2(1-R)^2 = 6(0.9048)^2(0.0952)^2 \approx 6(0.8187)(0.00906) \approx 0.0445$

$$
R_s^{(2/4)} = 0.6703 + 0.2822 + 0.0445 = 0.9970
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with 1-out-of-4 (parallel) system:**

$$
R_s^{(1/4)} = 1 - (1-R)^4 = 1 - (0.0952)^4 = 1 - 0.0000822 \approx 0.99992
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
The parallel (1-out-of-4) system achieves 99.992% reliability, slightly higher than the 2-out-of-4 system at 99.70%. However, the 2-out-of-4 system offers better load-sharing and is preferred in aerospace applications where simultaneous full-power operation of all components is undesirable.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**B (a)** Explain the concept of a k-out-of-m:G system and contrast it with the k-out-of-m:F system. Give one real-world example of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — k-out-of-m:F (Function) — 3 marks:**
The k-out-of-m:F system succeeds if **at least k** components are functioning. The system remains operational as long as the count of working components stays at or above the threshold k. Most engineering redundancy systems fall under this category.

Example: A 2-out-of-4 aircraft engine system — the aircraft flies as long as 2 or more engines work.

**Step 2 — k-out-of-m:G (Gather) — 3 marks:**
The k-out-of-m:G system succeeds if and only if **exactly k** components are functioning. This is a much stricter condition used in signal-gathering or voting circuits where a precise number of inputs is required.

Example: A fire alarm that requires **exactly 3 out of 5** sensors to trigger before activation (to avoid false positives from 2 sensors and ignore the case where all 5 trigger due to a sensor malfunction).

**Step 3 — Key Difference — 1 mark:**
The :F variant is **monotonic** (reliability decreases monotonically as components fail). The :G variant is **non-monotonic** — the system may succeed with fewer working components in some configurations than with more, which complicates reliability analysis.

---

**B (b)** Three pumps are installed in a water supply station such that at least 2 pumps must operate for adequate water pressure. If the reliabilities of the three pumps are R₁ = 0.90, R₂ = 0.85, and R₃ = 0.80, calculate the system reliability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

This is a 2-out-of-3 system with non-identical components. The system succeeds if:
- All 3 pumps work, OR
- Any 2 specific pumps work (while the third fails).

**Step 1 — Enumerate success cases (5 marks):**

Let Aᵢ denote the event that pump i works. The system succeeds if A₁A₂A₃ occurs (all three work) OR (any 2 of the 3 work):

$$
R_s = P(A_1 A_2 A_3) + P(A_1 A_2 \bar{A_3}) + P(A_1 \bar{A_2} A_3) + P(\bar{A_1} A_2 A_3)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term using independence:**

$P(A_1 A_2 A_3) = 0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.80 = 0.6120$

$P(A_1 A_2 \bar{A_3}) = 0.90 \times 0.85 \times (1-0.80) = 0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.20 = 0.1530$

$P(A_1 \bar{A_2} A_3) = 0.90 \times (1-0.85) \times 0.80 = 0.90 \times 0.15 \times 0.80 = 0.1080$

$P(\bar{A_1} A_2 A_3) = (1-0.90) \times 0.85 \times 0.80 = 0.10 \times 0.85 \times 0.80 = 0.0680$

**Step 3 — Sum the probabilities (2 marks):**

$$
R_s = 0.6120 + 0.1530 + 0.1080 + 0.0680 = 0.9410
$$

**Conclusion:** The water supply station has a 94.10% probability of maintaining adequate pressure over the mission duration. The dominant contribution comes from the all-three-working case (61.2%), reflecting that all pumps are reasonably reliable individually.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the independence assumption:** When you write the binomial reliability formula, KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark if you do not explicitly mention that the components fail independently. Always include a phrase like "assuming statistically independent component failures."
>
> 2. **Confusing :F and :G notation:** Some students write "k-out-of-m system" without specifying whether it is :F (function) or :G (gather). Always clarify in your first sentence.
>
> 3. **Sign error in complement:** When computing the parallel case (k = 1), students sometimes forget that the complement is $(1-R)^m$, not $R^m$. Remember: parallel system fails only when ALL components fail.
>
> 4. **Unit confusion with exponential reliability:** When given failure rate λ in failures/hour and time t in hours, students occasionally write $R = e^{\lambda t}$ instead of $e^{-\lambda t}$. The negative sign is critical.
>
> 5. **Skipping subset enumeration:** For non-identical components, students often blindly apply the identical-component formula. This is a **logical error** worth 2–3 marks. Always enumerate subsets for non-identical cases.
>
> 6. **Not verifying boundary cases:** Examiners award bonus marks when students show that the derived formula reduces to $R^m$ for series and $1-(1-R)^m$ for parallel. Skipping this verification forfeits 1–2 marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

Use this as your final revision checklist before the KTU Module 1 examination:

- [x] A **k-out-of-m system** has m components and functions when **at least k** of them work.
- [x] The system fails only when more than **(m − k)** components fail.
- [x] Master formula for identical independent components: $R_s = \sum_{i=k}^{m} \binom{m}{i} R^{i} (1-R)^{m-i}$
- [x] **Series** system corresponds to **k = m** → $R_s = R^m$
- [x] **Parallel** system corresponds to **k = 1** → $R_s = 1 - (1-R)^m$
- [x] **2-out-of-3 (TMR)** is the most common fault-tolerant architecture in digital electronics.
- [x] For **non-identical** components, enumerate subsets manually using $\prod R_j \prod (1-R_j)$.
- [x] The k-out-of-m:F system is **monotonic** (more working components = higher reliability).
- [x] The k-out-of-m:G system is **non-monotonic** and requires exactly k working components.
- [x] Always state the **independence assumption** when writing the binomial formula.
- [x] Use **exponential distribution** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ when given a constant failure rate and mission time.
- [x] Real-world examples: aircraft engines, nuclear cooling pumps, server clusters, TMR circuits.
- [x] Verify your formula by checking it collapses correctly at k = 1 and k = m.
- [x] KTU frequently tests the boundary cases (series, parallel, TMR) as short numerical problems worth 3–5 marks each.
- [x] Practice at least 5 numerical problems involving different (k, m) combinations before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standby Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Standby Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standby System (Passive / Cold Redundant System):** A redundant configuration in which one or more identical components (called *standby* or *reserve* units) remain *inactive* and unpowered until the *active* (online) component fails. Upon failure of the active unit, a **switching device** detects the failure and immediately brings a standby unit online.

In Reliability Engineering terminology, a Standby System is classified under **Hardware Redundancy Techniques**, specifically as **Passive Redundancy**, where the redundant element does not share the load during normal operation. The system is operational as long as **at least one** working component is connected to the load.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Mapping (PEMET747 – Module 1):**
> This topic directly maps to **CO1** – *Apply reliability concepts to engineering systems using probability theory and statistical distributions.* Subtopics covered include cold standby, hot standby, imperfect switching, and MTTF computations for redundant configurations.

## 1.2 Classification of Standby Systems

A standby system is fundamentally classified by the **operating state of the reserve component** and the **reliability of the switching mechanism**:

- **Cold Standby** — The reserve unit is unpowered; its failure rate in standby is approximately **zero** ($\lambda_s \approx 0$).
- **Warm Standby** — The reserve unit is partially powered; it has a reduced but non-zero failure rate ($\lambda_s < \lambda_o$).
- **Hot Standby** — The reserve unit operates in parallel; its failure rate is identical to the online unit ($\lambda_s = \lambda_o$). Mathematically equivalent to an **Active Parallel System**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The Backup Generator:**
> Imagine a hospital that depends on city electricity. A **diesel generator** sits idle in the basement. The grid (active unit) supplies power. The instant the grid fails, an **Automatic Transfer Switch (ATS)** detects the outage and starts the generator within milliseconds. The generator was *not running* before, so it suffered no wear and tear — this is **cold standby**. Now imagine the generator running but disconnected from the load — that is **hot standby** (wearing out, but no load stress). The ATS represents the **switching device**, and its reliability determines whether the transition succeeds.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Bolded Constants & Metrics)

- **Active Component (Online Unit):** The unit currently delivering the system function. Failure rate $\lambda$.
- **Standby Component (Reserve Unit):** Inactive unit waiting for activation. Failure rate in standby $\lambda_s$.
- **Switching Device:** Mechanism that transfers the load from failed active unit to a standby unit. Failure rate $\lambda_{sw}$.
- **Switching Reliability ($R_{sw}$):** Probability that the switch successfully operates on demand.
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF):** Expected operating time before first system failure.
- **System Reliability $R_s(t)$:** Probability that the system performs its intended function over $[0, t]$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Working Principle

A generic standby system with **one active unit** and **$n-1$ standby units** operates as follows:

1. **Initial State ($t=0$):** The active unit begins operation; all standby units are held in reserve.
2. **Normal Operation:** The active unit delivers the full system load. Standby units experience either *zero stress* (cold) or *reduced stress* (warm/hot).
3. **First Failure Event:** The active unit fails at some random time $T_1 \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$.
4. **Switching Action:** The switching device detects failure and attempts to connect the first standby unit.
5. **If Switch Succeeds:** The standby unit (now active) takes over the load with failure rate $\lambda$ (cold standby unit must first "wake up" — its initial failure rate may be $\lambda$ after activation).
6. **If Switch Fails:** The entire system fails immediately.
7. **Cascade:** The process repeats for each subsequent failure until either all units are exhausted or the switch fails.
8. **System Failure:** Occurs when *all* components have failed **OR** the switching device fails.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight — Why Cold Standby Beats Parallel (Hot) Redundancy:**
> Because cold reserve units do not age while idle, they provide *fresh* replacement capacity. A cold-standby system therefore achieves *higher reliability* than a parallel system of identical components over the same mission time. This is why mission-critical equipment (e.g., spacecraft, military radar) uses cold standby.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** — they appear in nearly every KTU end-semester question on this topic.

| **Configuration** | **Reliability Expression $R_s(t)$** | **MTTF** | **Boundary Conditions** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single component | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $n=1$, no redundancy |
| Cold standby (1 active + 1 reserve) | $e^{-\lambda t}(1+\lambda t)$ | $\dfrac{3}{2\lambda}$ | $R_{sw}=1$, $\lambda_s=0$ |
| Cold standby (1 active + 2 reserve) | $e^{-\lambda t}\left(1+\lambda t+\dfrac{(\lambda t)^2}{2!}\right)$ | $\dfrac{11}{6\lambda}$ | $R_{sw}=1$, $\lambda_s=0$ |
| Cold standby (1 active + $n-1$ reserve) | $e^{-\lambda t}\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\dfrac{(\lambda t)^k}{k!}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{k}$ | $R_{sw}=1$, $\lambda_s=0$ |
| Hot standby (1 active + 1 reserve) | $2e^{-\lambda t}-e^{-2\lambda t}$ | $\dfrac{3}{2\lambda}$ | Identical to parallel system |
| Standby with imperfect switch | $R_{sw}\cdot e^{-\lambda t}(1+\lambda t)$ | $\dfrac{R_{sw}(3-R_{sw})}{2\lambda}$ | $R_{sw}<1$, $\lambda_s=0$ |
| Cold standby (1 active + $n-1$, identical $\lambda_s$) | $e^{-\lambda t}\cdot e^{-\lambda_s t}\cdot\left[\sum \ldots\right]$ | Complex integral | Warm standby case |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** In every formula above, $\lambda$ is the **failure rate of the online unit** in **$\text{failures/hour}$**. Time $t$ is in **hours**. MTTF is also in **hours**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Application** |
|---|---|
| Aerospace | Redundant flight computers on Boeing 777 (Triple Modular Redundancy + standby modes) |
| Nuclear Power | Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) — multiple pumps in cold standby |
| Telecommunications | Backup fiber links; redundant power supplies in base stations |
| Healthcare | ICU backup ventilators; hospital standby generators |
| Data Centers | N+1 UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supply) configuration with cold reserve |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Engineering Trade-off:** Cold standby saves *wear* but adds *switching complexity* and *detection latency*. In systems where switch reliability is poor (e.g., mechanical relays in harsh environments), hot standby may actually outperform cold standby.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reliability of a 1-Active + 1-Cold-Standby System

**System Description:** Component A is online (active) with failure rate $\lambda$. Component B is cold standby ($\lambda_s = 0$). Perfect switch ($R_{sw} = 1$).

**System survives time $t$ if:**
- **Case 1:** Component A does not fail during $[0, t]$.
- **Case 2:** Component A fails at some time $x < t$, the switch successfully connects B, and B then survives the remaining interval $(x, t]$.

By the **law of total probability**, system reliability is:

$$R_s(t) = P(\text{A survives}) + P(\text{A fails at } x, \text{ B survives } (t-x))$$

$$R_s(t) = \underbrace{e^{-\lambda t}}_{\text{Case 1}} + \underbrace{\int_{0}^{t} f_A(x)\cdot R_{sw}\cdot R_B(t-x)\,dx}_{\text{Case 2}}$$

**Substituting the exponential PDF of A:** $f_A(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ and $R_B(t-x) = e^{-\lambda(t-x)}$:

$$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \int_{0}^{t} \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\cdot (1)\cdot e^{-\lambda(t-x)}\,dx$$

$$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\int_{0}^{t} (1)\,dx$$

$$\int_{0}^{t} 1\,dx = t$$

$$\therefore \quad R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \lambda t\, e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\boxed{R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\,(1 + \lambda t)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The factor $(1+\lambda t)$ is the **reliability enhancement factor**. As $t \to 0$, $R_s \to 1$ (correct). As $t \to \infty$, $R_s \to 0$ (correct, since standby units are finite).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MTTF of a 1-Active + 1-Cold-Standby System

The MTTF is the expected time until system failure, given perfect switching:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_s(t)\,dt$$

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}(1+\lambda t)\,dt$$

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt + \lambda\int_{0}^{\infty} t\,e^{-\lambda t}\,dt$$

We use the standard integrals:
$$\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \frac{1}{\lambda} \qquad \text{and} \qquad \int_{0}^{\infty} t\,e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \lambda\cdot\frac{1}{\lambda^{2}} = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{2}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_{\text{cold-1+1}} = \frac{2}{\lambda}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction Notice:** A common student error is writing $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{3}{2\lambda}$. The correct value for **cold standby (1 active + 1 reserve) with perfect switch** is $\dfrac{2}{\lambda}$. The value $\dfrac{3}{2\lambda}$ applies to **hot standby** (i.e., a parallel system with 2 identical components). See Derivation 3 below for confirmation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MTTF of Hot Standby (Parallel System) for Verification

For a parallel system of 2 identical components, both online with failure rate $\lambda$:

$$R_p(t) = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$$

$$\text{MTTF}_p = \int_{0}^{\infty}\bigl(2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}\bigr)\,dt$$

$$\text{MTTF}_p = 2\cdot\frac{1}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{2\lambda} = \frac{4-1}{2\lambda} = \frac{3}{2\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{3}{2\lambda}} \quad \text{(Hot Standby)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Insight:** Cold standby MTTF $\left(\dfrac{2}{\lambda}\right)$ **exceeds** hot standby MTTF $\left(\dfrac{3}{2\lambda}\right)$ by a factor of $\dfrac{4}{3} \approx 1.333$. This **33% improvement** quantifies the engineering advantage of cold redundancy.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cold Standby with $n$ Identical Components (General Case)

**System:** 1 active + $(n-1)$ cold standby units, all with active failure rate $\lambda$, perfect switch.

**Logic:** The system survives to time $t$ if at most $(n-1)$ components have failed. Equivalent to saying that the number of failures $N(t)$ observed by time $t$ is $\leq n-1$.

Since failures occur as a Poisson process with rate $\lambda$:

$$P[N(t) = k] = \frac{(\lambda t)^{k}\, e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

$$\therefore \quad R_s(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \frac{(\lambda t)^{k}\, e^{-\lambda t}}{k!} = e^{-\lambda t}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{(\lambda t)^{k}}{k!}$$

$$\boxed{R_s^{(n)}(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{(\lambda t)^{k}}{k!}}$$

**MTTF for the $n$-component cold-standby system:**

$$\text{MTTF}_n = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_s^{(n)}(t)\,dt$$

$$\text{MTTF}_n = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{(\lambda t)^{k}}{k!}\,dt = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{1}{k!}\int_{0}^{\infty}(\lambda t)^{k}e^{-\lambda t}\,dt$$

Using the substitution $u = \lambda t$, $du = \lambda\,dt$:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty}(\lambda t)^{k}e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}\int_{0}^{\infty}u^{k}e^{-u}\,du = \frac{\Gamma(k+1)}{\lambda} = \frac{k!}{\lambda}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{MTTF}_n = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{1}{k!}\cdot\frac{k!}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(1) = \frac{n}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_n^{\text{cold}} = \frac{n}{\lambda}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Sanity Checks:** For $n=2$: $\dfrac{2}{\lambda}$ ✓ matches Derivation 2. For $n=1$: $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ ✓ matches single-component MTTF.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Cold Standby with Imperfect Switching

**System:** 1 active + 1 cold standby, switching reliability $R_{sw} < 1$.

The system survives if *either* A never fails *or* A fails AND switch works AND B survives:

$$R_s(t) = R_A(t) + R_{sw}\cdot\int_{0}^{t} f_A(x)\,R_B(t-x)\,dx$$

$$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + R_{sw}\cdot \lambda t\,e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\boxed{R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\bigl(1 + R_{sw}\,\lambda t\bigr)}$$

**MTTF with imperfect switch:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty}e^{-\lambda t}(1+R_{sw}\lambda t)\,dt = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{R_{sw}}{\lambda} = \frac{1+R_{sw}}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_{\text{imperfect}} = \frac{1+R_{sw}}{\lambda}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write $\dfrac{R_{sw}(3-R_{sw})}{2\lambda}$ as the MTTF for imperfect switching — this formula is for a **parallel system with imperfect switch**, not a cold standby system.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Metric** | **Single Unit** | **Parallel (Hot)** | **Cold Standby (n=2)** | **Cold Standby (n=3)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $R_s(t)$ | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | $2e^{-\lambda t}-e^{-2\lambda t}$ | $e^{-\lambda t}(1+\lambda t)$ | $e^{-\lambda t}(1+\lambda t+\frac{(\lambda t)^2}{2})$ |
| $R_s$ at $\lambda t=1$ | $0.368$ | $0.600$ | $0.736$ | $0.920$ |
| $R_s$ at $\lambda t=2$ | $0.135$ | $0.252$ | $0.406$ | $0.677$ |
| MTTF | $1/\lambda$ | $3/(2\lambda) = 1.5/\lambda$ | $2/\lambda$ | $3/\lambda$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite Numerical:** A standard KTU question asks: *"Compare the reliability of a parallel system vs. a cold standby system at $t = 1/\lambda$. Which is better?"* — Answer: Cold standby gives $\approx 0.736$ vs. parallel $0.600$. Cold standby wins.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cold Standby System

The following diagram illustrates the functional flow of a **1-Active + 1-Cold-Standby** system with imperfect switching.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Power Supply] --> A1[Active Unit A]
    A1 --> A2{Online Operational}
    A2 -- "A is healthy" --> A3[Output to Load]
    A2 -- "Failure Detected" --> A4[Switching Device]
    A4 -- "Switch Fails - Rs less than 1" --> A5[System Failure]
    A4 -- "Switch Succeeds - Rs equals 1" --> A6[Activate Standby Unit B]
    A6 --> A2
    S0[Cold Standby Unit B - lambda s equals 0] -.-> A4
    F1[Failure Rate lambda - Active Mode] --- A1
    F2[Failure Rate lambda sw - Switch] --- A4
    style A0 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
    style A3 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style A5 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style A6 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style S0 fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- Solid arrows represent the **normal operational flow** (active power path).
- Dotted arrows represent the **reserve path** — the standby unit is dormant until activation.
- Color coding: **Blue** = input, **Green** = success, **Red** = system failure, **Yellow** = switch event, **Gray** = dormant reserve.

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram — Cold Standby (1 Active + 1 Reserve, Perfect Switch)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateS2
    stateS2 : State 2 - Both Units Working - A online - B cold standby
    stateS1 : State 1 - Only A Working - A online - B not yet needed
    stateS0 : State 0 - System Failed - Both units failed or switch failed
    stateS2 --> stateS1 : Unit A fails - lambda - Perfect Switch engages
    stateS1 --> stateS0 : Unit B fails - lambda
    stateS2 --> stateS0 : Unit A fails AND Switch fails - lambda times 1 minus Rs
    note right of stateS2 : Initial state - Highest reliability
    note right of stateS1 : Single working unit - Reduced reliability
    note right of stateS0 : Absorbing state - No recovery possible
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- **State 2:** Both units intact — system fully operational. Initial state at $t=0$.
- **State 1:** A has failed, B is now active. Single point of failure remains.
- **State 0:** Absorbing (failed) state. Reached if B fails **or** switch fails during transition from State 2.
- The transition rate $\lambda$ from State 1 → State 0 confirms that the activated standby unit *now* has the active failure rate.

## 4.3 Reliability Comparison — Parallel vs. Cold Standby vs. Series

```mermaid
graph LR
    P0[Parallel System] --> P1[Both components online]
    P1 --> P2[Failure if BOTH fail]
    P2 --> P3[Rs equals 2e to the power of negative lambda t minus e to the power of negative 2 lambda t]
    C0[Cold Standby System] --> C1[One online - one dormant]
    C1 --> C2[Failure if BOTH fail - including switch]
    C2 --> C3[Rs equals e to the power of negative lambda t times 1 plus lambda t]
    S0[Series System] --> S1[All components in chain]
    S1 --> S2[Failure if ANY fails]
    S2 --> S3[Rs equals product of individual R values]
    P3 --> CMP{Reliability Ranking at lambda t greater than 0}
    C3 --> CMP
    S3 --> CMP
    CMP --> RES1[Cold Standby - Highest Rs]
    CMP --> RES2[Parallel - Moderate Rs]
    CMP --> RES3[Series - Lowest Rs]
    style RES1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style RES2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style RES3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q:** Differentiate between **active redundancy** and **passive (standby) redundancy** with one example each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | **Aspect** | **Active Redundancy** | **Passive (Standby) Redundancy** |
> |---|---|---|
> | Load Sharing | All units share load during normal operation | Only active unit carries load |
> | Failure Detection | Not required — others automatically compensate | Required — switching device must detect failure |
> | Example | Two pumps running in parallel, both feeding a pipe | One pump running, second pump idle but ready |
> | Formula | $R_p = 1-(1-R)^n$ | $R_s = e^{-\lambda t}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{(\lambda t)^k}{k!}$ |
>
> *(1 mark per correct comparison row; 3 marks full.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q:** Define **cold standby** and **hot standby**. State one key formula for each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Cold Standby:** The reserve unit is unpowered; its in-standby failure rate is approximately **zero**. Formula: $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1+\lambda t)$ for one active + one reserve with perfect switch. **(1.5 marks)**
> - **Hot Standby:** The reserve unit operates at full rated conditions; its failure rate equals the online unit. Formula: $R_s(t) = 2e^{-\lambda t}-e^{-2\lambda t}$ (identical to parallel system). **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]** *(Module 1 — Standby Systems)*
>
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the reliability of a system consisting of **two identical components**, where one is active and the other is in cold standby with a **perfect switching device**. Also compute the **MTTF**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A control system has an active unit with failure rate $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour and one cold standby unit. The mission time is $t = 500$ hours. The switching device has reliability $R_{sw} = 0.95$.
> **(i)** Compute the system reliability $R_s(500)$.
> **(ii)** Compute the MTTF of the system.
> **(iii)** Compare with a purely parallel (hot standby) system at the same mission time. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Define system events.** The system survives time $t$ if either:
- Active unit A survives the entire interval $[0, t]$ → Probability = $e^{-\lambda t}$. **(1 mark)**
- A fails at time $x < t$, switch engages perfectly, and B survives the remaining $(t-x)$ → Probability obtained via integration. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2: Write reliability integral.**

$$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \int_{0}^{t} \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\cdot (1)\cdot e^{-\lambda(t-x)}\,dx \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the integral.**

$$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\int_{0}^{t} dx = e^{-\lambda t} + \lambda t\,e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\boxed{R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1+\lambda t)} \quad \textbf{(1 mark for boxed result)}$$

**Step 4: Compute MTTF.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}(1+\lambda t)\,dt = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{2}{\lambda} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.001$ /hour, $t = 500$ hours, $R_{sw} = 0.95$.

**(i) System Reliability with Imperfect Switch:**

$$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\bigl(1 + R_{sw}\,\lambda t\bigr)$$

Compute $\lambda t = 0.001 \times 500 = 0.5$. **(0.5 mark)**

$$e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

$$R_s = 0.6065 \times (1 + 0.95 \times 0.5) = 0.6065 \times (1 + 0.475)$$

$$R_s = 0.6065 \times 1.475 = 0.8946 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) MTTF with Imperfect Switch:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1+R_{sw}}{\lambda} = \frac{1+0.95}{0.001} = \frac{1.95}{0.001}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = 1950 \text{ hours}} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(iii) Comparison with Hot Standby (Parallel):**

$$R_{hot}(500) = 2e^{-0.5} - e^{-1.0} = 2(0.6065) - 0.3679$$

$$R_{hot}(500) = 1.2130 - 0.3679 = 0.8451 \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Conclusion:** Cold standby with imperfect switch ($R_s = 0.8946$) still **outperforms** hot standby ($R_{hot} = 0.8451$) by approximately **5.85%** at this mission time, despite the imperfect switch. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model]** *(Module 1 — Standby Systems)*
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working of a cold standby redundant system** with a perfect switching device. Derive the expression for its **reliability function and MTTF**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A satellite communication system uses three identical transceivers: **one active** and **two in cold standby**, all with failure rate $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures/hour. The switching device is perfect.
> **(i)** Derive the reliability expression for this 1+2 cold standby system.
> **(ii)** Compute $R_s(1000)$ and MTTF.
> **(iii)** What is the reliability improvement over a single transceiver at the same mission time? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram Explanation (3 marks):**

The system consists of: (1) Active unit (online with failure rate $\lambda$), (2) Cold standby unit (unpowered, $\lambda_s \approx 0$), (3) Perfect switch (transitions load from failed active to standby unit upon failure detection), (4) Load.

**Working:** Active unit delivers the load during normal operation. Upon failure, the perfect switch instantaneously transfers load to the cold standby unit, which then operates at the active failure rate $\lambda$.

**Reliability Derivation (2 marks):**

$$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \int_{0}^{t}\lambda e^{-\lambda x}\,e^{-\lambda(t-x)}\,dx = e^{-\lambda t}(1+\lambda t)$$

**MTTF Derivation (2 marks):**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}(1+\lambda t)\,dt = \frac{2}{\lambda}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 marks]

**(i) Reliability Expression (3 marks):**

Using the general formula for 1 active + $(n-1)$ cold standby with $n = 3$:

$$\boxed{R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\left(1 + \lambda t + \frac{(\lambda t)^{2}}{2!}\right)} = e^{-\lambda t}\left(1 + \lambda t + \frac{(\lambda t)^{2}}{2}\right)$$

**(ii) Compute $R_s(1000)$ and MTTF (2.5 marks):**

$\lambda t = 0.0005 \times 1000 = 0.5$.

$$e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

$$R_s = 0.6065 \times \left(1 + 0.5 + \frac{0.25}{2}\right) = 0.6065 \times (1 + 0.5 + 0.125)$$

$$R_s = 0.6065 \times 1.625 = 0.9856$$

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{n}{\lambda} = \frac{3}{0.0005} = 6000 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Reliability Improvement over Single Unit (1.5 marks):**

$$R_{single}(1000) = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

$$\text{Improvement} = \frac{0.9856 - 0.6065}{0.6065} \times 100\% = 62.51\%$$

The 1+2 cold standby system provides a **62.5% reliability gain** over a single transceiver at $t = 1000$ hours.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the $R_{sw}$ factor** when the problem mentions a non-perfect switch — students routinely write $e^{-\lambda t}(1+\lambda t)$ instead of $e^{-\lambda t}(1+R_{sw}\lambda t)$. **−2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing cold standby MTTF $\left(\dfrac{2}{\lambda}\right)$ with hot standby MTTF $\left(\dfrac{3}{2\lambda}\right)$** — examiners immediately deduct marks. Always state the standby type explicitly in your answer.
> 3. **Wrong integration limits:** The integral for cold standby runs from $0$ to $t$, not $0$ to $\infty$. The MTTF integral runs from $0$ to $\infty$. Mixing these up is a frequent error. **−1 mark.**
> 4. **Skipping the Poisson interpretation** for the general $n$-component formula — KTU rewards students who justify $P[N(t)=k]$ via the Poisson distribution.
> 5. **Unit mismatch:** Always quote MTTF in **hours** (or whatever unit $\lambda$ is given in). Writing just $\dfrac{2}{\lambda}$ without units costs **−0.5 mark**.

---

## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Standby Systems**

- ☐ **Definition:** Standby redundancy keeps reserve units *inactive*; load is transferred via a switch upon failure of the active unit.
- ☐ **Three Types:** Cold ($\lambda_s = 0$), Warm ($\lambda_s < \lambda$), Hot ($\lambda_s = \lambda$).
- ☐ **Master Formula:** $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\dfrac{(\lambda t)^k}{k!}$ — applies to **cold standby** with 1 active + $(n-1)$ reserves, **perfect switch**.
- ☐ **MTTF General:** $\text{MTTF}_n = \dfrac{n}{\lambda}$ for cold standby with $n$ components and perfect switch.
- ☐ **Special Case MTTFs:** $n=1 \Rightarrow 1/\lambda$; $n=2 \Rightarrow 2/\lambda$; $n=3 \Rightarrow 3/\lambda$.
- ☐ **Hot Standby = Parallel:** $R_s = 2e^{-\lambda t}-e^{-2\lambda t}$, MTTF $= 3/(2\lambda)$.
- ☐ **Imperfect Switch Formula:** $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1+R_{sw}\lambda t)$; MTTF $= (1+R_{sw})/\lambda$.
- ☐ **Reliability Ranking (for $t > 0$):** Cold Standby > Hot Standby (Parallel) > Single Unit > Series.
- ☐ **Engineering Insight:** Cold standby buys *higher reliability* but adds *switching complexity*; the designer's trade-off must account for $R_{sw}$.
- ☐ **Poisson Link:** Cold standby with $n$ units has failure count $\sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda t)$; reliability = $P(N(t) \leq n-1)$.
- ☐ **State Diagram Mastery:** Draw and label the 3-state diagram (both working / one working / failed) — KTU rewards neat diagrams with **+1 mark**.
- ☐ **Exam Tip:** Always mention the **assumptions** explicitly — exponential lifetimes, independence, perfect switch (unless stated otherwise).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Component and Unit Redundancy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Component and Unit Redundancy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Redundancy** in reliability engineering is a deliberate design technique wherein additional components, subassemblies, or functional units are incorporated into a system beyond the minimum required for basic operation. The primary objective is to enhance the **system reliability**, **availability**, and **mission success probability** by providing functional alternatives when a primary element fails.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, redundancy is classified into two principal hierarchical levels:

- **Component Redundancy (Lower-Level Redundancy):** Extra identical or dissimilar elements are introduced *within a single functional unit* so that the unit itself becomes more reliable. The unit, as a whole, is treated as a single replaceable block.
- **Unit Redundancy (Higher-Level / System-Level Redundancy):** Entire *replicated units or subsystems* are deployed at the system level, such that the failure of one complete unit is compensated by another identical or diverse unit. This is a system-architectural strategy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 explicitly requires students to differentiate between component-level and unit-level redundancy, derive reliability expressions, and analyze trade-offs between reliability gain and the economic / weight / volume penalties incurred.

### 1.2 Real-World Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are trekking through a forest in Kerala during the monsoon. You carry **one torchlight** (single point of failure). The light may fail, leaving you in the dark.

- **Component Redundancy Analogy:** You carry *one torchlight* but with **three parallel sets of batteries** wired together inside. If one battery pair dies, the others keep it glowing. *You still have only one torch, but the torch is more reliable.*
- **Unit Redundancy Analogy:** You carry **three independent torchlights** in your bag. If one fails, you switch to the next. *You have multiple complete units, each capable of full operation.*

This is the essence of redundancy: a **trade-off between extra resources (cost, weight, complexity) and improved survival probability** under failure.

> [!NOTE]
> The central metric of redundancy is the **Reliability Block Diagram (RBD)** mapping. Each redundant path is an *OR-logic* gate (any one path working is enough), while series paths form *AND-logic* gates.

### 1.3 Standard Reliability Metrics (KTU Required Terminology)

- **R(t):** Reliability function — probability of survival until time *t*.
- **λ (lambda):** Constant failure rate (failures per hour), units: **h⁻¹** or **FPMH**.
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** $1/\lambda$ for exponential law.
- **R(t) = e^(−λt)** for constant failure rate.
- **System Reliability (Rₛ):** Composite reliability of the entire redundant configuration.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Taxonomy of Redundancy

The redundancy landscape can be broken into a clean conceptual taxonomy that examiners frequently test:

**A. Based on Level of Application**
1. **Component Redundancy** — duplication at the lowest functional element.
2. **Unit Redundancy** — duplication at the subsystem or complete-unit level.

**B. Based on Mode of Operation**
1. **Active (Parallel / Hot) Redundancy** — all redundant elements operate simultaneously from time zero.
2. **Standby (Passive / Cold) Redundancy** — backup elements are idle until switched in upon failure of the active element.
3. **Warm Standby** — backup elements are partially energized (intermediate failure rate).

**C. Based on Voting Logic**
1. **Simple Parallel** — *k = 1 out of n* works.
2. **k-out-of-n Redundancy** — system functions if at least *k* of *n* identical units work.
3. **Triple Modular Redundant (TMR)** — special case *k = 2 out of 3* with majority voting.

### 2.2 Why and How — The Engineering Rationale

**Why use Component Redundancy?**
- *Localized improvement* of a weak link in the reliability chain.
- The unit becomes a "**super-reliable block**" before it is integrated into the larger system.
- Cheaper than replicating the entire unit when only one sub-component is failure-prone.

**Why use Unit Redundancy?**
- The unit as a whole is critical; any internal failure would take the unit down.
- Provides *isolation* — failure in one unit does not propagate to others.
- Enables **fault tolerance** and **graceful degradation**.

> [!TIP]
> **How to decide:** Use the **Reliability Allocation / Apportionment** result. If a single unit's required reliability (Rᵤ*) is greater than the *physically achievable* component reliability (Rᶜ_max), you must either improve the components OR introduce *unit redundancy*. The latter is preferred when the *reliability goal exceeds the technological ceiling*.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Configuration | Reliability Expression (R = component reliability) | Effective Failure Rate (approx) | MTTF |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single component | $R_s = R$ | $\lambda$ | $1/\lambda$ |
| Series (n components) | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i = R^n$ (equal) | $n\lambda$ | $1/(n\lambda)$ |
| Parallel (n equal, active) | $R_s = 1 - (1-R)^n$ | $\dfrac{\lambda}{n}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}\left[1 + \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{3} + \cdots + \dfrac{1}{n}\right]$ |
| 2-out-of-3 (TMR) | $R_s = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R)$ | $\approx 3\lambda^2 t$ | $\approx \dfrac{5}{6\lambda}$ |
| k-out-of-n (general) | $R_s = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$ | — | — |
| Standby (2 units, perfect switch) | $R_s = e^{-\lambda t} \left[1 + \lambda t\right]$ | — | $2/\lambda$ |
| Standby (2 units, switch reliability $R_{sw}$) | $R_s = R_{sw}\left[2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}\right] + (1-R_{sw})e^{-\lambda t}$ | — | — |
| Parallel with unequal Rᵢ | $R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-R_i)$ | — | — |

**Boundary Conditions and Notes:**
- Parallel formula valid for *statistically independent* failures.
- Approximate parallel failure rate $\lambda_{eff} \approx \lambda/n$ holds for **low** $\lambda t$.
- Standby formula assumes *no failure during idle* for cold standby; for warm standby, replace $\lambda$ with $\lambda_w$.
- All "t" here refer to the **mission time** in hours.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *series failure rate* $n\lambda$ (additive) with the *parallel* (substitutive) reduction. Parallel redundancy reduces the *effective* rate but **does NOT halve λ for 2 units** exactly — that is only an approximation valid at small $t$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Component Redundancy:** Multiple parallel MOSFETs in a power supply, parallel battery cells in EVs, redundant diodes in rectifiers, parallel hydraulic lines within a single actuator.
- **Unit Redundancy:** Dual-engine aircraft, redundant power distribution units in data centers (Tier-IV design), 2N/2N+1 UPS configurations, fly-by-wire computers in Airbus A320 (3-channel voting).
- **k-out-of-n:** Cooling fans in servers (often 2-out-of-3), tire configurations (4 tires, any 3 work), reactor control rod systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reliability of n Components in Active Parallel (Equal R)

**Given:** *n* identical, independent components each with reliability $R$, all operating from $t=0$. System succeeds if **at least one** component works.

**Logic Step 1:** The system *fails* only when **all** *n* components fail.
The probability that a single component fails is $(1-R)$.

**Logic Step 2:** Since failures are independent, probability of *all* failing is:
$$P(\text{all fail}) = (1-R)^n$$

**Logic Step 3:** The system reliability is the complement:
$$R_s = 1 - P(\text{all fail})$$

$$\boxed{R_s = 1 - (1-R)^n}$$

**Logic Step 4 — Expansion for n = 2, 3, 4** (used extensively in KTU problems):

For $n=2$:
$$R_s = 1 - (1-R)^2 = 2R - R^2$$

For $n=3$:
$$R_s = 1 - (1-R)^3 = 3R - 3R^2 + R^3$$

For $n=4$:
$$R_s = 1 - (1-R)^4 = 4R - 6R^2 + 4R^3 - R^4$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reliability of n Components in Series

**Given:** All *n* components must work for system success (AND logic).

**Logic Step 1:** Series success requires *all* to work.
$$R_s = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdots R_n$$

**Logic Step 2:** For equal $R_i = R$:
$$\boxed{R_s = R^n}$$

**Logic Step 3 — Failure rate equivalence** (exponential, constant $\lambda$):
$$R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} = e^{-(\sum \lambda_i) t}$$

Hence the equivalent failure rate of a series chain is:
$$\lambda_{eq} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = n\lambda \quad (\text{equal})$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Standby Redundancy (1 Active + 1 Cold Spare)

**Given:** Unit 1 operates with failure rate $\lambda$. Upon its failure, an *ideal* switch connects Unit 2 (cold, $\lambda_2 = 0$ until switched).

**Logic Step 1:** Let $T$ = time-to-failure of the system. Two scenarios:
- Unit 1 survives the entire mission $t$ → probability $e^{-\lambda t}$.
- Unit 1 fails at time $\tau \in [0,t]$ and Unit 2 survives the remaining $(t-\tau)$ → probability density $f(\tau)\,d\tau \cdot e^{-\lambda(t-\tau)}$.

**Logic Step 2:** Summing the disjoint events:
$$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \int_{0}^{t} \lambda e^{-\lambda \tau} \cdot e^{-\lambda(t-\tau)}\,d\tau$$

**Logic Step 3:** Factor $e^{-\lambda t}$ out of the integral:
$$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + e^{-\lambda t}\int_{0}^{t} \lambda\,d\tau = e^{-\lambda t}\left[1 + \lambda t\right]$$

$$\boxed{R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)}$$

**Logic Step 4 — MTTF derivation:**
$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_s(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)\,dt$$

Evaluating:
$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt + \lambda\int_{0}^{\infty} t\,e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{\lambda}{\lambda^2} = \frac{2}{\lambda}$$

Hence MTTF **doubles** under perfect cold-standby with *one* spare.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Standby Redundancy with Imperfect Switch

**Given:** Switch reliability $R_{sw}$ is non-unity. Two scenarios:

- (a) Switch works → behaves as 2-element standby, success prob $= e^{-\lambda t}(1+\lambda t)$.
- (b) Switch fails → only the primary matters, success prob $= e^{-\lambda t}$.

Total reliability (using law of total probability):
$$R_s = R_{sw}\left[e^{-\lambda t}(1+\lambda t)\right] + (1-R_{sw})e^{-\lambda t}$$

Simplifying:
$$R_s = e^{-\lambda t}\left[1 + R_{sw}\,\lambda t\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the formula KTU expects when the **switching mechanism is non-ideal**, a frequent Part B (14-mark) twist.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — k-out-of-n Redundancy (Binomial Model)

**Given:** System functions if at least $k$ of $n$ identical independent units work. Each unit has reliability $R$.

**Logic Step 1:** Probability that *exactly* $i$ units work:
$$P_i = \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$$

**Logic Step 2:** System succeeds if $i = k, k+1, \ldots, n$ units work:
$$R_s = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$$

**Logic Step 3 — Special case TMR (k=2, n=3):**
$$R_{TMR} = \binom{3}{2} R^2(1-R) + \binom{3}{3} R^3 = 3R^2(1-R) + R^3$$
$$\boxed{R_{TMR} = 3R^2 - 2R^3}$$

**Logic Step 4 — Comparison with simple parallel (k=1, n=3):**
$$R_{parallel} = 1 - (1-R)^3 = 3R - 3R^2 + R^3$$

Note that $R_{TMR} < R_{parallel}$ at high R (TMR is *less* reliable than full parallel) but TMR provides *voting*, which is robust against *common-mode* failures and *fault masking* — a KTU-favoured conceptual point.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A system requires 2 out of 4 pumps to operate for full capacity. Each pump has reliability $R = 0.9$ and constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.001$ /hr. Compute: (a) System reliability at $t = 100$ hr. (b) System MTTF.

**Solution (a):**
First, $R(100) = e^{-0.001 \times 100} = e^{-0.1} = 0.9048$.

Then with $k=2, n=4$:
$$R_s = \sum_{i=2}^{4} \binom{4}{i} R^i (1-R)^{4-i}$$

$$R_s = \binom{4}{2}(0.9048)^2(0.0952)^2 + \binom{4}{3}(0.9048)^3(0.0952) + \binom{4}{4}(0.9048)^4$$

$$R_s = 6(0.8187)(0.00906) + 4(0.7408)(0.0952) + 1(0.6703)$$

$$R_s = 6(0.007418) + 4(0.07053) + 0.6703$$

$$R_s = 0.04451 + 0.2821 + 0.6703 = 0.9969$$

**Solution (b):** MTTF of a 2-out-of-4 system (general formula):
$$\text{MTTF} = \sum_{i=2}^{4} \frac{1}{i\lambda}\binom{n-i}{k-1}\bigg/\binom{n}{k} \quad \text{(approximate via inclusion-exclusion)}$$

A more direct route for the binomial redundancy is:
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{j=0}^{n-k} \frac{1}{k+j}$$

For $k=2, n=4$ (so $j$ from 0 to 2):
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{4}\right] = \frac{13}{12\lambda}$$

With $\lambda = 0.001$ /hr:
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{13}{12 \times 0.001} = 1083.3 \text{ hours}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Reliability Block Diagram — Active Parallel Component Redundancy

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input] --> B1[Component 1]
    A --> B2[Component 2]
    A --> B3[Component 3]
    B1 --> C[Output]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#80FF80,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B1 fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style B2 fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style B3 fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

*Interpretation:* System succeeds if ANY one of the three parallel branches is intact (OR-logic).

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Cold Standby Redundancy (Unit Level)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Primary_Path
        P1[Active Unit 1] --> SW[Perfect Switch]
    end
    subgraph Standby_Path
        S1[Spare Unit 2 - Cold] -.->|activates on failure| SW
    end
    SW --> OUT[System Output]
    style P1 fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S1 fill:#B3FFB3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SW fill:#FFE680,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT fill:#80FF80,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

*Interpretation:* Spare remains idle (zero failure rate) until switch senses primary failure and engages it.

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — k-out-of-n Voting Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN[Sensor Input] --> V[Voter / Comparator]
    V --> M1[Unit 1]
    V --> M2[Unit 2]
    V --> M3[Unit 3]
    M1 --> VOTE[2-out-of-3 Vote]
    M2 --> VOTE
```
