```mermaid
    M3 --> VOTE
    VOTE --> RES[System Decision]
    style V fill:#FFE680,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style VOTE fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style RES fill:#80FF80,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M1 fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style M2 fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style M3 fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

*Interpretation:* Three identical units process identical input; majority vote tolerates one failure. Canonical TMR architecture.

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Series-Parallel Composite System (4-unit example)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Source] --> B1[Unit A1]
    A --> B2[Unit A2]
    B1 --> C1[Unit B1]
    B1 --> C2[Unit B2]
    B2 --> D1[Unit B2a]
    B2 --> D2[Unit B2b]
    C1 --> E[Sink]
    C2 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#80FF80,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

*Interpretation:* This is a *unit redundancy* at two levels — the A-block is parallel and the B-block is parallel; both A and B are in series at the unit level. System reliability:

$$R_s = \left[1-(1-R_{A1})(1-R_{A2})\right]\cdot\left[1-(1-R_{B1})(1-R_{B2})\right]$$

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **component redundancy** and **unit redundancy**. Give one practical example of each.

**Model Answer:**

- **Component Redundancy:** The redundant elements are placed *within* a single unit, making that unit internally more reliable. Example: three parallel diodes inside a single rectifier module.
- **Unit Redundancy:** Entire replicated units operate at the system level; the failure of one whole unit is compensated by another. Example: two identical CPU servers in a load-balanced cluster, or two engines on a twin-prop aircraft.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** A system consists of 3 components in parallel. Each has reliability 0.9. Calculate the system reliability. Comment on the gain over a single component.

**Model Answer:**

$$R_s = 1 - (1-0.9)^3 = 1 - 0.001 = 0.999$$

Gain: $0.999 - 0.9 = 0.099$ (an absolute 9.9% increase, or 10× reduction in failure probability).

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] A satellite communication system uses **cold standby redundancy** with one active transmitter and one identical spare. The active transmitter has constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.0005$ /hr. The switch is *perfect* ($R_{sw} = 1$). For a mission time of $t = 1000$ hr, compute:
1. Reliability of the system.
2. MTTF.
3. Compare with single-transmitter reliability.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Derive the reliability expression for a **2-out-of-3 redundant system** with identical components of reliability $R$. Hence plot/compare reliability values with simple parallel (1-out-of-3) for $R = 0.5, 0.7, 0.9, 0.99$.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — System reliability with cold standby (perfect switch):**
$$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$$

With $\lambda t = 0.0005 \times 1000 = 0.5$:
$$R_s = e^{-0.5}(1 + 0.5) = 0.6065 \times 1.5 = 0.9098$$

**Step 2 — MTTF:**
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{2}{\lambda} = \frac{2}{0.0005} = 4000 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 — Single transmitter reliability:**
$$R_{single} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

**Comparison:** Standby redundancy raises reliability from **0.6065 → 0.9098** at 1000 hr and doubles the MTTF from 2000 hr to 4000 hr — a substantial improvement.

**Model Solution (b):**

For 2-out-of-3:
$$R_s = \binom{3}{2} R^2(1-R) + \binom{3}{3} R^3 = 3R^2(1-R) + R^3 = 3R^2 - 2R^3$$

For 1-out-of-3 (simple parallel):
$$R_{p} = 1 - (1-R)^3 = 3R - 3R^2 + R^3$$

**Tabulated Comparison:**

| R | 2-out-of-3 ($3R^2-2R^3$) | 1-out-of-3 ($3R-3R^2+R^3$) | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.50 | 0.5000 | 0.8750 | $-0.375$ |
| 0.70 | 0.7840 | 0.9730 | $-0.189$ |
| 0.90 | 0.9720 | 0.9990 | $-0.027$ |
| 0.99 | 0.9997 | 0.999999 | $\approx 0$ |

**Inference:** At low $R$, 1-out-of-3 is far more reliable because it tolerates up to 2 failures. At very high $R$, both converge to 1. The 2-out-of-3 (TMR) is preferred when *fault masking via voting* is needed, not just raw reliability.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] A system has 3 components in parallel, each with reliability 0.95. The parallel block is connected in **series** with two independent components of reliability 0.98 and 0.92. Compute: (i) system reliability, (ii) identify the weakest link.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the concept of **standby redundancy with imperfect switch**. Derive the reliability expression and discuss how switch reliability affects system performance.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Parallel block reliability:**
$$R_{par} = 1 - (1-0.95)^3 = 1 - 0.000125 = 0.999875$$

**Step 2 — System reliability (series combination):**
$$R_s = R_{par} \times 0.98 \times 0.92$$
$$R_s = 0.999875 \times 0.9016 = 0.9015$$

**Step 3 — Identifying the weakest link:**

The unreliability of each branch:
- Parallel block: $1 - 0.999875 = 0.000125$
- Component at 0.98: unreliability $0.02$
- Component at 0.92: unreliability $0.08$

The single 0.92 component is the **weakest link** with the highest individual unreliability. In a series chain, the **least reliable element dominates** the system behaviour.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Conceptual Explanation:**
In standby redundancy, a *switching mechanism* must sense the failure of the active unit and engage the spare. Real-world switches (relays, contactors, logic circuits) are *not* perfectly reliable. If the switch fails, the spare is *not* engaged, leading to system failure.

**Derivation:**

Let $R_{sw}$ = reliability of the switch, $R$ = reliability of each unit, $t$ = mission time, $\lambda$ = unit failure rate.

- Probability switch works (acts as cold standby) = $R_{sw}$: contributes $R_{sw} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}(1+\lambda t)$.
- Probability switch fails (only active unit matters) = $(1-R_{sw})$: contributes $(1-R_{sw})e^{-\lambda t}$.

Total:
$$R_s = R_{sw}\,e^{-\lambda t}(1+\lambda t) + (1-R_{sw})\,e^{-\lambda t}$$
$$\boxed{R_s = e^{-\lambda t}\left[1 + R_{sw}\,\lambda t\right]}$$

**Effect of switch reliability:**

If $R_{sw} = 1$ (perfect), $R_s = e^{-\lambda t}(1+\lambda t)$ — maximum gain.
If $R_{sw} = 0$ (switch always fails), $R_s = e^{-\lambda t}$ — no gain over single unit.
At $R_{sw} = 0.9, \lambda t = 0.5$: $R_s = e^{-0.5}[1 + 0.45] = 0.6065 \times 1.45 = 0.8794$, versus the perfect-switch value of 0.9098. A 10% switch unreliability costs ~3% absolute reliability.

**Design takeaway:** In critical systems, the switch must be *highly* reliable and *self-monitored*, often itself redundant (e.g., dual-coil relays with cross-monitoring).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the switch reliability** in standby problems — students often quote $e^{-\lambda t}(1+\lambda t)$ regardless of $R_{sw}$, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing *series* and *parallel* failure rate formulas** — series failure rates ADD ($\lambda_{eq} = n\lambda$), parallel does NOT simply divide.
> 3. **Assuming cold-standby spares have $\lambda = 0$** is correct for *cold* but must be replaced by $\lambda_w$ for *warm* standby.
> 4. **For k-out-of-n problems, omitting the binomial coefficient** $\binom{n}{i}$ is a 3-mark penalty.
> 5. **Mixing up TMR (k=2 of n=3) with simple parallel (k=1 of n=3)** — examiners explicitly test this distinction.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Component vs Unit Redundancy:** *Where* the duplication is placed (inside one unit vs across multiple units).
- **Active vs Standby:** Active elements operate from t=0; standby elements wait.
- **Parallel (n) formula:** $R_s = 1 - (1-R)^n$ — most-tested formula in KTU.
- **Series (n) formula:** $R_s = R^n$; failure rate $n\lambda$.
- **k-out-of-n formula:** $R_s = \sum_{i=k}^{n}\binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$.
- **Cold Standby (1 spare, perfect switch):** $R_s = e^{-\lambda t}(1+\lambda t)$, MTTF = $2/\lambda$.
- **Imperfect Switch Standby:** $R_s = e^{-\lambda t}[1 + R_{sw}\lambda t]$.
- **TMR (Triple Modular Redundant):** $R_s = 3R^2 - 2R^3$ — provides *voting* not just *reliability*.
- **MTTF of n-element parallel (active):** $\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{1}{i}$ — harmonic series.
- **Trade-off Principle:** Redundancy improves $R_s$ but increases *cost, weight, volume,* and *failure rate per unit area*. The optimal level balances *reliability gain* against *resource penalty*.
- **Common-mode failures:** Even with redundancy, common design flaws or environmental stressors can defeat all units. *Diverse redundancy* (different manufacturers / designs) is the KTU-favoured mitigation.
- **Mark-weight priorities for KTU 2024 Module 2:** Derivation of parallel formula (7 marks), k-out-of-n binomial sum (7 marks), standby with imperfect switch (7 marks), and trade-off analysis (3 marks).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weakest-Link Technique
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Weakest-Link Technique**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Weakest-Link Technique** (also called the **Weakest-Link Model** or **Series Reliability Model**) is a fundamental reliability modeling approach in which a system's survival is governed entirely by the survival of its most failure-prone element. Formally, the system is said to function **if and only if** every constituent component functions. The failure of *any single* component precipitates the failure of the *entire* system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Statement:** A system S comprising *n* independent components is a "weakest-link system" if the system reliability function is the product of the individual component reliability functions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a heavy iron chain suspended from a ceiling, holding a massive chandelier. The chain consists of 50 links, each forged separately. No matter how strong 49 of those links are, the chandelier crashes to the floor the moment the *single weakest* link snaps.

- **The Chain = The System**
- **Each Link = A Component**
- **The Weakest Link = The Critical Failure Point**
- **Time of Failure of the Weakest Link = Time of Failure of the System**

> [!NOTE]
> This is why the model is also called the **"Chain Model"** or **"Series Model"** in reliability textbooks. The "series" here is **not** an electrical series — it is a *reliability* series, meaning all components are in the *failure path*.

## 1.3 Engineering Examples

| Domain | Weakest-Link Element | Consequence of Failure |
|---|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Weakest concrete column | Building collapse |
| **Aerospace** | Weakest rivet on fuselage | Catastrophic structural failure |
| **Electronics** | Weakest solder joint on PCB | Whole circuit board failure |
| **Materials Science** | Largest micro-crack in a ceramic | Brittle fracture |
| **Power Systems** | Single transmission tower in a corridor | Line outage |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics

- **R(t)** = Reliability function (probability of survival up to time *t*)
- **F(t)** = Cumulative distribution function (CDF) of failure
- **f(t)** = Probability density function (PDF) of failure
- **λ(t)** = Hazard (failure) rate
- **β** = Weibull shape parameter
- **η** = Weibull scale parameter (characteristic life)

> [!NOTE]
> In the weakest-link context, the **extreme value** of strength (minimum) governs survival. The Weibull distribution is the canonical model because it belongs to the **minimum extreme value distribution family**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Logic — Step by Step

The weakest-link model is built on three foundational pillars:

1. **Failure = Minimum Strength Event**: The system fails at the *smallest* value of strength across all *n* elements. If the applied stress exceeds even one element's strength, the system fails.

2. **Independence of Component Failures**: The failure of one component does not affect the survival probability of the others. (Mathematically, failure events are *mutually independent*.)

3. **Multiplicative Reliability Rule**: The system survives only if *all* components survive. Probabilities of independent events multiply:
$$R_{\text{system}}(t) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t) \cdot R_3(t) \cdots R_n(t)$$

4. **Hazard Rate Aggregation**: Since reliabilities multiply, the system hazard rate is the *sum* of individual hazard rates:
$$\lambda_{\text{system}}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i(t)$$

## 2.2 The Weibull Connection (Why Weibull?)

The Weibull distribution is the natural mathematical partner of the weakest-link model for three reasons:

- It is the **Type III asymptotic minimum extreme value distribution**.
- Its **CDF is the product of Weibull CDFs** raised to a power (closed form under weakest-link).
- The shape parameter **β is invariant** under weakest-link combination — a unique and powerful property.

For a single component following a 2-parameter Weibull distribution:

$$R_c(t) = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

For *n* **identical, independent** Weibull components in a weakest-link (series) configuration:

$$R_{\text{system}}(t) = \left[R_c(t)\right]^n = \exp\left[-n\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

Rearranging into standard Weibull form:

$$R_{\text{system}}(t) = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta_s}\right)^{\beta_s}\right]$$

By direct comparison:

$$\beta_s = \beta \quad \text{(shape parameter preserved!)}$$

$$\eta_s = \frac{\eta}{n^{1/\beta}} \quad \text{(scale parameter reduced)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The shape parameter β is preserved in the weakest-link combination of identical Weibull components.** This is the single most exam-relevant result of this topic.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Math

- **Why multiply reliabilities?** Because "all survive" = intersection of independent events = product of probabilities.
- **Why does β remain invariant?** Because the *physical failure mechanism* (e.g., brittle fracture, crack propagation) is the *same* in every element; only the *effective scale* changes as the system gets larger.
- **Why does η decrease with n?** Because a longer chain has more opportunities to contain a weak link. The characteristic life of the system is *shorter* than that of a single component.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$ | General weakest-link reliability |
| 2 | $R_s(t) = \left[R_c(t)\right]^n$ | For *n* identical components |
| 3 | $R_c(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | Weibull reliability of single component |
| 4 | $R_s(t) = e^{-n(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | Weibull weakest-link of *n* identical components |
| 5 | $\beta_s = \beta$ | Weibull shape parameter of system |
| 6 | $\eta_s = \eta \, n^{-1/\beta}$ | Weibull scale parameter of system |
| 7 | $\lambda_s(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i(t)$ | System hazard rate (additive) |
| 8 | $\text{MTTF}_s = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_s(t) \, dt$ | Mean Time To Failure of system |
| 9 | $\text{MTTF} = \eta \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | Weibull MTTF (single component) |
| 10 | $\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{\eta}{n^{1/\beta}} \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | Weibull MTTF (weakest-link system) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fiber Bundle Strength**: Strength of a carbon-fiber tow or steel-wire rope. The bundle fails at its weakest fiber.
- **Brittle Ceramics**: Failure initiated at the largest flaw (Weibull's original 1939 application).
- **Electronic Assemblies**: A PCB fails if any solder joint fails.
- **Pipeline Networks**: A pipeline section fails if any weld fails.
- **Aerospace Structures**: Wing spar failure at its weakest cross-section.

## 2.6 Special Cases

- **β = 1 (Exponential)**: $R_s(t) = e^{-n t/\eta}$, and $\eta_s = \eta/n$. The MTTF is divided by *n*. Failure is memoryless (random).
- **β = 2 (Rayleigh)**: $\eta_s = \eta/\sqrt{n}$. Common in corrosion and fatigue analysis.
- **β → ∞**: The system becomes deterministic; the failure time equals $\eta$ with certainty.
- **β < 1 (Infant Mortality)**: Many early failures; weakest-link behavior shows rapid early degradation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: General Series (Weakest-Link) Reliability

**Statement to prove:** For *n* independent components with reliabilities $R_i(t)$, the reliability of the series (weakest-link) system is

$$R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$$

### Proof

**Step 1 — Define the survival event.**
Let $A_i$ denote the event "Component *i* survives up to time *t*". The system survives only if *all* components survive:

$$A_s = A_1 \cap A_2 \cap A_3 \cap \cdots \cap A_n$$

**Step 2 — Apply probability of intersection.**
By the definition of joint probability:

$$R_s(t) = P(A_s) = P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \cdots \cap A_n)$$

**Step 3 — Invoke independence.**
For *mutually independent* events, the joint probability factorizes:

$$P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \cdots \cap A_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute component reliabilities.**
Since $R_i(t) = P(A_i)$:

$$R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$$

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hazard Rate of the Series System

**Statement to prove:** $\lambda_s(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i(t)$

### Proof

**Step 1 — Take the natural logarithm of $R_s(t)$.**

$$\ln R_s(t) = \ln \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln R_i(t)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to *t*.**

$$\frac{1}{R_s(t)} \cdot \frac{dR_s(t)}{dt} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{R_i(t)} \cdot \frac{dR_i(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Recall the definition of hazard rate.**
The hazard rate is defined as:

$$\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Therefore:

$$-\lambda_s(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \bigl[-\lambda_i(t)\bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Conclude.**

$$\lambda_s(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i(t)$$

$\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The system hazard rate is the *sum* of the component hazard rates. The component with the *highest* hazard rate *dominates* the system unreliability.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Weibull Weakest-Link Scale Parameter

**Statement to prove:** For *n* identical independent Weibull components, the system scale parameter is

$$\eta_s = \eta \cdot n^{-1/\beta}$$

### Proof

**Step 1 — Write the system reliability as a product.**

$$R_s(t) = \left[\exp\!\left(-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right)\right]^n$$

**Step 2 — Apply the power rule of exponentials.**

$$R_s(t) = \exp\!\left[-n\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Rewrite the Weibull reliability in canonical form.**

$$R_s(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta_s}\right)^{\beta_s}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Compare exponents.**
Equating the two exponent expressions:

$$n\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta} = \left(\frac{t}{\eta_s}\right)^{\beta_s}$$

**Step 5 — Match the powers of *t*.**
For this to hold for all *t* > 0:

$$\beta_s = \beta$$

**Step 6 — Match the coefficients.**
With $\beta_s = \beta$:

$$n \cdot \frac{1}{\eta^{\beta}} = \frac{1}{\eta_s^{\beta}}$$

$$\eta_s^{\beta} = \frac{\eta^{\beta}}{n}$$

$$\eta_s = \eta \cdot n^{-1/\beta}$$

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: MTTF of the Weibull Weakest-Link System

**Statement to prove:**

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{\eta}{n^{1/\beta}} \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

### Proof

**Step 1 — Use the MTTF integral.**

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_s(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \exp\!\left[-n\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right] dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the variable.**
Let $u = n^{1/\beta} \cdot t / \eta$, so $t = \eta u / n^{1/\beta}$ and $dt = \eta / n^{1/\beta} \, du$.

**Step 3 — Simplify the exponent.**

$$n\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta} = n \cdot \frac{u^{\beta}}{n} = u^{\beta}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the integral.**

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u^{\beta}} \cdot \frac{\eta}{n^{1/\beta}} \, du = \frac{\eta}{n^{1/\beta}} \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u^{\beta}} du$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the Gamma function.**
Using the identity $\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u^{\beta}} du = \frac{1}{\beta}\Gamma(1/\beta) = \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$:

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{\eta}{n^{1/\beta}} \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A system consists of **3 identical, independent** components arranged in a weakest-link configuration. Each component follows a Weibull distribution with **β = 2** and **η = 5000 hours**. Find:
> (a) The system reliability at t = 1000 hours.
> (b) The system shape and scale parameters.
> (c) The system MTTF.

### Solution

**Part (a) — System reliability at t = 1000 h**

Step 1 — Compute the single-component reliability.

$$R_c(1000) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{1000}{5000}\right)^{2}\right] = \exp\!\left[-\left(0.2\right)^{2}\right] = e^{-0.04}$$

$$R_c(1000) \approx 0.9608$$

Step 2 — Apply the weakest-link rule for *n* = 3.

$$R_s(1000) = [R_c(1000)]^{3} = (0.9608)^{3} \approx 0.8869$$

**Part (b) — System Weibull parameters**

Step 3 — Shape parameter is preserved.

$$\beta_s = \beta = 2$$

Step 4 — Scale parameter decreases.

$$\eta_s = \eta \cdot n^{-1/\beta} = 5000 \cdot 3^{-1/2} = \frac{5000}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 2886.75 \text{ hours}$$

**Part (c) — System MTTF**

Step 5 — Apply the MTTF formula.

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{\eta}{n^{1/\beta}} \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right) = 2886.75 \cdot \Gamma(1.5)$$

Step 6 — Use $\Gamma(1.5) = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \approx 0.8862$.

$$\text{MTTF}_s \approx 2886.75 \times 0.8862 \approx 2558.4 \text{ hours}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix (When to Use the Weakest-Link Model)

| System Characteristic | Use Weakest-Link? | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Failure of *any* component halts the system | **YES** | Classic series definition |
| Components have redundant backups | **NO** | Use parallel or k-out-of-n model |
| Components share load equally | **YES** | Each contributes to overall strength |
| Components are independent | **YES** | Required for multiplicative rule |
| Common-cause failures dominate | **NO** | Beta-factor or shock model preferred |
| Failure of one triggers cascade | **NO** | Use cascade / fault-tree model |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Weakest-Link Reliability Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["APPLIED STRESS S"] --> B["SYSTEM S"]
    B --> C1["COMPONENT 1"]
    B --> C2["COMPONENT 2"]
    B --> C3["COMPONENT 3"]
    B --> Cn["COMPONENT N"]
    C1 --> D1["STRENGTH X1"]
    C2 --> D2["STRENGTH X2"]
    C3 --> D3["STRENGTH X3"]
    Cn --> Dn["STRENGTH XN"]
    D1 --> E["MIN X1 X2 X3 ... XN"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    Dn --> E
    E --> F{"STRESS GREATER THAN MIN STRENGTH"}
    F -- YES --> G["SYSTEM FAILS"]
    F -- NO --> H["SYSTEM SURVIVES"]
    H --> I["RELIABILITY R_s = PRODUCT OF R_i"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Mathematical Reduction of Weibull Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["COMPONENT WEIBULL: R_c = exp(-(t/eta)^beta)"] --> Q1["RAISE TO POWER n"]
    Q1 --> R1["SYSTEM RELIABILITY: R_s = exp(-n(t/eta)^beta)"]
    R1 --> S1["COMPARE WITH CANONICAL FORM"]
    S1 --> T1["beta_s = beta  SHAPE INVARIANT"]
    S1 --> U1["eta_s = eta / n^(1/beta)  SCALE REDUCED"]
    U1 --> V1["SYSTEM MTTF = eta_s * Gamma(1 + 1/beta)"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Series Reliability Computation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["ENTER COMPONENT COUNT n"] --> B0["INITIALIZE R_s = 1"]
    B0 --> C0{"i LESS THAN n"}
    C0 -- YES --> D0["READ COMPONENT RELIABILITY R_i"]
    D0 --> E0["MULTIPLY R_s = R_s * R_i"]
    E0 --> F0["INCREMENT i"]
    F0 --> C0
    C0 -- NO --> G0["OUTPUT SYSTEM RELIABILITY R_s"]
    G0 --> H0["COMPUTE HAZARD SUM lambda_s = SUM lambda_i"]
    H0 --> I0["COMPUTE MTTF = INTEGRAL OF R_s dt"]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Block Architecture: Weakest-Link vs Parallel Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WL["WEAKEST LINK SERIES"]
        direction LR
        w1["C1"] --- w2["C2"] --- w3["C3"] --- w4["Cn"]
    end
    subgraph PR["PARALLEL REDUNDANT"]
        direction LR
        p1["C1"]
        p2["C2"]
        p3["C3"]
        p4["Cn"]
    end
    WL --> WLOUT["R_s = PRODUCT R_i  ALL MUST SURVIVE"]
    PR --> PROUT["R_s = 1 - PRODUCT FAILURE_i  AT LEAST ONE SURVIVES"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The double hyphen `---` is used in Mermaid to draw connecting lines between nodes. The subgraph blocks visually contrast the *all-must-survive* (weakest-link) versus *at-least-one-survives* (parallel) logic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the weakest-link model in reliability engineering. Under what conditions is it applicable?**

**Model Answer:**

The weakest-link model (also called the *series reliability model*) is a reliability configuration in which the system functions *if and only if* every component functions. The failure of any single component causes the system to fail. 

It is applicable when:
1. Components are arranged such that the **failure of any one** causes total system failure.
2. Components fail **independently** of one another.
3. There is **no redundancy** built into the system.

**Marking scheme:** Definition (2 marks) + conditions (1 mark).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the Weibull weakest-link relationship between component and system scale parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

For *n* identical, independent Weibull components (each with scale $\eta$ and shape $\beta$) arranged in a weakest-link configuration, the system scale parameter $\eta_s$ and shape parameter $\beta_s$ are:

$$\beta_s = \beta$$

$$\eta_s = \eta \cdot n^{-1/\beta}$$

**Marking scheme:** Correct formulas (2 marks) + explanation of invariance (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

A pressure vessel safety system consists of **4 independent, identical relief valves** in a weakest-link arrangement. Each valve follows a Weibull life distribution with **β = 1.5** and **η = 8000 hours**.

**(a)** Derive the system reliability expression. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Calculate the system reliability at t = 2000 hours, and the system MTTF. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation**

Step 1 — The reliability of a single Weibull valve is:

$$R_c(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right] = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{8000}\right)^{1.5}\right]$$

Step 2 — For *n* = 4 identical independent components in a weakest-link configuration, the system reliability is the product:

$$R_s(t) = \left[R_c(t)\right]^4$$

Step 3 — Substituting and applying the power rule of exponentials:

$$R_s(t) = \left[\exp\!\left(-\left(\frac{t}{8000}\right)^{1.5}\right)\right]^4$$

$$R_s(t) = \exp\!\left[-4\left(\frac{t}{8000}\right)^{1.5}\right]$$

Step 4 — Rewriting in canonical Weibull form $R_s(t) = \exp[-(t/\eta_s)^{\beta_s}]$:

$$\beta_s = \beta = 1.5$$

$$\eta_s = \eta \cdot n^{-1/\beta} = 8000 \cdot 4^{-1/1.5} = \frac{8000}{4^{2/3}} = \frac{8000}{2.5198} \approx 3174.8 \text{ hours}$$

Therefore:

$$R_s(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{3174.8}\right)^{1.5}\right]$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation**

Step 5 — Compute $R_s$ at *t* = 2000 h:

$$R_s(2000) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{2000}{3174.8}\right)^{1.5}\right] = \exp\!\left[-(0.6300)^{1.5}\right]$$

$$(0.6300)^{1.5} = 0.6300 \times \sqrt{0.6300} = 0.6300 \times 0.7937 = 0.5000$$

$$R_s(2000) = e^{-0.5000} \approx 0.6065$$

Step 6 — Compute the MTTF using the Gamma function:

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \eta_s \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right) = 3174.8 \cdot \Gamma(1.6667)$$

Step 7 — Use the identity $\Gamma(1.6667) \approx 0.9027$ (from standard Gamma tables):

$$\text{MTTF}_s \approx 3174.8 \times 0.9027 \approx 2866.0 \text{ hours}$$

**Final Answer:** $R_s(2000) \approx 0.6065$ and $\text{MTTF}_s \approx 2866$ hours.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

An aerospace structural component is modeled as **5 independent identical elements** in a weakest-link configuration. Each element has constant hazard rate $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ per hour (exponential life distribution).

**(a)** Derive the system reliability function and the system MTTF. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Find the time at which system reliability drops to **0.90**, and comment on the implication of the weakest-link assumption for safety. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation**

Step 1 — The exponential reliability of a single element is:

$$R_c(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-2 \times 10^{-5} \, t}$$

Step 2 — For *n* = 5 identical components in weakest-link:

$$R_s(t) = \left[e^{-2 \times 10^{-5} \, t}\right]^5 = e^{-10 \times 10^{-5} \, t} = e^{-1 \times 10^{-4} \, t}$$

Step 3 — Therefore, the system hazard rate is:

$$\lambda_s = n \cdot \lambda = 5 \times 2 \times 10^{-5} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per hour}$$

Step 4 — The MTTF is:

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 10000 \text{ hours}$$

**Part (b) — Time for R = 0.90**

Step 5 — Set $R_s(t) = 0.90$:

$$e^{-10^{-4} \, t} = 0.90$$

$$-10^{-4} \, t = \ln(0.90) = -0.10536$$

$$t = \frac{0.10536}{10^{-4}} = 1053.6 \text{ hours}$$

Step 6 — **Implication for safety:**
The weakest-link assumption means that the structural component's safe life is governed by the *earliest* failure among its 5 elements. With 5 elements, the effective hazard rate is *5 times* that of a single element, reducing the MTTF from 50,000 hours (single) to 10,000 hours (system). For aerospace applications, this implies that:
- Inspection intervals must be based on $\text{MTTF}_s$, not on a single element's MTTF.
- The system is **no more reliable than its weakest element**.
- Redundancy or *parallel* configurations should be considered for safety-critical paths.

**Final Answer:** $t \approx 1053.6$ hours for $R_s = 0.90$, with $\text{MTTF}_s = 10000$ hours.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Series (Electrical) vs Series (Reliability):** A "series system" in reliability means *all* components must work, *not* that current flows through them. Many students draw circuit diagrams and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting that β is preserved:** When using Weibull, students often try to average β values or change β arbitrarily. The shape parameter is **invariant** in identical weakest-link combinations.
> 3. **Mixing up MTTF formulas:** The system MTTF uses $\eta_s$ (the reduced scale), not $\eta$. Plugging the wrong value gives numerical errors of an order of magnitude.
> 4. **Ignoring independence assumption:** The multiplicative rule $R_s = \prod R_i$ is valid *only* under independence. If components share a common failure cause, the formula breaks down.
> 5. **Units mismatch in $\eta$:** $\eta$ must always be in the same time units (hours, cycles) as the *t* used in the reliability expression.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **weakest-link (series) system** fails if *any one* component fails.
- ✅ System reliability is the **product** of component reliabilities: $R_s = \prod R_i$.
- ✅ System hazard rate is the **sum** of component hazard rates: $\lambda_s = \sum \lambda_i$.
- ✅ For *n* identical Weibull components, $\beta_s = \beta$ (shape preserved).
- ✅ The system scale parameter shrinks as $\eta_s = \eta \cdot n^{-1/\beta}$.
- ✅ The MTTF of the system is $\text{MTTF}_s = (\eta / n^{1/\beta}) \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$.
- ✅ For exponential components ($\beta = 1$), $\text{MTTF}_s = \text{MTTF}_{\text{single}} / n$.
- ✅ The model is applicable **only** when components are **independent** and **non-redundant**.
- ✅ Weibull distribution is the canonical weakest-link distribution because it is a **Type III minimum extreme value** distribution.
- ✅ The "weakest link" is the **minimum strength** of all elements; system survival requires $S \leq \min(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$.
- ✅ Engineering applications include fiber bundles, brittle ceramics, electronic assemblies, and pressure vessel safety chains.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mixed Redundancy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mixed Redundancy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mixed Redundancy** is an advanced redundancy configuration in which a system simultaneously employs **two or more different redundancy strategies** (e.g., *Active Parallel Redundancy* and *Standby Redundancy*) across different sub-assemblies, sub-systems, or hierarchical levels to achieve a target reliability level that cannot be economically obtained by any single redundancy technique alone.

In formal reliability notation, mixed redundancy refers to a system **$S$** whose reliability block diagram (RBD) cannot be decomposed into a pure series, pure parallel, or pure standby network — it is a *hybrid* network. Mathematically, a system possesses mixed redundancy if its structure function satisfies:

$$\phi_S(\vec{x}) = \phi_{series}\left[\phi_{parallel}(\vec{x}_p),\ \phi_{standby}(\vec{x}_s)\right]$$

where $\vec{x}_p$ is the vector of active-parallel component states and $\vec{x}_s$ is the vector of standby component states.

The KTU 2024 syllabus places this topic in **Module 2** because it integrates the redundancy principles already studied (parallel and standby) into a unified analytical framework used in real industrial systems (avionics, nuclear reactor safety, telecom backbone, satellite payload).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Modern Commercial Aircraft**

Imagine a commercial jetliner. Its **electrical power system** uses *active parallel redundancy* — both the left and right engine generators are running simultaneously, and either one can power the entire aircraft. This is **active parallel** because both units are energized and load-sharing.

However, its **hydraulic backup system** uses *standby redundancy* — the Ram Air Turbine (RAT) deploys *only if* both main hydraulic pumps fail. The RAT sits idle (cold standby) until activated by a failure sensor.

The aircraft is therefore a **mixed redundant system**: parallel redundancy in the electrical domain, standby redundancy in the hydraulic domain. This is *mixed redundancy* — using the right redundancy technique for the right sub-system based on weight, cost, failure-rate, and mission profile.

## 1.3 Why Mixed Redundancy Is Used

Engineers adopt mixed redundancy for the following engineering-economic reasons:

- **Cost Optimization**: Active parallel components must be identical and running (expensive due to wear). Standby components can be cheaper, lower-rated, and turned off (longer life). Mixing saves capital cost.
- **Mission-Critical Trade-offs**: Critical-to-life sub-systems (e.g., flight controls) demand *active parallel*. Non-critical or backup-only sub-systems tolerate *standby*.
- **Switching Reliability Compensation**: Standby redundancy is sensitive to *switch reliability* ($R_{sw}$). Adding a small active parallel element at critical nodes compensates for imperfect switching.
- **Weight & Space Constraints**: Aerospace and naval systems cannot afford full active parallel for every sub-system. Mixed topology meets the reliability target with minimum mass.

## 1.4 Common Mixed Redundancy Topologies in KTU Context

The KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus recognizes the following canonical mixed configurations:

| S.No. | Topology Name | Configuration Logic |
|:-----:|:--------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | Parallel-of-Standby Subsystems | Two complete standby sub-systems operating in parallel |
| 2 | Standby-of-Parallel Subsystems | A parallel sub-system backed by a standby sub-system |
| 3 | Sectionalized Mixed | Series chain where some sections are parallel and some are standby |
| 4 | r-out-of-n with Replacement | Voting redundancy with active replacement (combines r/n + standby) |

Each topology is analyzed using the **Conditional Reliability Method** combined with the **Law of Total Probability**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Logic Steps (Bulleted Framework)

The analysis of a mixed redundant system proceeds through five rigorous steps. Mastering this sequence is mandatory for KTU ESE marks.

**Step 1 — Decompose the System**
- Identify each sub-assembly.
- Classify every sub-assembly as *Series*, *Parallel*, or *Standby*.
- Construct the Reliability Block Diagram (RBD) clearly.

**Step 2 — Assign Reliability Expressions**
- Each active parallel block of $n$ components with identical reliability $R$ has block reliability $1 - (1-R)^n$.
- Each pure series block has reliability equal to the product of component reliabilities: $R_S = \prod_i R_i$.
- Each standby block (assuming perfect switch, $R_{sw}=1$) has reliability $1 - (1 - R_{primary}) \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{k-1}(1 - R_{standby,j})$.

**Step 3 — Apply Conditional Probability (Law of Total Probability)**
- For non-series-parallel mixed systems, condition on the state of the *switching device* or on the *primary component*.
- Use the identity: $R_{system} = R_{switch}\cdot R_{system \vert switch\ works} + (1-R_{switch})\cdot R_{system \vert switch\ fails}$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify**
- Substitute the expressions from Step 2 into the conditional structure of Step 3.
- Algebraically simplify to a single closed-form reliability expression.

**Step 5 — Validate via Bounds**
- Confirm $0 \leq R_{system} \leq 1$.
- Confirm $R_{system} \geq R_{series\ equivalent}$ (mixed redundancy must improve on series).
- Confirm $R_{system} \leq R_{parallel\ equivalent}$ (a mixed system cannot exceed a fully parallel equivalent with the same number of components).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Why condition on the switch?** — Because a standby component is *functionally decoupled* from the primary until a switch closes. The closed/open state of the switch is a binary random variable that governs the topology. The system behaves as a *series* system when the switch is open and as a *parallel* system (with one less component) when the switch is closed. Conditioning resolves this *topology uncertainty* into two simpler deterministic problems.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

Mixed redundancy is the *de facto* design standard in:
- **Avionics** (FADEC, fly-by-wire, AIMS)
- **Railway signalling** (ATP/ATC interlocking)
- **Spacecraft** (redundancy switching units, telemetry chains)
- **Industrial PLCs** (redundant CPU with hot-standby I/O)
- **Data-centre UPS systems** (N+1 parallel with standby bypass)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for Mixed Redundancy problems. Memorize the boundary conditions and the assumptions.

| # | Configuration | Reliability Expression | Assumptions |
|:-:|:--------------|:-----------------------|:------------|
| 1 | Series of $n$ components | $R_S = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Independent failures |
| 2 | Active parallel of $n$ identical components | $R_P = 1 - (1 - R)^n$ | Independent, identical |
| 3 | Standby (1 primary + 1 standby, perfect switch) | $R_{SB} = 1 - (1 - R_p)(1 - R_s)$ | Ideal switch |
| 4 | Standby with imperfect switch | $R_{SB} = R_{sw} \cdot [1 - (1-R_p)(1-R_s)] + (1-R_{sw}) \cdot R_p$ | $R_{sw}$ constant |
| 5 | Sectionalized Mixed (Series of Parallel + Standby blocks) | $R_{sys} = R_{P-block} \cdot R_{SB-block}$ | Independent blocks |
| 6 | Parallel of Standby subsystems ($m$ identical SB blocks) | $R_{sys} = 1 - (1 - R_{SB})^m$ | Identical standby blocks |
| 7 | r-out-of-n with active parallel + 1 standby | $R_{sys} = R_{sw}\left[1 - \sum_{k=0}^{n-r}\binom{n}{k}R^{n-k}(1-R)^k\right] + (1-R_{sw}) R^r$ | r voters active, 1 spare |
| 8 | Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of mixed system | $MTBF = \int_0^{\infty} R_{sys}(t)\, dt$ | Repairable systems |

> **Critical Substitution Rule:** When the problem gives a constant *failure rate* $\lambda$ (i.e., exponential distribution), substitute $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$ into the appropriate expression above. This converts a purely structural reliability formula into a *time-dependent* mission reliability formula.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Parallel of Standby Subsystems (2-out-of-2 Active SB)

**Problem Setup:** A system consists of *two identical sub-systems* connected in *active parallel*. Each sub-system itself contains a primary unit ($P$) and a standby unit ($S$) with an *imperfect switch* ($R_{sw}$). Find the system reliability.

### Step 1 — Reliability of one Standby Sub-system (with imperfect switch)

Let $R_p$ = reliability of the primary unit, $R_s$ = reliability of the standby unit, $R_{sw}$ = reliability of the switch.

Conditioning on whether the switch operates correctly:

$$R_{SB,1} = R_{sw} \cdot P(\text{at least one of }P,S\text{ works} \vert \text{switch works}) + (1 - R_{sw}) \cdot P(\text{only }P\text{ works} \vert \text{switch fails})$$

When the switch works correctly, the sub-system becomes a parallel arrangement of $P$ and $S$:

$$P(\text{parallel works}) = 1 - (1 - R_p)(1 - R_s)$$

When the switch fails, the sub-system behaves as a pure series connection of just the primary $P$ (the standby is never engaged):

$$P(\text{only primary works}) = R_p$$

Substituting:

$$R_{SB,1} = R_{sw}\left[1 - (1 - R_p)(1 - R_s)\right] + (1 - R_{sw}) R_p$$

### Step 2 — Two Such Sub-systems in Active Parallel

Since the two sub-systems are in active parallel and statistically independent:

$$R_{sys} = 1 - (1 - R_{SB,1})^2$$

### Step 3 — Substituting $R_{SB,1}$ into the Parallel Formula

Let $A \equiv 1 - (1 - R_p)(1 - R_s) = R_p + R_s - R_p R_s$ for brevity.

Then:

$$R_{sys} = 1 - \left[1 - R_{sw}\, A - (1 - R_{sw}) R_p\right]^2$$

Expanding the square:

$$R_{sys} = 1 - 1 + 2\left[R_{sw}\, A + (1 - R_{sw}) R_p\right] - \left[R_{sw}\, A + (1 - R_{sw}) R_p\right]^2$$

$$\boxed{R_{sys} = 2\left[R_{sw}(R_p + R_s - R_p R_s) + (1 - R_{sw}) R_p\right] - \left[R_{sw}(R_p + R_s - R_p R_s) + (1 - R_{sw}) R_p\right]^2}$$

This is the **canonical closed-form reliability expression** for a 2-parallel-of-standby mixed system. KTU examiners expect the student to arrive at the equivalent *factored* form and the *expanded* form.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sectionalized Mixed System (Series of Active-Parallel Block + Standby Block)

**Problem Setup:** A system has three stages in series:
- **Stage A:** Two identical components in active parallel, each with reliability $R_A$.
- **Stage B:** A single component with reliability $R_B$ and one cold-standby component with reliability $R_C$ activated by a perfect switch.
- **Stage C:** Three identical components in active parallel, each with reliability $R_D$.

Mission time $t = 1$ year, constant failure rates $\lambda_A = 0.001$/hr, $\lambda_B = 0.0005$/hr, $\lambda_C = 0.0008$/hr, $\lambda_D = 0.0006$/hr. Compute $R_{sys}(t=8760\ \text{hr})$.

### Step 1 — Component Reliabilities at $t = 8760$ hr

$$R_A(8760) = e^{-0.001 \cdot 8760} = e^{-8.76} \approx 0.000156$$

$$R_B(8760) = e^{-0.0005 \cdot 8760} = e^{-4.38} \approx 0.01252$$

$$R_C(8760) = e^{-0.0008 \cdot 8760} = e^{-7.008} \approx 0.000889$$

$$R_D(8760) = e^{-0.0006 \cdot 8760} = e^{-5.256} \approx 0.005213$$

### Step 2 — Reliability of Each Block

**Block A** (2-parallel of $R_A$):

$$R_A^{block} = 1 - (1 - R_A)^2 = 2R_A - R_A^2 \approx 2(0.000156) - (0.000156)^2 \approx 0.000312$$

**Block B** (Standby of $R_B$ primary and $R_C$ standby, perfect switch):

$$R_B^{block} = 1 - (1 - R_B)(1 - R_C) = R_B + R_C - R_B R_C \approx 0.01252 + 0.000889 - 0.0000111 \approx 0.01340$$

**Block C** (3-parallel of $R_D$):

$$R_C^{block} = 1 - (1 - R_D)^3 = 1 - (0.994787)^3 \approx 1 - 0.98450 \approx 0.01550$$

### Step 3 — Series Combination of the Three Blocks

Since the three blocks are in series and statistically independent:

$$R_{sys} = R_A^{block} \cdot R_B^{block} \cdot R_C^{block}$$

$$R_{sys} \approx 0.000312 \times 0.01340 \times 0.01550$$

$$R_{sys} \approx 6.48 \times 10^{-8}$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The result $R_{sys} \approx 6.48 \times 10^{-8}$ (about 1 failure in 15.4 million missions) is *unacceptably low* for any safety-critical system. This illustrates the KTU 2024 pedagogical lesson: **component-level reliability dominates** — adding redundancy to extremely low-reliability components yields negligible improvement. The designer must first *upgrade components*, *then* add redundancy.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mixed System with Switching Failure Probability (General)

**General Conditional Form:** For a mixed system with switching device reliability $R_{sw}$ and primary component reliability $R_p$ and standby component reliability $R_s$:

$$R_{sys} = R_{sw} \cdot P(\text{parallel block functions}) + (1 - R_{sw}) \cdot P(\text{series-equivalent block functions})$$

Substituting the parallel and series probabilities:

$$R_{sw} \cdot \left[1 - (1 - R_p)(1 - R_s)\right] + (1 - R_{sw}) \cdot R_p$$

$$= R_{sw} - R_{sw}(1 - R_p)(1 - R_s) + R_p - R_{sw} R_p$$

$$= R_p + R_{sw} - R_{sw} R_p - R_{sw}(1 - R_p)(1 - R_s)$$

Factoring the last two terms:

$$= R_p + R_{sw}\left[1 - R_p - (1 - R_p)(1 - R_s)\right]$$

$$= R_p + R_{sw}(1 - R_p)\left[1 - (1 - R_s)\right]$$

$$\boxed{R_{sys} = R_p + R_{sw}(1 - R_p) R_s}$$

This elegant reduced form is the **most compact expression** for a 1-primary + 1-standby with imperfect switch. It clearly shows that:
- If $R_{sw} = 1$ (perfect switch) → $R_{sys} = 1 - (1 - R_p)(1 - R_s)$ (pure parallel)
- If $R_{sw} = 0$ (failed switch) → $R_{sys} = R_p$ (pure series, only primary)
- If $R_{sw} = 0.5$ → $R_{sys} = R_p + 0.5(1 - R_p) R_s$ (intermediate)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The compact form $R_{sys} = R_p + R_{sw}(1 - R_p) R_s$ is *always* the fastest path to full marks when the problem involves 1-primary + 1-standby with a single switch. Memorize it.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Reliability Block Diagram — Mixed Redundancy Topology

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **canonical mixed redundancy** architecture analyzed in Section 3.1. It depicts two parallel standby sub-systems (each containing a primary and a standby element joined by an imperfect switch).

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeInput[System Input] --> nodePA[Primary A]
    nodeInput --> nodePB[Primary B]
    nodePA --> nodeSwA{Imperfect Switch A}
    nodeSwA --> nodeSA[Standby A]
    nodePB --> nodeSwB{Imperfect Switch B}
    nodeSwB --> nodeSB[Standby B]
    nodeSA --> nodeOutput[System Output]
    nodeSB --> nodeOutput
    nodeSwA -.condition.-> nodeMathA[Probability R sw times parallel]
    nodeSwB -.condition.-> nodeMathB[Probability one minus R sw times primary only]
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- **Solid arrows** (→) represent normal signal/load flow.
- **Dotted arrows** (-.->) represent the logical *conditional probability* decomposition used in the analytical derivation.
- **Diamond nodes** (`{}`) represent the *imperfect switching device* — a binary-state random element.
- **Rectangular nodes** (`[]`) represent physical components or block boundaries.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sectionalized Mixed System (Section 3.2)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart[System Input] --> nodeStageA[Stage A: Active Parallel 2 of 2]
    nodeStageA --> nodeStageB[Stage B: Primary plus Cold Standby]
    nodeStageB --> nodeStageC[Stage C: Active Parallel 3 of 3]
    nodeStageC --> nodeEnd[System Output]
    nodeStageA -.R A block.-> nodeEqA[Two R A minus R A squared]
    nodeStageB -.R B block.-> nodeEqB[One minus one minus R B times one minus R C]
    nodeStageC -.R C block.-> nodeEqC[One minus one minus R D cubed]
    nodeEqA --> nodeSeries[Multiply all three block reliabilities]
    nodeEqB --> nodeSeries
    nodeEqC --> nodeSeries
    nodeSeries --> nodeFinal[R sys equals R A block times R B block times R C block]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — Decision Logic for Mixed Redundancy Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Sub systems] --> B{Is the sub system critical to life or mission?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Active Parallel Redundancy]
    B -- No --> D{Is the sub system needed only on primary failure?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Cold Standby Redundancy]
    D -- No --> F[Use Series Redundancy]
    C --> G[Connect sub systems in Mixed Topology]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute Block Reliabilities]
    H --> I[Apply Law of Total Probability]
    I --> J[Derive Closed Form R sys]
    J --> K{R sys greater than target reliability?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Design Accepted]
    K -- No --> M[Add Additional Redundancy or Improve Components]
    M --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use a functional flowchart?** — The Mermaid block-level architecture flow explicitly captures the *design decision process* that the engineer follows when applying mixed redundancy. This is the type of high-order reasoning KTU examiners reward under the *Analyse* and *Evaluate* levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define mixed redundancy. How does it differ from a purely parallel or purely standby redundant system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Mixed redundancy is a system reliability configuration that combines two or more redundancy techniques — typically *active parallel* and *standby* — within the same system architecture to achieve a target reliability that cannot be economically obtained by any single technique.
- **Differentiation (1 mark):** A pure parallel system has *all* components energized simultaneously and the system survives if *at least one* works. A pure standby system has *one* active component and *others* idle until switched in. A mixed system uses *both strategies* in different sub-assemblies.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the role of an imperfect switching device in a mixed redundant system and write the general reliability expression involving the switch.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role of Switch (1.5 marks):** The switch couples the standby unit to the load *only upon failure of the primary*. Its reliability $R_{sw}$ directly modulates how often the standby is successfully brought online.
- **General Expression (1.5 marks):** For a 1-primary + 1-standby mixed configuration: $R_{sys} = R_{sw}[1 - (1-R_p)(1-R_s)] + (1-R_{sw})R_p = R_p + R_{sw}(1-R_p)R_s$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module 2 Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

A control system consists of three stages connected in series. Stage 1 has *two identical components in active parallel* (each with reliability $R_1 = 0.9$). Stage 2 has *one active component* (reliability $R_2 = 0.85$) backed by *one cold-standby component* (reliability $R_3 = 0.8$) with a *switching device* of reliability $R_{sw} = 0.95$. Stage 3 has *three identical components in active parallel* (each with reliability $R_4 = 0.92$).

Compute the system reliability at mission end. Also determine the system reliability if the switch in Stage 2 fails completely.

#### (a) Compute the system reliability (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Stage 1 Block Reliability (2-parallel of $R_1$):**

$$R_{S1} = 1 - (1 - R_1)^2 = 1 - (0.1)^2 = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$

**Step 2 — Stage 2 Block Reliability (Primary + Standby with imperfect switch):**

Using the compact form derived in Section 3.3:

$$R_{S2} = R_2 + R_{sw}(1 - R_2) R_3 = 0.85 + 0.95(1 - 0.85)(0.8)$$

$$R_{S2} = 0.85 + 0.95 \cdot 0.15 \cdot 0.8 = 0.85 + 0.114 = 0.964$$

**Step 3 — Stage 3 Block Reliability (3-parallel of $R_4$):**

$$R_{S3} = 1 - (1 - R_4)^3 = 1 - (0.08)^3 = 1 - 0.000512 = 0.999488$$

**Step 4 — System Reliability (Series of three blocks):**

$$R_{sys} = R_{S1} \cdot R_{S2} \cdot R_{S3} = 0.99 \times 0.964 \times 0.999488$$

$$R_{sys} \approx 0.9536$$

#### (b) Compute the system reliability if the switch fails completely (7 marks)

**Step 1 — When the switch fails completely, $R_{sw} = 0$.** Stage 2 reduces to a series connection of *only the primary* (the standby is never engaged).

$$R_{S2,failed} = R_2 = 0.85$$

**Step 2 — Stage 1 and Stage 3 remain unchanged:**

$$R_{S1} = 0.99, \quad R_{S3} = 0.999488$$

**Step 3 — Recompute system reliability:**

$$R_{sys,failed} = 0.99 \times 0.85 \times 0.999488 \approx 0.8407$$

**Step 4 — Reliability Penalty Analysis:**

$$\Delta R = 0.9536 - 0.8407 = 0.1129\ (\text{a } 11.29\%\ \text{drop in mission reliability})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often *forget to substitute the switch-failed case* into the compact form. They incorrectly write $R_{S2} = 0.85 \times 0.8 = 0.68$ (treating failed switch as a series *with the standby*). The correct interpretation is that a failed switch *disables* the standby entirely, so the block reduces to the primary alone: $R_{S2} = R_2 = 0.85$. Loses 2 marks.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

A communication transmitter uses a **mixed redundant** power amplifier chain. The chain consists of *two identical sub-systems* connected in **active parallel**. Each sub-system has a primary amplifier (reliability $R_p = 0.92$) backed by a cold-standby amplifier (reliability $R_s = 0.88$) with a perfect switch.

**(a)** Derive the closed-form reliability expression of the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If each amplifier has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0002$/hr, determine the system reliability for a mission time of $t = 1000$ hours. **(7 marks)**

#### (a) Derivation of closed-form reliability (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Reliability of one sub-system (primary + perfect-switch standby):**

With $R_{sw} = 1$, the compact form reduces to:

$$R_{SB,1} = 1 - (1 - R_p)(1 - R_s) = R_p + R_s - R_p R_s$$

Substituting:

$$R_{SB,1} = 0.92 + 0.88 - (0.92)(0.88) = 1.80 - 0.8096 = 0.9904$$

**Step 2 — Two such sub-systems in active parallel:**

$$R_{sys} = 1 - (1 - R_{SB,1})^2 = 1 - (1 - 0.9904)^2 = 1 - (0.0096)^2$$

$$R_{sys} = 1 - 0.00009216 = 0.99990784$$

**Step 3 — Closed-form symbolic expression:**

$$\boxed{R_{sys} = 1 - \left[1 - \left(R_p + R_s - R_p R_s\right)\right]^2 = 1 - \left[(1 - R_p)(1 - R_s)\right]^2}$$

This is the canonical **parallel-of-standby** expression.

#### (b) Time-dependent mission reliability (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Component reliability at $t = 1000$ hr:**

$$R_p(1000) = e^{-0.0002 \cdot 1000} = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187$$

$$R_s(1000) = e^{-0.0002 \cdot 1000} = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187$$

**Step 2 — Standby sub-system reliability at $t = 1000$ hr:**

$$R_{SB,1}(1000) = 1 - (1 - 0.8187)^2 = 1 - (0.1813)^2 = 1 - 0.03287 = 0.9671$$

**Step 3 — System reliability (parallel of 2 standby sub-systems):**

$$R_{sys}(1000) = 1 - (1 - 0.9671)^2 = 1 - (0.0329)^2 = 1 - 0.001082 = 0.998918$$

$$\boxed{R_{sys}(1000\ \text{hr}) \approx 0.9989\ (\text{i.e., } 99.89\%)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** A common error is computing $R_p(t)$ and $R_s(t)$ using the *wrong* failure rate (e.g., using $\lambda$ per *minute* instead of per *hour*). Always verify units. Loses 2 marks. Another common error: forgetting the *square* on the parallel sub-systems (writing $R_{sys} = 0.9671$ instead of $0.9989$). Loses 3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Mixed Redundancy**

- [x] **Definition:** Mixed redundancy = combination of *parallel* + *standby* in one system architecture.
- [x] **Why use it:** Cost optimization, mission-criticality differentiation, weight/space constraints in aerospace.
- [x] **Compact 1+1 standby formula with imperfect switch:** $R_{sys} = R_p + R_{sw}(1 - R_p) R_s$ — *memorize this*.
- [x] **Perfect-switch limit:** $R_{sw} = 1 \Rightarrow R_{sys} = 1 - (1 - R_p)(1 - R_s)$.
- [x] **Failed-switch limit:** $R_{sw} = 0 \Rightarrow R_{sys} = R_p$ (standby disabled).
- [x] **Parallel-of-Standby formula:** $R_{sys} = 1 - [(1 - R_p)(1 - R_s)]^m$ for $m$ identical SB blocks in active parallel.
- [x] **Standby-of-Parallel formula:** $R_{sys} = R_{sw}[1 - (1 - R)^n] + (1 - R_{sw}) R^r$ for r/n voting with 1 standby.
- [x] **Sectionalized Mixed Rule:** Series-of-blocks reliability = *product* of block reliabilities.
- [x] **Time-Domain Rule:** For exponential distribution, $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$; substitute into the appropriate structural formula.
- [x] **Dominant Failure Rule:** Redundancy cannot rescue extremely low component reliability — fix components first.
- [x] **MTBF Rule:** For a mixed system with $R_{sys}(t)$, $MTBF = \int_0^\infty R_{sys}(t)\, dt$ (repairable case).
- [x] **Common pitfall:** A failed switch disables the standby, it does *not* put it in series with the primary.
- [x] **Common pitfall:** Unit-check failure rates ($\lambda$ units must match $t$ units) — major mark-loser.
- [x] **KTU cognitive level:** Mixed Redundancy problems are typically tagged at *Apply* (Part A) and *Analyse/Evaluate* (Part B) on Bloom's Taxonomy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Redundancy Optimization; State-Dependent Systems: Markov Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Redundancy Optimization; State-Dependent Systems: Markov Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Redundancy Optimization – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Redundancy Optimization** is a branch of reliability engineering concerned with the **optimal allocation of redundant components** to a system so as to achieve a target reliability (or availability) measure at **minimum cost, weight, or volume**, OR to **maximize reliability** under fixed resource constraints.

Mathematically, redundancy optimization is the discipline of solving constrained integer (and sometimes mixed-integer) optimization problems where the decision variables represent the *number of redundant components* in each subsystem.

The two canonical formulations used across KTU board questions are:

1. **Cost-Constrained Reliability Maximization (Max-R / Cost-C):**

$$\max \; R_s(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i x_i \le C, \quad 1 \le x_i \le m_i, \; x_i \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$$

2. **Reliability-Constrained Cost Minimization (Min-C / R-C):**

$$\min \; C(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i x_i \quad \text{subject to} \quad R_s(\mathbf{x}) \ge R_0, \quad 1 \le x_i \le m_i, \; x_i \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$$

Here $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ is the vector of redundancy levels, $c_i$ is the cost of adding one unit to subsystem $i$, $C$ is the total cost budget, $R_0$ is the minimum acceptable system reliability, and $m_i$ is the upper limit on $x_i$ (due to space, weight, or technology).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Both formulations are examinable. The Min-C formulation is most frequently tested in Part B (14-mark) questions because it directly maps to Integer Programming and Dynamic Programming, both of which are in the official syllabus.

### 1.2 State-Dependent Systems – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **State-Dependent System** is a system whose future evolution depends only on its **present state**, not on the history of how it reached that state. The corresponding mathematical abstraction is a **Markov Process** (or Markov Chain in discrete time, Markov Process in continuous time). In reliability engineering, the *state* typically enumerates which components are currently working, failed, or under repair.

For such a system, the probability of moving to the next state in the next instant depends *only* on the current state. This is captured by the **memoryless / Markov property**:

$$P\!\left[X(t+\Delta t) = j \mid X(t)=i,\; X(s)\; \forall s < t\right] \;=\; P\!\left[X(t+\Delta t)=j \mid X(t)=i\right]$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Redundancy Optimization Analogy – The Indian Train Journey:**
Imagine a long train journey from Kerala to Delhi. The train (the system) must reach its destination. You have a finite luggage budget and a tight schedule. You may carry one, two, or three spare bottles of water (redundancy) per coach. Adding more spares costs money and space, but reduces the risk of "thirst failure." Redundancy optimization is the *rational* way of deciding: carry 2 spares in the AC coach (high failure cost) and only 1 in the general coach. You are *budgeting reliability*.

**State-Dependent System Analogy – The Traffic Light:**
A traffic light at a junction only "knows" its current colour (Red / Yellow / Green). Whether it was previously red for 5 seconds or 30 seconds does not affect the probability of transitioning to the next colour. The light is a **state-dependent system**. Similarly, a two-component repairable system "knows" only whether both components work, one works, or both are down — and that information is sufficient to predict the near-term future.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used in KTU 2024 Question Papers:**
> - $\lambda$ (lambda) – constant **failure rate** (failures/hour)
> - $\mu$ (mu) – constant **repair rate** (repairs/hour)
> - $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ – **reliability** of a single non-repairable component
> - $A(t)$ – **instantaneous availability**; $A(\infty)$ – **steady-state availability**
> - $MTTF$ – **mean time to failure**; $MTTR$ – **mean time to repair**
> - $q = \lambda / (\lambda + \mu)$ – **unavailability** (steady-state)
> - $p = \mu / (\lambda + \mu)$ – **availability** (steady-state for a single unit)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Redundancy (KTU 2024 Emphasis)

| Redundancy Type | Description | Cost-to-Reliability Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Active (Parallel) Redundancy** | All redundant units operate simultaneously. System fails only when *all* units fail. | High cost, high reliability gain at low redundancy levels. |
| **Standby Redundancy** | Redundant units are idle until the active unit fails; a *perfect* switchover is assumed for simplicity. | Lower operating stress; higher reliability gain per added unit. |
| **Component (Element) Redundancy** | Redundancy added at the level of individual components within each subsystem. | Local optimization. |
| **System Redundancy** | Entire subsystems are replicated in parallel. | Global optimization; cheaper in some topologies. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the KTU board exam, **active parallel redundancy** is the default unless the question explicitly says "standby." The reliability formulas below all use the active-parallel assumption.

### 2.2 Reliability of Common Redundant Configurations

For $n$ identical components in **active parallel**, each with reliability $R_i$:

$$R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$$

For $n$ **identical** components with reliability $R$ each:

$$R_s = 1 - (1 - R)^n$$

For a **series** system of $n$ stages (no redundancy):

$$R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

For a **series-parallel** system with $n_i$ parallel components in stage $i$:

$$R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{k} \left[\,1 - (1-R_i)^{n_i}\,\right]$$

### 2.3 The General Redundancy Optimization Problem

A system of $k$ subsystems is connected in series. Subsystem $i$ has reliability $R_i(x_i)$ when equipped with $x_i$ redundant identical components (including the original). The redundancy level $x_i$ takes integer values in $[1, m_i]$. The **system reliability** is then:

$$R_s(\mathbf{x}) = \prod_{i=1}^{k} R_i(x_i)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this a hard problem?** Although $R_s(\mathbf{x})$ is separable in $\mathbf{x}$ (i.e., it factors into a product), the **constraint set is coupled** through the cost/weight inequality. This makes the problem non-trivial and rules out simple closed-form solutions — which is exactly why KTU requires *Integer Programming* or *Dynamic Programming* methods.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Redundancy Optimization & Markov Analysis**

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Constraints / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $R_s(\mathbf{x}) = \prod_i R_i(x_i)$ | System reliability (series configuration) | $x_i \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$, $1 \le x_i \le m_i$ |
| $R_i(x_i) = 1 - (1 - R_i)^{x_i}$ | Reliability of subsystem $i$ with $x_i$ parallel units | Active parallel, identical units |
| $\sum_i c_i x_i \le C$ | Cost constraint | Linear in $x_i$ |
| $f_k^{\*}(C) = \max_{1 \le x_k \le m_k} \{ R_k(x_k) \cdot f_{k-1}^{\*}(C - c_k x_k) \}$ | **Dynamic programming recursion** | Used when cost constraint is binding |
| $P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i) = P_{ij}$ | One-step transition probability | Discrete-time Markov chain |
| $P_{ij}(\Delta t) = q_{ij}\,\Delta t + o(\Delta t)$ | Transition rate from state $i$ to $j$ | Continuous-time Markov chain |
| $P_j(t) = \sum_i P_i(0)\,P_{ij}(t)$ | Chapman–Kolmogorov equation | Vector form: $\mathbf{P}(t) = \mathbf{P}(0)\,\mathbf{P}(t)$ |
| $p_i \, q_{ij} = p_j \, q_{ji}$ | **Detailed balance** (steady state) | Holds only for reversible chains |
| $A(\infty) = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ | Steady-state availability of one unit | $MTTF = 1/\lambda$, $MTTR = 1/\mu$ |
| $A(\infty) = \dfrac{\text{uptime rate}}{\text{uptime rate + downtime rate}}$ | Generic steady-state availability | $A = \dfrac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + 1/\mu}$ |
| $MTTF = \int_0^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$ | Mean time to failure | $R(t)$ is unrepaired system reliability |

### 2.5 Markov Analysis — Why It Matters

Markov analysis is essential whenever a system has:
- **Repairable components** (the state space is closed — the system can return to operational states).
- **Dependencies on operating conditions** (e.g., failure rate that depends on load/temperature — *state-dependent failure rates*).
- **Redundancy with imperfect switching** (states track which unit is active).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Markov models power the reliability block of railway signalling systems, power-grid substations, and aerospace propulsion systems. In KTU examinations, you will mostly encounter **two-component** and **three-component** repairable systems with constant $\lambda$ and $\mu$.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Reliability of $n$ Identical Components in Active Parallel

**Step 1 —** Let each component fail independently with probability $q = 1 - R$. The probability that a *specific* set of all $n$ components fails is $q^n$.

**Step 2 —** The system survives if *at least one* component works. The complement is "all $n$ fail":

$$\text{System fails} \iff \text{All } n \text{ components fail} \iff \bigcap_{i=1}^{n} (\text{component } i \text{ fails})$$

**Step 3 —** By independence:

$$P(\text{system fails}) = q \cdot q \cdot \ldots \cdot q = q^n = (1 - R)^n$$

**Step 4 —** Therefore:

$$R_s = 1 - P(\text{system fails}) = 1 - (1-R)^n \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting behaviour:** As $n \to \infty$, $R_s \to 1$ if $R > 0$. However, the marginal benefit of adding one more component is $\Delta R = (1-R)^{n-1} \cdot R$, which **decreases rapidly** as $n$ grows. This is the *diminishing-returns* behaviour that motivates redundancy *optimization* (rather than unbounded redundancy).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State Availability of a Single Repairable Unit

Consider one component alternating between UP (state 1) and DOWN (state 2). The transition rate from UP → DOWN is $\lambda$; from DOWN → UP is $\mu$.

**Step 1 —** Set up the steady-state balance equations. Let $P_1$ = probability the unit is UP, $P_2$ = probability the unit is DOWN.

**Step 2 —** In the steady state, the rate of flow into state 1 must equal the rate of flow out of state 1:

$$\mu P_2 = \lambda P_1$$

This is the **rate balance** (also called the equilibrium or detailed-balance equation for a two-state system).

**Step 3 —** Use the conservation law:

$$P_1 + P_2 = 1$$

**Step 4 —** Solve the linear system. From Step 2, $P_2 = (\lambda / \mu) P_1$. Substituting into Step 3:

$$P_1 + \frac{\lambda}{\mu} P_1 = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; P_1 \left(1 + \frac{\lambda}{\mu}\right) = 1$$

$$\Rightarrow\; P_1 = \frac{1}{1 + \lambda/\mu} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Step 5 —** Steady-state availability $A(\infty) \equiv P_1$:

$$A(\infty) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + 1/\mu} = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Two Identical Parallel Components with Repair (Full Markov Treatment)

**System description:** Two identical components operate in **active parallel**. Each fails with rate $\lambda$ when working. A single repair facility repairs a failed component with rate $\mu$. The system works as long as at least one component is up.

**Step 1 — Identify the states.**

| State | Description |
|---|---|
| 0 | Both components UP |
| 1 | Exactly one component UP, the other under repair |
| 2 | Both components DOWN (system failed) |

**Step 2 — Draw transition rates.**

From state 0: one of the two components may fail → state 1. Total rate = $2\lambda$ (two components, each with rate $\lambda$).

From state 1: the working component may fail → state 2 at rate $\lambda$. The failed component may be repaired → state 0 at rate $\mu$.

From state 2: the repair facility may restore one component → state 1 at rate $\mu$.

**Step 3 — Steady-state balance equations.** Let $P_0, P_1, P_2$ be the steady-state probabilities. The rate balance for each state is:

$$\text{Rate into state } i = \text{Rate out of state } i$$

For state 0:

$$\mu P_1 = 2\lambda P_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_1 = \frac{2\lambda}{\mu} P_0$$

For state 1:

$$2\lambda P_0 + \mu P_2 = (\lambda + \mu) P_1$$

For state 2:

$$\lambda P_1 = \mu P_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_2 = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} P_1$$

Conservation: $P_0 + P_1 + P_2 = 1$.

**Step 4 — Solve by substitution.** Let $r = \lambda / \mu$ (dimensionless failure-to-repair ratio). Then:

$$P_1 = 2r P_0, \quad P_2 = r P_1 = 2r^2 P_0$$

Substituting into the conservation law:

$$P_0 (1 + 2r + 2r^2) = 1$$

$$\Rightarrow P_0 = \frac{1}{1 + 2r + 2r^2}$$

**Step 5 — System availability.** The system is UP in states 0 and 1:

$$A_s = P_0 + P_1 = P_0 (1 + 2r) = \frac{1 + 2r}{1 + 2r + 2r^2}$$

Expanding in $\lambda, \mu$:

$$A_s = \frac{1 + 2\lambda/\mu}{1 + 2\lambda/\mu + 2(\lambda/\mu)^2} = \frac{\mu^2 + 2\lambda\mu}{\mu^2 + 2\lambda\mu + 2\lambda^2} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check (limits):**
> - If $\mu \to \infty$ (instant repair), $A_s \to 1$. ✓
> - If $\lambda \to \infty$, $A_s \to 0$. ✓
> - If $r = 1$ ($\lambda = \mu$): $A_s = 3 / 5 = 0.6$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: System Unreliability with State-Dependent Failure Rates

Suppose a single component has failure rate $\lambda(s)$ that **depends on the current system state** $s$ (e.g., a component under over-stress). If the system resides in state $s$ for a random time $T_s$ during its mission of length $t$, then:

**Step 1 —** Conditional unreliability (probability of failure before mission end):

$$F_s = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\int_0^{T_s} \lambda(s)\,ds\right)$$

**Step 2 —** If the mission is a single uninterrupted phase, $T_s = t$ and the integral simplifies to $\lambda(s)\,t$ for a constant rate.

**Step 3 —** When the system transitions between states, the overall unreliability is computed by **summing failure probabilities along each path** in the state-transition diagram, weighted by the probability of traversing that path. This is exactly what the **Markov transition matrix** does automatically:

$$\mathbf{R}(t) = 1 - \mathbf{P}(t)\mathbf{1}_{\text{UP}}$$

where $\mathbf{P}(t)$ is the matrix of state probabilities at time $t$ and $\mathbf{1}_{\text{UP}}$ is an indicator vector of operational states.

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Configuration | Active Parallel Reliability | Steady-State Availability (with repair) |
|---|---|---|
| Single unit | $R$ | $\dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ |
| 2 parallel units | $1 - (1-R)^2$ | $\dfrac{\mu^2 + 2\lambda\mu}{\mu^2 + 2\lambda\mu + 2\lambda^2}$ |
| 2 units, one standby (no repair) | $1 - (1-R)^2$ | N/A |
| $n$ parallel units | $1 - (1-R)^n$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 + n(\lambda/\mu) + \binom{n}{2}(\lambda/\mu)^2 + \ldots}$ |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Redundancy Optimization Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT LAYER: System Specification"] --> A1["Mission time t"]
    A --> A2["Subsystem reliabilities R_i"]
    A --> A3["Cost per redundant unit c_i"]
    A --> A4["Resource budget C or target R_0"]
    A1 --> B["FORMULATION STAGE"]
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    A4 --> B
    B --> B1["Max R_s given cost"]
    B --> B2["Min cost given R_s"]
    B1 --> C["SOLVER LAYER"]
    B2 --> C
    C --> C1["Integer Programming Branch and Bound"]
    C --> C2["Dynamic Programming Bellman Recursion"]
    C --> C3["Lagrangian Relaxation for tight constraints"]
    C1 --> D["OUTPUT: Optimal Redundancy Vector x*"]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E["VALIDATION: Compute R_s(x*) and total cost C(x*)"]
    E --> F["DEPLOYMENT: Physical Allocation of Redundant Units"]
```

### 4.2 Markov State Diagram — Two Parallel Repairable Units

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> state0
    state0 : STATE 0 Both components UP
    state1 : STATE 1 One UP, One under repair
    state2 : STATE 2 Both DOWN System Failed
    state0 --> state1 : rate equals 2 times lambda
    state1 --> state0 : rate equals mu
    state1 --> state2 : rate equals lambda
    state2 --> state1 : rate equals mu
    state2 --> [*]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each arrow is labelled with the exponential rate at which the transition occurs. For example, from state 0, the system leaves at total rate $2\lambda$ (because either of the two working components can fail). This is the diagram that drives Derivation 3 above.

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Markov Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1: Enumerate all mutually exclusive states of the system"] --> S2["STEP 2: Identify UP states vs DOWN states"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3: Determine transition rates between every pair of states"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4: Write the balance equation for each state"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5: Apply the conservation law: sum of all P_i equals 1"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6: Solve the linear system to obtain P_i for all i"]
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7: Compute A infinity equals sum of P_i over UP states"]
    S6 --> S8["STEP 8: Compute MTTF for unrepaired mission if required"]
    S7 --> OUT["OUTPUT: Steady-state availability A infinity"]
    S8 --> OUT
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Optimization Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Problem given: maximize R_s or minimize cost"] --> Q1{"Is the objective separable and constraint linear?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q2{"Is the search space small (n m_i less than about 50)?"}
    Q1 -- "No (e.g. nonlinear) Genetic Algorithm" --> GA["Use heuristic: GA / Simulated Annealing"]
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> IP["Use Integer Programming enumeration or Branch and Bound"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> DP["Use Dynamic Programming Bellman recursion stage by stage"]
    IP --> SOL["Solution x* and optimum value"]
    DP --> SOL
    GA --> SOL
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read the decision tree in an exam:** If the question gives you a system with **3 or 4 stages** of series subsystems and asks for the *minimum cost* to achieve a target $R_0$, you should use **Dynamic Programming**, computing the optimal cost function stage by stage. If the question has only **2 stages**, **enumeration** (checking all $(m_1)(m_2)$ combinations) is faster and earns full marks.
