## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Q1(a)]**

**Q1. Define redundancy optimization. Differentiate between active and standby redundancy.**

**Model Answer:**

Redundancy optimization is the process of selecting the *number* and *type* of redundant components for each subsystem so as to either maximize system reliability for a fixed cost or minimize cost for a fixed reliability target.

| Feature | Active Redundancy | Standby Redundancy |
|---|---|---|
| Operating state of spares | All units energized and working | Spare units idle, not energized |
| Switching | Not required | Required (assumed perfect for KTU) |
| Failure rate of spares | Equal to the primary | Effectively zero (no stress) |
| Reliability gain | Smaller per added unit | Larger per added unit |
| Typical formula | $1 - (1-R)^n$ | $1 + nR$ for small $R$ approximation |

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q1(b)]**

**Q2. State the Markov property. Why is it useful in reliability analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Markov property** states that the future probabilistic evolution of a process depends only on its present state, not on its past history. Formally:

$$P[X(t+\Delta t) = j \mid X(t) = i, X(s)\; \forall s < t] = P[X(t+\Delta t) = j \mid X(t) = i]$$

**Why it is useful in reliability analysis:**

- It permits a *finite-state* representation of arbitrarily complex repairable systems.
- Steady-state availability can be obtained by solving a system of linear algebraic equations, avoiding the need to solve stiff ODEs.
- It accommodates **state-dependent failure rates**, which classical exponential models cannot.
- It seamlessly handles **repair, standby, and switching** phenomena.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2, Q2]**

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** A system consists of three subsystems connected in series. The reliabilities of the individual components are $R_1 = 0.9$, $R_2 = 0.85$, $R_3 = 0.8$. The costs of adding one redundant unit to each subsystem are $c_1 = 2$, $c_2 = 3$, $c_3 = 1$. The total budget is $C = 7$. The maximum number of additional components per subsystem is $m_1 = m_2 = m_3 = 3$. Formulate the problem and find the optimal redundancy allocation using **enumeration / integer programming**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the optimal allocation found in part (a), compute the system reliability. Comment on the result.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Formulation:**

Maximize: $R_s(x_1, x_2, x_3) = [1 - (0.1)^{x_1}] \cdot [1 - (0.15)^{x_2}] \cdot [1 - (0.2)^{x_3}]$

Subject to: $2x_1 + 3x_2 + x_3 \le 7$, $1 \le x_i \le 3$, $x_i \in \mathbb{Z}^+$.

**Enumerate feasible integer combinations:**

> [!NOTE]
> Enumeration: for $m_1 = m_2 = m_3 = 3$, there are at most $3^3 = 27$ candidates. The cost constraint removes most of them.

We systematically list all integer triples $(x_1, x_2, x_3)$ with cost $2x_1 + 3x_2 + x_3 \le 7$ and compute $R_s$:

| $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $x_3$ | Cost $2x_1+3x_2+x_3$ | Feasible? | $R_1$ | $R_2$ | $R_3$ | $R_s$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 6 | Yes | 0.9000 | 0.8500 | 0.8000 | 0.6120 |
| 1 | 1 | 2 | 7 | Yes | 0.9000 | 0.8500 | 0.9600 | 0.7344 |
| 1 | 1 | 3 | 8 | No | — | — | — | — |
| 1 | 2 | 1 | 8 | No | — | — | — | — |
| 2 | 1 | 1 | 8 | No | — | — | — | — |
| 1 | 1 | 2 | 7 | Yes (repeat) | — | — | — | 0.7344 |
| 1 | 2 | 2 | 9 | No | — | — | — | — |
| 2 | 1 | 2 | 10 | No | — | — | — | — |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 10 | No | — | — | — | — |
| 1 | 2 | 1 | 8 | No | — | — | — | — |
| 2 | 1 | 1 | 8 | No | — | — | — | — |
| 1 | 1 | 2 | 7 | Yes (best so far) | — | — | — | 0.7344 |
| 1 | 1 | 3 | 8 | No | — | — | — | — |
| 2 | 1 | 2 | 10 | No | — | — | — | — |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 10 | No | — | — | — | — |
| 1 | 2 | 2 | 9 | No | — | — | — | — |
| 2 | 1 | 1 | 8 | No | — | — | — | — |
| 2 | 1 | 2 | 10 | No | — | — | — | — |
| 1 | 1 | 2 | 7 | Yes (optimal) | — | — | — | 0.7344 |
| 1 | 2 | 1 | 8 | No | — | — | — | — |

**Other feasible candidates to check:**

- $(x_1, x_2, x_3) = (2, 1, 1)$: cost = 6, feasible, $R_s = [1 - 0.01] \cdot 0.85 \cdot 0.8 = 0.99 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 0.8 = 0.6732$.
- $(x_1, x_2, x_3) = (3, 1, 1)$: cost = 8, infeasible.
- $(x_1, x_2, x_3) = (1, 1, 2)$: cost = 7, feasible, $R_s = 0.9 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 0.96 = 0.7344$.
- $(x_1, x_2, x_3) = (1, 1, 1)$: cost = 6, feasible, $R_s = 0.612$.

> [!TIP]
> **Computational shortcut — Systematically generated full list (cost $\le 7$):**
> $(1,1,1), (2,1,1), (1,1,2), (1,1,3), (3,1,1), (1,2,1), (2,1,2), (1,2,2), (2,2,1), (3,1,2), (1,3,1)$. Filtering by cost $\le 7$ keeps only: $(1,1,1), (2,1,1), (1,1,2)$. The maximum is at $(1,1,2)$.

**Result:** The optimal redundancy vector is $\mathbf{x}^{*} = (1, 1, 2)$.

**Part (b) — Reliability and commentary:**

The optimal system reliability is:

$$R_s^{*} = (1 - 0.1^1)(1 - 0.15^1)(1 - 0.2^2) = 0.9 \times 0.85 \times 0.96 = 0.7344$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commentary:** The optimizer chose to invest redundancy in **subsystem 3**, the *cheapest* to upgrade (cost = 1) but with the *lowest baseline reliability* (0.8). The fact that adding one extra unit to subsystem 3 changes its reliability from 0.8 → 0.96 (a 20% gain) at a cost of just 1 is more efficient than upgrading subsystem 1 (cost 2, gain 0.9 → 0.99) or subsystem 2 (cost 3, gain 0.85 → 0.9775). This demonstrates the **cost-reliability trade-off** central to redundancy optimization.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** A system has two identical components operating in active parallel. Each component has a constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.01$/hour and a constant repair rate $\mu = 0.5$/hour. A single repair facility is shared. Draw the **state transition diagram** and compute the **steady-state availability** of the system.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the mission time is $t = 100$ hours and no repair is permitted (components are non-repairable during the mission), find the **system reliability** using the active-parallel formula and the **MTTF** of the system.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Markov analysis with repair:**

**State 0:** Both components UP. **State 1:** One UP, one under repair. **State 2:** Both DOWN.

**Transition rates:**
- $0 \to 1$: total rate $2\lambda = 0.02$/hr (either of the two working components can fail).
- $1 \to 0$: rate $\mu = 0.5$/hr (failed component is repaired).
- $1 \to 2$: rate $\lambda = 0.01$/hr (the remaining good component fails).
- $2 \to 1$: rate $\mu = 0.5$/hr (one of the failed components is repaired).

**Steady-state balance equations:**

$$2\lambda P_0 = \mu P_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_1 = \frac{2\lambda}{\mu} P_0 = \frac{0.02}{0.5} P_0 = 0.04 P_0$$

$$\lambda P_1 = \mu P_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_2 = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} P_1 = \frac{0.01}{0.5} \cdot 0.04 P_0 = 0.02 \cdot 0.04 P_0 = 0.0008 P_0$$

**Conservation:** $P_0 + P_1 + P_2 = 1$:

$$P_0 (1 + 0.04 + 0.0008) = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; P_0 = \frac{1}{1.0408} = 0.9608$$

$$P_1 = 0.04 \times 0.9608 = 0.03843$$

$$P_2 = 0.0008 \times 0.9608 = 0.000769$$

**Steady-state availability (system is UP in states 0 and 1):**

$$A(\infty) = P_0 + P_1 = 0.9608 + 0.03843 = 0.9992$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-check using the closed-form result from Derivation 3:**
> $A_s = (\mu^2 + 2\lambda\mu) / (\mu^2 + 2\lambda\mu + 2\lambda^2) = (0.25 + 0.01)/(0.25 + 0.01 + 0.0002) = 0.26/0.2602 = 0.9992$. ✓

**Part (b) — Mission reliability without repair:**

Without repair, the failure rate of each component is $\lambda = 0.01$/hr. The reliability of a single component over $t = 100$ hr is:

$$R = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.01 \times 100} = e^{-1} = 0.3679$$

Active parallel reliability:

$$R_s(t) = 1 - (1 - R)^2 = 1 - (1 - 0.3679)^2 = 1 - (0.6321)^2 = 1 - 0.3995 = 0.6005$$

**MTTF of the system:**

For a non-repairable system, $MTTF_s = \int_0^{\infty} R_s(t)\, dt$. For two parallel components each with exponential life:

$$MTTF_s = \int_0^{\infty} [1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^2] dt = \int_0^{\infty} [2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}] dt$$

$$= 2 \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{2\lambda} = \frac{2}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{2\lambda} = \frac{4 - 1}{2\lambda} = \frac{3}{2\lambda}$$

Substituting $\lambda = 0.01$/hr:

$$MTTF_s = \frac{3}{2 \times 0.01} = \frac{3}{0.02} = 150 \text{ hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** For a single component, $MTTF = 1/\lambda = 100$ hr. Two in parallel gives $MTTF = 150$ hr, a 50% improvement — less than the *doubling* one might naively expect, because both components share the same exponential life distribution.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Redundancy Optimization & Markov Analysis (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\lambda$ and $\mu$ in transition rates.** $\lambda$ is the *failure* rate (UP → DOWN); $\mu$ is the *repair* rate (DOWN → UP). Inverting these will give $A(\infty) > 1$, which the examiner marks as "conceptual error — 2 marks deducted."
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $2\lambda$ factor.** When two components are working, the *total* rate of leaving the all-UP state is $2\lambda$, not $\lambda$. Students who write $P_0$ rate balance as $\lambda P_0 = \mu P_1$ lose 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Treating standby as parallel.** Standby redundancy assumes the idle unit has *zero* failure rate. If a question says "standby," you MUST change the from-state-0 transition rate to $\lambda$ (not $2\lambda$), because the spare is not energized.
>
> 4. **Using continuous decision variables in redundancy problems.** $x_i$ must be a *positive integer*. Reporting $x_2 = 1.37$ as optimal is a fatal error. Round *up* the value of $x$ that *satisfies* the constraint; never interpolate.
>
> 5. **Omitting the conservation law.** The balance equations alone are under-determined. You MUST write $P_0 + P_1 + \ldots + P_n = 1$ before solving. Examiners specifically check for this line.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Redundancy Optimization & Markov Analysis**

- **Redundancy Optimization** allocates extra components to subsystems to meet a reliability or cost target. Two formulations: **Max-R / Cost-C** and **Min-C / R-C**.
- **Active parallel reliability** of $n$ identical units: $R_s = 1 - (1 - R)^n$.
- **Series system reliability** of $k$ stages: $R_s = \prod_i R_i$.
- **Standby redundancy** (perfect switch, identical components): $R_s = 1 - (1-R)\prod_{k=2}^{n}(1 - R \cdot \text{switch reliability}_{k-1})$.
- **Dynamic programming recursion** for the cost-constrained problem:
  $f_k^{\*}(C) = \max_{1 \le x_k \le m_k} \left\{ R_k(x_k) \cdot f_{k-1}^{\*}(C - c_k x_k) \right\}$.
- **Markov property**: future state depends only on the present state, not on the past.
- **Two-state availability** of a single unit: $A(\infty) = \mu / (\lambda + \mu) = MTTF / (MTTF + MTTR)$.
- **Two-unit parallel with repair** (Derivation 3 closed form):
  $A_s = (\mu^2 + 2\lambda\mu) / (\mu^2 + 2\lambda\mu + 2\lambda^2)$.
- **Two-unit parallel MTTF** (no repair): $MTTF_s = 3/(2\lambda)$.
- **Transition rate from "all UP" state** with $k$ working components: $k\lambda$ (in active parallel).
- **Steady-state balance** for state $i$: $\sum_{j} q_{ji} P_j = \sum_{j} q_{ij} P_i$ (rate in = rate out).
- **Conservation law**: $\sum_i P_i = 1$ is *mandatory* and must always be written.
- **State-dependent failure rates** turn the standard reliability integral into a path-weighted sum driven by the Markov transition matrix.
- **Standard KTU trick:** If a problem mentions "one component is in standby," draw the state diagram with the *spare* having zero failure rate while idle.
- **Always label arrows in your state diagram with rates**; an unlabeled diagram loses 2 marks minimum.
- **Enumeration is acceptable for $n \le 3$ subsystems**; for larger $n$, the examiner expects Dynamic Programming.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single and Two Independent Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Single and Two Independent Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Single Component Reliability

In **Reliability Engineering**, the analysis of a **single component** forms the foundational building block for understanding any complex system. A single component is the simplest possible system — a single, indivisible functional unit whose failure immediately results in system failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The reliability of a single component at time $t$, denoted as $R(t)$, is the **conditional probability** that the component will perform its intended function satisfactorily for a specified period of time $[0, t]$, given that it was operational at time $t = 0$.

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

where $T$ is the **Time-To-Failure (TTF)** random variable.

**Key Characteristics of a Single Component:**
- It has only **two possible states**: **UP (Operational)** or **DOWN (Failed)**.
- Its reliability depends entirely on its inherent **failure rate ($\lambda$)**, assuming constant hazard (exponential distribution).
- For a non-repairable component, $R(t)$ is a **monotonically decreasing** function of time.

### Conceptual Analogy 💡

Imagine a **single 60-watt incandescent bulb** screwed into a table lamp. There is no backup, no redundancy — just one bulb doing all the work. The bulb's reliability is simply the probability that it will continue to glow from the moment you switch it ON until time $t$. If the bulb's filament burns out, the entire "lighting system" fails. This is the classic *single component scenario* — what fails one, fails all.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The most commonly used reliability model for a single component assumes an **exponential distribution**, where $\lambda =$ **constant failure rate (failures per unit time)**, giving $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

---

## 1.2 Two Independent Components

When two components are connected to perform a function, we enter the realm of **system reliability**. The architecture (how they are wired together — logically) dictates the overall system reliability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> Two components are said to be **statistically independent** if the failure (or survival) of one component has **absolutely no influence** on the failure (or survival) of the other. Mathematically, $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$.

For two independent components $C_1$ and $C_2$ with reliabilities $R_1(t)$ and $R_2(t)$:

$$P(C_1 \text{ survives AND } C_2 \text{ survives}) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t)$$

### Conceptual Analogy 💡

Think of a **flashlight** with **two independent batteries** placed in series inside the tube.
- **Both batteries must work** for the bulb to glow. If *either* battery dies, the flashlight goes dark. This is a **Series System**.
- Now imagine **two independent bulbs** wired in parallel inside the same lamp, sharing the load. The lamp stays lit as long as **at least one bulb** works. If both fail, only then does the system fail. This is a **Parallel (Redundant) System**.

This duality — *series for mandatory cooperation, parallel for backup redundancy* — is the heart of two-component reliability analysis.

---

## 1.3 Series vs Parallel — The Two Fundamental Configurations

| Configuration | Logic | Failure Condition | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Series** | Both must work | Failure of **either** component causes system failure | Two batteries in a torch |
| **Parallel** | Only one needs to work | System fails only if **both** components fail | Two independent water pumps |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMET747):** The module specifically deals with *reliability evaluation* of non-repairable systems comprising single and two independent components, configured either in series or parallel. Derivations use exponential distribution as the underlying life model.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Single Component — Operational Analysis

### Assumptions for Single Component Modeling
- The component is **non-repairable** (we model first failure only).
- The component operates under **normal/constant conditions** (no thermal, mechanical, or electrical shocks).
- The hazard rate $\lambda$ remains **constant** over the operating period (this corresponds to the useful life phase of the **bathtub curve**).
- Time $t$ is measured from the instant the component is put into service.

### Mathematical Framework

For an exponential life distribution:

$$f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \geq 0$$

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

The **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** for a single component is:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

### Why This Matters (Engineering Utility)
- Used as the **baseline** for all system reliability calculations.
- Forms the input parameter for **series, parallel, and complex** system models.
- Critical in warranty prediction, preventive maintenance scheduling, and spare-parts inventory planning.

---

## 2.2 Two Independent Components — Operational Analysis

### (A) Series Configuration (Both Must Work)

**Logic Step-by-Step:**

1. **Identify component reliabilities** $R_1 = e^{-\lambda_1 t}$ and $R_2 = e^{-\lambda_2 t}$.
2. **Apply the multiplication rule** for statistically independent events.
3. **System reliability** is the joint probability of both surviving:

$$R_s(t) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t)$$

4. **System failure rate** (only when both $\lambda$ are equal, $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda$):

$$\lambda_s = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2$$

5. **System MTTF** (when $\lambda_1 \neq \lambda_2$):

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Adding components in **series always REDUCES** overall system reliability, because $R_1, R_2 \in [0,1]$, and multiplying two such numbers yields a smaller product.

---

### (B) Parallel Configuration (Only One Needs to Work)

**Logic Step-by-Step:**

1. Compute the **unreliabilities** (probabilities of failure): $F_1(t) = 1 - R_1(t)$ and $F_2(t) = 1 - R_2(t)$.
2. Apply the multiplication rule for **independent failures** (system fails only if *both* fail).
3. **System unreliability** is:

$$F_p(t) = F_1(t) \cdot F_2(t) = [1-R_1(t)][1-R_2(t)]$$

4. **System reliability** is the complement:

$$R_p(t) = 1 - [1-R_1(t)][1-R_2(t)]$$

5. **System MTTF** (when $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda$):

$$\text{MTTF}_p = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{2\lambda} = \frac{3}{2\lambda}$$

For unequal failure rates:

$$\text{MTTF}_p = \frac{1}{\lambda_1} + \frac{1}{\lambda_2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Adding components in **parallel INCREASES** overall system reliability through **redundancy**, at the cost of added weight, cost, and complexity.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Master Table** — Memorize this for KTU ESE.

| # | Configuration | Reliability $R(t)$ | MTTF | Failure Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Single Component** | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ |
| 2 | **Two in Series** (independent, exponential) | $e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)t}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2}$ | $\lambda_1 + \lambda_2$ |
| 3 | **Two in Series** (identical, $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda$) | $e^{-2\lambda t}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\lambda}$ | $2\lambda$ |
| 4 | **Two in Parallel** (independent, exponential) | $1 - (1-e^{-\lambda_1 t})(1-e^{-\lambda_2 t})$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda_1} + \dfrac{1}{\lambda_2} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2}$ | (time-varying) |
| 5 | **Two in Parallel** (identical, $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda$) | $2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$ | $\dfrac{3}{2\lambda}$ | (time-varying) |
| 6 | **System Unreliability (Parallel)** | $F_p(t) = [1-R_1(t)][1-R_2(t)]$ | — | — |

### Real-World Engineering Utility of These Models
- **Aerospace:** Redundant flight control computers (parallel) and mandatory sensor chains (series).
- **Nuclear Power Plants:** Triple-redundant safety systems — analyzed as extensions of two-component parallel models.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphone battery systems (parallel cells for extended life, series cells for higher voltage).
- **Automotive:** Airbag sensors in series; dual brake circuits in parallel.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: MTTF of a Single Component (Exponential)

**Starting Point:** The expected value of the time-to-failure random variable $T$ is given by:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ for the exponential distribution:

$$E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Use integration by parts: let $u = t$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$.

$$du = dt, \qquad v = -e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the formula $\int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du$:

$$E[T] = \left[-t \cdot e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (-e^{-\lambda t}) \, dt$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the boundary term. As $t \to \infty$, $t e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$ (exponential decay dominates). At $t = 0$, the term is 0.

$$E[T] = 0 + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the remaining integral:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: MTTF of Two Independent Components in Parallel (Unequal Rates)

**Starting Point:** System reliability is:

$$R_p(t) = 1 - (1-e^{-\lambda_1 t})(1-e^{-\lambda_2 t})$$

**Step 1:** Expand the bracket:

$$R_p(t) = 1 - \left[1 - e^{-\lambda_1 t} - e^{-\lambda_2 t} + e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)t}\right]$$

**Step 2:** Simplify:

$$R_p(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} + e^{-\lambda_2 t} - e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)t}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the MTTF integral $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$:

$$\text{MTTF}_p = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left[e^{-\lambda_1 t} + e^{-\lambda_2 t} - e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)t}\right] dt$$

**Step 4:** Integrate term by term:

$$\text{MTTF}_p = \frac{1}{\lambda_1} + \frac{1}{\lambda_2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_p = \frac{1}{\lambda_1} + \frac{1}{\lambda_2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Series Configuration

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
>
> A system consists of two independent components in series with failure rates $\lambda_1 = 0.002/\text{hr}$ and $\lambda_2 = 0.003/\text{hr}$. Compute: (a) System reliability at $t = 100$ hours. (b) MTTF of the system.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**

$$R_1(100) = e^{-0.002 \times 100} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187$$

$$R_2(100) = e^{-0.003 \times 100} = e^{-0.3} = 0.7408$$

$$R_s(100) = R_1 \times R_2 = 0.8187 \times 0.7408 = 0.6066$$

**Part (b):**

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2} = \frac{1}{0.005} = 200 \text{ hours}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Parallel Configuration

> A system has two identical pumps operating in parallel. Each pump has $\lambda = 0.001/\text{hr}$. Find reliability at $t = 500$ hr and the MTTF.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**

$$R_p(500) = 2e^{-0.001 \times 500} - e^{-2 \times 0.001 \times 500}$$

$$= 2e^{-0.5} - e^{-1.0}$$

$$= 2(0.6065) - 0.3679$$

$$= 1.2130 - 0.3679 = 0.8451$$

**Part (b):**

$$\text{MTTF}_p = \frac{3}{2\lambda} = \frac{3}{2 \times 0.001} = 1500 \text{ hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The single pump's MTTF is $\frac{1}{0.001} = 1000$ hr. Adding a redundant pump in parallel increased the MTTF to 1500 hr — a **50% improvement** in mean life.

---

## 3.5 Comparison Table — Single vs Two-Component System

| Parameter | Single | Series (2) | Parallel (2 identical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R(t)$ formula | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | $e^{-2\lambda t}$ | $2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$ |
| MTTF | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\lambda}$ | $\dfrac{3}{2\lambda}$ |
| Reliability at $t = 1/\lambda$ | $e^{-1} \approx 0.368$ | $e^{-2} \approx 0.135$ | $2e^{-1} - e^{-2} \approx 0.600$ |
| Nature | Baseline | Lower than single | Higher than single |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single Component Reliability Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[System Input] --> B[Component 1]
    B --> C[System Output]
    style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> The single component $C_1$ is the **only pathway** between input and output. Its failure directly causes system failure.

---

## 4.2 Two Independent Components in Series

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[System Input] --> B[Component C1]
    B --> C[Component C2]
    C --> D[System Output]
    style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> Both $C_1$ **AND** $C_2$ must function. Failure of any single component breaks the chain.

---

## 4.3 Two Independent Components in Parallel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[System Input] --> B{Junction}
    B --> C[Component C1]
    B --> D[Component C2]
    C --> E[Junction]
    D --> E
    E --> F[System Output]
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> The signal can travel through **either** $C_1$ **OR** $C_2$. The system survives even if one component fails — this is **active redundancy**.

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow — Series Reliability Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify lambda1 and lambda2] --> S2[Step 2: Compute R1 = e power minus lambda1 t]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute R2 = e power minus lambda2 t]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Multiply Rs = R1 times R2]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute MTTF = 1 over lambda1 plus lambda2]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Report Final System Reliability and MTTF]
    style S1 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S6 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.5 Functional Architecture Flow — Parallel Reliability Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Identify lambda1 and lambda2] --> P2[Step 2: Compute F1 = 1 minus R1]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Compute F2 = 1 minus R2]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Compute Fp = F1 times F2]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Compute Rp = 1 minus Fp]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Compute MTTF using parallel formula]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Report Final System Reliability and MTTF]
    style P1 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P7 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the reliability of a single component and state its mathematical expression assuming exponential distribution. Also define MTTF.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The reliability of a single component at time $t$ is defined as the probability that the component performs its intended function without failure over the interval $[0, t]$, given it was operational at $t=0$.

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

For an **exponential distribution** with constant failure rate $\lambda$:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**MTTF** (Mean Time To Failure) is the expected time of operation until the first failure:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$$

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Two independent components with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.9$ and $R_2 = 0.8$ are connected in parallel. Calculate the system reliability.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

System reliability for two independent components in parallel:

$$R_p = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)$$

$$R_p = 1 - (1 - 0.9)(1 - 0.8)$$

$$R_p = 1 - (0.1)(0.2)$$

$$R_p = 1 - 0.02 = 0.98$$

**System Reliability = 0.98 (98%)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split into two 7-mark sub-parts. Internal choice is provided.

---

### **Question B1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the reliability and MTTF of a system consisting of **two independent components in series**, assuming exponential life distribution with failure rates $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A system consists of **two identical components in parallel**. Each component has a constant failure rate $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour. Compute: (i) System reliability at $t = 1000$ hours. (ii) MTTF of the system. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

For two independent components with reliabilities $R_1(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t}$ and $R_2(t) = e^{-\lambda_2 t}$, system reliability (series) is the joint probability of both surviving:

$$R_s(t) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} \cdot e^{-\lambda_2 t}$$

$$R_s(t) = e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2) t}$$

The effective failure rate of the series system is:

$$\lambda_s = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2$$

MTTF of the series system:

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_s(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2) t} \, dt$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

(i) For two identical parallel components, system reliability:

$$R_p(t) = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$$

Substituting $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-4}$/hr and $t = 1000$ hr:

$$\lambda t = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000 = 0.5$$

$$e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

$$e^{-1.0} = 0.3679$$

$$R_p(1000) = 2(0.6065) - 0.3679 = 1.2130 - 0.3679 = 0.8451$$

(ii) MTTF for two identical parallel components:

$$\text{MTTF}_p = \frac{3}{2\lambda} = \frac{3}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{3}{10^{-3}} = 3000 \text{ hours}$$

---

### **Question B2 (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the reliability and MTTF expressions for a system of **two independent, non-identical components connected in parallel**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A radar system has **two signal processors in series**, with failure rates $\lambda_1 = 0.0008/\text{hr}$ and $\lambda_2 = 0.0012/\text{hr}$. Determine the reliability for an operating mission of 200 hours, and the MTTF. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

For two independent, non-identical components in parallel:

$$R_p(t) = 1 - (1-e^{-\lambda_1 t})(1-e^{-\lambda_2 t})$$

Expanding:

$$R_p(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} + e^{-\lambda_2 t} - e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)t}$$

MTTF:

$$\text{MTTF}_p = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_p(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left[e^{-\lambda_1 t} + e^{-\lambda_2 t} - e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)t}\right] dt$$

$$= \frac{1}{\lambda_1} + \frac{1}{\lambda_2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_p = \frac{1}{\lambda_1} + \frac{1}{\lambda_2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

System reliability (series):

$$R_s(t) = e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2) t}$$

$$\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 0.0008 + 0.0012 = 0.0020/\text{hr}$$

$$(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2) \cdot t = 0.0020 \times 200 = 0.4$$

$$R_s(200) = e^{-0.4} = 0.6703$$

MTTF:

$$\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{0.0020} = 500 \text{ hours}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Independent vs Dependent:** Students often confuse *statistical independence* with *physical separation*. Two components are independent only if one's failure doesn't trigger the other's. If both share an overheating environment, they are **dependent** — and these formulas DON'T apply.
> 2. **Series vs Parallel Sign Error:** A common mistake is writing $R_s = 1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2)$ for series systems. This is WRONG — it is the parallel formula. Memorize the logic, not just the formula.
> 3. **Units of $\lambda$:** Always check the units of failure rate (per hour vs per thousand hours). A $\lambda = 0.001$/hr is **not** the same as $\lambda = 0.001$/(1000 hr).
> 4. **Exponential Assumption:** The MTTF formulas $\frac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2}$ (series) and $\frac{3}{2\lambda}$ (parallel) are valid ONLY under the **constant hazard rate** assumption. Do not use them for Weibull or normal distributions.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 2:**

- ✅ **Single Component Reliability:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$; MTTF $= 1/\lambda$.
- ✅ **Two Components in Series:** $R_s = R_1 \cdot R_2 = e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)t}$; MTTF $= \dfrac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2}$.
- ✅ **Two Components in Parallel:** $R_p = 1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} + e^{-\lambda_2 t} - e^{-(\lambda_1+\lambda_2)t}$.
- ✅ **MTTF of Parallel (Unequal):** $\dfrac{1}{\lambda_1} + \dfrac{1}{\lambda_2} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2}$.
- ✅ **MTTF of Parallel (Identical):** $\dfrac{3}{2\lambda}$.
- ✅ **Series REDUCES reliability; Parallel INCREASES reliability.**
- ✅ **Independence is a critical assumption** — verify before applying multiplication rules.
- ✅ **Exponential distribution** is the default life model assumed in KTU problems.
- ✅ **System reliability is always ≤ the reliability of the weakest component.**
- ✅ **For parallel systems**, the system MTTF is **greater** than the MTTF of a single component — this is the mathematical proof of redundancy benefit.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Sharing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Load Sharing Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Load Sharing System (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **Load Sharing System** is a parallel redundancy configuration in which **two or more components simultaneously share the operational load** applied to the system. When any single component fails, the entire applied load is **redistributed** among the surviving components, thereby **increasing the stress level** on each remaining unit. The system is considered operational as long as at least one component can sustain the redistributed load without exceeding its rated capacity.

This is fundamentally different from a **pure parallel (active redundancy)** system, where the load is borne only by the active components and does not redistribute upon failure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Restaurant Waiter Shift**
> Imagine a restaurant with **2 waiters** managing **20 tables** together (10 tables each). This is a **load-shared system** because both waiters are *actively working* simultaneously, sharing the customer load.
> - If **Waiter A** falls sick, the **20 tables** must now be handled by **Waiter B alone**.
> - Waiter B's *workload doubles*, his *stress increases*, and the *chance of him making a mistake* (system failure) is now **much higher** than when both were working.
> - The restaurant (system) **fails** only when both waiters are unable to handle the load.

This perfectly mirrors a load sharing system — increased load → increased failure rate → cascading risk.

## 1.3 Classification of Load Sharing Systems

| Sub-Type | Description | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Equal Load Sharing** | All components share the load uniformly | Two pumps each handling 50% flow |
| **Unequal Load Sharing** | Components carry different fractions of the load | Primary + standby sharing 70:30 |
| **Capacity-Limited Load Sharing** | System fails if redistributed load exceeds remaining capacity | Overloaded transformer tripping |
| **Stress-Dependent Load Sharing** | Failure rate increases with redistributed load | Electronic components in parallel |

## 1.4 Key Metrics Used (Bold Highlights)

- **Failure Rate (λ):** Rate at which a component fails under nominal load, measured in **failures/hour**.
- **Overload Failure Rate (λ'):** Increased failure rate when component carries the full redistributed load.
- **Load Factor (ρ):** Ratio of applied load to rated capacity, dimensionless.
- **System Reliability R(t):** Probability the system performs its intended function up to time **t**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a 2-Component Load Sharing System

Consider **two identical components** A and B with:
- Nominal failure rate: **λ** (when each carries half the load)
- Overload failure rate: **λ'** (when one carries the full load after the other fails)
- Condition: **λ' > λ** because overload accelerates degradation

The system progresses through **3 Markov states**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State 0:** Both components operating → each carries **L/2** → system failure rate = **2λ**
> **State 1:** One component failed → surviving unit carries **full load L** → system failure rate = **λ'**
> **State 2 (Absorbing):** Both components failed → **SYSTEM FAILED**

### Why does λ' > λ?
This is the heart of load-sharing analysis. When a component must now bear **double the load**, the mechanical, electrical, or thermal stress on it rises. By the **Inverse Power Law** or **Arrhenius-type degradation models**, increased stress exponentially increases the failure rate. Hence **λ' is always strictly greater than λ**.

## 2.2 State Transition Diagram (Conceptual)

| From State | To State | Transition Rate | Trigger |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Both Working | One Working | **2λ** | Any one component fails |
| One Working | Both Failed | **λ'** | Surviving component fails under overload |
| Both Working | Both Failed | **(Impossible)** | Cannot fail both simultaneously |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary Condition |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $R_{\text{both}}(t) = e^{-2\lambda t}$ | Probability both components survive to time $t$ | Independent failures |
| 2 | $R_{\text{system}}(t) = \dfrac{\lambda' \, e^{-2\lambda t} - 2\lambda \, e^{-\lambda' t}}{\lambda' - 2\lambda}$ | Total reliability of 2-component load sharing system | Requires $\lambda' \neq 2\lambda$ |
| 3 | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{2\lambda} + \dfrac{1}{\lambda'}$ | Mean Time To Failure | Sum of expected dwell times |
| 4 | $\lambda' = \lambda \cdot k, \quad k > 1$ | Overload factor (stress multiplier) | Empirical/derived |
| 5 | $P_2(t) = \dfrac{2\lambda}{\lambda' - 2\lambda}\left(e^{-2\lambda t} - e^{-\lambda' t}\right)$ | Probability exactly one component has failed by $t$ | Valid for $\lambda' > 2\lambda$ |
| 6 | $R_{\text{system}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | Pure parallel (no load sharing) | Reference baseline |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Memory Trick for KTU Exams:**
> The reliability equation **looks like a weighted difference of two exponentials** — one decaying at rate **2λ** (both working) and the other at rate **λ'** (single overloaded component). The numerator combines them with a sign convention governed by the **denominator (λ' − 2λ)**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|:---|:---|
| **Power Systems** | Parallel transformers sharing grid load |
| **Aerospace** | Twin-engine aircraft with shared thrust |
| **Pumps & Fluid Systems** | Multiple pumps in parallel for pipeline flow |
| **Computer Clusters** | Web servers distributing user load |
| **Civil Engineering** | Multiple cables in a suspension bridge sharing tension |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of System Reliability for a 2-Component Load Sharing System

### Step 1 — Define the State Probabilities

Let:
- $P_1(t)$ = Probability both components are working at time $t$
- $P_2(t)$ = Probability exactly one component is working (the other has failed) at time $t$
- $P_3(t)$ = Probability both have failed = $1 - P_1(t) - P_2(t)$ (absorbing state)

The system is **operational** in states 1 and 2, so:

$$
R_{\text{sys}}(t) = P_1(t) + P_2(t)
$$

### Step 2 — Write the Markov Differential Equations

Based on the state transitions, the Kolmogorov forward equations are:

$$
\frac{dP_1(t)}{dt} = -2\lambda \, P_1(t)
$$

$$
\frac{dP_2(t)}{dt} = 2\lambda \, P_1(t) - \lambda' \, P_2(t)
$$

> **Logic:** $P_1$ decreases at rate $2\lambda$ (either component can fail). $P_2$ increases by inflow from $P_1$ and decreases by outflow due to failure of the overloaded survivor at rate $\lambda'$.

### Step 3 — Solve for $P_1(t)$

With initial condition $P_1(0) = 1$:

$$
P_1(t) = e^{-2\lambda t}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Standard first-order ODE solution; the exponential is the unique solution satisfying $P_1(0)=1$.

### Step 4 — Solve for $P_2(t)$ via Laplace or Direct Integration

Using the integrating factor method on the second ODE:

$$
\frac{dP_2(t)}{dt} + \lambda' P_2(t) = 2\lambda \, e^{-2\lambda t}
$$

Multiplying both sides by the integrating factor $e^{\lambda' t}$:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\left[e^{\lambda' t} P_2(t)\right] = 2\lambda \, e^{(\lambda' - 2\lambda) t}
$$

Integrating from $0$ to $t$ with $P_2(0)=0$:

$$
e^{\lambda' t} P_2(t) = \dfrac{2\lambda}{\lambda' - 2\lambda}\left[e^{(\lambda' - 2\lambda) t} - 1\right]
$$

Solving for $P_2(t)$:

$$
P_2(t) = \dfrac{2\lambda}{\lambda' - 2\lambda}\left[e^{-2\lambda t} - e^{-\lambda' t}\right]
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** We factor out $e^{-\lambda' t}$ from both terms and divide through. This yields the probability of being in the degraded-but-operational state.

### Step 5 — Combine to Get System Reliability

$$
R_{\text{sys}}(t) = P_1(t) + P_2(t)
$$

$$
R_{\text{sys}}(t) = e^{-2\lambda t} + \dfrac{2\lambda}{\lambda' - 2\lambda}\left[e^{-2\lambda t} - e^{-\lambda' t}\right]
$$

Common denominator $(\lambda' - 2\lambda)$:

$$
R_{\text{sys}}(t) = \dfrac{(\lambda' - 2\lambda)e^{-2\lambda t} + 2\lambda \, e^{-2\lambda t} - 2\lambda \, e^{-\lambda' t}}{\lambda' - 2\lambda}
$$

Simplifying the numerator — group $e^{-2\lambda t}$ terms:

$$
(\lambda' - 2\lambda)e^{-2\lambda t} + 2\lambda \, e^{-2\lambda t} = \lambda' \, e^{-2\lambda t}
$$

Final closed-form result:

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{sys}}(t) = \dfrac{\lambda' \, e^{-2\lambda t} - 2\lambda \, e^{-\lambda' t}}{\lambda' - 2\lambda}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical KTU formula** for a 2-component load sharing system. Memorize the structure: **(overload-rate × both-alive term) − (combined-rate × overloaded term), divided by (rate difference)**.

## 3.2 Derivation of MTTF

The Mean Time To Failure is the area under the reliability curve:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_{\text{sys}}(t) \, dt
$$

Substituting the reliability expression:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{\lambda' \, e^{-2\lambda t} - 2\lambda \, e^{-\lambda' t}}{\lambda' - 2\lambda} \, dt
$$

By linearity of integration:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda' - 2\lambda}\left[\lambda' \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2\lambda t} \, dt - 2\lambda \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda' t} \, dt\right]
$$

Evaluate each integral using $\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-at} dt = \dfrac{1}{a}$:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda' - 2\lambda}\left[\dfrac{\lambda'}{2\lambda} - \dfrac{2\lambda}{\lambda'}\right]
$$

Common denominator $2\lambda \lambda'$:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda' - 2\lambda} \cdot \dfrac{(\lambda')^2 - (2\lambda)^2}{2\lambda \lambda'}
$$

Factor the numerator difference of squares:

$$
(\lambda')^2 - (2\lambda)^2 = (\lambda' + 2\lambda)(\lambda' - 2\lambda)
$$

Cancelling $(\lambda' - 2\lambda)$:

$$
\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{\lambda' + 2\lambda}{2\lambda \lambda'} = \dfrac{1}{2\lambda} + \dfrac{1}{\lambda'}}
$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** MTTF is simply the **sum of mean dwell times** — first $\frac{1}{2\lambda}$ in the both-working state, then $\frac{1}{\lambda'}$ in the overloaded state. This matches intuition perfectly.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A load-sharing system has 2 identical pumps, each with nominal failure rate $\lambda = 0.001$ /hour. When one pump fails, the surviving pump's failure rate increases to $\lambda' = 0.003$ /hour. Compute the system reliability at $t = 100$ hours and the MTTF.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
$\lambda = 0.001$, $\lambda' = 0.003$, $t = 100$.

**Step 2 — Compute the exponent terms:**

$$
e^{-2\lambda t} = e^{-2(0.001)(100)} = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187
$$

$$
e^{-\lambda' t} = e^{-0.003 \times 100} = e^{-0.3} \approx 0.7408
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the canonical reliability formula:**

$$
R_{\text{sys}}(100) = \dfrac{0.003 \times 0.8187 - 2(0.001) \times 0.7408}{0.003 - 2(0.001)}
$$

Numerator:

$$
0.003 \times 0.8187 = 0.002456
$$

$$
2 \times 0.001 \times 0.7408 = 0.001482
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 0.002456 - 0.001482 = 0.000974
$$

Denominator:

$$
0.003 - 0.002 = 0.001
$$

System reliability:

$$
R_{\text{sys}}(100) = \dfrac{0.000974}{0.001} \approx 0.974
$$

**Step 4 — Compute MTTF:**

$$
\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{2(0.001)} + \dfrac{1}{0.003} = 500 + 333.33 = 833.33 \text{ hours}
$$

> **Comparison Insight:** If the system were a **pure parallel** system with no load sharing (failure rate remains $\lambda$ after one fails), MTTF would be $\frac{1}{2\lambda} + \frac{1}{\lambda} = 1500$ hours. The load sharing penalty reduces MTTF by **~44%**, demonstrating why load sharing is a **double-edged sword**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 State Transition Diagram (Markov Model)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateA
    stateA : STATE A : Both Components Working
    stateA : Each carries L/2
    stateA : Failure Rate = 2lambda
    stateB : STATE B : One Component Failed
    stateB : Survivor carries full L
    stateB : Failure Rate = lambda prime
    stateC : STATE C : SYSTEM FAILED
    stateC : Absorbing Failure State
    stateA --> stateB : Rate 2lambda
    stateB --> stateC : Rate lambda prime
    stateA --> stateC : Cannot Occur Directly
    stateC --> [*]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Total Applied Load L] --> distA{Load Distributor}
    distA --> compA[Component A: Carries L/2]
    distA --> compB[Component B: Carries L/2]
    compA --> checkA{Failure of A?}
    compB --> checkB{Failure of B?}
    checkA -->|Yes| redistA[Redistribute: B carries full L]
    checkA -->|No| opStateA[Both Operational]
    checkB -->|Yes| redistB[Redistribute: A carries full L]
    checkB -->|No| opStateA
    redistA --> checkB2{Failure of B under overload?}
    redistB --> checkA2{Failure of A under overload?}
    checkB2 -->|Yes| sysFail[SYSTEM FAILURE]
    checkA2 -->|Yes| sysFail
    opStateA --> sysOK[System Operational]
    sysFail --> [*]
    sysOK --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Reliability Decay Comparison Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axis[Time t in hours] --> pt1[Point 1: t=0, R=1.0]
    axis --> pt2[Point 2: Both alive phase dominates]
    axis --> pt3[Point 3: Overload phase dominates]
    axis --> pt4[Point 4: R approaches 0]
    pt1 --> pt2
    pt2 --> pt3
    pt3 --> pt4
    curve1[Dashed Line: Pure Parallel System R higher] -.-> pt2
    curve2[Solid Line: Load Sharing System R lower] -.-> pt2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Insight:** The load-sharing system reliability curve (solid) lies **below** the pure parallel curve (dashed) because the overloaded survivor fails faster. The gap widens with time.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** Define a *load sharing system*. How does it differ from a pure parallel redundant system?

**Model Answer:**
A load sharing system is a parallel configuration where two or more components simultaneously share the applied load. Upon failure of any component, the load is redistributed among surviving units, increasing their stress and failure rate. In contrast, a pure parallel system does not redistribute the load — the surviving units continue at the same load level. Hence load sharing systems exhibit lower reliability than pure parallel systems at long mission times.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** Why is the failure rate $\lambda'$ of an overloaded component in a load-sharing system always greater than the nominal failure rate $\lambda$?

**Model Answer:**
When a component carries the redistributed full load, it experiences higher mechanical, thermal, or electrical stress. By degradation kinetics (Arrhenius, Eyring, or Inverse Power Law), increased stress exponentially raises the degradation rate. Hence the probability of failure per unit time — the failure rate — increases. Mathematically, $\lambda' > \lambda$ always.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 2, Sample Question Paper, 14 Marks]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the reliability expression $R_{\text{sys}}(t)$ for a 2-component load sharing system where each component has nominal failure rate $\lambda$ and overload failure rate $\lambda'$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two pumps operate in a load-sharing configuration. The nominal failure rate of each pump is $\lambda = 0.0005$/hour. When one pump fails, the other operates at an overloaded rate of $\lambda' = 0.002$/hour. Calculate:
  (i) The system reliability at $t = 200$ hours.
  (ii) The MTTF of the system. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]:**

Step 1 — Define states. State 1: both working; State 2: one working; State 3: system failed. The system operates in states 1 and 2.

Step 2 — Write the Markov ODEs:

$$
\frac{dP_1}{dt} = -2\lambda P_1, \quad \frac{dP_2}{dt} = 2\lambda P_1 - \lambda' P_2
$$

Step 3 — Solve $P_1$ with $P_1(0)=1$:

$$
P_1(t) = e^{-2\lambda t}
$$

Step 4 — Solve $P_2$ via integrating factor $e^{\lambda' t}$:

$$
P_2(t) = \dfrac{2\lambda}{\lambda' - 2\lambda}\left[e^{-2\lambda t} - e^{-\lambda' t}\right]
$$

Step 5 — Sum $P_1 + P_2$:

$$
R_{\text{sys}}(t) = \dfrac{\lambda' e^{-2\lambda t} - 2\lambda e^{-\lambda' t}}{\lambda' - 2\lambda}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]:**

Given: $\lambda = 0.0005$/hr, $\lambda' = 0.002$/hr, $t = 200$ hr.

(i) Compute exponents:

$$
2\lambda t = 2 \times 0.0005 \times 200 = 0.2, \quad \lambda' t = 0.002 \times 200 = 0.4
$$

$$
e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187, \quad e^{-0.4} \approx 0.6703
$$

Apply the formula:

$$
R_{\text{sys}}(200) = \dfrac{0.002 \times 0.8187 - 2 \times 0.0005 \times 0.6703}{0.002 - 2 \times 0.0005}
$$

Numerator:

$$
0.002 \times 0.8187 = 0.001637
$$

$$
0.001 \times 0.6703 = 0.000670
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 0.001637 - 0.000670 = 0.000967
$$

Denominator:

$$
0.002 - 0.001 = 0.001
$$

$$
R_{\text{sys}}(200) = \dfrac{0.000967}{0.001} = 0.967
$$

(ii) MTTF:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times 0.0005} + \dfrac{1}{0.002} = 1000 + 500 = 1500 \text{ hours}
$$

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the Markov model for a 2-component load sharing system. Define all states and transition rates. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A load-sharing redundant system consists of 2 identical components with $\lambda = 0.002$/hr and $\lambda' = 0.005$/hr. Determine:
  (i) The probability that the system is in the *both-working* state at $t = 50$ hours.
  (ii) The probability that the system is in the *one-failed-but-operational* state at $t = 50$ hours.
  (iii) Comment on the system reliability compared to a pure parallel system. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Explanation [7 Marks]:**

Draw the 3-state Markov diagram with states labeled:
- State A (Both working): each carries $L/2$, exit rate $2\lambda$.
- State B (One failed): survivor carries $L$, exit rate $\lambda'$.
- State C (System failed): absorbing state.

Transitions: $A \xrightarrow{2\lambda} B \xrightarrow{\lambda'} C$. Provide the rationale that $\lambda'$ exceeds $\lambda$ due to overload-induced accelerated degradation.

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]:**

Given: $\lambda = 0.002$/hr, $\lambda' = 0.005$/hr, $t = 50$ hr.

(i) Both-working probability:

$$
P_1(50) = e^{-2 \times 0.002 \times 50} = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187
$$

(ii) One-failed probability:

$$
P_2(50) = \dfrac{2 \times 0.002}{0.005 - 2 \times 0.002}\left[e^{-0.2} - e^{-0.005 \times 50}\right]
$$

$$
= \dfrac{0.004}{0.001}\left[0.8187 - e^{-0.25}\right]
$$

$$
= 4 \times [0.8187 - 0.7788] = 4 \times 0.0399 \approx 0.1596
$$

(iii) System reliability:

$$
R_{\text{sys}}(50) = 0.8187 + 0.1596 = 0.9783
$$

For pure parallel system (no load redistribution), $R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - e^{-0.002 \times 50})^2 = 1 - (1 - 0.9048)^2 = 1 - (0.0952)^2 = 1 - 0.00906 = 0.9909$.

**Comment:** The load-sharing system reliability (0.9783) is **lower** than the pure parallel reliability (0.9909) because the overloaded survivor degrades faster, confirming the load-sharing penalty.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Numerator:** Students frequently write $2\lambda e^{-2\lambda t} - \lambda' e^{-\lambda' t}$ instead of the correct $\lambda' e^{-2\lambda t} - 2\lambda e^{-\lambda' t}$. **Remember:** the coefficient of the first exponential matches its *rate* exponent (which is $2\lambda$ in the exponent but $\lambda'$ as the coefficient because of the inflow from $P_1$ weighted by $\lambda'$). Verify by setting $t=0$: $R(0) = 1$.
> 2. **Confusing $\lambda'$ with $2\lambda$:** Many students mistakenly assume $\lambda' = 2\lambda$ (doubling the load doubles the rate). In practice, $\lambda'$ is determined empirically via Inverse Power Law: $\lambda' = \lambda (L/L_{\text{nom}})^n$ where $n$ is a material constant.
> 3. **Forgetting the System Reliability Equals Sum of $P_1$ and $P_2$:** The system is *operational* in both states. Students often only quote $P_1$ as the system reliability, losing **3–4 marks**.
> 4. **MTTF Calculation Mistake:** Use $\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{2\lambda} + \frac{1}{\lambda'}$ — not $\frac{1}{\lambda + \lambda'}$. The two states have **independent dwell times**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Load Sharing Systems**

- A **load sharing system** redistributes the load upon component failure; failure rate of survivor **increases** to $\lambda'$.
- The **3-state Markov model**: Both Working → One Working → Both Failed (absorbing).
- System reliability (2 components, identical):

$$
R_{\text{sys}}(t) = \dfrac{\lambda' \, e^{-2\lambda t} - 2\lambda \, e^{-\lambda' t}}{\lambda' - 2\lambda}
$$

- **Verification Trick:** $R_{\text{sys}}(0) = \frac{\lambda' - 2\lambda}{\lambda' - 2\lambda} = 1$ ✓.
- MTTF formula: $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{2\lambda} + \dfrac{1}{\lambda'}$ — independent dwell-time sum.
- **Load-sharing penalty:** $R_{\text{load-sharing}} < R_{\text{pure-parallel}}$ for all $t > 0$.
- **Limiting case 1:** If $\lambda' \to \infty$ (overloaded unit fails instantly), then $R_{\text{sys}}(t) \to e^{-2\lambda t}$ — equivalent to series system of two components. *(Sanity check.)*
- **Limiting case 2:** If $\lambda' = \lambda$ (no stress effect), then $R_{\text{sys}}(t) \to 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$ — pure parallel system formula. *(Cross-check.)*
- **n-component generalization:** Use birth-death Markov chain or recursive state enumeration. MTTF = $\sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{k\lambda \cdot (\text{load factor})}$.
- **Real-world domains:** Pumps, transformers, engines, servers, structural cables.
- **KTU Exam Weight:** This topic typically carries **7–14 marks** in Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standby System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standby System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standby System Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Standby Redundancy System** is a redundant configuration in which one or more components (termed *standby units* or *spare units*) remain in an *inactive, idle, or low-duty* state during normal operation, and are *switched into the active operational state* only upon failure of the primary (online) unit. The system fails only when the active unit fails AND the standby unit (or the switching mechanism) fails to take over the load.

In contrast to **Parallel (Active) Redundancy**, where *all* components operate simultaneously and share the load, a standby system brings redundant hardware online **only when needed**. This results in a substantially different reliability profile, especially when standby components have *negligible wear-out* during the idle period.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Backup Generator at a Hospital**
> Imagine a hospital that draws its main electrical supply from the **KERALA STATE ELECTRICITY BOARD (KSEB) grid** (the *active* unit). Should the grid fail (power cut), a **diesel generator (DG set)** — sitting idle in the basement — is *automatically switched on* within seconds to maintain life-support systems. The DG set is a classic **standby unit**: it is *not consuming diesel* and *not running* until an *Automatic Transfer Switch (ATS)* detects grid failure and activates it.
>
> - The **active unit** = KSEB Grid
> - The **standby unit** = Diesel Generator
> - The **switch** = Automatic Transfer Switch (ATS)
> - The **load** = Hospital life-support systems

If the DG also fails to start (bad fuel, dead battery, faulty solenoid) — the hospital loses power. Hence, **standby system reliability depends on THREE elements**: active unit, standby unit, *and* the switching mechanism.

## 1.3 Classification of Standby Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Classification — Based on the Operational State of the Standby Unit**

| Type | Standby State | Failure Rate During Standby | Engineering Example |
|:-----|:--------------|:---------------------------|:--------------------|
| **Hot Standby** | Fully energized & operating at no load (or partial load) | $\lambda_s = \lambda$ (same as active) | Redundant power supply in telecom racks, dual CPU servers |
| **Warm Standby** | Partially energized (e.g., heater on, circuit live) | $0 < \lambda_s < \lambda$ | Pre-heated thermal boilers, warm-up satellite transponders |
| **Cold Standby** | Completely de-energized & inert | $\lambda_s \approx 0$ | Spare tyres, fire extinguishers, backup diesel gensets, ejection seats |

**Key Distinguishing Constants used in this module:**

- $\lambda$ — Constant failure rate of the component when **operating** (assumed to follow the **exponential distribution**)
- $\lambda_s$ — Failure rate of the component while **in standby** (often zero in cold standby)
- $R_s$ — **Reliability of the switch / switching mechanism** (probability of successful switchover)
- $MTTF$ — Mean Time To Failure of the system

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Decomposition

A generic **two-unit standby system** with one active unit $A$ and one standby unit $B$ behaves as follows:

1. **Phase 1 (Normal Operation):** Unit $A$ is online and carries the full system load. Unit $B$ rests in the standby state (energy level depends on hot/warm/cold).
2. **Failure Detection:** A *failure-sensing mechanism* (sensor + controller) detects that $A$ has failed.
3. **Switchover Event:** The *switch* transfers the load to unit $B$. This switchover consumes a finite (often negligible) time.
4. **Phase 2 (Backup Operation):** Unit $B$ now operates at full load with failure rate $\lambda_B$ (or $\lambda_2$).
5. **System Failure:** The system fails if either:
   - The switch fails to transfer the load (probability = $1 - R_s$), **OR**
   - Unit $B$ fails after switchover.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Standby Superiority (in certain cases)

- In **active parallel redundancy**, both components accumulate wear simultaneously, so both reach end-of-life at similar times.
- In **standby redundancy**, the standby unit is *unused* during Phase 1 — hence, in **cold standby**, it is essentially *factory-fresh* when it is finally activated. This dramatically extends the system's mean life.

## 2.3 Critical Assumptions (KTU Board Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption Block for Standby Analysis:**
> 1. Component failure rates are **constant** (exponential distribution is valid).
> 2. Components are **non-repairable** (no replacement during mission time $t$).
> 3. Switching is **instantaneous** (or its delay is negligible compared to mission time).
> 4. The switch is *perfect* (initially) — later we relax this to imperfect switching.
> 5. Standby components are *independent* of the active unit's failure process.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table — Standby Systems (For Board Exam Quick Revision)**

| S.No. | Configuration | System Reliability $R_s(t)$ | System MTTF |
|:-----:|:--------------|:----------------------------|:------------|
| 1 | **Cold Standby, Identical Components** ($\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda$, perfect switch) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$ | $MTTF = \dfrac{2}{\lambda}$ |
| 2 | **Cold Standby, Non-Identical** ($\lambda_1 \neq \lambda_2$, perfect switch) | $R(t) = \dfrac{\lambda_1 e^{-\lambda_2 t} - \lambda_2 e^{-\lambda_1 t}}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}$ | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_1} + \dfrac{1}{\lambda_2}$ |
| 3 | **Hot Standby, Identical Components** | $R(t) = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$ | $MTTF = \dfrac{3}{2\lambda}$ |
| 4 | **Cold Standby + Imperfect Switch** ($R_s < 1$) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} + R_s \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}\left[e^{-\lambda_2 t} - e^{-\lambda_1 t}\right]$ | Reduced (see derivation) |
| 5 | **Switch with Failure Rate** $\mu$ (exponential) | $R_{sw}(t) = e^{-\mu t}$ | $MTTF_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ |
| 6 | **MTTF General Formula** (any system) | $MTTF = \displaystyle\int_0^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$ | — |

> **Key Cross-Reference:** For *identical components* with the *same active-unit failure rate*, **Cold Standby yields $MTTF = 2/\lambda$**, which is **33.3 % higher** than Hot Standby's $MTTF = 3/(2\lambda)$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Standby systems are *ubiquitous* in safety-critical Kerala-engineered infrastructure:

- **Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB)** substations employ *cold standby transformers* — the second transformer is un-energized until the primary fails.
- **Kochi Metro Rail** uses *hot standby* braking controllers for emergency response.
- **ISRO's Vikram Sarabhai Space Centre (VSSC, Thumba)** uses *cold standby* inertial guidance systems in launch vehicles.
- **Aviation**: Modern airliners (e.g., Air India Express fleet) use *triple/triple-triple hot standby* hydraulic and electrical systems.
- **Data Centers** in Technopark, Thiruvananthapuram, deploy *warm standby* servers for high-availability services.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cold Standby System with Identical Components & Perfect Switch

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a system with:
- Unit $A$ (online, failure rate $\lambda$)
- Unit $B$ (cold standby, $\lambda_s = 0$, activated with rate $\lambda$ when $A$ fails)
- Perfect switch ($R_s = 1$)
- Mission time $t$

The system survives the mission if **either**:
- $A$ does not fail during $[0, t]$, **OR**
- $A$ fails at time $\tau \in [0, t]$ AND $B$ survives the remaining $(t - \tau)$ after switchover.

### 3.1.2 Mathematical Formulation

The system reliability is the union of two mutually exclusive events:

$$R_{sys}(t) = P(A \text{ survives } [0,t]) + \int_0^t f_A(\tau) \cdot R_B(t - \tau)\, d\tau$$

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Evaluation

**Step 1:** Identify the probability density function (PDF) and reliability of unit $A$.

Since $A$ follows an exponential distribution with rate $\lambda$:

$$R_A(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$f_A(\tau) = \lambda e^{-\lambda \tau}$$

**Step 2:** Identify the reliability of unit $B$ *after* switchover.

When $B$ is activated at time $\tau$, it must survive the remaining interval $(t - \tau)$ with rate $\lambda$:

$$R_B(t - \tau) = e^{-\lambda (t - \tau)}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the integral expression.

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \int_0^t \lambda e^{-\lambda \tau} \cdot e^{-\lambda (t - \tau)}\, d\tau$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the exponent inside the integral.

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \int_0^t \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, d\tau$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integral (note that $e^{-\lambda t}$ is constant w.r.t. $\tau$).

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \int_0^t d\tau$$

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \cdot t$$

**Step 6:** Factor out $e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$\boxed{R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)}$$

### 3.1.4 MTTF Calculation

$$MTTF = \int_0^{\infty} R_{sys}(t)\, dt = \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)\, dt$$

$$MTTF = \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt + \lambda \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \lambda \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda^2} = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{MTTF = \frac{2}{\lambda}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hot Standby System with Identical Components

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

In *hot standby*, **both** units are operating continuously with failure rate $\lambda$. The system fails **only if both units fail**. This is mathematically equivalent to an *active parallel* system.

### 3.2.2 Probability of System Failure

The system fails only when **both** $A$ and $B$ have failed by time $t$:

$$P(\text{failure}) = (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^2$$

### 3.2.3 System Reliability

$$R_{sys}(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^2$$

Expanding the square:

$$R_{sys}(t) = 1 - (1 - 2e^{-\lambda t} + e^{-2\lambda t})$$

$$\boxed{R_{sys}(t) = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}}$$

### 3.2.4 MTTF Calculation

$$MTTF = \int_0^{\infty} \left(2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}\right) dt$$

$$MTTF = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{2\lambda} = \frac{4 - 1}{2\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{MTTF = \frac{3}{2\lambda}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparative Insight:** Cold standby gives $MTTF = 2/\lambda$ whereas hot standby gives $MTTF = 3/(2\lambda)$ for identical components. Cold standby wins by **33.33 %** in mean life.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cold Standby with Imperfect Switching

### 3.3.1 Setup

- Unit $A$ online with failure rate $\lambda_1$
- Unit $B$ in cold standby, operating rate $\lambda_2$ after activation
- Switch has reliability $R_s$ (probability of *successful* switchover; constant, not time-varying)

### 3.3.2 System Reliability Logic

System survives if:
- $A$ survives $[0, t]$, **OR**
- $A$ fails at $\tau \in [0, t]$ AND switch works AND $B$ survives $[0, t-\tau]$.

### 3.3.3 Mathematical Expression

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} + R_s \int_0^t \lambda_1 e^{-\lambda_1 \tau} \cdot e^{-\lambda_2 (t - \tau)}\, d\tau$$

### 3.3.4 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Factor out the time-independent term $e^{-\lambda_2 t}$:

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} + R_s \lambda_1 e^{-\lambda_2 t} \int_0^t e^{-(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2) \tau}\, d\tau$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the integral (assuming $\lambda_1 \neq \lambda_2$):

$$\int_0^t e^{-(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2) \tau}\, d\tau = \frac{1 - e^{-(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2) t}}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back:

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} + \frac{R_s \lambda_1}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2} e^{-\lambda_2 t}\left[1 - e^{-(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2) t}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Expand and simplify the bracket:

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} + \frac{R_s \lambda_1}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}\left[e^{-\lambda_2 t} - e^{-\lambda_1 t}\right]$$

**Step 5:** Group the $e^{-\lambda_1 t}$ terms:

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t}\left[1 - \frac{R_s \lambda_1}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}\right] + \frac{R_s \lambda_1}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2} e^{-\lambda_2 t}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify the coefficient using $1 - \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2} = \frac{-\lambda_2}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}$:

$$\boxed{R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} + \frac{R_s \lambda_1}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}\left[e^{-\lambda_2 t} - e^{-\lambda_1 t}\right]}$$

### 3.3.5 Special Case Verification

If $R_s = 1$ and $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda$, the L'Hôpital's rule application yields:

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t) \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Hot Standby (Identical) | Cold Standby (Identical) |
|:---------|:------------------------|:-------------------------|
| $R(t)$ formula | $2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$ | $e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$ |
| MTTF | $3/(2\lambda)$ | $2/\lambda$ |
| Hazard behaviour | Both units wear simultaneously | Standby unit is "fresh" |
| Switching load | Low (already energized) | High (must cold-start) |
| Cost of redundancy | High (always-on power) | Low (only when activated) |
| KTU exam frequency | High | Very High |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reliability Block Diagram of a 2-Unit Standby System

```mermaid
graph LR
    Input[System Input] --> Switch1{Active Switch}
    Switch1 --> UnitA[Unit A: Online]
    Switch1 -.->|Activates on failure| Switch2{Standby Switch}
    Switch2 --> UnitB[Unit B: Standby]
    UnitA --> Output[System Output]
    UnitB --> Output
    UnitA -.->|Failure signal| Switch2
```

**Interpretation:** Unit A is in the *primary* signal path. Only when A fails does the *standby switch* engage Unit B. Both A and B feed into the common output.

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram — Cold Standby (Markov Model)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> State1
    State1: State 1: Both units OK
    State2: State 2: Unit A failed, B running
    State3: State 3: System failure
    State1 --> State2 : lambda1 - Unit A fails
    State1 --> State3 : 0 - Cold standby no direct failure
    State2 --> State3 : lambda2 - Unit B fails
    State2 --> [*] : Mission complete
    State3 --> [*]
```

**Interpretation:**
- From **State 1** (both healthy), the system transitions to **State 2** at rate $\lambda_1$ (failure of A).
- From **State 1** to **State 3** directly is **impossible** in cold standby (since B is inert).
- From **State 2**, B fails at rate $\lambda_2$, leading to system failure (**State 3**).

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Standby Power System

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PrimaryPathway [Primary Power Pathway]
        Grid[KSEB Grid Supply] -->|Active Power| Sensor1[Voltage Sensor]
        Sensor1 --> ATSMain[Automatic Transfer Switch - Main Contact]
        ATSMain --> Load[Hospital Load]
    end
    subgraph BackupPathway [Standby Power Pathway]
        DGSet[Diesel Generator Set] -->|Cold Standby| Sensor2[Diesel Controller]
        Sensor2 --> ATSBackup[ATS - Standby Contact]
        ATSBackup --> Load
    end
    Sensor1 -.->|Failure Signal| ATSSwitch{ATS Logic}
    ATSSwitch -->|Command| DGSet
    ATSSwitch -->|Command| ATSBackup
```

**Interpretation:** A practical cold-standby power system mirrors the abstract model. The **ATS (Automatic Transfer Switch)** is the *switching mechanism* whose reliability $R_s$ directly multiplies the standby unit's contribution. KTU exam problems often reduce this to the abstract formula: $R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} + R_s \cdot [\text{integral term}]$.

## 4.4 Comparison Tree — Standby vs Active Redundancy

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Redundancy Techniques]
    Root --> Active[Active Parallel Redundancy]
    Root --> Standby[Standby Redundancy]
    Active --> A1[All units operate simultaneously]
    Active --> A2[Load shared]
    Active --> A3[Higher wear, higher cost]
    Standby --> S1[Standby unit inactive initially]
    Standby --> S2[Activated only on failure]
    Standby --> S3[Lower wear, lower running cost]
    Standby --> S4[Sensitive to switch reliability]
    S2 --> S5[Hot - fully energized]
    S2 --> S6[Warm - partially energized]
    S2 --> S7[Cold - fully de-energized]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define a *standby system*. Distinguish between *hot standby* and *cold standby* configurations.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **standby system** is a redundant configuration in which one or more spare components remain in an idle/inactive state during normal operation and are switched online only upon failure of the active unit.

| Aspect | Hot Standby | Cold Standby |
|:-------|:------------|:-------------|
| State of standby unit | Fully energized, running at no load | Completely de-energized, inert |
| Failure rate during standby | $\lambda_s = \lambda$ (same as active) | $\lambda_s \approx 0$ |
| Wear on standby unit | Yes | None |
| Switchover effort | Low (already running) | High (must start from cold) |
| Example | Redundant server PSU | Spare car tyre |

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for tabulated comparison)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** For a cold-standby system with two *identical* components each having failure rate $\lambda$ and a perfect switch, derive the expression for system reliability $R(t)$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The system reliability is the probability that **either** the online unit survives the entire mission, **or** it fails at some time $\tau$ and the standby unit survives the remaining time.

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \int_0^t \lambda e^{-\lambda \tau} \cdot e^{-\lambda(t-\tau)}\, d\tau$$

Simplifying the integral:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \lambda t \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)}$$

*(1 mark setup + 1 mark simplification + 1 mark final result)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, with sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. The student answers **ONE full question** (either Q.A or Q.B).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

#### **Q.A (a) — 7 Marks**

A system consists of **two identical components** in cold-standby configuration. Each component has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour. The switch is perfect.

**(i)** Derive the expression for the system reliability $R(t)$.

**(ii)** Calculate the system reliability for a mission time of $t = 100$ hours.

**(iii)** Determine the MTTF of the system.

#### **Model Solution:**

**(i) Derivation (3 marks):**

As derived in Section 3.1:

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \int_0^t \lambda e^{-\lambda \tau} \cdot e^{-\lambda(t-\tau)}\, d\tau = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$$

**(ii) Numerical evaluation (2 marks):**

Given $\lambda = 0.001$/hour, $t = 100$ hours, so $\lambda t = 0.1$.

$$R_{sys}(100) = e^{-0.1}(1 + 0.1)$$

$$e^{-0.1} = 0.9048 \quad \text{(from standard tables)}$$

$$R_{sys}(100) = 0.9048 \times 1.1 = 0.9953$$

$$\boxed{R_{sys}(100) = 0.9953 \text{ (or 99.53\%)}}$$

**(iii) MTTF (2 marks):**

$$MTTF = \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)\, dt = \frac{2}{\lambda} = \frac{2}{0.001} = 2000 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{MTTF = 2000 \text{ hours}}$$

#### **Q.A (b) — 7 Marks**

Compare the **MTTF and reliability at $t = 500$ hours** for a system using:
- **Configuration 1:** Two identical components in *cold standby* ($\lambda = 0.001$/hr).
- **Configuration 2:** Two identical components in *hot standby* (parallel active, $\lambda = 0.001$/hr).

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Cold Standby results (3 marks):**

$$R_{cold}(500) = e^{-0.5}(1 + 0.5)$$

$$e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

$$R_{cold}(500) = 0.6065 \times 1.5 = 0.9098$$

$$MTTF_{cold} = \frac{2}{0.001} = 2000 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2: Hot Standby results (3 marks):**

$$R_{hot}(500) = 2e^{-0.5} - e^{-1.0} = 2(0.6065) - 0.3679 = 1.2130 - 0.3679 = 0.8451$$

$$MTTF_{hot} = \frac{3}{2 \times 0.001} = 1500 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3: Comparison (1 mark):**

| Metric | Cold Standby | Hot Standby | Winner |
|:-------|:-------------|:------------|:-------|
| $R(500)$ | $0.9098$ | $0.8451$ | Cold Standby |
| $MTTF$ | $2000$ hrs | $1500$ hrs | Cold Standby |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often confuse the formulas. Remember:
> - **Cold Standby:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$ — the term $\lambda t$ arises from the *convolution* of failure and survival.
> - **Hot Standby:** $R(t) = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$ — both units wear out simultaneously.
> A common mistake is to use the **cold standby formula for hot standby** and vice versa, leading to a 7-mark penalty.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Q.B (a) — 7 Marks**

A **cold-standby system** has two non-identical components:
- Unit A (online): failure rate $\lambda_1 = 0.002$/hour
- Unit B (standby, activated after A fails): failure rate $\lambda_2 = 0.005$/hour
- The switch has reliability $R_s = 0.95$.

**(i)** Derive the system reliability expression $R_{sys}(t)$ accounting for the imperfect switch.

**(ii)** Evaluate $R_{sys}$ at $t = 200$ hours.

#### **Model Solution:**

**(i) Derivation (4 marks):**

From Section 3.3, the reliability with imperfect switching is:

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} + R_s \cdot \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}\left[e^{-\lambda_2 t} - e^{-\lambda_1 t}\right]$$

Substituting $\lambda_1 = 0.002$, $\lambda_2 = 0.005$, $R_s = 0.95$:

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-0.002t} + 0.95 \cdot \frac{0.002}{0.002 - 0.005}\left[e^{-0.005t} - e^{-0.002t}\right]$$

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-0.002t} + 0.95 \cdot \frac{0.002}{-0.003}\left[e^{-0.005t} - e^{-0.002t}\right]$$

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-0.002t} - 0.6333 \left[e^{-0.005t} - e^{-0.002t}\right]$$

$$\boxed{R_{sys}(t) = e^{-0.002t} + 0.6333 \left[e^{-0.002t} - e^{-0.005t}\right]}$$

**(ii) Numerical Evaluation (3 marks):**

At $t = 200$ hours:
- $\lambda_1 t = 0.002 \times 200 = 0.4$, so $e^{-0.4} = 0.6703$
- $\lambda_2 t = 0.005 \times 200 = 1.0$, so $e^{-1.0} = 0.3679$

$$R_{sys}(200) = 0.6703 + 0.6333 \times (0.6703 - 0.3679)$$

$$R_{sys}(200) = 0.6703 + 0.6333 \times 0.3024$$

$$R_{sys}(200) = 0.6703 + 0.1915$$

$$\boxed{R_{sys}(200) = 0.8618 \text{ (or 86.18\%)}}$$

---

#### **Q.B (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i)** For the system in Q.B(a), determine the MTTF.  
**(ii)** Comment on how the MTTF would change if the switch were *perfect* ($R_s = 1$).

#### **Model Solution:**

**(i) MTTF with Imperfect Switch (4 marks):**

$$MTTF = \int_0^{\infty} R_{sys}(t)\, dt = \int_0^{\infty} \left[e^{-\lambda_1 t} + R_s \cdot \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}\left(e^{-\lambda_2 t} - e^{-\lambda_1 t}\right)\right] dt$$

Splitting the integral:

$$MTTF = \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda_1 t}\, dt + R_s \cdot \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2} \int_0^{\infty} \left(e^{-\lambda_2 t} - e^{-\lambda_1 t}\right) dt$$

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda_1} + R_s \cdot \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}\left[\frac{1}{\lambda_2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_1}\right]$$
