$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda_1} + R_s \cdot \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2} \cdot \frac{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}{\lambda_1 \lambda_2}$$

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda_1} + \frac{R_s}{\lambda_2}$$

Substituting values:

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{0.002} + \frac{0.95}{0.005} = 500 + 190 = 690 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{MTTF = 690 \text{ hours}}$$

**(ii) Comparison with Perfect Switch (3 marks):**

For a perfect switch ($R_s = 1$):

$$MTTF_{perfect} = \frac{1}{\lambda_1} + \frac{1}{\lambda_2} = 500 + 200 = 700 \text{ hours}$$

$$\Delta MTTF = 700 - 690 = 10 \text{ hours}$$

> The imperfect switch reduces the MTTF by $10$ hours (a $1.43\%$ reduction). This shows that for systems with **highly reliable switches** ($R_s \to 1$), the MTTF approaches the perfect-switch value. However, in safety-critical applications, even a $1-2\%$ reduction is unacceptable — therefore, *fault-tolerant switching circuits* with redundancy are deployed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Common Mistake (MTTF with imperfect switch):**
> A frequent student error is to use the *perfect-switch MTTF formula* ($1/\lambda_1 + 1/\lambda_2$) directly, ignoring $R_s$. The correct general formula is:
> $$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda_1} + \frac{R_s}{\lambda_2}$$
> The $R_s$ factor only multiplies the **second term** because the switch is only invoked *after* unit A fails. Students who write $R_s(1/\lambda_1 + 1/\lambda_2)$ **lose 3 full marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Standby Systems (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep)**

- ☐ A **standby system** has the redundant unit *inactive* during normal operation; it is **switched online** only upon primary unit failure.
- ☐ **Cold Standby** = standby unit failure rate $\approx 0$; **Hot Standby** = standby unit failure rate equals active unit's.
- ☐ For **identical components** with perfect switch:
  - Cold Standby: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$, $MTTF = 2/\lambda$
  - Hot Standby: $R(t) = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$, $MTTF = 3/(2\lambda)$
- ☐ For **non-identical** components in cold standby: $R(t) = \dfrac{\lambda_1 e^{-\lambda_2 t} - \lambda_2 e^{-\lambda_1 t}}{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}$
- ☐ **Imperfect switch** (reliability $R_s$) multiplies *only* the standby contribution: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} + R_s \cdot [\text{standby integral term}]$
- ☐ The **MTTF** of any reliability system is $MTTF = \int_0^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$
- ☐ **Cold Standby beats Hot Standby** in $MTTF$ by **33.33%** for identical components.
- ☐ The switching mechanism's reliability is *critical*: a weak switch can wipe out the benefit of redundancy.
- ☐ **Exponential distribution** is assumed throughout (constant failure rate).
- ☐ System survives if **either** the active unit lives the full mission **or** it fails early AND the standby survives the remainder.
- ☐ In KTU board exams, always write the **setup equation first**, then perform the **convolution integral** explicitly — this fetches full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degraded Systems; Reliability Allocation: Equal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Degraded Systems; Reliability Allocation: Equal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Degraded Systems — Formal Definition

A **Degraded System** is an engineered system that, upon failure of one or more of its constituent components (or sub-systems), does **not** transition immediately to a complete "failed" state. Instead, it continues to perform its intended function, but at a **reduced level of performance, capacity, or capability**. Such systems are said to operate in one or more **degraded modes** of operation.

In the language of KTU 2024 Scheme reliability theory, a degraded system exhibits a **multi-state behaviour** rather than the classical binary "working / failed" duality. Each state corresponds to a specific combination of component health, and each carries its own performance rating and probability of occurrence.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Degraded Systems**
> A degraded system is a **multi-state reliability configuration** in which the loss of a subset of components merely *degrades* the system's output (e.g., thrust, bandwidth, throughput, cooling) rather than collapsing the entire mission. Reliability of such systems is evaluated using **degraded-mode analysis** combined with **mission reliability** and **conditional reliability** formulations.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Multi-Engine Aircraft

Imagine a long-haul commercial airliner with **four engines**. If one engine fails mid-flight, does the aircraft crash? Of course not. It continues flying on three engines — at slightly lower speed, with higher fuel consumption, and at a reduced climb rate. The aircraft has entered a **degraded mode**.

- **Full state:** 4 engines running → 100% thrust.
- **Degraded state 1:** 3 engines running → 75% thrust.
- **Degraded state 2:** 2 engines running → 50% thrust (still flyable in emergencies).
- **Failed state:** 0 or 1 engine running → unable to maintain controlled flight.

This is a perfect real-world instance of a **degraded system** — the *mission* (reaching the destination) survives partial component loss. The reliability engineer must therefore compute the probability that the aircraft **successfully completes the mission** across **all** acceptable states, not just the "all components up" state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from Pure Parallel Redundancy**
> In a pure parallel system, the system is *binary* (works or fails). In a **degraded system**, there are **multiple intermediate performance states** between "perfect" and "failed." This makes the reliability problem fundamentally a **multi-state problem** rather than a two-state Boolean problem.

### 1.3 Reliability Allocation — Formal Definition

**Reliability Allocation** is the **inverse** of the reliability prediction problem. Given a **target system reliability $R_s$** that must be achieved for a given mission time, the task is to distribute (or "allocate") this target reliability *down* to the individual components (or sub-systems) so that, when these components are configured together, the overall system meets or exceeds the requirement.

The general allocation problem can be stated mathematically as:

$$
\text{Given: } R_s(t) \text{ (target system reliability)} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \text{Find } R_1(t), R_2(t), \ldots, R_n(t)
$$

subject to feasibility constraints (cost, weight, complexity, technology maturity, etc.).

### 1.4 The *Equal* Allocation Method — Intuition

The **Equal Allocation method** is the *simplest, most naive* allocation technique. It is founded on two assumptions:

1. **All components are equally important** to the system's mission.
2. **All components are equally complex** to design, build, and qualify.

Under these assumptions, the target system reliability is divided **evenly** among the $n$ components of a series configuration. The intuition is: *"If every part is just as important and just as hard as every other part, give each part the same reliability budget."*

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Equal" is rarely used in industry but always taught in KTU**
> Real systems are highly *non-uniform* — a flight control computer is far more critical (and far more reliable) than a cabin light bulb. The Equal method is a **pedagogical starting point** that lets the student see the basic algebraic structure of allocation *before* learning weighted, AGREE, or optimal-cost methods.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Degraded System

A degraded system can be modelled as a collection of $k$ discrete performance states. For each state $j$, we define:

- **State probability $P_j$** — the probability that the system occupies state $j$ at mission time $t$.
- **Performance level $G_j$** — the fraction (or absolute value) of the *nominal* system output available in state $j$.
- **Acceptability condition** — only states satisfying $G_j \geq G_{\min}$ count as *mission success*.

| Symbol | Meaning | Units / Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $k$ | Total number of discrete performance states | Integer $\geq 2$ |
| $G_j$ | Performance level in state $j$ | $0 \le G_j \le 1$ (fraction) |
| $G_{\min}$ | Minimum acceptable performance threshold | $0 < G_{\min} \le 1$ |
| $P_j(t)$ | Probability of being in state $j$ at time $t$ | $0 \le P_j \le 1$ |
| $R_{\text{mission}}(t)$ | Mission reliability (success probability) | $0 \le R \le 1$ |
| $R_{\text{cond}}$ | Conditional reliability (given state) | $0 \le R \le 1$ |

### 2.2 Degraded-Mode Failure Rate

A particularly important concept for KTU is the **degraded-mode failure rate** of the overall system. If the system has $k$ degraded states, each with its own constant failure rate $\lambda_j$, the **effective system failure rate** is the probability-weighted sum:

$$
\lambda_{\text{sys}} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{k} P_j \,\lambda_j
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility — Why this matters**
> In aerospace, mission planners use this $\lambda_{\text{sys}}$ to compute the **probability of mission abort**. In telecom, operators use it to plan **degraded-service SLAs** (e.g., "99.9% of customers experience full bandwidth"). In power grids, it underpins **loss-of-load expectation (LOLE)** studies.

### 2.3 Conditional Reliability in Degraded Systems

When a system has entered degraded state $j$, the probability that it survives the *remaining* mission of duration $t_2$ is the **conditional reliability**:

$$
R_{\text{cond}}(t_2 \,\vert\, \text{state } j) \;=\; e^{-\lambda_j \, t_2}
$$

and the **unreliability** (probability the system collapses from a degraded state to total failure) over the residual mission is:

$$
F_{\text{degraded}}(t_2) \;=\; 1 - e^{-\lambda_j \, t_2}
$$

### 2.4 The Equal Allocation Method — Step-by-Step Logic

#### Step 1: Identify the series structure
The equal method is valid only for components in a **series configuration** (i.e., the system fails if *any single* component fails). For a series system of $n$ independent components, the system reliability is:

$$
R_s(t) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)
$$

#### Step 2: Impose the equal constraint
Set $R_1 = R_2 = \cdots = R_n = R_c$ (the *common* component reliability). Then:

$$
R_s \;=\; R_c \cdot R_c \cdots R_c \;=\; R_c^{\,n}
$$

#### Step 3: Solve for $R_c$

$$
R_c \;=\; \sqrt[n]{R_s} \;=\; R_s^{\,1/n}
$$

#### Step 4: Convert to a failure rate (if exponential life is assumed)
If each component's reliability is exponential with constant failure rate $\lambda_c$ and mission time $t$:

$$
R_c(t) \;=\; e^{-\lambda_c \, t} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \lambda_c \;=\; -\frac{\ln R_s}{n \, t}
$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Domain / Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Series-system reliability (fundamental) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $R_c = R_s^{\,1/n}$ | **Equal allocation** — component reliability | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\lambda_c = -\dfrac{\ln R_s}{n\,t}$ | Equal allocation — exponential failure rate | failures / hour |
| 4 | $\lambda_{\text{sys}} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} P_j \,\lambda_j$ | Degraded-mode effective system failure rate | failures / hour |
| 5 | $R_{\text{mission}} = \displaystyle\sum_{j\,:\,G_j \ge G_{\min}} P_j$ | Mission reliability (sum of acceptable states) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $R_{\text{cond}}(t_2 \vert j) = e^{-\lambda_j t_2}$ | Conditional reliability in state $j$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{MTBF}_{\text{sys}} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{\text{sys}}}$ | Mean time between failures (degraded) | hours |
| 8 | $G_{\text{avg}} = \displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{k} P_j \, G_j$ | Expected (average) performance level | Fraction of nominal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU convention:** In the equal allocation formula $R_c = R_s^{1/n}$, the **series assumption is non-negotiable**. If the system has any parallel redundancy, you must first collapse the parallel blocks to their *equivalent series reliabilities* before applying the equal-allocation formula.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Avionics** — Mission computers and flight-control surfaces are allocated equal reliability when redundancy is symmetric.
- **Power distribution** — Identical parallel transformers are allocated equal reliability budgets.
- **Server farms** — Identical identical blades in a rack are allocated equal reliability for simplicity.
- **Pipeline networks** — Pipe segments of equal diameter and material are allocated equal reliability.

The method is also the **baseline** against which more sophisticated methods (AGREE, effective complexity, optimal cost) are compared in KTU examination answers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equal Allocation Formula for a Series System

**Given:** A system composed of $n$ components in series. The required system reliability over mission time $t$ is $R_s(t)$. The components are **independent** and **exponentially distributed**.

**Find:** The common component reliability $R_c$ and the common component failure rate $\lambda_c$.

**Step 1 — Write the series reliability equation.** For $n$ independent components in series:

$$
R_s(t) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the equal-allocation constraint.** By definition of the equal method, all component reliabilities are identical:

$$
R_1(t) \;=\; R_2(t) \;=\; \cdots \;=\; R_n(t) \;=\; R_c(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the product.** Since each factor in the product is the same:

$$
R_s(t) \;=\; R_c(t) \cdot R_c(t) \cdot \ldots \cdot R_c(t) \quad (n \text{ times})
$$

**Step 4 — Express as a power.** The product of $n$ identical factors is a power:

$$
R_s(t) \;=\; \bigl[R_c(t)\bigr]^{n}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the $n$-th root of both sides.** Solving for $R_c$:

$$
R_c(t) \;=\; \bigl[R_s(t)\bigr]^{1/n} \;=\; \sqrt[n]{R_s(t)}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the exponential model.** For an exponential component with constant failure rate $\lambda_c$:

$$
R_c(t) \;=\; e^{-\lambda_c t}
$$

**Step 7 — Equate and take the natural logarithm.** Setting this equal to the result of Step 5:

$$
e^{-\lambda_c t} \;=\; R_s^{\,1/n}
$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $\lambda_c$.** Apply $\ln$ to both sides:

$$
-\lambda_c \, t \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\,\ln R_s
$$

$$
\lambda_c \;=\; -\frac{\ln R_s}{n \, t}
$$

This is the **final working formula** that KTU examiners expect for any equal-allocation problem in the exponential-life regime. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Degraded-Mode Effective Failure Rate

**Given:** A system with $k$ performance states. The system occupies state $j$ with probability $P_j$ and the failure rate *from* state $j$ is $\lambda_j$.

**Find:** The single effective system failure rate $\lambda_{\text{sys}}$.

**Step 1 — Interpret the situation.** The system can be in any of $k$ states at any given moment. Each state carries its own *risk* of total failure.

**Step 2 — Apply the law of total probability for failure rates.** The expected (effective) failure rate is the probability-weighted sum of state-specific rates:

$$
\lambda_{\text{sys}} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{k} P_j \,\lambda_j
$$

**Step 3 — Sanity-check the boundary cases.**

- If $k=1$ (only one state, $P_1=1$): $\lambda_{\text{sys}} = \lambda_1$. ✓
- If $\lambda_j = \lambda$ for all $j$ (homogeneous): $\lambda_{\text{sys}} = \lambda \sum P_j = \lambda$. ✓

This is the **fundamental degraded-mode aggregation formula**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Equal Allocation

**Problem.** A satellite communication system consists of $n = 5$ sub-systems in series. The mission duration is $t = 8760$ hours (one year). The required system reliability is $R_s = 0.95$. Assuming exponential life and the *equal* allocation method, find the common component reliability $R_c$ and the common component failure rate $\lambda_c$.

**Solution.**

**Step 1 — Apply the equal allocation formula:**

$$
R_c \;=\; R_s^{\,1/n} \;=\; 0.95^{\,1/5}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerical value.** Take the 5th root:

$$
R_c \;=\; 0.95^{0.2} \;\approx\; 0.98974
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to failure rate.** Use the exponential conversion formula:

$$
\lambda_c \;=\; -\frac{\ln R_s}{n \, t} \;=\; -\frac{\ln 0.95}{5 \times 8760}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the numerator.** $\ln(0.95) \approx -0.05129$, hence $-\ln(0.95) \approx 0.05129$.

**Step 5 — Divide by the denominator:**

$$
\lambda_c \;\approx\; \frac{0.05129}{43800} \;\approx\; 1.171 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per hour}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to FITs (Failures In Time, $\times 10^{9}$ per hour):**

$$
\lambda_c \;\approx\; 1171 \text{ FITs}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify by back-substitution:**

$$
R_s \;=\; R_c^{\,5} \;=\; (0.98974)^5 \;\approx\; 0.95 \quad\checkmark
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{R_c \approx 0.9897 \quad\text{and}\quad \lambda_c \approx 1.17 \times 10^{-6} \text{ hr}^{-1}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Degraded Mode Aggregation

**Problem.** A radar system has three operational states:

| State $j$ | Performance $G_j$ | Probability $P_j$ | Failure rate $\lambda_j$ (per hr) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 (Full) | 1.00 | 0.80 | $1 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| 2 (Degraded) | 0.60 | 0.15 | $5 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| 3 (Failed) | 0.00 | 0.05 | — |

Compute the effective system failure rate $\lambda_{\text{sys}}$ and the MTBF.

**Solution.**

**Step 1 — Identify acceptable states.** The mission requires $G_{\min} \ge 0.50$. States 1 and 2 qualify; state 3 does not.

**Step 2 — Compute the mission reliability:**

$$
R_{\text{mission}} \;=\; P_1 + P_2 \;=\; 0.80 + 0.15 \;=\; 0.95
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the effective system failure rate over the *acceptable* states:**

$$
\lambda_{\text{sys}} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{2} P_j \,\lambda_j \;=\; (0.80)(1 \times 10^{-5}) + (0.15)(5 \times 10^{-5})
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the sum:**

$$
\lambda_{\text{sys}} \;=\; 0.8 \times 10^{-5} + 0.75 \times 10^{-5} \;=\; 1.55 \times 10^{-5} \text{ hr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the MTBF:**

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{sys}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{sys}}} \;=\; \frac{1}{1.55 \times 10^{-5}} \;\approx\; 64516 \text{ hours}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\lambda_{\text{sys}} \approx 1.55 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{hr}^{-1}, \quad \text{MTBF} \approx 64{,}516\ \text{hours}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture — Degraded System State Machine

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the *functional architecture* of a degraded system as a **state-transition machine** with mission-reliability gating.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["STATE 0: NOMINAL FULL OPERATION G0 = 1.00"]:::ok
    S1["STATE 1: DEGRADED MODE 1 G1 = 0.75"]:::warn
    S2["STATE 2: DEGRADED MODE 2 G2 = 0.50"]:::warn
    S3["STATE 3: TOTAL FAILURE G3 = 0.00"]:::fail
    DEC["MISSION DECISION GATE  G >= Gmin ACCEPT  G < Gmin REJECT"]:::gate
    OUT["MISSION SUCCESS PROBABILITY Rmission"]:::success

    S0 -->|component failure lambda1| S1
    S0 -->|multiple failure lambda2| S2
    S1 -->|further failure lambda3| S2
    S1 -->|critical failure lambdaF| S3
    S2 -->|final failure lambdaF2| S3

    S0 --> DEC
    S1 --> DEC
    S2 --> DEC
    S3 --> DEC
    DEC -->|sum of acceptable states| OUT

    classDef ok fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#a5d6a7,color:#ffffff
    classDef warn fill:#f57f17,stroke:#ffe082,color:#000000
    classDef fail fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#ef9a9a,color:#ffffff
    classDef gate fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#90caf9,color:#ffffff
    classDef success fill:#4a148c,stroke:#ce93d8,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **Green node (S0):** the nominal, fully operational state.
- **Orange nodes (S1, S2):** intermediate degraded states with reduced capability.
- **Red node (S3):** the total-failure state, beyond recovery.
- **Blue decision gate (DEC):** enforces the minimum acceptable performance $G_{\min}$.
- **Purple output node (OUT):** aggregates the probabilities of all acceptable states to yield mission reliability $R_{\text{mission}}$.

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Equal Reliability Allocation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT: Target System Reliability Rs"]:::in
    B["INPUT: Number of Series Components n"]:::in
    C["INPUT: Mission Duration t"]:::in
    D["STEP 1: Apply Equal Constraint R1 = R2 = ... = Rn = Rc"]:::proc
    E["STEP 2: Solve Rc = Rs to the power 1 divided by n"]:::proc
    F["STEP 3: Convert to Failure Rate lambda_c = -ln Rs divided by n times t"]:::proc
    G["OUTPUT: Component Reliability Rc"]:::out
    H["OUTPUT: Component Failure Rate lambda_c"]:::out

    A --> D
    B --> D
    C --> F
    D --> E
    E --> F
    E --> G
    F --> H

    classDef in fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000000
    classDef proc fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000000
    classDef out fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **Blue nodes (A, B, C):** the three user-supplied inputs.
- **Amber nodes (D, E, F):** the three sequential algorithmic steps of the equal-allocation method.
- **Green nodes (G, H):** the two numerical deliverables required by every KTU exam answer.

### 4.3 Relationship Map — Degraded Systems vs Equal Allocation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT["RELIABILITY ENGINEERING MODULE 2"]:::root
    DS["DEGRADED SYSTEMS"]:::branch
    RA["RELIABILITY ALLOCATION"]:::branch
    EQA["EQUAL ALLOCATION"]:::leaf
    AGA["AGREE ALLOCATION"]:::leaf
    OPT["OPTIMAL / COST-BASED ALLOCATION"]:::leaf
    DMFA["DEGRADED MODE FAILURE RATE"]:::leaf
    MR["MISSION RELIABILITY"]:::leaf
    CR["CONDITIONAL RELIABILITY"]:::leaf

    ROOT --> DS
    ROOT --> RA
    RA --> EQA
    RA --> AGA
    RA --> OPT
    DS --> DMFA
    DS --> MR
    DS --> CR

    classDef root fill:#311b92,stroke:#b39ddb,color:#ffffff
    classDef branch fill:#004d40,stroke:#80cbc4,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#bf360c,stroke:#ffab91,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A.1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define a degraded system. How does it differ from a pure parallel-redundant system?**

**Model Answer:**

A **degraded system** is one that continues to operate — at a reduced level of performance, capacity, or capability — even after one or more of its components have failed. The system exhibits multiple intermediate operational states between "fully working" and "totally failed."

**Difference from a pure parallel-redundant system:**

- A *pure parallel* system is **binary**: it either works or it does not.
- A *degraded* system is **multi-state**: it can occupy any of several intermediate performance levels.

In a parallel system, the engineer is interested only in the *probability* of success or failure. In a degraded system, the engineer must also characterise the *level* of success — i.e., the *quality* of the mission, not merely its existence.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse "degraded" with "parallel." Remember — degraded refers to a **state** of the system, not a **configuration**. A system in any configuration (series, parallel, hybrid) can have degraded states.

---

**Q.A.2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**State the equal reliability allocation method and write the formula for the common component reliability.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Equal Reliability Allocation method** distributes the target system reliability $R_s$ **equally** among $n$ components of a **series** system, on the assumption that all components are equally important and equally complex.

**Formula for common component reliability:**

$$
R_c \;=\; R_s^{\,1/n}
$$

**Corresponding exponential failure rate (if mission time is $t$):**

$$
\lambda_c \;=\; -\frac{\ln R_s}{n \, t}
$$

The method is the *baseline* against which weighted, AGREE, and optimal-cost methods are compared.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 ESE rule: Each Part-B question carries 14 marks and typically has an internal choice. Two independent alternatives (Q.A and Q.B) are provided.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** A system consists of **6 identical components in series**. The target system reliability over a mission time of **2000 hours** is **0.90**. Assuming exponential life, use the **equal allocation method** to determine:

1. The common component reliability $R_c$.
2. The common component failure rate $\lambda_c$.
3. The MTBF of each component.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of a **degraded system** with a suitable block diagram. For a system with three performance states having probabilities $P_1 = 0.85$, $P_2 = 0.10$, $P_3 = 0.05$ and corresponding failure rates $\lambda_1 = 2 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{hr}^{-1}$, $\lambda_2 = 1 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{hr}^{-1}$, $\lambda_3 = 5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{hr}^{-1}$, compute the **effective system failure rate** and the **mission reliability** (assuming states 1 and 2 are acceptable). **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Apply the equal-allocation formula:**

$$
R_c \;=\; R_s^{\,1/n} \;=\; 0.90^{\,1/6}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the 6th root:**

$$
R_c \;=\; 0.90^{0.1667} \;\approx\; 0.9826
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to failure rate:**

$$
\lambda_c \;=\; -\frac{\ln R_s}{n \, t} \;=\; -\frac{\ln 0.90}{6 \times 2000}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$
\lambda_c \;=\; -\frac{(-0.10536)}{12000} \;=\; \frac{0.10536}{12000} \;\approx\; 8.78 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{hr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute MTBF:**

$$
\text{MTBF} \;=\; \frac{1}{\lambda_c} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{8.78 \times 10^{-6}} \;\approx\; 1.139 \times 10^{5}\ \text{hours}
$$

**Final Answers:**

$$
\boxed{R_c \approx 0.9826,\quad \lambda_c \approx 8.78 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{hr}^{-1},\quad \text{MTBF} \approx 1.139 \times 10^{5}\ \text{hours}}
$$

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Identify acceptable states.** States 1 and 2 are acceptable; state 3 is not.

**Step 2 — Compute mission reliability:**

$$
R_{\text{mission}} \;=\; P_1 + P_2 \;=\; 0.85 + 0.10 \;=\; 0.95
$$

**Step 3 — Compute effective system failure rate** (sum only over acceptable states):

$$
\lambda_{\text{sys}} \;=\; P_1 \lambda_1 + P_2 \lambda_2
$$

$$
\lambda_{\text{sys}} \;=\; (0.85)(2 \times 10^{-5}) + (0.10)(1 \times 10^{-4})
$$

$$
\lambda_{\text{sys}} \;=\; 1.7 \times 10^{-5} + 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \;=\; 2.7 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{hr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the system MTBF (optional follow-up):**

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{sys}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2.7 \times 10^{-5}} \;\approx\; 37037\ \text{hours}
$$

**Final Answers:**

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{mission}} = 0.95,\quad \lambda_{\text{sys}} = 2.7 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{hr}^{-1}}
$$

A suitable block diagram should accompany the answer showing three states (full / degraded / failed), the performance levels, and the mission-decision gate. *(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A control system for an unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) is composed of **4 sub-systems in series**. The mission duration is **500 hours** and the required system reliability is **0.992**. Using the **equal allocation method**, determine the common component reliability and the corresponding failure rate in FITs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With reference to **degraded systems**, derive an expression for the **degraded-mode effective failure rate**. A navigation system has two degraded states with $P_1 = 0.90$, $P_2 = 0.07$, and $\lambda_1 = 1 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{hr}^{-1}$, $\lambda_2 = 5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{hr}^{-1}$. Find the effective system failure rate and the expected performance level if $G_1 = 1.0$ and $G_2 = 0.5$. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Equal-allocation formula:**

$$
R_c \;=\; R_s^{\,1/n} \;=\; 0.992^{\,1/4}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the 4th root:**

$$
R_c \;\approx\; 0.99799
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to failure rate:**

$$
\lambda_c \;=\; -\frac{\ln R_s}{n \, t} \;=\; -\frac{\ln 0.992}{4 \times 500}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$
\lambda_c \;=\; -\frac{(-0.008032)}{2000} \;\approx\; 4.016 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{hr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to FITs** (1 FIT = $10^{-9}$ per hour):

$$
\lambda_c \;\approx\; 4016\ \text{FITs}
$$

**Final Answers:**

$$
\boxed{R_c \approx 0.9980,\quad \lambda_c \approx 4.02 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{hr}^{-1}\ \ (\approx 4016\ \text{FITs})}
$$

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation of degraded-mode effective failure rate:**

For a system with $k$ performance states, each state $j$ is occupied with probability $P_j$ and has its own failure rate $\lambda_j$. The expected rate of catastrophic failure is the probability-weighted sum:

$$
\lambda_{\text{sys}} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{k} P_j \,\lambda_j
$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$
\lambda_{\text{sys}} \;=\; (0.90)(1 \times 10^{-6}) + (0.07)(5 \times 10^{-5})
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term:**

$$
\lambda_{\text{sys}} \;=\; 9.0 \times 10^{-7} + 3.5 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 4.4 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{hr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the expected performance level:**

$$
G_{\text{avg}} \;=\; P_1 G_1 + P_2 G_2 \;=\; (0.90)(1.0) + (0.07)(0.5)
$$

$$
G_{\text{avg}} \;=\; 0.90 + 0.035 \;=\; 0.935
$$

**Final Answers:**

$$
\boxed{\lambda_{\text{sys}} = 4.4 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{hr}^{-1},\quad G_{\text{avg}} = 0.935}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the series assumption** in equal allocation: Students often blindly apply $R_c = R_s^{1/n}$ to systems that contain parallel blocks. This is wrong — first collapse parallel blocks to *equivalent series* reliabilities, *then* apply equal allocation.
> 2. **Mixing up $n$ and $1/n$:** The component reliability is always the **$n$-th root** of the system reliability, not the $n$-th power. Use $R_c = R_s^{1/n}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $\lambda_c = -\ln R_s / (nt)$: Since $\ln R_s < 0$ for $R_s < 1$, the negative sign is essential to keep $\lambda_c > 0$.
> 4. **Including the failed state in mission reliability:** When computing $R_{\text{mission}}$, exclude the failed state ($P_3$). Only sum over states where $G_j \ge G_{\min}$.
> 5. **Unit mismatches:** $t$ must be in the *same time unit* as the failure rate. If $\lambda$ is in failures/hour, $t$ must be in hours.
> 6. **Independence assumption:** The equal-allocation formula assumes components are *statistically independent*. If they share common cause failure modes, you must subtract that out first.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Degraded Systems & Equal Allocation**

- ✅ A **degraded system** has **multiple performance states** — not just working or failed.
- ✅ **Mission reliability** = sum of probabilities of all states with performance above the minimum threshold $G_{\min}$.
- ✅ **Conditional reliability** in state $j$ over residual time $t_2$ is $e^{-\lambda_j t_2}$.
- ✅ **Effective degraded-mode failure rate** is the *probability-weighted sum* of state-specific rates: $\lambda_{\text{sys}} = \sum P_j \lambda_j$.
- ✅ **Equal allocation** is the simplest method: $R_c = R_s^{1/n}$, valid **only for series systems**.
- ✅ **Exponential form** of equal allocation: $\lambda_c = -\ln R_s / (n t)$.
- ✅ The method **assumes equal importance and equal complexity** of all components.
- ✅ Always **collapse parallel blocks to equivalent series reliabilities** before applying the equal formula.
- ✅ Always use the **negative sign** in the $\lambda_c$ formula and the **same time unit** for $t$ and $\lambda$.
- ✅ **MTBF** of each component is $1/\lambda_c$ (exponential life).
- ✅ The equal method is the **baseline** for comparison with weighted, AGREE, and optimal methods.
- ✅ For multi-state (degraded) systems, **don't forget to exclude** the failed state from mission reliability.
- ✅ In KTU exams, **show all steps** of the calculation — the formula alone is *not* sufficient for full marks.
- ✅ Always **state the assumptions** (independence, exponential life, series configuration) at the start of your answer.
- ✅ **Unit conversion trap:** 1 FIT = $10^{-9}$ failures per hour. Always convert carefully.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARINC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ARINC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ARINC — Aeronautical Radio, Incorporated: Foundations in Reliability Engineering

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **ARINC (Aeronautical Radio, Incorporated)** is a U.S.-based company, originally founded in 1929 as a cooperative of airlines, radio manufacturers, and the U.S. government. It is a leading publisher of technical standards, specifications, and guidance documents for avionics, cabin systems, and ground-based aerospace systems. Within the discipline of **Reliability Engineering**, ARINC is studied as a benchmark for avionics hardware/software reliability specification, integrated modular avionics (IMA) partitioning, and standardized reliability testing of line-replaceable units (LRUs).

**Key Identity Attributes of ARINC:**

| Attribute | Specification |
|---|---|
| Full Name | Aeronautical Radio, Incorporated |
| Founded | 1929 |
| Headquarters | Annapolis, Maryland, USA |
| Acquired by | Rockwell Collins (2013), now part of Collins Aerospace (RTX/Collins) |
| Core Domain | Avionics Standards, Reliability, Maintainability |
| Standards Style | ARINC 4xx, 6xx, 7xx, 8xx series |
| Famous Standards | ARINC 429, ARINC 653, ARINC 664, ARINC 600 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Module 2** of *PEMET747 — Reliability Engineering*, ARINC is treated as an exemplar of **how industry standards translate reliability theory (MTBF, MTTR, Availability) into contractual avionics specifications**. It complements the theoretical study of reliability prediction and system effectiveness.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Highway Code of the Skies"

Imagine an airport where every aircraft mechanic, designer, and pilot follows their own rules of maintenance. One technician says, "An autopilot module should last 5,000 hours." Another says, "No, 8,000 hours." A third refuses to document failures at all. Result: chaos, crashes, and lawsuits.

Now imagine a single authoritative document — like the **Highway Code** for road users — that says:

- Every avionics box must demonstrate an **MTBF ≥ 50,000 flight hours**
- Every Line-Replaceable Unit (LRU) must be tested under **DO-160 environmental conditions**
- Every software partition must be **ARINC 653-compliant** so that a failure in a navigation app does NOT crash the autopilot.

**That authoritative document is, in spirit, ARINC.**

ARINC acts as a **standardization body** that converts the *abstract mathematics of reliability* into *concrete, auditable, contractual numbers* that any airline, manufacturer, or regulator can rely upon.

---

## 1.3 Why ARINC Matters in Reliability Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> Three reasons an avionics reliability engineer must master ARINC:
> 1. **Contractual MTBF:** Almost every LRU contract for a Boeing or Airbus aircraft specifies a minimum ARINC-derived MTBF. Failure to meet it triggers warranty penalties.
> 2. **Architectural Reliability:** ARINC 653 enforces **time and space partitioning**, a built-in architectural form of fault isolation that improves software reliability.
> 3. **Diagnostic Standards:** ARINC 624 and ARINC 615 govern how Built-In Test (BIT) and data loading must function — directly affecting **Maintainability (MTTR)** and **Availability (A)**.

**The Reliability Engineer's Typical ARINC Deliverables:**

- A **Predicted MTBF** report per ARINC methodology
- An **FMEA / FMECA** aligned with ARINC work-product templates
- **Environmental Qualification** data per RTCA DO-160 (closely tied to ARINC)
- **Diagnostic Coverage** and **BIT performance** evidence per ARINC 624

---

## 1.4 Summary Snapshot for First-Time Readers

- **ARINC = Standards** for reliable, interchangeable, certifiable avionics.
- **Module-2 Link:** Demonstrates *industrial application* of system reliability parameters (λ, MTBF, MTTR, A).
- **Pedagogical Purpose:** Converts textbook reliability formulas into **real aerospace specifications**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ARINC in the Reliability Engineering Workflow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In Module 2, you study reliability **prediction**, **allocation**, and **apportionment**. ARINC defines the *frame* inside which these are practically executed for avionics systems.

### Step 1 — Reliability Allocation to LRUs

The aircraft-level reliability requirement (e.g., **Aircraft MTBF target = 500 flight hours for full mission**) is **apportioned** down to each avionics LRU.

$$\text{System MTBF target} \rightarrow \text{Apportionment} \rightarrow \text{Per-LRU } \lambda_{\text{LRU}}$$

### Step 2 — Reliability Prediction per ARINC Methodology

ARINC endorses reliability prediction methods such as:

- **MIL-HDBK-217** (older but referenced)
- **Telcordia SR-332** (telecom-derived, often used in avionics commercial parts)
- **FIDES** (European process-based)
- **Manufacturer field data** (preferred where available)

### Step 3 — Hardware / Software Co-Design (ARINC 653)

ARINC 653 introduces the **APEX (Application/Executive) interface** between:

- The **hardware** (avionics module)
- The **application software** (autopilot, FMS, displays, TCAS)

This separation is the cornerstone of **Software Reliability** in integrated architectures.

### Step 4 — Maintainability & Diagnostic Specification

ARINC 624 defines the role of **Centralized Fault Display / Built-In Test (BITE)** and **Maintenance Message Generation** — directly setting targets for:

- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)**
- **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)**
- **False Alarm Rate**

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each ARINC Mandate

| ARINC Mandate | Reliability Reasoning |
|---|---|
| Specify MTBF per LRU | Without quantified λ, redundancy math (k-out-of-n) is unworkable |
| Enforce partitioning (653) | A failure in one function must not propagate → improves system-level λ |
| Standardize BIT (624) | Faster fault detection → lower MTTR → higher availability A |
| Mandate environmental testing | Component λ is environment-dependent; DO-160 simulates altitude, temperature, EMI |
| Specify LRU Mean Time Between Unscheduled Removals (MTBUR) | Captures real-world operational reliability, not just lab prediction |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically foundational** and used when answering ARINC-context reliability problems in KTU exams. They are NOT invented — they are standard reliability identities.

| Symbol | Formula | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\lambda$ (Failure Rate) | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Failures per hour, per cycle, etc. |
| $\text{MTBF}$ | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean operating time between failures (hours) |
| $\text{MTTR}$ | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum t_{\text{repair}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | Mean downtime per failure (hours) |
| Availability $A$ | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Fraction of time system is operational (0 ≤ A ≤ 1) |
| Inherent Availability $A_i$ | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | (Same form — best case) |
| Achieved Availability $A_a$ | $A_a = \dfrac{\text{MTBM}}{\text{MTBM} + \text{MDT}}$ | Includes logistics delay (MDT) |
| Reliability $R(t)$ | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential (constant λ) |
| Unreliability $F(t)$ | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | CDF of exponential |
| Series λ | $\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | For n independent components |
| Parallel λ (2 units) | $\lambda_p = \dfrac{2 \lambda^2}{2 \lambda + \mu}$ | Both must fail (approx) |
| Series MTBF | $\text{MTBF}_s = \left[\sum \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}_i}\right]^{-1}$ | For independent units |
| Redundancy k-out-of-n | $R_{k/n} = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$ | At least k of n must work |
| Diagnostic Coverage | $\text{DC} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DETECTED}}}{\lambda_{\text{TOTAL}}}$ | Fraction of failures detected by BIT |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Integrity Rule:** In all exam notes, write absolute value as $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ — **never** use the bare pipe `|x|` inside table cells, or the markdown table breaks.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Airbus A350 / Boeing 787** avionics contractually demand ARINC 653-compliant IMA modules with predicted λ in the order of **200–500 FITs** (failures per $10^9$ hours) per function.
- **ARINC 664 (AFDX)** uses **deterministic Ethernet** to ensure that critical control messages arrive within bounded latency — a *timeliness* form of reliability for real-time systems.
- A **typical ARINC 600 LRU** is specified to have **MTBF ≥ 30,000 hours** and a **3-level BIT** (Initiated, Continuous, Maintenance) that pushes DC above 95 %.

---

## 2.5 The Reliability Specification "Cascade" Triggered by ARINC

```
Airline / Customer Need
        │
        ▼
Aircraft-Level MTBF / Availability Target
        │
        ▼
System-Level Reliability Allocation (Apportionment)
        │
        ▼
LRU MTBF Contractual Specification (per ARINC 600/650)
        │
        ▼
Component / Part λ Prediction (per Telcordia / MIL-HDBK-217 / FIDES)
        │
        ▼
Verification by DO-160 Environmental + ARINC 624 BIT Tests
        │
        ▼
Field MTBUR / In-Service Reliability Feedback Loop
```

This cascade is precisely what Module 2 intends the student to internalize.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Series System MTBF from Component λs

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most common derivation in KTU Part-B questions on system reliability with reference to ARINC-specified LRUs.

**Given:** $n$ independent Line-Replaceable Units (LRUs) are connected in a **series reliability block diagram**, each with constant failure rate $\lambda_i$.

**Step 1 — Write the system reliability as the product (independent series):**

$$R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t}$$

**Step 2 — Combine exponents (property of exponentials):**

$$R_s(t) = e^{-\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \, t} = e^{-\lambda_s \, t}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the system failure rate:**

$$\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$

**Step 4 — Convert failure rate to MTBF (reciprocal):**

$$\text{MTBF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}$$

**Step 5 — Express in MTBF form (alternative writing):**

$$\text{MTBF}_s = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}_i}\right]^{-1}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation (ARINC context):** When an ARINC 600 LRU specification demands an MTBF of 30,000 h, and that LRU contains 5 sub-modules, the sub-modules must collectively be designed so that the reciprocal sum of their MTBFs equals 30,000 h. This is the **backbone of the Apportionment step** in the reliability cascade.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Parallel (Redundant) LRU Availability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ARINC-standard avionics (e.g., flight-control computers) are typically **dual-redundant** (k-out-of-n with n = 2, k = 2 requires both, but in *active-standby* only one is needed at a time). The student's task is to derive the **combined reliability** of two parallel LRUs.

**Given:** Two identical LRUs, each with failure rate $\lambda$ and repair rate $\mu$.

**Step 1 — Single LRU availability (Markov / steady state):**

$$A_{\text{single}} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Step 2 — Two parallel LRUs fail only when BOTH are unavailable.** For independent unavailabilities $\bar{A}_1, \bar{A}_2$:

$$\bar{A}_{\text{parallel}} = \bar{A}_1 \cdot \bar{A}_2 = \left(1 - A_{\text{single}}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Parallel availability:**

$$A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - A_{\text{single}})^2 = 2 A_{\text{single}} - A_{\text{single}}^2$$

**Step 4 — Reliability form (no repair, mission time $t$):**

$$R_{\text{parallel}}(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^2 = 2 e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2 \lambda t}$$

**Step 5 — Generalized k-out-of-n form:**

$$R_{k/n}(t) = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} \left(e^{-\lambda t}\right)^i \left(1 - e^{-\lambda t}\right)^{n-i}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** ARINC 653 (IMA) architectures pack **multiple virtual functions on shared hardware**, and rely on **partitioning + redundancy**. The above derivations prove *why* dual-redundant LRUs can push mission reliability above 0.9999 over a 10-hour flight.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — ARINC 600 LRU Apportionment

**Problem (typical KTU Part B style):**
> An aircraft avionics system consists of 3 LRUs in series. The contractual system MTBF target is **12,000 flight hours**. LRU-1 and LRU-3 are commercial-off-the-shelf (COTS) units with already-known MTBFs of **30,000 h** and **60,000 h**, respectively. Determine the MTBF required for LRU-2.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the series MTBF formula:**

$$\frac{1}{\text{MTBF}_s} = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}_1} + \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}_2} + \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}_3}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values:**

$$\frac{1}{12{,}000} = \frac{1}{30{,}000} + \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}_2} + \frac{1}{60{,}000}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the left side and the known terms:**

$$\frac{1}{12{,}000} = 8.3333 \times 10^{-5} \text{ h}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{30{,}000} = 3.3333 \times 10^{-5} \text{ h}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{60{,}000} = 1.6667 \times 10^{-5} \text{ h}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the known terms from the system requirement:**

$$\frac{1}{\text{MTBF}_2} = 8.3333 \times 10^{-5} - 3.3333 \times 10^{-5} - 1.6667 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 5 — Perform the arithmetic:**

$$\frac{1}{\text{MTBF}_2} = 8.3333 \times 10^{-5} - 5.0000 \times 10^{-5} = 3.3333 \times 10^{-5} \text{ h}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Take the reciprocal:**

$$\text{MTBF}_2 = \frac{1}{3.3333 \times 10^{-5}} = 30{,}000 \text{ h}$$

> **Answer:** LRU-2 must be designed to meet a minimum **MTBF = 30,000 flight hours**, consistent with typical ARINC 600 contractual requirements.

---

## 3.4 Derivation C — Effective Failure Rate with Diagnostic Coverage

> [!NOTE]
> ARINC 624 specifies **Built-In Test (BIT)**. Not all failures are detected. Let **DC** be the *diagnostic coverage* (fraction detected), and $\lambda_{\text{total}}$ the total LRU failure rate.

**Step 1 — Split the failure rate:**

$$\lambda_{\text{detected}} = \text{DC} \cdot \lambda_{\text{total}}$$

$$\lambda_{\text{undetected}} = (1 - \text{DC}) \cdot \lambda_{\text{total}}$$

**Step 2 — The MTTR for detected failures is **low** (BIT-guided repair).** Let $MTTR_{\text{D}}$ be the repair time for a detected failure.

**Step 3 — The MTTR for undetected failures is **high** (manual troubleshooting).** Let $MTTR_{\text{U}}$ be the repair time for an undetected failure.

**Step 4 — Effective MTTR (weighted average):**

$$\text{MTTR}_{\text{eff}} = \text{DC} \cdot MTTR_{\text{D}} + (1 - \text{DC}) \cdot MTTR_{\text{U}}$$

**Step 5 — Effective Availability:**

$$A_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}_{\text{eff}}}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A higher **DC** (better BIT) sharply reduces $MTTR_{\text{eff}}$, even if $\lambda_{\text{total}}$ stays the same. This is why ARINC 624 demands **≥ 95 % DC** for critical LRUs.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Formula in an ARINC Problem

| Exam-Style Clue in Question | Use This Formula |
|---|---|
| "LRUs in series, find system MTBF" | $\text{MTBF}_s = \left[\sum \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}_i}\right]^{-1}$ |
| "Dual-redundant, mission time $t$" | $R_p(t) = 2 e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$ |
| "k-out-of-n voting" | $R_{k/n} = \sum \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$ |
| "BIT detects X% of failures" | $MTTR_{\text{eff}} = DC \cdot MTTR_D + (1-DC) \cdot MTTR_U$ |
| "Operational availability with logistics" | $A_o = \dfrac{\text{MTBM}}{\text{MTBM} + \text{MDT}}$ |
| "Field data — exponential fit" | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ where $\lambda = N/t_{\text{total}}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — ARINC Reliability Specification Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Airline Need] --> B[Aircraft Level MTBF Target]
    B --> C[System Level Apportionment]
    C --> D1[LRU 1 MTBF Contract]
    C --> D2[LRU 2 MTBF Contract]
    C --> D3[LRU 3 MTBF Contract]
    D1 --> E1[Module Level Design]
    D2 --> E2[Module Level Design]
    D3 --> E3[Module Level Design]
    E1 --> F1[Part Level lambda Prediction]
    E2 --> F2[Part Level lambda Prediction]
    E3 --> F3[Part Level lambda Prediction]
    F1 --> G[Qualification per DO 160 and ARINC 624]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Field MTBUR Feedback to Reliability]
    H --> B
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** This is the **closed-loop ARINC reliability specification cascade**. A change in field data (H) re-opens the system-level MTBF target (B) — a true reliability engineering feedback loop.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — ARINC 653 Partitioning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HW[Avionics Hardware Module] --> OS[ARINC 653 Operating System]
    OS --> P1[Spatial Partition 1 FMS]
    OS --> P2[Spatial Partition 2 Autopilot]
    OS --> P3[Spatial Partition 3 Display]
    OS --> P4[Spatial Partition 4 TCAS]
    P1 --> T1[Time Slot 1]
    P2 --> T2[Time Slot 2]
    P3 --> T3[Time Slot 3]
    P4 --> T4[Time Slot 4]
    T1 --> M1[Major Frame Cycle]
    T2 --> M1
    T3 --> M1
    T4 --> M1
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** ARINC 653 enforces both **spatial partitioning** (memory isolation) and **temporal partitioning** (deterministic time slots). A software fault in *TCAS* cannot corrupt the *Autopilot* memory, and the *Autopilot* time slot is guaranteed — dramatically improving system-level software reliability.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Dual-Redundant LRU (Active/Standby)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Active LRU A] --> BUS[Avionics Data Bus ARINC 429 or 664]
    A2[Standby LRU B] --> BUS
    BUS --> M[Monitor and BIT Logic]
    M --> ACT1{Is A healthy?}
    ACT1 -- Yes --> USEA[Use A output]
    ACT1 -- No --> USEB[Switch to B]
    USEA --> OUT[Output to cockpit]
    USEB --> OUT
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The BIT logic continuously monitors Active LRU *A*. If a fault is detected (DC-dependent), the system switches to Standby LRU *B*. This is the **reliability-kernel** behind ARINC-compliant flight-critical computers.

---

## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture — ARINC-Compliant LRU Internals

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PWR[Power Supply Module] --> PWRM[Power Monitoring BIT]
    CPU[Processor and Memory] --> CPUM[Processor BIT]
    IO[Input Output Interface ARINC 429] --> IOM[IO BIT]
    SENSOR[Sensor Signal Conditioning] --> SENSORM[Sensor BIT]
    PWRM --> BITPROC[Central BIT Processor]
    CPUM --> BITPROC
    IOM --> BITPROC
    SENSORM --> BITPROC
    BITPROC --> BITE[External BITE Port per ARINC 624]
    BITPROC --> ALERT[Cockpit Fault Display]
    CPU --> APEX[ARINC 653 APEX Interface]
    APEX --> APPS[Application Software]
    APPS --> OUT1[Flight Function 1]
    APPS --> OUT2[Flight Function 2]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** A single physical LRU hosts:
> - **Functional blocks** (Power, CPU, I/O, Sensors) each with local BIT.
> - **Central BIT Processor** that aggregates fault data → external BITE and cockpit alert.
> - **APEX / ARINC 653** layer that lets multiple software functions safely co-exist.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define ARINC and state its relevance to Reliability Engineering.
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** **ARINC (Aeronautical Radio, Incorporated)** is a U.S. aerospace standards body, founded in 1929, that publishes technical specifications for avionics, cabin systems, and related ground equipment.
- **(1 mark)** It defines contractual reliability targets (e.g., **MTBF per LRU ≥ 30,000 h**), maintainability procedures (**ARINC 624 BIT**), and architectural fault-containment rules (**ARINC 653 partitioning**).
- **(1 mark)** In *Reliability Engineering*, ARINC translates abstract reliability parameters (λ, MTBF, MTTR, A) into industrial specifications that govern design, qualification, and field performance of avionics systems.

---

### Q2. What is the role of ARINC 653 in software reliability of Integrated Modular Avionics (IMA)?
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** ARINC 653 defines the **APEX (Application/Executive) interface** between the **Operating System** of an IMA module and the **multiple application software** that share that module.
- **(1 mark)** It mandates **spatial partitioning** (memory isolation between applications) and **temporal partitioning** (deterministic time-slot scheduling) so that a fault in one application cannot corrupt another.
- **(1 mark)** The result is a **fault-contained software architecture** that significantly improves system-level software reliability and supports **k-out-of-n** redundancy at function level.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When answering ARINC-based numerical problems, students often **skip the assumption statement** (e.g., "exponential distribution, constant λ, independent units"). Examiners explicitly award 1 mark for this — and 1 mark for stating units. **State assumptions + units** at the top of your solution.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** *(7 marks)* With the help of a block diagram, describe the **ARINC reliability specification cascade** from the aircraft level down to the component level. Highlight the role of MTBF allocation, environmental qualification (DO-160), and field MTBUR feedback.
>
> **(b)** *(7 marks)* An aircraft avionics system has **three LRUs in series** with contractual MTBF targets of **20,000 h, 30,000 h, and 60,000 h** respectively. Calculate (i) the **system MTBF**, (ii) the **system reliability for a 5-hour flight**, and (iii) the **system availability** if the MTTR for the whole system is **0.5 h**.

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

- **(2 marks)** **Block diagram** (see Section 4.1 of these notes) showing the four-tier cascade:
  Aircraft-Level MTBF → System Apportionment → LRU MTBF Contracts → Component λ Prediction.
- **(2 marks)** **MTBF allocation**: The aircraft-level MTBF is apportioned to subsystems using methods such as **equal apportionment, AGREE apportionment, or ARINC-weighted apportionment**, producing per-LRU λ targets.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Environmental qualification (RTCA DO-160)**: Each LRU must be tested under altitude, temperature, vibration, EMI, and lightning conditions representative of the airframe environment. DO-160 test reports are submitted as part of the LRU's reliability evidence package.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Field MTBUR feedback**: After delivery, the airline reports **Mean Time Between Unscheduled Removals (MTBUR)**. Discrepancies between predicted MTBF and observed MTBUR trigger a **reliability growth** program (e.g., Duane model, AMSAA).

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

- **(0.5 marks)** **Assumption statement:** LRUs are independent, failure rates are constant, exponential distribution applies.

- **(1 mark) (i) System MTBF:**

$$\frac{1}{\text{MTBF}_s} = \frac{1}{20{,}000} + \frac{1}{30{,}000} + \frac{1}{60{,}000}$$

$$\frac{1}{\text{MTBF}_s} = 5.00 \times 10^{-5} + 3.33 \times 10^{-5} + 1.67 \times 10^{-5} = 1.00 \times 10^{-4} \text{ h}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTBF}_s = 10{,}000 \text{ hours}}$$

- **(1 mark) (ii) System failure rate:**

$$\lambda_s = 1.00 \times 10^{-4} \text{ h}^{-1}$$

- **(1.5 marks) (iii) System reliability for t = 5 h:**

$$R_s(5) = e^{-\lambda_s \cdot t} = e^{-1.00 \times 10^{-4} \times 5} = e^{-5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$R_s(5) = 0.999500 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

- **(1.5 marks) (iv) System availability:**

$$A_s = \frac{\text{MTBF}_s}{\text{MTBF}_s + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}000 + 0.5} = 0.9999500$$

$$\boxed{A_s \approx 0.99995 \;\; (99.995\,\%)}$$

- **(1 mark) Engineering interpretation:** An availability of **0.99995** means that, on average, the system is unavailable less than 26 minutes per year — meeting the **dispatch availability** typical of ARINC 600 commercial avionics.

---

### Part B — Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the significance of **ARINC 653 spatial and temporal partitioning** in improving system software reliability of an Integrated Modular Avionics (IMA) module. Use a labelled block diagram.
>
> **(b)** *(7 marks)* A flight management computer is **dual-redundant** (two identical LRUs, each with **MTBF = 50,000 h** and **MTTR = 2 h**). Assuming exponential failures, compute: (i) the **single-LRU availability**, (ii) the **dual-redundant parallel availability**, and (iii) the **reliability of the parallel configuration for a 10-hour mission**. Comment on the value of redundancy in ARINC avionics.

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

- **(2 marks)** **Block diagram** (see Section 4.2 of these notes) — Hardware → ARINC 653 OS → Spatial Partitions → Time Slots → Major Frame.
- **(2 marks)** **Spatial partitioning** means each application owns a **private memory region**; one application's memory fault cannot overwrite another's data. This **eliminates common-mode software failures** at memory level.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Temporal partitioning** means each application is scheduled into a **deterministic time slot** within a repeating **Major Frame** (e.g., 40 ms cycle). A runaway task in one slot cannot consume another slot's CPU time.
- **(1.5 marks)** **Result for reliability:** The combined effect is that software-induced failures in one function are **locally contained**. The system-level software failure rate is approximated as the **sum of the contained rates**, dramatically lower than the unpartitioned case.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

- **(0.5 marks)** **Assumption:** Identical LRUs, independent failures, exponential distribution.

- **(1.5 marks) (i) Single-LRU availability:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{50{,}000}{50{,}000 + 2} = \frac{50{,}000}{50{,}002} \approx 0.99996$$

- **(1.5 marks) (ii) Dual-redundant parallel availability:**

The system fails only when **both** LRUs are unavailable. Unavailability of each:

$$\bar{A}_1 = 1 - 0.99996 = 4.0 \times 10^{-5}$$

Parallel unavailability:

$$\bar{A}_p = (\bar{A}_1)^2 = (4.0 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 1.6 \times 10^{-9}$$

Parallel availability:

$$\boxed{A_p = 1 - 1.6 \times 10^{-9} \approx 0.999999998 \;\; (99.9999998\,\%)}$$

- **(1.5 marks) (iii) Reliability for 10-hour mission:**

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{50{,}000} = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ h}^{-1}$$

Single-LRU reliability:

$$R_1(10) = e^{-2.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 10} = e^{-2.0 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.9998000$$

Dual-redundant reliability (mission, no repair):

$$R_p(10) = 2 R_1(10) - [R_1(10)]^2$$

$$R_p(10) = 2(0.9998000) - (0.9998000)^2 = 1.9996000 - 0.9996000 = 0.99999996$$

- **(1.5 marks) (iv) Engineering comment:** Dual redundancy raises **mission reliability** from 0.9998 (single) to **0.99999996** (dual) for a 10-hour flight. The **availability** rises from "four nines" to "almost ten nines". This is the mathematical justification for ARINC-mandated dual-redundancy in flight-critical avionics.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in ARINC-based reliability questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting assumptions** (constant λ, exponential, independence) — *lose ~1 mark per part*.
> 2. **Confusing MTBF and MTBUR** — MTBF is for *non-repairable* components during useful life; MTBUR is the *operational* analog including no-fault-found removals. Examiners deduct marks for misusing them.
> 3. **Summing MTBFs of series components** (a frequent error) — the correct operation is the **sum of reciprocals**, not the sum of MTBFs.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert units** (hours vs. flight hours vs. cycles).
> 5. **Skipping the engineering interpretation** in part (b) — ARINC questions specifically demand a "real-world comment" tying the math to an avionics application. Examiners explicitly allocate **1–2 marks** for this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top-of-mind bullets for last-minute revision before KTU exam on ARINC (Module 2, PEMET747):**

- **ARINC** = Aeronautical Radio, Incorporated; sets contractual reliability/maintainability norms for avionics.
- The **cascade** runs: Aircraft MTBF → Apportionment → LRU MTBF → Part λ → DO-160 qualification → Field MTBUR feedback.
- **ARINC 600** → LRU form-factor and electrical interface.
- **ARINC 624** → Built-In Test (BIT), maintenance messages, diagnostic coverage.
- **ARINC 653** → Integrated Modular Avionics — **spatial + temporal partitioning** for software reliability.
- **ARINC 664 (AFDX)** → Deterministic Ethernet for time-critical data.
- **MTBF** and **λ** are reciprocals: $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$, $\lambda = 1/\text{MTBF}$.
- **Series system MTBF** uses **reciprocal-sum** form, **NOT** arithmetic sum of MTBFs.
- **Parallel system** reliability (no repair) = $1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2)$.
- **k-out-of-n** formula: $R_{k/n} = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$.
- **Availability** = $\text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$ — a *steady-state* measure.
- **Reliability** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is the *mission* (time-dependent) measure.
- **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** = fraction of failures detected by BIT; high DC ⇒ low MTTR ⇒ high A.
- Always **state assumptions** (exponential, independent, constant λ) before solving.
- Always **state units** in your final answer (hours, dimensionless, etc.).
- Always **end with an engineering interpretation** ("this 0.99995 availability means …") to claim the interpretation mark.
- **DO-160** is the **environmental test standard** that backs ARINC LRUs — it is RTCA-published but contractually co-referenced by ARINC.
- **MTBUR** (Mean Time Between Unscheduled Removals) is the *field* reliability indicator; it can be lower than predicted MTBF due to **no-fault-found** removals and **Canary** failures.
- **Reliability Growth Models** (Duane, AMSAA-Crow) are typically invoked when field MTBUR falls short of predicted MTBF, feeding back into the cascade.

> **Final Note:** The reliability formulas in this topic are **identical to those in the textbook reliability chapters** — ARINC adds the *industrial* and *contractual* dimension. Mastering both the **math** and the **ARINC context** is what scores a full 14/14 in Part B.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proportional Apportionments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Proportional Apportionments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Proportional Apportionment** is a deterministic reliability allocation technique used during the early design phase of complex engineering systems (such as aircraft, automobiles, electronic networks, and process plants) to distribute the overall system unreliability budget among its constituent subsystems or components in direct proportion to pre-defined weighting factors.

Mathematically, the system unreliability $Q_s$ (or equivalently the system failure rate $\lambda_s$ for an exponential model) is partitioned such that the apportioned unreliability of the $i^{th}$ subsystem, $Q_i$, is given by the relation:

$$Q_i = \left( \frac{w_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j} \right) \cdot Q_s$$

where $w_i$ represents the **weighting factor** of the $i^{th}$ subsystem, and the denominator is the aggregate of all weighting factors in the architecture.

The most commonly adopted weighting criteria in KTU-prescribed syllabus frameworks are:
1. **Complexity Factor ($C_i$)** – proportional to the number of components.
2. **Criticality Factor ($K_i$)** – severity-weighted.
3. **Mission Time Factor ($t_i$)** – operational exposure duration.
4. **Environmental Stress Factor ($E_i$)** – thermal, vibrational, or humidity loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Proportional Apportionment falls under the broader umbrella of *Reliability Allocation* methods. It is a **non-optimization, non-ARINC** approach — purely algebraic and direct, making it one of the easiest allocation schemes to apply during preliminary system design.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a group of **five friends** sharing a single pizza that costs ₹500, but the bill must be split **proportional to the number of slices each one ate**. If the weights (slices consumed) are 2, 3, 4, 1, and 5, the total slices = 15. Friend 1 pays $\frac{2}{15} \times 500$ = ₹66.67, Friend 2 pays ₹100, and so on. **Proportional Apportionment in reliability works on exactly the same logic**, except instead of money, the "currency" being split is the **allowable system unreliability** $Q_s$.

The engineering intuition is straightforward: **a subsystem that is more complex, more critical, or operated for longer durations should absorb a proportionally larger share of the unreliability budget.** This ensures that the system engineer does not over-specify a simple subassembly (wasting cost and mass) nor under-specify a critical one (compromising safety).

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant of Standardisation:** The only "constant" inherent to the proportional method is the **Sum of Weights Normalisation Constraint**:
> $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i = Q_s$$
> This constraint guarantees that the allocated unreliabilities, when combined through the system reliability block diagram (series configuration assumed), exactly reproduce the top-level reliability requirement.

---

## 1.3 Why Proportional Apportionment is Taught in Reliability Engineering

In KTU's Reliability Engineering (PEMET747) curriculum, this topic forms the foundational building block for advanced allocation techniques such as:
- **AGREE (Advisory Group on Reliability of Electronic Equipment) method**
- **ARINC Apportionment method**
- **Goal Structuring Notation (GSN) methods**

A student mastering proportional apportionment first develops the **cognitive muscle** of how reliability "flows down" from system-level specifications to sub-system level targets — a skill directly examined in **Part B (14-mark) questions** of the KTU End Semester Examination.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Proportional Apportionment

The procedural flow of the proportional apportionment algorithm follows six discrete logic steps:

- **Step 1 — Identify System Architecture:** Decompose the system into $n$ functional subsystems using a Reliability Block Diagram (RBD). For proportional apportionment, the standard assumption is a **series configuration** (since any series failure breaks the chain).

- **Step 2 — Compute Weighting Vector:** For every subsystem $i$, evaluate its weighting factor $w_i$ from the chosen criterion. If complexity is the metric, then $w_i$ equals the **number of replaceable units (NRUs)** in that subsystem, e.g., $w_i = N_i$.

- **Step 3 — Aggregate Weights:** Compute the denominator $\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j$, which acts as the *normalisation factor* ensuring the allocations sum correctly.

- **Step 4 — Define System-Level Target:** Specify either $R_s$ (system reliability goal) or $Q_s = 1 - R_s$ as the top-down requirement. The KTU board typically provides this directly in numerical problems.

- **Step 5 — Apportion Each Subsystem:** Apply $Q_i = \frac{w_i}{\sum w_j} \cdot Q_s$ for every $i$.

- **Step 6 — Convert to Reliability Targets:** Convert back using $R_i = 1 - Q_i$ for reporting to the design team, or to failure rate $\lambda_i = -\frac{\ln R_i}{t}$ if a constant failure rate is mandated.

### 2.1.1 The Underlying 'Why'

- **Why proportional?** It is *unbiased* and *transparent*. There is no subjective optimisation; every subsystem is treated by a single arithmetic rule.
- **Why use complexity?** Empirical reliability data (MIL-HDBK-217 style) shows that more components statistically yield more failures, so larger $N_i$ justifies higher $Q_i$.
- **Why normalise?** To preserve the **series reliability identity** $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$, which requires that the multiplicative chain yields exactly $R_s$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following consolidated table encapsulates every formula a KTU 2024 student must memorise for this topic.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables / Units | Boundary Conditions / Validity |
|---|--------------|--------------------------|--------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | **Unreliability Apportionment (Complexity-Based)** | $Q_i = \dfrac{N_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} N_j} \cdot Q_s$ | $Q_i$ = subsystem unreliability (dimensionless), $N_i$ = number of components | Series configuration, small $Q$ (≲ 0.1) for linearisation |
| 2 | **Failure Rate Apportionment** | $\lambda_i = \dfrac{N_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} N_j} \cdot \lambda_s$ | $\lambda_i$ in **failures/hour**, $\lambda_s$ system rate | Assumes exponential distribution, constant failure rate |
| 3 | **Time-Weighted Apportionment** | $Q_i = \dfrac{N_i t_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} N_j t_j} \cdot Q_s$ | $t_i$ = mission duration of subsystem $i$ in hours | For systems with non-uniform operating intervals |
| 4 | **Criticality-Weighted Apportionment** | $Q_i = \dfrac{N_i K_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} N_j K_j} \cdot Q_s$ | $K_i$ = criticality factor (dimensionless integer) | Aerospace, military, and safety-critical domains |
| 5 | **Subsystem Reliability Goal** | $R_i = 1 - Q_i$ | Dimensionless | Always in $[0, 1]$ |
| 6 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $\text{MTBF}_i = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_i}$ | Hours | For exponential distribution only |
| 7 | **Verification Identity** | $R_s \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Dimensionless | Exact if all $Q_i$ are statistically independent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Note for Students:** When writing $R_i$ in your answer scripts, **always use `\vert` or `\mid`** instead of the bare pipe character `\vert` inside table rows, since KTU's online answer-upload portal sometimes mis-renders raw pipe characters in markdown. This same caution applies to absolute-value notations like $\vert x \vert$.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

Proportional apportionment is heavily utilised in the following engineering verticals:

- **Aerospace Engineering:** Pre-Phase A NASA / ISRO missions where reliability targets (typically $R_s \geq 0.999$) are cascaded to propulsion, avionics, and structural subsystems using complexity ratios.
- **Automotive Industry:** ISO 26262-compliant functional safety allocation in Electronic Control Units (ECUs).
- **Telecommunications:** Base transceiver station reliability budgeting across RF, baseband, and power modules.
- **Nuclear Power Plant Design:** Apportioning reactor trip-system unreliability among sensors, logic solvers, and actuators.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphone motherboard reliability budgets across SoC, PMIC, and RF subsystems during early concept design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Proportional Apportionment Formula

The proportional apportionment formula is derived from the **series reliability identity**. Consider a system composed of $n$ independent subsystems in series. The system reliability is the product of individual reliabilities:

$$R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

Taking natural logarithms of both sides:

$$\ln R_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln R_i$$

For small unreliabilities (a standard KTU assumption when $Q_s \leq 0.1$), we apply the approximation $\ln(1 - Q) \approx -Q$. Thus:

$$\ln R_s \approx -Q_s \quad \text{and} \quad \ln R_i \approx -Q_i$$

Substituting these approximations:

$$-Q_s \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} (-Q_i) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$$

Cancelling the negative sign:

$$Q_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$$

Now we impose the proportionality constraint: each $Q_i$ must be in proportion to its weighting factor $w_i$. Therefore:

$$Q_i = k \cdot w_i$$

where $k$ is the proportionality constant. Summing over all subsystems:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i = k \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = Q_s$$

Solving for $k$:

$$k = \frac{Q_s}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$$

Back-substituting into $Q_i = k \cdot w_i$:

$$\boxed{Q_i = \left( \frac{w_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j} \right) \cdot Q_s}$$

This completes the rigorous derivation of the proportional apportionment identity.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A satellite communication system is to be designed with a target reliability of **$R_s = 0.95$** over a mission time of **$t = 1000$ hours**. The system consists of three subsystems in series:

- **Subsystem A (Antenna Assembly):** Contains $N_A = 50$ components.
- **Subsystem B (Transmitter Electronics):** Contains $N_B = 200$ components.
- **Subsystem C (Power Supply Unit):** Contains $N_C = 150$ components.

Using **proportional apportionment based on complexity**, determine the allocated reliability and failure rate for each subsystem.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the system unreliability:**

$$Q_s = 1 - R_s = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05$$

**Step 2 — Compute the aggregate weight (sum of components):**

$$\sum_{j=1}^{3} N_j = N_A + N_B + N_C = 50 + 200 + 150 = 400 \text{ components}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the proportionality constant:**

$$k = \frac{Q_s}{\sum_{j=1}^{3} N_j} = \frac{0.05}{400} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Apportion unreliability to each subsystem:**

For Subsystem A:

$$Q_A = k \cdot N_A = (1.25 \times 10^{-4}) \times 50 = 6.25 \times 10^{-3}$$

For Subsystem B:

$$Q_B = k \cdot N_B = (1.25 \times 10^{-4}) \times 200 = 2.50 \times 10^{-2}$$

For Subsystem C:

$$Q_C = k \cdot N_C = (1.25 \times 10^{-4}) \times 150 = 1.875 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the apportionment identity:**

$$Q_A + Q_B + Q_C = 0.00625 + 0.025 + 0.01875 = 0.0500 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Compute allocated reliabilities:**

$$R_A = 1 - Q_A = 1 - 0.00625 = 0.99375$$

$$R_B = 1 - Q_B = 1 - 0.02500 = 0.97500$$

$$R_C = 1 - Q_C = 1 - 0.01875 = 0.98125$$

**Step 7 — Compute allocated failure rates (assuming exponential distribution):**

Using $\lambda_i = -\frac{\ln R_i}{t}$:

$$\lambda_A = -\frac{\ln(0.99375)}{1000} = -\frac{(-0.006266)}{1000} = 6.266 \times 10^{-6} \text{ failures/hour}$$

$$\lambda_B = -\frac{\ln(0.97500)}{1000} = -\frac{(-0.025318)}{1000} = 2.5318 \times 10^{-5} \text{ failures/hour}$$

$$\lambda_C = -\frac{\ln(0.98125)}{1000} = -\frac{(-0.018923)}{1000} = 1.8923 \times 10^{-5} \text{ failures/hour}$$

**Step 8 — Compute allocated MTBF:**

$$\text{MTBF}_A = \frac{1}{\lambda_A} = \frac{1}{6.266 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 159,592 \text{ hours}$$

$$\text{MTBF}_B = \frac{1}{\lambda_B} = \frac{1}{2.5318 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 39,497 \text{ hours}$$

$$\text{MTBF}_C = \frac{1}{\lambda_C} = \frac{1}{1.8923 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 52,847 \text{ hours}$$

### 3.2.3 Analytical Matrix of Results

| Subsystem | Components $N_i$ | Weight Fraction $\frac{N_i}{\sum N_j}$ | Allocated $Q_i$ | Allocated $R_i$ | Allocated $\lambda_i$ (per hr) | Allocated MTBF (hours) |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------------------------|------------------|------------------|------------------------------|------------------------|
| A — Antenna | 50 | 0.125 | $6.25 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.99375 | $6.266 \times 10^{-6}$ | 159,592 |
| B — Transmitter | 200 | 0.500 | $2.50 \times 10^{-2}$ | 0.97500 | $2.5318 \times 10^{-5}$ | 39,497 |
| C — Power Supply | 150 | 0.375 | $1.875 \times 10^{-2}$ | 0.98125 | $1.8923 \times 10^{-5}$ | 52,847 |
| **System Total** | **400** | **1.000** | **0.0500** | **0.9500** | — | — |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Insight

The Transmitter (Subsystem B), with 50% of the components, has been allocated **50% of the system unreliability budget** — the heaviest burden. This is precisely the *engineering trade-off* captured by proportional apportionment: design effort must now be concentrated on Subsystem B to meet its tight reliability target, while Subsystem A enjoys a relaxed target, allowing the use of cheaper, lighter, or simpler components.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Proportional Apportionment

The Mermaid block diagram below captures the complete procedural flow of the proportional apportionment algorithm, from system-level requirement capture down to subsystem-level MTBF assignment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start: System Reliability Requirement Rs]) --> inputNode[Input: System Unreliability Qs and Mission Time t]
    inputNode --> archNode{Identify Series RBD Architecture}
    archNode --> compNode[Compute Weighting Vector wi for each Subsystem]
    compNode --> sumNode[Aggregate Sum of Weights Sigma wj]
    sumNode --> kNode[Compute Proportionality Constant k = Qs / Sigma wj]
    kNode --> apporNode[Apportion Qi = k times wi for each Subsystem]
    apporNode --> convNode[Convert to Reliability Ri = 1 minus Qi]
    convNode --> lambdaNode[Derive Failure Rate lambdai using exponential model]
    lambdaNode --> mtbfNode[Compute MTBFi = 1 divided by lambdai]
    mtbfNode --> verifyNode{Verify Identity: Sum Qi equals Qs}
    verifyNode -- Yes --> outNode([Output: Subsystem Reliability and MTBF Targets])
    verifyNode -- No --> errorNode[Recheck Weighting Vector and Allocation]
    errorNode --> compNode
    classDef io fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef error fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    class startNode,outNode io
    class inputNode,compNode,sumNode,kNode,apporNode,convNode,lambdaNode,mtbfNode process
    class archNode,verifyNode decision
    class errorNode error
```

## 4.2 Subsystem-Level Allocation Cascade Diagram

The following diagram illustrates the *cascading* nature of proportional apportionment, showing how a single top-level requirement branches into three subsystem-level targets proportional to component counts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sysBlock[System Level: Rs = 0.95, Qs = 0.05] --> splitBlock{Proportional Split by Complexity}
    splitBlock --> blockA[Subsystem A: NA = 50, QA = 0.00625, RA = 0.99375]
    splitBlock --> blockB[Subsystem B: NB = 200, QB = 0.025, RB = 0.975]
    splitBlock --> blockC[Subsystem C: NC = 150, QC = 0.01875, RC = 0.98125]
    blockA --> mtbfA[MTBFA approx 159592 hours]
    blockB --> mtbfB[MTBFB approx 39497 hours]
```
