    blockC --> mtbfC[MTBFC approx 52847 hours]

    classDef sub fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class mtbfA,mtbfB,mtbfC mtbf
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define proportional apportionment of reliability. State the formula used for allocating unreliability among subsystems based on component complexity.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Proportional apportionment is a top-down reliability allocation technique in which the overall system unreliability $Q_s$ is divided among the subsystems in direct proportion to their weighting factors, most commonly the number of components $N_i$ contained in each subsystem. *(1 mark)*

The formula used for unreliability allocation based on component complexity is:

$$Q_i = \left( \frac{N_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} N_j} \right) \cdot Q_s$$

where $N_i$ is the number of components in the $i^{th}$ subsystem, $Q_s$ is the system unreliability, and $n$ is the total number of subsystems. *(2 marks)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List any three weighting factors used in proportional apportionment of reliability.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three most commonly used weighting factors in proportional apportionment are: *(1 mark each)*

1. **Complexity Factor ($N_i$ or $C_i$):** Number of components in the subsystem.
2. **Criticality Factor ($K_i$):** Severity-weighted importance of the subsystem in mission success or safety.
3. **Mission Time Factor ($t_i$):** Operational duration (in hours) for which the subsystem is actively functioning.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT skip the verification step** $\sum Q_i = Q_s$. Many students lose **2 full marks** by omitting this proof.
> - **Do NOT confuse** proportional apportionment with **AGREE method**. AGREE uses an *exponential weighting formula* ($\lambda_i = N_i \lambda_s / N_s \cdot E_i$), whereas proportional apportionment uses a *linear multiplicative formula*.
> - **Always show the substitution** of $w_i$ values into the formula explicitly — vague "by formula" answers fetch only partial marks.
> - **Units check:** Failure rate $\lambda_i$ MUST be expressed in *failures/hour* or *failures/million hours* (FITS), not left dimensionless.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the proportional apportionment formula for unreliability allocation, starting from the series reliability identity. State clearly any assumptions made during derivation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A radar tracking system consists of four subsystems in series: Transmitter ($N_1 = 80$ components), Receiver ($N_2 = 120$ components), Signal Processor ($N_3 = 200$ components), and Display Unit ($N_4 = 100$ components). The system reliability requirement over a 500-hour mission is $R_s = 0.96$. Using proportional apportionment, compute the allocated reliability and failure rate for each subsystem. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Series Reliability Identity:**

$$R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Take natural logarithm:**

$$\ln R_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln R_i \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply small-unreliability approximation $\ln(1 - Q) \approx -Q$:**

$$\ln(1 - Q_s) \approx -Q_s \quad \text{and} \quad \ln(1 - Q_i) \approx -Q_i \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute to obtain:**

$$Q_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5 — Impose proportionality constraint $Q_i = k \cdot w_i$:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i = k \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = Q_s \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $k$ and back-substitute:**

$$k = \frac{Q_s}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_i = \frac{w_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j} \cdot Q_s \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 7 — State assumptions:** *(1 mark)*
- Subsystems are statistically independent.
- System is in series configuration.
- Unreliabilities are small ($Q_s \leq 0.1$) for the linear approximation to hold.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute system unreliability:**

$$Q_s = 1 - R_s = 1 - 0.96 = 0.04 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Aggregate weights:**

$$\sum_{j=1}^{4} N_j = 80 + 120 + 200 + 100 = 500 \quad \text{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $k$:**

$$k = \frac{Q_s}{\sum N_j} = \frac{0.04}{500} = 8 \times 10^{-5} \quad \text{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Step 4 — Apportion each subsystem's unreliability:**

$$Q_1 = 8 \times 10^{-5} \times 80 = 6.4 \times 10^{-3}, \quad R_1 = 0.9936$$

$$Q_2 = 8 \times 10^{-5} \times 120 = 9.6 \times 10^{-3}, \quad R_2 = 0.9904$$

$$Q_3 = 8 \times 10^{-5} \times 200 = 1.6 \times 10^{-2}, \quad R_3 = 0.9840$$

$$Q_4 = 8 \times 10^{-5} \times 100 = 8.0 \times 10^{-3}, \quad R_4 = 0.9920$$

*(2 marks for tabulated apportionment)*

**Step 5 — Compute allocated failure rates over $t = 500$ hours:**

$$\lambda_i = -\frac{\ln R_i}{t}$$

$$\lambda_1 = -\frac{\ln(0.9936)}{500} = 1.282 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per hour}$$

$$\lambda_2 = -\frac{\ln(0.9904)}{500} = 1.924 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per hour}$$

$$\lambda_3 = -\frac{\ln(0.9840)}{500} = 3.218 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per hour}$$

$$\lambda_4 = -\frac{\ln(0.9920)}{500} = 1.609 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per hour}$$

*(1.5 marks for $\lambda$ computation)*

**Step 6 — Verification:**

$$\sum Q_i = 0.0064 + 0.0096 + 0.016 + 0.008 = 0.04 \quad \checkmark$$

*(0.5 mark)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of proportional apportionment with an appropriate real-world analogy. List any four weighting factors used in reliability allocation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An industrial process control system consists of three subsystems: Sensor Module (40 components, mission time 800 hours), Controller Module (120 components, mission time 1000 hours), and Actuator Module (90 components, mission time 600 hours). The system reliability goal over a 1000-hour mission is $R_s = 0.98$. Using **time-weighted proportional apportionment**, determine the allocated reliability and MTBF for each subsystem. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Conceptual Explanation (7 Marks):**

**Proportional Apportionment Defined:** *(2 marks)*
Proportional apportionment is a deterministic, top-down reliability budgeting technique that distributes the system-level unreliability $Q_s$ among constituent subsystems using a linear proportionality rule based on pre-selected weighting factors. Each subsystem receives a share of $Q_s$ directly proportional to its weighting factor $w_i$, ensuring that $\sum Q_i = Q_s$.

**Real-World Analogy:** *(2 marks)*
Consider an office of 50 employees sharing a single corporate bonus pool of ₹10 lakhs. If bonuses are to be divided *proportional to years of service*, then an employee with 5 years' tenure receives $\frac{5}{\sum \text{years}} \times 10{,}00{,}000$. Proportional apportionment operates identically, except the "bonus pool" is the *system unreliability budget*, and the "years of service" is replaced by *complexity*, *criticality*, or *mission time* factors.

**Four Weighting Factors:** *(3 marks — 0.75 each)*
1. **Complexity Factor ($N_i$):** Count of components in subsystem.
2. **Criticality Factor ($K_i$):** Severity of failure consequence.
3. **Mission Time Factor ($t_i$):** Operational exposure duration.
4. **Environmental Stress Factor ($E_i$):** Harshness of operating conditions.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute system unreliability:**

$$Q_s = 1 - 0.98 = 0.02 \quad \text{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute time-weighted complexity products $N_i t_i$:**

For Sensor Module:

$$N_1 t_1 = 40 \times 800 = 32{,}000 \text{ component-hours}$$

For Controller Module:

$$N_2 t_2 = 120 \times 1000 = 120{,}000 \text{ component-hours}$$

For Actuator Module:

$$N_3 t_3 = 90 \times 600 = 54{,}000 \text{ component-hours}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Sum the weighted products:**

$$\sum N_j t_j = 32{,}000 + 120{,}000 + 54{,}000 = 206{,}000 \quad \text{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute proportionality constant $k$:**

$$k = \frac{Q_s}{\sum N_j t_j} = \frac{0.02}{206{,}000} = 9.7087 \times 10^{-8} \quad \text{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Step 5 — Apportion unreliability:**

$$Q_1 = k \cdot N_1 t_1 = 9.7087 \times 10^{-8} \times 32{,}000 = 3.107 \times 10^{-3}, \quad R_1 = 0.99689$$

$$Q_2 = k \cdot N_2 t_2 = 9.7087 \times 10^{-8} \times 120{,}000 = 1.165 \times 10^{-2}, \quad R_2 = 0.98835$$

$$Q_3 = k \cdot N_3 t_3 = 9.7087 \times 10^{-8} \times 54{,}000 = 5.243 \times 10^{-3}, \quad R_3 = 0.99476$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 6 — Verification:**

$$\sum Q_i = 0.00311 + 0.01165 + 0.00524 = 0.02000 \quad \checkmark$$

*(0.5 mark)*

**Step 7 — Compute allocated MTBF using $\text{MTBF}_i = \dfrac{t_i}{-\ln R_i}$:**

$$\text{MTBF}_1 = \frac{800}{-\ln(0.99689)} = \frac{800}{0.003111} \approx 257{,}158 \text{ hours}$$

$$\text{MTBF}_2 = \frac{1000}{-\ln(0.98835)} = \frac{1000}{0.011716} \approx 85{,}354 \text{ hours}$$

$$\text{MTBF}_3 = \frac{600}{-\ln(0.99476)} = \frac{600}{0.005255} \approx 114{,}177 \text{ hours}$$

*(2 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

Use this high-density checklist as your **last-minute revision sheet** before the KTU exam:

- ✅ **Proportional Apportionment** is the *simplest* reliability allocation method; it linearly divides $Q_s$ based on $w_i$.
- ✅ **Core Formula:** $Q_i = \dfrac{w_i}{\sum w_j} \cdot Q_s$ — must be **memorised verbatim**.
- ✅ **Identity Check:** Always verify $\sum Q_i = Q_s$ for full marks.
- ✅ **Series Assumption:** Apportionment formulas are derived assuming a **series reliability block diagram**.
- ✅ **Weighting Criteria:** Complexity ($N_i$), Criticality ($K_i$), Mission Time ($t_i$), Environmental Stress ($E_i$).
- ✅ **Conversion Tools:** $R_i = 1 - Q_i$ and $\lambda_i = -\dfrac{\ln R_i}{t}$.
- ✅ **MTBF Identity:** $\text{MTBF}_i = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_i}$ for exponential distribution.
- ✅ **Linearisation Validity:** The approximation $\ln(1 - Q) \approx -Q$ is valid only when $Q \leq 0.1$.
- ✅ **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to convert $R_s$ to $Q_s$ before apportionment.
- ✅ **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing *proportional apportionment* with the **AGREE method** (which uses an exponential penalty term).
- ✅ **Common Mistake #3:** Not expressing failure rates with units (e.g., *failures per hour* or *FITS*).
- ✅ **Real-world Use:** Aerospace, automotive ECU design, nuclear plant safety systems, telecom networks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AGREE Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** AGREE Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **AGREE Method** is a classical **bottom-up, parts-count reliability prediction technique** for complex electronic systems, originally formalized in the **AGREE Report (1957)** by the U.S. Department of Defense's *Advisory Group on Reliability of Electronic Equipment* (chaired by Stanford Alexander).

It predicts the **reliability of a series-configured electronic equipment** by aggregating the failure rates of its individual constituent parts, assuming that **all parts operate continuously** during the equipment's useful life and exhibit **constant (time-independent) failure rates**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> "The AGREE method is a reliability prediction procedure that estimates the Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) and mission reliability of a series electronic system by summing the products of the quantity and failure rate of each generic part type used in the equipment."

The governing statement of the AGREE method is:

$$
\text{Reliability}_{\text{system}}(t) = \exp(-\lambda_{s}\,t) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{t}{\theta_{s}}\right)
$$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are building a **long chain (necklace)** where **every link must remain intact** for the chain to hold. The AGREE method says:

> "If you know the **breaking probability** of each **type of link** (small ring, large ring, clasp, decorative bead) AND you know **how many of each type** you used, you can predict the probability that the **entire chain survives a given time**."

| Real-World Object | Reliability Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Link of a chain | A single electronic part (resistor, capacitor, IC) |
| Chain breakage = system failure | Any part failing = system fails |
| Type of link (gold vs silver) | Generic part category with distinct failure rate |
| Number of each link type | Quantity $N_i$ of each part type |
| Total chain strength | System MTBF $\theta_s$ |

**Key takeaway:** AGREE assumes the system is **only as strong as the weakest combination** — failure of ANY single part kills the system (series model).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Failure rate unit:** failures per **$10^6$ hours** (FPMH) — used in military handbooks like MIL-HDBK-217
- **MTBF unit:** hours (for systems with repair) or **$10^6$ hours**
- **Reliability dimension:** dimensionless probability in **[0, 1]**
- **Mission time $t$:** hours
- **Exponential distribution parameter:** $\lambda_{s}$ (constant) in **failures/hour**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The AGREE method belongs to the *Part-Count Reliability Prediction* family and is the **forerunner** to later models like MIL-HDBK-217 and Telcordia SR-332.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Reliability Model

The AGREE method rests on **three foundational pillars**:

1. **Series Reliability Block Diagram (RBD):** The system functions ONLY if every part functions.
2. **Independence of Part Failures:** Failure of one part does not influence the failure probability of another.
3. **Constant Failure Rate Regime:** The system operates in the **useful life period** (after infant mortality, before wear-out) — this justifies the **exponential distribution** assumption.

---

## 2.2 Logical Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Decompose the system into generic part categories.**
For each generic part type $i$, identify:
- $N_i$ = total **quantity** of that part used
- $\lambda_i$ = **failure rate** of that part (failures/hour)

**Step 2 — Aggregate the part-level failure rates.**
For a series system with constant individual failure rates, the **system failure rate** is the linear sum:

$$
\lambda_{s} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_{i}\,\lambda_{i}
$$

**Why linear addition?** Because each part is an independent exponential random variable. The convolution of exponentials with the same parameter yields an Erlang distribution, and its mean equals the sum of individual means.

**Step 3 — Compute MTBF.**
Since $E[T] = 1/\lambda$ for exponential distribution:

$$
\theta_{s} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{s}} = \dfrac{1}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_{i}\,\lambda_{i}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute mission reliability** for a required mission duration $t$:

$$
R_{s}(t) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{t}{\theta_{s}}\right) = \exp(-\lambda_{s}\,t)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute unreliability (probability of failure):**

$$
Q_{s}(t) = F_{s}(t) = 1 - R_{s}(t) = 1 - \exp(-\lambda_{s}\,t)
$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam Quick Reference)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | System Failure Rate | $\lambda_{s} = \sum N_{i}\,\lambda_{i}$ | Sum of (quantity × part failure rate) | failures/hr |
| 2 | System MTBF | $\theta_{s} = 1 \,/\, \lambda_{s}$ | Reciprocal of system failure rate | hours |
| 3 | Mission Reliability | $R_{s}(t) = \exp(-\lambda_{s}\,t)$ | Reliability for mission of duration $t$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Mission Unreliability | $Q_{s}(t) = 1 - \exp(-\lambda_{s}\,t)$ | Probability of system failure | dimensionless |
| 5 | Part-Count Summation Index | $i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$ | Generic part type index | — |
| 6 | Series Reliability Product | $R_{s}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_{i}(t)$ | For $n$ independent series parts | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always convert part failure rates to a **common unit (failures/hour)** before summing. If the handbook gives $\lambda$ in FPMH ($\times 10^{-6}$/hr), multiply by $10^{-6}$ first.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Avionics** | Pre-design reliability budgeting for cockpit electronics |
| **Defence Systems** | Original purpose — MIL-spec electronic equipment |
| **Telecom Hardware** | Early-stage MTBF estimation for base-station cards |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Quick reliability screening during PCB BOM review |
| **Space Electronics** | Baseline prediction before detailed physics-of-failure analysis |

---

## 2.5 Assumptions & Limitations

**Assumptions:**
- Series configuration (no redundancy)
- Constant failure rate (useful life period)
- Part independence
- All parts powered and operating
- Failure rates from generic handbooks (not part-specific stress)

**Limitations:**
- Cannot model **redundant or parallel** subsystems
- Ignores **stress, temperature, and environmental derating**
- Treats all parts of a generic type as identical
- Inaccurate for **wear-out dominated** systems (mechanical parts)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation of the AGREE Failure-Rate Summation

### Derivation A — From Series Reliability Product to Summation

We start with the **series reliability product rule** for $n$ independent parts:

$$
R_{s}(t) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} R_{j}(t)
$$

Applying the AGREE constant-failure-rate assumption, each part $j$ has reliability:

$$
R_{j}(t) = \exp(-\lambda_{j}\,t)
$$

Therefore the system reliability becomes:

$$
R_{s}(t) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} \exp(-\lambda_{j}\,t)
$$

Using the exponential product identity $\prod e^{x_{i}} = e^{\sum x_{i}}$:

$$
R_{s}(t) = \exp\!\left(-\sum_{j=1}^{n}\lambda_{j}\,t\right) = \exp\!\left(-t\,\sum_{j=1}^{n}\lambda_{j}\right)
$$

**Identifying the system failure rate:**

$$
\lambda_{s} = \sum_{j=1}^{n}\lambda_{j}
$$

**Grouping by generic part type** (since there are $N_i$ identical parts of type $i$):

$$
\lambda_{s} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_{i}\,\lambda_{i}
$$

$$
\therefore \quad \boxed{\lambda_{s} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_{i}\,\lambda_{i}}
$$

This completes the formal proof of the AGREE summation formula.

---

### Derivation B — Derivation of System MTBF

For an exponential distribution with constant rate $\lambda_{s}$:

$$
f(t) = \lambda_{s}\,\exp(-\lambda_{s}\,t), \quad t \geq 0
$$

The mean (MTBF) is:

$$
\theta_{s} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t\,f(t)\,dt
$$

Splitting the integral:

$$
\theta_{s} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t\,\lambda_{s}\,\exp(-\lambda_{s}\,t)\,dt
$$

Using the standard result $\int_{0}^{\infty} t\,e^{-at}\,dt = 1/a^{2}$ with $a = \lambda_{s}$:

$$
\theta_{s} = \lambda_{s} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{s}^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{s}}
$$

$$
\therefore \quad \boxed{\theta_{s} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{s}} = \dfrac{1}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_{i}\,\lambda_{i}}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example (Board-Exam Style Numerical)

### **Problem Statement**

An electronic control unit consists of the following parts. Compute the system **MTBF** and the **reliability for a 50-hour mission**.

| Part Type $i$ | Quantity $N_i$ | Failure Rate $\lambda_i$ (per hour) |
|---|---|---|
| Resistors | 80 | $0.50 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Capacitors | 40 | $0.20 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Transistors | 25 | $1.00 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| ICs | 10 | $2.50 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Connectors | 5 | $0.80 \times 10^{-6}$ |

---

### **Solution — Step 1: Compute the Product $N_i \lambda_i$ for Each Part Type**

$$
N_{1}\lambda_{1} = 80 \times 0.50 \times 10^{-6} = 40.0 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{f/hr}
$$

$$
N_{2}\lambda_{2} = 40 \times 0.20 \times 10^{-6} = 8.0 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{f/hr}
$$

$$
N_{3}\lambda_{3} = 25 \times 1.00 \times 10^{-6} = 25.0 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{f/hr}
$$

$$
N_{4}\lambda_{4} = 10 \times 2.50 \times 10^{-6} = 25.0 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{f/hr}
$$

$$
N_{5}\lambda_{5} = 5 \times 0.80 \times 10^{-6} = 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{f/hr}
$$

---

### **Solution — Step 2: Sum the Column to Obtain $\lambda_s$**

$$
\lambda_{s} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} N_{i}\lambda_{i}
$$

$$
\lambda_{s} = (40.0 + 8.0 + 25.0 + 25.0 + 4.0) \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\lambda_{s} = 102.0 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{failures/hour}
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda_{s} = 1.02 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{failures/hour}}
$$

---

### **Solution — Step 3: Compute System MTBF**

$$
\theta_{s} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{s}} = \dfrac{1}{1.02 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\theta_{s} = 9803.92 \; \text{hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_{s} \approx 9804 \; \text{hours}}
$$

---

### **Solution — Step 4: Compute Mission Reliability for $t = 50$ hours**

$$
R_{s}(50) = \exp(-\lambda_{s} \cdot 50)
$$

Substituting $\lambda_s$:

$$
R_{s}(50) = \exp\!\left(-1.02 \times 10^{-4} \times 50\right)
$$

$$
R_{s}(50) = \exp(-5.10 \times 10^{-3})
$$

Using $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x + x^{2}/2$ for small $x$:

$$
R_{s}(50) \approx 1 - 0.00510 + 0.0000130 \approx 0.99491
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{s}(50) \approx 0.9949 \;\; (99.49\%)}
$$

---

### **Solution — Step 5: Interpretation**

The probability that this electronic unit operates **without failure for 50 continuous hours** is **99.49 %**, with an expected time between failures of about **9800 hours** (≈ 1.12 years of continuous operation).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick Solve Table for Common Variants

| Variant Asked | Formula to Use | Substitution Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| Find $\lambda_s$ given part data | $\lambda_{s} = \sum N_{i}\lambda_{i}$ | Direct summation |
| Find MTBF | $\theta_{s} = 1 / \lambda_{s}$ | Invert system failure rate |
| Find $R_s(t)$ | $R_{s}(t) = e^{-\lambda_{s}t}$ | Use $\lambda_s$ and given $t$ |
| Find mission time for given $R^*$ | $t = -\ln(R^{*}) \,/\, \lambda_{s}$ | Solve exponential for $t$ |
| Find part $\lambda$ needed for $R^*$ target | $\lambda_{\text{req}} = -\ln(R^{*})/(N_{i}\,t)$ | Algebraic rearrangement |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AGREE Methodology — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below captures the **sequential flow** of the AGREE reliability prediction procedure, from system definition through final reliability reporting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define System Boundary] --> B[List All Generic Part Types i = 1 to k]
    B --> C[For Each Part Type Obtain Ni and Lambda i]
    C --> D[Compute Product Ni times Lambda i]
    D --> E[Sum Over All Part Types to Get Lambda s]
    E --> F[Compute System MTBF Theta s = 1 over Lambda s]
    F --> G{Mission Time t Given?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Compute Rs of t = exp of negative Lambda s times t]
    G -- No --> I[Report Theta s and Lambda s Only]
    H --> J[Compare Rs with Target Reliability]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Rs Meets Target?}
    K -- Yes --> L[END: Design Acceptable]
    K -- No --> M[Initiate Design Improvement Loop]
    M --> B
```

---

## 4.2 Series Reliability Block Diagram Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Part 1 Lambda 1] --> SUM((System Lambda s))
    P2[Part 2 Lambda 2] --> SUM
    P3[Part 3 Lambda 3] --> SUM
    Pn[Part n Lambda n] --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[R s of t = exp of negative Lambda s times t]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each block represents a **generic part category** in series.
- The aggregator node **$\lambda_s$** implements the AGREE summation $\sum N_i \lambda_i$.
- The output node computes the **mission reliability** via the exponential function.

---

## 4.3 Component-Count Aggregation Schematic (Tabular View)

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $i$ | Generic part-type index |
| $N_i$ | Number of parts of type $i$ |
| $\lambda_i$ | Failure rate per part of type $i$ |
| $N_i \lambda_i$ | Aggregate failure contribution of type $i$ |
| $\lambda_s = \sum N_i \lambda_i$ | **System failure rate** (AGREE core) |
| $\theta_s = 1/\lambda_s$ | System MTBF |
| $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda_s t}$ | Mission reliability |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the AGREE method of reliability prediction. List any four assumptions underlying it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **AGREE (Advisory Group on Reliability of Electronic Equipment) method** is a part-count reliability prediction technique used to estimate the MTBF and mission reliability of **series-configured electronic systems** by summing the products of the quantity and failure rate of each generic part type.

**Assumptions (any four, ½ mark each):**
1. The system is configured in **series** (any part failure causes system failure).
2. All parts have **constant failure rates** (useful life period).
3. Part failures are **statistically independent**.
4. All parts are **continuously energized** during operation.
5. Part failure rates are obtained from **generic handbooks** (not part-specific).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the AGREE formula for system failure rate and explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
\lambda_{s} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_{i}\,\lambda_{i}
$$

**Meaning of each term:**
- $\lambda_{s}$ = **System failure rate** (failures/hour)
- $k$ = **Number of distinct generic part types** in the system
- $N_{i}$ = **Quantity** of parts of the $i$-th generic type used
- $\lambda_{i}$ = **Failure rate** per individual part of the $i$-th type (failures/hour)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the AGREE formula for the failure rate of a series electronic system consisting of $n$ independent parts each having constant failure rate $\lambda_j$. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A radar transmitter module contains the following parts. Compute (i) the system failure rate, (ii) the system MTBF, and (iii) the reliability for a mission of 100 hours. **(7 marks)**

| Part Type | Quantity $N_i$ | $\lambda_i$ (per $10^{6}$ hours) |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------------------------|
| Resistors | 120 | 0.40 |
| Capacitors (Tantalum) | 35 | 0.60 |
| Transistors (Si) | 18 | 1.50 |
| Diodes | 24 | 0.90 |
| ICs (Digital) | 8 | 3.00 |
| Connectors | 4 | 1.20 |

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumption 1 — Series configuration:** System reliability = product of part reliabilities:

$$
R_{s}(t) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} R_{j}(t)
$$

**Assumption 2 — Constant failure rate** for each part $j$:

$$
R_{j}(t) = \exp(-\lambda_{j}\,t)
$$

Substituting:

$$
R_{s}(t) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} \exp(-\lambda_{j}\,t)
$$

Applying the identity $\prod e^{x_{j}} = e^{\sum x_{j}}$:

$$
R_{s}(t) = \exp\!\left(-t\sum_{j=1}^{n}\lambda_{j}\right) = \exp(-\lambda_{s}\,t)
$$

**Equating exponents:**

$$
\boxed{\lambda_{s} = \sum_{j=1}^{n}\lambda_{j}}
$$

**Grouping by generic part type** (with $N_i$ identical parts of type $i$):

$$
\boxed{\lambda_{s} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_{i}\,\lambda_{i}}
$$

This is the **AGREE formula**.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert $\lambda_i$ from FPMH to failures/hour** by multiplying by $10^{-6}$.

| $i$ | $N_i$ | $\lambda_i$ (f/hr) | $N_i \lambda_i$ (f/hr) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resistors | 120 | $0.40 \times 10^{-6}$ | $48.0 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Capacitors | 35 | $0.60 \times 10^{-6}$ | $21.0 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Transistors | 18 | $1.50 \times 10^{-6}$ | $27.0 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Diodes | 24 | $0.90 \times 10^{-6}$ | $21.6 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| ICs | 8 | $3.00 \times 10^{-6}$ | $24.0 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Connectors | 4 | $1.20 \times 10^{-6}$ | $4.8 \times 10^{-6}$ |

**Step 2 — Sum to obtain $\lambda_s$:**

$$
\lambda_{s} = (48.0 + 21.0 + 27.0 + 21.6 + 24.0 + 4.8) \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\lambda_{s} = 146.4 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{failures/hour}
$$

**Step 3 — System MTBF:**

$$
\theta_{s} = \dfrac{1}{146.4 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 6830.6 \; \text{hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Mission reliability for $t = 100$ hr:**

$$
R_{s}(100) = \exp(-146.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 100) = \exp(-0.01464)
$$

$$
R_{s}(100) \approx 0.9855 \;\; (98.55\%)
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\lambda_s = 146.4 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour
- $\theta_s \approx 6831$ hours
- $R_s(100) \approx 0.9855$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for applying the AGREE method to predict the reliability of an electronic system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An avionics subsystem consists of the following parts. Compute the system failure rate and the MTBF. If the mission duration is 200 hours, find the probability that the system survives the mission. **(7 marks)**

| Part Type | Quantity $N_i$ | $\lambda_i$ (per $10^{6}$ hours) |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------------------------|
| Carbon Resistors | 60 | 0.10 |
| Ceramic Capacitors | 30 | 0.30 |
| Si Transistors | 20 | 0.80 |
| ICs (Linear) | 6 | 2.00 |
| Relays | 3 | 5.00 |
| Switches | 4 | 2.50 |

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Define the system boundary** and identify all subassemblies. *(1 mark)*
2. **List every generic part type** used in the system. *(1 mark)*
3. For each generic part type $i$, obtain from the **reliability handbook**: *(1 mark)*
   - Quantity $N_i$
   - Failure rate $\lambda_i$
4. **Compute the product** $N_i \lambda_i$ for each part type. *(1 mark)*
5. **Sum** all products to obtain the **system failure rate** $\lambda_s = \sum N_i \lambda_i$. *(1 mark)*
6. **Calculate the system MTBF** as $\theta_s = 1/\lambda_s$. *(1 mark)*
7. **Evaluate mission reliability** for the specified duration $t$ using $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda_s t}$. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert $\lambda_i$:**

| $i$ | $N_i$ | $\lambda_i$ (f/hr) | $N_i \lambda_i$ (f/hr) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon Resistors | 60 | $0.10 \times 10^{-6}$ | $6.0 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Ceramic Capacitors | 30 | $0.30 \times 10^{-6}$ | $9.0 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Si Transistors | 20 | $0.80 \times 10^{-6}$ | $16.0 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Linear ICs | 6 | $2.00 \times 10^{-6}$ | $12.0 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Relays | 3 | $5.00 \times 10^{-6}$ | $15.0 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Switches | 4 | $2.50 \times 10^{-6}$ | $10.0 \times 10^{-6}$ |

**Step 2 — Sum:**

$$
\lambda_{s} = (6.0 + 9.0 + 16.0 + 12.0 + 15.0 + 10.0) \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\lambda_{s} = 68.0 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{failures/hour}
$$

**Step 3 — MTBF:**

$$
\theta_{s} = \dfrac{1}{68.0 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 14705.9 \; \text{hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Mission reliability for $t = 200$ hr:**

$$
R_{s}(200) = \exp(-68.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 200) = \exp(-0.0136)
$$

$$
R_{s}(200) \approx 0.9865 \;\; (98.65\%)
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\lambda_s = 68.0 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour
- $\theta_s \approx 14706$ hours
- $R_s(200) \approx 0.9865$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in AGREE Method Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–2 marks):** The most frequent error. If $\lambda_i$ is given in *failures per $10^6$ hours*, students forget to multiply by $10^{-6}$ before summing. Always normalize to **failures/hour**.
> 2. **Confusing MTBF with MTTF (–1 mark):** AGREE gives **MTBF** (repairable system context). Use MTTF only for non-repairable single components.
> 3. **Summing $N_i$ instead of $N_i \lambda_i$ (–3 marks):** The summation is over **failure-rate contributions**, not just part counts. Read the formula $\sum N_i \lambda_i$ carefully.
> 4. **Wrong exponential sign (–2 marks):** The reliability expression is $\exp(-\lambda_s t)$ — never $\exp(+\lambda_s t)$.
> 5. **Missing assumption statement (–1 mark):** In Part (a) derivations, the examiner expects the student to **explicitly state** the series, constant-rate, and independence assumptions before deriving.
> 6. **Misinterpreting $\theta_s$:** If the problem gives failure rate as FPMH = 1.02, the actual $\lambda_s = 1.02 \times 10^{-6}$ — division by zero or wrong powers of ten is a common slip.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for AGREE Method:**

- **Origin:** AGREE Report, U.S. DoD, **1957** — first formal parts-count method.
- **Scope:** Series-configured **electronic** systems, **useful-life period**.
- **Core formula:** $\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i \lambda_i$.
- **MTBF:** $\theta_s = 1 / \lambda_s$.
- **Mission reliability:** $R_s(t) = \exp(-\lambda_s t)$.
- **Unit vigilance:** Always convert FPMH ($\times 10^{-6}$/hr) to **failures/hour** before summing.
- **Three pillars:** Series RBD + Constant $\lambda$ + Independent parts.
- **Strengths:** Simple, data-light, ideal for early design budgeting.
- **Weaknesses:** Cannot model **redundancy**, ignores **stress/environment**, generic data only.
- **Successor models:** MIL-HDBK-217, Telcordia SR-332, PRISM, RIAC 217Plus.
- **Examiner's keyword triggers:** "part-count prediction," "series electronic system," "generic failure rate," "useful life," "bottom-up summation."
- **Quick sanity check:** More parts or higher individual failure rates → larger $\lambda_s$ → smaller $\theta_s$ and lower $R_s(t)$. Always verify monotonicity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Tree Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Fault Tree Analysis ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a deductive (top-down) analytical technique used in reliability engineering to systematically determine the combinations of basic component failures that can lead to an undesired system-level event, known as the **Top Event**. It employs **Boolean logic gates** (AND, OR) to graphically represent the causal (failure) pathways from the basic events (lowest-level component failures) upward to the top event. The qualitative output is a set of **Minimal Cut Sets (MCS)** — smallest combinations of component failures whose simultaneous occurrence guarantees the top event. The quantitative output is the **Top Event Probability** $P(T)$ computed from these minimal cut sets. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Terminology (Board-Exam Favourites):** > - **Top Event:** The undesired system failure being analyzed (root of the tree). > - **Basic Event:** Failure of a single component — no further decomposition. > - **Intermediate Event:** An event arising from the logical combination of lower events. > - **Cut Set:** Any set of basic events whose joint occurrence causes the top event. > - **Minimal Cut Set (MCS):** A cut set from which no basic event can be removed without losing the cut-set property. --- ### Conceptual Analogy — "Why Does the House Have No Power?" Imagine your home suddenly goes dark. You stand at the **distribution board** (this is your *Top Event*: "House Has No Electricity") and trace backward: Was it the **main fuse (MF)** blown, the **MCB tripped (MCB)**, or an **incoming supply failure (SUP)**? Any one of these causes darkness — that's an **OR gate**. Now look inside MCB tripping: the MCB must trip **AND** the load must exceed the rating — that's an **AND gate**. This backward "tree" of causes — from symptom back to root components — is exactly what a **Fault Tree** is. Each "branch" is a logic gate; each "leaf" is a basic component failure. ``` Symptom (Top Event) | [Logic Gate] / \ A B <-- could be basic events OR intermediate events | | [Gate] Basic / \ ... ... ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why use FTA in Engineering?** It is mandated in safety-critical industries — aerospace (NASA, ISRO), nuclear power (AERB), automotive (ISO 26262), and oil & gas. It pinpoints **single-point failures**, computes **top-event probability**, and feeds directly into **risk-based inspection**. ### Standard Symbols Used in FTA | Symbol | Name | Meaning | |--------|------|---------| | Rectangle | Intermediate Event | Event resulting from logic gates above | | Circle | Basic Event | Lowest-level component failure | | Diamond | Undeveloped Event | Insufficient information / deliberately not expanded | | House | External/Conditional Event | Event expected to occur normally | | AND gate (flat D-shape) | Conjunction | Output occurs if **ALL** inputs occur | | OR gate (crescent) | Disjunction | Output occurs if **ANY** input occurs | | Inhibit gate | Conditional AND | Output occurs if input occurs **AND** condition holds | --- ### Real-World Engineering Applications - **Aerospace:** Boeing / Airbus compute the probability of an aircraft crash due to dual-engine failure using FTA. - **Nuclear:** Reactor core-melt probability assessment uses minimal cut sets. - **Automotive:** ISO 26262 mandates FTA for ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level) determination. - **Process Plants:** Hazard & Operability (HAZOP) is often complemented by FTA to quantify accident scenarios. > [!TIP] > **Quick Board Memory Hook:** *"Fault Tree grows UP from causes to TOP event — like a tree growing towards the sky, with roots being component failures."* ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Construction Methodology — Step-by-Step **Step 1:** Define the **Top Event** precisely (e.g., "Pump P-101 fails to deliver rated flow"). **Step 2:** Identify the **immediate causes** of the top event — these become the first-level inputs. **Step 3:** Connect these immediate causes using the appropriate **logic gate** (AND if all must occur simultaneously; OR if any one is sufficient). **Step 4:** Recursively decompose each intermediate event until all remaining events are either: - **Basic Events** (component failures — stop here), or - **Undeveloped Events** (with documented justification for non-decomposition). **Step 5:** Verify the tree using the **"No Miracles" rule** — the events should be logically related, not mysterious. **Step 6:** Extract **Cut Sets**, reduce to **Minimal Cut Sets**, then perform quantitative evaluation. ### 2.2 Qualitative Analysis — Finding Minimal Cut Sets A **Cut Set** is any combination of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence causes the Top Event. A **Minimal Cut Set** is a cut set such that if any basic event is removed, the remaining set is no longer a cut set. #### Method 1: Boolean Algebra Reduction (Most Common in Exams) Steps: 1. Write the **structure function** of the fault tree in Boolean form. 2. Expand all AND-OR combinations into a **sum-of-products (SOP)** form. 3. Apply **Boolean laws** (Idempotent, Absorption) to reduce. 4. Each remaining product term is a **Minimal Cut Set**. #### Method 2: Karnaugh Map (K-Map) Method Useful for trees with ≤ 4 variables. Plot the SOP expression on a K-map; each prime implicant corresponds to a minimal cut set. ### 2.3 Quantitative Analysis — Top Event Probability #### Case 1: AND Gate If outputs of basic events $A, B, C$ feed an AND gate: $$P(T) = P(A \cap B \cap C)$$ For **independent** events: $$P(T) = P(A) \cdot P(B) \cdot P(C)$$ #### Case 2: OR Gate $$P(T) = 1 - P(\overline{A} \cap \overline{B} \cap \overline{C})$$ $$P(T) = 1 - [1 - P(A)] \cdot [1 - P(B)] \cdot [1 - P(C)]$$ #### Case 3: Top Event with Multiple MCS If MCS are $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$: $$P(T) = P\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} C_i\right)$$ **Exact (Inclusion-Exclusion):** $$P(T) = \sum_i P(C_i) - \sum_{i [!NOTE] > **Golden Rule (Examiner Favourite):** When asked to find minimal cut sets, ALWAYS show the Boolean expansion step-by-step using $X + XY = X$ (absorption) and $X + X = X$ (idempotent). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples ### 3.1 Example 1 — Boolean Reduction to Minimal Cut Sets **Problem:** Construct the fault tree, find MCS, and compute top event probability for the system described: > A motor fails to start if the **power supply (PS)** fails **OR** both the **contactor (C)** AND the **overload relay (OLR)** fail simultaneously. Let: - $T$ = Motor fails to start (Top Event) - $A$ = Power supply fails - $B$ = Contactor fails - $C$ = Overload relay fails #### Step 1: Write the Structure Function The fault tree structure gives: $$T = A + (B \cdot C)$$ (Where $+$ is OR, $\cdot$ is AND.) #### Step 2: Expand to Sum-of-Products (SOP) The expression is already in SOP: $$T = A + BC$$ #### Step 3: Apply Boolean Reduction Check absorption: $A + ABC = A$, but here we have $A + BC$, where the terms share no common factor. So **no further reduction**. #### Step 4: Identify Minimal Cut Sets Each product term is a minimal cut set: - **MCS-1:** $\{A\}$ (single-point failure) - **MCS-2:** $\{B, C\}$ (dual failure) #### Step 5: Compute Top Event Probability Using exact inclusion-exclusion: $$P(T) = P(A) + P(BC) - P(A \cdot BC)$$ For independent basic events: $$P(T) = P(A) + P(B) \cdot P(C) - P(A) \cdot P(B) \cdot P(C)$$ Substituting $P(A) = 0.02$, $P(B) = 0.03$, $P(C) = 0.04$: $$P(T) = 0.02 + (0.03)(0.04) - (0.02)(0.03)(0.04)$$ $$P(T) = 0.02 + 0.0012 - 0.000024$$ $$\boxed{P(T) = 0.021176}$$ Since $P(C_i) \ll 1$, the rare-event approximation gives: $$P(T) \approx 0.02 + 0.0012 = 0.0212 \text{ (close to exact)}$$ --- ### 3.2 Example 2 — Multi-Level Fault Tree Reduction **Problem:** A system fails when the **Top Event T** occurs. Construct the fault tree structure as: - $T = G_1 + G_2$ - $G_1 = A \cdot G_3$ - $G_2 = B + C$ - $G_3 = B + C$ Where $A, B, C$ are basic events. #### Step 1: Substitute Backwards $$T = (A \cdot G_3) + (B + C)$$ #### Step 2: Substitute $G_3 = B + C$ $$T = A \cdot (B + C) + (B + C)$$ #### Step 3: Apply Boolean Expansion $$T = AB + AC + B + C$$ #### Step 4: Apply Idempotent & Absorption Laws Since $B + AB = B$ (absorption): $$T = B + C + AC$$ Since $C + AC = C$ (absorption): $$\boxed{T = B + C}$$ #### Step 5: Interpret the Result The **minimal cut sets** are: - **MCS-1:** $\{B\}$ — single-point failure - **MCS-2:** $\{C\}$ — single-point failure **Engineering Insight:** Event $A$ is **irrelevant** to the top event — removing $A$ from the system would not affect $P(T)$. This is critical for **cost-effective redesign** — eliminate redundancies around $A$. --- ### 3.3 Example 3 — Quantitative Evaluation with Large Tree **Problem:** A fault tree yields three minimal cut sets: - $C_1 = \{X_1, X_2\}$ with $P(X_1) = 0.01, P(X_2) = 0.01$ - $C_2 = \{X_3\}$ with $P(X_3) = 0.005$ - $C_3 = \{X_1, X_3\}$ with $P(X_1) = 0.01, P(X_3) = 0.005$ All events independent. Compute exact $P(T)$. #### Step 1: Compute Individual Cut Set Probabilities $$P(C_1) = P(X_1) \cdot P(X_2) = 0.01 \times 0.01 = 0.0001$$ $$P(C_2) = P(X_3) = 0.005$$ $$P(C_3) = P(X_1) \cdot P(X_3) = 0.01 \times 0.005 = 0.00005$$ #### Step 2: Compute Pairwise Intersections $$P(C_1 \cap C_2) = P(X_1) \cdot P(X_2) \cdot P(X_3) = (0.01)(0.01)(0.005) = 5 \times 10^{-7}$$ $$P(C_1 \cap C_3) = P(X_1) \cdot P(X_2) \cdot P(X_3) = 5 \times 10^{-7}$$ (both $C_1$ and $C_3$ require $X_1$ and $X_3$ plus $C_1$ adds $X_2$) $$P(C_2 \cap C_3) = P(X_1) \cdot P(X_3) = 0.01 \times 0.005 = 0.00005$$ (both require $X_1$ and $X_3$) #### Step 3: Compute Triple Intersection $$P(C_1 \cap C_2 \cap C_3) = P(X_1) \cdot P(X_2) \cdot P(X_3) = 5 \times 10^{-7}$$ #### Step 4: Apply Inclusion-Exclusion $$P(T) = \sum P(C_i) - \sum_{i ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic Fault Tree Architecture ```mermaid graph TD nodeT["TOP EVENT T
System Failure"] nodeG1["GATE G1 (OR)
Either X1 OR X2 fails"] nodeG2["GATE G2 (AND)
X3 AND X4 BOTH fail"] nodeX1["Basic Event X1
Component 1 fails"] nodeX2["Basic Event X2
Component 2 fails"] nodeX3["Basic Event X3
Component 3 fails"] nodeX4["Basic Event X4
Component 4 fails"] nodeG3["GATE G3 (OR)
X5 OR X6 fails"] nodeX5["Basic Event X5
Component 5 fails"] nodeX6["Basic Event X6
Component 6 fails"] nodeT --> nodeG1 nodeT --> nodeG2 nodeG1 --> nodeX1 nodeG1 --> nodeX2 nodeG2 --> nodeX3 nodeG2 --> nodeG4["Gate G4 (AND)
X4 and G3 fail"] nodeG4 --> nodeX4 nodeG4 --> nodeG3 nodeG3 --> nodeX5 nodeG3 --> nodeX6 style nodeT fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeG1 fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#000 style nodeG2 fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#000 style nodeG3 fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#000 style nodeG4 fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#000 style nodeX1 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#000 style nodeX2 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#000 style nodeX3 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#000 style nodeX4 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#000 style nodeX5 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#000 style nodeX6 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — FTA Analytical Workflow ```mermaid graph LR stepA["Define Top Event
Precisely"] stepB["Identify Immediate
Causes"] stepC["Apply Logic Gates
AND / OR"] stepD["Decompose to
Basic Events"] stepE["Write Boolean
Expression"] stepF["Expand to
Sum-of-Products"] stepG["Apply Boolean
Reduction"] stepH["Extract Minimal
Cut Sets"] stepI["Compute
P(T)"] stepJ["Birnbaum
Importance"] stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI --> stepJ style stepA fill:#4d96ff,color:#fff style stepJ fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cut Set Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD nodeCut["ALL CUT SETS
of Fault Tree"] nodeMin["MINIMAL CUT SETS
Cannot be reduced"] node1pt["Single-Point
Cut Sets
Critical"] node2pt["Dual-Point
Cut Sets"] nodeKpt["K-Order
Cut Sets"] nodeCut --> nodeMin nodeMin --> node1pt nodeMin --> node2pt nodeMin --> nodeKpt style nodeCut fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff style nodeMin fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#000 style node1pt fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff style node2pt fill:#f39c12,color:#fff style nodeKpt fill:#95a5a6,color:#fff ``` ### 4.4 Textual Representation of a Fault Tree (For Exam Drawing) ``` ┌──────────────────┐ │ TOP EVENT (T) │ │ Motor Fails to │ │ Start │ └────────┬─────────┘ │ ┌──────────┴──────────┐ │ │ ┌─────┴──────┐ ┌──────┴─────┐ │ GATE G1 │ │ GATE G2 │ │ (OR) │ │ (AND) │ └─────┬──────┘ └──────┬─────┘ │ │ ┌───────┼───────┐ ┌─────┴─────┐ │ │ │ │ │ ┌─┴─┐ ┌──┴──┐ ┌─┴─┐ ┌─┴─┐ ┌───┴────┐ │ PS│ │ C-1 │ │ C-2│ │OLR│ │Manual │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │Reset │ └───┘ └─────┘ └───┘ └───┘ │Missing │ └────────┘ PS = Power Supply Failure (Basic Event) C-1 = Contactor 1 Failure (Basic Event) C-2 = Contactor 2 Failure (Basic Event) OLR = Overload Relay Failure (Basic Event) ``` **Structure Function:** $$T = PS + (C1 \cdot C2) + (OLR \cdot \text{MR})$$ where MR = Manual Reset failure. **Minimal Cut Sets:** - $\{PS\}$ — single-point failure (most critical) - $\{C1, C2\}$ — dual-point failure (redundant contactor design) - $\{OLR, MR\}$ — dual-point failure ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define Fault Tree Analysis. List any four symbols used in fault tree construction.** **Model Answer:** > **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a deductive analytical technique used to identify and quantify the combinations of component failures that can lead to an undesired system-level event, called the **Top Event**. It uses Boolean logic gates (AND, OR) in a graphical tree structure to depict failure pathways. > > **Four Symbols Used in FTA:** > > 1. **Rectangle** – Intermediate Event (resulting from logic gates) > 2. **Circle** – Basic Event (component-level failure) > 3. **Diamond** – Undeveloped Event (not expanded) > 4. **AND/OR Gate** – Logical combination of inputs **Marking Scheme (Typical):** Definition — 1 mark, Four symbols — 2 marks (0.5 each). --- #### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between a Cut Set and a Minimal Cut Set.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Cut Set | Minimal Cut Set | |--------|---------|-----------------| | Definition | Set of basic events whose joint occurrence causes the Top Event | A cut set from which **no basic event can be removed** without losing the property | | Reduction | May contain redundant events | Cannot be reduced further | | Generation | All cut sets come from Boolean expansion | Found after applying absorption laws | | Example | $\{A, B, C\}$ causes top event | If $\{A, B\}$ also causes top event, then $\{A,B,C\}$ is **not** minimal | **Marking Scheme:** Difference — 2 marks, Example — 1 mark. --- ### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q (a): Construct the Fault Tree and determine the Minimal Cut Sets for a system whose Top Event T is described by the following structure:** $$T = A + B \cdot C$$ $$B = D + E$$ $$C = F \cdot G$$ **Compute the top event probability given:** $P(A) = 0.05$, $P(D) = 0.02$, $P(E) = 0.03$, $P(F) = 0.01$, $P(G) = 0.02$. **Assume all basic events are independent. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Substitute $B = D + E$ and $C = FG$ into $T$: $$T = A + (D+E) \cdot (FG)$$ **Step 2:** Expand: $$T = A + DFG + EFG$$ **Step 3:** Apply Boolean Reduction: Check absorption: $A + DFG + EFG$ — does $A$ absorb either? No, since $DFG$ and $EFG$ do not contain $A$. **Step 4:** Identify Minimal Cut Sets: - **MCS-1:** $\{A\}$ (single-point) - **MCS-2:** $\{D, F, G\}$ (triple) - **MCS-3:** $\{E, F, G\}$ (triple) **Step 5:** Compute individual MCS probabilities: $$P(C_1) = P(A) = 0.05$$ $$P(C_2) = P(D) \cdot P(F) \cdot P(G) = 0.02 \times 0.01 \times 0.02 = 4 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$P(C_3) = P(E) \cdot P(F) \cdot P(G) = 0.03 \times 0.01 \times 0.02 = 6 \times 10^{-6}$$ **Step 6:** Apply Rare-Event Approximation (since all cut set probabilities $\ll 1$): $$P(T) \approx 0.05 + 4 \times 10^{-6} + 6 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\boxed{P(T) \approx 0.05001}$$ **Marking Scheme:** Tree construction — 2 marks, MCS extraction — 3 marks, $P(T)$ calculation — 2 marks. --- **Q (b): Explain the Birnbaum Importance Measure. For the system $T = A + BC$, compute $I_B(A)$ and $I_B(B)$ given:** $P(A) = 0.05$, $P(B) = 0.04$, $P(C) = 0.03$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition (3 marks):** The **Birnbaum Importance** of a component $i$ is defined as: $$I_B(i) = P(T \mid \text{component } i \text{ fails}) - P(T \mid \text{component } i \text{ works})$$ It measures the sensitivity of the top event probability to the state of component $i$. **Computing $I_B(A)$ (2 marks):** - $P(T \mid A = 1) = 1$ (OR gate — input already failed) - $P(T \mid A = 0) = P(B) \cdot P(C) = 0.04 \times 0.03 = 0.0012$ $$I_B(A) = 1 - 0.0012 = 0.9988$$ **Computing $I_B(B)$ (2 marks):** - $P(T \mid B = 1) = P(A) + P(C) - P(A) \cdot P(C) = 0.05 + 0.03 - (0.05)(0.03) = 0.0785$ *(Reasoning: With $B=1$, $T = A + C$ via OR gate.)* - $P(T \mid B = 0) = P(A) = 0.05$ $$I_B(B) = 0.0785 - 0.05 = 0.0285$$ **Conclusion:** $I_B(A) \gg I_B(B)$, so component $A$ is more critical. --- #### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q (a): A system consists of three components A, B, C in a configuration where the system fails if A fails OR (B AND C both fail). Build the Fault Tree, derive the Minimal Cut Sets using Boolean algebra, and compute the unreliability for 1000 hours given:** $\lambda_A = 0.001$/hr, $\lambda_B = 0.002$/hr, $\lambda_C = 0.003$/hr. **Assume exponential reliability. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Structure Function (1 mark)** $$T = A + B \cdot C$$ **Step 2: Minimal Cut Sets (2 marks)** - $MCS_1 = \{A\}$ - $MCS_2 = \{B, C\}$ **Step 3: Reliability of Each Component at $t = 1000$ hr (2 marks)** $$R_A(1000) = e^{-0.001 \times 1000} = e^{-1} = 0.3679$$ $$R_B(1000) = e^{-0.002 \times 1000} = e^{-2} = 0.1353$$ $$R_C(1000) = e^{-0.003 \times 1000} = e^{-3} = 0.0498$$ **Step 4: Unreliabilities (1 mark)** $$Q_A = 1 - 0.3679 = 0.6321$$ $$Q_B = 1 - 0.1353 = 0.8647$$ $$Q_C = 1 - 0.0498 = 0.9502$$ **Step 5: Compute $P(T)$ Using Exact Inclusion-Exclusion (1 mark)** $$P(T) = Q_A + Q_B \cdot Q_C - Q_A \cdot Q_B \cdot Q_C$$ $$P(T) = 0.6321 + (0.8647)(0.9502) - (0.6321)(0.8647)(0.9502)$$ $$P(T) = 0.6321 + 0.8217 - 0.5194$$ $$\boxed{P(T) = 0.9344}$$ --- **Q (b): Compare Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) and Event Tree Analysis (ETA) in detail. Discuss the industrial applications of FTA. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Aspect | FTA | ETA | |--------|-----|-----| | Approach | Deductive (Top-Down) | Inductive (Bottom-Up) | | Direction | From Top Event to causes | From initiating event to consequences | | Logic | AND / OR gates | Binary branches (success/failure) | | Outcome | Minimal Cut Sets + $P(T)$ | Sequence of outcomes | | Use Case | Single undesired event | Multiple possible outcomes | | Application | Equipment failure | Accident progression | **Industrial Applications of FTA (3 marks):** 1. **Aerospace:** Quantifying dual-engine failure probability for aircraft certification (FAA). 2. **Nuclear Power Plant:** Core damage frequency assessment per AERB guidelines. 3. **Chemical Process Safety:** Identifying accident pathways in refineries (OSHA PSM). 4. **Automotive Safety:** ASIL decomposition per ISO 26262. 5. **Oil & Gas:** Subsea blowout preventer (BOP) reliability assessment. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting Boolean reduction:** Listing cut sets but NOT removing redundant ones using $X + XY = X$. Examiner deducts 1-2 marks. > 2. **Assuming independence without stating:** Always explicitly write "Assume all basic events are independent" — otherwise inclusion-exclusion formulas become invalid. > 3. **Confusing OR and AND gates:** "System fails if A AND B fail" means **both** must fail; OR means **either** is enough. Mixing these loses the entire problem's marks. > 4. **Using rare-event approximation when $P(C_i)$ is large:** Always check $P(C_i) < 0.1$ before applying $P(T) \approx \sum P(C_i)$. > 5. **Ignoring single-point failures:** Single-point MCS are the **most critical** — they appear in every FTA question and students often miss them. > 6. **Not drawing the tree:** Even when asked for minimal cut sets, drawing the fault tree earns **at least 1-2 extra marks** in board exams. > 7. **Forgetting to state the Top Event definition:** Define it precisely in the first line of any FTA solution. --- ## Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist - **FTA is a deductive (top-down) technique** — starts from the undesired Top Event and traces causes backward to basic events. - **Gates:** OR (any input causes output), AND (all inputs must occur). - **Symbols:** Rectangle (intermediate), Circle (basic), Diamond (undeveloped), House (conditional). - **Cut Set:** Any combination of basic events causing top event. - **Minimal Cut Set (MCS):** Smallest cut set — found using Boolean absorption laws. - **Boolean Laws to Remember:** Idempotent ($X+X=X$), Absorption ($X+XY=X$), De Morgan's, Distributive. - **AND gate probability:** Product of input probabilities. - **OR gate probability:** $1 - \prod(1 - p_i)$. - **Top event probability (exact):** Inclusion-Exclusion. - **Top event probability (approx):** Sum of MCS probabilities when rare. - **Single-point MCS** = highest priority for reliability improvement. - **Birnbaum Importance:** $I_B(i) = P(T\mid i\text{ fails}) - P(T\mid i\text{ works})$ — identifies most critical components. - **Industry applications:** Aerospace, nuclear, automotive (ISO 26262), process plants, oil & gas. - **Quantitative Evaluation Steps:** (1) Identify MCS → (2) Compute $P(C_i)$ → (3) Apply Inclusion-Exclusion or Rare-Event Approximation. - **Path Set / Minimal Path Set** = dual concept (events that must work for system success). - **Always state assumptions:** Independence of basic events, exponential failure distribution (if using $\lambda t$).

topics.title: Event Tree Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Event Tree Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** is a forward-inductive (or "deductive" in the safety engineering sense of propagating consequences forward) analytical technique used in reliability and risk engineering to map out the *possible outcomes* of an **initiating event** by sequentially branching through the success or failure of successive **safety functions** (or mitigating barriers). Each branch of the tree represents a unique sequence of functional successes and failures, terminating in a well-defined system state (safe, failed, accident, etc.) whose probability can be computed as the product of the conditional probabilities along the path.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, ETA is classified as a **probabilistic risk assessment (PRA)** method that complements Fault Tree Analysis (FTA). While FTA is a *backward* deductive technique (working from the undesired top event down to basic causes), ETA is a *forward* inductive technique (working from the initiating event forward to the consequences).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMET747, Module 3):**
> Event Tree Analysis is grouped with Fault Tree Analysis as the two pillars of Boolean/Probabilistic safety analysis. The KTU examiner typically expects students to: (i) draw an event tree, (ii) label the branches, (iii) identify the outcome sequences, and (iv) compute the probability of each terminal outcome.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Nomenclature)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Initiating Event (IE)** | The first event in the sequence that challenges the system (e.g., pipe rupture, pump trip, loss of power). |
| **Safety Function / Functional Event** | A pre-defined system response designed to mitigate the initiating event (e.g., emergency cooling, isolation valve closure). |
| **Branch Point (Node)** | A point in the tree where a functional event either succeeds (upper branch) or fails (lower branch). |
| **Outcome Sequence (Path)** | The unique string of S (success) and F (failure) labels from the IE to a terminal state. |
| **End State / Consequence** | The final condition of the system (e.g., Safe Shutdown, Minor Release, Major Accident). |
| **Path Probability** | The product of conditional probabilities of all branch outcomes along a single path. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Domino and Net" Intuition

Imagine you are a doctor monitoring a patient. The patient is admitted with a **chest pain** (this is your **initiating event**). Now you ask a sequence of yes/no questions:
1. Did the **ECG** show abnormalities? (Safety Function 1)
2. Did the **clot-buster drug** work? (Safety Function 2)
3. Did the **emergency stent** procedure succeed? (Safety Function 3)

Each "Yes" or "No" opens a new branch in your mental "event tree." By the time you reach the end of the decision chain, the patient is in one of several distinct final states: *fully recovered*, *stable but monitored*, *critical*, or *fatal*. ETA in engineering does exactly the same thing — it follows the *mitigating functions* of a system after a failure has occurred, and calculates how likely each final state is.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ETA Matters in Industry:**
> ETA is the *backbone* of Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA) Level 2 and Level 3 analyses in nuclear power plants, chemical process industries (CPI), offshore oil & gas platforms, and aerospace launch systems. The Three Mile Island (1979) and Chernobyl (1986) post-accident investigations relied heavily on event trees to reconstruct accident sequences.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Conditional probability notation:** $P(S_i \mid \text{path})$ — probability that functional event $i$ succeeds, *given* that the previous path was taken.
- **Branch probability convention:** $P(\text{success}) = p_i$, $P(\text{failure}) = q_i = 1 - p_i$.
- **Total probability of an end state:** Sum of path probabilities of all sequences leading to that end state.
- **Units:** All probabilities are dimensionless, in the range $[0, 1]$. Frequencies are typically expressed in **per reactor-year (pryr)** or **per plant-year (ppy)** when integrated over a year of operation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Philosophy: Why ETA Works

ETA rests on three foundational postulates:

1. **The system is in a defined initial state** when the initiating event occurs.
2. **A finite, ordered set of safety functions** is *designed* to respond to the initiating event.
3. **Each safety function has only two possible outcomes at the branch point** — success (S) or failure (F) — i.e., the analysis is *binary*. (Advanced ETA may use multi-valued branches, but KTU syllabus is strictly binary.)

## 2.2 Construction of an Event Tree — Step-by-Step Logic

The construction is sequential and disciplined:

1. **Identify the Initiating Event (IE).** This is the *start* node. IE may itself have a known frequency $f_{\text{IE}}$ (e.g., 1E-3 per year for a large LOCA).
2. **List the Safety Functions (F1, F2, ..., Fn)** in the *temporal order* in which they are *expected* to operate. This order is critical and is determined by the system's designed accident-response procedure.
3. **For each safety function, draw two branches:** the **UP branch (S)** is conventionally success; the **DOWN branch (F)** is conventionally failure.
4. **At each subsequent branch point, replicate the branches** so that the total number of paths equals $2^n$ for $n$ safety functions.
5. **Label each terminal path** with a unique outcome sequence (e.g., S-S-F-S) and assign it to a defined **end state** (e.g., Safe Shutdown, Core Damage, Release).
6. **Compute path probability** as the product of conditional branch probabilities.
7. **Aggregate** paths that lead to the same end state to find the *frequency* of that consequence.

## 2.3 The Branch Probability Rule (BPR)

For any binary branch with success probability $p_i$ and failure probability $q_i$:

$$p_i + q_i = 1$$

For two independent safety functions $F_1$ and $F_2$, the joint probability of a specific outcome sequence (e.g., S then F) is:

$$P(\text{S-F}) = p_1 \cdot q_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Independence Assumption:** KTU-level ETA assumes that the safety functions are *statistically independent* unless stated otherwise. In industrial practice, common-cause failures (CCF) are explicitly modeled with parametric factors (e.g., the $\alpha$-factor model or Beta-Factor model), but these are *beyond* the KTU PEMET747 Module 3 scope.

## 2.4 The Total Probability Theorem Applied to ETA

The probability (or frequency) of a particular end state $E_k$ is the sum of the probabilities of all paths that terminate in $E_k$:

$$P(E_k) = P(\text{IE}) \cdot \sum_{j \in \text{paths to } E_k} \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_{i,j}^{\text{S}} \cdot q_{i,j}^{\text{F}}$$

where the inner product is taken over the $n$ safety functions of path $j$, with $p_{i,j}^{\text{S}}$ denoting the success probability on that path and $q_{i,j}^{\text{F}}$ the failure probability.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Branch probability sum | $p_i + q_i = 1$ | $p_i$ = success, $q_i$ = failure |
| 2 | Path probability (n functions) | $P(\text{path}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | $P_i$ is $p_i$ or $q_i$ depending on path |
| 3 | End-state probability (no IE freq.) | $P(E_k) = \sum_{j \in E_k} P(\text{path}_j)$ | Conditional on IE having occurred |
| 4 | End-state frequency (with IE freq.) | $f(E_k) = f_{\text{IE}} \cdot P(E_k \mid \text{IE})$ | $f_{\text{IE}}$ in per year |
| 5 | Total number of paths | $2^n$ | For $n$ binary safety functions |
| 6 | Complement rule | $P(\text{not } E_k) = 1 - P(E_k)$ | Only if $E_k$ and its complement cover all paths |
| 7 | Path independence check | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | Valid only if $A \perp B$ |
| 8 | Minimal cut set from ETA | A set of function failures $F_i$ that *alone* leads to accident | Identified by inspection of F-branches |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip — Formula Sheet Usage:**
> Memorize the **path probability product** and the **end-state sum** as your two workhorses. Almost every KTU 2024 ESE question on ETA reduces to: *build the tree → compute path probabilities → sum to get end-state frequency.*

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Nuclear Power Plants (NPPs):** PSA Level 2 accident sequence evaluation; core damage frequency (CDF) and large early release frequency (LERF) calculations.
- **Offshore Oil & Gas:** Blowout scenario analysis on drilling rigs; escape, evacuation, and rescue (EER) modeling.
- **Chemical Process Industry (CPI):** HAZOP-LOPA integration, layered protection analysis.
- **Aviation & Space:** Launch vehicle failure mode propagation; aircraft accident sequence reconstruction.
- **Civil Engineering:** Dam break flood consequence mapping, structural fire propagation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — The Path Probability Product

We begin with the foundational probability law for sequential independent events.

**Step 1 — Two-event product law.**

For two statistically independent events $A$ and $B$, the joint probability of *both occurring* is:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

**Step 2 — Extension to $n$ events.**

By the *chain rule of probability* applied to $n$ independent events $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$:

$$P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \cdots \cap A_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i)$$

**Step 3 — Specialization to a single path of an event tree.**

A specific path in the event tree corresponds to a *sequence* of branch outcomes. Let the $i$-th safety function on a particular path $j$ have outcome $X_{i,j}$, where $X_{i,j} = S$ (success) with probability $p_i$ or $X_{i,j} = F$ (failure) with probability $q_i = 1 - p_i$. The path probability is:

$$P(\text{path}_j) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_{i,j})$$

**Step 4 — Specialization to a worked path S-F-S (functions 1, 2, 3).**

$$P(\text{S-F-S}) = p_1 \cdot q_2 \cdot p_3 = p_1 (1 - p_2) p_3$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Reactor Loss-of-Coolant Accident (LOCA)

This is the **canonical KTU 2024 ESE-style problem** on Event Tree Analysis. Work through it line by line.

### Problem Statement (Model Question)

A nuclear reactor is analyzed for a **Loss-of-Coolant Accident (LOCA)**. The initiating event (IE) has a frequency $f_{\text{IE}} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ per reactor-year. The engineered safety features that respond in sequence are:

- **F1 — Reactor Trip (SCRAM):** $P(\text{success}) = p_1 = 0.999$
- **F2 — Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) Injection:** $p_2 = 0.99$
- **F3 — Containment Spray System (CSS):** $p_3 = 0.98$
- **F4 — Containment Isolation (CI):** $p_4 = 0.995$

Determine:
**(a)** The probability of each of the 16 outcome sequences.
**(b)** The frequency of *Core Damage* (defined here as the end state reached when any failure of F1, F2, or F3 occurs).
**(c)** The frequency of *Safe Shutdown* (all four functions succeed).

### Step 1 — Construct the Event Tree (Logic)

The tree has 4 binary branch points. Total number of outcome paths = $2^4 = 16$. The tree structure is:

```
                                                    [S-S-S-S] → Safe Shutdown
                                                  /
                                [S-S-S] ────── F4
                              /                \
                            S3                  [S-S-S-F] → CI Failure (Minor Release)
                          /                      
                       [S-S]                  
                     /     \                 
                   S2      [S-S-F-S] → CSS Fails
                  /         \
              [S]            [S-S-F-F] → CSS+CI Fail
             /  \
           S1    [S-F-S-S] → ECCS Fails
          /       \
       (IE)      [S-F-S-F] → ECCS+CI Fail
                 
       ... and so on for F1-Fail branch (8 more paths) ...
```

### Step 2 — Compute All 16 Path Frequencies

We compute $P(\text{path}) = \prod_{i=1}^{4} p_i^{a_i} (1 - p_i)^{1 - a_i}$ where $a_i = 1$ for success and $a_i = 0$ for failure. Then $f(\text{path}) = f_{\text{IE}} \cdot P(\text{path})$.

First, tabulate the failure probabilities:

$$q_1 = 1 - 0.999 = 0.001$$
$$q_2 = 1 - 0.99 = 0.01$$
$$q_3 = 1 - 0.98 = 0.02$$
$$q_4 = 1 - 0.995 = 0.005$$

**Path 1 (S-S-S-S):** All four succeed. Probability = $p_1 p_2 p_3 p_4$.

$$P_1 = 0.999 \times 0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.995$$

Compute step by step:

$$\begin{aligned}
0.999 \times 0.99 &= 0.98901 \\
0.98901 \times 0.98 &= 0.9692298 \\
0.9692298 \times 0.995 &= 0.9643836510
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$f_1 = f_{\text{IE}} \cdot P_1 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.9643836510 = 9.6438 \times 10^{-4} \text{ / yr}$$

**Path 2 (S-S-S-F):** F1, F2, F3 succeed; F4 fails. Probability = $p_1 p_2 p_3 q_4$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_2 &= 0.999 \times 0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.005 \\
0.999 \times 0.99 &= 0.98901 \\
0.98901 \times 0.98 &= 0.9692298 \\
0.9692298 \times 0.005 &= 0.004846149
\end{aligned}$$

$$f_2 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.8461 \times 10^{-3} = 4.8461 \times 10^{-6} \text{ / yr}$$

**Path 3 (S-S-F-S):** F1, F2 succeed; F3 fails; F4 succeeds. Probability = $p_1 p_2 q_3 p_4$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_3 &= 0.999 \times 0.99 \times 0.02 \times 0.995 \\
0.999 \times 0.99 &= 0.98901 \\
0.98901 \times 0.02 &= 0.0197802 \\
0.0197802 \times 0.995 &= 0.019681299
\end{aligned}$$

$$f_3 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.9681 \times 10^{-2} = 1.9681 \times 10^{-5} \text{ / yr}$$

**Path 4 (S-S-F-F):** F1, F2 succeed; F3, F4 fail.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_4 &= 0.999 \times 0.99 \times 0.02 \times 0.005 \\
0.98901 \times 0.02 &= 0.0197802 \\
0.0197802 \times 0.005 &= 9.8901 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}$$

$$f_4 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.8901 \times 10^{-5} = 9.8901 \times 10^{-8} \text{ / yr}$$

**Path 5 (S-F-S-S):** F1 succeeds; F2 fails; F3, F4 succeed.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_5 &= 0.999 \times 0.01 \times 0.98 \times 0.995 \\
0.999 \times 0.01 &= 0.00999 \\
0.00999 \times 0.98 &= 0.0097902 \\
0.0097902 \times 0.995 &= 0.009741249
\end{aligned}$$

$$f_5 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.7412 \times 10^{-3} = 9.7412 \times 10^{-6} \text{ / yr}$$

**Path 6 (S-F-S-F):** F1, F3, F4 succeed; F2 fails.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_6 &= 0.999 \times 0.01 \times 0.98 \times 0.005 \\
0.00999 \times 0.98 &= 0.0097902 \\
0.0097902 \times 0.005 &= 4.8951 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}$$

$$f_6 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.8951 \times 10^{-5} = 4.8951 \times 10^{-8} \text{ / yr}$$

**Path 7 (S-F-F-S):** F1, F4 succeed; F2, F3 fail.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_7 &= 0.999 \times 0.01 \times 0.02 \times 0.995 \\
0.00999 \times 0.02 &= 0.0001998 \\
0.0001998 \times 0.995 &= 1.98801 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}$$

$$f_7 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.9880 \times 10^{-4} = 1.9880 \times 10^{-7} \text{ / yr}$$

**Path 8 (S-F-F-F):** F1, F4 succeed; F2, F3 fail.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_8 &= 0.999 \times 0.01 \times 0.02 \times 0.005 \\
0.00999 \times 0.02 &= 0.0001998 \\
0.0001998 \times 0.005 &= 9.99 \times 10^{-7}
\end{aligned}$$

$$f_8 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.99 \times 10^{-7} = 9.99 \times 10^{-10} \text{ / yr}$$

**Path 9 (F-S-S-S):** F1 fails; F2, F3, F4 succeed.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_9 &= 0.001 \times 0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.995 \\
0.001 \times 0.99 &= 9.9 \times 10^{-4} \\
9.9 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.98 &= 9.702 \times 10^{-4} \\
9.702 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.995 &= 9.65349 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}$$

$$f_9 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.6535 \times 10^{-4} = 9.6535 \times 10^{-7} \text{ / yr}$$

**Path 10 (F-S-S-F):** F1, F4 fail; F2, F3 succeed.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{10} &= 0.001 \times 0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.005 \\
9.702 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.005 &= 4.851 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}$$

$$f_{10} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.851 \times 10^{-6} = 4.851 \times 10^{-9} \text{ / yr}$$

**Path 11 (F-S-F-S):** F1, F3 fail; F2, F4 succeed.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{11} &= 0.001 \times 0.99 \times 0.02 \times 0.995 \\
0.001 \times 0.99 &= 9.9 \times 10^{-4} \\
9.9 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.02 &= 1.98 \times 10^{-5} \\
1.98 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.995 &= 1.9701 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}$$

$$f_{11} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.9701 \times 10^{-5} = 1.9701 \times 10^{-8} \text{ / yr}$$

**Path 12 (F-S-F-F):** F1, F3, F4 fail; F2 succeeds.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{12} &= 0.001 \times 0.99 \times 0.02 \times 0.005 \\
1.98 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.005 &= 9.9 \times 10^{-8}
\end{aligned}$$

$$f_{12} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.9 \times 10^{-8} = 9.9 \times 10^{-11} \text{ / yr}$$

**Path 13 (F-F-S-S):** F1, F2 fail; F3, F4 succeed.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{13} &= 0.001 \times 0.01 \times 0.98 \times 0.995 \\
0.001 \times 0.01 &= 1 \times 10^{-5} \\
1 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.98 &= 9.8 \times 10^{-6} \\
9.8 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.995 &= 9.751 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}$$

$$f_{13} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.751 \times 10^{-6} = 9.751 \times 10^{-9} \text{ / yr}$$

**Path 14 (F-F-S-F):** F1, F2, F4 fail; F3 succeeds.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{14} &= 0.001 \times 0.01 \times 0.98 \times 0.005 \\
9.8 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.005 &= 4.9 \times 10^{-8}
\end{aligned}$$

$$f_{14} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.9 \times 10^{-8} = 4.9 \times 10^{-11} \text{ / yr}$$

**Path 15 (F-F-F-S):** F1, F2, F3 fail; F4 succeeds.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{15} &= 0.001 \times 0.01 \times 0.02 \times 0.995 \\
1 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.02 &= 2 \times 10^{-7} \\
2 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.995 &= 1.99 \times 10^{-7}
\end{aligned}$$

$$f_{15} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.99 \times 10^{-7} = 1.99 \times 10^{-10} \text{ / yr}$$

**Path 16 (F-F-F-F):** All four fail.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{16} &= 0.001 \times 0.01 \times 0.02 \times 0.005 \\
2 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.005 &= 1 \times 10^{-9}
\end{aligned}$$

$$f_{16} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 \times 10^{-9} = 1 \times 10^{-12} \text{ / yr}$$

### Step 3 — Answer to (b): Frequency of Core Damage

**Core Damage is defined as: any failure of F1 OR F2 OR F3** (per the problem statement). So Core Damage = the *complement* of "all of F1, F2, F3 succeed." Let us identify all 16 paths and isolate those where F1, F2, F3 are ALL successful.

The paths with F1-S, F2-S, F3-S (regardless of F4) are:
- Path 1: S-S-S-S → Safe Shutdown
- Path 2: S-S-S-F → CI Failure (not core damage per the definition)

So the probability of "no core damage" = $P_1 + P_2 = 0.964383651 + 0.004846149 = 0.969229800$.

Probability of **core damage** = $1 - 0.969229800 = 0.030770200$.

Frequency of core damage:

$$f_{\text{CD}} = f_{\text{IE}} \cdot 0.030770200 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.030770200 = 3.077 \times 10^{-5} \text{ / yr}$$

Equivalently, summing all 14 damaging paths directly:

$$f_{\text{CD}} = \sum_{k=3}^{16} f_k = 1.9681 \times 10^{-5} + 9.8901 \times 10^{-8} + \ldots \approx 3.077 \times 10^{-5} \text{ / yr}$$

### Step 4 — Answer to (c): Frequency of Safe Shutdown

Safe Shutdown corresponds to Path 1 only (S-S-S-S). Therefore:

$$f_{\text{SD}} = f_1 = 9.644 \times 10^{-4} \text{ / reactor-year}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** $f_{\text{SD}} + f_{\text{non-SD}} = 9.644 \times 10^{-4} + (1.0 \times 10^{-3} - 9.644 \times 10^{-4}) = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ per reactor-year. This matches the initiating-event frequency, as it must.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Identifying Minimal Cut Sets from ETA

A **minimal cut set** is the smallest combination of safety-function failures that, by itself, leads to an undesired end state. From the tree above, the minimal cut sets for *Core Damage* are:

- {F1}, {F2}, {F3} — any *single* failure of these three is sufficient.

The minimal cut sets for *Major Release* (defined as failure of CI along with any of F2, F3) would be: {F2, F4}, {F3, F4}.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Connection:** Notice that the minimal cut sets identified here are the *Boolean dual* of the minimal path sets (success sets) of the event tree. This duality is what allows ETA and FTA to be cross-verified in industrial PSA studies.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Event Tree for the LOCA Worked Example (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid block renders the **complete 4-function event tree** corresponding to the worked example in Section 3.2. Each leaf node carries the path label, the path probability, and the end-state classification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initA((IE: LOCA)) --> f1
    f1{F1: SCRAM} -->|S p=0.999| s1A[S]
    f1 -->|F q=0.001| f1A[F]
    s1A --> f2A{F2: ECCS}
    f1A --> f2B{F2: ECCS}
    f2A -->|S p=0.99| s2A[S]
    f2A -->|F q=0.01| f2Af[F]
    f2B -->|S p=0.99| s2B[S]
    f2B -->|F q=0.01| f2Bf[F]
    s2A --> f3A{F3: CSS}
    f2Af --> f3B{F3: CSS}
    s2B --> f3C{F3: CSS}
    f2Bf --> f3D{F3: CSS}
    f3A -->|S p=0.98| s3A[S]
    f3A -->|F q=0.02| f3Af[F]
    f3B -->|S p=0.98| s3B[S]
    f3B -->|F q=0.02| f3Bf[F]
    f3C -->|S p=0.98| s3C[S]
    f3C -->|F q=0.02| f3Cf[F]
    f3D -->|S p=0.98| s3D[S]
    f3D -->|F q=0.02| f3Df[F]
    s3A --> f4A{F4: CI}
    f3Af --> f4B{F4: CI}
    s3B --> f4C{F4: CI}
    f3Bf --> f4D{F4: CI}
    s3C --> f4E{F4: CI}
    f3Cf --> f4F{F4: CI}
    s3D --> f4G{F4: CI}
    f3Df --> f4H{F4: CI}
    f4A -->|S p=0.995| endA((S-S-S-S: Safe Shutdown))
    f4A -->|F q=0.005| endB((S-S-S-F: CI Fail))
    f4B -->|S p=0.995| endC((S-S-F-S: Core Damage CSS))
    f4B -->|F q=0.005| endD((S-S-F-F: Core Damage CSS+CI))
    f4C -->|S p=0.995| endE((S-F-S-S: Core Damage ECCS))
    f4C -->|F q=0.005| endF((S-F-S-F: Core Damage ECCS+CI))
    f4D -->|S p=0.995| endG((S-F-F-S: Core Damage ECCS+CSS))
    f4D -->|F q=0.005| endH((S-F-F-F: Core Damage ECCS+CSS+CI))
    f4E -->|S p=0.995| endI((F-S-S-S: Core Damage SCRAM))
    f4E -->|F q=0.005| endJ((F-S-S-F: Core Damage SCRAM+CI))
    f4F -->|S p=0.995| endK((F-S-F-S: Core Damage SCRAM+CSS))
    f4F -->|F q=0.005| endL((F-S-F-F: Core Damage SCRAM+CSS+CI))
    f4G -->|S p=0.995| endM((F-F-S-S: Core Damage SCRAM+ECCS))
    f4G -->|F q=0.005| endN((F-F-S-F: Core Damage SCRAM+ECCS+CI))
    f4H -->|S p=0.995| endO((F-F-F-S: Core Damage SCRAM+ECCS+CSS))
    f4H -->|F q=0.005| endP((F-F-F-F: Core Damage ALL))
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Event Tree Analysis

The following block diagram maps the *procedural workflow* of conducting an ETA — useful when a student is asked in an examination to "explain the steps of ETA" rather than solve a numerical problem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Step 1: Identify Initiating Event] --> step2[Step 2: List Safety Functions in Order]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Assign p and q to Each Function]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Draw Binary Branches at Each Node]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Label All 2 to the n Paths]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Compute Path Probabilities]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Classify End States]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Sum Path Probs by End State]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9: Multiply by IE Frequency if Required]
    step9 --> step10[Step 10: Identify Minimal Cut Sets]
```

## 4.3 Comparison: ETA vs FTA (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ETA_FLOW[Event Tree Analysis - Forward Inductive]
        ie[Initiating Event] --> func1[Function 1]
        func1 --> func2[Function 2]
        func2 --> func3[Function 3]
