```mermaid
        func3 --> ends[End States]
    end
    subgraph FTA_FLOW[Fault Tree Analysis - Backward Deductive]
        top[Top Event] --> gate[AND OR Gates]
        gate --> basic[Basic Events]
    end
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`
**Define Event Tree Analysis. What is meant by an 'initiating event' in ETA?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Event Tree Analysis (ETA) is a **forward-inductive probabilistic safety assessment technique** that models the *propagation* of an undesired initiating event through a sequence of safety functions, branching at each step into success (S) or failure (F), to enumerate all possible outcome sequences and their probabilities (1 mark).

An **initiating event (IE)** is the *first event* in the sequence — the disturbance that challenges the system and triggers the engineered safety features. It is the root node of the event tree and may have an associated frequency (e.g., 1E-3/yr) (1 mark).

The IE may be internal (equipment failure, human error) or external (seismic event, flood). The set of all IEs is typically identified through a Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA) or Master Logic Diagram (1 mark).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Differentiate between an Event Tree Analysis and a Fault Tree Analysis in 5 points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Event Tree Analysis (ETA) | Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward-inductive | Backward-deductive |
| 2 | Starts from the initiating event | Starts from the undesired top event |
| 3 | Enumerates *consequences* | Enumerates *causes* |
| 4 | Uses binary branch points | Uses logic gates (AND, OR, NOT) |
| 5 | Computes outcome frequencies | Computes top-event probability |
| 6 | Useful for accident sequence modeling | Useful for single-failure analysis |
| 7 | Used in PSA Level 2/3 | Used in PSA Level 1 |

(Any 5 contrasting points earn full 3 marks.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]`

**(a)** Define Event Tree Analysis and explain the steps involved in the construction of an event tree with a suitable example diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemical reactor has an initiating event "Overpressure" with frequency $1 \times 10^{-2}$ per year. The mitigating safety functions, in the order of their operation, are:

- **F1 — Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) opens:** $P(\text{success}) = 0.998$
- **F2 — Emergency Cooling System (ECS) activates:** $P(\text{success}) = 0.97$
- **F3 — Containment Isolation (CI):** $P(\text{success}) = 0.99$

The end state "**Rupture**" is defined as the failure of *any one* of F1, F2, or F3. Compute the frequency of Rupture per year. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks

**Definition (2 marks):** ETA is a forward-inductive analytical technique that traces the possible outcomes of an initiating event through a sequence of binary branch points representing the success or failure of safety functions, producing a tree of all possible accident sequences with associated probabilities.

**Construction steps (5 marks):**

1. **Identify the IE** — from PHA or operational experience (1 mark).
2. **Identify safety functions** in their designed temporal order (1 mark).
3. **Assign success and failure probabilities** $p_i$ and $q_i = 1 - p_i$ to each function (1 mark).
4. **Draw binary branches** at each branch point, following the convention of upper = success, lower = failure (1 mark).
5. **Label all $2^n$ terminal paths** with their outcome sequences and compute path probabilities as the product of branch probabilities (1 mark).

*(Include the schematic tree similar to Figure 4.1 above.)*

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**

$$f_{\text{IE}} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \text{ / yr}, \quad p_1 = 0.998, \quad p_2 = 0.97, \quad p_3 = 0.99$$

**Step 1 — Compute failure probabilities:**

$$q_1 = 0.002, \quad q_2 = 0.03, \quad q_3 = 0.01$$

**Step 2 — Compute probability of "no rupture" (all three functions succeed):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{S-S-S}) &= p_1 \cdot p_2 \cdot p_3 \\
&= 0.998 \times 0.97 \times 0.99 \\
&= 0.998 \times 0.97 = 0.96806 \\
&= 0.96806 \times 0.99 = 0.958379
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute probability of "rupture" (at least one failure):**

$$P(\text{Rupture} \mid \text{IE}) = 1 - 0.958379 = 0.041621$$

**Step 4 — Compute frequency of Rupture:**

$$f_{\text{Rupture}} = f_{\text{IE}} \cdot P(\text{Rupture} \mid \text{IE}) = 1 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.041621 = 4.162 \times 10^{-4} \text{ / yr}$$

**Final Answer:** $f_{\text{Rupture}} = 4.162 \times 10^{-4}$ per year.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, compare Event Tree Analysis (ETA) and Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) in terms of direction of analysis, output, and typical industrial application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An offshore platform has an initiating event "**Gas Leak**" with a frequency of $5 \times 10^{-3}$ per year. The safety functions are:

- **F1 — Gas Detection System:** $P(\text{success}) = 0.995$
- **F2 — Automatic Shutdown (SD) System:** $P(\text{success}) = 0.98$
- **F3 — Fire Water Deluge System:** $P(\text{success}) = 0.96$
- **F4 — Emergency Evacuation:** $P(\text{success}) = 0.99$

The end state "**Uncontrolled Fire**" is reached if **F1 succeeds** but **any of F2, F3, F4 fails**. Compute the frequency of "Uncontrolled Fire" per year. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | ETA | FTA |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Forward (Initiating event → Consequences) | Backward (Top event → Basic causes) |
| Symbolic logic | Branching paths, no gates | Boolean logic gates (AND, OR) |
| Typical output | Outcome frequencies / accident sequences | Top-event probability / minimal cut sets |
| Industrial use | PSA Level 2/3, accident sequence modeling | PSA Level 1, single-failure analysis |
| Order of analysis | Begins with the disturbance | Begins with the consequence |

**Schematic (2 marks):** Provide a forward-flow block diagram for ETA and a top-down logic-gate diagram for FTA. *(See Section 4.3.)*

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**

$$f_{\text{IE}} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ / yr}, \quad p_1 = 0.995, \quad p_2 = 0.98, \quad p_3 = 0.96, \quad p_4 = 0.99$$

**Step 1 — Failure probabilities:**

$$q_1 = 0.005, \quad q_2 = 0.02, \quad q_3 = 0.04, \quad q_4 = 0.01$$

**Step 2 — End-state definition:** Uncontrolled Fire requires F1 = S and at least one of {F2, F3, F4} = F.

**Step 3 — Probability of "Uncontrolled Fire" given IE:**

By inclusion–exclusion, the probability of F2, F3, F4 *all* succeeding is:

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{F2-S} \cap \text{F3-S} \cap \text{F4-S}) &= p_2 p_3 p_4 \\
&= 0.98 \times 0.96 \times 0.99 \\
&= 0.98 \times 0.96 = 0.9408 \\
&= 0.9408 \times 0.99 = 0.931392
\end{aligned}$$

Probability of *at least one* of {F2, F3, F4} failing:

$$P(\geq 1 \text{ fail in } \{F2, F3, F4\}) = 1 - 0.931392 = 0.068608$$

**Step 4 — Joint probability with F1-S:**

$$P(\text{Uncontrolled Fire} \mid \text{IE}) = p_1 \times 0.068608 = 0.995 \times 0.068608 = 0.068265$$

**Step 5 — Frequency of Uncontrolled Fire:**

$$f_{\text{UF}} = f_{\text{IE}} \times 0.068265 = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.068265 = 3.413 \times 10^{-4} \text{ / yr}$$

**Final Answer:** $f_{\text{Uncontrolled Fire}} = 3.413 \times 10^{-4}$ per year.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing sequence ordering:** Safety functions must be listed in their *temporal order of operation* — a function that physically operates *first* must appear as F1. Reversing the order yields a different tree and a wrong answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the $f_{\text{IE}}$ multiplier:** If the problem gives an initiating-event *frequency* (per year) and asks for the end-state *frequency*, you must multiply the conditional probability by $f_{\text{IE}}$. Many students answer with a probability when a frequency was asked — instant loss of 2 marks.
> 3. **Treating dependent events as independent:** The independence assumption $P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B)$ only holds for statistically independent safety functions. In KTU problems, this is given or implied.
> 4. **Arithmetic slip in scientific notation:** Always carry $10^{-2}$, $10^{-3}$ etc. as a separate factor and double-check the *exponent* of the final answer.
> 5. **Summing the wrong paths:** Read the end-state definition *carefully*. "Failure of F1 OR F2 OR F3" means *at least one fails*; use the complement rule. "Failure of F1 AND F2 AND F3" means *all three fail*; multiply $q_1 q_2 q_3$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ETA is FORWARD; FTA is BACKWARD.** Memorize this mantra.
- **Three prerequisites** for ETA: (1) defined initiating event, (2) ordered list of safety functions, (3) binary success/failure at each branch.
- **Total number of paths** for $n$ binary safety functions = $2^n$.
- **Path probability** = product of branch probabilities along the path (independence assumed).
- **End-state probability** = sum of path probabilities leading to that end state.
- **End-state frequency** = (initiating event frequency) × (conditional end-state probability).
- **Complement rule shortcut:** $P(\text{at least one fails}) = 1 - P(\text{all succeed})$.
- **Minimal cut sets** can be read directly off an event tree by inspecting the F-branches.
- **Industries of relevance:** Nuclear PSA, offshore oil & gas, chemical process safety, aerospace, civil (dam/structural) safety.
- **KTU exam weightage:** Typically 1 short question (3 marks) + 1 long question (7–14 marks) per module; allocate ~30% of your Module 3 study time to ETA.
- **Common KTU trick:** The IE frequency may be omitted; in that case, report a *probability* (dimensionless) and not a *frequency* (per year). Read the question twice.
- **Units to remember:** Probability is dimensionless $[0, 1]$; Frequency is per unit time (usually per year, sometimes per reactor-year for NPPs).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FMEA and FMECA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** FMEA and FMECA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of FMEA

**Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** is a systematic, structured, and proactive analytical methodology used to evaluate a system, design, process, or service to identify potential failure modes, their causes, and their effects on system performance, safety, or functionality. It was originally developed by the U.S. military (documented in MIL-STD-1629 in 1949 and later refined in MIL-STD-1629A in 1980).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMET747):** FMEA is a step-by-step approach for identifying all possible failures in a system, product, process, or service, determining the cause and effect of each failure, and ranking them according to their severity, frequency of occurrence, and detectability using the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of FMECA

**Failure Mode, Effects, and Criticality Analysis (FMECA)** is an extension of FMEA that adds a **Criticality Analysis** component. While FMEA qualitatively ranks failures, FMECA quantitatively classifies failures based on their criticality—the combined measure of failure severity and probability of occurrence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** FMEA is qualitative (uses RPN ranking), whereas FMECA incorporates **Criticality Numbers (CN)** for quantitative prioritization, often visualized through a **Criticality Matrix**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car and want to identify what could go wrong before it actually happens. You would mentally check each component:

- **Brake System**: What if brake pads wear out? → Effect: Car won't stop properly → Severity: HIGH
- **Headlights**: What if the bulb burns out? → Effect: Reduced visibility at night → Severity: LOW

This is exactly what FMEA does, but in a structured, documented, and prioritized way for **every component** in a system.

**Real-World Analogy — Hospital Pre-Surgery Checklist:**

> [!TIP]
> Think of FMEA as a **doctor's pre-surgery safety checklist**. Before any operation, the medical team identifies every possible complication (failure modes), determines how severe each would be (severity), how likely it is to occur (occurrence), and how easily it can be detected early (detection). This systematic approach prevents catastrophic outcomes—exactly the goal of FMEA in engineering.

### 1.4 Types of FMEA

| Type | Application Focus | Typical Users |
|------|------------------|---------------|
| **System FMEA** | Overall system architecture, interfaces | System Engineers |
| **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** | Product/component design defects | Design Engineers |
| **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** | Manufacturing and assembly processes | Manufacturing Engineers |
| **Service FMEA (SFMEA)** | Service delivery and customer interaction | Service Quality Teams |
| **Software FMEA** | Software bugs and integration failures | Software Engineers |

### 1.5 Historical Context & Standards

- **1949**: U.S. Department of Defense developed the original procedure.
- **1960s**: NASA adopted FMEA for the Apollo space program.
- **1970s**: Automotive industry (Ford, GM) adopted FMEA.
- **1980**: **MIL-STD-1629A** formalized the procedure.
- **2008**: **IEC 60812** standardized FMEA/FMECA methodology internationally.
- **2019**: **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook** unified American and European automotive approaches.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU examinations, students must remember that FMEA is a **proactive** (before failure occurs) reliability tool, as opposed to **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** which is **reactive** (after failure occurs).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Objectives of FMEA

The primary objectives of conducting an FMEA are:

1. **Identify** all potential failure modes of a system or component.
2. **Evaluate** the effects of each failure on system operation.
3. **Determine** the causes of each failure mode.
4. **Prioritize** failures using quantitative metrics.
5. **Recommend** corrective actions to eliminate or reduce risks.
6. **Document** the analysis for future reference and continuous improvement.

### 2.2 Core Terminology

- **Failure Mode**: The manner in which a component, system, or process fails to perform its intended function (e.g., broken, cracked, short-circuited, leaking).
- **Effect**: The consequence of the failure mode on the system's operation, function, or the user.
- **Cause**: The underlying reason for the failure mode (defect, error, or condition).
- **Severity (S)**: A numerical estimate of how serious the effect of the failure is to the user or system.
- **Occurrence (O)**: A numerical estimate of how often the failure mode is expected to occur.
- **Detection (D)**: A numerical estimate of the likelihood that the failure will be detected before reaching the customer/user.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step FMEA Methodology

The structured procedure for conducting an FMEA consists of **10 sequential steps**:

**Step 1: Define the Scope and Boundaries**
- Identify the system, subsystem, or process to be analyzed.
- Establish the level of analysis (component level, assembly level, etc.).
- Define operating conditions and environmental factors.

**Step 2: Assemble the Cross-Functional Team**
- Include experts from design, manufacturing, quality, reliability, and service.
- Diverse teams yield more comprehensive failure identification.

**Step 3: Identify Functions of Each Element**
- Document the intended function of every component or process step.
- Functions may be primary, secondary, or tertiary.

**Step 4: Identify Potential Failure Modes**
- For each function, list all the ways it could fail.
- Use brainstorming, historical data, and engineering judgment.

**Step 5: Determine Effects of Each Failure Mode**
- Assess the consequence on system performance, safety, cost, and customer satisfaction.

**Step 6: Determine Causes of Each Failure Mode**
- Identify the root mechanisms leading to each failure.
- Use tools like fishbone diagrams and fault tree analysis.

**Step 7: Assign Severity (S), Occurrence (O), and Detection (D) Ratings**
- Use standardized 1-10 rating scales.
- Higher numbers indicate worse situations (more severe, more frequent, harder to detect).

**Step 8: Calculate the Risk Priority Number (RPN)**
- RPN = S × O × D
- Values range from 1 (lowest risk) to 1000 (highest risk).

**Step 9: Recommend Corrective Actions**
- Focus on items with high RPN first.
- Actions may reduce severity, occurrence, or improve detection.

**Step 10: Re-evaluate After Actions**
- Recalculate RPN after implementing corrective actions to verify risk reduction.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description | Range / Units |
|-------------------|------------------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Risk Priority Number** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Combined risk metric | $1 \leq RPN \leq 1000$ |
| **Criticality Number (Approach 1)** | $C_{m} = S \times O \times \beta$ | Mode criticality | $1 \leq C_{m} \leq 1000$ |
| **Criticality Number (Approach 2)** | $C_{r} = \sum C_{m}$ | System criticality (sum over all modes) | Item specific |
| **Failure Rate (FMECA)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{\text{Failures}}{\text{Time}}$ | Occurrence measure | failures/hour or FITs |
| **Probability of Failure** | $P_{f} = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | For constant failure rate | Dimensionless |
| **Item Criticality** | $C_{r} = \lambda \alpha \beta t$ | (MIL-STD-1629A approach) | Per $10^6$ hours |
| **Severity Multiplier** | $\beta$ | Failure mode probability ratio | $0 < \beta \leq 1$ |
| **Loss Function (Taguchi)** | $L(x) = k(x - T)^{2}$ | Quality loss per item | Monetary units |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Criticality Analysis Formula (MIL-STD-1629A):**
>
> $$C_{r} = \lambda_{p} \times \alpha \times \beta \times t$$
>
> Where:
> - $C_{r}$ = Criticality of item (failures per $10^6$ hours)
> - $\lambda_{p}$ = Failure rate of the item
> - $\alpha$ = Failure mode ratio (fraction of failures in that mode)
> - $\beta$ = Probability that the failure mode will result in the identified effect
> - $t$ = Mission time in hours

### 2.5 Standard Rating Scales (1-10)

#### Severity Scale (S)

| Rating | Effect | Criteria |
|--------|--------|----------|
| 1 | None | No effect on system |
| 2-3 | Minor | Slight inconvenience noticed by discerning customer |
| 4-6 | Moderate | Loss of primary function; degraded performance |
| 7-8 | High | Major loss of function; unsafe operation |
| 9-10 | Very High | Safety hazard; non-compliance with regulations |

#### Occurrence Scale (O)

| Rating | Failure Rate | Description |
|--------|--------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\leq 0.01$ per $10^6$ hrs | Almost never |
| 3 | $\approx 0.1$ per $10^6$ hrs | Low |
| 5 | $\approx 1$ per $10^6$ hrs | Moderate |
| 7 | $\approx 10$ per $10^6$ hrs | High |
| 9 | $\approx 100$ per $10^6$ hrs | Very High |
| 10 | $> 100$ per $10^6$ hrs | Almost certain |

#### Detection Scale (D)

| Rating | Detection Probability | Criteria |
|--------|----------------------|----------|
| 1 | Very High | Almost certain detection by current controls |
| 5 | Moderate | Some chance of detection |
| 7 | Low | Low chance of detection |
| 10 | Almost Impossible | No current control; remote chance |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

FMEA/FMECA is indispensable in:

- **Aerospace**: Identifying single-point failures in aircraft systems.
- **Automotive**: ISO/TS 16949 and IATF 16949 require Design FMEA and Process FMEA.
- **Medical Devices**: FDA mandates FMEA for risk management (ISO 14971).
- **Nuclear Industry**: Probabilistic safety assessment relies on FMECA.
- **Manufacturing**: Reducing defects and warranty costs.
- **Software Engineering**: Pre-deployment failure identification.

> [!TIP]
> **Why FMEA is Critical in Modern Engineering:** In an era of complex, interconnected systems (IoT, autonomous vehicles, aerospace), even a single unaddressed failure mode can cascade into catastrophic consequences. FMEA provides the systematic foresight to address these risks **before** they manifest in the field.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Brake System FMEA

**Scenario:** An automotive engineer is conducting a Design FMEA on an electronic brake system. Consider the following component failures:

| Item | Function | Failure Mode | Effect | Severity (S) | Cause | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) |
|------|----------|--------------|--------|--------------|-------|----------------|---------------|
| Brake Pad | Generate friction | Excessive wear | Reduced braking efficiency | 8 | Material degradation | 6 | 4 |
| Brake Disc | Provide friction surface | Cracking | Brake judder, vibration | 9 | Thermal fatigue | 4 | 7 |
| Hydraulic Line | Transmit fluid pressure | Leakage | Brake pedal goes soft | 9 | Corrosion | 3 | 5 |
| Master Cylinder | Generate hydraulic pressure | Seal failure | Complete brake failure | 10 | Seal aging | 2 | 6 |

**Step 1: Calculate RPN for each failure mode**

For Brake Pad Excessive Wear:

$$RPN_{pad} = S \times O \times D = 8 \times 6 \times 4$$

$$RPN_{pad} = 192$$

For Brake Disc Cracking:

$$RPN_{disc} = S \times O \times D = 9 \times 4 \times 7$$

$$RPN_{disc} = 252$$

For Hydraulic Line Leakage:

$$RPN_{line} = S \times O \times D = 9 \times 3 \times 5$$

$$RPN_{line} = 135$$

For Master Cylinder Seal Failure:

$$RPN_{cyl} = S \times O \times D = 10 \times 2 \times 6$$

$$RPN_{cyl} = 120$$

**Step 2: Rank by RPN (Descending Priority)**

$$\text{Rank Order: Disc Cracking (252) > Pad Wear (192) > Line Leak (135) > Cylinder Seal (120)}$$

**Step 3: Criticality Classification**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Items with $RPN > 200$ are typically classified as **HIGH PRIORITY** and require immediate corrective action.
> Items with $100 < RPN \leq 200$ are **MEDIUM PRIORITY**.
> Items with $RPN \leq 100$ are **LOW PRIORITY**.

In our example:
- **Brake Disc Cracking** (RPN = 252): HIGH priority → Action: Improve material, add thermal sensors.
- **Brake Pad Wear** (RPN = 192): MEDIUM priority → Action: Improve wear indicator sensitivity.
- **Hydraulic Line Leak** (RPN = 135): MEDIUM priority → Action: Improve corrosion-resistant coating.
- **Master Cylinder Seal** (RPN = 120): MEDIUM priority → Action: Increase inspection frequency.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: FMECA Criticality Calculation

**Scenario:** A hydraulic pump in an aircraft has the following data (per MIL-STD-1629A approach):

- Failure rate of pump: $\lambda_p = 150 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour
- Mission time: $t = 1000$ hours
- Three failure modes with mode ratios ($\alpha$) and effect probabilities ($\beta$):

| Failure Mode | $\alpha$ | $\beta$ |
|--------------|---------|---------|
| Shaft seizure | 0.40 | 1.0 (catastrophic) |
| Seal leakage | 0.35 | 0.5 (overheat risk) |
| Bearing wear | 0.25 | 0.2 (gradual degradation) |

**Step 1: Apply the Criticality Formula**

$$C_r = \lambda_p \times \alpha \times \beta \times t$$

For **Shaft Seizure**:

$$C_{r1} = (150 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.40 \times 1.0 \times 1000$$

$$C_{r1} = 150 \times 10^{-6} \times 400$$

$$C_{r1} = 0.06 = 60 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per } 10^6 \text{ hours}$$

For **Seal Leakage**:

$$C_{r2} = (150 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.35 \times 0.5 \times 1000$$

$$C_{r2} = 150 \times 10^{-6} \times 175$$

$$C_{r2} = 0.02625 = 26.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per } 10^6 \text{ hours}$$

For **Bearing Wear**:

$$C_{r3} = (150 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.25 \times 0.2 \times 1000$$

$$C_{r3} = 150 \times 10^{-6} \times 50$$

$$C_{r3} = 0.0075 = 7.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per } 10^6 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Total Item Criticality**

$$C_{r,total} = C_{r1} + C_{r2} + C_{r3}$$

$$C_{r,total} = 60 \times 10^{-3} + 26.25 \times 10^{-3} + 7.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$C_{r,total} = 93.75 \times 10^{-3} \text{ failures per } 10^6 \text{ hours}$$

**Interpretation**: The pump can be expected to contribute approximately **0.094 failures per million hours** in a critical manner. Shaft seizure alone accounts for **64%** of total criticality, demanding top-priority corrective design intervention.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Comparison of FMEA vs FMECA

For the same brake disc failure (S = 9, O = 4, D = 7):

**FMEA Approach (Qualitative RPN):**

$$RPN = 9 \times 4 \times 7 = 252$$

**FMECA Approach (Quantitative Criticality with mode probability):**

Assume $\alpha = 0.6$ (60% of disc failures are cracking) and $\beta = 0.8$ (80% of cracks lead to detectable effect).

Given $\lambda_p = 80 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour and mission time $t = 500$ hours:

$$C_r = (80 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.6 \times 0.8 \times 500$$

$$C_r = 80 \times 10^{-6} \times 240$$

$$C_r = 19.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ failures per } 10^6 \text{ hours}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why Both Metrics Matter:** RPN provides a relative ranking for action prioritization within an FMEA study. FMECA's $C_r$ provides an absolute measure of failure contribution to system unreliability, useful for safety-critical decisions.

### 3.4 Derived Relationship: RPN and Criticality

In certain FMECA implementations, the mode criticality can be related to the RPN as:

$$C_m = RPN \times k_{f}$$

Where $k_f$ is a calibration factor based on the specific failure rate. For practical KTU problems, $C_m = S \times O$ is commonly used as a simplified criticality metric when detection-related information is unavailable.

### 3.5 Probabilistic Verification of FMECA Results

For a system with constant failure rate $\lambda$, the probability of failure during time $t$ is:

$$P_f(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

For small $\lambda t$ (typical in reliability analysis):

$$P_f(t) \approx \lambda t$$

This validates the linear use of $\lambda \times t$ in the criticality formula $C_r = \lambda_p \times \alpha \times \beta \times t$.

**Numerical Verification:**

With $\lambda_p = 150 \times 10^{-6}$/hour and $t = 1000$ hours:

$$P_f \approx 150 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 = 0.15$$

This means a **15% probability** of pump failure during the 1000-hour mission—confirming why criticality analysis is essential for mission planning.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FMEA/FMECA Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Step1: Define Scope and Boundaries] --> node2[Step2: Assemble Cross-Functional Team]
    node2 --> node3[Step3: Identify System Functions]
    node3 --> node4[Step4: Identify Failure Modes]
    node4 --> node5[Step5: Determine Effects on System]
    node5 --> node6[Step6: Identify Root Causes]
    node6 --> node7[Step7: Assign S O D Ratings]
    node7 --> node8[Step8: Calculate RPN and Criticality]
    node8 --> node9{Decision: RPN Threshold Exceeded?}
    node9 -- Yes --> node10[Step9: Recommend Corrective Actions]
    node9 -- No --> node12[Document and Archive]
    node10 --> node11[Step10: Re-Evaluate RPN After Action]
    node11 --> node8
    node11 --> node12
    node12 --> node13[Continuous Improvement Loop]
```

### 4.2 FMEA Worksheet Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Item or Component] --> nodeB[Function]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Failure Mode]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Effect on System]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Cause of Failure]
    nodeE --> nodeF[S Rating]
    nodeE --> nodeG[O Rating]
    nodeE --> nodeH[D Rating]
    nodeF --> nodeI[RPN Calculation]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Action Priority]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Corrective Action]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Responsibility]
    nodeK --> nodeM[Target Date]
```

### 4.3 Criticality Matrix Diagram (FMECA)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[FMECA Criticality Matrix] --> nodeB[Vertical Axis Severity 1 to 10]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Horizontal Axis Occurrence Probability]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Region I: Catastrophic S gt 9 High Occ]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Region II: Critical S 7 to 9 Med Occ]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Region III: Marginal S 4 to 6 Low Occ]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Region IV: Minor S lt 4 Rare Occ]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Immediate Action Required]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Action Plan Within 30 Days]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Monitor and Review Quarterly]
    nodeG --> nodeK[Acceptable Risk No Action]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of FMEA-to-FMECA Transition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[System Definition Block] --> node2[Function Identification Block]
    node2 --> node3[Failure Mode Enumeration Block]
    node3 --> node4[Effect Analysis Block]
    node4 --> node5[Cause Analysis Block]
    node5 --> node6[RPN Computation Engine]
    node6 --> node7{Decision Block: Include Criticality?}
    node7 -- No --> node8[FMEA Output Report]
    node7 -- Yes --> node9[Criticality Number Calculation Block]
    node9 --> node10[Criticality Matrix Generator]
    node10 --> node11[FMECA Output Report]
    node8 --> node12[Corrective Action Module]
    node11 --> node12
    node12 --> node13[RPN Reduction Verification]
    node13 --> node14[Final Risk Assessment]
```

### 4.5 Action Priority Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Computed RPN Value] --> nodeB{RPN gt 200?}
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeC[HIGH PRIORITY Immediate Corrective Action]
    nodeB -- No --> nodeD{100 lt RPN le 200?}
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE[MEDIUM PRIORITY Action Within 30 Days]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeF{RPN le 100?}
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG[LOW PRIORITY Monitor Periodically]
    nodeF -- No --> nodeH[Review Detection Rating]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Document and Verify]
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Feedback to Design Team]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When asked to draw or describe the FMEA process, always begin with **scope definition** and end with **re-evaluation after corrective actions**. Most students lose marks by omitting the iterative re-evaluation step.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: Define FMEA. List its key advantages.** *(Model Question)*

> **Model Answer:**
> **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** is a systematic, proactive reliability methodology used to identify and evaluate potential failure modes of a system, product, or process before they occur in service.
>
> **Key Advantages:**
> 1. Identifies potential failures early in the design phase, reducing redesign costs.
> 2. Improves product quality, safety, and customer satisfaction.
> 3. Provides a documented record for regulatory compliance (e.g., ISO 9001, IATF 16949).
> 4. Facilitates prioritization of resources toward high-risk items via RPN.
> 5. Enhances cross-functional communication among engineering teams.
> 6. Supports continuous improvement through iterative re-evaluation.

**Question 2: Differentiate between FMEA and FMECA.** *(Model Question)*

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Aspect | FMEA | FMECA |
> |--------|------|-------|
> | Full Form | Failure Mode and Effects Analysis | Failure Mode, Effects and Criticality Analysis |
> | Analysis Type | Primarily qualitative | Quantitative extension of FMEA |
> | Output Metric | Risk Priority Number (RPN) | Criticality Number (CN) and Criticality Matrix |
> | Ranking Basis | S, O, D triad | Severity × Probability of occurrence |
> | Use Case | General reliability assessment | Safety-critical and aerospace systems |
> | Standard Reference | IEC 60812 | MIL-STD-1629A |

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A: 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for conducting a **Design FMEA** with a neat illustration of the FMEA worksheet structure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For an **automotive brake disc system**, the following failure modes have been identified. Calculate the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** for each failure mode and rank them in order of priority for corrective action. **(7 Marks)**

| Failure Mode | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) |
|--------------|--------------|----------------|---------------|
| Disc cracking due to thermal fatigue | 9 | 4 | 7 |
| Disc surface scoring | 6 | 7 | 5 |
| Disc corrosion | 7 | 5 | 8 |
| Disc thickness variation | 5 | 8 | 4 |

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Procedure for Design FMEA:**

**Step 1: Scope Definition** — Identify the system boundary. For a brake disc, this includes the disc itself, callipers, pads, and rotor assembly interface.

**Step 2: Team Assembly** — Design engineer, materials specialist, manufacturing engineer, quality engineer, and reliability engineer.

**Step 3: Function Identification** — The disc provides a friction surface to convert kinetic energy into thermal energy during braking.

**Step 4: Failure Mode Enumeration** — Cracking, scoring, corrosion, thickness variation, warping.

**Step 5: Effect Determination** — Reduced braking efficiency, vibration, noise, safety hazard.

**Step 6: Cause Identification** — Thermal cycling, material defects, environmental exposure, manufacturing tolerances.

**Step 7: Rating Assignment** — Apply standardized 1-10 scales for S, O, D.

**Step 8: RPN Calculation** — $RPN = S \times O \times D$

**Step 9: Action Recommendation** — Focus on highest RPN items.

**Step 10: Re-evaluation** — Recalculate RPN after actions are implemented.

*(Refer to Section 4.2 for the FMEA worksheet structure diagram.)*

**(b) RPN Calculation:**

For **Disc Cracking**:

$$RPN_1 = 9 \times 4 \times 7 = 252$$

For **Disc Surface Scoring**:

$$RPN_2 = 6 \times 7 \times 5 = 210$$

For **Disc Corrosion**:

$$RPN_3 = 7 \times 5 \times 8 = 280$$

For **Disc Thickness Variation**:

$$RPN_4 = 5 \times 8 \times 4 = 160$$

**Priority Ranking (Highest to Lowest):**

$$\text{Disc Corrosion (280)} > \text{Disc Cracking (252)} > \text{Disc Scoring (210)} > \text{Thickness Variation (160)}$$

**Corrective Action Recommendations:**
- **Disc Corrosion (280)**: Apply anti-corrosion coating; improve material grade.
- **Disc Cracking (252)**: Conduct thermal stress analysis; improve heat treatment.
- **Disc Scoring (210)**: Specify pad material compatibility.
- **Thickness Variation (160)**: Tighten machining tolerances.

---

#### QUESTION B: 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the **FMECA methodology** as per **MIL-STD-1629A**. Derive the criticality formula and explain each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A flight control servo motor has a failure rate $\lambda_p = 250 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour. For a mission time of 500 hours, calculate the **item criticality** for the following failure modes: **(7 Marks)**

| Failure Mode | Mode Ratio ($\alpha$) | Effect Probability ($\beta$) |
|--------------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| Open circuit in windings | 0.30 | 1.0 |
| Short circuit in windings | 0.45 | 0.9 |
| Bearing seizure | 0.20 | 0.7 |
| Brush wear | 0.05 | 0.3 |

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) FMECA Methodology per MIL-STD-1629A:**

The U.S. Department of Defense published MIL-STD-1629A in 1980 to standardize the FMECA procedure for military systems. The methodology involves three phases:

**Phase 1 — Failure Mode and Effects Analysis:** Identify all failure modes, their effects, and causes for each item in the system.

**Phase 2 — Criticality Analysis:** Quantitatively classify the severity of failure mode combinations using the criticality formula.

**Phase 3 — Corrective Action Identification:** Rank items by criticality and recommend design changes.

**Derivation of the Criticality Formula:**

The criticality of an item is defined as the expected number of failures of a particular severity level per mission time. It is derived as follows:

Let $N$ = total number of failures during mission time $t$.

Expected number of item failures in mode $j$:

$$N_j = \lambda_p \times \alpha_j \times t$$

Expected number of catastrophic failures (where failure mode results in the critical effect):

$$N_{critical} = N_j \times \beta_j = \lambda_p \times \alpha_j \times \beta_j \times t$$

Expressed per $10^6$ hours for normalization:

$$C_r = \lambda_p \times \alpha_j \times \beta_j \times t \quad \text{(failures per } 10^6 \text{ hours)}$$

**Term Definitions:**
- $C_r$ = Criticality of the item in a particular failure mode (per $10^6$ hours).
- $\lambda_p$ = Failure rate of the item (failures per hour).
- $\alpha$ = Failure mode ratio (fraction of total item failures attributable to that mode).
- $\beta$ = Conditional probability that the failure mode will result in the identified critical effect.
- $t$ = Mission duration or operating time in hours.

**(b) Criticality Calculation:**

Using the formula $C_r = \lambda_p \times \alpha \times \beta \times t$:

For **Open Circuit in Windings**:

$$C_{r1} = (250 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.30 \times 1.0 \times 500$$

$$C_{r1} = 250 \times 10^{-6} \times 150 = 0.0375 \text{ failures per } 10^6 \text{ hours}$$

For **Short Circuit in Windings**:

$$C_{r2} = (250 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.45 \times 0.9 \times 500$$

$$C_{r2} = 250 \times 10^{-6} \times 202.5 = 0.0506 \text{ failures per } 10^6 \text{ hours}$$

For **Bearing Seizure**:

$$C_{r3} = (250 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.20 \times 0.7 \times 500$$

$$C_{r3} = 250 \times 10^{-6} \times 70 = 0.0175 \text{ failures per } 10^6 \text{ hours}$$

For **Brush Wear**:

$$C_{r4} = (250 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.05 \times 0.3 \times 500$$

$$C_{r4} = 250 \times 10^{-6} \times 7.5 = 0.001875 \text{ failures per } 10^6 \text{ hours}$$

**Total Item Criticality:**

$$C_{r,total} = 0.0375 + 0.0506 + 0.0175 + 0.001875 = 0.1075 \text{ per } 10^6 \text{ hours}$$

**Conclusion:** Short circuit in windings contributes approximately **47%** of the total criticality and demands the highest priority for design improvement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Severity with Occurrence**: Severity measures *how bad* the effect is, while Occurrence measures *how often* it happens. Many students interchange these.
> 2. **Forgetting to recalculate RPN**: After recommending corrective actions, the RPN must be recalculated to show improvement. Marks are awarded only when re-evaluation is explicitly shown.
> 3. **Omitting $\beta$ in FMECA**: Students often compute $C_r = \lambda_p \times \alpha \times t$ without the $\beta$ (effect probability) term. The MIL-STD-1629A formulation **requires** all four factors.
> 4. **Wrong RPN range**: RPN ranges from 1 to 1000, not 1 to 100. Ensure students use the correct product of three 1-10 scales.
> 5. **Skipping the iteration loop**: FMEA is not a one-time activity. Always mention the re-evaluation and continuous improvement aspect.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PEMET747 — Module 3:**

- **FMEA** = Failure Mode and Effects Analysis; **FMECA** = Failure Mode, Effects, and Criticality Analysis.
- FMEA is **qualitative**; FMECA is **quantitative**.
- Standard reference for FMEA: **IEC 60812**; for FMECA: **MIL-STD-1629A**.
- **RPN Formula**: $RPN = S \times O \times D$, where $S, O, D \in \{1, 2, \dots, 10\}$.
- RPN ranges from **1** (lowest risk) to **1000** (highest risk).
- **Threshold for action**: Typically RPN > 200 demands immediate corrective action.
- **Criticality Formula (MIL-STD-1629A)**: $C_r = \lambda_p \times \alpha \times \beta \times t$.
- **$\alpha$** = failure mode ratio; **$\beta$** = conditional probability of critical effect.
- **Severity Scale**: 1 (no effect) → 10 (safety hazard, catastrophic).
- **Occurrence Scale**: 1 (almost never) → 10 (almost certain, very frequent).
- **Detection Scale**: 1 (almost certain detection) → 10 (almost impossible to detect).
- FMEA has **four main types**: System, Design (DFMEA), Process (PFMEA), Service.
- FMEA is a **proactive** (before failure) tool; RCA is **reactive** (after failure).
- **Criticality Matrix** plots Severity vs Occurrence; the top-right corner is the most critical region.
- FMEA reduces warranty costs, improves safety, ensures **regulatory compliance** (FDA, ISO 14971, IATF 16949).
- Always remember the **10-step FMEA procedure**: Scope → Team → Functions → Failure Modes → Effects → Causes → S/O/D Ratings → RPN → Actions → Re-evaluation.
- For aerospace/safety-critical applications, **FMECA is mandatory** over basic FMEA.
- The **Action Priority (AP)** approach (introduced in AIAG-VDA 2019) is a modern alternative to RPN that considers Severity, Occurrence, and Detection separately without multiplication.
- **Taguchi Loss Function**: $L(x) = k(x - T)^2$ relates quality loss to deviation from target.
- FMEA outputs a **worksheet table** with 12-15 columns; this is the primary deliverable.
- **Software FMEA** is increasingly important in cyber-physical and IoT systems.
- Always document the **cross-functional team members** in FMEA reports for traceability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tie-Set and Cut-Set Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Tie-Set and Cut-Set Methods # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Graph-Theoretic Modeling of Engineering Systems In Reliability Engineering, a complex engineering system (such as a power distribution grid, a computer network, a chemical plant pipeline, or a flight control system) is mathematically represented as a **reliability block diagram** (RBD), which is a special case of a graph $G = (V, E)$ where: - **Vertices (Nodes)** represent connection points, junctions, or terminals. - **Edges (Branches)** represent system *components* (pumps, switches, transformers, processors, valves, etc.) connecting the source (input node $s$) to the sink (output node $t$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A system is said to be *coherent* if (i) each component is *binary* (either working or failed), (ii) the system structure function is *monotonic* in its arguments, and (iii) every component is *relevant* to the system. Tie-Set and Cut-Set methods apply **only to coherent systems**. --- ## 1.2 Tie-Set (Minimal Path Set) ### Formal Definition A **Tie-Set** $T_i$ is a *minimal* set of components such that **if every component in $T_i$ works, the system works** (provides a successful path from source to sink), and if any single component of $T_i$ is removed, the resulting set is no longer a path. - The minimum number of such minimal paths is the **Path Order** $P_k$. - If a system has $m$ components and the minimum number of components in any tie-set is $k$, then the system is said to be of **order $k$** or a **$k$-out-of-$m$ structure**. ### Intuitive Analogy — The "City Road Network" 🛣️ Imagine you live in a city and need to reach your office. There are **5 possible routes** from home to office (each route = a Tie-Set). A Tie-Set is the *minimum* list of roads that, if all are open, *guarantees* you reach work. If a single road on your chosen route is closed for construction, that particular route fails — but you can try another Tie-Set. As long as **at least one Tie-Set is fully intact**, the system functions. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The system *fails* only when **every** Tie-Set is broken. The word "Tie" historically comes from topology — a path that "ties" the input to the output node. --- ## 1.3 Cut-Set (Minimal Cut Set) ### Formal Definition A **Cut-Set** $C_i$ is a *minimal* set of components such that **if every component in $C_i$ fails, the system fails**, and if any single component of $C_i$ is removed, the resulting set no longer causes system failure. - The minimum number of components in any cut-set is the **Cut Order** $c_k$. ### Intuitive Analogy — The "Botulinum Strike" Analogy 🎯 Continuing the road analogy: a Cut-Set is a *strategic choke point*. Think of a city's traffic system where only **2 specific bridges** connect the eastern and western halves. If *both* bridges are bombed simultaneously, the city is split in two. A Cut-Set is the **smallest group of failures** that disconnects the system. The analyst's job is to find the *smallest, most lethal* cut-sets (the **"single points of failure"** are 1st-order cut-sets). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Value:** Cut-Set analysis is the foundation of **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** and **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** used in aerospace, nuclear, and process safety engineering. --- ## 1.4 The Duality Principle The **dual network** $G^*$ of a planar network $G$ is constructed by placing a node inside every mesh (loop) of $G$ and drawing a branch through every original edge. The fundamental result is: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Duality Theorem:** The *Tie-Sets* of network $G$ are in **one-to-one correspondence** with the *Cut-Sets* of its dual $G^*$, and vice versa. This is the single most powerful result in KTU Module 3 — once you can find tie-sets, you can immediately write the cut-sets (and vice versa) by inspecting the dual. --- ## 1.5 Why These Methods Are Indispensable in KTU Reliability Studies For systems with $m \leq 5$ components in simple series/parallel combinations, conditional probability methods (Boolean reduction) are adequate. But for **non-series, non-parallel (bridge) networks**, these methods *break down*. Tie-Set and Cut-Set methods provide a **systematic, algorithmic, and rigorous** approach for **any arbitrary** network topology. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Tie-Set Method (Path Enumeration Method) The system works if and only if **at least one** tie-set is fully intact. Therefore, the system reliability is the **probability of the union** of all tie-set events: $$R_s = P\!\left( \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} T_i \right)$$ By the **Inclusion–Exclusion Principle**, expanding the union: $$R_s = \sum_{i} P(T_i) - \sum_{i [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** For a system with $m$ components, the *theoretical* number of minimal paths is bounded by $\sum_{k=k_{min}}^{m} \binom{m}{k}$, but in practice, this number is much smaller due to the graph topology. --- ## 2.2 The Cut-Set Method (Cut Enumeration Method) The system fails if and only if **at least one** cut-set is fully failed. Therefore, the system *unreliability* is the probability of the union of all cut-set events: $$Q_s = 1 - R_s = P\!\left( \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} C_i \right)$$ Expanding using Inclusion–Exclusion: $$Q_s = \sum_{i} P(C_i) - \sum_{i [!NOTE] > The following table contains the **complete, exam-ready formula set** for Module 3 of PEMET747. Memorize this entire table. | **#** | **Concept / Method** | **Formula** | **When to Use** | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Series System Reliability | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{m} R_i$ | All components in a single line; *any* failure breaks the system. | | 2 | Parallel System Reliability | $R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{m} (1 - R_i) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{m} Q_i$ | Components in parallel; system fails only if *all* fail. | | 3 | Tie-Set System Reliability | $R_s = P(\bigcup_i T_i) = P(T_1 \cup T_2 \cup \cdots \cup T_n)$ | Non-series/non-parallel systems, evaluated via inclusion–exclusion. | | 4 | Cut-Set System Unreliability | $Q_s = P(\bigcup_i C_i) = P(C_1 \cup C_2 \cup \cdots \cup C_n)$ | Same, but using minimal cuts; useful for low $R_i$ (low reliability) cases. | | 5 | Inclusion–Exclusion (General) | $P(\bigcup_i A_i) = \sum_i P(A_i) - \sum_{i [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pedagogy Note:** Examiners award **partial credit for correct enumeration** even if the inclusion–exclusion arithmetic has a slip. Always **list every minimal tie-set and minimal cut-set explicitly** in your answer script before any calculation. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Inclusion–Exclusion for Tie-Sets Let $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$ be the events that the $n$ minimal paths are intact. We want $R_s = P(T_1 \cup T_2 \cup \cdots \cup T_n)$. **Step 1 (Base case $n = 2$):** From probability axioms, $$P(T_1 \cup T_2) = P(T_1) + P(T_2) - P(T_1 \cap T_2)$$ The intersection $P(T_1 \cap T_2)$ is the probability that *both* paths work, i.e., *all* components appearing in the union of $T_1$ and $T_2$ work. By independence: $$P(T_1 \cap T_2) = \prod_{k \in T_1 \cup T_2} R_k$$ **Step 2 (Inductive step):** Assume the formula holds for $n-1$ events. Write $T_1 \cup T_2 \cup \cdots \cup T_n = (T_1 \cup T_2 \cup \cdots \cup T_{n-1}) \cup T_n$. Then $$\begin{aligned} P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} T_i\right) &= P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n-1} T_i\right) + P(T_n) - P\!\left[\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n-1} T_i\right) \cap T_n\right] \\[4pt] &= P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n-1} T_i\right) + P(T_n) - P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n-1} (T_i \cap T_n)\right) \end{aligned}$$ Applying the induction hypothesis to both unions yields the full inclusion–exclusion expansion. **Step 3 (Computational form):** $$\begin{aligned} R_s &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(T_i) - \sum_{1 \leq i < j \leq n} P(T_i \cap T_j) \\ &\quad + \sum_{1 \leq i < j < k \leq n} P(T_i \cap T_j \cap T_k) - \cdots + (-1)^{n+1} P(T_1 \cap T_2 \cap \cdots \cap T_n) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 (Truncation logic):** When $R_i$ are close to 1, products of unreliabilities $Q_i$ become negligible quickly, allowing truncation at the second or third order. The **alternating sign** guarantees the partial sums alternately *overestimate* and *underestimate* the true value. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — The Classical Bridge Network > [!NOTE] > This is the **single most important worked example** for KTU Module 3. The bridge network (5 components) is non-series, non-parallel, and **cannot** be solved by simple product formulas. The minimum required treatment shows up in nearly every KTU past paper. ### Network Description Consider the **Wheatstone bridge network** with 5 components and reliability values as marked: | Component | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ | $E$ | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Reliability $R_i$ | 0.90 | 0.85 | 0.80 | 0.92 | 0.95 | The source is node 1, the sink is node 2. Edges: $A = (1,3)$, $B = (1,4)$, $C = (3,4)$, $D = (3,2)$, $E = (4,2)$. ### Step A — Enumerate All Minimal Tie-Sets (Paths) A systematic DFS from node 1 to node 2 yields: $$T_1 = \{A, D\}, \quad T_2 = \{B, E\}, \quad T_3 = \{A, C, E\}, \quad T_4 = \{B, C, D\}$$ *Verification of minimality:* Each set is a path. Removing any single edge breaks the path. ✓ (No set contains a smaller path as a proper subset.) ### Step B — Compute Single-Path Probabilities $$\begin{aligned} P(T_1) &= P(A) \cdot P(D) = 0.90 \times 0.92 = 0.8280 \\ P(T_2) &= P(B) \cdot P(E) = 0.85 \times 0.95 = 0.8075 \\ P(T_3) &= P(A) \cdot P(C) \cdot P(E) = 0.90 \times 0.80 \times 0.95 = 0.6840 \\ P(T_4) &= P(B) \cdot P(C) \cdot P(D) = 0.85 \times 0.80 \times 0.92 = 0.6256 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step C — Compute Two-Path Intersections The components appearing in the union of any two paths, and the resulting product: | Pair | Union of Components | $P(T_i \cap T_j)$ | Numerical Value | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | | $T_1 \cap T_2$ | $\{A, B, D, E\}$ | $R_A R_B R_D R_E$ | $0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 = 0.6686$ | | $T_1 \cap T_3$ | $\{A, C, D, E\}$ | $R_A R_C R_D R_E$ | $0.90 \times 0.80 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 = 0.6293$ | | $T_1 \cap T_4$ | $\{A, B, C, D\}$ | $R_A R_B R_C R_D$ | $0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.80 \times 0.92 = 0.5628$ | | $T_2 \cap T_3$ | $\{A, B, C, E\}$ | $R_A R_B R_C R_E$ | $0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.80 \times 0.95 = 0.5814$ | | $T_2 \cap T_4$ | $\{B, C, D, E\}$ | $R_B R_C R_D R_E$ | $0.85 \times 0.80 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 = 0.5943$ | | $T_3 \cap T_4$ | $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ | $R_A R_B R_C R_D R_E$ | $0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.80 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 = 0.5356$ | ### Step D — Compute Three-Path Intersections $$\begin{aligned} P(T_1 \cap T_2 \cap T_3) &= R_A R_B R_C R_D R_E = 0.5356 \\ P(T_1 \cap T_2 \cap T_4) &= R_A R_B R_C R_D R_E = 0.5356 \\ P(T_1 \cap T_3 \cap T_4) &= R_A R_B R_C R_D R_E = 0.5356 \\ P(T_2 \cap T_3 \cap T_4) &= R_A R_B R_C R_D R_E = 0.5356 \end{aligned}$$ (All four triple intersections equal the product of all five reliabilities since each triple contains all 5 components.) ### Step E — Compute Four-Path Intersection $$P(T_1 \cap T_2 \cap T_3 \cap T_4) = R_A R_B R_C R_D R_E = 0.5356$$ ### Step F — Apply Inclusion–Exclusion $$\begin{aligned} R_s &= \bigl[0.8280 + 0.8075 + 0.6840 + 0.6256\bigr] \\ &\quad - \bigl[0.6686 + 0.6293 + 0.5628 + 0.5814 + 0.5943 + 0.5356\bigr] \\ &\quad + \bigl[0.5356 + 0.5356 + 0.5356 + 0.5356\bigr] \\ &\quad - \bigl[0.5356\bigr] \end{aligned}$$ **Arithmetic evaluation, line by line:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Sum}_1 &= 0.8280 + 0.8075 + 0.6840 + 0.6256 = 2.9451 \\ \text{Sum}_2 &= 0.6686 + 0.6293 + 0.5628 + 0.5814 + 0.5943 + 0.5356 = 3.5720 \\ \text{Sum}_3 &= 0.5356 \times 4 = 2.1424 \\ \text{Sum}_4 &= 0.5356 \end{aligned}$$ **Combining:** $$R_s = 2.9451 - 3.5720 + 2.1424 - 0.5356 = 0.9799$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer for the Bridge Network:** $R_s = 0.9799$ (i.e., the system is approximately **97.99% reliable** over one mission cycle). This is the canonical KTU Module 3 numerical answer. --- ## 3.3 The Same Bridge — Solved by Cut-Sets (Method Comparison) ### Step A — Enumerate Minimal Cut-Sets The minimal cuts separating source (1) from sink (2) are: $$C_1 = \{A, B\}, \quad C_2 = \{D, E\}, \quad C_3 = \{A, C, E\}, \quad C_4 = \{B, C, D\}$$ Note: $C_3$ and $C_4$ are the *dual* of $T_3$ and $T_4$ from the previous section. ✓ Duality confirmed. ### Step B — Compute Single-Cut Unreliabilities $$\begin{aligned} P(C_1) &= Q_A \cdot Q_B = 0.10 \times 0.15 = 0.0150 \\ P(C_2) &= Q_D \cdot Q_E = 0.08 \times 0.05 = 0.0040 \\ P(C_3) &= Q_A \cdot Q_C \cdot Q_E = 0.10 \times 0.20 \times 0.05 = 0.0010 \\ P(C_4) &= Q_B \cdot Q_C \cdot Q_D = 0.15 \times 0.20 \times 0.08 = 0.0024 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step C — Compute Two-Cut Intersections | Pair | $P(C_i \cap C_j)$ | Value | | :---: | :--- | :---: | | $C_1 \cap C_2$ | $Q_A Q_B Q_D Q_E$ | $0.10 \times 0.15 \times 0.08 \times 0.05 = 0.000060$ | | $C_1 \cap C_3$ | $Q_A Q_B Q_C Q_E$ | $0.10 \times 0.15 \times 0.20 \times 0.05 = 0.000150$ | | $C_1 \cap C_4$ | $Q_A Q_B Q_C Q_D$ | $0.10 \times 0.15 \times 0.20 \times 0.08 = 0.000240$ | | $C_2 \cap C_3$ | $Q_A Q_C Q_D Q_E$ | $0.10 \times 0.20 \times 0.08 \times 0.05 = 0.000080$ | | $C_2 \cap C_4$ | $Q_B Q_C Q_D Q_E$ | $0.15 \times 0.20 \times 0.08 \times 0.05 = 0.000120$ | | $C_3 \cap C_4$ | $Q_A Q_B Q_C Q_D Q_E$ | $0.10 \times 0.15 \times 0.20 \times 0.08 \times 0.05 = 0.000012$ | ### Step D — Three-Cut and Four-Cut Intersections All three-cut and four-cut intersections are bounded above by $0.000012$ (the product of all five unreliabilities), so they contribute **negligibly** and may be safely truncated. ### Step E — Apply Inclusion–Exclusion to Cut-Sets $$\begin{aligned} Q_s &\approx \sum_i P(C_i) - \sum_{i [!NOTE] > **Cross-Check:** The cut-set result $0.9783$ differs from the tie-set result $0.9799$ only in the *fourth decimal place*. The difference arises from truncation of higher-order inclusion–exclusion terms in the cut-set expansion. The *true* value is bounded between them and can be refined with more terms. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Wheatstone Bridge Network — Component Layout ```mermaid graph LR node1((S: Source 1)) node2((T: Sink 2)) node3((Node 3)) node4((Node 4)) node1 -- "A : 0.90" --- node3 node1 -- "B : 0.85" --- node4 node3 -- "C : 0.80" --- node4 node3 -- "D : 0.92" --- node2 node4 -- "E : 0.95" --- node2 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Source node `1` connects to nodes `3` and `4` via components `A` and `B`. Node `3` connects to `4` via bridge component `C`. Node `3` reaches the sink `2` via `D`; node `4` reaches the sink `2` via `E`. --- ## 4.2 Tie-Set (Path) Identification Map ```mermaid graph TD ts1["T1: A then D
2 components"] ts2["T2: B then E
2 components"] ts3["T3: A then C then E
3 components"] ts4["T4: B then C then D
3 components"] out["System Success
= T1 OR T2 OR T3 OR T4"] ts1 --> out ts2 --> out ts3 --> out ts4 --> out ``` **Reading the diagram:** The four minimal tie-sets are shown as parallel "OR" branches feeding into the system-success node, visually expressing the *union* formula $R_s = P(T_1 \cup T_2 \cup T_3 \cup T_4)$. --- ## 4.3 Cut-Set Identification Map ```mermaid graph TD cs1["C1: A and B fail
2 components"] cs2["C2: D and E fail
2 components"] cs3["C3: A and C and E fail
3 components"] cs4["C4: B and C and D fail
3 components"] out["System Failure
= C1 OR C2 OR C3 OR C4"] cs1 --> out cs2 --> out cs3 --> out cs4 --> out ``` **Reading the diagram:** The four minimal cut-sets are the *dual* paths; their union equals the system-failure event. The lower-order cuts ($C_1$, $C_2$) dominate the unreliability calculation. --- ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Reliability Analysis Process ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA["Step 1: Draw RBD
Identify Source and Sink"] stepB["Step 2: Enumerate
Minimal Tie-Sets"] stepC["Step 3: Verify
Minimality"] stepD["Step 4: Build Union
Expression R = P U Ti"] stepE["Step 5: Apply Inclusion
Exclusion Expansion"] stepF["Step 6: Truncate or
Use Bonferroni Bounds"] stepG["Step 7: Cross-Validate
Using Dual Cut-Sets"] stepH["Final Rs and Qs
Numerical Result"] stepA --> stepB stepB --> stepC stepC --> stepD stepD --> stepE stepE --> stepF stepF --> stepG stepG --> stepH ``` **Reading the architecture:** A reliability engineer follows this 7-step pipeline from graph drawing to numerical answer. The cross-validation loop (Step 7) ensures arithmetic and conceptual correctness. --- ## 4.5 Decision Tree — When to Choose Tie-Set vs. Cut-Set ```mermaid flowchart TD qStart{"System has
high or low
component R?"} qStart -- "All R_i close to 1
(high reliability)" --> cutPath["Use Cut-Set Method
Compute Qs, then Rs = 1 minus Qs"] qStart -- "Some R_i small
(low reliability)" --> tiePath["Use Tie-Set Method
Compute Rs directly via P U Ti"] qStart -- "Bridge network or
non-planar graph" --> dualPath["Compute Both
Use Duality for Cross-Check"] cutPath --> finalRes["Report Rs and Qs"] tiePath --> finalRes dualPath --> finalRes ``` **Reading the decision tree:** Pick the method that yields the **fastest convergence** of the inclusion–exclusion series. For high-reliability systems, cut-sets converge in 1–2 terms. For low-reliability systems, tie-sets converge in 1–2 terms. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 ESE):** Part A = 3 marks each (2 questions, answer briefly). Part B = 14 marks each (Module Internal Choice — answer either the full Question A *or* the full Question B). Each Part B has sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** Define a *Tie-Set* and a *Cut-Set* in a reliability network. State one key difference between them. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Tie-Set** (or Minimal Path) is a minimal set of components whose simultaneous *success* ensures system success. A **Cut-Set** (or Minimal Cut) is a minimal set of components whose simultaneous *failure* causes system failure. The key difference: a tie-set is a *success-side* construct defined by the *union* of paths, whereas a cut-set is a *failure-side* construct defined by the *union* of cuts. They are duals of each other in a planar network. *(Award 1 mark for Tie-Set definition, 1 mark for Cut-Set definition, 1 mark for the difference.)* --- ### Question 2 **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** State the Inclusion–Exclusion Principle for two events $A$ and $B$. Why is it used in the Tie-Set method? **Model Answer (3 marks):** For two events $A$ and $B$: $$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$ It is used in the Tie-Set method because the system reliability is $R_s = P(T_1 \cup T_2 \cup \cdots \cup T_n)$, the probability of the *union* of all minimal-path events. The union operation must be expanded into a sum of products of individual path reliabilities, and inclusion–exclusion is the only exact method that achieves this without any approximation. *(Award 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for naming the formula, 1 mark for justification of use in tie-set method.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]`** Consider a system with **4 components** in the configuration: components 1 and 2 are in series; this series combination is in parallel with component 3; and the entire arrangement is in series with component 4. The reliabilities are $R_1 = 0.95, R_2 = 0.90, R_3 = 0.92, R_4 = 0.98$. ### Part (a) — 7 Marks Enumerate all minimal Tie-Sets and all minimal Cut-Sets for the system. Verify the duality between them. ### Part (b) — 7 Marks Compute the system reliability $R_s$ using **(i)** the Tie-Set method and **(ii)** the Cut-Set method. Comment on the agreement between the two. ### Model Solution **Part (a) — Tie-Set and Cut-Set Enumeration:** The system structure is: Source $\xrightarrow{1,2 \text{ (series)}} X \xrightarrow{\text{parallel with } 3} Y \xrightarrow{4} \text{Sink}$, which is equivalent to Source $\xrightarrow{1 \text{ or } 3} \text{Mid} \xrightarrow{2} X \xrightarrow{\text{parallel with } 3} Y \xrightarrow{4} \text{Sink}$ ... Let us re-draw the RBD carefully. The RBD: from source, two parallel branches (Branch P: 1 then 2; Branch Q: 3 alone) rejoin, then component 4 leads to sink. The system succeeds if either Branch P (i.e., both 1 and 2 work) OR Branch Q (i.e., 3 works), AND then component 4 works. **Minimal Tie-Sets:** - $T_1 = \{1, 2, 4\}$ (Path: through 1, then 2, then 4) - $T_2 = \{3, 4\}$ (Path: through 3, then 4) Both are minimal (removing any component disconnects the path). ✓ **Minimal Cut-Sets (by duality / direct inspection):** - $C_1 = \{1, 3\}$ (Failure of both 1 and 3 disconnects source from the rest) - $C_2 = \{2, 3\}$ (Failure of both 2 and 3 disconnects the parallel leg) - $C_3 = \{4\}$ (Single point of failure at the output) **Duality Check:** Tie-sets $\{T_1, T_2\}$ vs. Cut-sets $\{C_1, C_2, C_3\}$. They are *not* one-to-one because the graph is non-planar in its drawn form; however, building the formal dual of the planar embedding yields the correspondence. (4 marks for tie-set enumeration, 2 marks for cut-set enumeration, 1 mark for duality comment.) --- **Part (b) — Reliability Calculation:** **(i) Tie-Set Method:** $$\begin{aligned} P(T_1) &= R_1 R_2 R_4 = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.98 = 0.83790 \\ P(T_2) &= R_3 R_4 = 0.92 \times 0.98 = 0.90160 \\ P(T_1 \cap T_2) &= R_1 R_2 R_3 R_4 = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.92 \times 0.98 = 0.77087 \end{aligned}$$ By inclusion–exclusion: $$R_s = P(T_1) + P(T_2) - P(T_1 \cap T_2) = 0.83790 + 0.90160 - 0.77087 = 0.96863$$ **(ii) Cut-Set Method:** $$\begin{aligned} P(C_1) &= Q_1 Q_3 = 0.05 \times 0.08 = 0.0040 \\ P(C_2) &= Q_2 Q_3 = 0.10 \times 0.08 = 0.0080 \\ P(C_3) &= Q_4 = 0.02 \end{aligned}$$ Two-cut intersections: $$\begin{aligned} P(C_1 \cap C_2) &= Q_1 Q_2 Q_3 = 0.05 \times 0.10 \times 0.08 = 0.00040 \\ P(C_1 \cap C_3) &= Q_1 Q_3 Q_4 = 0.05 \times 0.08 \times 0.02 = 0.00008 \\ P(C_2 \cap C_3) &= Q_2 Q_3 Q_4 = 0.10 \times 0.08 \times 0.02 = 0.00016 \end{aligned}$$ Three-cut intersection: $$P(C_1 \cap C_2 \cap C_3) = Q_1 Q_2 Q_3 Q_4 = 0.05 \times 0.10 \times 0.08 \times 0.02 = 0.0000080$$ Inclusion–exclusion for unreliability: $$\begin{aligned} Q_s &= [0.0040 + 0.0080 + 0.0200] - [0.00040 + 0.00008 + 0.00016] + [0.0000080] \\ &= 0.0320 - 0.00064 + 0.0000080 \\ &= 0.03137 \end{aligned}$$ $$R_s = 1 - Q_s = 1 - 0.03137 = 0.96863$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Comment:** Both methods give $R_s = 0.96863$ **exactly** because no truncation was performed. The two methods are mathematically equivalent and the agreement confirms correctness. --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]`** For the **5-component bridge network** described in Section 3.2 (with $R_A = 0.90, R_B = 0.85, R_C = 0.80, R_D = 0.92, R_E = 0.95$): ### Part (a) — 7 Marks List all four minimal Tie-Sets and all four minimal Cut-Sets. Apply the **Duality Principle** to confirm the correspondence. Use the **Tie-Set method** to compute the system reliability $R_s$. ### Part (b) — 7 Marks Use the **Cut-Set method** to compute the system unreliability $Q_s$ and hence $R_s$. Compare the computational effort and accuracy with the Tie-Set method. ### Model Solution **Part (a) — Tie-Set Method:** The four minimal tie-sets are $T_1 = \{A,D\}, T_2 = \{B,E\}, T_3 = \{A,C,E\}, T_4 = \{B,C,D\}$ (enumerated in Section 3.2 Step A). The four minimal cut-sets are $C_1 = \{A,B\}, C_2 = \{D,E\}, C_3 = \{A,C,E\}, C_4 = \{B,C,D\}$. **Duality check:** $T_3$ and $C_3$ have identical component sets, as do $T_4$ and $C_4$. The pairs $T_1 \leftrightarrow C_2$ and $T_2 \leftrightarrow C_1$ are the *dual* paths in the planar dual network (mesh ↔ node). The one-to-one correspondence holds. ✓ Using the values from Section 3.2 Step F: $\boxed{R_s = 0.9799}$. *(Full 7 marks: 2 for enumeration, 2 for duality, 3 for the final numerical answer with inclusion–exclusion arithmetic.)* --- **Part (b) — Cut-Set Method:** Using the values from Section 3.3 Step E (truncated at the 2-cut level): $$Q_s \approx 0.0224 - 0.000662 = 0.021738 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_s = 1 - 0.021738 = 0.9783$$ **Comparison with Tie-Set method:** | **Aspect** | **Tie-Set Method** | **Cut-Set Method** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Computational effort | 4 paths, requires 4+6+4+1 = 15 product terms | 4 cuts, truncated at 2-cut, only 4+6 = 10 product terms | | Convergence speed | Slow (5+ terms needed) | Fast (2 terms sufficient) | | Numerical accuracy at truncation | N/A (exact) | Lower bound on $Q_s$ → upper bound on $R_s$ | | Final $R_s$ | **0.9799** (exact) | **0.9783** (slight underestimate) | | Best suited when | Component reliabilities are *low* | Component reliabilities are *high* | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** In this example, $R_i \in [0.80, 0.95]$ — all moderately high. Hence the *cut-set* method converges faster and is preferred for production engineering analyses where the inclusion–exclusion series is bounded by a tolerance of $10^{-6}$. *(Full 7 marks: 2 for cut-set arithmetic, 2 for $R_s = 1 - Q_s$, 3 for the comparison table.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting minimality:** Students often list *any* path (not the *minimal* path). For example, $\{A, C, D, E\}$ is a path but **not minimal** because $\{A, D\} \subset \{A, C, D, E\}$. Listing non-minimal paths inflates the inclusion–exclusion terms and produces an *incorrect* upper bound. **Always verify minimality before writing.** > 2. **Sign errors in Inclusion–Exclusion:** The signs alternate $+, -, +, -, \ldots$. Forgetting the alternation or applying all positive signs is the most common arithmetic mistake. **Tip:** Count the number of events in the intersection; the sign is $(-1)^{k+1}$ for an intersection of $k$ events. > 3. **Using $R_i$ instead of $Q_i$ in the cut-set method:** A cut-set event is the *failure* of components, so the probability is $\prod (1 - R_k) = \prod Q_k$. Mixing $R$ and $Q$ in the cut-set product is a guaranteed mark-loss. > 4. **Forgetting the higher-order intersections in the cut-set method:** The first-order term $0.0224$ is an *upper bound* on $Q_s$. Forgetting to subtract the second-order terms will *overestimate* $Q_s$ and *underestimate* $R_s$. > 5. **Not showing the duality explicitly:** KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** specifically for the duality statement and verification. Always write *"By the Duality Principle, the cut-sets of $G$ are the tie-sets of the dual network $G^*$"* in your answer. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Tie-Set (Minimal Path):** Minimum set of components whose *success* ensures system success. Use Tie-Set method when component reliabilities are *low* (faster convergence). - [x] **Cut-Set (Minimal Cut):** Minimum set of components whose *failure* causes system failure. Use Cut-Set method when component reliabilities are *high* (faster convergence). - [x] **System Reliability via Tie-Sets:** $R_s = P(T_1 \cup T_2 \cup \cdots \cup T_n)$. Always expand using **Inclusion–Exclusion Principle**. - [x] **System Unreliability via Cut-Sets:** $Q_s = P(C_1 \cup C_2 \cup \cdots \cup C_n)$. Always expand using **Inclusion–Exclusion Principle**. - [x] **Duality:** Tie-Sets of $G$ $\iff$ Cut-Sets of $G^*$. Use this to cross-validate and to derive one from the other. - [x] **Component Independence Assumption:** Intersections factor as products: $P(T_i \cap T_j) = \prod_{k \in T_i \cup T_j} R_k$. *This is the KTU default unless explicitly stated otherwise.* - [x] **Alternating Sign Rule:** The $k$-fold intersection term carries sign $(-1)^{k+1}$ in inclusion–exclusion. - [x] **First-Order Bound:** $R_s \leq \sum P(T_i)$ and $Q_s \leq \sum P(C_i)$ — always overestimates, useful for rapid screening. - [x] **Truncation is Valid** for high-reliability components because product of unreliabilities shrinks rapidly. - [x] **The Bridge Network (5 components)** is the canonical KTU exam problem. Memorize its tie-sets $\{AD, BE, ACE, BCD\}$ and cut-sets $\{AB, DE, ACE, BCD\}$. - [x] **Always verify minimality** of each tie-set and cut-set before applying any formula. - [x] **De Morgan's Law** connects the two methods: $R_s = 1 - Q_s$ at every stage of the calculation. - [x] **Single-Point-of-Failure** = first-order cut-set ($c_1 = 1$). Its absence is a critical design goal in fault-tolerant systems.

topics.title: Design for Reliability: Load-Strength Interference and Safety Margin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design for Reliability: Load-Strength Interference and Safety Margin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Load-Strength Interference (LSI):** A probabilistic design philosophy in which both the **Load** (L) applied to a component and the **Strength** (S) possessed by the component are treated as **continuous random variables** with their own probability density functions (PDFs) — $f_L(\ell)$ and $f_S(s)$ respectively. The component fails whenever the instantaneous load exceeds the instantaneous strength, i.e., the event $\{L > S\}$ occurs in the **interference region** where the two PDFs overlap.

> [!NOTE]
> **Safety Margin (M):** The algebraic difference between Strength and Load,
> $$M = S - L$$
> The component is reliable if and only if $M > 0$. The reliability is therefore
> $$R = P(M > 0) = P(S > L)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Factor (n):** The classical (deterministic) ratio of nominal strength to nominal load, $n = S_{nom}/L_{nom}$. In modern probabilistic design it is replaced by the **Reliability Index ($\beta$)** which is the number of standard deviations by which the mean safety margin exceeds zero.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dam and River" Analogy

Imagine a dam holding back a river:

- **Load (L)** = the water level pressing against the dam wall (varies with rainfall — random).
- **Strength (S)** = the height of the dam wall (varies because of construction tolerance, ageing, erosion — random).
- **Failure** = the dam wall is overtopped (water level exceeds wall height).

If you **only** used the *deterministic* average water level (e.g., "100-year flood") and the *average* wall height, you would miss the fact that on a few unlucky days the water may surge (large L) while the wall has slightly degraded (small S). That rare simultaneous occurrence is the **interference region** — the only region where the dam actually fails. Reliability engineering forces us to compute the probability of landing inside that dangerous overlap.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for a KTU student:** *"L pushes, S resists; whenever L overtakes S, the part quits."*

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in This Module

| Metric | Symbol | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| Mean Load | $\mu_L$ or $\bar{L}$ | First moment of $f_L(\ell)$ |
| Mean Strength | $\mu_S$ or $\bar{S}$ | First moment of $f_S(s)$ |
| Standard deviation of Load | $\sigma_L$ | Spread of load |
| Standard deviation of Strength | $\sigma_S$ | Spread of strength |
| Reliability | $R$ | Probability of survival |
| Probability of Failure | $P_f = 1 - R$ | Probability of failure |
| Safety Margin | $M = S - L$ | Random variable |
| Reliability Index | $\beta$ | $= \mu_M / \sigma_M$ |

All of the above quantities are **dimensionless probabilities** except $\mu_L, \mu_S$ (same units as load, e.g., N, MPa, V) and $\sigma_L, \sigma_S$ (same units as load).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Why LSI Theory Exists

Classical (deterministic) design uses a single number for load and a single number for strength. This **ignores variability** and yields an answer that is either *unsafe* (variance underestimated) or *over-engineered* (variance overestimated). LSI theory improves on this by:

1. Treating every engineering quantity (load, material strength, dimensions, surface finish, temperature) as a **random variable** because of manufacturing tolerances, environmental fluctuations, and measurement uncertainty.
2. **Convolving** the two distributions to obtain the distribution of $M = S - L$ (or directly integrating the joint PDF).
3. Computing $R = P(S > L)$ from the area of the safe region in the L–S plane.

### 2.2 Fundamental Reliability Equation

The component survives whenever the point $(L,S)$ lies **above the line $S = L$** in the L–S plane. Assuming $L$ and $S$ are **statistically independent**, the joint PDF factorises:

$$f_{L,S}(\ell, s) = f_L(\ell)\cdot f_S(s)$$

The reliability is therefore the double integral over the *safe* half-plane:

$$R = P(S > L) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\int_{\ell}^{\infty} f_S(s)\,f_L(\ell)\,ds\,d\ell$$

Equivalently, integrating in the reverse order:

$$R = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\int_{-\infty}^{s} f_L(\ell)\,f_S(s)\,d\ell\,ds = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F_L(s)\,f_S(s)\,ds$$

> **Why two forms?** The second form ($F_L(s)\,f_S(s)$) is preferred numerically — it is a single integral that any spreadsheet or numerical integration tool can evaluate.

### 2.3 The Safety Margin Distribution

Since $M = S - L$, the **mean safety margin** and **standard deviation of safety margin** are the first two moments of this new random variable.

If $S$ and $L$ are **independent** (almost always assumed in introductory KTU problems):

$$\mu_M = \mu_S - \mu_L$$

$$\sigma_M^{2} = \sigma_S^{2} + \sigma_L^{2} \quad (\text{independent variables ADD variances, NOT standard deviations})$$

If $S$ and $L$ are **correlated** with correlation coefficient $\rho$:

$$\sigma_M^{2} = \sigma_S^{2} + \sigma_L^{2} - 2\rho\,\sigma_S\sigma_L$$

### 2.4 The Reliability Index ($\beta$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Reliability Index** is the number of sigma by which the mean safety margin sits above zero. For the Normal–Normal case:
> $$\beta = \dfrac{\mu_M}{\sigma_M} = \dfrac{\mu_S - \mu_L}{\sqrt{\sigma_S^{2} + \sigma_L^{2}}}$$
> and $R = \Phi(\beta)$, where $\Phi(\cdot)$ is the standard normal CDF.

The classical safety factor $n = \mu_S/\mu_L$ and the reliability index $\beta$ are **different quantities**:

| $n = \mu_S/\mu_L$ | $\beta$ | Approx. $R$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 0.0 | 0.500 |
| 1.5 | 3.0 | 0.9987 |
| 2.0 | 4.5 | 0.999997 |
| 3.0 | 6.0 | $\approx 1 - 10^{-9}$ |

> **Engineering Insight:** A safety factor of 2.0 does **not** automatically guarantee "high" reliability — the spread $\sigma_L, \sigma_S$ matters just as much as the ratio of the means.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reliability (general) | $R = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F_L(s)\,f_S(s)\,ds$ | Independent L, S |
| 2 | Probability of Failure | $P_f = 1 - R$ | — |
| 3 | Safety Margin | $M = S - L$ | Random variable |
| 4 | Mean of $M$ | $\mu_M = \mu_S - \mu_L$ | Linear combination |
| 5 | Variance of $M$ | $\sigma_M^{2} = \sigma_S^{2} + \sigma_L^{2}$ | Independent |
| 6 | Variance of $M$ (correlated) | $\sigma_M^{2} = \sigma_S^{2} + \sigma_L^{2} - 2\rho\sigma_S\sigma_L$ | $\rho \neq 0$ |
| 7 | Reliability Index | $\beta = \mu_M / \sigma_M$ | Normal L, S |
| 8 | Reliability (Normal L, S) | $R = \Phi(\beta)$ | Both Normal |
| 9 | Deterministic Safety Factor | $n = S_{nom}/L_{nom}$ | Deterministic |
| 10 | Required $\beta$ for target $R_0$ | $\beta_0 = \Phi^{-1}(R_0)$ | Inverting Normal CDF |

> ⚠️ **KTU Pitfall:** When two random variables are **subtracted**, the variances **add** (do not subtract). When two random variables are **added**, the variances still **add** if they are independent. Only the **mean** changes sign on subtraction.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Aerospace structural components** — fatigue load spectrum vs. material ultimate strength.
- **Pressure vessels** — internal pressure variations vs. burst-pressure distribution.
- **Electronic circuits** — supply-voltage variation vs. breakdown-voltage distribution.
- **Civil engineering bridges** — traffic load (vehicle weights) vs. girder capacity.
- **Rotating machinery shafts** — torque demand vs. torsional strength under varying material properties.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — General Reliability from Joint PDF

**Goal:** Show that the survival region in the L–S plane integrates to give $R = \int F_L(s)\,f_S(s)\,ds$.

**Step 1.** The event of survival is $\{S > L\}$. In the $(L,S)$ plane this is the region *above* the 45° line $S = L$.

**Step 2.** The probability of any event in 2-D is the integral of the joint PDF over that region:

$$R = P(S>L) = \iint_{s>\ell} f_{L,S}(\ell,s)\,d\ell\,ds$$

**Step 3.** Assuming statistical independence, $f_{L,S}(\ell,s) = f_L(\ell)\cdot f_S(s)$.

$$R = \iint_{s>\ell} f_L(\ell)\,f_S(s)\,d\ell\,ds$$

**Step 4.** Convert the double integral into iterated integrals. Integrate over $L$ first (for a fixed $S = s$, $L$ ranges from $-\infty$ to $s$):

$$R = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!f_S(s)\left[\int_{-\infty}^{s} f_L(\ell)\,d\ell\right]ds$$

**Step 5.** The inner integral is by definition the **CDF** of $L$ evaluated at $s$:

$$F_L(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{s} f_L(\ell)\,d\ell$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,R = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F_L(s)\,f_S(s)\,ds\,}$$

> **Interpretation:** $f_S(s)\,ds$ is the probability that the strength lies in the tiny strip $ds$ around $s$; $F_L(s)$ is the conditional probability that the load is less than that strength. Multiplying and adding over all $s$ gives the total survival probability.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Safety-Margin Statistics (Independent Case)

**Goal:** Find $\mu_M$ and $\sigma_M^{2}$ when $M = S - L$, with $S$ and $L$ independent.

**Step 1.** Use linearity of expectation for the mean:

$$\mu_M = E[S - L] = E[S] - E[L] = \mu_S - \mu_L$$

**Step 2.** Recall that for any two random variables $X, Y$:
