This translates economically to the **Marginal Equivalence Principle**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marginal Cost = Marginal Benefit Principle:**
> At $R^*$, the *marginal cost* of producing one more unit of reliability equals the *marginal savings* from preventing one more failure.

Mathematically:

$$\frac{dC_R}{dR}\bigg|_{R=R^*} = -\frac{dC_U}{dR}\bigg|_{R=R^*}$$

### 2.4 Common Cost Models Used in Reliability Economics

Engineers use empirical cost models to make this analysis tractable.

**Model 1 — Exponential Investment, Linear Failure Cost:**

$$C_R(R) = a \left[ \exp(bR) - 1 \right]$$

$$C_U(R) = c \left(1 - R\right)$$

where $a, b, c$ are empirical constants determined from historical data.

**Model 2 — Reciprocal Investment Cost (Kapur & Lamberson form):**

$$C_R(R) = \frac{A}{1 - R}$$

This expresses the intuition that achieving $R = 0.99$ costs much more than $R = 0.90$.

**Model 3 — Power Law Failure Cost:**

$$C_U(R) = B (1 - R)^n$$

where $n \ge 1$ is a sensitivity exponent.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $C_T(R) = C_R(R) + C_U(R)$ | Fundamental total cost identity |
| 2 | $\dfrac{dC_T}{dR} = \dfrac{dC_R}{dR} + \dfrac{dC_U}{dR} = 0$ | Optimum condition (first order) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{d^2C_T}{dR^2}\big\vert_{R^*} > 0$ | Second-order condition (true minimum) |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dC_R}{dR} = -\dfrac{dC_U}{dR}$ | Marginal equivalence principle |
| 5 | $C_R(R) = a\left[\exp(bR) - 1\right]$ | Exponential investment cost model |
| 6 | $C_R(R) = \dfrac{A}{1-R}$ | Reciprocal / Kapur cost model |
| 7 | $C_U(R) = c(1-R)$ | Linear unreliability cost |
| 8 | $C_U(R) = B(1-R)^n$ | Power-law unreliability cost |
| 9 | $C_T(R) = a\left[\exp(bR) - 1\right] + c(1-R)$ | Combined model (Model 1+7) |
| 10 | $R^* = 1 - \dfrac{\sqrt{ac}}{b}$ | Optimum for Model 9 (special case) |
| 11 | $\lambda_{opt} = \dfrac{c}{b}\exp(bR^*)$ | Optimal failure rate relation |
| 12 | $N_f = N(1-R)$ | Expected failures per period for $N$ units |
| 13 | $C_U^{total} = c \cdot N_f$ | Total failure cost over a period |
| 14 | $PV = \dfrac{C}{(1+i)^t}$ | Present value for life-cycle cost analysis |
| 15 | $LCC = C_{acq} + C_{op} + C_{maint} + C_{down} - C_{salvage}$ | Life-Cycle Cost (LCC) identity |

> [!NOTE]
> **All absolute-value symbols** in tables have been replaced with `\vert` to comply with markdown safety (e.g., $\dfrac{d^2C_T}{dR^2}\big\vert_{R^*}$).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** NASA trades off redundancy cost vs mission-failure cost (loss of spacecraft = ~₹8,000 crore).
- **Nuclear Power Plants:** Reliability investment is justified because failure cost = human life + massive liability.
- **Consumer Electronics (TV, smartphones):** Manufacturers target ~R = 0.99 over 2 years because warranty costs dominate beyond that.
- **Automotive:** ISO 26262 / ASIL ratings use reliability-cost trade-offs to assign safety levels.
- **Medical Devices (Pacemakers):** Reliability investment is virtually unlimited because failure cost = death.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimum Reliability for the Combined Exponential–Linear Model

**Given:**

$$C_R(R) = a\left[\exp(bR) - 1\right], \quad C_U(R) = c(1-R)$$

$$C_T(R) = a\left[\exp(bR) - 1\right] + c(1-R)$$

**Step 1 — Write the total cost function explicitly.**

$$C_T(R) = a\exp(bR) - a + c - cR$$

**Step 2 — Apply the first-order optimality condition $\dfrac{dC_T}{dR} = 0$.**

$$\frac{dC_T}{dR} = a \cdot b \cdot \exp(bR) - c = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\exp(bR)$.**

$$a b \exp(bR) = c \implies \exp(bR) = \frac{c}{ab}$$

**Step 4 — Take natural logarithm to isolate $R$.**

$$\ln\left[\exp(bR)\right] = \ln\left(\frac{c}{ab}\right)$$

$$bR = \ln\left(\frac{c}{ab}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the optimum reliability $R^*$.**

$$R^* = \frac{1}{b}\ln\left(\frac{c}{ab}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Verify the second-order condition (true minimum).**

$$\frac{d^2C_T}{dR^2} = ab^2 \exp(bR) \big\vert_{R=R^*} = ab^2 \cdot \frac{c}{ab} = bc > 0$$

Since $b, c > 0$, this is positive. Therefore $R^*$ is a **minimum**. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:**
> $$R^* = \dfrac{1}{b}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{c}{ab}\right)$$
> This is the closed-form optimum for the exponential–linear combined cost model. It is a frequent **14-mark derivation** in KTU ESE.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimum Reliability for the Reciprocal Cost Model

**Given:**

$$C_R(R) = \frac{A}{1-R}, \quad C_U(R) = B(1-R)$$

**Step 1 — Form total cost.**

$$C_T(R) = \frac{A}{1-R} + B(1-R)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $R$.**

$$\frac{dC_T}{dR} = \frac{A}{(1-R)^2} - B$$

**Step 3 — Set equal to zero.**

$$\frac{A}{(1-R)^2} = B \implies (1-R)^2 = \frac{A}{B}$$

**Step 4 — Take square root.**

$$1 - R = \sqrt{\frac{A}{B}} \quad (\text{taking positive root since } R < 1)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $R^*$.**

$$R^* = 1 - \sqrt{\frac{A}{B}}$$

**Step 6 — Check second derivative.**

$$\frac{d^2C_T}{dR^2} = \frac{2A}{(1-R)^3}\bigg\vert_{R^*} > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** As $B \to A$, $R^* \to 0$. As $B \gg A$, $R^* \to 1$. This is consistent: high failure cost justifies high reliability.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A manufacturer estimates that the cost of reliability investment follows $C_R(R) = 1000\left[\exp(2R) - 1\right]$ ₹ per unit, and the cost of unreliability is $C_U(R) = 5000(1 - R)$ ₹ per unit. Find the optimum reliability and the minimum total cost.

**Solution:**

Using the derived formula with $a = 1000$, $b = 2$, $c = 5000$:

$$R^* = \frac{1}{2}\ln\left(\frac{5000}{1000 \times 2}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\ln(2.5)$$

$$R^* = \frac{1}{2}(0.9163) = 0.4581$$

Substituting back:

$$C_T(0.4581) = 1000\left[\exp(0.9163) - 1\right] + 5000(1 - 0.4581)$$

$$C_T = 1000\left[2.500 - 1\right] + 5000(0.5419)$$

$$C_T = 1500 + 2709.5 = 4209.5 \text{ ₹}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** Always check the *feasibility* of $R^*$. If $R^* < 0$ or $R^* > 1$, the optimum lies at the boundary (i.e., $R^* = 0$ or $R^* = 1$), and the standard calculus method fails.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cost Behaviour Summary

| Reliability Regime | $C_R$ behaviour | $C_U$ behaviour | $C_T$ behaviour | Decision |
|-------------------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|----------|
| $R < R^*$ | Rising slowly | Falling fast | Falling | Invest more in reliability |
| $R = R^*$ | Rising | Falling | **Minimum** | **STOP — optimum** |
| $R > R^*$ | Rising exponentially | Almost zero | Rising | Stop spending |
| $R \to 1$ | $C_R \to \infty$ | $C_U \to 0$ | $C_T \to \infty$ | Impractical |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Reliability Cost Trade-off Curve

The following Mermaid block renders the classic *bathtub-shaped total cost curve* that is mandatory for KTU Module 3 answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Cost Behaviour Domain"]
        direction TB
        A["Low Reliability Zone
        R = 0.0 to 0.4
        Cr = LOW
        Cu = HIGH
        Ct = HIGH"]
    end

    subgraph S2["Optimal Zone"]
        direction TB
        B["R STAR equals 0.7 to 0.9
        Cr equals Cu
        Ct equals MINIMUM
        DECISION: STOP INVESTING"]
    end

    subgraph S3["Over-engineering Zone"]
        direction TB
        C["High Reliability Zone
        R = 0.95 to 1.0
        Cr = VERY HIGH
        Cu = near zero
        Ct = HIGH again"]
    end

    A --> B
    B --> C
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Reliability Cost Optimization Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input1["Define System Function
    and Customer Requirements"]
    input2["Collect Failure Data
    Historical Failure Rates"]
    input3["Estimate Cost Coefficients
    a, b, c, A, B constants"]

    core1["Build Cost Model
    Cr of R and Cu of R"]
    core2["Form Total Cost
    Ct equals Cr plus Cu"]
    core3["Apply Optimality
    dCt over dR equals zero"]

    decision{"Is R star
    between 0 and 1"}

    out1["Optimal Reliability
    R star identified"]
    out2["Boundary optimum
    R star equals 0 or 1"]
    feedback["Iterate with New
    Design Parameters"]

    input1 --> core1
    input2 --> core3
    input3 --> core1
    core1 --> core2
    core2 --> core3
    core3 --> decision
    decision -- Yes --> out1
    decision -- No --> out2
    out1 --> feedback
    feedback --> core1
    out2 --> feedback
```

### 4.3 Cost Component Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LCC["Life Cycle Cost LCC"]

    LCC --> INV["Investment Cost Cr"]
    LCC --> FAIL["Failure Cost Cu"]

    INV --> D1["Design and Development"]
    INV --> D2["Component Upgradation"]
    INV --> D3["Redundancy Addition"]
    INV --> D4["Preventive Maintenance"]
    INV --> D5["Quality Testing and Burn in"]

    FAIL --> F1["Repair and Warranty"]
    FAIL --> F2["Production Downtime Loss"]
    FAIL --> F3["Spare Inventory Cost"]
    FAIL --> F4["Liability and Legal Cost"]
    FAIL --> F5["Customer Goodwill Loss"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid not a hand-drawn graph?**
> Board examiners reward well-structured *flow logic* for the cost optimization process. A clean block diagram of the *inputs → model → optimum → feedback* loop scores full marks in KTU 2024 valuation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "Cost of Unreliability". List any four components that contribute to it.**

**Model Answer:**

The *Cost of Unreliability* ($C_U$) is the total expenditure incurred by an organization due to system or component failures over the product's life cycle. It is a *decreasing* function of reliability $R$: as $R$ improves, $C_U$ falls.

Four major components:

1. **Warranty and repair costs** — paid to customers for failed units.
2. **Downtime losses** — production or service revenue lost during outages.
3. **Spare parts inventory cost** — capital tied up in buffer stock.
4. **Liability and legal costs** — compensation for accidents or data loss.

A fifth common item is *loss of brand reputation / customer goodwill*.

*(Examiner's note: a clean definition + 4 bullets fetches full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State and explain the marginal equivalence principle used in reliability optimization.**

**Model Answer:**

The *Marginal Equivalence Principle* states that the **optimum reliability level $R^*$** is reached when the **marginal cost** of producing one more unit of reliability equals the **marginal savings** obtained from preventing one additional failure.

Mathematically:

$$\frac{dC_R}{dR}\bigg\vert_{R^*} = -\frac{dC_U}{dR}\bigg\vert_{R^*}$$

**Physical meaning:**
- Left side = extra ₹ spent on design, redundancy, or testing to lift $R$ slightly.
- Right side = extra ₹ saved by avoiding one more warranty/downtime event.
- When these are equal, total cost $C_T = C_R + C_U$ is **minimised**.

Beyond $R^*$, the left side exceeds the right side — additional investment is **uneconomical**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for optimum reliability $R^*$ when the cost of reliability is $C_R(R) = a\left[\exp(bR) - 1\right]$ and the cost of unreliability is $C_U(R) = c(1-R)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a particular system, $a = 2000$, $b = 1.5$, and $c = 8000$. Compute the optimum reliability and the minimum total cost per unit. Also comment on whether the result is feasible. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Total cost:

$$C_T(R) = a\left[\exp(bR) - 1\right] + c(1-R)$$

First-order optimality:

$$\frac{dC_T}{dR} = ab\exp(bR) - c = 0$$

$$\exp(bR) = \frac{c}{ab} \implies bR = \ln\!\left(\frac{c}{ab}\right)$$

$$\boxed{R^* = \frac{1}{b}\ln\!\left(\frac{c}{ab}\right)}$$

Second derivative:

$$\frac{d^2C_T}{dR^2} = ab^2\exp(bR) = bc > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

Therefore $R^*$ is a **true minimum**.

**Part (b):**

Substitute $a = 2000$, $b = 1.5$, $c = 8000$:

$$R^* = \frac{1}{1.5}\ln\!\left(\frac{8000}{2000 \times 1.5}\right) = \frac{1}{1.5}\ln(2.6667)$$

$$R^* = \frac{0.9808}{1.5} = 0.6539$$

**Minimum total cost:**

$$C_T(0.6539) = 2000\left[\exp(1.5 \times 0.6539) - 1\right] + 8000(1 - 0.6539)$$

$$C_T = 2000\left[\exp(0.9808) - 1\right] + 8000(0.3461)$$

$$C_T = 2000(2.6667 - 1) + 2768.8 = 3333.4 + 2768.8$$

$$\boxed{C_T^{min} = 6102.2 \text{ ₹ per unit}}$$

**Feasibility:** Since $0 < R^* = 0.6539 < 1$, the result is **feasible** and represents a practical design target. Reliability improvement beyond 0.6539 would cost more than it saves.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the cost-reliability trade-off with a neatly labelled sketch of the total cost curve. Describe the three distinct regions of the curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A consumer electronics firm models its costs as:
$$C_R(R) = \frac{800}{1-R} \quad \text{and} \quad C_U(R) = 200(1-R)$$
Determine the optimum reliability and the corresponding minimum total cost. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

The cost-reliability trade-off arises because:

- $C_R$ (investment in reliability) is a **monotonically increasing** function of $R$, often rising steeply as $R \to 1$.
- $C_U$ (failure consequences) is a **monotonically decreasing** function of $R$.
- The sum $C_T = C_R + C_U$ has a **U-shape (bathtub-like)** with a unique minimum.

**Three Regions:**

| Region | Description | Engineering Decision |
|--------|-------------|----------------------|
| **I — Under-engineering** ($R < R^*$) | $C_U$ dominates; failures are frequent. | Invest more in reliability. |
| **II — Optimum** ($R = R^*$) | $C_R = C_U$; $C_T$ is minimum. | **STOP** — ideal target. |
| **III — Over-engineering** ($R > R^*$) | $C_R$ rises sharply; $C_U$ is negligible. | Stop spending; the system is over-engineered. |

A clean sketch should show:
- $C_R$ curve rising exponentially.
- $C_U$ curve falling linearly / exponentially.
- $C_T$ curve with a clear minimum labelled $R^*$.
- Intersection of $C_R$ and $C_U$ slightly to the left of $R^*$.

**Part (b):**

$$C_T(R) = \frac{800}{1-R} + 200(1-R)$$

Differentiate:

$$\frac{dC_T}{dR} = \frac{800}{(1-R)^2} - 200 = 0$$

$$\frac{800}{(1-R)^2} = 200 \implies (1-R)^2 = 4$$

$$1 - R = 2 \implies R^* = -1$$

**Wait — re-checking:**
Taking the positive root: $1 - R = 2 \Rightarrow R = -1$, which is infeasible. Taking the negative root: $1 - R = -2 \Rightarrow R = 3$, also infeasible.

This means the derivative has **no real root inside $(0,1)$**. We must therefore check the **boundary**:

- At $R = 0$: $C_T = 800 + 200 = 1000$ ₹
- At $R = 1$: $C_T \to \infty$

So the **minimum total cost occurs at $R = 0$** with $C_T = 1000$ ₹ — implying the firm should **not invest in reliability** under this cost structure. This is the *boundary optimum* case.

**Corrected analytical insight:**
This happens when $A > B$, i.e., $800 > 200$. Under such economics, reliability investment is uneconomical.

**Engineered fix:** If the firm insists on $R = 0.9$, then $C_T = 800/0.1 + 200(0.1) = 8000 + 20 = 8020$ ₹ — far higher. So either:
- The unreliability cost coefficient $B$ must be raised (e.g., $B = 8000$), or
- The investment coefficient $A$ must be reduced.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the second derivative check.** Many students find $R^*$ but do not confirm it is a *minimum*. Always verify $\frac{d^2C_T}{dR^2} > 0$.
> 2. **Ignoring boundary cases.** If $R^*$ falls outside $(0, 1)$, the optimum lies at $R = 0$ or $R = 1$. Students often force an invalid answer.
> 3. **Mixing up $C_R$ and $C_U$.** Always label which curve is which on the diagram; symmetry of the U-shape often confuses students.
> 4. **Not commenting on feasibility.** Even if you compute $R^* = 0.85$, you must state whether it is *practically achievable* given engineering constraints.
> 5. **Skipping units.** ₹/unit, hours, or years — every numerical answer must carry units.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core identity:** $C_T(R) = C_R(R) + C_U(R)$. Memorize this — it is the seed of every problem.
- **Optimum condition:** $\frac{dC_T}{dR} = 0$ AND $\frac{d^2C_T}{dR^2} > 0$.
- **Marginal principle:** $\frac{dC_R}{dR} = -\frac{dC_U}{dR}$ at $R^*$.
- **Standard exponential–linear model optimum:** $R^* = \dfrac{1}{b}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{c}{ab}\right)$.
- **Standard reciprocal model optimum:** $R^* = 1 - \sqrt{\dfrac{A}{B}}$ — only valid when $B > A$.
- **Boundary cases:** When $R^* < 0$ or $R^* > 1$, the optimum is at $R = 0$ or $R = 1$.
- **Curve shape:** $C_R$ rises (often exponentially), $C_U$ falls, $C_T$ has a U-shape.
- **Always check feasibility** and **always state units**.
- **Real-world link:** Aerospace → high $R^*$; Consumer electronics → moderate $R^*$; Low-criticality items → may justify $R^* = 0$.
- **Life-Cycle Cost (LCC)** = Acquisition + Operation + Maintenance + Downtime − Salvage value.
- **KTU 2024 weightage:** This topic typically carries **one 14-mark question (with sub-parts)** plus a 3-mark short answer in the End Semester Examination.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definitions and Basic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Definitions and Basic Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Reliability Engineering**, a **System** is defined as a collection of components, sub-systems, or assemblies that are physically and/or functionally interconnected to perform a specific operational task or mission under stipulated environmental conditions. **System Reliability**, therefore, is the probability that a given system, operating under defined environmental and operational conditions, will adequately perform its intended function for a specified period of time.

Mathematically, for a system $S$ composed of $n$ components, the system reliability $R_S(t)$ at time $t$ is expressed as:

$$R_S(t) = P[T_S > t]$$

where $T_S$ is the random variable representing the time-to-failure of the system $S$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:**
> The phrase *"adequately perform its intended function"* is the cornerstone of system reliability. A system is considered to have **failed** the moment it can no longer meet the minimum acceptable performance criteria, even if some of its individual components are still functioning.

## 1.2 Core Terminology (Syllabus Anchored)

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Component Reliability $R_i(t)$** | Probability that component $i$ performs its function for time $t$. |
| **System Failure** | An event where the system is unable to perform its intended function. |
| **Reliability Block Diagram (RBD)** | A graphical representation showing the logical connectivity of components required for system success. |
| **Mission Time $t$** | The total duration for which the system is expected to operate without failure. |
| **Success Path** | A set of components whose collective operation ensures system success. |
| **Failure Path (Cut)** | A set of components whose collective failure causes system failure. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Office Wi-Fi Network**
>
> Imagine your office Wi-Fi network. The internet reaches your laptop through a chain: **Internet Line → Router → Switch → Your Laptop**. If *any one* of these breaks, you lose connectivity. This is a **Series System** — every component must work.
>
> Now, suppose the office installs a **backup internet line** that activates only when the primary fails. The system survives as long as *at least one* of the two lines works. This is a **Parallel (Redundant) System**.
>
> Reliability engineering is essentially the mathematics of such "chains" and "backups" — figuring out the probability that your overall system works given that each part has its own probability of failure.

## 1.4 System Effectiveness Hierarchy

The quality of a system is captured by a 3-tier hierarchy:

1. **Component Reliability** — the basic building block.
2. **System Reliability** — the integrated performance of the whole.
3. **System Effectiveness** — the combination of reliability, availability, and performance capability.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked for "system reliability," always clarify whether the system is **series, parallel, or mixed**. The single word "system" is incomplete without specifying its configuration.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The theoretical foundation of system reliability rests on **Boolean modeling** of component states. Each component is treated as a **two-state device**: working (1) or failed (0).

**Step 1 — State Space Definition:**
For $n$ components, the total number of possible system states is $2^n$. Each state is a binary vector representing the operational status of all components.

**Step 2 — Structure Function $\phi(\mathbf{X})$:**
A structure function $\phi(\mathbf{X})$ maps the component state vector $\mathbf{X} = (X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$ to a system state.

$$\phi(\mathbf{X}) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if system functions (success)} \\ 0 & \text{if system fails} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Monotonicity Property:**
A *coherent system* is one where:
- $\phi(1, 1, \ldots, 1) = 1$ (all working → system works)
- $\phi(0, 0, \ldots, 0) = 0$ (all failed → system fails)
- Replacing any failed component with a working one cannot worsen system state.

**Step 4 — Independence Assumption:**
Component failures are **statistically independent** unless stated otherwise. This is the standard KTU assumption.

**Step 5 — Reliability Computation:**
Using the structure function and the probability that each $X_i = 1$ (i.e., $R_i$), compute:

$$R_S = E[\phi(\mathbf{X})] = \sum_{\mathbf{x}} \phi(\mathbf{x}) \cdot P(\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{x})$$

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters

- **Step 1** establishes the *enumerable* set of possibilities.
- **Step 2** abstracts the system logic into a tractable mathematical form.
- **Step 3** rules out pathological systems (e.g., those that self-heal — not considered in KTU problems).
- **Step 4** allows multiplicative probability calculations; without it, we would need joint distributions.
- **Step 5** is the central formula that ties everything to measurable quantities.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Configuration | Reliability Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Series System (n components)** | $R_S = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | All components must work. |
| **Parallel System (n components)** | $R_S = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | At least one must work. |
| **Two-component Parallel** | $R_S = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$ | Special case of above. |
| **Series-Parallel Mixed** | Combine using above iteratively | Use block reduction. |
| **k-out-of-n System** | $R_S = \sum_{j=k}^{n} \binom{n}{j} R^j (1-R)^{n-j}$ | At least $k$ of $n$ must work. |
| **System Failure Probability** | $F_S = 1 - R_S$ | Complement of reliability. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exam answers, **never use** the vertical bar $|$ for absolute value inside a markdown table. Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to prevent rendering errors. Example: Use $P(A \mid B)$ not $P(A | B)$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Aircraft control systems use parallel redundancy on critical surfaces (e.g., triple-redundant flight computers).
- **Power Engineering:** The national grid uses parallel transmission paths so that loss of one line does not blackout cities.
- **Manufacturing:** A production line is a series system; bottleneck machines require parallel backup.
- **Data Centers:** RAID storage configurations are classic k-out-of-n systems (e.g., RAID 5 tolerates 1 of 4 disk failures).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Series System Reliability

**Given:** $n$ components connected in series, each with reliability $R_i(t)$, all independent.

**To Prove:** $R_S(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$

### Derivation Walkthrough

**Statement 1:** The system succeeds if and only if *all* components succeed. By definition of series configuration:

$$\{S \text{ works}\} \iff \bigcap_{i=1}^{n} \{C_i \text{ works}\}$$

**Statement 2:** Apply the probability operator to both sides:

$$R_S = P[S \text{ works}] = P\left[\bigcap_{i=1}^{n} \{C_i \text{ works}\}\right]$$

**Statement 3:** Apply the **multiplication rule for independent events**. Since components are independent:

$$P\left[\bigcap_{i=1}^{n} A_i\right] = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i)$$

**Statement 4:** Substitute the component reliabilities:

$$\therefore \quad R_S = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P[C_i \text{ works}] = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

$$\blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Parallel System Reliability

**Given:** $n$ components in parallel, each with reliability $R_i$, all independent.

**To Prove:** $R_S = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$

### Derivation Walkthrough

**Statement 1:** A parallel system fails **only if all components fail**. So:

$$F_S = P[S \text{ fails}] = P\left[\bigcap_{i=1}^{n} \{C_i \text{ fails}\}\right]$$

**Statement 2:** The probability of component $C_i$ failing is $(1 - R_i)$. By independence:

$$F_S = \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$

**Statement 3:** Using the complement rule, $R_S = 1 - F_S$:

$$R_S = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$

$$\blacksquare$$

**Verification for $n = 2$:**
$R_S = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2) = 1 - 1 + R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2 = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$. ✓

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: k-out-of-n System

**Given:** Identical components, each with reliability $R$. System works if at least $k$ of $n$ work.

**To Prove:** $R_S = \sum_{j=k}^{n} \binom{n}{j} R^j (1-R)^{n-j}$

### Derivation Walkthrough

**Statement 1:** Let $X$ = number of working components. $X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, R)$.

**Statement 2:** System works iff $X \geq k$:

$$R_S = P[X \geq k]$$

**Statement 3:** The PMF of a Binomial is $P(X = j) = \binom{n}{j} R^j (1-R)^{n-j}$.

**Statement 4:** Sum the PMF from $j = k$ to $j = n$:

$$R_S = \sum_{j=k}^{n} \binom{n}{j} R^j (1-R)^{n-j}$$

$$\blacksquare$$

**Special Case Check:** For $k = 1$ (parallel), the sum becomes $\sum_{j=1}^{n} \binom{n}{j} R^j (1-R)^{n-j} = 1 - (1-R)^n$ (using binomial theorem). ✓ Matches the parallel formula.

**Special Case Check:** For $k = n$ (series), only the $j = n$ term survives, giving $R^n$. ✓ Matches the series formula.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Series | Parallel | k-out-of-n |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimum working components | $n$ | $1$ | $k$ |
| Failure condition | Any one fails | All fail | Fewer than $k$ work |
| $R_S$ vs component $R$ | $R_S \leq \min(R_i)$ | $R_S \geq \max(R_i)$ | In between |
| Effect of adding a component | Decreases $R_S$ | Increases $R_S$ | Depends on $k$ |
| Best suited for | Safety-critical chains | Mission-critical backups | Voter/redundant setups |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram: System Reliability Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Component C1] --> nodeB[Component C2]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Component C3]
    nodeC --> nodeD[System Output Success]

    nodeE[Series Reliability] --- nodeF[Product of R1 R2 R3]
    nodeG[Parallel Reliability] --- nodeH[1 minus Product of 1 minus Ri]
    nodeI[k out of n] --- nodeJ[Binomial Sum from j=k to n]

    nodeF --> nodeK[Lower Bound on R System]
    nodeH --> nodeL[Upper Bound on R System]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Intermediate R System]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Series-Parallel Decomposition

```mermaid
graph LR
    blockA[Input Source A] --> blockB[Parallel Block P1]
    blockC[Input Source B] --> blockB
    blockB --> blockD[Series Component S1]
    blockD --> blockE[Series Component S2]
    blockE --> blockF[Output]
    blockG[Block Subsystem K out of N] --- blockH[Redundant Array Voting]
    blockH --> blockF
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[System Design Question] --> q1{All components essential?}
    q1 -- Yes --> series[Use Series Configuration]
    q1 -- No --> q2{All identical with redundancy?}
    q2 -- Yes --> koutn[Use k out of n Configuration]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Need backup paths?}
    q3 -- Yes --> parallel[Use Parallel Configuration]
    q3 -- No --> mixed[Use Mixed Series Parallel]
```

## 4.4 Diagram Caption Notes

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The **first flowchart** shows the logical deduction path: components → configuration formula → system reliability.
> - The **second diagram** illustrates how subsystems (parallel, series, k-out-of-n) combine to form a complete system architecture.
> - The **decision tree** is a quick reference for choosing the correct configuration during exam problem-solving.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "Reliability Block Diagram" (RBD). How is it different from a physical schematic diagram?**

**Model Answer:**
A Reliability Block Diagram (RBD) is a logical representation of how components in a system must function to achieve system success. The blocks represent *functional* components, not physical parts, and are connected in series or parallel based on reliability logic.
- **Physical schematic** shows *how components are connected* (wires, pipes, etc.).
- **RBD** shows *how component functions combine* to produce system success.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse an RBD with a circuit diagram. Marks are lost when the answer treats RBD as a circuit schematic instead of a *functional* model.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the monotonicity property of a coherent system structure function.**

**Model Answer:**
A coherent system structure function $\phi(\mathbf{X})$ is **monotonic non-decreasing**, meaning:
- If component $i$ fails ($X_i = 0$) and the system was working, replacing $i$ with a working version cannot cause the system to fail.
- Mathematically: $X_i \leq Y_i$ for all $i$ implies $\phi(\mathbf{X}) \leq \phi(\mathbf{Y})$.
- This ensures reliability improves (or stays same) when any component is upgraded.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**A system consists of 3 components in series, with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.92$, $R_3 = 0.90$. To improve reliability, an identical backup is added to $C_2$ in parallel. Compute:**
**(a)** The reliability of the original series system. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The reliability after adding the parallel backup for $C_2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** For a series system of 3 components:

$$R_{S,\text{original}} = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3$$

$$R_{S,\text{original}} = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.90$$

Step-by-step multiplication:
$$0.95 \times 0.92 = 0.8740$$
$$0.8740 \times 0.90 = 0.7866$$

$$\boxed{R_{S,\text{original}} = 0.7866}$$

**Part (b):** The parallel backup makes the new $C_2$ block a 2-out-of-2 (parallel) system:

$$R_{2,\text{new}} = 1 - (1 - R_2)(1 - R_2) = 1 - (1 - 0.92)^2$$

$$(1 - 0.92)^2 = (0.08)^2 = 0.0064$$

$$R_{2,\text{new}} = 1 - 0.0064 = 0.9936$$

Now treating the system as a series connection of $C_1$, $C_2^{\text{new}}$, and $C_3$:

$$R_{S,\text{new}} = R_1 \times R_{2,\text{new}} \times R_3$$

$$R_{S,\text{new}} = 0.95 \times 0.9936 \times 0.90$$

$$0.95 \times 0.9936 = 0.94392$$

$$0.94392 \times 0.90 = 0.849528$$

$$\boxed{R_{S,\text{new}} = 0.8495 \text{ (approximately)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (7-mark sub-parts):**
> 1. Students often forget to **square** $(1 - R_2)$ for the parallel formula — only one $(1 - R_2)$ term instead of two. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. Students may incorrectly treat the parallel backup as *in series* with $C_2$ instead of replacing it. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. Not showing intermediate steps (just writing the final answer) is penalized. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. Units: $R_S$ is dimensionless — but you must show the formula before substituting numerical values. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**A computer server uses 4 identical hard drives arranged in a RAID-like configuration such that the system works if at least 3 of the 4 drives function. Each drive has a reliability of 0.98. Determine:**
**(a)** Whether the system is best modeled as a series, parallel, or k-out-of-n system. Justify. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compute the system reliability using the appropriate formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** The system requires *at least 3* of the 4 drives to work. By definition:
- It is **not a series system** (because not *all 4* need to work — one can fail).
- It is **not a simple parallel system** (because not just *1* needs to work — at least 3 are required).
- It is a **k-out-of-n system with $k = 3$, $n = 4$**.

This is also called a **3-out-of-4 redundant system**, commonly used in fault-tolerant storage and aerospace voting systems.

**Part (b):** Apply the k-out-of-n formula with $k = 3$, $n = 4$, $R = 0.98$:

$$R_S = \sum_{j=3}^{4} \binom{4}{j} R^j (1-R)^{4-j}$$

Expand the sum:

$$R_S = \binom{4}{3} R^3 (1-R)^{1} + \binom{4}{4} R^4 (1-R)^{0}$$

**Term 1:** $j = 3$

$$\binom{4}{3} = 4, \quad R^3 = (0.98)^3 = 0.941192$$

$$(1 - R)^1 = 0.02$$

$$\text{Term 1} = 4 \times 0.941192 \times 0.02 = 0.07529536$$

**Term 2:** $j = 4$

$$\binom{4}{4} = 1, \quad R^4 = (0.98)^4 = 0.92236816$$

$$(1 - R)^0 = 1$$

$$\text{Term 2} = 1 \times 0.92236816 \times 1 = 0.92236816$$

**Sum:**

$$R_S = 0.07529536 + 0.92236816 = 0.99766352$$

$$\boxed{R_S \approx 0.9977}$$

**Interpretation:** The 3-out-of-4 RAID system has a reliability of **99.77%**, which is significantly higher than any single drive (98%) — justifying the use of redundancy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert (k-out-of-n problems):**
> 1. **Forgetting** the binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{j}$ is a common error. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Missing** the second term ($j = 4$ in this case) because students stop at $j = k$. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Not verifying** that the answer lies between $R$ and $1$ — for k-out-of-n, the answer must satisfy $R^n \leq R_S \leq 1$. If not, you've made an arithmetic error. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Expressing reliability as a percentage without justification** — always keep it as a probability (between 0 and 1) in formula form before converting.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Definitions and Basic Concepts):**
> - **System Reliability** = $P[\text{system performs function for time } t]$. Definition must include "intended function."
> - **Series:** All components essential. Formula: $R_S = \prod R_i$.
> - **Parallel:** At least one must work. Formula: $R_S = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$.
> - **k-out-of-n:** At least $k$ of $n$ work. Formula: $R_S = \sum_{j=k}^{n} \binom{n}{j} R^j (1-R)^{n-j}$.
> - **Series** is the *weakest link*; **Parallel** is the *strongest link*; **k-out-of-n** is in between.
> - **Assumption:** Component failures are *independent* unless stated otherwise.
> - **Coherent system:** Monotonic in components, all-fail → system fails, all-work → system works.
> - **RBD vs Schematic:** RBD is *functional logic*, not physical wiring.
> - **Reliability of identical components** simplifies formulas using powers of $R$.
> - **Bound check:** $R_S$ for series $\leq \min(R_i)$; for parallel $\geq \max(R_i)$.
> - **K-out-of-n answer range:** $R^n \leq R_S \leq 1$, with strict inequality if $0 < R < 1$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inherent Availability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inherent Availability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Inherent Availability ($A_i$)** is the steady-state probability that a repairable system will be operating satisfactorily at any random point in time, *considering only the inherent design characteristics* of the equipment — namely, the mean operating time between failures and the mean active corrective maintenance (repair) time. It deliberately **excludes** logistics delays, administrative delays, preventive maintenance downtime, and supply-chain waiting periods.

Mathematically, it is defined as:

$$
A_i \;=\; \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

where:
- **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures (hours)
- **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair — the *active* corrective maintenance time (hours)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Inherent Availability is the **theoretical upper bound** of equipment availability and is the metric most frequently used by design engineers to compare competing equipment designs before deployment.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you own a personal car. Over a year, you drive it for **350 days** (it's parked/drivable) and it spends **15 days in the workshop** for actual repair work (engine overhaul, brake jobs, etc.). The *inherent availability* of your car as a designed machine is:

$$
A_i = \frac{350}{350 + 15} \approx 0.958 \;\; (95.8\%)
$$

Notice that this calculation deliberately **ignores** the days you waited for spare parts (logistics delay) or the days the garage was closed on Sundays (administrative delay). It only counts the time the car is *capable* of running versus the time the mechanic is *actively working on it*. This makes $A_i$ a pure measure of the *machine's own design quality*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this distinction important?**
> Because once a design is finalized, logistics and administrative factors are largely outside the manufacturer's control. $A_i$ isolates what *engineering* can improve — better components (higher MTBF) or modular designs (lower MTTR).

## 1.3 Placement Within the Availability Hierarchy

Reliability engineering defines **three nested availability metrics**:

| Hierarchy Level | Metric | Formula (typical) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (Highest) | Inherent Availability $A_i$ | $\dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \overline{M}_{\text{ct}}}$ |
| 2 (Middle) | Achieved Availability $A_a$ | $\dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MDT}}$ |
| 3 (Lowest) | Operational Availability $A_o$ | $\dfrac{\text{MTBM}}{\text{MTBM} + \text{MDT}}$ |

where $\overline{M}_{\text{ct}}$ is mean active corrective maintenance time, **MDT** is mean downtime (including delays), and **MTBM** is mean time between maintenance actions.

The fundamental ordering is:

$$
A_i \;\ge\; A_a \;\ge\; A_o
$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — The Alternating Renewal Process

A repairable system undergoes a stochastic cycle of two alternating states:

$$
\text{UP (Operating)} \xrightarrow{\text{Failure}} \text{DOWN (Under Repair)} \xrightarrow{\text{Repair Complete}} \text{UP} \rightarrow \cdots
$$

Each "UP" interval is a random variable characterized by a reliability distribution $R(t)$ and each "DOWN" interval is characterized by a maintainability distribution $M(t)$.

### Logical Step-by-Step Reasoning

1. **Define availability function**: $A(t) = P(\text{system is UP at time } t)$.
2. **Take the limit** as $t \to \infty$ (system reaches statistical equilibrium / steady-state).
3. **Apply Renewal Reward Theorem**: The long-run fraction of time spent UP equals the ratio of expected UP duration to the expected cycle duration.
4. **Identify expectations**: $E[\text{UP}] = \text{MTBF}$, $\,E[\text{DOWN}] = \text{MTTR}$.
5. **Conclude**: $A_i = \dfrac{E[\text{UP}]}{E[\text{UP}] + E[\text{DOWN}]}$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula you must memorize for the KTU board exam on this topic.

| Symbol | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A_i$ | $\dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Inherent availability | dimensionless |
| $U_i$ | $1 - A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Inherent unavailability | dimensionless |
| $\text{MDT}_{\text{annual}}$ | $8760 \times (1 - A_i)$ | Annual downtime | hours/year |
| $\lambda$ | $\dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Failure rate (constant) | failures/hour |
| $\mu$ | $\dfrac{1}{\text{MTTR}}$ | Repair rate (constant) | repairs/hour |
| $A_i$ (Poisson form) | $\dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ | Inherent availability in terms of $\lambda,\mu$ | dimensionless |
| $A(t)$ | $R(t) + \int_0^{t} R(t-x)\,dM(x)$ | Point availability (general renewal) | dimensionless |
| $\lim_{t \to \infty} A(t)$ | $\dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Steady-state availability | dimensionless |
| Failures/year | $\dfrac{8760 \times A_i}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Expected failure count | failures/year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions:**
> - When $\text{MTBF} \to \infty$ (perfect equipment), $A_i \to 1$
> - When $\text{MTTR} \to 0$ (instant repair), $A_i \to 1$
> - When $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTR}$, $A_i = 0.5$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Inherent availability is the **canonical specification metric** used in:
- **Aerospace**: Military procurement specifications (MIL-STD-721, MIL-HDBK-217).
- **Nuclear Power Plants**: Safety system availability targets ($A_i > 0.999$ demanded).
- **Manufacturing**: OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) calculation inputs.
- **Telecommunications**: Base transceiver station design SLAs.
- **Medical Devices**: MRI, CT scanner uptime guarantees to hospitals.

The metric gives a *design-independent* benchmark — meaning two vendors offering different logistical support models can still be compared fairly on the intrinsic reliability-maintainability of their machines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Inherent Availability from First Principles

We begin with the **general point-availability function** for a single-unit repairable system subject to an alternating renewal process.

### Step 1 — Define the reliability and maintainability functions

Let:
- $F(t)$ = Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of the time-to-failure random variable.
- $G(t)$ = CDF of the time-to-repair random variable.

Then the reliability function is:

$$
R(t) = 1 - F(t) = P(\text{system survives to time } t)
$$

And the maintainability function is:

$$
M(t) = G(t) = P(\text{repair completed within time } t)
$$

The corresponding probability density functions are:

$$
f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt}, \qquad g(t) = \frac{dG(t)}{dt}
$$

### Step 2 — Formulate the point-availability equation

By the law of total probability applied to the renewal process, the system is UP at time $t$ if either:
- **Case (a):** It has been UP the whole time $[0, t]$, *or*
- **Case (b):** It had at least one failure-repair cycle completed before $t$ and is currently UP.

This gives the **Volterra integral equation** of renewal theory:

$$
A(t) = R(t) + \int_{0}^{t} R(t - x)\, dG(x)
$$

### Step 3 — Take the Laplace transform

Taking the Laplace transform $\mathcal{L}\{A(t)\} = A^{*}(s)$ with $\mathcal{L}\{R(t)\} = R^{*}(s)$ and $\mathcal{L}\{G(t)\} = G^{*}(s)$:

$$
A^{*}(s) = R^{*}(s) + R^{*}(s) \cdot G^{*}(s) = R^{*}(s)\bigl[1 + G^{*}(s)\bigr]
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Final Value Theorem

Using $\lim_{t \to \infty} A(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s\,A^{*}(s)$ and recognizing that $\lim_{s \to 0} s\,R^{*}(s) = E[\text{UP}] = \text{MTBF}$ (with analogous result for $G$):

$$
A_i = \lim_{t \to \infty} A(t) = \frac{E[\text{UP}]}{E[\text{UP}] + E[\text{DOWN}]}
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the engineering terms

$$
\boxed{\,A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}\,}
$$

## 3.2 Special Case — Exponential Time-to-Failure and Time-to-Repair

When both the failure time and repair time follow **exponential distributions** (a common KTU assumption for tractable problems):

- $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ where $\lambda = 1/\text{MTBF}$
- $M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu t}$ where $\mu = 1/\text{MTTR}$

The transient point-availability becomes:

$$
A(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} + \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}\, e^{-(\lambda + \mu)t}
$$

As $t \to \infty$, the exponential term vanishes, leaving:

$$
A_i = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{1/\text{MTTR}}{1/\text{MTBF} + 1/\text{MTTR}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\text{MTBF} \cdot \text{MTTR}$:

$$
A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

This **confirms** that the steady-state result is distribution-free — it depends only on the means, regardless of whether the underlying distributions are exponential, Weibull, lognormal, etc.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A CNC milling machine in an automated production cell has been observed over 12 months. During this period it logged 3600 hours of actual cutting time and was under active repair for 72 hours. Compute the inherent availability, annual downtime, and expected number of failures per year.

**Solution:**

Given data:
- Total observation period $T = 12 \text{ months} = 12 \times 30 \times 24 = 8640 \text{ hours}$
- Uptime (operating) $= 3600$ hours (which includes both productive and idle-but-functional periods)
- Downtime (active repair) $= 72$ hours

**Step 1:** Compute MTBF and MTTR.

Assume failures occurred 3 times during the year (typical estimate). Then:

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \frac{3600}{3} = 1200 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Repair Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \frac{72}{3} = 24 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $A_i$.

$$
A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{1200}{1200 + 24} = \frac{1200}{1224} = 0.9804
$$

**Step 3:** Compute annual downtime.

$$
\text{Downtime}_{\text{annual}} = 8760 \times (1 - A_i) = 8760 \times 0.0196 = 171.7 \text{ hours/year}
$$

**Step 4:** Expected failures per year.

$$
N_{\text{failures/year}} = \frac{8760 \times A_i}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{8760 \times 0.9804}{1200} = 7.16 \text{ failures/year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 2% unavailability sounds small, but at $24\times 7$ operation it accumulates to ~172 hours of lost production annually — equivalent to **7 working days** of stoppage.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Availability Hierarchy

The following diagram captures the engineering flow from raw component behaviour up to the various availability metrics that procurement and operations teams use.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component Failure Behavior R of t] --> B[System Reliability R sys of t]
    C[Repair Process Behavior M of t] --> D[System Maintainability M sys of t]
    B --> E[Alternating Renewal Process]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Point Availability A of t]
    F --> G[Steady State A sub infinity]
    G --> H[Inherent Availability Ai]
    G --> I[Achieved Availability Aa]
    G --> J[Operational Availability Ao]
    H --> K[Design Specification Mil Std 721]
    I --> L[Plant Performance Benchmark]
    J --> M[Logistics Integrated Metric]
```

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram of a Repairable System

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OperatingState
    OperatingState --> RepairState : Failure occurs lambda dt
    RepairState --> OperatingState : Repair complete mu dt
    note right of OperatingState : MTBF expected uptime hours
    note left of RepairState : MTTR expected downtime hours
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — From Design to $A_i$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blkA[Hardware Design Specification] --> blkB[MTBF hours]
    blkC[Modular Architecture Serviceability] --> blkD[MTTR hours]
    blkB --> blkE[Numerator of Ai fraction]
    blkD --> blkF[Denominator addition]
    blkE --> blkG[Ai equals MTBF over MTBF plus MTTR]
    blkF --> blkG
    blkG --> blkH[Compare with target Ai greater than 0.99]
    blkH --> blkI{Accept Design?}
    blkI -->|Yes| blkJ[Release to Production]
    blkI -->|No| blkK[Re engineer for higher MTBF or lower MTTR]
    blkK --> blkA
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define inherent availability. State the conditions under which it is the appropriate metric to use.
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMET747]`

**Model Answer:**
Inherent availability $A_i$ is defined as the steady-state probability that a system is operating at any random instant, considering only its inherent design characteristics — i.e., the mean time between failures and the mean active corrective maintenance time.

$$
A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Conditions of applicability:**
1. The system must be **repairable** (alternating renewal process).
2. The system must be in **steady-state** (long operational history).
3. Only **active corrective** repair time is included — logistics, administrative, and preventive-maintenance delays are excluded.
4. It is most appropriate during the **design and procurement phase** when logistics support infrastructure is not yet defined.

### Q2. How does inherent availability differ from operational availability?
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Inherent Availability $A_i$ | Operational Availability $A_o$ |
|---|---|---|
| Numerator | MTBF (between failures) | MTBM (between any maintenance) |
| Denominator addition | Mean active corrective repair time | Mean total downtime (incl. logistics) |
| Includes preventive maintenance? | No | Yes |
| Includes supply delays? | No | Yes |
| Includes administrative delays? | No | Yes |
| Typical value | Highest | Lowest |
| Used in | Design specification | Field operations review |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistakes that cost students marks on this topic:
> 1. **Confusing MTBF with MTBCF** — for systems with non-critical failures, MTBF > MTBCF. For KTU 2024, unless stated otherwise, assume MTBF is between *all* failures.
> 2. **Mixing 8760 hours/year with 24×7 calendar** — if the plant operates only 8 hours/day, use $8 \times 365 = 2920$ hours/year as the operational period.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert units** when MTBF is given in days and MTTR in hours.
> 4. **Stating final answer without showing the formula** — always write the equation before substituting.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4, Main Question]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the inherent availability of a single-unit repairable system operating under an alternating renewal process with exponential failure and repair distributions. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial compressor has MTBF = 600 hours and MTTR = 12 hours. The plant operates continuously (8760 h/year). Compute:
   (i) The inherent availability $A_i$
   (ii) The annual downtime
   (iii) The expected number of failures per year
   (iv) The percentage improvement in $A_i$ if a redesigned valve train raises MTBF to 900 hours. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Derivation:**

*Assumptions:*
1. The system is a single unit with two states: UP or DOWN.
2. The time-to-failure $T_f$ follows an exponential distribution with rate $\lambda$.
3. The time-to-repair $T_r$ follows an exponential distribution with rate $\mu$.
4. After repair, the system is *as good as new* (renewal).
5. The process is in steady state.

*Derivation:*

At any time $t > 0$, the system is UP if either it never failed up to $t$ **OR** it failed once and was repaired before $t$, etc. The transient availability is:

$$
A(t) = R(t) + \int_{0}^{t} R(t - x)\, g(x)\, dx
$$

where $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ and $g(x) = \mu e^{-\mu x}$.

Solving via Laplace transform or by direct convolution:

$$
A(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} + \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}\, e^{-(\lambda + \mu)t}
$$

Taking $t \to \infty$:

$$
A_i = \lim_{t \to \infty} A(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}
$$

Substituting $\lambda = 1/\text{MTBF}$ and $\mu = 1/\text{MTTR}$:

$$
\boxed{\,A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}\,}
$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: MTBF = 600 h, MTTR = 12 h, $T_{\text{year}} = 8760$ h.

**(i)** Inherent availability:

$$
A_i = \frac{600}{600 + 12} = \frac{600}{612} = 0.9804
$$

**(ii)** Annual downtime:

$$
D_{\text{annual}} = 8760 \times (1 - 0.9804) = 8760 \times 0.0196 = 171.7 \text{ hours/year}
$$

**(iii)** Expected number of failures per year:

$$
N_f = \frac{8760 \times A_i}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{8760 \times 0.9804}{600} = 14.31 \text{ failures/year}
$$

**(iv)** With MTBF raised to 900 h:

$$
A_i^{\text{new}} = \frac{900}{900 + 12} = \frac{900}{912} = 0.9868
$$

Percentage improvement:

$$
\% \Delta = \frac{A_i^{\text{new}} - A_i}{A_i} \times 100 = \frac{0.9868 - 0.9804}{0.9804} \times 100 = 0.66\%
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Alternative]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the concept of availability of a repairable system. Distinguish clearly between point availability, interval availability, and steady-state (inherent) availability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A server farm has the following data over a quarter (90 days): 4 failures occurred, total operating time was 6480 hours, total active repair time was 20 hours, total logistics delay was 60 hours, and total preventive maintenance was 40 hours. The system operates 24 hours per day. Compute:
   (i) MTBF and MTTR
   (ii) Inherent availability $A_i$
   (iii) Achieved availability $A_a$
   (iv) Operational availability $A_o$
   (v) Comment on the ranking of these values. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Conceptual Explanation:**

A *repairable system* can be returned to service after failure. Its availability is the probability that it performs its intended function at a specified instant or over a specified interval.

*Block diagram:*

```
   +-----------------+         Failure         +-----------------+
   |   UP State      |------------------------>|   DOWN State    |
   |  (Operating)    |                         |   (Repairing)   |
   |                 |<------------------------|                 |
   +-----------------+        Repair           +-----------------+
            ^                                          |
            |------------------------------------------|
                    Alternating Renewal Cycle
```

*Three types of availability:*
1. **Point Availability $A(t)$**: Probability the system is UP at the exact instant $t$.
2. **Interval Availability $A_I(t_1, t_2)$**: Fraction of time interval $[t_1, t_2]$ during which system was UP.
3. **Steady-State Availability $A(\infty) = A_i$**: Limiting value as $t \to \infty$, equal to $\text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$ for the inherent case.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given data: 4 failures in 90 days; operating time = 6480 h; active repair = 20 h; logistics delay = 60 h; PM = 40 h.

Total calendar time $T = 90 \times 24 = 2160$ h. *(Note: we use MTBF and MTTR definitions, not the calendar time, but operations period is 24/7.)*

Wait — let us use the simpler formulation. Operating hours between failures imply the system was available 6480 of the operational hours. Total scheduled hours = 6480 + 20 + 60 + 40 = 6600 h? Actually let us use the textbook convention:

**(i) MTBF and MTTR:**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \frac{6480}{4} = 1620 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Active Repair Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \frac{20}{4} = 5 \text{ hours}
$$

**(ii) Inherent Availability:**

$$
A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{1620}{1620 + 5} = \frac{1620}{1625} = 0.9969
$$

**(iii) Achieved Availability:**

$$
\text{MDT} = \frac{20 + 60}{4} = \frac{80}{4} = 20 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
A_a = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MDT}} = \frac{1620}{1620 + 20} = \frac{1620}{1640} = 0.9878
$$

**(iv) Operational Availability:**

$$
\text{MTBM} = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Maintenance Actions}} = \frac{6480}{4 + N_{pm}}
$$

Assuming 1 PM cycle per quarter (typical): $N_{pm} = 1$, so total maintenance actions = 5.

$$
\text{MTBM} = \frac{6480}{5} = 1296 \text{ hours}
$$

Total downtime including PM = 20 + 60 + 40 = 120 h.

$$
\text{MDT}_{\text{op}} = \frac{120}{5} = 24 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
A_o = \frac{1296}{1296 + 24} = \frac{1296}{1320} = 0.9818
$$

**(v) Comment:**

The ranking is:

$$
A_i (0.9969) \;>\; A_a (0.9878) \;>\; A_o (0.9818)
$$

This confirms the theoretical ordering. The gap between $A_i$ and $A_a$ (~0.91%) is due to logistics delays; the gap between $A_a$ and $A_o$ (~0.6%) is due to preventive-maintenance intrusions. The data centre design is excellent on the inherent side but has room for improvement in supply-chain logistics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Inherent Availability**

- **Definition:** $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ — steady-state, design-level metric.
- **Excludes:** logistics delay, administrative delay, preventive maintenance, supply-chain waiting.
- **Includes only:** operating time + *active corrective* repair time.
- **Theoretical ordering:** $A_i \ge A_a \ge A_o$ (always).
- **Distribution-free result:** The formula depends only on means, not on the underlying distribution shape (Exponential, Weibull, Lognormal).
- **Exponential case identity:** $A_i = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$.
- **Annual downtime:** $8760 \times (1 - A_i)$ for continuous operations (24×7).
- **Design benchmark target:** $A_i \ge 0.99$ for high-reliability industrial systems; $A_i \ge 0.999$ for aerospace / nuclear.
- **Improvement levers:** Increase MTBF (better components, derating, redundancy) **OR** decrease MTTR (modular design, better diagnostics, trained crew).
- **Renewal assumption:** "As good as new" after repair — fundamental to applying the renewal-reward theorem.
- **KTU must-derive:** the Volterra integral $A(t) = R(t) + \int_0^t R(t-x)\,dG(x)$ and its steady-state limit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Achieved Availability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Achieved Availability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Achieved Availability ($A_a$)** is a measure of system performance that quantifies the probability that a system, when called upon, is in an operating state. It is defined as the ratio of the **uptime** (the time during which the equipment is actually available for use) to the **total period of observation time** (uptime + total downtime).

In rigorous KTU 2024 Scheme terminology:

> [!NOTE]
> **Achieved Availability ($A_a$)** is the long-run fraction of time that a repairable system is in the *up* (operating) state, considering *all* sources of unavailability, including active corrective repair time, preventive maintenance time, administrative delays, and logistic supply delays.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$A_a = \frac{\text{Mean Up Time (MUT)}}{\text{Mean Up Time (MUT)} + \text{Mean Down Time (MDT)}}$$

Where:
- **MUT (Mean Up Time)** = Average operating duration between failures/maintenance events.
- **MDT (Mean Down Time)** = Average time the system is unavailable for use (sum of *all* downtimes).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **public bus** plying the Kochi–Trivandrum route. 

- Sometimes it is in the **garage being repaired** (this is *inherent* unavailability).
- Sometimes it is **waiting for a spare part** that has not arrived from Bangalore (logistic delay).
- Sometimes it is **undergoing mandatory fitness inspection** (preventive maintenance).
- Sometimes the **driver is on strike** or paperwork is pending (administrative delay).

**Inherent Availability** only counts the first scenario. **Achieved Availability** counts **all four** scenarios combined. Therefore, $A_a$ is *always less than or equal to* $A_i$.

It is the metric that reflects the *real-world* operational truth that the fleet manager experiences daily.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Achieved Availability ($A_a$)**: Dimensionless, expressed as a fraction (0 to 1) or percentage (0% to 100%).
- **Inherent Availability ($A_i$)**: Theoretical upper bound; $A_a \le A_i$.
- **Operational Availability ($A_o$)**: Synonym used in military/defence standards (MIL-STD-721).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The distinction between *inherent* and *achieved* availability is a high-frequency Module 4 question. Always remember: *Achieved* considers logistics + administrative + preventive maintenance, while *inherent* considers only the active repair time (MTTR).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Time Hierarchy of System States

Every repairable system oscillates between two macro-states: **UP** and **DOWN**. However, the *DOWN* state itself has a layered structure:

1. **Active Corrective Maintenance (M)** – Wrench-on-the-equipment repair time.
2. **Logistic Delay Time (MLDT)** – Waiting for spares, transport, vendor support.
3. **Administrative Delay Time (MADT)** – Paperwork, approvals, scheduling.
4. **Preventive Maintenance Time (Mpt)** – Scheduled servicing, overhauls, inspections.

**Achieved Availability** aggregates all these into a single denominator.

### 2.2 Why "Achieved" Differs From "Inherent"

| Metric | Numerator Includes | Denominator Includes | Perspective |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inherent Availability ($A_i$)** | MTBF (operating time) | MTBF + MTTR | Design-stage, ideal logistics |
| **Achieved Availability ($A_a$)** | MTBM (mean time between maintenance) | MTBM + MDT | Real-world, field-operational |

The core "**Why**": $A_a$ is a *field-measured* metric. It captures the *full socio-technical* cost of downtime, making it the true indicator of mission-readiness for the end-user.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_a = \dfrac{\text{Up Time}}{\text{Up Time} + \text{All Down Time}}$ | Master definition | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $A_a = \dfrac{\text{MUT}}{\text{MUT} + \text{MDT}}$ | Mean value form | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $A_a = \dfrac{\text{MTBM}}{\text{MTBM} + \text{MDT}}$ | Using Mean Time Between Maintenance | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{MDT} = \text{M} + \text{MLDT} + \text{MADT}$ | Total downtime decomposition | Hours |
| 5 | $A_a = \dfrac{\text{MTBM}}{\text{MTBM} + \text{M} + \text{MLDT} + \text{MADT}}$ | Expanded form | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{MTBM} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_a}$ | Achieved failure rate (corrective + preventive) | Hours |
| 7 | $A_a \le A_i$ | Fundamental inequality | — |
| 8 | $\text{Unavailability}, \bar{A}_a = 1 - A_a$ | Complement | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern Note:** When using $\text{MTBM}$, include **both** corrective and preventive maintenance events in the denominator's failure count. The achieved failure rate $\lambda_a$ is *higher* than the inherent failure rate $\lambda$ because preventive maintenance also creates downtime.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Defence**: Used in **RAM-C (Reliability, Availability, Maintainability, Cost)** trade-off studies to evaluate fleet readiness.
- **Manufacturing**: Used in **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** calculations as the *Availability* factor.
- **Telecommunications**: Determines **Service Level Agreements (SLA)** of 99.99% (four-nines) availability.
- **Power Systems**: Computes **Loss of Load Probability (LOLP)** in generation planning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Achieved Availability from Up Time and Down Time

Let $T$ be the total observation period. During $T$:
- The system is **UP** for a total cumulative time $T_{\text{up}}$.
- The system is **DOWN** for a total cumulative time $T_{\text{down}}$.

By conservation of time:

$$T = T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}$$

The fundamental definition of achieved availability is the fraction of total time the system is in the operational state:

$$A_a = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{T_{\text{up}}}{T}$$

Substituting the conservation equation:

$$A_a = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{T_{\text{up}}}{T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}}$$

As the observation window $T$ grows large, $T_{\text{up}}/T$ converges to the mean up time per cycle, and $T_{\text{down}}/T$ converges to the mean down time per cycle. So:

$$A_a = \frac{\text{MUT}}{\text{MUT} + \text{MDT}}$$

**Why each step matters:**
- The *conservation* of time enforces the boundary that everything must be classified as either UP or DOWN — no hidden states.
- The *limit* operation ensures we are measuring long-run steady-state behaviour, not transient.

### 3.2 Derivation of MDT Decomposition

Any down event consists of distinct sequential phases. The total down time per event is the sum of all these phases:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MDT} &= \text{M} + \text{MLDT} + \text{MADT} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- **M** = mean active corrective maintenance time (the *wrench-on* time).
- **MLDT** = mean logistic delay time (waiting for parts, transport, vendors).
- **MADT** = mean administrative delay time (paperwork, approvals).

Therefore the full $A_a$ expression becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_a &= \frac{\text{MUT}}{\text{MUT} + \text{M} + \text{MLDT} + \text{MADT}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Proof of the Fundamental Inequality $A_a \le A_i$

By definition, the *inherent* availability is:

$$A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

Since $\text{MTTR}$ is only the **active** repair time, and the achieved availability includes *more* down-time terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MDT} &= \text{MTTR} + \text{MLDT} + \text{MADT} \\
\Rightarrow \text{MDT} &\ge \text{MTTR}
\end{aligned}
$$

Also, by convention, $\text{MUT} = \text{MTBF}$ (mean up time between failures equals mean time between failures in a non-perfect-maintenance model). Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_a &= \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MDT}} \le \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = A_i
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, **$A_a \le A_i$**, with equality only when $\text{MLDT} = \text{MADT} = 0$ (instantaneous logistics and zero administration).

### 3.4 Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A CNC milling machine has been observed for **4000 hours** of operation. During this period, it experienced:
> - 6 corrective failures, each requiring an average active repair time of 6 hours.
> - 3 preventive maintenance events, each taking an average of 8 hours.
> - Total logistic delay of 24 hours.
> - Total administrative delay of 12 hours.
>
> Compute the **Achieved Availability**.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Total Downtime (Corrective):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Corrective Downtime} &= 6 \text{ events} \times 6 \text{ hours/event} = 36 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Total Preventive Maintenance Time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Preventive Time} &= 3 \text{ events} \times 8 \text{ hours/event} = 24 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Sum all downtime sources:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Downtime} &= 36 + 24 + 24 + 12 = 96 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Compute Up Time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{up}} &= 4000 - 96 = 3904 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5 — Compute Achieved Availability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_a &= \frac{3904}{4000} = 0.976 = 97.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $A_a = 0.976$ or **97.6 %**.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for Exam

| Aspect | Inherent Availability ($A_i$) | Achieved Availability ($A_a$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Includes Preventive Maintenance?** | No | **Yes** |
| **Includes Logistic Delays?** | No | **Yes** |
| **Includes Administrative Delays?** | No | **Yes** |
| **Domain of Use** | Design, lab testing | Field operations, mission readiness |
| **Who computes it?** | Design engineers | Operations & maintenance managers |
| **Numerical Value** | Higher (upper bound) | Lower (realistic field value) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Time-State Decomposition Diagram (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of System Life Cycle] --> B[OPERATING STATE - MUT]
    B --> C{Failure Event?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Active Corrective Repair M]
    D --> E[Logistic Delay MLDT]
    E --> F[Administrative Delay MADT]
    F --> G[Preventive Maintenance Mpt]
    G --> B
    C -- No --> H[Scheduled Preventive Maintenance]
    H --> I[Preventive Time Mpt]
    I --> B
    B --> Z[End of Observation Window T]

    classDef statefill fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    classDef delayfill fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef startend fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333,color:#000

    class A,Z startend
    class B statefill
    class D,E,F,I delayfill
```

```mermaid

    F --> J

    H --> L
    I --> L

```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Relationship Between Availability Types

```mermaid

    B --> B1[Up Time Tup]
    B --> B2[Down Time Tdown]

    B2 --> B2b[Preventive Mpt]
    B2 --> B2c[Logistic MLDT]
    B2 --> B2d[Administrative MADT]

    A1[Inherent Availability Ai] --> C[Includes Only]
    C --> C1[MTBF]
    C --> C2[MTTR]

```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define **Achieved Availability**. How is it different from **Inherent Availability**?

**Model Answer:**
Achieved Availability ($A_a$) is the fraction of total time that a system is in an operating state, considering *all* sources of downtime including active repair, preventive maintenance, logistic delays, and administrative delays. It is given by:

$$A_a = \frac{\text{MUT}}{\text{MUT} + \text{MDT}}$$

The difference is that **Inherent Availability** considers *only* the active corrective repair time (MTTR), assuming ideal logistics and zero administrative delay, while **Achieved Availability** captures the *real field* conditions and is therefore always **less than or equal to** $A_i$.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2.** Mention any **two factors** that cause Achieved Availability to be lower than Inherent Availability.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Logistic Delay Time (MLDT)** – Waiting for spare parts, transport, and vendor support increases total down time without contributing to actual repair.
2. **Administrative Delay Time (MADT)** – Paperwork, scheduling conflicts, and procedural approvals add to the down time.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]**

### **Question A (Choice 1)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for **Achieved Availability** from first principles. Clearly show the decomposition of Mean Down Time.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A radar system operates 24 hours a day. Over 6 months (180 days), it was down for a total of 270 hours, which included 100 hours of active repair, 80 hours of preventive maintenance, 50 hours of logistic delay, and 40 hours of administrative delay. Compute:
- (i) Inherent Availability
- (ii) Achieved Availability
- (iii) Comment on the gap between the two.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
The observation window $T$ splits into $T_{\text{up}}$ and $T_{\text{down}}$.

$$T = T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}$$

For long-run steady state, Achieved Availability is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_a &= \frac{T_{\text{up}}}{T} = \frac{T_{\text{up}}}{T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to mean values:

$$A_a = \frac{\text{MUT}}{\text{MUT} + \text{MDT}}$$

Decomposition of MDT:

$$\text{MDT} = \text{M} + \text{MLDT} + \text{MADT} + \text{Mpt}$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_a = \frac{\text{MUT}}{\text{MUT} + \text{M} + \text{MLDT} + \text{MADT} + \text{Mpt}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b):**

Total time in 6 months:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 24 \times 180 = 4320 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

Up time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{up}} &= 4320 - 270 = 4050 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Inherent Availability** (uses only active repair time MTTR = 100 h, assuming one failure cluster):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_i &= \frac{4050}{4050 + 100} = \frac{4050}{4150} = 0.9759 = 97.59\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Achieved Availability** (uses all down time):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_a &= \frac{4050}{4320} = 0.9375 = 93.75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Comment:**
The gap $\Delta A = 97.59\% - 93.75\% = 3.84\%$. This gap is entirely due to *logistic + administrative + preventive* delays. This shows that even a well-designed system (high $A_i$) can suffer significant real-world unavailability due to support and supply chain inefficiencies.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the **time-state structure** of a repairable system. Mark MUT, MDT, M, MLDT, MADT, and Mpt in the diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A production line has **MTBF = 500 hours**, **MTTR = 10 hours**, **Mean Logistic Delay = 6 hours**, **Mean Administrative Delay = 4 hours**, and **Mean Preventive Time = 8 hours**. Calculate (i) $A_i$, (ii) $A_a$, and (iii) the percentage loss of availability due to non-corrective factors.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
A repairable system cycles between UP and DOWN states. The DOWN state is further decomposed as:
- Active Corrective Maintenance (**M**)
- Logistic Delay (**MLDT**)
- Administrative Delay (**MADT**)
- Preventive Maintenance (**Mpt**)

```
    ┌─────── MUT ───────┐
    │                   │
    ▼                   │
[  OPERATING  ]──Fail──▶[  M  ]──▶[ MLDT ]──▶[ MADT ]
    ▲                                  │
    │                                  ▼
    │                          [ Preventive Mpt ]
    │                                  │
    └────────── MUT ──────────────────┘
```

**Part (b):**

Total Down Time per cycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MDT} &= 10 + 6 + 4 + 8 = 28 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Inherent Availability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_i &= \frac{500}{500 + 10} = \frac{500}{510} = 0.9804 = 98.04\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Achieved Availability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_a &= \frac{500}{500 + 28} = \frac{500}{528} = 0.9470 = 94.70\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Percentage loss of availability due to non-corrective factors:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta A_{\%} &= \frac{A_i - A_a}{A_i} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{0.9804 - 0.9470}{0.9804} \times 100 \\
&= 3.41\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This 3.41 % loss is the penalty paid for logistic, administrative, and preventive-maintenance overheads.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** compute $A_a$ using $A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ — this is $A_i$, not $A_a$. Using MTTR alone in the denominator when asked for $A_a$ will fetch *zero* marks.
> 2. **Unit consistency**: When MDT is given in *hours*, MTBF must also be in *hours*. Mixing units (e.g., MTBF in hours, MLDT in days) is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Total Time**: If the problem gives operating hours and downtime separately, the total time is their sum, not an additional quantity. Many students mistakenly double-count.
> 4. **Numerator of $A_a$** is **Up Time** (not MTBF alone), although in single-component models they coincide.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Achieved Availability ($A_a$)** is the *field-true* availability — what the operator actually experiences.
- **Master Formula:** $A_a = \frac{\text{MUT}}{\text{MUT} + \text{MDT}}$.
- **MDT Composition:** $\text{MDT} = \text{M} + \text{MLDT} + \text{MADT} + \text{Mpt}$.
- **Key Inequality:** $A_a \le A_i$, with equality only in the absence of logistic, administrative, and preventive delays.
- **MTBM** (Mean Time Between Maintenance) is the natural "Mean Up Time" when both corrective and preventive interventions are counted.
- **Achieved Failure Rate ($\lambda_a$)** > **Inherent Failure Rate ($\lambda$)** because scheduled maintenance adds down events.
- **Application Domains:** Aerospace (mission readiness), Telecom (SLAs), Power Systems (LOLP), Manufacturing (OEE).
- **Operational Availability ($A_o$)** is the military/defence synonym for $A_a$.
- **Unavailability** $\bar{A}_a = 1 - A_a$ is often used in RAM block diagrams.
- For KTU: always express the answer as a *fraction* AND a *percentage* to maximize presentation marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operational Availability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operational Availability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Operational Availability (Ao)** is a comprehensive availability metric used in reliability and maintainability engineering that quantifies the fraction of calendar time a system, equipment, or component is operationally capable of performing its intended mission. Unlike inherent availability, which considers only *corrective maintenance downtime*, **operational availability accounts for the total operational downtime experienced in real-world field conditions**.

Mathematically, it is defined as the ratio of **Mean Time Between Maintenance (MTBM)** to the sum of MTBM and **Mean Downtime (MDT)**, expressed as:

$$A_o = \frac{Uptime}{Total\ Time} = \frac{MTBM}{MTBM + MDT}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Distinction**: In the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus, Operational Availability (Ao) is treated as the *realistic* availability metric. The acronym **Ao** is universally adopted in KTU board valuation scripts and answer keys. Always write the subscript clearly.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Family Car You Need on Monday Morning"

Imagine you own a family car. In theory, the engine is reliable — it might run perfectly 99% of the time it's being driven. **But consider what else happens:**

- The car is in the workshop for an **oil change** (scheduled maintenance).
- The mechanic has to **wait 3 days for a spare part** to arrive from another city (supply/logistics delay).
- You leave it at the **service station on Saturday** and **pick it up Monday** (administrative/personal delay).
