- The car was actually in perfect mechanical condition, but you *could not use it*.

This is exactly what **operational availability** captures. It does **not** care whether the downtime was caused by an actual failure, a planned preventive action, or a logistics delay — if the system could not do its mission, it is counted as **down**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU Emphasizes Ao**: Industry rarely operates under "ideal" inherent availability conditions. Field data, supply chains, manpower, and scheduling realities dominate. The KTU board therefore tests *Operational Availability* as the practical metric that bridges theoretical reliability with engineering reality.

### Categorization of Downtime in Ao

Operational availability aggregates **all classes of downtime** under one umbrella:

| Downtime Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Active Repair Downtime** | Time spent actually fixing a failure |
| **Logistics Delay** | Waiting for spares, tools, or transport |
| **Administrative Delay** | Paperwork, approvals, shift handovers |
| **Preventive Maintenance** | Scheduled servicing, overhauls |
| **Idle / Standby Downtime** | Time when the system is not mission-required |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The denominator of Ao is always **calendar (or mission) time**, *not* operating hours. This is a common board exam trap — students confuse MTBF with MTBM.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Theoretical Breakdown

Operational Availability is rooted in the principle that **availability is fundamentally a function of time-based logistics and operational realities**, not just component reliability. The conceptual hierarchy progresses as follows:

**Step 1 — Recognize the Mission Window.**
Define the total observation window as $T_{total}$, which represents calendar time (e.g., one year = 8760 hours).

**Step 2 — Separate Time into Two Buckets.**
Every moment in $T_{total}$ belongs either to the *uptime* set or *downtime* set. Operationally, **uptime** means the system can perform its mission when called upon.

**Step 3 — Define Mean Time Between Maintenance (MTBM).**
$MTBM$ represents the average elapsed time between successive maintenance events (corrective *or* preventive). It is the *operational* analogue of MTBF.

$$MTBM = \frac{Total\ Operating\ Time + Total\ Active\ Time}{Number\ of\ Maintenance\ Events}$$

**Step 4 — Define Mean Downtime (MDT).**
$MDT$ is the average duration the system is unavailable per maintenance event — *including delays*.

$$MDT = MTTR + MWT + ADT$$
where $MWT$ = Mean Waiting Time (logistics) and $ADT$ = Administrative Delay Time.

**Step 5 — Combine into Ao.**
The ratio $MTBM / (MTBM + MDT)$ yields a dimensionless probability between 0 and 1, often expressed as a percentage.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_o = \dfrac{MTBM}{MTBM + MDT}$ | Primary definition of Operational Availability | dimensionless (or %) |
| 2 | $A_o = \dfrac{Uptime}{Total\ Time}$ | Mission-time perspective availability | dimensionless |
| 3 | $MTBM = \dfrac{Total\ Calendar\ Time}{Number\ of\ Maintenance\ Actions}$ | Operational MTBF analogue | hours |
| 4 | $MDT = MTTR + MWT + ADT$ | Total mean downtime including logistics | hours |
| 5 | $U = 1 - A_o$ | Operational Unavailability | dimensionless |
| 6 | $A_o(t) = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} A(t)\,dt$ | Time-averaged availability | dimensionless |
| 7 | $D_o = MDT \cdot \lambda$ | Fractional downtime per unit time | dimensionless |
| 8 | $A_o = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR + MWT + ADT}$ | Expanded practical form | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for KTU Problems**:
> - $0 \le A_o \le 1$
> - As $MDT \to 0$, $A_o \to 1$ (ideal system).
> - As $MTBM \to 0$, $A_o \to 0$ (system perpetually down).
> - For purely corrective-maintenance systems: $A_o \to A_i$ (Inherent Availability).

### Why Operational Availability Matters in Engineering

- **Defense & Aerospace**: Mission-readiness depends on logistics pipelines, not just MTBF.
- **Manufacturing**: Production loss is calculated using Ao, not Ai.
- **Healthcare**: Hospital equipment must be available *when needed*, irrespective of failure mode.
- **Telecommunications**: Network downtime from any cause (planned or unplanned) drives SLA penalties.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — From First Principles (Time-Based Definition)

Consider a system observed over a calendar period $T$. During this period:
- The system experiences $N$ maintenance events.
- The total downtime incurred is $T_D$.
- The total uptime is therefore $T_U = T - T_D$.

The operational availability is defined as the proportion of calendar time the system is operationally available:

$$A_o = \frac{T_U}{T} = \frac{T - T_D}{T} = 1 - \frac{T_D}{T}$$

Now, $T_D$ can be expressed as the sum of downtimes over $N$ events:

$$T_D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{D,i}$$

If the *average* downtime per event is $MDT$, then:

$$T_D = N \cdot MDT$$

Substituting into the availability expression:

$$A_o = 1 - \frac{N \cdot MDT}{T}$$

But the average time between maintenance events is:

$$MTBM = \frac{T}{N} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad N = \frac{T}{MTBM}$$

Substituting this back:

$$A_o = 1 - \frac{(T/MTBM) \cdot MDT}{T} = 1 - \frac{MDT}{MTBM}$$

Rearranging to obtain the canonical form:

$$\boxed{A_o = \frac{MTBM}{MTBM + MDT}}$$

This is the derivation the KTU board expects when 14-mark derivations are asked.

### Derivation 2 — Decomposition of MDT into its Sub-Components

In real-world KTU numerical problems, MDT is often broken into three explicit components:

$$MDT = MTTR + MWT + ADT$$

where:
- $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair (active repair)
- $MWT$ = Mean Waiting Time (spares, technicians, transport)
- $ADT$ = Administrative Delay Time (approvals, paperwork)

Substituting:

$$A_o = \frac{MTBM}{MTBM + MTTR + MWT + ADT}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A radar system has the following data over one year (8760 hours):
- Number of failures: **4**
- Total repair time: **40 hours**
- Total waiting time for spares: **80 hours**
- Total administrative delay: **20 hours**
- Total preventive maintenance time: **60 hours**
- Number of preventive maintenance actions: **3**

**Calculate the Operational Availability.**

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute total maintenance events:

$$N = N_{failures} + N_{preventive} = 4 + 3 = 7$$

Step 2 — Compute total downtime:

$$T_D = 40 + 80 + 20 + 60 = 200\ hours$$

Step 3 — Compute MTBM:

$$MTBM = \frac{T}{N} = \frac{8760}{7} = 1251.43\ hours$$

Step 4 — Compute MDT:

$$MDT = \frac{T_D}{N} = \frac{200}{7} = 28.57\ hours$$

Step 5 — Compute Ao:

$$A_o = \frac{MTBM}{MTBM + MDT} = \frac{1251.43}{1251.43 + 28.57} = \frac{1251.43}{1280.00}$$

$$\boxed{A_o = 0.9777 \approx 97.77\%}$$

Step 6 — Verify using the alternate form:

$$A_o = 1 - \frac{T_D}{T} = 1 - \frac{200}{8760} = 1 - 0.0228 = 0.9772$$

A small rounding difference exists; the conceptual answer remains **Ao ≈ 97.7%**.

### Analytical Comparison Matrix — Ao vs. Other Availability Metrics

| Parameter | Inherent Availability $A_i$ | Achieved Availability $A_a$ | Operational Availability $A_o$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Downtime considered | Corrective only | Corrective + Active PM | All downtimes (logistics, admin, PM) |
| Uses MTBF? | Yes | Yes | No (uses MTBM) |
| Uses MTTR? | Yes | Yes | Yes (subset of MDT) |
| Includes logistics delays? | No | No | Yes |
| Realistic for field use? | Low | Medium | High |
| Typical denominator | Operating time | Operating time | Calendar time |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 — Operational Availability Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Calendar Time Window T] --> A1{Is System Operational?}
    A1 -- Yes --> A2[Uptime Bucket]
    A1 -- No --> A3[Downtime Bucket]
    A3 --> A4[Active Repair Time MTTR]
    A3 --> A5[Logistics Waiting MWT]
    A3 --> A6[Administrative Delay ADT]
    A3 --> A7[Preventive Maintenance Time]
    A2 --> A8[Sum Uptime]
    A4 --> A9[Sum Downtime T_D]
    A5 --> A9
    A6 --> A9
    A7 --> A9
    A8 --> A10[Ao = Uptime / Total Time]
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11[Operational Availability Output]
```

### Diagram 2 — Time Decomposition Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[Total Calendar Time T] --> U[Uptime T_U]
    T --> D[Downtime T_D]
    D --> D1[MTTR - Active Repair]
    D --> D2[MWT - Logistics Delay]
    D --> D3[ADT - Admin Delay]
    D --> D4[PMT - Preventive Maintenance]
    D1 --> MDT[MDT Mean Downtime]
    D2 --> MDT
    D3 --> MDT
    D4 --> MDT
    U --> MTBM[MTBM Mean Time Between Maintenance]
    MTBM --> AO[Ao = MTBM / MTBM + MDT]
    MDT --> AO
```

### Diagram 3 — Comparison Flow of Availability Metrics

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[System Time Record] --> B1{Which downtimes included?}
    B1 -- Only corrective MTTR --> Bi[Inherent Availability Ai]
    B1 -- Corrective plus active PM --> Ba[Achieved Availability Aa]
    B1 -- All downtimes logistics admin PM --> Bo[Operational Availability Ao]
    Bi --> Out[Output Availability Percentage]
    Ba --> Out
    Bo --> Out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip (KTU Exam)**: When asked to "draw the availability hierarchy," mark the *broadening* of the downtime base as you move from $A_i \to A_a \to A_o$. This visual progression is a frequently scored board question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define Operational Availability. How does it differ from Inherent Availability?

**Model Answer:**
Operational Availability ($A_o$) is the fraction of calendar time a system is operationally ready, defined as $A_o = \dfrac{MTBM}{MTBM + MDT}$.

While Inherent Availability ($A_i$) considers only **corrective maintenance** (uses MTBF and MTTR), Operational Availability accounts for **all downtimes**, including logistics delays, administrative delays, and preventive maintenance. It is calculated using **calendar time** as the denominator rather than operating time, making it more representative of real-world field conditions. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
State any two reasons why Operational Availability is always **less than** Inherent Availability for a practical system.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Logistics Delays (MWT):** Real systems experience waiting periods for spares, technicians, and transport, which increase downtime but are not counted in $A_i$.
2. **Administrative Delays (ADT):** Paperwork, approvals, and procedural hold-ups add non-repair downtime that does not exist in the ideal $A_i$ formulation.
Therefore $A_o \le A_i$ in all practical scenarios. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students use **MTBF instead of MTBM** when computing $A_o$. This is incorrect — operational availability demands MTBM.
> 2. Forgetting to include **PM downtime** in $T_D$ is a frequent 2-mark deduction.
> 3. Confusing **calendar time** with **operating time** as the denominator loses marks.
> 4. Always show the substitution step clearly; partial credit is heavily dependent on intermediate work.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Derive the operational availability formula $A_o = \dfrac{MTBM}{MTBM + MDT}$ starting from the basic time-based definition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diesel generator operates in a remote telecom site. Over 6 months (4380 hours), it experienced:
- 5 corrective failures with total repair time of **75 hours**
- Logistics waiting time of **45 hours**
- Administrative delay of **15 hours**
- 2 scheduled preventive maintenance actions lasting **20 hours each**
Calculate the Operational Availability of the generator. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Step 1 — Let $T$ = total observation time, $T_U$ = uptime, $T_D$ = downtime.

$$A_o = \frac{T_U}{T} = 1 - \frac{T_D}{T}$$

Step 2 — If $N$ maintenance events occur:

$$T_D = N \cdot MDT \quad \text{and} \quad MTBM = \frac{T}{N}$$

Step 3 — Substituting:

$$A_o = 1 - \frac{N \cdot MDT}{T} = 1 - \frac{MDT}{MTBM}$$

Step 4 — Simplifying:

$$\boxed{A_o = \frac{MTBM}{MTBM + MDT}} \quad \text{(7 marks awarded for derivation steps)}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Step 1 — Total maintenance events:

$$N = 5 + 2 = 7\ events$$

Step 2 — Total downtime:

$$T_D = 75 + 45 + 15 + (2 \times 20) = 75 + 45 + 15 + 40 = 175\ hours$$

Step 3 — MTBM:

$$MTBM = \frac{4380}{7} = 625.71\ hours$$

Step 4 — MDT:

$$MDT = \frac{175}{7} = 25\ hours$$

Step 5 — Operational Availability:

$$A_o = \frac{625.71}{625.71 + 25} = \frac{625.71}{650.71} = 0.9616$$

$$\boxed{A_o \approx 96.16\%} \quad \text{(7 marks)}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the concept of Operational Availability. Discuss with a suitable example how logistics and administrative delays affect $A_o$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A naval radar system has the following annual data:
- Calendar time observed: **8760 hours**
- Mean Time Between Maintenance (MTBM): **1500 hours**
- Mean Time To Repair (MTTR): **15 hours**
- Mean Waiting Time (MWT): **10 hours**
- Administrative Delay Time (ADT): **5 hours**

Calculate:
(i) Operational Availability.
(ii) Operational Unavailability in hours per year.
(iii) Compare the result with Inherent Availability assuming MTBF = 1600 hours. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Operational Availability is the *realistic* availability metric reflecting the proportion of calendar time a system is mission-capable. It incorporates **all sources of downtime**, not just active repairs. For example, in a remote military radar station, even if the Mean Time To Repair is excellent (say 4 hours), the system may spend 48 hours waiting for a replacement module from a distant depot. This logistics delay directly reduces operational availability even though the *technical* repair itself was fast. Similarly, administrative protocols for safety clearance before maintenance can add further non-productive downtime. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

(i) Operational Availability:

$$MDT = MTTR + MWT + ADT = 15 + 10 + 5 = 30\ hours$$

$$A_o = \frac{MTBM}{MTBM + MDT} = \frac{1500}{1500 + 30} = \frac{1500}{1530}$$

$$\boxed{A_o \approx 0.9804 \text{ or } 98.04\%}$$

(ii) Operational Unavailability:

$$U_o = 1 - A_o = 1 - 0.9804 = 0.0196$$

Annual downtime hours:

$$D_o = U_o \times T = 0.0196 \times 8760 = 171.7\ hours/year$$

(iii) Inherent Availability (for comparison):

$$A_i = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{1600}{1600 + 15} = \frac{1600}{1615}$$

$$\boxed{A_i \approx 0.9907 \text{ or } 99.07\%}$$

**Comparison:**
- $A_i = 99.07\%$ (only corrective maintenance)
- $A_o = 98.04\%$ (includes logistics and admin delays)
- The difference $\Delta A = 1.03\%$ represents the penalty paid in real-world operations. **(7 marks)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Operational Availability** uses **MTBM**, not MTBF — this is the most critical distinction.
- The denominator of $A_o$ is **calendar/mission time**, never operating time.
- $MDT$ aggregates **all** downtime categories: repair, logistics, admin, and preventive maintenance.
- $A_o \le A_i \le A_a$ is the typical ordering depending on which delays are included.
- Ao answers the question: *"When the user needed it, was it ready?"* — not just *"Was it technically reliable?"*
- Always express the final answer as a **decimal (0 to 1)** or a **percentage** — both are accepted in KTU valuation.
- For KTU derivations, the **time-based approach** (starting from $T_U/T$) is the canonical method.
- Watch for **trap questions** where $MTBF = MTBM$ is implied but multiple PM events exist — they are not equal.
- A high $A_o$ (≥ 95%) is the industry benchmark for mission-critical systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip**: When asked to "compare" availability metrics in a 7-mark sub-part, always end with a numerical percentage comparison — examiners allocate marks for the quantitative contrast, not just the conceptual explanation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Availability of Series and Parallel Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Availability of Series and Parallel Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is System Availability?

**Availability** is a probabilistic measure that expresses the degree to which a system is in an operable (working) state at a randomly chosen instant of time or over a defined mission period. It bridges the gap between the two extreme viewpoints of reliability (only failures matter) and maintainability (only repairs matter).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Steady-State Availability):**
> Steady-state availability $A$ is the limiting probability that a system will be operational at a randomly chosen instant in the long run, given it is repaired and put back into service after every failure.
>
> $$A = \lim_{t \to \infty} A(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$
>
> where $\lambda$ is the **failure rate**, $\mu$ is the **repair rate**, **MTBF** is the Mean Time Between Failures, and **MTTR** is the Mean Time To Repair.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hospital ICU Analogy"

Imagine a hospital ICU with two life-support machines (A and B) keeping a critical patient alive.

- **Series Configuration:** Suppose both machines are connected in a **single chain** — oxygen line into A, then output of A goes into B. The patient survives **only if BOTH machines work simultaneously**. If even one fails, the patient is at risk. This is a **series system**.
- **Parallel Configuration:** Now imagine two **independent** life-support machines connected to the patient at the same time. The patient survives as long as **at least one machine** is operational. Even if both fail, the patient dies; but if only one fails, the patient is still safe. This is a **parallel system (redundant)**.

The availability of each machine individually is governed by how often it fails ($\lambda$) and how fast it is repaired ($\mu$). When you combine machines, the **series combination gives the LOWEST availability** (because both must work), while the **parallel combination gives the HIGHEST availability** (because only one needs to work).

## 1.3 Three Levels of Availability

| Level | Mathematical Symbol | Meaning |
|-------|--------------------|---------| 
| Instantaneous Availability | $A(t)$ | Probability system is up at exact time $t$, starting up at $t=0$. |
| Steady-State Availability | $A$ or $A(\infty)$ | Long-run fraction of time the system is operational. |
| Average Availability over $[0,T]$ | $\bar{A}(T)$ | Total uptime divided by mission duration $T$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Golden Rule:**
> When the word "availability" is used **without** the qualifier "instantaneous" and the system has operated for a long time, the answer expected is the **steady-state availability** $A = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$.

## 1.4 The Single-Component Building Block

For a single repairable component, modeled as a two-state Markov chain (UP and DOWN), the transition diagram is:

$$\text{UP} \underset{\mu}{\overset{\lambda}{\rightleftarrows}} \text{DOWN}$$

In steady state, equating flow across the boundary gives:

$$P_{\text{UP}} \cdot \lambda = P_{\text{DOWN}} \cdot \mu$$

Since $P_{\text{UP}} + P_{\text{DOWN}} = 1$, we get:

$$P_{\text{UP}} = A = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}, \qquad P_{\text{DOWN}} = U = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}$$

where $U$ is **unavailability**. Note that $A + U = 1$ always.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Series System

In a **series configuration**, the system functions **if and only if every component** in the chain functions. There is **no redundancy**; the system is "weakest-link-dominated."

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. Identify component availability $A_i$ for each of $n$ components.
2. The probability that **component $i$ is UP** at a random instant is $A_i$.
3. The probability that **component $i$ is DOWN** at a random instant is $1 - A_i$.
4. Because components fail independently, the joint probability that **all components are UP** is the product of individual probabilities.
5. Therefore, system availability is the product of component availabilities.

### Core "Why" Behind the Series Formula
- **Why product?** Independence of component states at a random instant.
- **Why is $A_s \le \min(A_i)$?** Because multiplying numbers in $[0,1]$ always yields a result smaller than or equal to the smallest factor.
- **Engineering implication:** Adding a low-reliability component to a series chain **always** reduces overall availability.

## 2.2 Operational Logic of a Parallel System

In a **parallel (redundant) configuration**, the system functions as long as **at least one** component functions. The system fails only when **all** components fail simultaneously.

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. Compute unavailability of each component: $U_i = 1 - A_i$.
2. The system is in the failed state **only** when every component is failed.
3. Since failures are independent, $P(\text{all components DOWN}) = \prod U_i$.
4. Therefore, system availability = $1 - \prod U_i$.

### Core "Why" Behind the Parallel Formula
- **Why complement (1 minus)?** Because we compute failure first (easier — it's a single AND event) and then complement.
- **Why $A_p \ge \max(A_i)$?** Because $1 - \prod U_i$ is always at least as large as the largest $A_i$.
- **Engineering implication:** Adding redundant components to a parallel chain **always** improves availability, but with diminishing returns.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` in tables below** to keep Markdown tables safe.

| # | Configuration | Steady-State Availability Formula | Unavailability Formula |
|---|---------------|-----------------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Single component | $A = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda+\mu}$ | $U = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda+\mu}$ |
| 2 | Series of $n$ components | $A_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{\mu_i}{\lambda_i+\mu_i}$ | $U_s = 1 - A_s$ |
| 3 | Parallel of $n$ components | $A_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 - \dfrac{\mu_i}{\lambda_i+\mu_i}\right)$ | $U_p = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{\lambda_i}{\lambda_i+\mu_i}$ |
| 4 | Instantaneous availability | $A(t) = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda+\mu} + \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda+\mu} e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t}$ | — |
| 5 | Average availability over $T$ | $\bar{A}(T) = \dfrac{1}{T}\left[\dfrac{\mu T}{\lambda+\mu} + \dfrac{\lambda}{(\lambda+\mu)^2}\left(1-e^{-(\lambda+\mu)T}\right)\right]$ | — |
| 6 | Series (equal components) | $A_s = A^n$ | — |
| 7 | Parallel (equal components, 2) | $A_p = 2A - A^2$ | — |
| 8 | Parallel (equal components, 3) | $A_p = 3A - 3A^2 + A^3$ | — |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Configuration Used | Reason |
|-------------|--------------------|--------|
| **Aircraft engine twin-jet** | Parallel (active) | Safety-of-flight, must keep one engine running. |
| **Power grid transmission line** | Series | Each tower/segment must conduct, no redundancy. |
| **Data center cooling pump array** | Parallel (with N+1) | One pump can fail without overheating servers. |
| **Pipeline** | Series | Leak or blockage anywhere breaks the line. |
| **Brake-by-wire ECU** | Series | All sensors and actuators must respond correctly. |
| **Spacecraft computer** | Parallel (triple modular redundant) | One fault must not cause mission loss. |

## 2.5 Series vs Parallel — The Trade-off Triangle

> [!NOTE]
> **Reliability ↔ Cost ↔ Availability Trade-off:**
> - **Series** is *cheap* but *low availability* — good for non-critical links.
> - **Parallel** is *expensive* (extra hardware) but *high availability* — good for safety-critical links.
> - Real systems are typically a **combination (complex / bridge / k-out-of-n)** that balances both.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Availability of a Single Repairable Component

We model the component as a two-state continuous-time Markov chain with states UP (1) and DOWN (0).

**Step 1 — Write the balance (steady-flow) equations.**

In steady state, the probability of leaving state UP equals the probability of entering state UP:

$$\pi_1 \lambda = \pi_0 \mu$$

**Step 2 — Apply the normalization condition.**

Because the component must be in one of the two states at any instant:

$$\pi_0 + \pi_1 = 1$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system of linear equations.**

From Step 1: $\pi_0 = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu} \pi_1$. Substituting into Step 2:

$$\frac{\lambda}{\mu} \pi_1 + \pi_1 = 1$$

$$\pi_1 \left(\frac{\lambda}{\mu} + 1\right) = 1$$

$$\pi_1 \left(\frac{\lambda + \mu}{\mu}\right) = 1$$

$$\pi_1 = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Step 4 — Identify availability.**

The system is operational in state 1, so:

$$A = \pi_1 = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

> **Reasoning Logic:** This formula tells us availability improves as $\mu$ grows (faster repairs) and degrades as $\lambda$ grows (more frequent failures). Numerically, if $\lambda = 0.001$/hr and $\mu = 0.1$/hr, then $A = 0.1/0.101 = 0.9901$, i.e., 99.01% available.

## 3.2 Derivation: Instantaneous Availability $A(t)$

**Step 1 — Write the differential equations from the Markov chain.**

Let $P_1(t)$ = probability component is UP at time $t$. Then:

$$\frac{dP_1}{dt} = -\lambda P_1 + \mu (1 - P_1)$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange.**

$$\frac{dP_1}{dt} + (\lambda + \mu) P_1 = \mu$$

**Step 3 — Solve the first-order linear ODE.**

The integrating factor is $e^{(\lambda+\mu)t}$. Multiplying:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[P_1(t) e^{(\lambda+\mu)t}\right] = \mu e^{(\lambda+\mu)t}$$

Integrating both sides:

$$P_1(t) e^{(\lambda+\mu)t} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda+\mu} e^{(\lambda+\mu)t} + C$$

**Step 4 — Apply initial condition $P_1(0) = 1$ (component starts UP).**

At $t = 0$: $\quad 1 = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda+\mu} + C \implies C = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda+\mu}$

**Step 5 — Final expression.**

$$A(t) = P_1(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda+\mu} + \frac{\lambda}{\lambda+\mu} e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t}$$

> **Reasoning Logic:** The first term is the steady-state availability, and the second term is a transient that decays exponentially. As $t \to \infty$, $A(t) \to \mu/(\lambda+\mu)$, confirming that instantaneous availability converges to steady-state availability.

## 3.3 Derivation: Availability of a Series System of $n$ Components

**Step 1 — State the operational rule.**

Series system $S_s$ is UP if and only if all components $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ are UP simultaneously.

$$A_s = P\left(\bigcap_{i=1}^{n} \text{Component}_i \text{ is UP}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Apply independence.**

Components are assumed to fail and be repaired independently of one another:

$$A_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(\text{Component}_i \text{ is UP}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the single-component formula.**

$$A_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\mu_i}{\lambda_i + \mu_i}$$

**Step 4 — Special case (identical components).**

If all $n$ components have the same $\lambda$ and $\mu$:

$$A_s = \left(\frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}\right)^n = A^n$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Availability of a Parallel System of $n$ Components

**Step 1 — State the operational rule.**

Parallel system $S_p$ is UP if at least one component is UP. Equivalently, $S_p$ is DOWN only if all components are DOWN.

$$A_p = 1 - P\left(\bigcap_{i=1}^{n} \text{Component}_i \text{ is DOWN}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Apply independence of unavailability events.**

$$A_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(\text{Component}_i \text{ is DOWN}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} U_i$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $U_i = 1 - A_i = \dfrac{\lambda_i}{\lambda_i + \mu_i}$.**

$$A_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\lambda_i}{\lambda_i + \mu_i}$$

**Step 4 — Special case for $n = 2$ (identical components).**

$$A_p = 1 - \left(\frac{\lambda}{\lambda+\mu}\right)^2 = 1 - (1-A)^2 = 2A - A^2$$

**Step 5 — Special case for $n = 3$ (identical components).**

$$A_p = 1 - (1-A)^3 = 3A - 3A^2 + A^3$$

> **Reasoning Logic:** These polynomial expansions come from the binomial theorem. Notice that the parallel availability is *always* greater than $A$ (the individual component availability), but the marginal gain from adding more redundant components shrinks rapidly (diminishing returns).

## 3.5 Worked Example 1 — Series System Calculation

**Problem:** Three components are connected in series. Their failure rates and repair rates are:
- Component 1: $\lambda_1 = 0.005$/hr, $\mu_1 = 0.5$/hr
- Component 2: $\lambda_2 = 0.002$/hr, $\mu_2 = 0.4$/hr
- Component 3: $\lambda_3 = 0.003$/hr, $\mu_3 = 0.6$/hr

**Compute the steady-state availability of the system.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute individual availabilities.**

$$A_1 = \frac{\mu_1}{\lambda_1 + \mu_1} = \frac{0.5}{0.005 + 0.5} = \frac{0.5}{0.505} = 0.99010$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\mu_2}{\lambda_2 + \mu_2} = \frac{0.4}{0.002 + 0.4} = \frac{0.4}{0.402} = 0.99502$$

$$A_3 = \frac{\mu_3}{\lambda_3 + \mu_3} = \frac{0.6}{0.003 + 0.6} = \frac{0.6}{0.603} = 0.99502$$

**Step 2 — Multiply for series system.**

$$A_s = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 = 0.99010 \times 0.99502 \times 0.99502$$

**Step 3 — Sequential multiplication.**

$$0.99010 \times 0.99502 = 0.98517$$

$$0.98517 \times 0.99502 = 0.98021$$

**Step 4 — Final answer.**

$$\boxed{A_s = 0.98021 \approx 98.02\%}$$

> **Interpretation:** Although every individual component has availability above 99%, the **series chain** drops overall availability to about 98% — a classic KTU exam demonstration of how weakest-link dominates.

## 3.6 Worked Example 2 — Parallel System Calculation

**Problem:** Two identical pumps operate in parallel. Each pump has $\lambda = 0.01$/hr and $\mu = 0.2$/hr. Compute the steady-state availability of the pumping system.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Individual availability.**

$$A = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{0.2}{0.01 + 0.2} = \frac{0.2}{0.21} = 0.95238$$

**Step 2 — Individual unavailability.**

$$U = 1 - A = 0.04762 = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda+\mu} = \frac{0.01}{0.21}$$

**Step 3 — Parallel system availability (system DOWN only if BOTH pumps DOWN).**

$$A_p = 1 - U \cdot U = 1 - (0.04762)^2 = 1 - 0.002268 = 0.99773$$

**Step 4 — Final answer.**

$$\boxed{A_p = 0.99773 \approx 99.77\%}$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check using the equivalent form.**

$$A_p = 2A - A^2 = 2(0.95238) - (0.95238)^2 = 1.90476 - 0.90703 = 0.99773 \checkmark$$

> **Interpretation:** A single pump is 95.24% available, but two in parallel push system availability to **99.77%** — a remarkable improvement for the cost of one extra pump.

## 3.7 Worked Example 3 — Series-Parallel Combination

**Problem:** A system consists of two blocks $X$ and $Y$ in **series**. Block $X$ has two components in parallel ($X_1, X_2$), and block $Y$ has three components in series ($Y_1, Y_2, Y_3$). All components are identical with $\lambda = 0.002$/hr and $\mu = 0.5$/hr. Find system availability.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Component availability.**

$$A = \frac{0.5}{0.002 + 0.5} = \frac{0.5}{0.502} = 0.99602$$

**Step 2 — Block $X$ availability (parallel of two).**

$$A_X = 2A - A^2 = 2(0.99602) - (0.99602)^2 = 1.99204 - 0.99206 = 0.99998$$

Wait — let's recompute precisely: $(0.99602)^2 = 0.99206$, so $A_X = 1.99204 - 0.99206 = 0.99998$.

**Step 3 — Block $Y$ availability (series of three).**

$$A_Y = A^3 = (0.99602)^3$$

Compute step by step:

$$A^2 = 0.99206$$

$$A^3 = 0.99206 \times 0.99602 = 0.98811$$

**Step 4 — Overall system availability (X and Y in series).**

$$A_{\text{sys}} = A_X \cdot A_Y = 0.99998 \times 0.98811$$

$$A_{\text{sys}} = 0.98809$$

**Step 5 — Final answer.**

$$\boxed{A_{\text{sys}} \approx 0.9881 \approx 98.81\%}$$

> **Interpretation:** The parallel block $X$ contributes almost perfect availability (99.998%), while block $Y$ drags overall system availability down to 98.81% — engineers should now focus improvement efforts on block $Y$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Series System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[System Input] --> UNIT1[Component 1\nLambda 1 Mu 1]
    UNIT1 --> UNIT2[Component 2\nLambda 2 Mu 2]
    UNIT2 --> UNIT3[Component 3\nLambda 3 Mu 3]
    UNIT3 --> UNITN[Component N\nLambda N Mu N]
    UNITN --> OUTPUT[System Output]
    GATE1{Component 1 UP} -.->|NO| FAIL[System Fails]
    GATE2{Component 2 UP} -.->|NO| FAIL
    GATE3{Component 3 UP} -.->|NO| FAIL
    GATEN{Component N UP} -.->|NO| FAIL
```

> **Reading guide:** The horizontal chain represents the only path through which input becomes output. If any single block fails, the chain breaks and the system goes to the FAILED state. This visualizes why availability is multiplicative.

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Parallel System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[System Input] --> SPLIT{Active Splitter}
    SPLIT --> PATH1[Path 1\nComponent 1\nLambda 1 Mu 1]
    SPLIT --> PATH2[Path 2\nComponent 2\nLambda 2 Mu 2]
    SPLIT --> PATH3[Path 3\nComponent 3\nLambda 3 Mu 3]
    SPLIT --> PATHN[Path N\nComponent N\nLambda N Mu N]
    PATH1 --> COMBINE{Combiner}
    PATH2 --> COMBINE
    PATH3 --> COMBINE
    PATHN --> COMBINE
    COMBINE --> OUTPUT[System Output]
    CHECK1{All N paths DOWN} -.->|YES| FAIL2[System Fails]
```

> **Reading guide:** The system is split into $N$ parallel branches. The system output remains alive as long as at least one branch is alive. Only when **all** branches are simultaneously down does the system fail.

## 4.3 Block-Level Analytical Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Parameters\nLambda and Mu for each component] --> B{System Type}
    B -->|Series| C[Compute Individual A_i]
    B -->|Parallel| D[Compute Individual U_i]
    C --> E[Multiply A_i across components\nA_series equals product of A_i]
    D --> F[Multiply U_i across components\nU_parallel equals product of U_i]
    F --> G[Apply Complement\nA_parallel equals 1 minus U_parallel]
    E --> H[Final Availability Result]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compare and Conclude\nSeries Low, Parallel High]
```

> **Reading guide:** This is the algorithmic flow you should follow on the KTU exam — first classify the system, then choose the right branch of formulas, then combine. Memorizing this decision tree saves valuable time in the 14-mark question.

## 4.4 Markov-State Transition Diagram — Single Component (Foundation Concept)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> UP_STATE
    UP_STATE --> DOWN_STATE : Failure occurs at rate Lambda
    DOWN_STATE --> UP_STATE : Repair completes at rate Mu
    UP_STATE --> UP_STATE : Continues working
    DOWN_STATE --> DOWN_STATE : Continues failed
```

> **Reading guide:** Every availability calculation ultimately derives from this two-state Markov chain. Series and parallel systems are merely higher-level compositions of these chains. Master this first and the rest becomes straightforward.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q:** Define steady-state availability of a repairable system. Show that for a single repairable component with constant failure rate $\lambda$ and repair rate $\mu$, the steady-state availability is given by $A = \mu/(\lambda+\mu)$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Steady-state availability is defined as the limiting value of the probability that a system will be operational at a randomly chosen instant of time as time tends to infinity. Mathematically:
>
> $$A = \lim_{t \to \infty} A(t)$$

> **Derivation (1.5 marks):** Modeling the component as a two-state Markov chain with UP and DOWN states, the steady-flow balance gives $\pi_{\text{UP}} \lambda = \pi_{\text{DOWN}} \mu$. Combined with $\pi_{\text{UP}} + \pi_{\text{DOWN}} = 1$, solving yields:
>
> $$\pi_{\text{UP}} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$
>
> Since the system is operational in the UP state, $A = \pi_{\text{UP}} = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda+\mu}$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q:** Distinguish between availability and reliability. Why is availability a more meaningful metric for repairable systems?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Reliability (1 mark):** Reliability $R(t)$ is the probability that a system performs its intended function **without failure** under stated conditions for a specified time interval $[0, t]$. It is **non-repairable-oriented** and always a **decreasing** function of $t$.

> **Availability (1 mark):** Availability $A(t)$ is the probability that a system is **operational at a given instant**, regardless of whether it has failed and been repaired in the past. It applies to **repairable** systems and reaches a **steady-state** value in the long run.

> **Why more meaningful (1 mark):** Most real engineering systems (aircraft, power plants, computers) are **repaired after failure**, not discarded. Therefore, their long-term performance is better captured by availability, which considers the **interplay of failures AND repairs**, rather than by reliability alone.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Adapted]

**A system consists of three components A, B, and C arranged in series. Each component is repairable with constant failure rate and repair rate as given below:**

| Component | $\lambda$ (per hour) | $\mu$ (per hour) |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------------|
| A         | 0.005                | 0.50             |
| B         | 0.004                | 0.40             |
| C         | 0.003                | 0.60             |

**(a)** Determine the steady-state availability of each component and of the overall series system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The maintenance engineer proposes adding a redundant component B' identical to B in **parallel** with B. Compute the new system availability and discuss the improvement. **(7 marks)**

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Series System Availability (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute individual component availabilities.**

$$A_A = \frac{\mu_A}{\lambda_A + \mu_A} = \frac{0.50}{0.005 + 0.50} = \frac{0.50}{0.505} = 0.99010$$

$$A_B = \frac{\mu_B}{\lambda_B + \mu_B} = \frac{0.40}{0.004 + 0.40} = \frac{0.40}{0.404} = 0.99010$$

$$A_C = \frac{\mu_C}{\lambda_C + \mu_C} = \frac{0.60}{0.003 + 0.60} = \frac{0.60}{0.603} = 0.99502$$

**Step 2 — Compute series system availability.**

For a series system, all components must function:

$$A_{\text{series}} = A_A \cdot A_B \cdot A_C$$

$$A_{\text{series}} = 0.99010 \times 0.99010 \times 0.99502$$

**Step 3 — Sequential multiplication.**

$$0.99010 \times 0.99010 = 0.98030$$

$$0.98030 \times 0.99502 = 0.97541$$

**Step 4 — Final answer for (a).**

$$\boxed{A_{\text{series}} = 0.97541 \approx 97.54\%}$$

#### Part (b) — Adding Redundant Component in Parallel (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the new configuration.**

Components A and C remain in series. The block containing B now has B and B' in **parallel**.

**Step 2 — Compute availability of B-B' parallel block.**

Since B and B' are identical:

$$A_{BB'} = 1 - (1 - A_B)^2 = 2A_B - A_B^2$$

$$A_{BB'} = 2(0.99010) - (0.99010)^2 = 1.98020 - 0.98030 = 0.99990$$

**Step 3 — Compute new system availability (A, BB' block, C in series).**

$$A_{\text{new}} = A_A \cdot A_{BB'} \cdot A_C$$

$$A_{\text{new}} = 0.99010 \times 0.99990 \times 0.99502$$

**Step 4 — Sequential multiplication.**

$$0.99010 \times 0.99990 = 0.99000$$

$$0.99000 \times 0.99502 = 0.98507$$

**Step 5 — Final answer for (b).**

$$\boxed{A_{\text{new}} = 0.98507 \approx 98.51\%}$$

**Step 6 — Discussion.**

Improvement in availability:

$$\Delta A = A_{\text{new}} - A_{\text{series}} = 0.98507 - 0.97541 = 0.00966$$

$$\text{Relative improvement} = \frac{0.00966}{0.97541} \times 100\% \approx 0.99\%$$

**Conclusion:** Adding one redundant component B' (cost = extra hardware) raised system availability from 97.54% to 98.51%, an absolute gain of 0.97%. While the parallel block by itself became 99.99% available, the bottleneck shifted to components A and C. To gain more, A and C would also need redundancy.

---

### QUESTION B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Adapted]

**A computer server system has two identical processors operating in parallel. Each processor has failure rate $\lambda = 0.0001$ per hour and repair rate $\mu = 0.05$ per hour. The system is considered "available" as long as at least one processor is working.**

**(a)** Compute the steady-state availability and unavailability of a single processor and of the parallel system. Compare with the case where only one processor would have been used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the mission time is $T = 1000$ hours, determine the average availability over this mission period and the expected number of hours the parallel system is down. **(7 marks)**

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Steady-State Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Single processor availability.**

$$A = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{0.05}{0.0001 + 0.05} = \frac{0.05}{0.0501} = 0.99800$$

**Step 2 — Single processor unavailability.**

$$U = 1 - A = 0.00200$$

Or directly: $U = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda+\mu} = \dfrac{0.0001}{0.0501} = 0.00200 \checkmark$

**Step 3 — Parallel system availability.**

System fails only if both processors fail:

$$A_p = 1 - U \cdot U = 1 - (0.00200)^2 = 1 - 0.00000400 = 0.999996$$

Using equivalent form:

$$A_p = 2A - A^2 = 2(0.99800) - (0.99800)^2 = 1.99600 - 0.99600 = 1.00000$$

Wait — recheck: $(0.99800)^2 = 0.996004$, so $A_p = 1.99600 - 0.99600 = 1.00000$? That cannot be exactly 1. The correct value via the complement is:

$$A_p = 1 - (0.00200)^2 = 1 - 0.000004 = 0.999996$$

**Step 4 — Parallel system unavailability.**

$$U_p = 1 - A_p = 0.000004 = 4 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison.**

| Configuration | Availability | Unavailability |
|---------------|--------------|----------------|
| Single processor | 0.99800 (99.800%) | 0.00200 (0.200%) |
| Two in parallel | 0.999996 (99.9996%) | 0.000004 (0.0004%) |

$$\boxed{A_p = 0.999996, \quad U_p = 4 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Interpretation:** Parallel redundancy reduces unavailability by a factor of **500×** — a stunning improvement at the cost of one extra processor.

#### Part (b) — Average Availability and Downtime Over $T = 1000$ hours (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Instantaneous availability formula.**

$$A(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda+\mu} + \frac{\lambda}{\lambda+\mu} e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t}$$

With $\lambda + \mu = 0.0501$/hr:

$$A(t) = 0.99800 + 0.00200 \cdot e^{-0.0501 t}$$

**Step 2 — Average availability formula.**

$$\bar{A}(T) = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T A(t)\, dt$$

$$= \frac{1}{T}\left[\frac{\mu T}{\lambda+\mu} + \frac{\lambda}{\lambda+\mu} \cdot \frac{1-e^{-(\lambda+\mu)T}}{\lambda+\mu}\right]$$

$$= 0.99800 + \frac{0.00200}{0.0501 \cdot T}\left(1 - e^{-0.0501 T}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $T = 1000$ hours.**

Compute $0.0501 \times 1000 = 50.1$, so $e^{-50.1} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-22} \approx 0$ (effectively zero for engineering purposes).

$$\frac{0.00200}{0.0501 \times 1000} = \frac{0.00200}{50.1} = 3.992 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\bar{A}(1000) \approx 0.99800 + 3.992 \times 10^{-5} \times (1 - 0) = 0.99800 + 3.992 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\bar{A}(1000) \approx 0.99804$$

$$\boxed{\bar{A}(1000) \approx 0.99804 \approx 99.804\%}$$

**Step 4 — Expected downtime over 1000 hours.**

$$T_{\text{down}} = (1 - \bar{A}) \times T = (1 - 0.99804) \times 1000 = 0.00196 \times 1000 = 1.96 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with parallel configuration over same period.**

For parallel system: $U_p = 4 \times 10^{-6}$, so down time $\approx 1000 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} = 0.004$ hours $\approx 14.4$ seconds.

$$\boxed{T_{\text{down, single}} \approx 1.96 \text{ hours}, \qquad T_{\text{down, parallel}} \approx 0.004 \text{ hours}}$$

**Conclusion:** Over a 1000-hour mission, the single processor is expected to be down for almost 2 hours, whereas the parallel system is down for only about 14 seconds — a 490× reduction in downtime.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the failure rate units.** Students often quote $\lambda$ in failures per *year* but compute time in *hours*. Always normalize units before plugging into formulas. KTU examiners will not give partial credit if your units mismatch.
>
> 2. **Mixing up series vs parallel formulas.** Series uses **multiplication of availabilities** $A_s = \prod A_i$. Parallel uses **multiplication of unavailabilities followed by complement** $A_p = 1 - \prod U_i$. Mixing these is the single most common error and will yield zero credit even if the rest of the work is correct.
>
> 3. **Using reliability formulas ($R = e^{-\lambda t}$) for availability questions.** These are different concepts. Availability includes repair; reliability does not. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 4. **Rounding too early.** If $A_1 = 0.990099$ and $A_2 = 0.995025$, do **not** round to 0.990 and 0.995 before multiplying — keep at least 5 significant digits, then round the final answer.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to mention "steady-state" or "long-run"** in the conclusion. The KTU answer key looks for the qualifier, especially when the question says "availability" without specifying time.
>
> 6. **Not labelling the operational rule.** State explicitly: "System is UP if and only if..." for series, and "System is UP if at least one..." for parallel. Examiners award 1–2 marks for this logical clarity.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> **Important Things to Remember (Quick-Fire Revision)**

- **Availability** = probability system is operational at a random instant; applies to **repairable** systems.
- **Reliability** = probability of no failure over $[0, t]$; applies to **non-repairable** systems (until first failure).
- **Single-component steady-state availability:** $A = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$.
- **Single-component unavailability:** $U = 1 - A = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}$.
- **Series system:** $A_s = \prod A_i$ — multiplication of availabilities.
- **Parallel system:** $A_p = 1 - \prod U_i$ — multiplication of unavailabilities, then complement.
- **Series special case** (identical): $A_s = A^n$.
- **Parallel special case** (identical, 2): $A_p = 2A - A^2$.
- **Parallel special case** (identical, 3): $A_p = 3A - 3A^2 + A^3$.
- **Instantaneous availability:** $A(t) = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda+\mu} + \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda+\mu} e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t}$.
- **Average availability:** $\bar{A}(T) = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T A(t)\, dt$.
- **Engineering rule:** Series *reduces* availability (weakest-link). Parallel *increases* availability (redundancy).
- **Diminishing returns:** Each additional redundant component in parallel provides smaller incremental availability gain.
- **Decision rule for "at least $k$ out of $n$"**: This is **k-out-of-n redundancy**, a generalization of series ($k=n$) and parallel ($k=1$).
- **Always state assumptions:** independent failures, exponential failure and repair distributions, single repair crew per failed component.
- **Formula memory aid:** "*Series wants them all up, parallel wants none down*."
- **Numerical sanity check:** For any series system, $A_s < \min(A_i)$. For any parallel system, $A_p > \max(A_i)$. If your answer violates either, you have made an error.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maintenance: Preventive, Predictive, and Reliability-Centered Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maintenance: Preventive, Predictive, and Reliability-Centered Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Maintenance** in reliability engineering is defined as the combination of all technical, administrative, and managerial actions intended to retain an item in, or restore it to, a state in which it can perform its required function. Under the KTU 2024 scheme framework, maintenance is treated as a **decision-science discipline** that bridges reliability theory with operational economics.

Within Module 4, the three dominant modern maintenance philosophies addressed are:

1. **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** — Schedule-driven, time-based interventions performed to reduce the probability of failure or degradation of a functioning item.
2. **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** — Condition-based interventions triggered by real-time or near-real-time monitoring of physical parameters (vibration, temperature, acoustics, lubrication, etc.).
3. **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)** — A systematic engineering framework used to determine the *optimal* maintenance requirement of any physical asset in its operating context, based on its failure modes, failure consequences, and operational criticality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The distinguishing factor in board examinations is that students must articulate *why* a strategy is chosen for a given asset, not merely *what* it is. RCM is always presented as the **decision umbrella** that selects between PM and PdM (or Run-to-Failure) for each individual failure mode.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you own a **car**.

- **Preventive Maintenance** is like changing your engine oil every 5,000 km, regardless of how the engine *feels*. You trust the schedule, not the symptoms.
- **Predictive Maintenance** is like installing a vibration sensor on the engine. When the sensor detects abnormal frequencies, you open the hood *only when needed*. The schedule is replaced by symptoms.
- **Reliability-Centered Maintenance** is like sitting down with an automotive engineer, listing every possible way the car could fail (brake fade, alternator burnout, tire blowout, ECU glitch), assessing how each failure affects your safety and wallet, and then deciding — for *each* failure mode — whether to schedule it, monitor it, or simply drive until it breaks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core takeaway for students:** PM and PdM are *tools*; RCM is the *engineering methodology* that picks the right tool for every part of the system.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are **bold-flagged** because they appear in nearly every numerical KTU question on this module:

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Average time between consecutive failures of a repairable system. Unit: hours.
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** Average time to failure for a non-repairable item. Unit: hours.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** Average time required to restore a failed system. Unit: hours.
- **Availability (A):** Fraction of time the system is functionally operational. Dimensionless (0 ≤ A ≤ 1).
- **Failure Rate (λ):** Instantaneous rate of failure. Unit: failures per hour.
- **Criticality:** A weighted ranking of failure consequence severity.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Exam readiness cue:** A correct maintenance answer in a KTU valuation will *always* connect the chosen strategy to a quantitative reliability metric (MTBF ↑, MTTR ↓, Availability ↑).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Preventive Maintenance (PM) — The Scheduled Strategy

### 2.1.1 Concept and Logic Flow

PM is **time-driven** (or usage-driven: km, cycles, hours). It assumes degradation follows a predictable curve, hence the **bathtub-curve wear-out zone** is where PM provides maximum economic return.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Establish interval (T):** Choose a maintenance interval (Tₚₘ) typically based on manufacturer's recommendation, historical MTBF, or a fraction of design life.
- **Step 2 — Scheduled shutdown:** Take the system offline (or to reduced duty) at every multiple of Tₚₘ.
- **Step 3 — Inspection / Replacement:** Conduct visual checks, lubrication, calibration, and replacement of wear-prone components.
- **Step 4 — Log and adjust:** Record findings; if failures cluster, **shorten** Tₚₘ; if no defects appear, **lengthen** it (opportunity for RCM optimization).
- **Step 5 — Cost trade-off:** Compare cost of PM (labor + parts + downtime) vs. cost of unplanned failure (production loss + emergency repair).

### 2.1.2 Why PM Works — The "Why" Behind the Logic

PM attacks the **wear-out failures** of the bathtub curve before the failure rate λ(t) becomes steep. By replacing components at scheduled intervals, the system is kept permanently in the **Useful Life region** of the bathtub curve, where λ(t) ≈ constant and minimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary condition for KTU problems:** PM is only economically rational in the **wear-out region** of the bathtub curve. Performing PM in the infant-mortality region wastes money; performing it in the random-failure region offers no statistical benefit.

## 2.2 Predictive Maintenance (PdM) — The Condition-Driven Strategy

### 2.2.1 Concept and Logic Flow

PdM is **condition-driven**. Instead of acting on a calendar, the system is monitored continuously or periodically. When monitored parameters cross pre-set thresholds, an alarm triggers maintenance.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Sensor deployment:** Install sensors for vibration (accelerometers), temperature (thermocouples/IR), acoustic emission, oil debris, electrical current, ultrasonic, or visual (thermal imaging).
- **Step 2 — Baseline establishment:** Collect data during the system's known-healthy state. This baseline becomes the "fingerprint" of normal operation.
- **Step 3 — Continuous / periodic sampling:** Acquire data and extract diagnostic features (RMS vibration, peak-to-peak, kurtosis, crest factor, spectral harmonics).
- **Step 4 — Trend analysis and thresholding:** Apply statistical process control (SPC) or AI-based pattern recognition to detect deviations from baseline.
- **Step 5 — Triggered intervention:** Maintenance is performed **only when the trend indicates incipient failure**, not on a fixed schedule.

### 2.2.2 Why PdM Works — The "How" Behind the Logic

PdM exploits the fact that **physical degradation is a precursor to functional failure**. For example, a bearing does not fail instantly; its vibration spectrum progressively develops sideband frequencies around the running frequency as spalling develops. Detecting this signature **weeks before** catastrophic failure gives the maintenance team a planning window.

## 2.3 Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM) — The Decision Framework

RCM (developed in the late 1960s for the aviation industry, formalized in SAE JA1011 / SAE JA1012) is a **process**, not a maintenance type. It answers the question:

> *"What is the optimal maintenance requirement for each asset, given its operating context and the consequences of its failure?"*

### 2.3.1 The Seven RCM Questions (KTU-Favorite)

For every failure mode identified, RCM asks the following seven questions:

1. What is the function of the asset and its associated performance standards?
2. In what ways can it fail to fulfill its function?
3. What causes each functional failure?
4. What happens when each failure occurs?
5. In what way does each failure matter (safety, environmental, operational, economic)?
6. What can be done to prevent or predict each failure?
7. What should be done if a suitable preventive task cannot be found?

### 2.3.2 The RCM Decision Logic

Based on the answers, the maintenance planner selects one of four actions:

- **A — Scheduled Restoration (PM):** Overhaul/replace at fixed interval.
- **B — Scheduled Discard (PM):** Discard at end of useful life.
- **C — On-Condition (PdM):** Monitor and intervene when threshold exceeded.
- **D — Run-to-Failure (No scheduled task):** Accept failure; design redundancy or quick-change capability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU high-yield fact:** RCM is the only methodology that explicitly authorizes a *no-maintenance* decision (Run-to-Failure) when economic and safety analysis justifies it. PM and PdM are not permitted to skip maintenance.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric / Formula | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Steady-state Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless | Repairable system uptime fraction |
| 2 | Inherent Availability | $A_i = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + \bar{M}_ct}$ where $\bar{M}_{ct}$ is mean corrective time | dimensionless | Excludes logistics/administrative delay |
| 3 | Operational Availability | $A_o = \dfrac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime}$ | dimensionless | Includes all real-world delays |
| 4 | Failure Rate (exponential) | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | failures/hour | Constant-failure-rate region |
| 5 | Reliability (exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | Probability of survival to time t |
| 6 | Unreliability | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | Cumulative failure probability |
| 7 | PDF of failure | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | per hour | Probability density at age t |
| 8 | PM cost optimum (Barber-Larmour) | $T_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_p}{\lambda_d (C_f - C_p)}}$ | hours | Optimum PM interval minimizing total cost |
| 9 | Downtime per cycle | $T_d = T_p + \dfrac{T_c}{\lambda T} \cdot C_f$ (scaled) | hours | Average downtime expectation per cycle |
| 10 | Cost per hour optimum | $C_{min} = \sqrt{2 C_p \lambda_d (C_f - C_p)}$ | currency/hour | Minimum achievable maintenance cost rate |

Where:
- $C_p$ = cost of preventive maintenance action
- $C_f$ = cost of failure (corrective action)
- $\lambda_d$ = degradation rate of the component
- $T$ = PM interval
- $T_c$ = cycle time / mean downtime for corrective action

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU, formula 8 and 9 appear most frequently in 14-mark derivation questions.** Students are expected to know the cost trade-off rationale.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation:** RCM originated here; every commercial aircraft uses an MSG-3-derived RCM program. PM intervals are legally mandated; PdM is used for engines (vibration monitoring).
- **Power generation:** PdM monitors turbine vibration and generator stator temperatures; RCM determines which sub-systems are inspected weekly versus annually.
- **Manufacturing:** Industry 4.0 factories use PdM sensors on CNC spindles, robots, and conveyors, with RCM logic dictating the inspection database.
- **Oil & Gas:** RCM classifies safety-critical valves (e.g., ESD valves) as PM-mandatory and non-critical bearings as PdM-monitored.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Strategy for marks:** When a KTU question asks to "compare PM and PdM", always present a 2-column table with at least 6 comparison axes: trigger, cost, downtime, applicability region of bathtub curve, data requirement, and risk profile.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Availability of a Repairable System

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A repairable system is observed over a very long period. It alternates between **uptime** and **downtime**. Uptime is the average operating period between failures (i.e., MTBF). Downtime is the average repair duration (i.e., MTTR). Derive the long-run fraction of time the system is operational.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define a single duty cycle.**
A duty cycle consists of one full operational period followed by one full repair period.

$$
\text{Cycle length} = MTBF + MTTR
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the "good" portion of the cycle.**
The portion of the cycle during which the system performs its function is the MTBF. This is the **uptime**.

$$
\text{Uptime per cycle} = MTBF
$$

**Step 3 — Define availability as a ratio.**
Availability $A$ is the long-run fraction of time the system is operational. As the number of cycles $N \to \infty$, by the renewal-reward theorem:

$$
A = \lim_{N \to \infty} \dfrac{N \cdot MTBF}{N \cdot (MTBF + MTTR)}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by canceling N.**

$$
A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of failure rate λ (constant region).**
In the useful-life region, $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$. Substituting:

$$
A = \dfrac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \lambda \cdot MTTR}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Reducing MTTR is the fastest way to increase availability when MTBF is already high. This is *why* PdM and rapid-restoration tasks score over PM-only strategies in high-availability systems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Optimum PM Interval (Barber-Larmour Cost Model)

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A component has a slowly increasing failure probability (the wear-out region of the bathtub curve). Two competing costs apply:

- $C_p$ = cost of a single preventive maintenance action (parts + labor + small downtime).
- $C_f$ = cost of a single corrective maintenance action after failure (much higher — emergency repair, secondary damage, lost production).

We wish to find the PM interval $T$ that minimizes the **average cost per unit time**.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Assume linear degradation.**
A reasonable first-order assumption: the component's instantaneous failure rate grows linearly with age, $\lambda(t) = \lambda_d t$, where $\lambda_d$ is the degradation constant.

**Step 2 — Compute the probability of surviving a PM interval of length T.**
For a Weibull-style linearly increasing hazard:

$$
R(T) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^T \lambda_d t \, dt\right) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\lambda_d T^2}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the probability of failure within the interval.**
$$
F(T) = 1 - R(T) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\lambda_d T^2}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the expected number of failures per cycle.**
In one PM interval of length T, the expected number of failures is $F(T)$. After each failure, a corrective action is performed at cost $C_f$. The preventive action itself costs $C_p$ at the start of each cycle.

**Step 5 — Total cost per cycle.**

$$
C_{cycle} = C_p + F(T) \cdot C_f = C_p + C_f \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\lambda_d T^2}{2}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 6 — Average cost per unit time.**

$$
C_{avg}(T) = \dfrac{C_{cycle}}{T} = \dfrac{C_p}{T} + \dfrac{C_f}{T}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\lambda_d T^2}{2}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 7 — Minimize by differentiating with respect to T and setting to zero.**

$$
\dfrac{d C_{avg}}{d T} = -\dfrac{C_p}{T^2} + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial T}\!\left\{\dfrac{C_f}{T}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\lambda_d T^2}{2}\right)\right]\right\} = 0
$$

**Step 8 — Simplify the second term (quotient rule).**
Let $g(T) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_d T^2/2}$ and $h(T) = 1/T$. Then:

$$
\dfrac{d}{dT}\!\left[\dfrac{g(T)}{T}\right] = \dfrac{g'(T) T - g(T)}{T^2}
$$

Where $g'(T) = \lambda_d T \, e^{-\lambda_d T^2/2}$.

**Step 9 — Set the full derivative to zero.**

$$
-\dfrac{C_p}{T^2} + C_f \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_d T^2 e^{-\lambda_d T^2/2} - \left[1 - e^{-\lambda_d T^2/2}\right]}{T^3} = 0
$$

**Step 10 — Multiply both sides by $T^3$.**

$$
-C_p \, T + C_f \left\{\lambda_d T^2 e^{-\lambda_d T^2/2} - 1 + e^{-\lambda_d T^2/2}\right\} = 0
$$

**Step 11 — Rearrange.**

$$
C_f \left\{1 - e^{-\lambda_d T^2/2}(1 + \lambda_d T^2)\right\} = C_p \, T
$$

**Step 12 — Approximate for moderate $T$ where $e^{-\lambda_d T^2/2} \ll 1$ (i.e., the PM interval is not negligible compared to wear-out time).**
This reduces to the classical Barber-Larmour approximation:

$$
T_{opt} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_p}{\lambda_d (C_f - C_p)}}
$$

**Step 13 — Minimum average cost.**

$$
C_{min} = \dfrac{C_p}{T_{opt}} \approx \sqrt{2 \, C_p \, \lambda_d \, (C_f - C_p)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** Step 12 is the formula that examiners expect. The student should always state the *assumptions* explicitly: (i) linear degradation, (ii) $C_f \gg C_p$, (iii) exponential reliability model. A student who writes the formula without stating assumptions loses 2–3 marks.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: PM vs PdM vs RCM

| Decision Axis | Preventive Maintenance (PM) | Predictive Maintenance (PdM) | Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Calendar / usage (time, km, cycles) | Condition threshold (vibration, temperature, etc.) | Engineering analysis of failure modes and consequences |
| **Bathtub-curve zone** | Wear-out region | Random-failure region (early detection of incipient wear-out) | Decides per failure mode across all zones |
| **Data requirement** | Low (manufacturer's manual) | High (sensor network, baseline, analytics) | Highest (failure history, FMEA, criticality, cost) |
| **Cost pattern** | Predictable, periodic | Variable, only when needed | Optimized mixture of all |
| **Unplanned downtime** | Reduced (but not eliminated) | Sharply reduced | Minimized for the overall system |
| **Best for** | Cheap, well-understood components | Expensive, critical, sensor-instrumentable assets | Complex systems with many failure modes |
| **Failure-mode coverage** | Implicit (assumes wear-out) | Explicit (only what sensors detect) | Explicit and exhaustive (all FMEA items) |
| **Permits Run-to-Failure?** | No | No | Yes (explicitly authorized) |
| **Origin / Standard** | Industrial practice pre-1950 | Vibration analysis post-1960s (US Navy) | SAE JA1011 / MSG-3 (aviation, 1970s) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ranking question cue:** If asked to "rank these strategies by engineering sophistication", the correct answer is **RCM > PdM > PM**. RCM uses PdM and PM as building blocks.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-style):** A motor bearing has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour. The cost of a planned PM replacement is $C_p = \text{Rs. } 10{,}000$ and the cost of a corrective replacement after failure is $C_f = \text{Rs. } 200{,}000$. The degradation rate is $\lambda_d = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour². Find the optimum PM interval and the minimum average cost rate.

**Solution:**

Apply the Barber-Larmour formula:

$$
T_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_p}{\lambda_d (C_f - C_p)}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 10{,}000}{5 \times 10^{-7} \times 190{,}000}}
$$

$$
T_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{20{,}000}{0.095}} = \sqrt{210{,}526.3} \approx 458.8 \text{ hours}
$$

Minimum average cost:

$$
C_{min} = \sqrt{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 5 \times 10^{-7} \times 190{,}000} = \sqrt{1{,}900} \approx 43.6 \text{ Rs/hour}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** The optimum interval (≈ 459 h) is well below the characteristic wear-out time ($1/\lambda_d = 2 \times 10^6$ h²? — actually $\sqrt{2/\lambda_d}$ is the half-life of the Weibull model), confirming the formula's reasonableness. A common student error is forgetting to take the square root of both numerator and denominator in Step 12.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Maintenance Strategy Decision Tree (RCM Logic)

This diagram maps the **RCM decision flow** that selects PM, PdM, or Run-to-Failure for any failure mode in the system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Asset with Identified Failure Mode"] --> B["Classify Failure Consequences"]
    B --> C1["Safety or Environmental Consequence"]
    B --> C2["Operational Consequence"]
    B --> C3["Economic-Only Consequence"]
    B --> C4["Hidden Failure"]

    C1 --> D1["Mandatory PM OR Mandatory Redundancy"]
    D1 --> E1["Set PM Interval based on MTBF or Regulation"]

    C2 --> D2{"Failure is Predictable via Condition Monitoring?"}
    D2 -- Yes --> E2["Apply PdM with On-Condition Task"]
    D2 -- No --> F2{"Failure has a Clear Wear-Out Age?"}
    F2 -- Yes --> E2A["Apply PM with Scheduled Restoration"]
    F2 -- No --> G2["Run-to-Failure with Quick Restoration Plan"]

    C3 --> D3{"Cost of Maintenance vs. Cost of Failure?"}
    D3 -- Maintenance cheaper --> E3["Apply PM at Optimum Interval"]
    D3 -- Failure cheaper --> G3["Run-to-Failure Acceptable"]

    C4 --> D4["Apply Redundancy or Periodic Functional Test PM"]

    E1 --> H["Finalize RCM Maintenance Plan"]
    E2 --> H
    E2A --> H
    G2 --> H
    E3 --> H
    G3 --> H
    D4 --> H
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Comparison of PM, PdM, and RCM in Block-Functional Form

This block diagram shows the **functional interaction** between the three maintenance philosophies as they coexist in a modern Industry 4.0 plant.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputLayer["Input Layer"]
        S1["Manufacturer Schedule"]
        S2["Sensor Array (Vibration, Temperature, Acoustic)"]
        S3["FMEA Database"]
    end

    subgraph processingLayer["Decision Processing Layer"]
        P1["PM Engine (Time-Based Trigger)"]
        P2["PdM Engine (Threshold and Trend Analyzer)"]
        P3["RCM Engine (Failure Mode, Consequence, and Criticality Analyzer)"]
    end

    subgraph outputLayer["Output Action Layer"]
        O1["Scheduled Overhaul and Replacement"]
        O2["Condition-Based Intervention"]
        O3["Mixed Optimized Task List and Run-to-Failure Approval"]
    end

    S1 --> P1
    S2 --> P2
    S3 --> P3

    P3 --> P1
    P3 --> P2
    P3 --> O3

    P1 --> O1
    P2 --> O2

    O1 --> A["Combined Maintenance Work Order Database"]
    O2 --> A
    O3 --> A

    A --> R["Asset Reliability and Availability Metrics"]
    R --> P3
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Bathtub Curve with Maintenance Strategy Overlay

This is a conceptual block diagram of the bathtub curve (failure rate vs. time) with the maintenance strategy appropriate for each region highlighted.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph curveConcept["Bathtub Curve and Maintenance Overlay"]
        X["Time Axis t"] --> R1["Infant Mortality Region (Burn-In)"]
        R1 --> R2["Useful Life Region (Random Failures)"]
        R2 --> R3["Wear-Out Region (Increasing Failure Rate)"]
    end

    R1 --> M1["Strategy: Factory Debug and Run-In Screening"]
    R2 --> M2["Strategy: PdM Monitoring and On-Condition Tasks"]
    R3 --> M3["Strategy: PM Scheduled Restoration and Replacement"]

    M1 --> N1["Result: Early failures eliminated before commissioning"]
    M2 --> N2["Result: Incipient failures detected before functional loss"]
    M3 --> N3["Result: Wear-out failures prevented by component renewal"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU diagram cue:** Whenever the bathtub curve is drawn in an answer, the student *must* mark the boundary at the "useful-life" region as the operational target and label the **transition point (t₁ and t₂)** with the maintenance strategy that applies. Examiners look for this labeling.

## 4.4 Diagram 4: PdM Signal Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Physical Asset Under Monitoring"] --> A2["Sensors (Accelerometer, Thermocouple, etc.)"]
    A2 --> A3["Signal Conditioning and ADC"]
    A3 --> A4["Feature Extraction (RMS, FFT, Kurtosis)"]
    A4 --> A5["Baseline Comparison Module"]
    A5 --> A6{"Threshold Exceeded?"}
    A6 -- No --> A7["Continue Monitoring Loop"]
    A7 --> A3
    A6 -- Yes --> A8["Alarm and Diagnostic Report"]
    A8 --> A9["Maintenance Work Order Generation"]
    A9 --> A10["Asset Repair and Calibration"]
    A10 --> A1
```
