<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between Preventive Maintenance and Predictive Maintenance in three key aspects.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Preventive Maintenance | Predictive Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger basis** | Time-based or usage-based (calendar, hours, km, cycles) | Condition-based (vibration, temperature, acoustic emission, oil analysis) |
| **Decision data** | Manufacturer's manual, historical MTBF | Real-time sensor data and trend analysis |
| **Bathtub-curve zone** | Wear-out region (λ increasing) | Useful life and early wear-out (λ stable then rising) |
| **Cost pattern** | Fixed periodic cost regardless of component health | Variable cost, incurred only when thresholds trigger |
| **Unplanned downtime** | Reduced but not eliminated | Sharply reduced; failures detected in advance |
| **Equipment suitability** | Simple, non-critical, non-instrumented assets | Critical, expensive, sensor-instrumentable assets |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks split (3):** 1 mark for correct definition of each, 1 mark for at least 3 differences.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)? State any four of its key principles.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Reliability-Centered Maintenance is a systematic engineering methodology used to determine the most appropriate maintenance requirement for any physical asset in its operating context, based on its failure modes, failure consequences, and operational criticality. RCM was originally developed for the aviation industry and is formalized in standards such as SAE JA1011 and SAE JA1012.

**Four key principles:**

1. **Preserve system function:** RCM focuses on maintaining the *required function* of the asset rather than preserving its inherent reliability.
2. **Identify failure modes explicitly:** Every credible failure mode must be identified (using FMEA / FMECA) before any maintenance decision is made.
3. **Prioritize by consequences:** Safety, environmental, operational, and economic consequences are ranked, and the most severe drive mandatory maintenance tasks.
4. **Apply the right task:** For each failure mode, RCM selects among scheduled restoration, scheduled discard, on-condition task, or run-to-failure — whichever is technically and economically justified.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks split (3):** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for principles (½ mark each × 4).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose marks on Part B questions by (i) writing generic textbook content without applying it to the numerical problem, (ii) forgetting the units in the final answer, and (iii) skipping the *assumption statement* in derivations. Always begin a derivation with a clear "**Assumption:**" line.

### Question A (14 Marks) — **Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

A critical pump in a process plant has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures/hour. The average repair time is **MTTR = 8 hours**. The plant is considering two options:

- **Option 1 (PM only):** Replace the pump every 4000 hours. Each preventive replacement costs **Rs. 50,000** and the plant is shut down for 6 hours (production loss = Rs. 40,000 per hour).
- **Option 2 (PdM):** Install vibration sensors costing **Rs. 500,000** (one-time) and run continuously. The PdM system is expected to extend MTBF to 12,000 hours. Each corrective repair after PdM alert costs **Rs. 150,000** and 10 hours downtime.

Assume the planning horizon is **40,000 hours**.

**Compute the following:**
- **(a)** Steady-state availability under both options. (7 marks)
- **(b)** Total cost over 40,000 hours under both options, and recommend the better option. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Availability under PM (Option 1).**
Given: $\lambda = 0.0005$/h ⇒ $MTBF_{PM} = 1/\lambda = 2000$ hours. The PM intervention at 4000 h does not change the operational MTBF because the underlying failure rate is unchanged; the pump's operational uptime between interventions is governed by $MTBF = 2000$ h.

$MTTR_{PM} = 6$ hours (planned shutdown).

$$
A_{PM} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{2000}{2000 + 6} = \dfrac{2000}{2006} \approx 0.99701
$$

**Step 2 — Availability under PdM (Option 2).**
With PdM, $MTBF_{PdM} = 12{,}000$ hours. $MTTR_{PdM} = 10$ hours (after alert and repair).

$$
A_{PdM} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{12{,}000 + 10} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{12{,}010} \approx 0.99917
$$

**Step 3 — Comparative interpretation.**
PdM yields higher availability by $\Delta A \approx 0.00216$ (about 0.22%).

$$
\boxed{A_{PM} \approx 0.9970, \quad A_{PdM} \approx 0.9992}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Number of PM cycles in 40,000 h.**
$$
N_{PM} = \dfrac{40{,}000}{4000} = 10 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of failures under PM (between scheduled replacements).**
In each 4000-h PM interval, expected failures $\approx \lambda \times 4000 = 0.0005 \times 4000 = 2$ failures. (In a 4000-h interval, the pump would fail twice on average before scheduled PM.) We must still perform PM every 4000 h.

PM cost per cycle = preventive cost + cost of in-between corrective repairs.

- Preventive replacement cost: Rs. 50,000
- Corrective cost per failure: Rs. 150,000
- Number of corrective failures per cycle: 2

Cost per PM cycle = $50{,}000 + 2 \times 150{,}000 = 350{,}000$ Rs.

Total PM cost over 40,000 h = $10 \times 350{,}000 = 3{,}500{,}000$ Rs.

Plus downtime cost: every PM cycle has 1 PM action (6 h × Rs. 40,000 = Rs. 240,000) plus 2 corrective actions (10 h × Rs. 40,000 = Rs. 400,000 each) = $240{,}000 + 2 \times 400{,}000 = 1{,}040{,}000$ Rs per cycle.

Total downtime cost over 40,000 h = $10 \times 1{,}040{,}000 = 10{,}400{,}000$ Rs.

**Total PM system cost = 3,500,000 + 10,400,000 = Rs. 13,900,000**

**Step 3 — Cost under PdM (Option 2).**
Number of failures in 40,000 h: $N_f = 40{,}000 / 12{,}000 = 3.33 \approx 4$ failures.

- One-time sensor cost: Rs. 500,000
- Corrective repair cost: $4 \times 150{,}000 = 600{,}000$ Rs
- Downtime cost: $4 \times 10 \text{ h} \times 40{,}000 = 1{,}600{,}000$ Rs

**Total PdM cost = 500,000 + 600,000 + 1,600,000 = Rs. 2,700,000**

**Step 4 — Recommendation.**
$$
\Delta C = 13{,}900{,}000 - 2{,}700{,}000 = 11{,}200{,}000 \text{ Rs (savings with PdM)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PdM is decisively cheaper (savings ≈ Rs. 11.2 million) and offers higher availability.}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this answer scores full marks:** The student explicitly computes both availability and lifecycle cost, presents numbers with units, and makes a justified engineering recommendation. Examiners reward *quantitative justification* over *qualitative opinion*.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

(a) **Explain the seven RCM questions and the four RCM task categories.** (7 marks)

(b) **A compressor component has a linearly increasing failure rate $\lambda(t) = \lambda_d t$ with $\lambda_d = 10^{-6}$ per hour². The preventive maintenance cost is $C_p = \text{Rs. } 8{,}000$ and the corrective failure cost is $C_f = \text{Rs. } 100{,}000$. Using the Barber-Larmour model, find the optimum PM interval and the minimum average cost rate.** (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**The seven RCM questions** (1 mark each, full list required):

1. What are the functions and associated performance standards of the asset in its present operating context?
2. In what ways does it fail to fulfill its functions (functional failures)?
3. What causes each functional failure (failure modes and mechanisms)?
4. What happens when each failure occurs (failure effects)?
5. In what way does each failure matter (failure consequences — safety, environment, operation, cost)?
6. What can be done to predict or prevent each failure (proactive tasks and their frequencies)?
7. What should be done if a suitable proactive task cannot be found (default actions — redesign, run-to-failure, etc.)?

**The four RCM task categories:**

| Category | Description | Bathtub-curve zone |
|---|---|---|
| **A — Scheduled Restoration** | Overhaul/replace at a fixed interval to restore the component to like-new condition | Wear-out region |
| **B — Scheduled Discard** | Discard the component at a fixed interval regardless of its visible state (safety-critical items) | Wear-out region |
| **C — On-Condition Task** | Monitor a parameter; replace or repair when threshold is crossed (PdM-style) | Useful-life region (incipient failures) |
| **D — Run-to-Failure** | No scheduled task; design for easy replacement or accept the failure (RCM explicitly permits this) | Useful-life region (non-critical) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks split (7):** 4 marks for the seven questions (1 each × 7, or 0.5 each if compressed) and 3 marks for the four categories with at least one example.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Assumption:** Linear degradation model with $\lambda(t) = \lambda_d t$ and $C_f \gg C_p$ such that the exponential term $e^{-\lambda_d T^2/2}$ is negligible.

**Given:** $\lambda_d = 10^{-6}$/h², $C_p = 8{,}000$ Rs, $C_f = 100{,}000$ Rs.

**Step 1 — Apply the Barber-Larmour optimum interval formula.**

$$
T_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_p}{\lambda_d (C_f - C_p)}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
T_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 8{,}000}{10^{-6} \times (100{,}000 - 8{,}000)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the denominator.**

$$
10^{-6} \times 92{,}000 = 0.092
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator.**

$$
2 \times 8{,}000 = 16{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Final ratio and root.**

$$
T_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{16{,}000}{0.092}} = \sqrt{173{,}913.04} \approx 416.99 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the minimum average cost rate.**

$$
C_{min} = \sqrt{2 \, C_p \, \lambda_d \, (C_f - C_p)} = \sqrt{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 10^{-6} \times 92{,}000}
$$

**Step 7 — Simplify.**

$$
C_{min} = \sqrt{1.472} \approx 1.213 \text{ Rs/hour}
$$

**Final boxed answer:**

$$
\boxed{T_{opt} \approx 417 \text{ hours}, \quad C_{min} \approx 1.21 \text{ Rs/hour}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Setting $T = 417$ h and $C_f/C_p = 12.5$, the formula predicts the average number of failures per interval as roughly $F(T) \approx 1 - e^{-0.5 \lambda_d T^2} = 1 - e^{-0.0868} \approx 0.083$, meaning about 8% of intervals end in a failure — a healthy balance between over-maintenance and under-maintenance. This is the kind of sanity check that distinguishes a top-band answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-day exam prep):**

- **Maintenance** is the totality of actions to retain or restore an item to a functional state; in KTU it is treated as a *decision science*.
- **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** is *time/usage-based*; it is most effective in the **wear-out zone** of the bathtub curve.
- **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** is *condition-based*; relies on **sensors, baselines, and trend analysis**; it is most effective in the **useful-life zone** where incipient failure can be detected.
- **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)** is a *process*, not a maintenance type; it is the **decision framework** that selects between PM, PdM, and Run-to-Failure per failure mode.
- The **seven RCM questions** must be asked for *every* failure mode; the answers drive the choice among the **four task categories** (Scheduled Restoration, Scheduled Discard, On-Condition, Run-to-Failure).
- **Availability** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ — increasing MTBF (PdM) and decreasing MTTR (rapid response) both raise A.
- **Barber-Larmour optimum PM interval** $T_{opt} = \sqrt{2 C_p / [\lambda_d (C_f - C_p)]}$ — valid under the assumption of linear degradation and $C_f \gg C_p$.
- **Minimum cost rate** $C_{min} = \sqrt{2 C_p \lambda_d (C_f - C_p)}$.
- **KTU high-yield comparison axes** between PM and PdM: trigger, data requirement, bathtub-curve zone, cost pattern, downtime reduction, and equipment suitability.
- **Always** state assumptions in any derivation question, **always** carry units in numerical answers, and **always** end with a quantitative engineering recommendation.
- **Common trap:** Confusing MTTF (non-repairable) with MTBF (repairable). Use MTTF for items you throw away (bulbs, bearings rated for one life), MTBF for machines that get repaired.
- **Common trap:** Forgetting that RCM is *allowed* to authorize Run-to-Failure, while PM and PdM are not.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the four RCM task types — "R-S-O-N":** **R**estoration, **S**cheduled discard, **O**n-condition, **N**o-task (Run-to-failure). Use this in the exam if your mind blanks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maintainability: Instantaneous Repair Rate and Maintainability Function; MTTR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maintainability: Instantaneous Repair Rate and Maintainability Function; MTTR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Maintainability — Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Maintainability** is defined as the probability that a failed system, equipment, or component will be restored to its specified operational condition within a given period of time `t`, when maintenance action is performed in accordance with prescribed procedures and resources.

Mathematically, it is represented by the symbol **M(t)**.

$$
M(t) = P(T \le t)
$$

where `T` is the random variable denoting the **time-to-repair (TTR)** of the failed item, and `t` is the mission/allowable repair time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Maintainability is a *probability measure*, not a guarantee. It quantifies the *ease* and *speed* with which a system can be brought back to its working state.

### 1.2 Instantaneous Repair Rate — Definition

The **Instantaneous Repair Rate**, denoted by the Greek letter **μ(t)** (mu of t), is the rate at which a failed component is being repaired at time instant `t`, given that it has *not yet been repaired* up to that time. It is the maintainability counterpart of the failure rate **λ(t)** in reliability theory.

$$
\mu(t) = \frac{m(t)}{1 - M(t)}
$$

where `m(t)` is the **Repair Time Density Function** and `M(t)` is the Maintainability Function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of μ(t):** If μ(t) is constant, it means the maintenance crew's efficiency remains unchanged throughout the repair process — neither getting faster nor slower. A *decreasing* μ(t) implies early diagnosis takes time but once identified, repairs are quick; an *increasing* μ(t) implies tasks get progressively harder (e.g., disassembling, cleaning, reassembling).

### 1.3 Maintainability Function M(t) — Definition

The **Maintainability Function M(t)** is the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the time-to-repair. It gives the probability that repair is completed by time `t`.

$$
M(t) = \int_{0}^{t} m(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

where `m(τ)` is the repair time probability density function (PDF).

### 1.4 Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) — Definition

The **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** is the *expected* (average) value of the time required to restore a failed system to working order. It is the mean of the repair-time distribution.

$$
\text{MTTR} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot m(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} [1 - M(t)] \, dt
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Car Service Centre:** Imagine you bring your car to a service centre because the engine fails. Maintainability M(t) answers: *"What is the probability the mechanic finishes the repair within, say, 4 hours?"* The Instantaneous Repair Rate μ(t) answers: *"Given the car is still under repair after 3 hours, how fast is the mechanic working right now?"* The MTTR answers: *"On average, how long does a typical repair take across many such failures?"*

### 1.5 Common Repair Time Distributions (KTU High-Yield)

| Distribution | Repair Time PDF `m(t)` | Maintainability `M(t)` | MTTR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Exponential | $\mu \, e^{-\mu t}$ | $1 - e^{-\mu t}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\mu}$ |
| Weibull (repair) | $\alpha \beta^{\alpha} t^{\alpha-1} e^{-(\beta t)^{\alpha}}$ | $1 - e^{-(\beta t)^{\alpha}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\beta}\Gamma\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\alpha}+1\right)$ |
| Log-Normal | $\dfrac{1}{t\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(\ln t - \mu_L)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$ | $\Phi\!\left(\dfrac{\ln t - \mu_L}{\sigma}\right)$ | $\exp\!\left(\mu_L + \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2}\right)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For most KTU numerical problems, the **exponential repair time distribution** is assumed unless explicitly stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundational Logic of Maintainability

Maintainability is built on **four mutually consistent mathematical entities**. Understanding their inter-relationships is critical to solving KTU derivations and numericals.

**Step 1 — Repair Time Density Function m(t):**
This is the probability that repair completes in the infinitesimal window `[t, t + dt]`. It is analogous to the failure density `f(t)` in reliability.

$$
m(t) = \frac{dM(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Maintainability Function M(t):**
The cumulative probability that repair has been completed by time `t`. It rises from **0** (at `t = 0`, repair not yet started/complete) to **1** (as `t → ∞`, repair will eventually be done).

$$
M(0) = 0, \quad M(\infty) = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Instantaneous Repair Rate μ(t):**
Conditional density of being repaired at time `t`, given that repair has *not yet* been completed.

$$
\mu(t) = \frac{m(t)}{1 - M(t)} = \frac{dM(t)/dt}{1 - M(t)}
$$

**Step 4 — Mean Time To Repair (MTTR):**
The first moment of the repair-time density.

$$
\text{MTTR} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, m(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} [1 - M(t)] \, dt
$$

### 2.2 Derivation of the General Maintainability Equation

Starting from the definition of μ(t):

$$
\mu(t) = \frac{dM/dt}{1 - M(t)}
$$

Separating variables and integrating from 0 to `t`:

$$
\int_{0}^{t} \mu(\tau) \, d\tau = -\ln[1 - M(t)]
$$

Solving for `M(t)`:

$$
1 - M(t) = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} \mu(\tau) \, d\tau}
$$

$$
\boxed{M(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\int_{0}^{t} \mu(\tau) \, d\tau\right]}
$$

For a **constant repair rate** `μ`:

$$
M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu t}
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary/Remark |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maintainability Function | $M(t) = P(T \le t)$ | dimensionless | $M(0)=0$, $M(\infty)=1$ |
| 2 | Repair Density | $m(t) = \dfrac{dM(t)}{dt}$ | $\text{time}^{-1}$ | $m(t) \ge 0$, $\int_{0}^{\infty} m(t)\,dt = 1$ |
| 3 | Instantaneous Repair Rate | $\mu(t) = \dfrac{m(t)}{1-M(t)}$ | $\text{time}^{-1}$ | $\mu(t) \ge 0$ |
| 4 | Exponential M(t) | $1 - e^{-\mu t}$ | dimensionless | constant $\mu$ |
| 5 | MTTR (general) | $\int_{0}^{\infty} [1-M(t)]\,dt$ | time | always finite |
| 6 | MTTR (exponential) | $\dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | time | inverse of repair rate |
| 7 | Median Time To Repair (MTTR-50%) | $t_{0.5}$ s.t. $M(t_{0.5}) = 0.5$ | time | for exponential: $\dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ |
| 8 | Maximum Repair Time (MTTR-95%) | $t_{0.95}$ s.t. $M(t_{0.95}) = 0.95$ | time | for exponential: $\dfrac{\ln 20}{\mu}$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation Industry:** Aircraft Maintenance Check (A, B, C, D checks) durations are predicted using maintainability analysis to schedule turnaround time.
- **Manufacturing:** Mean Time To Repair on shop-floor CNC machines determines Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) and the availability factor.
- **Military Systems:** Design for maintainability influences logistic footprint, manpower planning, and mission readiness.
- **Power Plants:** Scheduled outage durations (MTTR for turbine/generator) determine annual energy generation loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Cross-Connection:** The trio **Reliability — Maintainability — Availability** forms Module 4's central theme. The **Inherent Availability** is given by:
> $$ A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} $$
> So MTTR is a *direct* lever for system availability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — M(t) from Instantaneous Repair Rate μ(t)

We start from the fundamental definition:

$$
\mu(t) = \frac{m(t)}{1 - M(t)} = \frac{dM/dt}{1 - M(t)}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\mu(t)[1 - M(t)] \, dt = dM(t)
$$

Integrating both sides from `0` to `t`:

$$
\int_{0}^{t} \mu(\tau) \, d\tau = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{dM}{1 - M} = -\ln[1 - M(t)] + \ln[1 - M(0)]
$$

Since $M(0) = 0$, the second term vanishes:

$$
\int_{0}^{t} \mu(\tau) \, d\tau = -\ln[1 - M(t)]
$$

Exponentiating:

$$
1 - M(t) = \exp\!\left[-\int_{0}^{t} \mu(\tau) \, d\tau\right]
$$

Therefore, the general maintainability function is:

$$
\boxed{M(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\int_{0}^{t} \mu(\tau) \, d\tau\right]}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — MTTR for Constant Repair Rate μ

For constant μ, the integral $\int_{0}^{t} \mu \, d\tau = \mu t$, so:

$$
M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu t}
$$

Then:

$$
m(t) = \frac{dM}{dt} = \mu \, e^{-\mu t}
$$

By definition of expectation:

$$
\text{MTTR} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, m(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, \mu e^{-\mu t} \, dt
$$

Integrating by parts, let `u = t`, `dv = μ e^(-μt) dt`, so `du = dt`, `v = -e^(-μt)`:

$$
\text{MTTR} = \big[-t e^{-\mu t}\big]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\mu t} \, dt = 0 + \frac{1}{\mu}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalent MTTR via the Alternative Integral

Start from:

$$
\text{MTTR} = \int_{0}^{\infty} [1 - M(t)] \, dt
$$

For exponential M(t):

$$
1 - M(t) = e^{-\mu t}
$$

So:

$$
\text{MTTR} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\mu t} \, dt = \left[\frac{-e^{-\mu t}}{\mu}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(\frac{-1}{\mu}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}
$$

This **cross-validates** Derivation 2. The two MTTR definitions are mathematically equivalent.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Standard)

> **Problem:** The repair time of a CNC machine follows an exponential distribution with a constant repair rate μ = 0.25 repairs/hour. Determine:
> **(a)** The probability that repair is completed within 5 hours.
> **(b)** The MTTR.
> **(c)** The median time to repair.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Using $M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu t}$ with `μ = 0.25/hr`, `t = 5 hr`:

$$
\mu t = 0.25 \times 5 = 1.25
$$

$$
M(5) = 1 - e^{-1.25} = 1 - 0.2865 = 0.7135
$$

So there is a **71.35%** probability that the CNC machine is repaired within 5 hours.

**Part (b):** MTTR:

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4 \text{ hours}
$$

**Part (c):** Median (50% probability): $M(t_{0.5}) = 0.5$

$$
1 - e^{-\mu t_{0.5}} = 0.5
$$

$$
e^{-\mu t_{0.5}} = 0.5
$$

$$
\mu t_{0.5} = \ln 2 \Rightarrow t_{0.5} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.6931}{0.25} = 2.7726 \text{ hours}
$$

So the **median repair time ≈ 2.77 hours**.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Reliability vs Maintainability

| Aspect | Reliability `R(t)` | Maintainability `M(t)` |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Prob. of *surviving* up to time `t` | Prob. of *being repaired* by time `t` |
| Density | `f(t)` = failure density | `m(t)` = repair density |
| Rate function | Failure rate $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Repair rate $\mu(t) = \dfrac{m(t)}{1-M(t)}$ |
| Boundary | $R(0) = 1$, $R(\infty) = 0$ | $M(0) = 0$, $M(\infty) = 1$ |
| Mean | MTBF = $\int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$ | MTTR = $\int_{0}^{\infty} [1-M(t)]\, dt$ |
| Behaviour | *Decreasing* with time | *Increasing* with time |
| Symbol | $R(t) = e^{-\int \lambda dt}$ | $M(t) = 1 - e^{-\int \mu dt}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common error is mixing up $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (which *decreases*) with $M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu t}$ (which *increases*). Always remember: **R starts at 1 and falls, M starts at 0 and rises.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Maintainability Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[System Failure Event] --> nodeB[Failure Detected and Logged]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Diagnosis and Fault Isolation]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Repair Strategy Selection}
    nodeD -->|Minor| nodeE[On Site Repair]
    nodeD -->|Major| nodeF[Workshop Repair]
    nodeD -->|Replacement| nodeG[Module Swap]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Verification and Testing]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[System Restored to Operation]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Update Maintenance Records]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Compute M of t and MTTR]
```

### 4.2 Relationship Diagram — Four Maintainability Entities

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Repair Time PDF m of t] --> nodeB[Integrate]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Maintainability M of t]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Differentiate]
    nodeD --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeE[Compute m over 1 minus M]
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Instantaneous Repair Rate mu of t]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Integrate 1 minus M over t]
    nodeG --> nodeH[MTTR]
    nodeA --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeI[Integrate mu dt over t]
    nodeI --> nodeC
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — From Failure to Repair Completion (Time Domain)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Maintainability Time-Line for a Typical Repair Event
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Detection
    Failure Occurs and Detected   :a1, 0, 1
    section Active Repair
    Diagnosis                     :a2, 1, 2
    Disassembly                   :a3, 3, 2
    Part Replacement              :a4, 5, 3
    Reassembly                    :a5, 8, 2
    section Verification
    Testing and Sign Off          :a6, 10, 1
    section Computed
    M of t at this instant        :crit, 11, 0
```

### 4.4 Reliability–Maintainability–Availability Triangle (Conceptual Link)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Reliability R of t] --> nodeD[Inherent Availability A sub i]
    nodeB[Maintainability M of t] --> nodeD
    nodeC[MTBF from R] --> nodeD
    nodeE[MTTR from M] --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeF[A sub i equals MTBF over MTBF plus MTTR]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

Define the term **Maintainability** as applied to engineering systems. Mention the standard symbol used and its range of values.

**Model Answer:**

Maintainability is the probability that a failed system or component will be repaired and restored to its specified operational condition within a given period of time `t`, when maintenance is performed as per prescribed procedures.

It is denoted by the symbol **M(t)**, where `t` is the allowable repair time.

The function satisfies the boundary conditions:

$$
M(0) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad M(\infty) = 1
$$

The value **0** indicates that no repair is complete at `t = 0`, while **1** indicates that repair is *certain* to be completed given infinite time.

**(3 Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for symbol, 1 for boundaries)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Distinguish between the **Instantaneous Repair Rate μ(t)** and the **Maintainability Function M(t)** with one suitable mathematical relationship connecting them.

**Model Answer:**

The **Maintainability Function M(t)** is the *cumulative* probability that the repair is completed within time `t`. It is a *global* measure covering the entire interval `[0, t]`.

The **Instantaneous Repair Rate μ(t)** is the *conditional* rate of repair completion at the exact instant `t`, given that the system is *still under repair* at that moment. It is a *local* measure for an infinitesimally small interval.

The mathematical relationship connecting them is:

$$
\mu(t) = \frac{dM(t)/dt}{1 - M(t)}
$$

where the numerator `dM/dt` is the repair time density function `m(t)`, and `1 − M(t)` is the probability that the system is *still awaiting repair completion* at time `t`.

**(3 Marks: 1 for M(t) definition, 1 for μ(t) definition, 1 for relationship)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**  `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the general expression for the **Maintainability Function M(t)** in terms of the **Instantaneous Repair Rate μ(t)**. Hence obtain the special form for a **constant repair rate μ**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The repair time of a hydraulic press in a manufacturing plant follows an exponential distribution with a constant repair rate of μ = 0.5 repairs/hour. Calculate:
- (i) The probability that repair is completed within 3 hours.
- (ii) The MTTR.
- (iii) The maximum time within which 90% of repairs will be completed. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a):**

We begin with the definition of the instantaneous repair rate:

$$
\mu(t) = \frac{m(t)}{1 - M(t)} = \frac{dM(t)/dt}{1 - M(t)}
$$

Separating the variables:

$$
\mu(t) \, dt = \frac{dM(t)}{1 - M(t)}
$$

Integrating both sides from `0` to `t`, and noting that at `t = 0`, `M(0) = 0`:

$$
\int_{0}^{t} \mu(\tau) \, d\tau = -\ln[1 - M(t)]
$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$
1 - M(t) = \exp\!\left[-\int_{0}^{t} \mu(\tau) \, d\tau\right]
$$

$$
\boxed{M(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\int_{0}^{t} \mu(\tau) \, d\tau\right]}
$$

For the special case where the repair rate is **constant** $\mu(t) = \mu$, the integral simplifies:

$$
\int_{0}^{t} \mu \, d\tau = \mu t
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu t}}
$$

This is the **exponential maintainability function**, analogous to the exponential reliability function `R(t) = e^(-λt)`.

**(7 Marks: 3 for derivation setup, 2 for integration, 2 for final form and special case)**

---

**Part (b):**

Given: `μ = 0.5 repairs/hour`.

**(i) Probability that repair completes within 3 hours:**

$$
M(3) = 1 - e^{-\mu t} = 1 - e^{-(0.5)(3)} = 1 - e^{-1.5}
$$

$$
e^{-1.5} = 0.2231
$$

$$
M(3) = 1 - 0.2231 = 0.7769
$$

**Probability ≈ 77.69%**

**(ii) Mean Time To Repair (MTTR):**

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2 \text{ hours}
$$

**(iii) Time for 90% of repairs to complete (MTTR-90%):**

$$
M(t_{0.90}) = 0.90
$$

$$
1 - e^{-\mu t_{0.90}} = 0.90 \Rightarrow e^{-\mu t_{0.90}} = 0.10
$$

$$
\mu t_{0.90} = -\ln(0.10) = 2.3026
$$

$$
t_{0.90} = \frac{2.3026}{0.5} = 4.6052 \text{ hours}
$$

**Maximum time for 90% repairs ≈ 4.61 hours**

**(7 Marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 3 for (iii))**

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is **MTTR**? Derive the expression for MTTR when the repair time follows an **exponential distribution** with constant rate μ. Show that the alternative integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} [1 - M(t)] dt$ yields the same result. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A radar system has a constant failure rate of 0.02 failures/hour and a constant repair rate of 0.5 repairs/hour. Determine:
- (i) The MTBF and MTTR.
- (ii) The steady-state **inherent availability** of the system.
- (iii) The probability that the system is operational for at least 50 hours without failure, given it is currently operational. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a):**

**Definition:** The Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) is the expected (average) value of the time required to repair a failed component and restore it to operational condition. It is the first moment of the repair-time probability distribution.

For an **exponential repair time distribution** with constant rate μ, the density is:

$$
m(t) = \mu \, e^{-\mu t}
$$

By definition of expectation:

$$
\text{MTTR} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, m(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, \mu e^{-\mu t} \, dt
$$

Applying integration by parts with `u = t` and `dv = μ e^(-μt) dt`, so `du = dt` and `v = -e^(-μt)`:

$$
\text{MTTR} = \big[-t e^{-\mu t}\big]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (-e^{-\mu t}) \, dt
$$

The first term vanishes (since $t e^{-\mu t} \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$):

$$
\text{MTTR} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\mu t} \, dt = \left[\frac{-e^{-\mu t}}{\mu}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(\frac{-1}{\mu}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu}}
$$

**Cross-verification using the alternative integral:**

For exponential M(t) = $1 - e^{-\mu t}$:

$$
\text{MTTR} = \int_{0}^{\infty} [1 - M(t)] \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\mu t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\mu}
$$

Both methods give the same result, confirming the consistency of maintainability theory.

**(7 Marks: 1 for definition, 3 for derivation 1, 3 for cross-verification)**

---

**Part (b):**

Given: $\lambda = 0.02$ failures/hr, $\mu = 0.5$ repairs/hr.

**(i) MTBF and MTTR:**

For constant failure rate, MTBF = 1/λ:

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.02} = 50 \text{ hours}
$$

For constant repair rate, MTTR = 1/μ:

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2 \text{ hours}
$$

**(ii) Inherent Availability:**

$$
A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{50}{50 + 2} = \frac{50}{52} = 0.9615
$$

**Inherent Availability ≈ 96.15%**

**(iii) Probability of surviving at least 50 hours, given currently operational:**

This is the reliability for t = 50 hr using exponential reliability:

$$
R(50) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.02)(50)} = e^{-1} = 0.3679
$$

**Probability ≈ 36.79%**

**(7 Marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 3 for (iii))**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Derivation:** A common mistake students make is writing $M(t) = e^{-\mu t}$ instead of $M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu t}$. Remember: maintainability is a *cumulative* quantity, so it must start at 0 and approach 1.
> 2. **Confusing λ and μ:** In numericals, $\lambda$ is the *failure* rate (used in MTBF), while $\mu$ is the *repair* rate (used in MTTR). Mixing these is the most frequent mark-loss reason.
> 3. **Forgetting Boundary Conditions:** When asked to state properties of M(t), always include $M(0) = 0$ and $M(\infty) = 1$. Examiners award marks explicitly for these.
> 4. **Inherent Availability vs Steady-State Availability:** $A_i$ assumes *perfect* logistics (instant spares and crew). Do *not* add downtime for logistic delays.
> 5. **Units Mismatch:** Always keep $\lambda$ in `1/time` and `t` in the *same* `time` unit. KTU examiners *will* deduct marks for unit inconsistencies.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Maintainability** = probability that repair is *completed* by time `t`. It is a CDF.
- **M(t)** ranges from **0 to 1**, increasing monotonically with `t`. Compare with `R(t)` which *decreases* from 1 to 0.
- **Instantaneous Repair Rate** $\mu(t) = \dfrac{m(t)}{1-M(t)}$ is the maintainability analogue of $\lambda(t)$ in reliability.
- **General Maintainability Equation:** $M(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\int_{0}^{t} \mu(\tau) d\tau\right]$.
- For **constant** $\mu$: $M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu t}$ and the repair time follows the **exponential distribution**.
- **MTTR (general):** $\displaystyle \int_{0}^{\infty} [1 - M(t)] \, dt$.
- **MTTR (exponential):** $\dfrac{1}{\mu}$.
- **MTBF (exponential reliability):** $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$.
- **Inherent Availability:** $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$.
- **Median Time To Repair (MTTR-50%):** $t_{0.5} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ for exponential.
- **90% Repair Time:** $t_{0.9} = \dfrac{\ln 10}{\mu}$ for exponential.
- **95% Repair Time:** $t_{0.95} = \dfrac{\ln 20}{\mu}$ for exponential.
- Repair time is often modelled by **Weibull** (flexibility) or **Log-Normal** (when most repairs cluster around a mode) distributions in real-world scenarios.
- Higher $\mu$ → faster repair → smaller MTTR → higher system availability.
- MTTR is *always positive* and *finite* for any physically valid M(t).
- M(t) and R(t) are *not independent* — both govern the system together through the availability metric.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Life Testing: Objectives, Types, and Censoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Life Testing?

**Life Testing** is a controlled, time-bound experimental procedure conducted in a reliability laboratory to obtain quantitative information about the failure characteristics, life distribution, and reliability parameters of a product, component, or system. It is the empirical backbone of reliability engineering because it transforms abstract probabilistic models into measurable, verifiable data.

In formal KTU 2024 terminology, life testing is defined as:

> **Definition:** Life testing is a reliability test methodology wherein a sample of $n$ identical items is operated continuously (or cyclically) under specified environmental and loading conditions until a pre-defined failure criterion is reached, with the primary objective of estimating the **probability distribution of time-to-failure (TTF)**, the **reliability function $R(t)$**, the **hazard rate $\lambda(t)$**, and associated confidence bounds.

Mathematically, the result of a life test on a single item yields a random variable:

$$
T = \text{Time-to-Failure (TTF)} \sim f(t)
$$

where $f(t)$ is the underlying probability density function of failure times.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 explicitly requires students to articulate the *objectives* of life testing, classify the *types* (complete vs. censored; destructive vs. non-destructive; Type I vs. Type II), and solve estimation problems using **censored data** — particularly under the **Exponential and Weibull distributions**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Heart Monitor

Imagine a cardiologist monitoring **100 patients** who have just received a new artificial heart valve. The cardiologist cannot wait 30 years for all 100 valves to fail — that would defeat the purpose of the study. So:

- Some patients will be observed until the valve **fails** during the study period (these give **complete data**).
- Some patients will still be **functioning perfectly** when the study funding runs out (these give **censored data**).
- Some patients will **drop out** of the study because they moved cities (these also yield censored data).

The cardiologist then *statistically combines* both failure and survival data to predict the **mean life, hazard rate, and warranty period** of the valve. **This is exactly what life testing does for engineering components** — it is the bridge between laboratory observation and life-cycle reliability prediction.

---

## 1.3 Primary Objectives of Life Testing

The KTU 2024 scheme categorizes the objectives into four hierarchical tiers:

| Tier | Objective | Engineering Outcome |
|------|-----------|---------------------|
| **1. Estimative** | Estimate parameters of the life distribution (e.g., $\lambda$ for exponential, $\beta, \eta$ for Weibull) | Enables reliability prediction |
| **2. Comparative** | Compare the reliability of two or more designs, suppliers, or materials | Supports design selection |
| **3. Demonstrative** | Demonstrate that a product meets a specified reliability target (e.g., $R(1000\,\text{h}) \geq 0.95$) | Required for customer acceptance |
| **4. Predictive** | Extrapolate test data to obtain warranty life, MTBF, and B-life characteristics | Drives field-maintenance policy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Distinction:** *Demonstrative tests* (also called **reliability demonstration tests** or **RDTs**) are usually pass/fail acceptance tests prescribed by MIL-STD-781 or IEC 61124. *Estimative tests*, by contrast, aim to extract maximum statistical information.

---

## 1.4 Standard Test Metrics

The following quantities are reported on every life-test report:

- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)** — for non-repairable items: $E[T] = \int_0^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** — for repairable systems
- **B10 Life** — the time by which 10% of the population is expected to have failed
- **B50 Life** — the median life (50% failure point)
- **Reliability $R(t_L)$** — probability of survival up to mission time $t_L$

All these are derived from the same underlying dataset produced by the life test.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Life Tests (Master Taxonomy)

Life tests are classified along **three independent axes**. A given test can be described by combining the choice from each axis.

### Axis 1: By Data Completeness
- **Complete (Uncensored) Life Test:** Every item in the sample is run to failure. Yields $n$ exact failure times $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n$.
- **Censored Life Test:** Some items are observed for only a part of their lifetime (not all $t_i$ are known exactly).

### Axis 2: By Termination Rule
- **Time-Truncated (Type I Censoring):** The test is stopped at a **pre-fixed time $T$**. The number of failures $r$ is random.
- **Failure-Truncated (Type II Censoring):** The test is stopped as soon as a **pre-fixed number of failures $r$** is observed. The total test time is random.

### Axis 3: By Item Response
- **Destructive Test:** Failure renders the item unusable (e.g., a tensile-strength test on a bolt). Usually accelerated.
- **Non-Destructive Test:** The item continues to function after the test (e.g., a vibration test on a PCB).

---

## 2.2 Why Censoring is Necessary — The Five Engineering Realities

Censoring is not a statistical compromise — it is an **engineering necessity** for the following reasons:

1. **Time Pressure:** A 50,000-hour test on light bulbs is commercially infeasible.
2. **Cost Pressure:** Each test specimen may cost ₹5,00,000 (e.g., a turbine blade).
3. **Competing Failures:** An item may fail due to *test-fixture failure*, not because of its intrinsic reliability — these are *suspensions*.
4. **Heterogeneous Dropout:** Items may be removed for reasons unrelated to the test (e.g., operator error, accidental damage).
5. **Success-Oriented Testing:** For safety-critical components (e.g., aircraft engines), the test is often *success-biased* because few failures are allowed.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — The Complete Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used throughout:** $n$ = sample size, $r$ = observed failures, $T$ = censoring time (Type I), $t_i$ = observed failure times, $\delta_i$ = censoring indicator.

### 2.3.1 Censoring Types at a Glance

| Censoring Type | Stopping Rule | Random Quantity | What is known for each un-failed unit |
|----------------|---------------|-----------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Type I (Time-Truncated)** | Stop at fixed $t = T$ | Number of failures $r$ | Only that $T_i \geq T$ |
| **Type II (Failure-Truncated)** | Stop at fixed $r$ | Total test time $T^*$ | Only that $T_i \geq t_r$ |
| **Progressive Type II** | Remove units at successive failures | Number removed | $T_i \geq t_i$ (varies) |
| **Random (Type III) Censoring** | Independent censoring times $C_i$ | Both $r$ and duration | $T_i$ known for failures, $T_i \geq C_i$ for censored |
| **Interval Censoring** | Inspections at fixed intervals | Both $r$ and durations | Failure occurred in $(L_i, R_i]$ |
| **Left Censoring** | Failure occurred before observation | Both $r$ and duration | $T_i \leq C_i$ |

### 2.3.2 Likelihood Function Under Censoring

The likelihood for a mixture of $r$ exact failure times and $(n-r)$ right-censored observations under **Exponential distribution** is:

$$
L(\lambda) = \prod_{i=1}^{r} f(t_i) \cdot \prod_{i=r+1}^{n} R(C_i)
$$

For exponential density $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ and reliability $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$
L(\lambda) = \prod_{i=1}^{r} (\lambda e^{-\lambda t_i}) \cdot \prod_{i=r+1}^{n} e^{-\lambda C_i}
$$

### 2.3.3 MLE of Failure Rate Under Type I and Type II Censoring

For an **exponential** life distribution with $r$ failures, total test time $\mathcal{T}$:

$$
\hat{\lambda}_{MLE} = \frac{r}{\mathcal{T}}
$$

where the **total test time on test** is:

| Test Type | Total Time on Test $\mathcal{T}$ |
|-----------|----------------------------------|
| Complete | $\mathcal{T} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ |
| Type I censored (no suspensions) | $\mathcal{T} = \sum_{i=1}^{r} t_i + (n - r) \cdot T$ |
| Type II censored | $\mathcal{T} = \sum_{i=1}^{r} t_i + (n - r) \cdot t_r$ |
| Multiple failure modes / suspensions | $\mathcal{T} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \min(t_i, C_i)$ |

### 2.3.4 Confidence Interval for the MTTF (Exponential, Type II Censoring)

For Type II censoring, the two-sided $(1-\alpha)$ confidence interval for the true MTTF $\theta = 1/\lambda$ is:

$$
\left[ \frac{2 \mathcal{T}}{\chi^2_{2r,\; 1-\alpha/2}}, \;\; \frac{2 \mathcal{T}}{\chi^2_{2r,\; \alpha/2}} \right]
$$

where $\chi^2_{2r,\; p}$ denotes the $100p$-th percentile of the chi-square distribution with $2r$ degrees of freedom.

### 2.3.5 Fisher's Approximation for Expected Failures in Type I Censoring

The expected number of failures under Type I censoring at time $T$ for an exponential test is:

$$
E[r] = n \left[ 1 - e^{-\hat{\lambda} T} \right]
$$

This is used to **size the test** (choose $n$ and $T$) so that a specified $E[r]$ is achieved.

### 2.3.6 Reliability Function Estimate

Once $\hat{\lambda}$ is obtained, the reliability estimate is:

$$
\hat{R}(t) = e^{-\hat{\lambda} t}
$$

And the confidence bound at level $1 - \alpha$ (using the chi-square pivot) is:

$$
R_L(t) = \exp\left[ -\frac{\chi^2_{2r,\; 1-\alpha}}{2 \mathcal{T}} \cdot t \right]
$$

### 2.3.7 Kaplan-Meier (Product-Limit) Estimator (Non-parametric, Random Censoring)

For non-parametric life testing with random censoring, the survival function is estimated as:

$$
\hat{S}(t) = \prod_{t_i \leq t} \left( \frac{n_i - d_i}{n_i} \right)
$$

where $n_i$ = number at risk just before $t_i$, and $d_i$ = number of failures at $t_i$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Life Testing |
|----------|------------------------------|
| **Aerospace** | Demonstrating 30-year fatigue life of turbine blades (Type I censored with Weibull analysis) |
| **Electronics** | Burn-in of integrated circuits to eliminate infant mortality (Type I, complete failure) |
| **Automotive** | Mileage accumulation tests for engine bearings (Type II censored) |
| **Nuclear** | Accelerated life testing of control rods (Type I, log-normal distribution) |
| **Medical Devices** | Reliability demonstration of pacemakers (Type II censored under FDA/IEC 60601) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **MLE under Type II censoring** is the most frequently asked topic in KTU end-semester examinations. Always remember: **the censored items contribute the "survival" term $R(C_i) = e^{-\lambda C_i}$** to the likelihood, never zero.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — MLE of $\lambda$ Under Type II Right Censoring (Exponential)

**Problem Setup:** $n$ identical components are placed on a Type II censored life test. The test is terminated as soon as the $r$-th failure occurs, at time $t_r$. The remaining $(n - r)$ items are still working at $t_r$, hence we only know $T_i \geq t_r$ for them.

The $r$ observed failure times are $t_1 \leq t_2 \leq \dots \leq t_r$, with $t_{r+1} = \dots = t_n = t_r$ (censored).

### Step 1: Write the Joint Likelihood

Each of the $r$ failed items contributes a density $f(t_i) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t_i}$.

Each of the $(n - r)$ censored items contributes the *survival* probability:

$$
P(T_i \geq t_r) = R(t_r) = e^{-\lambda t_r}
$$

So the joint likelihood is:

$$
L(\lambda) = \left[ \prod_{i=1}^{r} \lambda e^{-\lambda t_i} \right] \cdot \left[ e^{-\lambda t_r} \right]^{n-r}
$$

$$
L(\lambda) = \lambda^r \cdot \exp\left[ -\lambda \sum_{i=1}^{r} t_i - \lambda (n - r) t_r \right]
$$

### Step 2: Take the Log-Likelihood

$$
\ln L(\lambda) = r \ln \lambda - \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{r} t_i - \lambda (n - r) t_r
$$

$$
\ln L(\lambda) = r \ln \lambda - \lambda \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{r} t_i + (n - r) t_r \right]
$$

### Step 3: Differentiate with Respect to $\lambda$

$$
\frac{d \ln L}{d \lambda} = \frac{r}{\lambda} - \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{r} t_i + (n - r) t_r \right]
$$

### Step 4: Set the Derivative to Zero

$$
\frac{r}{\lambda} - \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{r} t_i + (n - r) t_r \right] = 0
$$

### Step 5: Solve for $\hat{\lambda}$

$$
\hat{\lambda}_{MLE} = \frac{r}{\sum_{i=1}^{r} t_i + (n - r) t_r}
$$

### Step 6: Recognize the Denominator as Total Time on Test

By definition, total time on test under Type II censoring is:

$$
\mathcal{T} = \sum_{i=1}^{r} t_i + (n - r) t_r
$$

Hence the compact result:

$$
\boxed{\hat{\lambda}_{MLE} = \frac{r}{\mathcal{T}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Second-order check:** $\dfrac{d^2 \ln L}{d \lambda^2} = -\dfrac{r}{\lambda^2} < 0$, confirming a **maximum**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MLE of $\lambda$ Under Type I Time Truncation (Exponential)

**Setup:** $n$ items are tested, test ends at fixed time $T$, yielding $r$ failures with times $t_1, \dots, t_r$ and $(n - r)$ survivors.

### Step 1: Write the Likelihood

$$
L(\lambda) = \left[ \prod_{i=1}^{r} \lambda e^{-\lambda t_i} \right] \cdot \left[ e^{-\lambda T} \right]^{n-r}
$$

$$
L(\lambda) = \lambda^r \cdot \exp\left[ -\lambda \sum_{i=1}^{r} t_i - \lambda (n - r) T \right]
$$

### Step 2: Log-Likelihood

$$
\ln L(\lambda) = r \ln \lambda - \lambda \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{r} t_i + (n - r) T \right]
$$

### Step 3: Differentiate and Set to Zero

$$
\frac{d \ln L}{d \lambda} = \frac{r}{\lambda} - \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{r} t_i + (n - r) T \right] = 0
$$

### Step 4: Solve

$$
\boxed{\hat{\lambda}_{MLE} = \frac{r}{\sum_{i=1}^{r} t_i + (n - r) T}}
$$

Here, the total time on test is:

$$
\mathcal{T}_{Type\;I} = \sum_{i=1}^{r} t_i + (n - r) T
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The structural similarity to Type II censoring is **not coincidental** — both estimators are of the form $\hat{\lambda} = r / \mathcal{T}$, where the total time on test is the sum of failure times plus an *effective* survival contribution from each censored unit.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (14-Mark KTU Style)

> **Problem (Examination-Standard):**
> A reliability engineer places $n = 10$ identical sealed lead-acid batteries on a **Type II censored life test**. The test is stopped when $r = 4$ batteries have failed. The ordered failure times (in hours) are:
> $$t_1 = 320, \quad t_2 = 510, \quad t_3 = 780, \quad t_4 = 1040$$
> Assuming an exponential life distribution, calculate:
> (a) The MLE of the failure rate $\lambda$ and the MTTF.
> (b) The 95% two-sided confidence interval for the true MTTF.
> (c) The point estimate of $R(t)$ at $t = 1000$ hours.

### Part (a) — MLE of $\lambda$ and MTTF

**Step 1: Compute total time on test.**

$$
\mathcal{T} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} t_i + (n - r) t_r
$$

$$
\mathcal{T} = (320 + 510 + 780 + 1040) + (10 - 4)(1040)
$$

$$
\mathcal{T} = 2650 + 6240 = 8890 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2: MLE of failure rate.**

$$
\hat{\lambda} = \frac{r}{\mathcal{T}} = \frac{4}{8890} = 4.499 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per hour}
$$

**Step 3: MTTF estimate.**

$$
\widehat{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\hat{\lambda}} = \frac{8890}{4} = 2222.5 \text{ hours}
$$

### Part (b) — 95% Confidence Interval for MTTF

The pivot $2 \mathcal{T} / \theta \sim \chi^2_{2r} = \chi^2_{8}$.

From standard chi-square tables (KTU-allowed):

$$
\chi^2_{8,\; 0.025} = 2.180, \quad \chi^2_{8,\; 0.975} = 17.535
$$

**Lower bound on MTTF:**

$$
\theta_L = \frac{2 \mathcal{T}}{\chi^2_{8,\; 0.975}} = \frac{2 \times 8890}{17.535} = \frac{17780}{17.535} \approx 1014.0 \text{ hours}
$$

**Upper bound on MTTF:**

$$
\theta_U = \frac{2 \mathcal{T}}{\chi^2_{8,\; 0.025}} = \frac{17780}{2.180} \approx 8155.0 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{95\% \; CI: \;\; 1014.0 \text{ hours} \leq \theta \leq 8155.0 \text{ hours}}
$$

### Part (c) — Reliability at $t = 1000$ hours

$$
\hat{R}(1000) = e^{-\hat{\lambda} \cdot 1000} = e^{-0.4499} = 0.6374
$$

**Lower 95% confidence bound on $R(1000)$:**

$$
R_L(1000) = \exp\left[ -\frac{\chi^2_{8,\; 0.95}}{2\mathcal{T}} \cdot t \right] = \exp\left[ -\frac{15.507}{17780} \cdot 1000 \right]
$$

$$
R_L(1000) = \exp(-0.8722) \approx 0.418
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer Recap:**
> $\hat{\lambda} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4}$ /hr, $\widehat{MTTF} = 2222.5$ hr, 95% CI: (1014, 8155) hr, $\hat{R}(1000) = 0.637$ with lower bound 0.418.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of Censoring Schemes

| Feature | Type I (Time-Truncated) | Type II (Failure-Truncated) | Random Censoring | Interval Censoring |
|---------|--------------------------|------------------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Stopping rule | Fixed time $T$ | Fixed failures $r$ | Independent $C_i$ | Periodic inspections |
| Total test time $\mathcal{T}$ | Random | Deterministic upper bound | Random | Random |
| Number of failures | Random ($0$ to $n$) | Deterministic ($=r$) | Random | Random |
| MLE formula (exponential) | $r / \mathcal{T}$ | $r / \mathcal{T}$ | $r / \mathcal{T}$ | Requires iterative MLE |
| Confidence interval | Asymmetric (binomial) | Symmetric pivot ($\chi^2$) | Approximate (Greenwood) | Complex |
| Use case | Calendar-bounded tests | Cost-bounded tests | Field-data studies | Periodic inspection studies |
| KTU weightage | **Very High** | **Very High** | Medium | Low |

## 4.2 Type I vs Type II Censoring — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TypeI[Type I Time Truncated]
        TI1[Start n units at t=0] --> TI2[Operate continuously]
        TI2 --> TI3{Failure occurs?}
        TI3 -- Yes --> TI4[Record failure time ti]
        TI3 -- No --> TI5[Continue until t equals T]
        TI4 --> TI5
        TI5 --> TI6[Stop test at fixed T]
        TI6 --> TI7[Total time equals nT minus sum of failure times]
    end

    subgraph TypeII[Type II Failure Truncated]
        TII1[Start n units at t=0] --> TII2[Operate continuously]
        TII2 --> TII3[Count failures r]
        TII3 -- r less than n --> TII4[Continue]
        TII3 -- r equals r --> TII5[Stop at t equals t_r]
        TII4 --> TII3
        TII5 --> TII6[Total time equals sum ti plus n minus r times t_r]
    end
```
