# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define life testing. State any two of its primary objectives. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Life testing is an experimental procedure in which a sample of $n$ items is operated under controlled conditions to obtain failure-time data, with the goal of characterizing the life distribution and estimating reliability parameters.

Two primary objectives:
1. **Estimative Objective:** To estimate the parameters of the underlying time-to-failure distribution (e.g., $\lambda$, $\beta$, $\eta$).
2. **Demonstrative Objective:** To demonstrate that a product achieves a pre-specified reliability target, e.g., $R(1000 \text{ hr}) \geq 0.95$ at a stated confidence level.

---

### Q2. Distinguish between Type I and Type II censoring with one example each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Type I Censoring | Type II Censoring |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Stopping rule | Test ends at **fixed time $T$** | Test ends at **fixed number of failures $r$** |
| Random quantity | Number of failures | Total test time |
| Information | Time on test fixed | Sample size of failures fixed |
| Example | A 1000-hour burn-in test on ICs | A test stopped at the 5th failure of a bearing |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule:** In KTU 2024 ESE, every Part B question provides an **OR** choice. Below, Question A and Question B are independent alternatives of equal weightage (each with two sub-parts of 7 marks).

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Type II Censored MLE and Confidence Interval

> **Problem:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
> Twenty identical electronic modules are subjected to a Type II censored life test. The test is terminated as soon as the 6th failure occurs. The ordered failure times in hours are:
> $$t_1 = 145, \;\; t_2 = 280, \;\; t_3 = 410, \;\; t_4 = 590, \;\; t_5 = 720, \;\; t_6 = 885$$
> Assume the life distribution is exponential.

#### Part (a) — Derive the MLE of the failure rate and MTTF. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Write the likelihood function.**

$$
L(\lambda) = \prod_{i=1}^{6} \lambda e^{-\lambda t_i} \cdot \left[ e^{-\lambda t_6} \right]^{20-6}
$$

**Step 2: Take the log-likelihood.**

$$
\ln L(\lambda) = 6 \ln \lambda - \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{6} t_i - 14 \lambda t_6
$$

**Step 3: Differentiate and equate to zero.**

$$
\frac{d \ln L}{d \lambda} = \frac{6}{\lambda} - \sum_{i=1}^{6} t_i - 14 t_6 = 0
$$

**Step 4: Compute total time on test.**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{6} t_i = 145 + 280 + 410 + 590 + 720 + 885 = 3030 \text{ hr}
$$

$$
\mathcal{T} = 3030 + 14 \times 885 = 3030 + 12390 = 15420 \text{ hr}
$$

**Step 5: Solve for MLEs.**

$$
\hat{\lambda} = \frac{6}{15420} = 3.891 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per hour}
$$

$$
\widehat{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\hat{\lambda}} = 2570 \text{ hours}
$$

#### Part (b) — Construct the 90% confidence interval for the true MTTF. (7 marks)

**Step 1: Identify the pivot degrees of freedom.**

Degrees of freedom: $\nu = 2r = 12$.

**Step 2: Read the chi-square critical values at $\alpha = 0.10$ (90% CI).**

$$
\chi^2_{12,\; 0.05} = 5.226, \quad \chi^2_{12,\; 0.95} = 21.026
$$

**Step 3: Apply the Type II censoring CI formula.**

$$
\theta_L = \frac{2 \mathcal{T}}{\chi^2_{12,\; 0.95}} = \frac{2 \times 15420}{21.026} = \frac{30840}{21.026} \approx 1467.0 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\theta_U = \frac{2 \mathcal{T}}{\chi^2_{12,\; 0.05}} = \frac{30840}{5.226} \approx 5902.0 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4: State the confidence interval.**

$$
\boxed{90\% \; CI: \;\; 1467 \text{ hr} \leq \theta \leq 5902 \text{ hr}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Type I Censoring and Test Sizing

> **Problem:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> A Type I censored life test is planned on a batch of $n = 25$ ball bearings. The test is to be run for $T = 2000$ hours. Past data suggests the failure rate is approximately $\lambda_0 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour.

#### Part (a) — Find the expected number of failures in the 2000-hour test. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Apply Fisher's expected-failures formula.**

$$
E[r] = n \left[ 1 - e^{-\lambda_0 T} \right]
$$

**Step 2: Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\lambda_0 T = (5 \times 10^{-4})(2000) = 1.0
$$

$$
E[r] = 25 \left[ 1 - e^{-1} \right] = 25 \times (1 - 0.3679) = 25 \times 0.6321
$$

$$
\boxed{E[r] = 15.8 \text{ failures}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately 16 of the 25 bearings are expected to fail within 2000 hours.

#### Part (b) — If the first 5 observed failure times (in hours) are $120, 240, 380, 610, 850$, find the MLE of $\lambda$ assuming exponential life. (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute total time on test under Type I censoring.**

With $r = 5$ failures and $n - r = 20$ survivors at $T = 2000$ hr:

$$
\mathcal{T} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} t_i + (n - r) T
$$

$$
\mathcal{T} = (120 + 240 + 380 + 610 + 850) + 20 \times 2000
$$

$$
\mathcal{T} = 2200 + 40000 = 42200 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the MLE formula.**

$$
\hat{\lambda} = \frac{r}{\mathcal{T}} = \frac{5}{42200} = 1.185 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per hour}
$$

**Step 3: MTTF estimate.**

$$
\widehat{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\hat{\lambda}} = 8440 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply $(n-r)$ by $T$** in Type I censoring (or by $t_r$ in Type II). This is the most common error and leads to a 4–5 mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing MTTF with MTBF** — the MLE $1/\hat{\lambda}$ is MTTF (non-repairable). MTBF appears in repairable contexts.
> 3. **Using $\chi^2$ with $2n$ DOF instead of $2r$ DOF** in the confidence interval formula. Always use $2r$ when censored.
> 4. **Stating "Censored items are ignored"** — they MUST be included in the likelihood via the $R(C_i)$ survival term.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 4**

- ✅ **Life test** = controlled experiment to characterize time-to-failure distribution.
- ✅ **Three classification axes:** Data completeness, Termination rule, Item response.
- ✅ **Type I censoring** = fixed time $T$, random failures $r$.
- ✅ **Type II censoring** = fixed failures $r$, random total time.
- ✅ **MLE under exponential censoring:** $\hat{\lambda} = r / \mathcal{T}$ (universal formula).
- ✅ **Total time on test $\mathcal{T}$** = sum of failure times + contribution from each survivor.
- ✅ **Confidence interval for MTTF (Type II):** $\left[ \dfrac{2\mathcal{T}}{\chi^2_{2r,\,1-\alpha/2}}, \;\; \dfrac{2\mathcal{T}}{\chi^2_{2r,\,\alpha/2}} \right]$.
- ✅ **Degrees of freedom for chi-square pivot = $2r$, NOT $2n$** in censored tests.
- ✅ **Reliability estimate:** $\hat{R}(t) = e^{-\hat{\lambda} t}$.
- ✅ **Fisher's expected-failures formula:** $E[r] = n(1 - e^{-\lambda_0 T})$ — used to size tests.
- ✅ **Censored units contribute $R(C_i)$**, never zero, in the likelihood.
- ✅ **Real-world relevance:** warranty life prediction, MIL-STD-781, IEC 61124 demonstrations.
- ✅ **For non-parametric random censoring:** Kaplan-Meier product-limit estimator is the KTU-approved method.
- ✅ **B10 / B50 / B90 life** are read from the empirical CDF, not from the MLE directly.
- ✅ **Always state assumptions** (exponentiality, independence, identical items) before solving.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Life Testing: Objectives, Types, and Censoring**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accelerated Life Testing, HALT, HASS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET747 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Accelerated Life Testing, HALT, HASS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Accelerated Life Testing (ALT)

### Formal Definition
**Accelerated Life Testing (ALT)** is a systematic reliability testing methodology in which the product is subjected to stress conditions (thermal, electrical, mechanical, humidity, or combined) deliberately elevated above normal operating levels to induce failures in a compressed time frame. The failure data obtained is then analytically extrapolated through a valid **Acceleration Model** to estimate the product's life, reliability, and failure distribution under **Normal Use Conditions (NUC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** ALT is a *quantitative* reliability tool used to predict life characteristics (MTTF, $B_x$ life, failure rate $\lambda$) at design stress by testing at accelerated stress. It strictly requires a **statistically validated acceleration model**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you want to know how long a chocolate bar will last before melting on a beach in summer. Instead of waiting for summer (months), you place the chocolate in an oven at $150^\circ\text{C}$. It melts in 2 minutes. Using the Arrhenius chemistry relationship, you can mathematically "back-calculate" that at normal $35^\circ\text{C}$ beach temperature, it would have lasted, say, 4 hours. ALT works identically — it forces the product to fail faster by *heating it up*, *pumping more current*, or *vibrating harder*, and then mathematically cools it back to real-world conditions.

### The Three Families of ALT Stress Profiles

| ALT Type | Stress Behaviour | Primary Use |
|----------|------------------|-------------|
| **Constant Stress ALT** | Stress held fixed at elevated level | Classical reliability estimation, Arrhenius/IPLM |
| **Step-Stress ALT** | Stress raised in discrete steps after fixed time | Reducing test time, handling threshold effects |
| **Progressive (Ramped) Stress ALT** | Stress increased continuously (linearly) | Finding failure thresholds, prelude to HALT |

### Core Metrics in ALT
- **Acceleration Factor (AF):** $AF = \dfrac{t_{\text{use}}}{t_{\text{acc}}} \gg 1$
- **Stress Levels:** Number of distinct elevated stresses (commonly 2–4) at which units are tested.
- **Sample Size:** Number of units tested at each level (typically $\geq 30$ for statistical validity).
- **Censoring:** Failure times recorded; suspended units (non-failed) handled via right-censoring.

> [!NOTE]
> The Arrhenius equation governs *thermal* acceleration, while the **Inverse Power Law Model (IPLM)** governs *non-thermal* stresses (voltage, humidity, pressure, vibration).

---

## 1.2 HALT — Highly Accelerated Life Testing

### Formal Definition
**HALT (Highly Accelerated Life Testing)** is a *qualitative*, discovery-oriented, step-stress reliability methodology used during the **design and development phase** to rapidly uncover the inherent weaknesses and latent defects of a product by progressively escalating combined environmental stresses (thermal, vibration, power cycling) until the unit reaches its **Operating Limit** and ultimately its **Destruct Limit**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> HALT does **NOT** predict field reliability numerically. It identifies *where* and *how* the product fails so that design robustness can be improved.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of HALT as a **"stress doctor"** for your product. Like a doctor who pushes your heart rate, breathing, and reflexes to extremes during an exercise stress test to find weak organs, HALT hammers your product with extreme cold, heat, vibration, and rapid thermal shocks to find *design weak links* (solder joints, connectors, capacitors, fasteners) before the customer ever sees them.

### The Four HALT Margins
1. **Lower Operating Limit (LOL):** Coldest temperature at which the unit still operates as specified.
2. **Upper Operating Limit (UOL):** Hottest temperature at which the unit still operates as specified.
3. **Lower Destruct Limit (LDL):** Coldest temperature at which physical destruction occurs.
4. **Upper Destruct Limit (UDL):** Hottest temperature at which physical destruction occurs.

The **Design Margin** is widened by redesign until UOL and LOL exceed the original specification envelope by typically **$\geq 20$–$25\%$**.

---

## 1.3 HASS — Highly Accelerated Stress Screening

### Formal Definition
**HASS (Highly Accelerated Stress Screening)** is a *production-stage* reliability screening process applied to **every manufactured unit** of a product that has already completed HALT. It uses stresses *derived from* the HALT margins (usually a fraction — about **70–80\%** of the destruct limit) to precipitate manufacturing and workmanship defects (latent defects) as detectable failures, ensuring only robust units reach the customer.

> [!NOTE]
> HASS is the *production* counterpart of HALT. If HALT is the "doctor's stress test," HASS is the "routine health check-up at the factory gate."

### Conceptual Analogy
After a car is designed and stress-tested by the manufacturer (HALT), every car coming off the assembly line is subjected to a short, brutal test track (HASS) that exposes cars with defective brake pads, loose bolts, or faulty sensors. The bad cars fail the test and are reworked. The good ones pass and ship to the dealer. HASS guarantees that no defective unit escapes into the market.

### Key Differences at a Glance

| Feature | HALT | HASS |
|---------|------|------|
| **Stage** | Design / Development | Production |
| **Goal** | Find design weaknesses | Screen manufacturing defects |
| **Scope** | A few prototype units | **100\% of production units** |
| **Duration** | Long (3–5 days per unit) | Short (typically < 1 hour per unit) |
| **Output** | Operating & Destruct Limits | Pass / Fail decision |
| **Outcome** | Design improvements | Defect removal / rework |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind ALT — Acceleration Models

ALT is mathematically grounded in a chosen **Acceleration Model** that relates lifetime at elevated stress to lifetime at normal stress. Three models dominate KTU-level treatments.

### Model 1: Arrhenius Model (Thermal Acceleration)
Applicable when the dominant failure mechanism is **chemically or thermally activated** (e.g., corrosion, dielectric breakdown, polymer degradation, electromigration).

The Arrhenius reaction-rate equation gives the time-to-failure $t$ at absolute temperature $T$ (in Kelvin):

$$
t = A \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

Where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential constant (material/process dependent)
- $E_a$ = **Activation Energy** (eV)
- $k_B$ = **Boltzmann Constant** $= 8.617 \times 10^{-5} \text{ eV/K}$
- $T$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin

The **Thermal Acceleration Factor** between an accelerated temperature $T_{\text{acc}}$ and the use temperature $T_{\text{use}}$ is:

$$
AF_T = \dfrac{t_{\text{use}}}{t_{\text{acc}}} = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{k_B}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{use}}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{acc}}}\right)\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $1/T$ and not $T$?** Chemical reaction rates depend on the *fraction* of molecules crossing the activation energy barrier, which is a Boltzmann probability $\exp(-E_a/k_BT)$. The reciprocal $1/T$ therefore linearises the logarithm of failure time — a cornerstone of reliability data analysis.

### Model 2: Inverse Power Law Model — IPLM (Non-Thermal Acceleration)
Used for stresses like voltage $V$, current $I$, pressure $P$, humidity, vibration $g$:

$$
t = \dfrac{A}{S^n}
$$

Where $S$ is the stress level and $n$ is the **acceleration exponent** (material dependent). The **Acceleration Factor** is:

$$
AF_{IPLM} = \left(\dfrac{S_{\text{acc}}}{S_{\text{use}}}\right)^{n}
$$

For voltage acceleration (commonly used in capacitor and insulation studies), $n$ typically ranges from **2 to 5**.

### Model 3: Eyring Model (Generalised Thermal-Mechanical)
More general than Arrhenius; reduces to Arrhenius as a special case. Often used for combined temperature-humidity:

$$
t = \dfrac{A}{T} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{B}{k_B T}\right) \cdot \exp\!\left(C \cdot V\right)
$$

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exam purposes, the **Arrhenius** model is the most commonly tested. Memorise the form of $AF_T$ and the meaning of $E_a$, $k_B$, and the $1/T$ dependence.

---

## 2.2 Logical Flow of an ALT Program

A properly designed ALT program follows this sequence:

1. **Identify the dominant failure mechanism(s)** of the product (e.g., solder fatigue, capacitor ageing).
2. **Choose a valid acceleration model** (Arrhenius for thermal, IPLM for voltage, etc.).
3. **Select at least 2–3 elevated stress levels** (often in geometric progression: e.g., 85°C, 105°C, 125°C).
4. **Allocate test units** ($\geq 30$ per level for Weibull/lognormal goodness-of-fit).
5. **Run tests to failure** (or censoring at a pre-set time).
6. **Fit the chosen life distribution** (Weibull, lognormal, exponential) to failure data at each stress.
7. **Verify model assumptions** (same failure mechanism at all stresses — check via probability plots).
8. **Extrapolate** to normal-use conditions and report MTTF, $B_{10}$ life, hazard rate, etc.

---

## 2.3 HALT Step-Stress Methodology

The HALT procedure follows a structured escalation:

1. **Baseline Test** — Verify functionality at ambient (25°C) and at spec limits.
2. **Thermal Step-Stress** — Lower temperature in steps of 5–10°C (e.g., +20, +10, 0, −10, −20, −30, −40°C) until failure; then raise temperature in similar steps (e.g., +30, +40, +50, +60, +70, +80, +90, +100°C).
3. **Vibration Step-Stress** — Increase $g$-RMS in steps (e.g., 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50 $g$-RMS) until failure.
4. **Combined Environment** — Apply thermal + vibration simultaneously (the most revealing part of HALT).
5. **Destruct Testing** — Push to physical destruction to find ultimate limits.
6. **Failure Analysis & Redesign** — Document each failure mode, root-cause it, redesign, and re-test.

> [!WARNING]
> HALT is destructive by design. Units that go through HALT are **not** shipped. They are prototypes used to improve the design, not to validate field reliability numerically.

---

## 2.4 HASS Methodology

HASS uses the HALT-derived limits to define a **Safe Operating Screen Envelope**:

- **Temperature Range:** Typically set to **$\approx 70$–$80\%$ of the Destruct Limit** found in HALT.
- **Vibration Level:** Typically set to **$\approx 50$–$70\%$ of the HALT destruct vibration level**.
- **Thermal Ramp Rate:** Aggressive — usually **$30$–$60^\circ\text{C/min}$** to induce thermal-shock stress.
- **Duration:** Compact — **15 min to 1 hour** per unit so as not to add excessive cost.

HASS includes two stages:
- **HASS Audit (Initial HASS):** Performed on first production units to verify the screen settings.
- **Production HASS:** Applied to every production unit thereafter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> HASS uses **temperature cycling + vibration** because these two stresses combined trigger **$\geq 90\%$ of common workmanship defects** (solder cracks, cold joints, loose fasteners, capacitor failures).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Key Variables | Typical Units |
|-------------------|------------|---------------|---------------|
| Arrhenius Time-to-Failure | $t = A \exp(E_a / k_B T)$ | $A, E_a, T$ | $t$ in h, $T$ in K |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k_B$ | — | $\mathbf{8.617 \times 10^{-5}}$ eV/K |
| Thermal Acceleration Factor | $AF_T = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{k_B}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{use}}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{acc}}}\right)\right]$ | $E_a, T$ | Dimensionless |
| Inverse Power Law Life | $t = A / S^n$ | $A, n$ | $t$ in h, $S$ in stress units |
| IPLM Acceleration Factor | $AF_{IPLM} = (S_{\text{acc}} / S_{\text{use}})^n$ | $S, n$ | Dimensionless |
| Acceleration Factor (General) | $AF = t_{\text{use}} / t_{\text{acc}}$ | $t$ | Dimensionless |
| HALT Step Increment | $\Delta T, \Delta g$ | — | °C, $g$-RMS |
| HASS Screen Envelope | $\approx 0.7 \text{–} 0.8 \times \text{Destruct Limit}$ | — | Fraction |
| Cumulative Hazard (Weibull) | $H(t) = (t / \eta)^{\beta}$ | $\beta, \eta$ | Dimensionless |
| MTTF (Weibull) | $\eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ | $\eta, \beta$ | h |

> [!NOTE]
> Always convert temperatures to **Kelvin** ($K = °C + 273.15$) before substituting into Arrhenius-based equations. This is a classic KTU answer-script pitfall.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of ALT | HALT | HASS |
|-------|-------------------|------|------|
| **Electronics (PCBs, ICs)** | Capacitor ageing, solder joint fatigue | Finding weakest component | Production screening |
| **Automotive (ECUs)** | Thermal cycling of under-hood electronics | Robustness to engine-bay temps | 100% ECU screening |
| **Aerospace (Avionics)** | Long-term reliability of flight computers | Pre-qualification robustness | Mandatory in DO-160 testing |
| **Medical Devices** | Battery life, pacemaker electronics | Patient-safety margins | FDA-mandated screening |
| **Semiconductors** | Electromigration, TDDB studies | HCI, NBTI analysis | Burn-in replacement |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Arrhenius Acceleration Factor

The Arrhenius equation (1889) describes the rate of a chemical/physical process:

$$
k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

This is the *rate* constant. Since time-to-failure is **inversely proportional** to the rate of the degradation process (faster rate $\Rightarrow$ shorter time to failure), we have:

$$
t = \dfrac{C}{k} = \dfrac{C}{A} \cdot \exp\!\left(+\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

where $C$ is a proportionality constant. Absorbing $C/A$ into a new constant $A'$:

$$
t = A' \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right) \quad \text{...(Eq. 1)}
$$

This is the standard form used in reliability engineering. For the same failure mechanism occurring at two different temperatures $T_{\text{use}}$ and $T_{\text{acc}}$:

$$
t_{\text{use}} = A' \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T_{\text{use}}}\right) \quad \text{...(Eq. 2)}
$$

$$
t_{\text{acc}} = A' \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T_{\text{acc}}}\right) \quad \text{...(Eq. 3)}
$$

Taking the ratio (this is the *definition* of the acceleration factor — how much faster failure occurs at elevated stress):

$$
AF_T = \dfrac{t_{\text{use}}}{t_{\text{acc}}} = \dfrac{A' \cdot \exp(E_a / k_B T_{\text{use}})}{A' \cdot \exp(E_a / k_B T_{\text{acc}})}
$$

The $A'$ constants cancel. The exponential ratio is split by the rule $\exp(x)/\exp(y) = \exp(x-y)$:

$$
AF_T = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T_{\text{use}}} - \dfrac{E_a}{k_B T_{\text{acc}}}\right]
$$

$$
AF_T = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{k_B}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{use}}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{acc}}}\right)\right]
$$

Since $T_{\text{acc}} > T_{\text{use}}$, the term inside the parentheses is **negative**, so $\exp(\text{negative}) < 1$… wait — we need to be careful with sign convention. Let us re-derive using a positive acceleration factor convention.

> **Convention Check:** At elevated stress, failure is *faster*, so $t_{\text{acc}} < t_{\text{use}}$, meaning $AF = t_{\text{use}}/t_{\text{acc}} > 1$. Since $T_{\text{acc}} > T_{\text{use}}$, the term $(1/T_{\text{use}} - 1/T_{\text{acc}}) > 0$, and the exponential is $> 1$. ✓

### Numerical Worked Example (Arrhenius AF)

**Problem:** A component has activation energy $E_a = 0.8$ eV. Compute the thermal acceleration factor between use at $T_{\text{use}} = 40^\circ\text{C}$ and accelerated testing at $T_{\text{acc}} = 125^\circ\text{C}$.

**Step 1: Convert temperatures to Kelvin.**

$$
T_{\text{use}} = 40 + 273.15 = 313.15 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_{\text{acc}} = 125 + 273.15 = 398.15 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the temperature term $(1/T_{\text{use}} - 1/T_{\text{acc}})$.**

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{use}}} = \dfrac{1}{313.15} = 3.1934 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{acc}}} = \dfrac{1}{398.15} = 2.5116 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{use}}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{acc}}} = (3.1934 - 2.5116) \times 10^{-3} = 0.6818 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the activation-energy term $E_a / k_B$.**

$$
\dfrac{E_a}{k_B} = \dfrac{0.8}{8.617 \times 10^{-5}} = 9284.16 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 4: Multiply and exponentiate.**

$$
\dfrac{E_a}{k_B} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{use}}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{acc}}}\right) = 9284.16 \times 0.6818 \times 10^{-3} = 6.330
$$

$$
AF_T = \exp(6.330) = 561.5
$$

**Interpretation:** One hour of test time at $125^\circ\text{C}$ simulates $\approx 561.5$ hours ($\approx 23.4$ days) of operation at $40^\circ\text{C}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the IPLM Acceleration Factor

The Inverse Power Law Model assumes lifetime decreases as a power of the applied stress:

$$
t = \dfrac{A}{S^n} \quad \text{...(Eq. 4)}
$$

At two different stress levels (use and accelerated):

$$
t_{\text{use}} = \dfrac{A}{S_{\text{use}}^{\,n}} \quad , \quad t_{\text{acc}} = \dfrac{A}{S_{\text{acc}}^{\,n}} \quad \text{...(Eq. 5)}
$$

Taking the ratio:

$$
AF_{IPLM} = \dfrac{t_{\text{use}}}{t_{\text{acc}}} = \dfrac{A / S_{\text{use}}^{\,n}}{A / S_{\text{acc}}^{\,n}} = \left(\dfrac{S_{\text{acc}}}{S_{\text{use}}}\right)^{n} \quad \text{...(Eq. 6)}
$$

The $A$ cancels; the powers simplify. This is the most common non-thermal acceleration model used for voltage, current, and mechanical stress.

### Numerical Example (IPLM AF)

**Problem:** An insulating material exhibits an inverse power law with $n = 3$. Compute the AF when voltage is raised from $V_{\text{use}} = 220$ V to $V_{\text{acc}} = 440$ V.

**Step 1: Identify stress ratio.**

$$
\dfrac{V_{\text{acc}}}{V_{\text{use}}} = \dfrac{440}{220} = 2
$$

**Step 2: Apply the IPLM AF formula.**

$$
AF = (2)^{3} = 8
$$

**Interpretation:** Doubling the voltage accelerates failure by a factor of 8 (cubic relationship).

---

## 3.3 Relationship Between AF and MTTF

If MTTF at use conditions is $\text{MTTF}_{\text{use}}$ and at accelerated conditions is $\text{MTTF}_{\text{acc}}$, then by definition of the acceleration factor:

$$
\text{MTTF}_{\text{use}} = AF \times \text{MTTF}_{\text{acc}}
$$

This is the central engineering utility of ALT — a few hours of accelerated testing yields an estimate of the field MTTF.

### Example: Estimating Field MTTF from ALT

**Problem:** A batch of electrolytic capacitors is tested at $T_{\text{acc}} = 105^\circ\text{C}$ and exhibits a Weibull MTTF of $850$ hours. The use temperature is $T_{\text{use}} = 55^\circ\text{C}$, and the activation energy is $E_a = 1.0$ eV. Estimate the field MTTF.

**Step 1: Convert temperatures.**

$$
T_{\text{use}} = 55 + 273.15 = 328.15 \text{ K} \quad , \quad T_{\text{acc}} = 105 + 273.15 = 378.15 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $(1/T_{\text{use}} - 1/T_{\text{acc}})$.**

$$
\dfrac{1}{328.15} - \dfrac{1}{378.15} = 3.0475 \times 10^{-3} - 2.6445 \times 10^{-3} = 4.030 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $E_a/k_B$.**

$$
\dfrac{1.0}{8.617 \times 10^{-5}} = 11{,}605.2 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 4: Exponentiate.**

$$
AF = \exp(11{,}605.2 \times 4.030 \times 10^{-4}) = \exp(4.677) = 107.4
$$

**Step 5: Estimate field MTTF.**

$$
\text{MTTF}_{\text{use}} = AF \times \text{MTTF}_{\text{acc}} = 107.4 \times 850 = 91{,}290 \text{ hours} \approx 10.4 \text{ years}
$$

---

## 3.4 Step-Stress ALT — Cumulative Damage Model (Quick Derivation)

In step-stress ALT, a unit is first stressed at $S_1$ for time $\tau_1$ and then at higher stress $S_2$ until failure at time $\tau_2$. Assuming the **Cumulative Damage Hypothesis**, the fraction of life consumed at $S_1$ plus that consumed at $S_2$ equals unity at failure. If $t_1$ and $t_2$ are the MTTFs at $S_1$ and $S_2$ respectively, then:

$$
\dfrac{\tau_1}{t_1} + \dfrac{\tau_2 - \tau_1}{t_2} = 1
$$

Solving for the failure time $\tau_2$:

$$
\tau_2 = \tau_1 + t_2 \left(1 - \dfrac{\tau_1}{t_1}\right) = \tau_1 + t_2 - \dfrac{\tau_1 \cdot t_2}{t_1}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\tau_2 = \tau_1 \left(1 - \dfrac{t_2}{t_1}\right) + t_2
$$

This result is used in **Nelson's cumulative damage model** and similar KTU-referenced treatments.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ALT Process Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Define Product and Mission Profile] --> mechA[Identify Dominant Failure Mechanism]
    mechA --> modelA{Select Acceleration Model}
    modelA --> arrheniusA[Arrhenius Model - Thermal]
    modelA --> iplmA[Inverse Power Law - Non Thermal]
    modelA --> eyringA[Eyring Model - Combined]
    arrheniusA --> stressA[Choose Elevated Stress Levels]
    iplmA --> stressA
    eyringA --> stressA
    stressA --> sampleA[Allocate Test Units n >= 30 per level]
    sampleA --> runA[Run Tests to Failure or Censoring]
    runA --> fitA[Fit Life Distribution Weibull or Lognormal]
    fitA --> checkA{Verify Same Failure Mechanism at All Stresses}
    checkA -->|No| stopA[STOP: Mechanism Changed - Invalid]
    checkA -->|Yes| extrapA[Extrapolate to Normal Use Conditions]
    extrapA --> outputA[Report MTTF, Bx Life, Hazard Rate at Use Conditions]
    outputA --> endA[End of ALT Program]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The ALT workflow is a *gated* process. The most critical decision gate is "Verify Same Failure Mechanism" — if accelerated stress changes the *physics* of failure, extrapolation back to use conditions becomes meaningless.

---

## 4.2 HALT Step-Stress Escalation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB[Start HALT on Prototype Unit] --> baseB[Baseline Functional Test at 25 C]
    baseB --> coolB[Step Down Cold: plus 20 to minus 60 C in steps of 10 C]
    coolB --> lolB[Identify Lower Operating Limit LOL]
    lolB --> ldlB[Continue to Lower Destruct Limit LDL]
    ldlB --> heatB[Step Up Hot: plus 30 to plus 120 C in steps of 10 C]
    heatB --> uolB[Identify Upper Operating Limit UOL]
    uolB --> udlB[Continue to Upper Destruct Limit UDL]
    udlB --> vibB[Vibration Step Stress: 5 to 50 g RMS in steps of 5 g]
    vibB --> combB[Combined Environment: Thermal plus Vibration]
    combB --> failB[Record All Failure Modes and Limits]
    failB --> rdB[Root Cause Analysis and Design Improvement]
    rdB --> retestB[Re Test Improved Prototype]
    retestB --> doneB[Updated HALT Margins - Move to HASS]
```

---

## 4.3 HASS Production Screening Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startC[Production Unit Arrives at HASS Station] --> presC[Pre Screen Functional Check]
    presC --> thermC[Rapid Thermal Cycling: minus 30 C to plus 85 C at 40 C per min]
    thermC --> vibC[Vibration Screen: 10 to 20 g RMS]
    vibC --> combC[Combined Thermal plus Vibration Profile]
    combC --> postC[Post Screen Functional Check]
    postC --> decC{Unit Passes All Specs}
    decC -->|No| reworkC[Send to Rework Station: Diagnose and Repair]
    decC -->|Yes| shipC[Ship to Customer]
    reworkC --> retestC[Return Unit to HASS for Re Verification]
    retestC --> postC
    shipC --> endC[End of HASS Cycle]
```

---

## 4.4 Comparison Diagram: ALT vs HALT vs HASS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phase1[Phase 1: Design] --> halt[HALT]
    halt --> limits[Operating and Destruct Limits]
    limits --> hass[HASS Setup]
    hass --> phase2[Phase 2: Production]
    phase2 --> hassRun[Run HASS on Every Unit]
    alt[ALT Parallel Track: Quantitative Life Prediction] --> report[Reliability Prediction Report]
    halt --> improve[Design Improvements]
    improve --> phase2
```

> **Engineering Interpretation:** HALT and HASS form a *coupled design-to-production reliability pipeline*, while ALT runs as a *parallel quantitative track* providing numerical life estimates for the KTU-expected reliability metrics (MTTF, $B_x$, $\lambda$).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define Accelerated Life Testing (ALT). Mention any two acceleration models used in ALT.**

**Model Answer:**
Accelerated Life Testing is a reliability test methodology in which products are subjected to stress levels (thermal, electrical, mechanical, etc.) deliberately higher than normal operating conditions to induce failures in a shorter time. The failure data is then extrapolated to normal use conditions using a valid acceleration model to estimate reliability parameters such as MTTF, $B_x$ life, and failure rate.

Two acceleration models:
1. **Arrhenius Model** — for thermally activated failure mechanisms: $t = A \exp(E_a / k_B T)$.
2. **Inverse Power Law Model (IPLM)** — for non-thermal stresses (voltage, humidity, vibration): $t = A / S^n$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between HALT and HASS in terms of purpose, stage of application, and outcome.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | HALT | HASS |
|--------|------|------|
| **Full Form** | Highly Accelerated Life Testing | Highly Accelerated Stress Screening |
| **Purpose** | Discover design weaknesses & limits | Screen manufacturing/workmanship defects |
| **Stage** | Design & development phase | Production phase |
| **Units Tested** | A few prototypes | Every production unit (100%) |
| **Outcome** | Operating & Destruct Limits; design improvements | Pass / Fail decision; rework if failed |
| **Duration** | Long (3–5 days per unit) | Short (minutes to 1 hour) |

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)
*Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Derive the Arrhenius acceleration factor expression starting from the Arrhenius rate equation. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A semiconductor device has activation energy $E_a = 0.7$ eV. Compute the acceleration factor when accelerated at $T_{\text{acc}} = 150^\circ\text{C}$ and used at $T_{\text{use}} = 50^\circ\text{C}$. If the MTTF at the accelerated condition is $1200$ hours, estimate the MTTF at use conditions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**
Starting from the Arrhenius rate equation for the rate of degradation $k$:

$$
k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

Since time-to-failure $t$ is inversely proportional to the rate:

$$
t = \dfrac{C}{k} = \dfrac{C}{A} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right) = A' \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

For the same failure mechanism at two temperatures $T_{\text{use}}$ and $T_{\text{acc}}$:

$$
t_{\text{use}} = A' \exp(E_a/k_B T_{\text{use}}), \quad t_{\text{acc}} = A' \exp(E_a/k_B T_{\text{acc}})
$$

Taking the ratio, $A'$ cancels:

$$
AF_T = \dfrac{t_{\text{use}}}{t_{\text{acc}}} = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{k_B}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{use}}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{acc}}}\right)\right]
$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The dominant failure mechanism remains the same at both $T_{\text{use}}$ and $T_{\text{acc}}$.
2. The activation energy $E_a$ is constant over the stress range.
3. No competing failure modes appear at elevated stress.
4. The Arrhenius relationship is valid over the temperature range used.

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Step 1:** Convert to Kelvin.

$$
T_{\text{use}} = 50 + 273.15 = 323.15 \text{ K}, \quad T_{\text{acc}} = 150 + 273.15 = 423.15 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $E_a / k_B$.

$$
\dfrac{E_a}{k_B} = \dfrac{0.7}{8.617 \times 10^{-5}} = 8123.6 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $(1/T_{\text{use}} - 1/T_{\text{acc}})$.

$$
\dfrac{1}{323.15} - \dfrac{1}{423.15} = 3.0946 \times 10^{-3} - 2.3633 \times 10^{-3} = 7.313 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $AF$.

$$
AF = \exp(8123.6 \times 7.313 \times 10^{-4}) = \exp(5.941) = 381.0
$$

**Step 5:** Compute $\text{MTTF}_{\text{use}}$.

$$
\text{MTTF}_{\text{use}} = AF \times \text{MTTF}_{\text{acc}} = 381.0 \times 1200 = 4.572 \times 10^{5} \text{ hours} \approx 52.2 \text{ years}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Explain with a neat flowchart the HALT procedure. List the four key limits identified during HALT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is HASS? Explain how the HALT margins are used to set the HASS screen envelope. Why is HASS applied to every production unit while HALT is applied only to prototypes? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** The HALT procedure involves a structured step-stress escalation:

1. **Baseline test** at ambient (25°C).
2. **Cold step-stress** in increments of 5–10°C down to typically −60°C or beyond.
3. **Hot step-stress** in increments of 5–10°C up to typically +120°C or beyond.
4. **Vibration step-stress** from low $g$-RMS (5$g$) to destruct levels (50$g$ or more).
5. **Combined environment** (thermal + vibration) — the most revealing phase.
6. **Record limits, perform failure analysis, redesign, retest.**

**Four Key Limits Identified:**

| Limit | Symbol | Meaning |
|-------|--------|---------|
| Lower Operating Limit | LOL | Coldest temp at which unit still operates per spec |
| Upper Operating Limit | UOL | Hottest temp at which unit still operates per spec |
| Lower Destruct Limit | LDL | Coldest temp at which physical destruction occurs |
| Upper Destruct Limit | UDL | Hottest temp at which physical destruction occurs |

The design margin is widened to ensure LOL and UOL exceed the original specification by $\geq 20$–$25\%$.

**Part (b):** HASS (Highly Accelerated Stress Screening) is a production-stage reliability test applied to every manufactured unit of a product that has already completed HALT. The HALT-derived limits are used to set the HASS screen envelope as follows:

- **Temperature screen range:** $\approx 70$–$80\%$ of the HALT destruct limits (with rapid ramp rate of 30–60°C/min).
- **Vibration screen level:** $\approx 50$–$70\%$ of the HALT destruct vibration level.
- **Duration:** 15 minutes to 1 hour per unit.

HASS is applied to **every production unit** because manufacturing and workmanship defects (solder cracks, cold joints, loose fasteners, component defects) occur randomly on the assembly line, and even a small defect rate can cause significant field failures. HALT, in contrast, is applied to prototypes because it is destructive and time-consuming; its purpose is to find design weaknesses so they can be fixed before production begins, not to validate reliability numerically.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert °C to **Kelvin** before substituting into Arrhenius — loses 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. Writing $E_a/k_B$ value with incorrect units or using $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K instead of **$8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K** — leads to wrong exponent and wrong AF.
> 3. Confusing **HALT** (design, qualitative) with **HASS** (production, screening) — examiners mark this strictly.
> 4. Stating that HALT "predicts field reliability" — it does **not**; it only identifies design margins.
> 5. Omitting the **same-mechanism assumption** when deriving the Arrhenius AF — full marks require this assumption to be stated.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ALT, HALT, HASS**

- **ALT** is *quantitative*; HALT and HASS are *qualitative/screening* tools.
- **Arrhenius Model** is for thermal stress; remember $t = A \exp(E_a / k_B T)$ and the AF formula with $1/T$ differences.
- **Boltzmann constant:** $\mathbf{8.617 \times 10^{-5}}$ eV/K (use this — not the J/K form — for eV-based Arrhenius).
- **IPLM** is for voltage, humidity, vibration: $AF = (S_{\text{acc}}/S_{\text{use}})^n$.
- **AF $> 1$** at elevated stress; always report which is the use and which is the accelerated condition.
- **MTTF scaling:** $\text{MTTF}_{\text{use}} = AF \times \text{MTTF}_{\text{acc}}$.
- **HALT** = Design phase, prototype units, finds operating & destruct limits, **destructive**, no field-reliability prediction.
- **HASS** = Production phase, every unit, screen envelope set to 70–80% of HALT destruct limits, **non-destructive** (or reworkable).
- **Four HALT limits:** LOL, UOL (operating), LDL, UDL (destruct).
- **Same-mechanism assumption** is critical for valid ALT extrapolation; verify with probability plots.
- **Convert all temperatures to Kelvin** before any Arrhenius calculation.
- **Sample size $\geq 30$** per stress level for Weibull/lognormal fitting.
- **Common defects screened by HASS:** solder cracks, cold joints, loose fasteners, defective capacitors, incorrect component placement.
- **HALT output $\rightarrow$ HASS input:** The destruct limits of HALT are the *reference* for setting HASS screen envelopes.
- **Step-stress ALT uses cumulative damage hypothesis** when deriving failure time across stress steps.
- **HALT is not a substitute for qualification testing** — it complements it by improving design margins.
- **HASS reduces burn-in time and cost** while providing equivalent or better defect screening effectiveness.
- **Reliability metrics obtained from ALT:** MTTF, $B_x$ life, $\lambda(t)$, $R(t)$, $F(t)$ at normal use conditions.
- **Practical acceleration ranges:** Typical thermal AF values range from $10$ to $1000$ depending on $E_a$ and $\Delta T$.
